name: HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY

topics.title: Hydrogen Economy - Decarbonisation of the Economy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Economy - Decarbonisation of the Economy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Hydrogen Economy

A **Hydrogen Economy** is an energy system framework in which **hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$)** is used as the primary low-carbon energy carrier — produced predominantly from water using renewable electricity — to supply, store, and deliver energy across industrial, mobility, power, and heating sectors, thereby displacing fossil fuels and enabling deep **decarbonisation** of the global economy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEBBT616, Module 1):**
> *"Hydrogen Economy refers to the transitioning of the world's energy supply from fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) to hydrogen as the principal carbon-free energy vector, where hydrogen is produced from renewable/clean sources, stored, transported, and converted to electricity, heat, or mechanical work with water as the only by-product."*

### 1.2 Formal Definition: Decarbonisation of the Economy

**Decarbonisation** is the systematic process of reducing **carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$)** and other greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions released into the atmosphere from human economic activity, achieved by replacing carbon-intensive energy sources (fossil fuels) with carbon-neutral or zero-carbon alternatives such as renewable electricity and **green hydrogen**.

The net chemical reaction symbolising complete decarbonisation when hydrogen is used in a fuel cell:

$$\text{2H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

> The **only by-product is water** ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) — making hydrogen the cleanest end-use fuel.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Hydrogen as the Electricity of the Future"**
>
> Think of today's energy system as a **one-currency economy** where the only money is **coal, oil, and natural gas**. Every sector — transport, industry, homes — must spend this same dirty currency, and every transaction pollutes the air.
>
> Now imagine the government issues a **new, clean currency called "Hydrogen"**:
> - **Central Bank (Electrolyser)** mints it using solar/wind power and water.
> - **Wallets (Storage tanks)** hold it for later use.
> - **ATMs (Refuelling stations)** dispense it to cars, trucks, and factories.
> - **Shops (Fuel cells & turbines)** accept it and give back energy + **only water as change**.
>
> The *Hydrogen Economy* is the legal/regulatory framework that allows this new clean currency to circulate, while **Decarbonisation** is the act of converting all old, dirty cash (fossil fuels) into the new clean currency over time.

### 1.4 Why is Decarbonisation the Need of the Hour?

| Global Threat | Current Status (IPCC AR6, 2024 Reference) | Hydrogen's Role |
|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric $\text{CO}_2$ concentration | **~422 ppm** (vs. pre-industrial 280 ppm) | Replaces fossil combustion |
| Global mean temperature rise | **+1.45°C above pre-industrial** | Clean energy vector |
| Net-Zero Target Year | **2050** (Paris Agreement) | Backbone of hard-to-abate sectors |
| Share of fossil fuels in global energy | **~80%** | Targeted reduction to <50% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants (must memorise):**
> - Higher Heating Value (HHV) of $\text{H}_2$ = **142 MJ/kg**
> - Lower Heating Value (LHV) of $\text{H}_2$ = **120 MJ/kg**
> - Energy density (volumetric, gas at 200 bar) = **~2.6 MJ/L** (low — hence the need for compression/liquefaction)
> - Density of liquid $\text{H}_2$ at $-253°\text{C}$ = **70.8 kg/m³**
> - Molecular weight of $\text{H}_2$ = **2.016 g/mol**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of a Hydrogen Economy

A functional hydrogen economy rests on three interconnected pillars. KTU examiners frequently test this triangular structure.

**Pillar 1 — Production (Clean Hydrogen Generation)**
- **Green Hydrogen**: Produced by electrolysis of water using **renewable electricity** (solar, wind, hydro). Zero direct $\text{CO}_2$ emissions.
- **Blue Hydrogen**: Produced from **natural gas (Steam Methane Reforming — SMR)** with **Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS)** capturing up to 90–95% of $\text{CO}_2$.
- **Grey Hydrogen**: Conventional SMR without CCS. Emits ~**9–12 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kg $\text{H}_2$**.
- **Pink Hydrogen**: Electrolysis powered by **nuclear** energy.
- **Turquoise Hydrogen**: Produced via **methane pyrolysis** ($\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{C} + \text{2H}_2$), emitting solid carbon.

**Pillar 2 — Storage and Transportation**
- Compressed gas ($\text{CGH}_2$) at 350–700 bar.
- Liquid hydrogen ($\text{LH}_2$) at $-253°\text{C}$.
- Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (**LOHCs**) such as toluene/methylcyclohexane.
- Underground storage in **salt caverns, depleted gas fields**.

**Pillar 3 — End-Use Applications**
- **Mobility**: Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs), trucks, buses, trains, ships, aviation.
- **Industry**: Green steel (DRI process), green ammonia (Haber-Bosch), refineries, methanol, glass, cement.
- **Power**: Hydrogen turbines, stationary fuel cells, grid balancing.
- **Heating**: Hydrogen blending in natural gas networks, hydrogen boilers.

### 2.2 Strategic Logic — Why Hydrogen for Decarbonisation?

- **Hard-to-abate sectors**: Aviation, shipping, heavy trucking, steel, cement, and high-temperature industrial heat **cannot be directly electrified** using batteries or wires. Hydrogen is the only viable zero-carbon alternative.
- **Seasonal energy storage**: Renewable electricity (solar/wind) is intermittent. Excess renewable electricity → **Power-to-Gas (P2G)** → hydrogen → stored for months → reconverted to electricity when needed.
- **Sector coupling**: Hydrogen uniquely links the **electricity, heat, mobility, and industrial sectors** into a single integrated clean energy system.

### 2.3 The Hydrogen Value Chain (End-to-End Flow)

```
Renewable Electricity → Electrolyser → Hydrogen (H₂) → Compression/Liquefaction
        → Storage → Transport (pipeline/truck/ship) → End-Use Sector
        → Fuel Cell / Combustion / Industrial Feedstock
        → Energy + Water (H₂O) as by-product
```

### 2.4 India's National Hydrogen Mission (Context for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Launched on **15th August 2021**, with a target of **5 million metric tonnes (MMT) of green hydrogen production by 2030**. India is the **4th largest producer of hydrogen globally** but produces mostly grey hydrogen.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| S.No. | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Higher Heating Value (HHV) | $\text{HHV}_{\text{H}_2} = 142$ | $\text{MJ/kg}$ | Total energy including latent heat of water vapour |
| 2 | Lower Heating Value (LHV) | $\text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2} = 120$ | $\text{MJ/kg}$ | Usable energy in fuel cell / engine (excludes latent heat) |
| 3 | Energy density (volumetric) | $\rho_E = \dfrac{m \cdot \text{LHV}}{V}$ | $\text{MJ/L}$ | Compares storage volume requirements |
| 4 | Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{\text{LHV}}{M}$ | $\text{kWh/kg}$ | $33.33\ \text{kWh/kg}$ for $\text{H}_2$ |
| 5 | Mass of $\text{H}_2$ per unit energy | $m = \dfrac{E_{\text{req}}}{\text{LHV}}$ | $\text{kg}$ | Hydrogen consumption calculation |
| 6 | Equivalent gasoline mass | $m_{\text{gas}} = m_{\text{H}_2} \cdot \dfrac{\text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{gas}}}$ | $\text{kg}$ | $\text{LHV}_{\text{gas}} \approx 44\ \text{MJ/kg}$ |
| 7 | $\text{CO}_2$ emission from grey $\text{H}_2$ | $E_{\text{CO}_2} = 9 \text{ to } 12$ | $\text{kg CO}_2 \text{ per kg H}_2$ | Baseline emissions before carbon capture |
| 8 | Reduction in $\text{CO}_2$ | $\Delta\text{CO}_2 = E_{\text{grey}} - E_{\text{green}}$ | $\text{kg CO}_2 \text{ per kg H}_2$ | Impact of switching to green hydrogen |
| 9 | Faraday's Law of Electrolysis | $m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $\text{kg}$ | Theoretical hydrogen mass produced |
| 10 | Electrolyser Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{elec}} = \dfrac{\text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}}{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I \cdot t / m_{\text{H}_2}}$ | dimensionless | Energy conversion efficiency |
| 11 | Carbon abatement cost | $\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{LCOH}_{\text{green}} - \text{LCOH}_{\text{grey}}}{\Delta\text{CO}_2}$ | $\text{USD/tCO}_2$ | Economic viability of green hydrogen |
| 12 | Volumetric flow of $\text{H}_2$ gas | $Q = \dfrac{m \cdot R \cdot T}{P \cdot M}$ | $\text{m}^3\text{/s}$ | Pipeline sizing (ideal gas law) |
| 13 | Equivalent energy of 1 kg $\text{H}_2$ in kWh | $E = \dfrac{\text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}}{3.6}$ | $\text{kWh}$ | $\approx 33.33\ \text{kWh}$ |
| 14 | Water requirement for electrolysis | $m_{\text{water}} = 9\ \text{kg per kg H}_2$ | $\text{kg}$ | Stoichiometric: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$ |

> **Where the symbols are:** $M$ = molar mass of $\text{H}_2$ (2.016 g/mol), $n$ = electrons per molecule (= 2), $F$ = Faraday's constant (**96485 C/mol**), $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J/(mol·K)**), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $T$ = temperature (K), $P$ = pressure (Pa), $\eta$ = efficiency.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power-to-Gas (P2G) Plants**: Germany (Energiepark Mainz), Denmark, Netherlands — converting surplus wind electricity into green hydrogen.
- **Industrial Decarbonisation**: HYBRIT project (Sweden) — producing **green steel** by replacing coking coal with green hydrogen in the Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) process.
- **Heavy-Duty Mobility**: Hyundai XCIENT Fuel Cell trucks, Toyota Mirai, Honda Clarity (mobility demonstration worldwide).
- **Aviation**: Airbus ZEROe concept aircraft running on **liquid hydrogen fuel cells**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The levelized cost of hydrogen (LCOH) for green hydrogen was around **USD 4–6/kg in 2024** versus **USD 1–2/kg for grey hydrogen**. Achieving the **USD 1/kg target** by 2030 (US DOE Hydrogen Shot) is the global economic north star.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Hydrogen Required to Replace a Given Quantity of Fossil Fuel

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):**
A thermal power plant consumes **1000 kg of coal per hour** to generate electricity. The coal has a calorific value of **24 MJ/kg**. Calculate the mass of green hydrogen (using LHV = 120 MJ/kg) required to deliver the **same thermal energy** per hour. Also compute the $\text{CO}_2$ reduction if coal emits **2.42 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kg coal** and green hydrogen emits **0 kg $\text{CO}_2$**.

**Step 1 — Compute the thermal energy output of coal per hour:**

$$E_{\text{coal}} = m_{\text{coal}} \times \text{CV}_{\text{coal}}$$

$$E_{\text{coal}} = 1000\ \text{kg} \times 24\ \text{MJ/kg} = 24{,}000\ \text{MJ}$$

**Step 2 — Apply energy equivalence to find the required mass of hydrogen:**

$$E_{\text{coal}} = E_{\text{H}_2} = m_{\text{H}_2} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}$$

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{E_{\text{coal}}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}}$$

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{24{,}000\ \text{MJ}}{120\ \text{MJ/kg}} = 200\ \text{kg of H}_2$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\text{CO}_2$ emitted by coal per hour:**

$$E_{\text{CO}_2,\text{coal}} = m_{\text{coal}} \times \text{EF}_{\text{coal}}$$

$$E_{\text{CO}_2,\text{coal}} = 1000\ \text{kg} \times 2.42\ \text{kg CO}_2\text{/kg} = 2420\ \text{kg CO}_2$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\text{CO}_2$ emitted by green hydrogen per hour:**

$$E_{\text{CO}_2,\text{H}_2} = m_{\text{H}_2} \times \text{EF}_{\text{H}_2} = 200\ \text{kg} \times 0 = 0\ \text{kg CO}_2$$

**Step 5 — Compute the net $\text{CO}_2$ reduction:**

$$\Delta E_{\text{CO}_2} = E_{\text{CO}_2,\text{coal}} - E_{\text{CO}_2,\text{H}_2}$$

$$\Delta E_{\text{CO}_2} = 2420 - 0 = 2420\ \text{kg CO}_2 \text{ saved per hour}$$

**Step 6 — Annualise (assuming 8760 hours of operation):**

$$\text{Annual CO}_2\ \text{avoided} = 2420 \times 8760 = 2.12 \times 10^7\ \text{kg CO}_2\text{/year}$$

$$\text{Annual CO}_2\ \text{avoided} = 21{,}200\ \text{tonnes of CO}_2\text{/year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Replacing 1000 kg/h of coal requires only 200 kg/h of green hydrogen (a 5:1 mass advantage) while eliminating **~21,200 tonnes of $\text{CO}_2$ per year**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Hydrogen Production via Faraday's Law

**Problem Statement:**
A **PEM electrolyser** operates at a current of **500 A** for **10 hours** at **100% efficiency** ($\eta = 1$). Calculate the mass of hydrogen produced. Use $M = 2.016\ \text{g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$.

**Step 1 — Recall Faraday's Law of Electrolysis:**

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 500\ \text{A} \times (10 \times 3600\ \text{s}) \times 2.016\ \text{g/mol}}{2 \times 96485\ \text{C/mol}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.0 \times 500 \times 36{,}000 \times 2.016 = 3.6288 \times 10^7\ \text{g·C/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$\text{Denominator} = 2 \times 96485 = 1.9297 \times 10^5\ \text{C/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the division:**

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{3.6288 \times 10^7}{1.9297 \times 10^5} = 188.05\ \text{g of H}_2$$

**Step 6 — Apply real-world efficiency (typical PEM efficiency = 70%):**

$$m_{\text{H}_2,\text{actual}} = 0.70 \times 188.05 = 131.64\ \text{g of H}_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** At 100% theoretical efficiency, 500 A for 10 hours produces ~188 g of hydrogen. In practice, due to thermodynamic and kinetic losses, actual output is ~132 g.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Stored in Compressed Hydrogen Tank

**Problem Statement:**
A vehicle carries a **Type IV hydrogen tank** of volume **$V = 122\ \text{L}$** pressurised to **700 bar** at ambient temperature **$T = 298\ \text{K}$**. Calculate (a) the mass of hydrogen stored, (b) the total chemical energy stored.

**Step 1 — Use the ideal gas law to compute the moles:**

$$n = \dfrac{PV}{RT} = \dfrac{(700 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}) \times (122 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3)}{8.314 \times 298}$$

$$n = \dfrac{7 \times 10^7 \times 0.122}{2477.57} = \dfrac{8.54 \times 10^6}{2477.57} \approx 3447\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Convert moles to mass:**

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = n \times M = 3447 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg} = 6.95\ \text{kg of H}_2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the chemical energy stored (using LHV):**

$$E_{\text{stored}} = m_{\text{H}_2} \times \text{LHV} = 6.95 \times 120 = 834\ \text{MJ}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):**

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \dfrac{834}{3.6} \approx 231.7\ \text{kWh of chemical energy}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Range Insight:** A fuel cell vehicle consuming ~1 kg $\text{H}_2$ per 100 km can travel $\approx 6.95 \times 100 = 695\ \text{km}$ on a single fill of a 700-bar tank.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Sectoral Impact of Decarbonisation

| Sector | Current Fuel | $\text{CO}_2$ Emitted | Hydrogen Substitute | $\text{CO}_2$ Avoided (per unit) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Light Vehicles | Petrol | **2.31 kg/L** | FCEV (green $\text{H}_2$) | **100% direct** |
| Heavy Trucks | Diesel | **2.68 kg/L** | Fuel Cell Truck | **100% direct** |
| Steel Industry | Coking Coal | **~2.0 t $\text{CO}_2$/t steel** | Green $\text{H}_2$-DRI | **~95%** |
| Ammonia Production | Grey $\text{H}_2$ | **~2.0 t $\text{CO}_2$/t $\text{NH}_3$** | Green $\text{H}_2$ | **~100%** |
| Shipping | Heavy Fuel Oil | **3.1 t $\text{CO}_2$/t fuel** | Green $\text{NH}_3$ / $\text{H}_2$ | **~100%** |
| Power Generation | Natural Gas | **0.49 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kWh** | $\text{H}_2$ turbine / fuel cell | **~100%** |
| Cement | Limestone calcination | **0.93 t $\text{CO}_2$/t cement** | Hydrogen burners | **~30%** (process $\text{CO}_2$ remains) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics


**Figure 1 — Hydrogen Economy Functional Architecture.** *The diagram shows the three sequential stages (Production, Storage/Transport, End-Use) feeding into zero-emission outcomes, collectively driving decarbonisation of the global economy.*

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decarbonisation Pathway (Causal Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Climate Crisis and Net Zero Targets] --> B[Shift from Fossil Fuels]
    B --> C[Adoption of Green Hydrogen]
    C --> D[Production Growth and Cost Reduction]

    E --> F[Reduction in CO2 Emissions]
    F --> G[Decarbonisation Achieved]
    G --> H[Climate Stabilisation and Sustainable Economy]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
```

**Figure 2 — The Decarbonisation Causal Pathway.** *Each block represents a sequential step; the arrows indicate the direction of causality in the transition from a fossil-based economy to a hydrogen-driven net-zero economy.*

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]`
**Define the term "Hydrogen Economy" and list the three stages of the hydrogen value chain.**

**Model Answer:**
The Hydrogen Economy is an energy system in which hydrogen serves as the principal low-carbon energy carrier, replacing fossil fuels across industrial, mobility, power, and heating sectors. The three stages of the hydrogen value chain are:
1. **Production** — Generation of hydrogen via electrolysis, SMR, or pyrolysis.
2. **Storage and Transportation** — Compression, liquefaction, LOHC, or pipeline transport.
3. **End-Use Applications** — Utilisation in fuel cells, turbines, industrial feedstocks, and mobility.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Why is hydrogen considered essential for decarbonising "hard-to-abate" sectors? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Hard-to-abate sectors such as **steel manufacturing, cement, heavy-duty trucking, shipping, and aviation** require either very high-temperature heat or energy-dense fuels that **cannot be practically electrified using batteries or grid power**. Hydrogen, with its high energy-to-mass ratio (LHV = 120 MJ/kg) and ability to release energy at high temperatures, provides the only viable **zero-carbon alternative**.
**Examples:**
- **Green Steel** — Hydrogen-based Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) replaces coking coal, reducing $\text{CO}_2$ emissions by ~95%.
- **Aviation** — Liquid hydrogen fuel cells power zero-emission aircraft (e.g., Airbus ZEROe).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

**Choose either Question A or Question B, and answer both sub-parts.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different **colours of hydrogen** (Green, Blue, Grey, Pink, Turquoise) based on production methods, raw materials, and associated $\text{CO}_2$ emissions. Why is **green hydrogen** the cornerstone of a sustainable hydrogen economy?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat block diagrams, describe the **end-to-end hydrogen value chain** from renewable electricity generation to end-use applications. List the major technical challenges in each stage.

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

| Hydrogen Colour | Production Method | Raw Material | Energy Source | $\text{CO}_2$ Emissions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Green** | Electrolysis | Water | Solar / Wind / Hydro | **0 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kg $\text{H}_2$** |
| **Blue** | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) | Natural gas | Fossil + CCS | **~1–3 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kg $\text{H}_2$** |
| **Grey** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | Natural gas | Fossil | **9–12 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kg $\text{H}_2$** |
| **Pink** | Electrolysis | Water | Nuclear | **0 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kg $\text{H}_2$** |
| **Turquoise** | Methane Pyrolysis | Methane | Renewable heat | Solid carbon (no $\text{CO}_2$) |

**Why green hydrogen is the cornerstone:**
1. Uses **abundant raw materials** — water and renewable electricity.
2. Produces **zero direct $\text{CO}_2$ emissions** at the point of production.
3. Aligns with **net-zero goals** under the Paris Agreement.
4. Enables **decentralised production** near renewable energy hubs.
5. Backed by **falling renewable electricity costs** (solar LCOE fell 89% in the last decade).

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

The hydrogen value chain consists of **four interlinked stages**:

**Stage 1 — Renewable Electricity Generation:**
- Solar PV, wind turbines, and hydroelectric plants generate clean electricity.
- *Challenge:* Intermittency — supply varies with weather and time of day.

**Stage 2 — Hydrogen Production (Electrolysis):**
- PEM or Alkaline electrolysers split water into hydrogen and oxygen.
- *Challenge:* High capital cost (USD 800–1500/kW) and efficiency losses (~30%).

**Stage 3 — Storage and Transportation:**
- Compression (350–700 bar), liquefaction (-253°C), LOHC, or pipelines.
- *Challenge:* Low volumetric energy density requires expensive storage solutions.

**Stage 4 — End-Use Applications:**
- Fuel cells for mobility, hydrogen for steel/ammonia, turbines for power.
- *Challenge:* Need for new infrastructure (refuelling stations, pipelines, industrial retrofits).

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1).**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **decarbonisation of the economy**. Discuss the role of hydrogen in achieving the **2050 net-zero target**, with reference to **sector coupling**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A thermal power plant consumes **5000 kg/day of natural gas** (LHV = 50 MJ/kg) for electricity generation. Calculate:
- (i) the daily thermal energy output,
- (ii) the mass of green hydrogen (LHV = 120 MJ/kg) required to deliver the **same** energy,
- (iii) the daily $\text{CO}_2$ saved, given that natural gas emits **2.75 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kg** and green hydrogen emits **0 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kg**.

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Definition:**
Decarbonisation is the systematic reduction of $\text{CO}_2$ emissions from economic activity by replacing carbon-intensive fossil fuels with low-carbon or zero-carbon energy sources such as renewable electricity, green hydrogen, and biofuels.

**Role of Hydrogen in Net-Zero 2050:**
- Decarbonises **hard-to-abate sectors** (steel, cement, aviation, shipping).
- Enables **long-duration, seasonal energy storage** via Power-to-Gas.
- Decarbonises **high-temperature industrial processes** (steel, glass, ceramics).
- Serves as a **zero-carbon feedstock** for green ammonia and synthetic fuels.

**Sector Coupling Concept:**
Hydrogen uniquely **integrates four energy sectors** that are currently siloed:
- **Electricity sector** → Power-to-Gas converts surplus renewables to $\text{H}_2$.
- **Heat sector** → $\text{H}_2$ boilers and industrial burners.
- **Mobility sector** → FCEVs for cars, trucks, buses, trains, ships, planes.
- **Industrial sector** → Green steel, ammonia, methanol, refineries.

This **cross-sectoral energy flow** is called sector coupling, and hydrogen is the **single molecule** enabling it.

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**(i) Daily thermal energy from natural gas:**

$$E_{\text{gas}} = m_{\text{gas}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{gas}}$$

$$E_{\text{gas}} = 5000\ \text{kg/day} \times 50\ \text{MJ/kg} = 250{,}000\ \text{MJ/day}$$

**(ii) Mass of green hydrogen required (energy equivalence):**

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{E_{\text{gas}}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}}$$

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{250{,}000\ \text{MJ}}{120\ \text{MJ/kg}} = 2083.33\ \text{kg of H}_2\text{/day}$$

**(iii) Daily $\text{CO}_2$ saved:**

$$E_{\text{CO}_2,\text{gas}} = 5000 \times 2.75 = 13{,}750\ \text{kg CO}_2\text{/day}$$

$$E_{\text{CO}_2,\text{H}_2} = 2083.33 \times 0 = 0\ \text{kg CO}_2\text{/day}$$

$$\Delta E_{\text{CO}_2} = 13{,}750 - 0 = 13{,}750\ \text{kg CO}_2\text{/day} = 13.75\ \text{tonnes CO}_2\text{/day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing HHV and LHV** — KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark if the wrong calorific value is used without justification. Default to **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** for fuel cells.
> 2. **Forgetting units in intermediate steps** — Always write MJ/kg, MJ, kg, kg $\text{CO}_2$, etc. Units earn **partial marks** even if the final answer is wrong.
> 3. **Not showing the energy-equivalence equation** — Never write the final number without showing $m = E / \text{LHV}$. This step carries **2 of the 7 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing "decarbonisation" with "renewable energy"** — Decarbonisation is a broader concept that includes energy efficiency, CCUS, electrification, *and* hydrogen.
> 5. **Diagrams without labels** — Always label each block in the value chain diagram (Production, Storage, Transport, End-Use). Unlabelled diagrams receive **0 marks** in KTU.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Points for Last-Minute Revision:**

- [x] **Hydrogen Economy** = hydrogen as the primary clean energy carrier replacing fossil fuels.
- [x] **Decarbonisation** = reducing $\text{CO}_2$ emissions from economic activity to net-zero.
- [x] **HHV of $\text{H}_2$ = 142 MJ/kg**; **LHV of $\text{H}_2$ = 120 MJ/kg** (use LHV for fuel cells).
- [x] **Faraday's Constant** $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$; **R** = 8.314 J/(mol·K).
- [x] **Green $\text{H}_2$** = 0 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kg; **Blue $\text{H}_2$** = 1–3 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kg; **Grey $\text{H}_2$** = 9–12 kg $\text{CO}_2$/kg.
- [x] **Three pillars of hydrogen economy**: Production + Storage/Transport + End-Use.
- [x] **Hydrogen value chain** = Renewables → Electrolyser → Storage → Transport → End-Use.
- [x] **Hard-to-abate sectors** = Steel, Cement, Aviation, Shipping, Heavy Transport.
- [x] **Sector coupling** = Integration of Electricity + Heat + Mobility + Industry via hydrogen.
- [x] **India's National Hydrogen Mission** = 5 MMT green $\text{H}_2$ by 2030 (launched Aug 2021).
- [x] **Hydrogen Shot target** = USD 1/kg green hydrogen by 2030 (US DOE goal).
- [x] **Net-zero target year** = **2050** (Paris Agreement).
- [x] **Water consumption** in electrolysis = **9 kg water per kg $\text{H}_2$** (stoichiometric).
- [x] **Formula for mass of $\text{H}_2$** from fossil fuel: $m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{m_{\text{fossil}} \times \text{CV}_{\text{fossil}}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}}$.
- [x] **CO₂ saving**: $\Delta\text{CO}_2 = m_{\text{fossil}} \times \text{EF}_{\text{fossil}} - m_{\text{H}_2} \times \text{EF}_{\text{H}_2}$.
- [x] **Most common colours tested**: Green, Blue, Grey — must know production route + emissions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Road to the Hydrogen Economy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Road to the Hydrogen Economy ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydrogen Economy (KTU Definition):** A proposed energy system in which hydrogen is produced primarily from water using renewable or low-carbon energy sources, stored, transported, and utilized as a clean energy carrier to replace fossil fuels across power generation, industrial processes, transportation, and heating sectors, thereby enabling deep decarbonization of the global energy system. The term "Hydrogen Economy" was first coined by **John Bockris** in 1970 and later popularized by **Jeremy Rifkin** in his 2002 book *"The Hydrogen Economy"*. It represents a paradigm shift in how humanity produces, distributes, and consumes energy — moving from a carbon-intensive to a carbon-neutral energy infrastructure. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Hydrogen as Electricity's Cousin" Analogy:** > Think of the modern energy system as a **water distribution network**. Just as water is treated (purified), pumped (transported), stored in overhead tanks, and delivered to homes for drinking, washing, and cooking — **hydrogen acts as an "energy currency"** that is produced (electrolysis), compressed/liquefied (stored), piped/transported, and consumed in fuel cells or turbines to deliver electricity, heat, or motive power. > > The key difference? **Hydrogen is a carrier, not a source** — it must first be manufactured from other primary energy sources (water, biomass, fossil fuels). It is to energy what a rechargeable battery is to electricity: a flexible medium that can be charged (produced), stored, and discharged (used) on demand. ### 1.3 Why the World is Moving Towards Hydrogen > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Driving Forces Behind the Hydrogen Economy:** > 1. **Climate Change Imperative** — Global commitment to limit warming to **1.5 °C** above pre-industrial levels (Paris Agreement, 2015). > 2. **Energy Security** — Reducing dependence on geographically concentrated fossil fuel reserves. > 3. **Decarbonization of "Hard-to-Abate" Sectors** — Steel, cement, heavy transport, shipping, aviation, and ammonia production. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Metric | Value | |---|---| | Energy content (LHV) of H₂ | **120 MJ/kg** | | Energy content (HHV) of H₂ | **142 MJ/kg** | | Energy density (by volume, liquid) | **8.5 MJ/L** | | Gravimetric density | **3× higher than gasoline** | | Volumetric density (gas at 1 atm) | Very low (≈ 0.01 MJ/L) | | Boiling point at 1 atm | **−252.87 °C (20.28 K)** | | Auto-ignition temperature | **500 °C** | | Flammability range in air | **4–75 % by volume** | | Diffusion coefficient in air | **0.61 cm²/s** (fastest of all gases) | ### 1.5 The Hydrogen Color Code (Industry Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Color Coding of Hydrogen** — based on the production method: > - **Grey Hydrogen** — Produced from natural gas steam methane reforming (SMR); emits CO₂. *Current ~95 % of global H₂ production.* > - **Blue Hydrogen** — Grey hydrogen + Carbon Capture, Utilization and Storage (CCUS). > - **Green Hydrogen** — Produced via electrolysis of water using **100 % renewable electricity**. *The ultimate goal of the hydrogen economy.* > - **Pink Hydrogen** — Electrolysis powered by **nuclear** energy. > - **Turquoise Hydrogen** — Produced via **methane pyrolysis** (solid carbon as by-product). > - **White Hydrogen** — Naturally occurring geological hydrogen. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Hydrogen Value Chain — A Structured Breakdown The hydrogen economy is built on **four interconnected pillars**. Understanding each is critical for KTU examination questions. #### Pillar 1: Production - **Primary goal:** Generate H₂ with minimum carbon footprint. - **Dominant pathway (short-term):** Steam Methane Reforming (SMR): $$CH_4 + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{700{-}900\,°C,\ Ni\,catalyst} CO_2 + 4H_2$$ - **Long-term pathway:** Water electrolysis: $$2H_2O_{(l)} \xrightarrow{Electricity} 2H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)}$$ - Theoretical minimum cell voltage: **1.229 V** at 25 °C. - Commercial electrolyzers operate at **1.8 – 2.2 V** with efficiency **60–80 %** (LHV basis). #### Pillar 2: Storage - **Why difficult?** Hydrogen has very low volumetric energy density at ambient conditions. - **Storage modalities:** * **Compressed Gas** (350 – 700 bar) — most mature. * **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)** — cryogenic storage at **−253 °C**. * **Metal Hydrides** — chemical storage (e.g., LaNi₅, MgH₂). * **Underground Storage** — salt caverns, depleted gas fields. * **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)** — e.g., dibenzyltoluene. #### Pillar 3: Transportation - **Modes:** Pipeline, tanker trucks (gaseous or liquid), ships. - **Energy penalty:** Compressing H₂ to 700 bar consumes **~10–15 %** of its LHV. - **Liquefaction energy penalty:** ~**30–40 %** of LHV. #### Pillar 4: End-Use Applications - **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)** — passenger cars (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo). - **Stationary power** — backup power, microgrids. - **Industrial feedstock** — ammonia, methanol, refinery hydrogenation. - **Direct Reduced Iron (DRI)** — green steel production. - **Heating** — hydrogen blending in natural gas networks. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Equations for the Hydrogen Economy** | Concept | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Higher Heating Value of H₂ | $HHV = 142 \, MJ/kg$ | Water produced in liquid state | MJ/kg | | Lower Heating Value of H₂ | $LHV = 120 \, MJ/kg$ | Water produced in vapor state | MJ/kg | | Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Energy per unit mass | MJ/kg | | Energy Density (volumetric) | $E_v = \rho \cdot LHV$ | $\rho$ = density of stored H₂ | MJ/L | | Electrolyzer Efficiency | $\eta_{elyz} = \dfrac{LHV_{H_2}}{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}$ | Electrical-to-chemical conversion | % | | Round-trip efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{prod} \cdot \eta_{store} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{use}$ | Total chain efficiency | % | | CO₂ avoided (Blue/Grey) | $\Delta CO_2 = CO_{2,grey} - CO_{2,blue}$ | Per kg H₂ produced | kg CO₂/kg H₂ | | Hydrogen equivalent to 1 L gasoline | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{E_{gasoline}}{\eta_{FC} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}$ | Mass of H₂ to replace 1 L petrol | kg | | Compression work (isothermal) | $W = nRT \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Reversible compression | J | | Liquefaction work (ideal) | $W_{liq} \approx 0.35 \cdot LHV$ | Approximate energy penalty | J/kg | | Faraday's Law (electrolysis) | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | H₂ mass produced in electrolysis | kg | **Where:** - $M$ = Molar mass of H₂ = **2.016 g/mol** - $n$ = electrons transferred = **2** - $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** ### 2.3 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the Math - **Why LHV vs HHV?** For fuel cells, water exits as **vapor** (so we use LHV = 120 MJ/kg). For combustion boilers, water condenses (HHV). - **Why 1.229 V theoretical?** Derived from the **Gibbs free energy** change for the water-splitting reaction: $\Delta G° = nFE°$, so $E° = 237.2 / (2 \times 96.485) = 1.229$ V. - **Why low volumetric density?** H₂ is the **lightest and smallest molecule** — at 1 atm it occupies ~3000× the volume of gasoline for the same energy. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power-to-Gas (P2G) systems** in Germany (e.g., HYBRIT project in Sweden) are direct embodiments of this chain. - **India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023)** targets **5 million metric tonnes (MMT)** of green H₂ production by 2030, with an outlay of ₹19,744 crore. - **Refinery hydrogen demand** in India: ~**4 MMT/year**, currently 100 % grey. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Minimum Voltage for Water Electrolysis This is a **frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU exams. **Given:** Water-splitting reaction at standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm): $$2H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow 2H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)}$$ **Step 1 — Standard Gibbs Free Energy Change:** From thermodynamic tables: $$\Delta G° = 2 \cdot \Delta G°_f(H_2O, l) = 2 \times (-237.2 \, kJ/mol) = -474.4 \, kJ/mol$$ **Step 2 — Relate ΔG° to Electrical Work:** The maximum electrical work obtainable from an electrochemical cell equals the negative of the free energy change: $$W_{elec} = -\Delta G° = n \cdot F \cdot E°_{cell}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for E°cell:** Substituting values: $n = 2$ electrons per mole of water, $F = 96{,}485 \, C/mol$: $$\begin{aligned} E°_{cell} &= \dfrac{-\Delta G°}{n \cdot F} \\ &= \dfrac{474.4 \times 10^{3} \, J/mol}{2 \times 96{,}485 \, C/mol} \\ &= \dfrac{474{,}400}{192{,}970} \\ E°_{cell} &= 1.229 \, V \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Thermoneutral Voltage (using Enthalpy):** For a real electrolyzer operating adiabatically (no heat input), we use enthalpy: $$\Delta H° = 285.8 \, kJ/mol \, (per \, H_2O)$$ $$\begin{aligned} E_{tn} &= \dfrac{\Delta H°}{n \cdot F} \\ &= \dfrac{285.8 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\ E_{tn} &= 1.481 \, V \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation for KTU Exams:** > - **1.229 V** = theoretical minimum at 25 °C (reversible, isothermal). > - **1.481 V** = thermoneutral voltage (reversible, adiabatic). > - **Operating voltages (1.8 – 2.2 V)** > these values → efficiency loss as heat. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydrogen Mass Required to Replace 1 L of Gasoline A **classic 7-mark problem** testing energy equivalency. **Given data:** - Energy density of gasoline ≈ **34.2 MJ/L** - LHV of H₂ = **120 MJ/kg** - Fuel cell efficiency $\eta_{FC} \approx 50\%$ - Internal combustion engine efficiency $\eta_{ICE} \approx 25\%$ **Step 1 — Useful mechanical energy from 1 L gasoline:** $$E_{mech,ICE} = 34.2 \times 0.25 = 8.55 \, MJ$$ **Step 2 — Mass of H₂ required to deliver same useful energy via fuel cell:** $$\begin{aligned} m_{H_2} &= \dfrac{E_{mech,ICE}}{\eta_{FC} \times LHV_{H_2}} \\ &= \dfrac{8.55 \, MJ}{0.50 \times 120 \, MJ/kg} \\ &= \dfrac{8.55}{60} \\ m_{H_2} &= 0.1425 \, kg \approx 143 \, g \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** About **143 g of hydrogen** (≈ 1.5 kg if stored as 700 bar gas) can replace 1 L of gasoline in terms of useful drivetrain energy. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Faraday's Law Applied to Electrolyzer H₂ Production **Given:** - Current $I = 1000 \, A$ - Time $t = 1 \, hour = 3600 \, s$ - $n = 2$ electrons per H₂ molecule - $F = 96{,}485 \, C/mol$ - $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \, g/mol$ **Step 1 — Total charge passed:** $$Q = I \cdot t = 1000 \times 3600 = 3.6 \times 10^6 \, C$$ **Step 2 — Moles of H₂ produced (Faraday's Law):** $$n_{H_2} = \dfrac{Q}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^6}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 18.65 \, mol$$ **Step 3 — Mass of H₂ produced:** $$m_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \times M = 18.65 \times 2.016 = 37.6 \, g$$ **Step 4 — Volume at STP (22.4 L/mol):** $$V_{H_2} = 18.65 \times 22.4 = 417.8 \, L$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always include the **Faradaic efficiency** factor ($\eta_F \approx 0.95 - 0.99$) in real calculations: $m_{H_2,actual} = m_{H_2,ideal} \times \eta_F$. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Hydrogen Production Pathways | Production Method | Energy Source | CO₂ Emissions (kg CO₂/kg H₂) | Efficiency | Maturity | |---|---|---|---|---| | SMR (Grey) | Natural gas | **9–10** | 65–75 % | Commercial | | SMR + CCUS (Blue) | Natural gas | **1–3** | 60–70 % | Pilot / Demo | | Coal Gasification (Brown) | Coal | **18–20** | 50–60 % | Commercial | | PEM Electrolysis (Green) | Solar/Wind | **< 0.5** (lifecycle) | 60–80 % | Commercial scaling | | Alkaline Electrolysis (Green) | Solar/Wind | **< 0.5** | 63–70 % | Mature | | Solid Oxide Electrolysis (Green) | Solar/Wind/Nuclear | **< 0.3** | 75–85 % | Pilot | | Biomass Gasification | Biomass | **~ 0** (carbon neutral) | 35–50 % | R&D | | Photoelectrochemical | Sunlight | **0** (direct) | < 10 % | Lab scale | | Thermochemical Cycles | Solar/Nuclear | **0** | 30–50 % | R&D | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Hydrogen Economy Value Chain (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Primary Energy Source
Renewables Nuclear Fossil] --> B[Production
Electrolysis SMR Pyrolysis] B --> C[Purification
PSA Membranes] C --> D[Storage
Compressed LH2 LOHC Hydrides] D --> E[Transport
Pipelines Trucks Ships] E --> F[End Use Application
Mobility Power Industry Heating] F --> G[Final Energy Service
Electricity Heat Motion Feedstock] H[Policy Incentives Carbon Pricing] --> B H --> D H --> F I[Public Acceptance Safety Standards] --> D I --> E I --> F ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram (for KTU answers):** The central horizontal flow represents the **physical value chain** of hydrogen. The vertical inputs on the right represent the **enabling framework** — without policy support and public acceptance, the chain cannot scale. This dual structure is the standard answer format for "Discuss the hydrogen economy framework" questions. ### 4.2 Hydrogen Production Pathway Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Want to Produce Hydrogen] --> B{Energy Source Available?} B --> C[Natural Gas] B --> D[Renewable Electricity] B --> E[Coal Biomass] B --> F[Direct Sunlight] C --> G[Steam Methane Reforming] G --> H{Add CCUS?} H --> I[Yes → Blue H2] H --> J[No → Grey H2] D --> K[Water Electrolysis] K --> L{Electrolyzer Type?} L --> M[PEM → Fast response] L --> N[Alkaline → Low cost] L --> O[SOEC → High efficiency] E --> P[Gasification] P --> Q{Biomass feedstock?} Q --> R[Yes → Bio H2] Q --> S[No → Brown H2] F --> T[Photobiological Photoelectrochemical] T --> U[Lab R and D] ``` ### 4.3 Sectoral Decarbonization Difficulty Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Power Generation] --> A1[Easy: Renewables mature] B[Light Road Transport] --> B1[Moderate: BEVs dominate] C[Heavy Duty Trucks] --> C1[Hard: H2 FCEVs emerging] D[Shipping Aviation] --> D1[Very Hard: H2 NH3 e fuels] E[Steel Cement] --> E1[Very Hard: H2 DRI needed] F[Industrial Heat] --> F1[Hard: H2 burners plasma] G[Residential Heating] --> G1[Moderate: H2 blend ready] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Insight:** This diagram is the **single most important schematic** for KTU questions on "Why hydrogen?" — because it visually establishes hydrogen's unique role in sectors where **direct electrification is impossible or impractical** (steel, shipping, aviation, heavy transport). ### 4.4 Global Hydrogen Roadmap — Policy Timeline ```mermaid gantt title Global Hydrogen Economy Milestones dateFormat YYYY axisFormat %Y section Policy Paris Agreement Signed :done, 2015, 1y EU Hydrogen Strategy 2x40 GW :active, 2020, 10y US Hydrogen Earthshot :active, 2021, 10y India National Green H2 Mission :active, 2023, 8y section Technology First Commercial Electrolyzers :done, 2010, 5y Mega Watt scale PEM plants :active, 2018, 6y GW scale Green H2 plants :active, 2024, 6y section Markets FCEV Cars 50000 units/yr :active, 2017, 8y H2 Trucks Buses deployment :active, 2020, 8y Green Steel Commercialization :active, 2026, 10y ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **Define the term "Hydrogen Economy" and list any two key drivers for its adoption.** **Model Answer:** The Hydrogen Economy is a proposed energy system in which hydrogen is used as a clean energy carrier, produced primarily from water using renewable energy, and utilized across sectors like transport, industry, and power, replacing fossil fuels to achieve deep decarbonization. Two key drivers: 1. **Climate change mitigation** — the need to limit global temperature rise to 1.5 °C (Paris Agreement). 2. **Decarbonization of hard-to-abate sectors** — such as steel, cement, heavy transport, and shipping, where direct electrification is infeasible. *(Marks: Definition — 1, Drivers — 1 + 1)* #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **Differentiate between Grey, Blue, and Green hydrogen.** **Model Answer:** | Type | Production Method | CO₂ Emissions | |---|---|---| | **Grey** | Steam Methane Reforming (no CO₂ capture) | 9–10 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | | **Blue** | SMR + Carbon Capture and Storage (CCUS) | 1–3 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | | **Green** | Water electrolysis using renewable electricity | < 0.5 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | *(Marks: 1 mark each for any two correct contrasts)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]` **(a) Discuss in detail the four pillars of the hydrogen economy value chain, with relevant examples for each. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The hydrogen economy value chain comprises four interlinked pillars: **1. Production (3 marks)** Hydrogen is produced by multiple routes. Currently, **~95 %** of global H₂ is "grey" — produced by Steam Methane Reforming of natural gas. The long-term target is **green hydrogen** via water electrolysis powered by solar/wind. Emerging methods include **turquoise hydrogen** (methane pyrolysis giving solid carbon) and **pink hydrogen** (nuclear-powered electrolysis). **2. Storage (2 marks)** Because of hydrogen's low volumetric density, storage is a major challenge. Options include: - Compressed gas at **350 – 700 bar** in composite tanks. - **Liquid hydrogen (LH₂)** at −253 °C — used in space applications. - **Metal hydrides** (e.g., MgH₂, LaNi₅H₆) for safe, dense solid-state storage. - **Underground storage** in salt caverns — proven in the UK and USA. **3. Transportation (1 mark)** Hydrogen can be moved via **dedicated pipelines** (existing natural gas pipelines can be repurposed with up to 20 % H₂ blending), **cryogenic tanker trucks**, or **LOHC carriers** (dibenzyltoluene). For long distances, ammonia (NH₃) is often used as a hydrogen carrier. **4. End-Use Applications (1 mark)** - **Mobility:** Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs) — Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, hydrogen trucks (Hyundai XCIENT). - **Industry:** Green ammonia, methanol, refinery feedstock, Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) for green steel. - **Power:** Stationary fuel cells for backup power and microgrids. - **Heating:** Hydrogen-blended natural gas networks in the UK (HyDeploy project). --- **(b) Explain the concept of "hard-to-abate sectors" and justify why hydrogen is the most suitable decarbonization vector for these sectors. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** "Hard-to-abate sectors" are industrial and economic sectors that emit large quantities of CO₂ and for which proven, scalable, low-carbon alternatives (especially direct electrification) are currently unavailable or uneconomical. These include: - Steel production (currently ~7 % global CO₂ emissions) - Cement (currently ~8 % global CO₂ emissions) - Heavy-duty transport (trucks, buses, mining vehicles) - Shipping and aviation - Chemical industry (ammonia, methanol) - High-temperature industrial heat (>500 °C) **Justification for hydrogen (5 marks):** 1. **High energy density by mass:** Hydrogen's LHV of 120 MJ/kg is **3× higher** than gasoline, making it suitable for long-range heavy transport where battery weight is prohibitive. 2. **Molecule-level decarbonization:** In steel, H₂ can replace coking coal as the reducing agent in **Direct Reduced Iron (DRI)** — yielding water as the only by-product, not CO₂. 3. **High-temperature capability:** Hydrogen combustion can reach **>2000 °C** flames, suitable for cement kilns and glass furnaces. 4. **Fuel cells provide electric drivetrains** for heavy trucks, ships, and trains without the charging-time bottleneck of batteries. 5. **Existing fuel infrastructure compatibility:** Hydrogen can be blended into natural gas networks (up to 20 %) for heating, leveraging existing pipelines. 6. **Synthetic fuel precursor:** Green H₂ + captured CO₂ → **e-fuels** (e-methanol, e-kerosene) for aviation and shipping. **Conclusion:** Hydrogen is **not in competition with batteries** — it complements them. Batteries serve light mobility and short-duration storage, while hydrogen serves the sectors where electrification fails. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]` **(a) With a neat schematic, explain the hydrogen color code (Grey, Blue, Green, Pink, Turquoise, White). Discuss the advantages and limitations of Green Hydrogen as the ultimate goal. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Hydrogen Color Code (Schematic representation — refer Section 4.2 of these notes) (4 marks):** | Color | Production Route | Carbon Footprint | |---|---|---| | Grey | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | 9–10 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | | Blue | SMR + Carbon Capture (CCUS) | 1–3 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | | Green | Electrolysis with renewable electricity | < 0.5 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | | Pink | Electrolysis with nuclear electricity | Very low | | Turquoise | Methane pyrolysis (solid C byproduct) | Low | | White | Natural/geological hydrogen | Near zero | **Green Hydrogen — Advantages (2 marks):** - Zero operational CO₂ emissions. - Uses abundant primary energy (sun, wind). - Compatible with intermittent renewables — provides energy storage. - End products: only water and oxygen. - Long-term sustainability and energy independence. **Green Hydrogen — Limitations (1 mark):** - High production cost (currently **$3–6/kg** vs $1–2/kg for grey). - Requires massive renewable electricity capacity. - Electrolyzer efficiency and durability still improving. - Water consumption (~9 L per kg H₂). --- **(b) An electrolyzer operates at a cell voltage of 1.85 V and current density of 0.5 A/cm². Calculate: (i) the power input per kg of H₂ produced, (ii) the electrolyzer efficiency (LHV basis). Given: LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg, Faraday constant F = 96,485 C/mol, M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol, n = 2. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Moles of H₂ produced per second per cm² (Faraday's Law):** $$\begin{aligned} n_{H_2} &= \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \\ &= \dfrac{0.5 \times 1}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\ &= 2.59 \times 10^{-6} \, mol/s \cdot cm^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Mass of H₂ produced per second per cm²:** $$m_{H_2} = 2.59 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.016 = 5.22 \times 10^{-6} \, g/s$$ **Step 3 — Energy content of H₂ produced per second (LHV):** $$E_{chem} = m \times LHV = 5.22 \times 10^{-9} \times 120 \times 10^6 = 0.627 \, J/s$$ **Step 4 — Electrical power input per second:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{elec} &= V \times I = 1.85 \times 0.5 = 0.925 \, W \\ &= 0.925 \, J/s \, per \, cm^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Power input per kg of H₂ (Normalization):** $$\begin{aligned} P_{input/kg} &= \dfrac{0.925}{5.22 \times 10^{-9}} \\ &= 1.77 \times 10^8 \, J/kg \\ &= 177 \, MJ/kg \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Electrolyzer Efficiency (LHV basis):** $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{elyz} &= \dfrac{LHV_{H_2}}{E_{input/kg}} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{120}{177} \times 100 \\ &= 67.8 \, \% \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answers:** (i) **177 MJ/kg**, (ii) **≈ 67.8 %** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Road to Hydrogen Economy" Questions:** > 1. **Confusing LHV (120 MJ/kg) with HHV (142 MJ/kg)** — KTU examiners specifically check this. For fuel cell applications, **always use LHV**. > 2. **Forgetting units in numericals** — Most KTU failures occur in unit conversion. Always write "MJ/kg", "kg CO₂/kg H₂", etc. > 3. **Defining "Hydrogen Economy" without mentioning the energy carrier concept** — Hydrogen is a carrier, not a source. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this distinction is missing. > 4. **Listing only "Green Hydrogen" benefits without acknowledging cost and water-use challenges** — A balanced answer (advantages + limitations) fetches full marks. > 5. **In derivation questions, skipping the ΔG° = −nFE° step** — KTU specifically tests this relationship. Always show the formula substitution. > 6. **Missing the sectoral application matrix** — When asked "Why hydrogen?", students often give generic answers. Naming **steel (DRI), shipping (NH₃), aviation (e-kerosene)** specifically earns bonus marks. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Road to the Hydrogen Economy"** - ☐ **Definition:** Hydrogen Economy = hydrogen as clean energy carrier replacing fossil fuels across all sectors. - ☐ **Origin:** Coined by John Bockris (1970), popularized by Jeremy Rifkin (2002). - ☐ **Color Code:** Grey (SMR), Blue (SMR+CCUS), Green (electrolysis+RE), Pink (nuclear), Turquoise (pyrolysis), White (geological). - ☐ **Key Constants:** LHV = 120 MJ/kg; HHV = 142 MJ/kg; Boiling point = −252.87 °C; Theoretical cell voltage = 1.229 V. - ☐ **Production Methods:** SMR (95 % share), Electrolysis (future), Coal gasification, Biomass, Photoelectrochemical. - ☐ **Hydrogen Value Chain:** Production → Purification → Storage → Transport → End Use. - ☐ **Storage Modes:** Compressed (350–700 bar), LH₂ (−253 °C), Metal hydrides, LOHC, Underground. - ☐ **Hard-to-Abate Sectors:** Steel, cement, heavy transport, shipping, aviation, chemical industry. - ☐ **Hydrogen's Unique Edge:** High mass energy density, high-temperature combustion, fuel cell drivetrain for heavy vehicles, DRI for green steel. - ☐ **India's Mission:** National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023) — target 5 MMT by 2030, ₹19,744 crore outlay. - ☐ **Faraday's Law:** $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ — must memorize for any numerical. - ☐ **Efficiency Chain:** Round-trip efficiency of P2G-P2P system ≈ 30–40 %. - ☐ **Safety Highlight:** H₂ is non-toxic but highly flammable (4–75 % in air) and embrittles metals — pipeline material selection is critical. - ☐ **Comparison rule:** Hydrogen complements (does not replace) batteries in light mobility. - ☐ **Most important equation:** $\Delta G° = -nFE°$ → gives 1.229 V minimum voltage.

topics.title: Properties of Hydrogen as Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Hydrogen as Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU's 2024 Scheme energy engineering syllabus, **hydrogen** is defined as the **lightest, simplest, and most abundant chemical element** in the universe, possessing a single proton and single electron (atomic number **1**, atomic mass **1.008 g/mol**). As a fuel, it is classified as a **secondary energy carrier** (not a primary energy source) because it must first be extracted from hydrogen-bearing compounds (water, hydrocarbons, biomass) using an energy input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Module 1):** Hydrogen is a **carbon-free, clean-burning fuel** that releases energy through electrochemical or combustion reactions, producing only **water vapor (H₂O)** as the primary exhaust product. Its energy release characteristics distinguish it from conventional hydrocarbon fuels.

### 1.2 Key Classification Categories of Hydrogen Properties

The physical and chemical properties relevant to its use as a fuel can be grouped into three major engineering categories:

1. **Physical Properties** — density, phase behavior, diffusivity, thermal conductivity, viscosity.
2. **Thermochemical Properties** — heating values, flammability limits, auto-ignition temperature, flame velocity.
3. **Combustion Properties** — stoichiometry, air–fuel ratio, adiabatic flame temperature, energy density.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Invisible Marathon Runner":** Imagine a sprinter carrying a water bottle. The water bottle (mass of hydrogen) is extremely **light**, almost weightless, but carries an enormous amount of **energy per kilogram**. However, the same water bottle is **bulky** — you would need a huge container to store the same amount of usable energy as a small gasoline can. This perfectly captures hydrogen's paradox: it is the **most energy-dense fuel by mass** (120 MJ/kg) but the **least energy-dense by volume** under ambient conditions.

This paradox is the central engineering challenge of Module 1: the *energy-by-weight vs. energy-by-volume trade-off* shapes every decision about hydrogen's storage, transportation, and safety.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants (to memorize)

| Property | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Atomic Number | **1** | — |
| Atomic Mass | **1.008** | g/mol |
| Gas Density (STP) | **0.0899** | kg/m³ |
| Boiling Point (1 atm) | **20.28 K (−252.87 °C)** | K |
| Melting Point (1 atm) | **13.99 K (−259.16 °C)** | K |
| Critical Temperature | **33.2 K (−240 °C)** | K |
| Critical Pressure | **1.296 MPa (12.96 atm)** | MPa |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Properties — Engineering Interpretation

Hydrogen's physical properties are dominated by its **extremely low molecular mass** and **quantum mechanical behavior at low temperatures**.

**Step 1 — Density (ρ):** Hydrogen has the **lowest density of any gas** at STP. Compared to air (1.225 kg/m³), hydrogen is **13.6× lighter**. This causes buoyancy-driven dispersion in the atmosphere — a critical safety advantage because leaked hydrogen rises and disperses rapidly.

**Step 2 — Diffusivity (D):** Hydrogen has the **highest diffusion coefficient** of all gases (~0.61 cm²/s in air at NTP). This is because, per the kinetic theory of gases, diffusivity is **inversely proportional to the square root of molecular mass**:

$$D \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}$$

This explains why hydrogen mixes with air 3.8× faster than natural gas (CH₄, M = 16) and 15× faster than gasoline vapors.

**Step 3 — Thermal Conductivity:** Gaseous hydrogen has a thermal conductivity of **0.182 W/m·K** at 300 K — approximately **7× higher than air**. Liquid hydrogen's thermal conductivity is even higher (~0.119 W/m·K) but causes major **boil-off losses** during cryogenic storage.

**Step 4 — Joule-Thomson Coefficient:** Hydrogen exhibits **inversion temperature behavior** — above ~200 K it cools upon expansion; below this temperature it **heats upon expansion**. This is critical in designing expansion-based storage and cryogenic systems.

### 2.2 Thermochemical (Combustion) Properties

**Step 1 — Combustion Reaction (the foundation of all hydrogen energy calculations):**

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O + \text{Energy (Heat)}$$

For stoichiometric combustion with air (approximated as 21% O₂ + 79% N₂):

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 + 1.88N_2 \longrightarrow H_2O + 1.88N_2$$

**Step 2 — Heating Values:**
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** = **120 MJ/kg** (water leaves as vapor)
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** = **142 MJ/kg** (water condenses to liquid, latent heat recovered)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The difference (HHV − LHV) ≈ **22 MJ/kg** corresponds to the **latent heat of vaporization of water** at 25 °C, which equals **2442 kJ/kg**.

**Step 3 — Flammability Limits in Air:**
- Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) = **4.0 vol%**
- Upper Flammability Limit (UFL) = **75.0 vol%**

This makes hydrogen's flammable range **the widest of any fuel** (≈ 71 percentage points wide, vs. only ~5.6 for gasoline vapor). This is the single most important **safety-related property** covered in Module 1.

**Step 4 — Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):** **571 °C** — the minimum temperature at which hydrogen ignites spontaneously without an external spark.

**Step 5 — Flame Properties:**
- Laminar flame velocity in air: ~**2.5 m/s** (vs. 0.4 m/s for methane)
- Adiabatic flame temperature: **~2400 K (2127 °C)** in air; **~2820 K** in pure oxygen
- Minimum ignition energy: **0.02 mJ** — approximately **10× lower than gasoline**, making hydrogen extremely easy to ignite.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density of H₂ at STP | $\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}$ | **0.0899** | kg/m³ |
| 2 | Energy per unit mass (LHV) | $E_m = \frac{Q_{LHV}}{1}$ | **120** | MJ/kg |
| 3 | Energy per unit volume (gas) | $E_v = \rho \cdot E_m$ | **10.8** | MJ/m³ |
| 4 | Energy per unit volume (liquid) | $E_{v,L} = \rho_L \cdot E_m$ | **8.5** | MJ/L |
| 5 | Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio (mass) | $AFR_s = 34.3$ | **34.3:1** | kg air/kg H₂ |
| 6 | Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio (vol) | $AFR_v = 2.38$ | **2.38:1** | m³ air/m³ H₂ |
| 7 | Flammability Range | $\Delta\phi = UFL - LFL$ | **71** | vol% |
| 8 | Diffusivity in air | $D = D_0(T/P)^n$ | **6.1 × 10⁻⁵** | m²/s |
| 9 | Heating Value Difference | $HHV - LHV = h_{fg,H_2O}$ | **22** | MJ/kg |
| 10 | Diffusion coefficient scaling | $D_H / D_{CH_4} = \sqrt{M_{CH_4}/M_{H_2}}$ | **3.74** | ratio |
| 11 | Buoyancy velocity (leak) | $v_b = \sqrt{2 g H (\rho_a - \rho_H)/\rho_a}$ | **1.2 to 6** | m/s |
| 12 | Stoichiometric O₂ needed | $n_{O_2} = 0.5 \, n_{H_2}$ | **0.5** | mol O₂/mol H₂ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Although hydrogen's **mass-based energy density (120 MJ/kg)** is **2.6× that of gasoline (46 MJ/kg)**, its **volumetric energy density** at ambient conditions (10.8 MJ/m³) is **one-three-thousandth** of gasoline (34,200 MJ/m³). This single fact drives the need for **compression, liquefaction, or material-based storage**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These properties are not abstract — they govern:
- **Fuel cell stack design** (hydrogen feed flow rates, pressure drop).
- **Cryogenic tank insulation** (boil-off rate calculations).
- **Ventilation system design** in enclosed spaces (buoyancy-driven dispersion).
- **Explosion-proof equipment selection** (flame arrestors, intrinsic safety).
- **Pipeline material selection** (hydrogen embrittlement of steels).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio (Mass Basis)

The combustion reaction in air is:

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 + 1.88N_2 \longrightarrow H_2O + 1.88N_2$$

**Step 1:** Identify molecular masses.
- M(H₂) = **2.016 g/mol**
- M(O₂) = **32.00 g/mol**
- M(N₂) = **28.014 g/mol**

**Step 2:** Mass of O₂ required per kg of H₂.

$$\text{Mass of } O_2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{32.00}{2.016} = 7.94 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg H}_2$$

**Step 3:** Air contains 21% O₂ by volume and approximately **23% by mass** (because of the O₂/N₂ mass difference).

**Step 4:** Compute mass of air required.

$$AFR_s = \frac{\text{Mass of air}}{\text{Mass of fuel}} = \frac{7.94}{0.23} = 34.5$$

$$\boxed{AFR_s \approx 34.3 \text{ kg air / kg H}_2}$$

This exceptionally **high AFR** means an enormous volume of air must be mixed with a small mass of hydrogen — a key design parameter for engines and burners.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Density Comparison (Mass vs. Volume)

**Step 1 — Mass energy density of hydrogen (LHV):**

$$E_m(H_2) = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric energy density of gaseous H₂ at NTP (0 °C, 1 atm):**

$$E_v = \rho \cdot E_m = 0.0899 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \times 120 \, \frac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}} = 10.79 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric energy density of liquid H₂ (at 20.28 K, density 70.8 kg/m³):**

$$E_v^{L} = 70.8 \times 120 = 8{,}496 \text{ MJ/m}^3 = 8.5 \text{ MJ/L}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with gasoline** (ρ = 740 kg/m³, LHV = 44 MJ/kg):

$$E_v(\text{gasoline}) = 740 \times 44 = 32{,}560 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Final ratio:**

$$\frac{E_v(\text{gasoline})}{E_v(\text{liquid } H_2)} = \frac{32{,}560}{8{,}496} \approx 3.83$$

$$\boxed{\text{Liquid hydrogen stores only } \approx 26\% \text{ of gasoline's energy per liter}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Diffusivity Ratio (Kinetic Theory of Gases)

For two gases A and B diffusing in the same medium at identical T, P:

$$D \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{M_{\text{eff}}}}$$

**Step 1 — Effective mass for H₂ in air (M_air = 28.97 g/mol):**

$$M_{eff,H_2} = \frac{2 \times 28.97}{2 + 28.97} = 1.87 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Effective mass for CH₄ in air (M_CH₄ = 16.04 g/mol):**

$$M_{eff,CH_4} = \frac{2 \times 16.04 \times 28.97}{16.04 \times 2 + 28.97 \times 1} = 12.93 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ratio:**

$$\frac{D_{H_2}}{D_{CH_4}} = \sqrt{\frac{M_{eff,CH_4}}{M_{eff,H_2}}} = \sqrt{\frac{12.93}{1.87}} = \sqrt{6.91}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{D_{H_2}}{D_{CH_4}} \approx 2.63}$$

Hydrogen diffuses **2.6× faster than methane**, which is why hydrogen leaks disperse quickly (both an advantage and a safety concern — easy to ignite if trapped).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Adiabatic Flame Temperature (Air)

The adiabatic flame temperature (AFT) is the maximum combustion temperature when no heat is lost. Using an energy balance:

$$\sum n_i \cdot H^{\circ}_f(\text{reactants}) = \sum n_j \cdot \left[ H^{\circ}_f + \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} C_p \, dT \right]_j$$

**Step 1 — For H₂ combustion in stoichiometric air:**

$$H_2 + 0.5 O_2 + 1.88 N_2 \longrightarrow H_2O(g) + 1.88 N_2$$

**Step 2 — Standard enthalpies of formation:**
- ΔH°f(H₂) = 0
- ΔH°f(O₂) = 0
- ΔH°f(N₂) = 0
- ΔH°f(H₂O, g) = **−241.82 kJ/mol**

**Step 3 — Energy balance (Heat released = sensible heat gained by products):**

$$241.82 = \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} \left[ C_{p,H_2O} + 1.88 \, C_{p,N_2} \right] dT$$

**Step 4 — Use average Cₚ values** (approx. 41 J/mol·K for H₂O(g), 29.1 J/mol·K for N₂):

$$241.82 \times 10^3 = (41 + 1.88 \times 29.1)(T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$241{,}820 = (41 + 54.71)(T_{ad} - 298) = 95.71 (T_{ad} - 298)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for T_ad:**

$$T_{ad} - 298 = \frac{241{,}820}{95.71} = 2526.5$$

$$T_{ad} = 2824.5 \text{ K} \approx 2825 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_{ad}(H_2 \text{ in air}) \approx 2400 \text{ K (using variable } C_p)}$$

With temperature-dependent Cₚ, the rigorous value drops to **~2400 K**, consistent with textbook data. In **pure oxygen** (no N₂ dilution), T_ad rises to **~2820 K** as shown above.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Hydrogen vs. Conventional Fuels

| Property | H₂ (gas) | CH₄ (natural gas) | Gasoline | Diesel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LHV (MJ/kg) | **120** | 50 | 44 | 42.5 |
| Density (kg/m³) | **0.09 (gas) / 70.8 (liq)** | 0.72 | 740 | 850 |
| Vol. Energy (MJ/L) | **8.5 (liq)** | 22.2 | 32,200 (per m³) | 36,200 |
| Flammability (% vol) | **4–75** | 5–15 | 1.4–7.6 | 0.6–7.5 |
| Auto-ignition (°C) | **571** | 540 | 280 | 210 |
| Flame speed (m/s) | **2.5** | 0.4 | 0.3–0.4 | — |
| Min. ignition energy (mJ) | **0.02** | 0.29 | 0.24 | — |
| Stoichiometric AFR | **34.3:1** | 17.2:1 | 14.7:1 | 14.5:1 |
| CO₂ per MJ | **0** | 56 g | 73 g | 75 g |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrogen Property Categorization (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hydrogen Fuel Properties] --> B[Physical Properties]
    A --> C[Chemical / Thermochemical]
    A --> D[Combustion Properties]
    A --> E[Safety Properties]

    B --> B1[Density: 0.0899 kg per m cubed]
    B --> B2[Boiling Point: 20.28 K]
    B --> B3[Critical Point: 33.2 K, 1.296 MPa]
    B --> B4[Diffusivity: 6.1 x 10 to power minus 5 m squared per s]

    C --> C1[LHV: 120 MJ per kg]
    C --> C2[HHV: 142 MJ per kg]
    C --> C3[HHV minus LHV equals water latent heat]
    C --> C4[Reactivity: forms water on combustion]

    D --> D1[Air Fuel Ratio: 34.3 to 1 by mass]
    D --> D2[Adiabatic Flame Temp: 2400 K in air]
    D --> D3[Flame Velocity: 2.5 m per s]
    D --> D4[Stoichiometry: 0.5 mol O2 per mol H2]

    E --> E1[Flammability Range: 4 to 75 percent]
    E --> E2[Auto Ignition: 571 degrees C]
    E --> E3[Min Ignition Energy: 0.02 mJ]
    E --> E4[Buoyancy: rises 6 times faster than air]
```

### 4.2 Hydrogen as Fuel — Block Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Hydrogen Molecule H2] --> propA[Property Evaluation]
    propA --> propA1[Mass Energy: 120 MJ per kg]
    propA --> propA2[Volume Energy: low at STP]
    propA1 --> useA[Application Selection]
    propA2 --> useA
    useA --> route1[Fuel Cell: PEMFC]
    useA --> route2[IC Engine: H2 ICE]
    useA --> route3[Combustion Turbine]
    route1 --> out1[Output: Electricity plus Water]
    route2 --> out2[Output: Mechanical Work plus NOx]
    route3 --> out3[Output: Power plus Water]
    out1 --> benefit[Carbon Free Emission Profile]
    out2 --> benefit
    out3 --> benefit
    benefit --> module[Storage and Transport Decisions]
    module --> storage1[Compressed Gas: 350 to 700 bar]
    module --> storage2[Liquid: minus 253 degrees C]
    module --> storage3[Solid State: Metal Hydrides]
```

### 4.3 Engineering Trade-off Triangle — Hydrogen as Fuel

```mermaid
graph TD
    trade[Three Pillars of Hydrogen Fuel Viability]
    trade --> p1[Energy Content per Mass: HIGH]
    trade --> p2[Energy Content per Volume: LOW]
    trade --> p3[Safety Management: CHALLENGING]
    p1 --> optA[Best mass based fuel: aerospace and rockets]
    p2 --> optB[Requires compression or liquefaction]
    p3 --> optC[Leak detection and ventilation critical]
    optA --> sol[Engineering Solution Set]
    optB --> sol
    optC --> sol
    sol --> sol1[Tank pressure optimization]
    sol --> sol2[Boil off mitigation in cryogenics]
    sol --> sol3[Flame arrestors and intrinsically safe design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each Mermaid block maps a property category to its engineering implication, culminating in the storage and safety decisions that dominate Modules 2 and 3 of the KTU syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
**List any six important properties of hydrogen as a fuel.**

**Model Answer (6 key points, each worth 0.5 marks):**

1. **Highest energy content per unit mass** — 120 MJ/kg (LHV), about 2.5× gasoline.
2. **Lowest density of any gas** — 0.0899 kg/m³ at STP; lighter than air by a factor of 14.4.
3. **Wide flammability range in air** — 4% to 75% by volume.
4. **High auto-ignition temperature** — 571 °C, but very low minimum ignition energy (0.02 mJ).
5. **Clean combustion product** — only water vapor; zero CO₂, CO, SOₓ, or particulates.
6. **Highest diffusivity of all gases** — leaks disperse rapidly in open air; but pose accumulation risk in enclosed spaces.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
**Differentiate between LHV and HHV of hydrogen fuel.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | LHV (Lower Heating Value) | HHV (Higher Heating Value) |
|---|---|---|
| Value for H₂ | **120 MJ/kg** | **142 MJ/kg** |
| Water product state | Leaves as **vapor** | Condenses to **liquid** |
| Latent heat recovered | **Not** recovered | **Recovered** (2442 kJ/kg) |
| Used in | Gas-phase applications, fuel cells, gas turbines | Boilers, condensing engines |
| Difference | HHV − LHV = **22 MJ/kg** (latent heat of vaporization of water) | — |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **physical properties of hydrogen** and discuss their engineering significance in storage and handling.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat derivations, compute the **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio** (by mass and by volume) for complete combustion of pure hydrogen. Compare it with that of methane.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Physical Properties and Significance:**

| # | Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Atomic mass | 1.008 g/mol | Lightest element — high buoyancy on release |
| 2 | Gas density (STP) | 0.0899 kg/m³ | Lighter than air → rises & disperses in leaks |
| 3 | Boiling point | 20.28 K | Requires cryogenic storage; boil-off loss challenge |
| 4 | Critical temp/pressure | 33.2 K / 1.296 MPa | Must be kept below 33.2 K for liquid phase |
| 5 | Diffusivity in air | 6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s | Rapid mixing; difficult leak detection |
| 6 | Thermal conductivity | 0.182 W/m·K | 7× air — high heat transfer but also cryogenic losses |
| 7 | Viscosity | 8.76 × 10⁻⁶ Pa·s | Lower than air → low friction loss in pipelines |
| 8 | Joule-Thomson coefficient | −0.03 K/atm at 300 K | Cools on expansion — useful for liquefaction |

> **Significance Summary (write in exam):** Hydrogen's extremely low density and high diffusivity make **leak detection difficult** but **atmospheric dispersion rapid**. Its **cryogenic requirements** demand multi-layer insulated tanks. The **inversion temperature** must be considered in Joule-Thomson-based expanders.

**Part (b) — Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio:**

**By Mass (derived in Section 3.1):**

$$H_2 + 0.5 O_2 + 1.88 N_2 \to H_2O + 1.88 N_2$$

$$AFR_{\text{mass}} = \frac{0.5 \times 32.00}{1 \times 2.016 \times 0.23} = \boxed{34.3 \text{ kg air / kg H}_2}$$

**By Volume (at same T, P):**

$$AFR_{\text{vol}} = \frac{0.5 \times (1/0.21)}{1} = \frac{0.5 \times 4.76}{1} = \boxed{2.38 \text{ m}^3 \text{ air / m}^3 H_2}$$

**Comparison with Methane (CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O):**

$$AFR_{\text{mass}}(CH_4) = \frac{2 \times 32}{1 \times 16 \times 0.23} = 17.4$$

$$\boxed{\text{H}_2 \text{ requires } 1.97 \times \text{ more air than CH}_4 \text{ per unit mass}}$$

**Engineering Implication:** Hydrogen's **high AFR** means a **larger combustion chamber or burner** is needed to admit the necessary air volume — a major design constraint in retrofits of gasoline engines.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **combustion properties of hydrogen** in detail. How do they compare with gasoline?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **adiabatic flame temperature** of a stoichiometric H₂–air mixture using an energy balance. Comment on the assumption made.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Combustion Properties:**

| Property | H₂ | Gasoline (Octane C₈H₁₈) |
|---|---|---|
| LHV (MJ/kg) | **120** | 44 |
| Flammability (vol %) | **4 – 75** | 1.4 – 7.6 |
| Auto-ignition (°C) | **571** | 280 |
| Laminar flame velocity (m/s) | **2.5** | 0.3 – 0.4 |
| Min. ignition energy (mJ) | **0.02** | 0.24 |
| AFR (mass) | **34.3:1** | 14.7:1 |
| Flame temperature (K) | **2400** | 2300 |
| Product | **H₂O** | CO₂ + H₂O + pollutants |

**Key observations (must be in answer):**
- Hydrogen's flame velocity is **~7× higher** than gasoline → faster energy release, risk of flashback.
- Min. ignition energy is **12× lower** → hot surfaces, sparks, static electricity easily ignite.
- Flammability range is **~10× wider** → hazardous mixtures form easily.
- Cleanest combustion possible (zero carbon).

**Part (b) — Adiabatic Flame Temperature:**

From Section 3.4 derivation:

$$H_2 + 0.5 O_2 + 1.88 N_2 \to H_2O(g) + 1.88 N_2$$

Energy balance:

$$\sum n_{\text{react}} H^{\circ}_f = \sum n_{\text{prod}} \left[ H^{\circ}_f + \int C_p \, dT \right]$$

$$0 = -241.82 + \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} (C_{p,H_2O} + 1.88 \cdot C_{p,N_2}) \, dT$$

Using constant Cₚ:

$$241{,}820 = (41 + 54.7)(T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$T_{ad} = 298 + \frac{241{,}820}{95.7} \approx 2825 \text{ K}$$

With **temperature-dependent Cₚ** (more accurate): $T_{ad} \approx 2400 \text{ K}$ (in air).

**Assumption stated:** 
- **Adiabatic** (no heat loss) — hence the *adiabatic* name.
- **Complete combustion** (no dissociation of H₂O at high T).
- **Constant Cₚ** (approximation; in reality Cₚ varies with T).
- **Equilibrium** products only; no soot or unburned fuel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing LHV vs. HHV values** — write both numbers clearly. Wrong value = 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations (adiabatic, complete combustion, ideal gas). Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Writing AFR without showing the derivation** — at least 1 mark is reserved for the mass-balance setup.
> 4. **Forgetting the conversion factor 0.23** when computing mass-based air requirements from volume percentages.
> 5. **Missing "vol %" units** for flammability limits — easy to lose 0.5 mark per question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] Hydrogen is the **lightest element** (M = 1.008 g/mol) and the **simplest**.
- [x] **LHV = 120 MJ/kg**, **HHV = 142 MJ/kg**; difference = latent heat of vaporization of water (22 MJ/kg).
- [x] **Density at STP = 0.0899 kg/m³** — 14.4× lighter than air → **buoyant dispersion**.
- [x] **Boiling point = 20.28 K**; **critical point = 33.2 K, 1.296 MPa**.
- [x] **Diffusivity is highest of all gases** — leaks disperse fast but are hard to detect.
- [x] **Flammability range 4–75 vol%** — widest of all common fuels.
- [x] **Auto-ignition temperature = 571 °C**; **min. ignition energy = 0.02 mJ** (very low!).
- [x] **Stoichiometric AFR = 34.3:1 (mass)** and **2.38:1 (volume)**.
- [x] **Adiabatic flame temperature ≈ 2400 K (air)** and ≈ 2820 K (pure O₂).
- [x] **Combustion product = H₂O only** — zero CO₂ emissions.
- [x] Hydrogen is a **secondary energy carrier**, not a primary fuel.
- [x] **Laminar flame velocity = 2.5 m/s** (about 7× gasoline).
- [x] Hydrogen's **volumetric energy density is low** → drives the need for compression, liquefaction, or material-based storage.
- [x] Hydrogen's **mass energy density is the highest of any fuel** → ideal for aerospace and weight-sensitive applications.
- [x] **Joule-Thomson inversion** must be considered in expansion systems.
- [x] Hydrogen causes **embrittlement** of steels (relevant to pipelines and pressure vessels).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Introduction to Infrastructure Requirement for Hydrogen Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Introduction to Infrastructure Requirement for Hydrogen Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Production Infrastructure** refers to the integrated network of physical facilities, utility supply systems, conversion equipment, and supporting logistics required to manufacture hydrogen at industrial scale. This includes feedstock supply (water, natural gas, biomass, electricity), production units (electrolyzers, reformers, gasifiers), auxiliary systems (cooling, purification, compression), and the upstream/downstream interfaces needed to deliver hydrogen of the required purity to storage, transport, or end-use systems.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the hydrogen value chain is broken into four pillars: **Production → Storage → Transportation → End Use**, and Module 1 establishes that *no single technology* can deliver hydrogen at scale without a deliberate, capital-intensive **infrastructure ecosystem**.

### The Three Colour Codes of Hydrogen (KTU Syllabus Anchor)

| Colour | Production Route | Carbon Intensity | Infrastructure Dependency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grey H₂** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | ~9–10 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | Natural gas pipeline + reformer + CCS optional |
| **Blue H₂** | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) | ~1–3 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | Grey H₂ assets + CO₂ pipeline + sequestration wells |
| **Green H₂** | Water Electrolysis (PEM/ALK/SOEC) powered by renewables | < 1 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | Renewable power plant + electrolyzer + demineralized water + grid balancing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Green hydrogen** is the focus of India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023), which targets **5 million metric tonnes (MMT) of green H₂ production by 2030**, requiring an estimated **125 GW of dedicated renewable energy capacity**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to set up a *large-scale tea stall chain* in Kerala. You cannot simply buy a kettle and expect to serve 10,000 customers daily. You need:

- A **water source** (deep borewell + water purifier)
- A **gas/electricity line** (continuous fuel)
- A **stove/kettle fleet** (conversion equipment)
- A **storage godown** (for bulk milk, sugar, tea leaves)
- **Delivery trucks** (transportation)
- A **reliable road/grid** (logistics backbone)

Hydrogen production is the same. The "kettle" is the electrolyzer or reformer, but the **infrastructure ecosystem** — water pipelines, renewable power plants, CO₂ sequestration wells, storage tanks, transport pipelines — is what actually determines whether the hydrogen economy becomes real or remains a lab curiosity.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
