- Lower Heating Value (LHV) of Hydrogen: **120 MJ/kg**
- Higher Heating Value (HHV) of Hydrogen: **142 MJ/kg**
- Energy density (volumetric, gaseous at 1 atm): **10.8 MJ/m³**
- Density of liquid hydrogen at 20 K: **70.8 kg/m³**
- Electrochemical equivalent: 1 kg H₂ ≈ **39.4 kWh** (HHV basis)
- Stoichiometric water consumption: **9 kg of water per kg of H₂** produced by electrolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Hydrogen Production Infrastructure

A greenfield hydrogen production facility requires six interlocking infrastructure layers. Each layer has a capacity ratio that determines the next layer's sizing.

1. **Renewable Power Generation Plant** (Solar PV / Wind farm)
2. **Power Conditioning & Grid Interface** (Rectifier / Inverter / Transformer)
3. **Water Treatment & Supply System** (Demineralization + Deionization)
4. **Electrolysis / Reforming Stack** (The core conversion unit)
5. **Gas Processing Train** (Purification, Drying, Compression)
6. **Storage & Dispatch Interface** (Buffer tanks, pipelines)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Sizing Logic

- **Step 1 — Power Plant Sizing**: To produce 1 tonne/day of green H₂, you need roughly **52.5 MWh of renewable electricity per day** (based on 53 kWh/kg real-world electrolyzer consumption including BoP losses). For 24/7 operation, this translates to a **dedicated renewable capacity of approximately 2.2 GW per million tonnes per annum (MTPA)** when accounting for capacity factors of ~0.25–0.35 for solar-wind hybrids.

- **Step 2 — Water Sizing**: Stoichiometric minimum = 9 kg H₂O per kg H₂. In practice, plant consumption is **18–22 litres of demineralized water per kg of H₂** because of cooling tower evaporation, blowdown, and electrolyzer membrane humidification.

- **Step 3 — Land Footprint**: A 1 GW renewable plant requires **~5 km² for solar PV** or **~50 km² for wind**. The electrolyzer hall adds **~0.05–0.1 km² per 100 MW of electrolyzer capacity**.

- **Step 4 — Capital Cost Ratio**: Approximate capital split for green H₂ plant — **Electrolyzer stack: 40%, Renewable power: 35%, BoP & water systems: 15%, Storage & compression: 10%**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{H_2} = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t \cdot n}{2F}$ | Faraday's law for electrolytic H₂ production | kg |
| 2 | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of H₂ produced from current $I$ over time $t$ | kg |
| 3 | $\eta_{elec} = \dfrac{\text{HHV}_{H_2}}{E_{input}}$ | Electrolyzer system efficiency | % |
| 4 | $P_{RE,req} = \dfrac{M_{H_2} \cdot e_{specific}}{CF \cdot 8760}$ | Required renewable capacity for target annual H₂ output | MW |
| 5 | $V_{H_2O} = 9 \cdot M_{H_2} \cdot k_{purity}$ | Water consumption per kg of H₂ (purity factor $k_{purity} \approx 1.8$ to $2.4$) | litres |
| 6 | $LCOH = \dfrac{CAPEX \cdot CRF + OPEX}{M_{H_2,annual}}$ | Levelized Cost of Hydrogen | USD/kg |
| 7 | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | Capital Recovery Factor, $i$ = interest rate, $N$ = project life | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{CRF}_{CO_2} = \dfrac{M_{CO_2}}{M_{H_2}}$ | Carbon footprint ratio (kg CO₂ per kg H₂) | kg/kg |
| 9 | $A_{land} = \dfrac{P_{RE,req}}{G_{spec} \cdot \eta_{PV}}$ | Land area for solar PV plant | m² |
| 10 | $V_{storage} = \dfrac{M_{H_2,buffer}}{\rho_{H_2,stp}}$ | Storage volume at STP (0.0899 kg/m³) | m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Faraday's constant** $F = 96485$ C/mol, and the **molar mass of H₂** $M = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol. The number of electrons transferred per H₂ molecule $n = 2$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This sizing framework is used by:
- **MNRE (Ministry of New & Renewable Energy, India)** for allocating green hydrogen production subsidies under SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition).
- **International Energy Agency (IEA)** in its *Global Hydrogen Review* annual reports.
- **Project developers** (L&T, Reliance, Adani, ACME) for feasibility studies of GW-scale hydrogen hubs in Gujarat, Rajasthan, and Tamil Nadu.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Law Applied to Electrolyzer Sizing

Starting from the fundamental principle that **2 moles of electrons** liberate **1 mole of H₂ gas**:

$$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$$

The charge $Q$ required to liberate $n_{H_2}$ moles of H₂ is:

$$Q = n_{H_2} \cdot n \cdot F$$

The mass of H₂ liberated is:

$$m_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \cdot M_{H_2}$$

Substituting $n_{H_2} = \dfrac{Q}{nF}$:

$$m_{H_2} = \dfrac{Q \cdot M_{H_2}}{nF} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{nF}$$

Therefore the theoretical production rate per unit current is:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot M_{H_2}}{nF} = \dfrac{I \cdot 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96485}$$

Simplifying:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.0445 \times 10^{-8} \cdot I \quad \text{(kg/s per Ampere)}$$

For 1 kA of current = 1000 A:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.0445 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s} = 37.6 \text{ kg/hour}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Sizing a 100 TPD Green Hydrogen Plant

**Given Data:**
- Target production: 100 tonnes/day = 100,000 kg/day of green H₂
- Specific energy consumption: 53 kWh/kg (real-world PEM electrolyzer)
- Renewable plant capacity factor: $CF = 0.30$ (typical solar-wind hybrid in India)
- Pure water consumption: 9 kg/kg + BoP losses factor 2.0

**Step 1 — Daily Energy Requirement**

$$E_{daily} = 100{,}000 \times 53 = 5.3 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 2 — Required Renewable Capacity**

Converting to annual and dividing by capacity factor and 8760 hours:

$$P_{RE} = \dfrac{5.3 \times 10^6 \times 365}{0.30 \times 8760} = \dfrac{1.9345 \times 10^9}{2628}$$

$$P_{RE} = 736{,}118 \text{ kW} \approx 736 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 — Daily Water Requirement**

$$V_{H_2O} = 100{,}000 \times 9 \times 2.0 = 1.8 \times 10^6 \text{ litres/day} = 1800 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Storage Volume at 200 bar (Standard Buffer for 1-day autonomy)**

$$V_{storage} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{200 \times 0.0899} = 5562 \text{ m}^3 \approx 5.6 \text{ million litres at 200 bar}$$

**Step 5 — Land Footprint (Solar PV based)**

With $G_{spec} = 200 \text{ W/m}^2$ and $\eta_{PV} = 0.20$:

$$A_{land} = \dfrac{736 \times 10^6}{200 \times 0.20} = 1.84 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^2 = 18.4 \text{ km}^2$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Infrastructure Ratio Benchmarks

| Infrastructure Component | Unit Ratio (per kg H₂) | Benchmark for 1 MTPA Plant |
|---|---|---|
| Renewable Power Required | 52.5–55 kWh | 52.5 TWh/year |
| Pure Water | 18–22 litres | 18–22 million m³/year |
| Land (Solar) | ~0.18 m²·year/kg | 18–20 km² |
| Land (Wind) | ~1.8 m²·year/kg | 180–200 km² |
| Electrolyzer Stack Power | ~10 kW | 1 GW stack capacity |
| CO₂ Avoided (vs Grey) | ~9 kg | 9 MT/year |
| Buffer Storage (200 bar) | ~0.055 m³ | 55,000 m³ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrogen Production Infrastructure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Solar PV Farm] --> B1[Power Collection Substation]
    A2[Onshore Wind Farm] --> B1
    A3[Grid Import Backup] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[HV Transformer 33kV to 11kV]
    C1 --> D1[Rectifier AC to DC]
    C1 --> D2[Power Electronics Filter]
    D1 --> E1[PEM Electrolyzer Stack 100MW]
    D2 --> E1
    F1[Raw Water Intake Borewell] --> G1[Multi Stage Filtration]
    G1 --> G2[Reverse Osmosis Unit]
    G2 --> G3[Mixed Bed Deionizer]
    G3 --> H1[Deionized Water Tank]
    H1 --> E1
    E1 --> I1[Gas Liquid Separator]
    I1 --> J1[PTSA Deoxidizer]
    J1 --> K1[Compressor to 200 bar]
    K1 --> L1[Buffer Storage Tanks]
    L1 --> M1[Dispatch Pipeline to Refinery]
    L1 --> M2[Tube Trailer Loading Bay]
    L1 --> M3[Onsite Ammonia Methanation Plant]
    E1 --> N1[Cooling Tower Heat Rejection]
    N1 --> O1[Atmosphere]
    E1 --> P1[Oxygen Vent to Atmosphere or Capture]
```

### 4.2 Infrastructure Layer Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Layer 1 Renewable Energy Source] --> L2[Layer 2 Power Conditioning]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Water Treatment Plant]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Electrolyzer Hall]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Gas Processing Train]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Storage and Dispatch]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 End Use Sector]
    L7 --> L8[Refineries Ammonia Steel Mobility Power]
```

### 4.3 Capacity Cascade Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Target: 1 MTPA Green Hydrogen] --> Q2[Required RE Capacity: 2.2 GW]
    Q2 --> Q3[Required Land: 20 to 25 km squared Solar]
    Q1 --> Q4[Required Water: 20 million m cube per year]
    Q4 --> Q5[Water Treatment Plant: 5500 m cube per day]
    Q1 --> Q6[Required Storage: 55000 m cube at 200 bar]
    Q6 --> Q7[Compressor Power: 25 to 30 MW]
    Q1 --> Q8[Total CAPEX: 1.0 to 1.4 Billion USD]
    Q8 --> Q9[Stack 40 percent Renewable 35 percent BoP 15 percent Storage 10 percent]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Exams**: Block diagrams in the ESE paper are evaluated for (a) completeness of all upstream/downstream interfaces, (b) correct identification of utility streams (water, power, heat, oxygen), and (c) logical sequencing of the conversion train. Students frequently lose marks by **omitting the oxygen by-product stream** and the **cooling water recirculation loop** — both are critical infrastructure elements.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

List any **three** infrastructure requirements for setting up a green hydrogen production plant of 100 TPD capacity. State the unit ratio of each requirement per kg of H₂.

**Model Answer:**

The three primary infrastructure requirements are:

1. **Renewable Electrical Power**: Approximately **53 kWh/kg H₂** of dedicated renewable energy input is required. For a 100 TPD plant operating at 30% capacity factor, this translates to **~736 MW of installed renewable capacity**.

2. **Demineralized Water**: Stoichiometrically 9 kg H₂O per kg H₂, but practical plant consumption is **18–22 litres/kg** when demineralization rejects, cooling tower evaporation, and membrane humidification are included.

3. **Buffer Storage Volume**: At 200 bar, approximately **0.055 m³/kg H₂** of buffer storage volume is required for one-day operational autonomy, equal to **~5500 m³ for a 100 TPD plant**.

*(Each point carries 1 mark: 0.5 for identification + 0.5 for the correct unit ratio.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

Differentiate between **Grey**, **Blue**, and **Green** hydrogen with respect to their production infrastructure and carbon footprint.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Grey H₂ | Blue H₂ | Green H₂ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedstock | Natural gas (CH₄) | Natural gas + CO₂ capture | Water + Renewable electricity |
| Core Equipment | Steam Methane Reformer | SMR + CCS unit | PEM/ALK/SOEC Electrolyzer |
| Carbon Footprint (kg CO₂/kg H₂) | 9–10 | 1–3 | < 1 |
| Key Infrastructure | Gas pipeline, reformer | Grey assets + CO₂ pipeline + wells | Solar/wind farm + electrolyzer + water plant |
| Maturity | Fully commercial | Pilot/Demo | Early commercial |

*(1 mark for correct definition of each colour; 1 mark for carbon footprint values; 1 mark for the infrastructure dependency column.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **complete infrastructure layout** of a green hydrogen production plant based on PEM electrolysis. Clearly identify all upstream, conversion, and downstream subsystems. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A green hydrogen plant of capacity 50 TPD is to be installed in Rajasthan. The plant uses solar PV as the primary energy source with a capacity factor of 0.28. The PEM electrolyzer consumes 54 kWh/kg H₂ at full load. Calculate: (i) the required solar PV capacity in MW, (ii) the annual pure water requirement in m³, and (iii) the buffer storage volume at 350 bar for 2 days of autonomy. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram and Subsystems:**

A PEM-based green hydrogen plant comprises the following subsystems:

- **Upstream**: Solar PV farm → DC combiner → Step-up transformer → AC collection bus.
- **Power Conditioning**: Grid-tie inverter → Rectifier → DC bus for electrolyzer → Transformer to cell voltage level.
- **Water Treatment**: Raw water storage → Sand filter → Activated carbon → Reverse osmosis → Mixed bed deionizer → Deionized water tank → DI water circulation pump.
- **Conversion Unit**: PEM electrolyzer stack (bipolar plates, membrane electrode assembly, anode and cathode catalyst layers) operating at 60–80°C, 30–80 bar differential pressure.
- **Gas Processing**: Phase separator → Catalytic deoxidizer (PTSA) → Molecular sieve dryer → Compressor train → Buffer storage.
- **Downstream**: Pipeline to refinery / ammonia plant / mobility refuelling station / steel sector via blending or direct reduction.
- **Auxiliary**: Cooling tower (electrolyzer heat rejection), oxygen vent or liquefaction unit, control room with DCS, fire & gas detection (H₂ sensors at 1% LEL threshold).

*Mark Allocation: Diagram 3 marks + Identification of 6 subsystems 3 marks + Brief explanation 1 mark.*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Production target = 50 TPD = 50,000 kg/day
- $e_{specific} = 54$ kWh/kg
- $CF = 0.28$
- Plant operates 365 days/year
- Pure water factor $k_{purity} = 2.0$
- Storage pressure = 350 bar
- Autonomy = 2 days

**(i) Required Solar PV Capacity:**

$$E_{daily} = 50{,}000 \times 54 = 2.7 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/day}$$

$$E_{annual} = 2.7 \times 10^6 \times 365 = 9.855 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$P_{RE} = \dfrac{9.855 \times 10^8}{0.28 \times 8760} = \dfrac{9.855 \times 10^8}{2452.8}$$

$$\boxed{P_{RE} \approx 401.8 \text{ MW} \approx 402 \text{ MW}}$$

**(ii) Annual Pure Water Requirement:**

$$V_{H_2O, daily} = 50{,}000 \times 9 \times 2.0 = 9 \times 10^5 \text{ litres/day} = 900 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

$$V_{H_2O, annual} = 900 \times 365 = 3.285 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$

$$\boxed{V_{H_2O, annual} \approx 3.29 \text{ lakh m}^3/\text{year}}$$

**(iii) Buffer Storage Volume at 350 bar for 2 days:**

Total H₂ to be buffered = 50,000 × 2 = 100,000 kg

Using compressed gas equation: $V = \dfrac{m \cdot R \cdot T}{M \cdot P}$ or approximate storage density at 350 bar ≈ 26 kg/m³.

$$V_{storage} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{26}$$

$$\boxed{V_{storage} \approx 3846 \text{ m}^3}$$

*Alternative method using ideal gas law:*
$V = \dfrac{nRT}{P} = \dfrac{(100000/2.016) \times 8.314 \times 298}{350 \times 10^5} \approx 3504$ m³. Either value within engineering approximation is acceptable.

*Mark Allocation: (i) 3 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks.*

---

**Question B. [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **infrastructure bottlenecks** that currently limit the scale-up of green hydrogen production in India. Reference the National Green Hydrogen Mission targets in your answer. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare the **capital expenditure (CAPEX) breakdown** of grey, blue, and green hydrogen plants per kg of H₂ produced. Identify which infrastructure component contributes the maximum share in each case. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Infrastructure Bottlenecks:**

1. **Renewable Power Availability**: Green H₂ requires 50–55 kWh/kg of firm renewable power. India's RE penetration in 2024 is ~43% of installed capacity, but **firm dispatchable RE** (with storage) remains expensive. Intermittency forces electrolyzers to operate below 50% capacity utilization.

2. **Water Scarcity**: Producing 5 MMT H₂/year (NGHM target) needs **100–110 million m³/year** of demineralized water — equivalent to the annual drinking water need of ~1 million people. Rajasthan, Tamil Nadu, and Gujarat (target hub states) are water-stressed.

3. **Electrolyzer Manufacturing**: India currently imports **>90% of electrolyzer stacks** (mainly from EU and China). The Mission's target of 5 MMT requires ~25–30 GW of electrolyzer manufacturing capacity, of which domestic capacity is < 1 GW.

4. **Land Acquisition**: A 1 MTPA green H₂ hub needs **20–25 km² of contiguous land** for solar + electrolyzer + storage. Land aggregation, forest clearances, and Right of Way (RoW) for pipelines cause 2–3 year delays.

5. **Grid Integration & Curtailment**: Electrolyzers are **flexible loads** (can ramp 10–100% in seconds) but transmission infrastructure is not yet designed to feed multi-GW variable loads at single points.

6. **Skilled Workforce**: PEM/SOEC stack assembly, BoP engineering, and H₂ safety codes require specialized training that is not yet mainstream in Indian engineering curricula.

*Mark Allocation: Each bottleneck 1 mark + Mission target reference 1 mark.*

**Part (b) — CAPEX Breakdown Comparison:**

| Cost Component | Grey H₂ (USD/kg) | Blue H₂ (USD/kg) | Green H₂ (USD/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedstock (Gas/Electricity) | 0.8–1.2 | 0.8–1.2 | 2.5–4.0 |
| CAPEX Amortization | 0.2–0.3 | 0.4–0.6 | 1.0–1.8 |
| CCS Infrastructure | — | 0.3–0.5 | — |
| O&M | 0.1–0.2 | 0.2–0.3 | 0.3–0.5 |
| **Total LCOH** | **1.2–1.8** | **2.0–3.0** | **4.0–6.0** |

- **Grey H₂**: Maximum share is the **feedstock (natural gas)** cost — ~65–70% of LCOH.
- **Blue H₂**: Maximum share is **feedstock + CCS amortization** — combined ~60%.
- **Green H₂**: Maximum share is the **CAPEX of electrolyzer stack + dedicated renewables** — combined ~50–55% of LCOH.

*Mark Allocation: Table 4 marks + Identification of max contributor for each 3 marks.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Errors**: Students often write water requirement as 9 kg/kg without accounting for the practical multiplier (1.8–2.4×). KTU evaluators explicitly test the **boP-adjusted** number.
> 2. **Forgetting the Oxygen Stream**: The O₂ co-product of electrolysis is an infrastructure element (vent stack, liquefaction, or sale to industry). Omitting it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing LH₂ vs GH₂ Density**: When solving storage volume, students use 70.8 kg/m³ (liquid) instead of 0.0899 kg/m³ (gaseous STP) without applying the 200/350 bar compression factor.
> 4. **Not Stating Assumptions**: Capacity factor, project life, and discount rate must be stated explicitly in CAPEX problems.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Green hydrogen is the strategic focus** of India's NGHM 2023 — targets 5 MMT by 2030, requiring ~125 GW of dedicated RE.
- The **infrastructure stack** has 6 layers: RE source → Power conditioning → Water treatment → Electrolyzer → Gas processing → Storage/dispatch.
- **Faraday's Law** is the foundation: $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{nF}$ where $n = 2$ for H₂.
- **Unit benchmarks to memorize**: 53 kWh/kg electricity, 9 kg/kg stoichiometric water (×2 practical), 0.055 m³/kg buffer at 200 bar, ~20 km²/MTPA solar land.
- **CAPEX split** of green H₂: Stack 40% + RE 35% + BoP 15% + Storage 10%.
- **Hydrogen colours** — Grey (SMR, ~9 kg CO₂/kg), Blue (SMR+CCS, ~2 kg CO₂/kg), Green (electrolysis, <1 kg CO₂/kg).
- **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg**, HHV = 142 MJ/kg — used in energy-equivalent calculations.
- **Buffer storage** at 200 bar: density ~16 kg/m³; at 350 bar: ~26 kg/m³; at 700 bar: ~40 kg/m³.
- **Storage equation** to remember: $V = \dfrac{m}{density_{P,T}}$ or via ideal gas $V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$.
- Always state **assumptions** (capacity factor, project life, discount rate, water multiplier) in numerical answers.
- **LCOH formula**: $\text{LCOH} = \dfrac{\text{CAPEX} \cdot \text{CRF} + \text{OPEX}_{annual}}{M_{H_2,annual}}$ — commonly tested.
- **Electrolysis co-product** is high-purity O₂ — infrastructure must include vent or capture.
- **Safety infrastructure** (H₂ sensors, flame detectors, vent stacks, exclusion zones) is graded in 14-mark questions even though syllabus emphasizes it in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Hydrogen Storage

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Hydrogen Storage):**
> Hydrogen storage refers to the containment of molecular hydrogen (H₂) in a usable form — as a compressed gas, a cryogenic liquid, or bound within solid/liquid carrier materials — such that it can be safely retained, transported, and released on demand to feed fuel cells, combustion engines, or industrial reactors without violating the operational pressure, temperature, and energy-density targets of the application.

The hydrogen molecule is the **smallest and lightest** molecule in existence. This single physical fact is the root of every storage challenge engineers face. Because H₂ has an extremely low molecular weight (≈ 2.016 g/mol) and a very low critical temperature (**T_c = 33.19 K / −240 °C**), it refuses to behave like conventional fuels. It cannot simply be poured into a tank at room temperature — it either leaks through microscopic pores, boils off, or demands absurdly high pressures.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hyperactive Puppy" 🐶

Imagine trying to store a hyperactive, very small puppy in your house. You have three options:
1. **Compress the puppy into a small steel cage** (Compressed Gas — 350–700 bar) → heavy cage, but the puppy is immediately accessible.
2. **Freeze the puppy into a tiny ice cube** (Liquid H₂ at −253 °C) → very energy-intensive to keep frozen, but you carry a lot of puppy in a small space.
3. **Chemically bond the puppy to a giant sponge that releases him only when warmed** (Metal Hydrides / LOHCs) → the sponge is heavy, but storage is safe and dense at low pressure.

Each option has trade-offs between **gravimetric density** (kg H₂ / kg system), **volumetric density** (kg H₂ / m³), **thermodynamic stability**, and **energy cost of release**. The KTU syllabus groups these as **Physical Storage** and **Material-Based Storage**.

### Why Hydrogen Storage is the Hardest Problem in the H₂ Value Chain

| Property | Value for H₂ | Comparison |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight | **2.016 g/mol** | 8× lighter than methane |
| Boiling point (1 atm) | **20.28 K (−252.87 °C)** | Colder than liquid nitrogen (77 K) |
| Critical temperature | **33.19 K** | Below ambient → impossible to liquefy at room T |
| Energy content (LHV) | **120 MJ/kg** | ~3× gasoline per kg, but ~4× lower per volume |
| Flammability range in air | **4–75 vol %** | Extremely wide; requires rigorous safety |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 demands a working knowledge of **compressed gas, liquid hydrogen, cryo-compressed, metal hydrides, complex hydrides, chemical hydrides, and sorption-based storage**, along with the **thermodynamic parameters** (ΔH, ΔS, plateau pressure) that govern reversible H₂ uptake/release.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Hydrogen Storage Methods

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) classifies storage into two families:

### A. Physical Storage (no chemical bond formed)
1. **Compressed Hydrogen Gas (CHG / CG-H₂)** — 350 bar (Type III) and 700 bar (Type IV).
2. **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)** — cryogenic at **20.3 K**.
3. **Cryo-Compressed Hydrogen (CcH₂)** — combines 250–350 bar + cryogenic temperature.

### B. Material-Based Storage (H₂ bound in/on a material)
4. **Metal Hydrides (MH)** — e.g., LaNi₅H₆, MgH₂, TiFeH₂.
5. **Complex Hydrides** — e.g., NaAlH₄, LiBH₄, LiNH₂.
6. **Chemical Hydrides** — e.g., NH₃, LOHCs (Methylcyclohexane / Toluene, N-ethylcarbazole).
7. **Sorption-Based (Physisorption)** — MOFs, activated carbon, CNTs at cryo-T.

---

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Foundation — The Van't Hoff Equation

Every reversible metal-hydride reaction obeys a thermal equilibrium that links temperature (T) and the equilibrium hydrogen plateau pressure (P_eq):

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T \cdot \Delta S$$

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ and $\Delta G = -RT \ln K_{eq} = RT \ln P_{eq}$ (for H₂ as the gas, with standard state of 1 bar). Therefore:

$$\ln P_{eq} = \frac{\Delta H}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} - \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

This is the **Van't Hoff equation** — the single most-tested equation on the entire Module 1 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for valuation:** "**Hot hydrides want to give up H₂; cold hydrides want to hold it.**" Higher T → higher P_eq → release. Lower T → lower P_eq → absorption.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\ln P_{eq} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$ | Van't Hoff equilibrium | P in bar, T in K, ΔH in J/mol |
| 2 | $\ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-point Van't Hoff form | — |
| 3 | $\rho_g = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{H_2}}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$ | Real-gas density (CHG) | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $\rho_v = \dfrac{M_{H_2}}{V_m}$ | Volumetric density | kg H₂ / m³ |
| 5 | $\rho_{wt\%} = \dfrac{n_{H} \cdot M_{H}}{M_{material}} \times 100$ | Gravimetric density | wt % |
| 6 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{H_2,stored}}{V_{tank}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | — |
| 7 | $\Delta H_{des} \approx 30\text{–}40\ \text{kJ/mol}\ H_2$ | Target for near-ambient MH | kJ/mol H₂ |
| 8 | $E_{comp} = \dfrac{n R T}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Ideal isentropic compression | J |
| 9 | $Q_{liq} \approx 13.6\ \text{MJ/kg}$ | Liquefaction energy demand | MJ/kg H₂ |
| 10 | $T_{boil}(H_2) = 20.28\ \text{K}$ | Boiling point at 1 atm | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** The Van't Hoff plot of $\ln P_{eq}$ vs $1/T$ is a straight line whose **slope gives $\Delta H$** and whose **intercept gives $-\Delta S$**. Board examiners love this plot — expect a 7-mark question asking you to determine ΔH and ΔS from two (T, P) data points.

---

## 2.3 Reversible Capacity, Plateau Pressure & Hysteresis

A **Pressure-Composition-Isotherm (PCT)** is the experimental fingerprint of any metal hydride.

- **α-phase (solid solution):** dilute H in metal, low H content.
- **α + β plateau:** two phases coexist; pressure is nearly constant. *This plateau pressure IS the Van't Hoff pressure at that T.*
- **β-phase (hydride):** H-rich, near stoichiometric.

**Key practical defects:**
- **Hysteresis** = the absorption plateau pressure is lower than the desorption plateau pressure at the same T → wasted thermodynamic driving force.
- **Slope of plateau** = non-ideality; flat plateaus preferred.
- **Activation** = some alloys (e.g., MgH₂) need several high-T cycles before full capacity is reached.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation — Van't Hoff Equation from First Principles

Consider the generic reversible metal-hydride reaction:

$$M_{(s)} + \dfrac{x}{2}\ H_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons MH_{x(s)}$$

**Step 1 — Write the equilibrium constant.**
For an H₂ gas at standard pressure P° = 1 bar, the equilibrium constant of this heterogeneous reaction reduces to the partial pressure of hydrogen:

$$K_{eq} = P_{H_2} / P^\circ = P_{eq}\ (\text{in bar})$$

**Step 2 — Use the standard thermodynamic relation.**
The change in Gibbs free energy at any state relates to the equilibrium constant by:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$$

Substituting K_eq = P_eq:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln P_{eq}$$

**Step 3 — Expand ΔG° into enthalpy and entropy terms.**

$$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for ΔG° and rearrange.**

$$-RT \ln P_{eq} = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$

Divide both sides by $-RT$:

$$\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$$

Reorder into the conventional slope-intercept form (note that ΔH of hydride formation is **negative** so $-\Delta H/R$ is **positive** when written as a function of $1/T$):

$$\boxed{\ln P_{eq} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\cdot \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}}$$

where ΔH and ΔS are the **molar enthalpy and entropy of *desorption*** (positive for endothermic release). This is the canonical KTU Van't Hoff form.

**Step 5 — Convert to a two-point working formula for exam problems.**
Subtracting the Van't Hoff equation evaluated at (T₁, P₁) from that at (T₂, P₂):

$$\ln P_2 - \ln P_1 = \dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This is the form used in numerical problems (e.g., to find the desorption temperature required for a target pressure of 1 bar).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem (KTU pattern):** A metal hydride LaNi₅H₆ has a desorption enthalpy ΔH = +30.8 kJ/mol and entropy ΔS = +108 J/mol·K. Calculate: (i) the equilibrium pressure at 298 K, (ii) the desorption temperature required to reach P_eq = 1 bar, (iii) comment on whether this alloy is suitable for a PEM fuel cell vehicle (operating ~350 K, 1–10 bar delivery).

### Part (i) — P_eq at 298 K

Given: ΔH = 30.8 × 10³ J/mol, ΔS = 108 J/mol·K, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, T = 298 K.

$$\ln P_{eq} = \dfrac{30800}{8.314 \times 298} - \dfrac{108}{8.314}$$

Compute each term separately:

$$\dfrac{30800}{8.314 \times 298} = \dfrac{30800}{2477.6} = 12.432$$

$$\dfrac{108}{8.314} = 12.990$$

Therefore:

$$\ln P_{eq} = 12.432 - 12.990 = -0.558$$

$$P_{eq} = e^{-0.558} = 0.572\ \text{bar}$$

**Interpretation:** At room temperature LaNi₅H₆ releases H₂ only at sub-atmospheric pressure — *not* useful for room-T delivery.

### Part (ii) — T required for P_eq = 1 bar

Set $\ln P_{eq} = 0$:

$$0 = \dfrac{30800}{8.314 \cdot T} - \dfrac{108}{8.314}$$

$$\dfrac{30800}{T} = 108 \quad\Rightarrow\quad T = \dfrac{30800}{108} = 285.2\ \text{K}\ (\approx 12\ ^\circ\text{C})$$

**Interpretation:** LaNi₅H₆ reaches 1 bar desorption pressure just above freezing — ideal for low-T PEM systems.

### Part (iii) — Engineering Comment

At 350 K, $\ln P_{eq} = 12.432 \cdot (298/350) - 12.990 = 10.59 - 12.99 = -2.40$, so P_eq ≈ **0.09 bar** — still below atmospheric, meaning the hydride is over-charged. The alloy is **excellent for room-T/near-ambient storage** but **unsuitable alone** for high-pressure PEMFC vehicle duty; it is typically paired with a small CHG buffer tank.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Performance Matrix of Storage Methods

| Method | ρ_wt % (system) | ρ_vol (kg H₂/m³) | T (°C) | P (bar) | Reversible? | Maturity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 350 bar CHG (Type III) | ~3–5 | ~20 | 25 | 350 | Yes | Commercial |
| 700 bar CHG (Type IV) | ~4–6 | ~35–40 | 25 | 700 | Yes | Commercial (Mirai, Nexo) |
| Liquid H₂ (LH₂) | ~5–10 | ~70 | −253 | 1 | Yes (boil-off) | Commercial (space, trucking) |
| Cryo-compressed | ~5–10 | ~50–60 | −253 | 250–350 | Yes | Pilot |
| LaNi₅H₆ (AB₅) | ~1.4 | ~90 | 25 | 1–5 | Yes | Commercial (small-scale) |
| MgH₂ | ~7.6 | ~110 | 300 | 1–10 | Yes (slow) | R&D |
| NaAlH₄ (Ti-catalyzed) | ~5 | ~60–90 | 80–200 | 1–100 | Partial | R&D |
| Liquid organic (MCH/Tol) | ~6–8 | ~50 | 200–300 | 1 | Yes (catalytic) | Pilot (CHJC) |
| MOF-210 (77 K, 80 bar) | ~8 (material) | ~50 | −196 | 80 | Yes | Lab |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DoE 2025 Targets for on-board light-duty vehicles:** 6.5 wt % system gravimetric, 50 g H₂/L volumetric. **Currently, no single technology simultaneously meets both targets** — hence the KTU syllabus treats this as an open engineering challenge.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow for Selecting a Storage Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrogen Storage Requirement] --> B{Application Scale}
    B -- Small Portable --> C{Operating Temperature}
    B -- Stationary --> D[Compressed Gas 350 bar OR Metal Hydride LaNi5]
    B -- Automotive --> E[PEMFC operating 350 K]
    B -- Heavy Truck/Aviation --> F[Liquid H2 or Cryo-Compressed]

    C -- Near Room T --> G[AB5 or AB2 Metal Hydride]
    C -- 250 to 350 C --> H[Complex Hydride NaAlH4]
    C -- > 300 C --> I[MgH2 or LiBH4]

    E --> J{Pressure Target}
    J -- 700 bar on-board --> K[Type IV CHG Tank]
    J -- 350 bar on-board --> L[Type III CHG Tank]

    F --> M{Cryogenic Infrastructure}
    M -- Available --> N[Liquid H2 20.3 K]
    M -- Limited --> O[Cryo-Compressed CcH2]

    G --> P[Suitable for laptops, sensors, backup power]
    H --> Q[Suitable for FC forklifts, mid-T PEM]
    I --> R[Suitable for grid / long-duration]
    K --> S[Mirai Nexo production vehicles]
    L --> T[City buses industrial]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Metal-Hydride Storage Tank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TankAssembly["MH Storage Vessel Architecture"]
        direction TB
        Inlet[H2 Inlet Valve 20 bar] --> Filter[Particulate Filter]
        Filter --> HX[Heat Exchanger Coil]
        HX --> Bed[Hydride Bed MgH2 Pellets]
        Bed --> Screen[Porous Sinter Disc]
        Screen --> Outlet[H2 Outlet to Fuel Cell]
        Bed -. Heat In from HX .-> Bed
        ExternalHeat[External Heat Source 300 C] -. Hot Fluid .-> HX
        Vent[Safety Pressure Relief 50 bar] --> Bed
    end

    InletController[Pressure Transducer + PLC] -. Feedback .-> Inlet
    OutletController[Temperature Sensor] -. Feedback .-> Bed
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this as a control loop:** During **charging**, high-pressure H₂ enters; the exothermic formation of the hydride releases heat that the HX removes (otherwise the bed self-heats and refuses to absorb more H₂). During **discharging**, external heat is supplied to drive the endothermic desorption.

## 4.3 PCT Isotherm Schematic (ASCII Reproducible Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low H content alpha phase] -->|H2 absorption| B[alpha + beta plateau region constant P]
    B -->|H2 absorption| C[Beta phase hydride near stoichiometry]
    C -->|H2 desorption| B
    B -->|H2 desorption| A
    note1[Plateau pressure = Van't Hoff P_eq at this T] -. Annotates .-> B
    note2[Hysteresis loop between absorption and desorption paths] -. Annotates .-> B
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**"Define 'gravimetric storage density' and 'volumetric storage density' of a hydrogen storage system. Why are both metrics reported together?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Gravimetric density** is the mass of hydrogen stored divided by the total mass of the storage system (tank + contents), expressed in **wt %** (or kg H₂/kg system). It quantifies how *light* the storage unit is for a given H₂ inventory.
- **Volumetric density** is the mass of hydrogen stored divided by the total volume occupied, in **kg H₂/m³** (or g/L). It quantifies how *compact* the storage is.
- Both must be reported because a system may excel in one dimension and fail in the other. Example: liquid H₂ has excellent volumetric density (~70 kg/m³) but suffers boil-off losses; metal hydrides like LaNi₅H₆ give ~90 kg/m³ but only ~1.4 wt %. Vehicle packaging requires **both** targets to be met simultaneously.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**"What is 'boil-off loss' in liquid hydrogen storage, and what design strategies mitigate it?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Boil-off loss (BOL)** is the continuous vaporization of LH₂ because no cryogenic insulation is perfect; ambient heat leak raises some LH₂ above its 20.28 K boiling point, producing gaseous H₂ that must be vented to avoid pressure rupture. Typical BOL rates in modern dewars are **0.1–0.3 % per day**.
- **Mitigation:** (i) multi-layer vacuum insulation (MLVI) with 30+ alternating reflecting layers; (ii) **ortho-para hydrogen conversion** during liquefaction (para-H₂ is energetically favoured at low T and reduces self-heating); (iii) **re-liquefaction cycles** using cryocoolers; (iv) **cryo-compressed** storage that tolerates and uses the boil-off gas by re-pressurising instead of venting.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — Choose ONE)

### Question A — 14 Marks

#### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the Van't Hoff equation for a reversible metal-hydride reaction and explain its engineering significance for designing a hydrogen storage bed.

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **State the generic reversible reaction** (1 mark):
$$M_{(s)} + \frac{x}{2}H_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons MH_{x(s)}$$

2. **Write equilibrium constant** (1 mark): For pure condensed phases + 1 gas, $K_{eq} = P_{H_2}$ (in bar, taking P° = 1 bar).

3. **Apply thermodynamic identity** $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ and equate to $\Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$ (2 marks):

$$\ln P_{eq} = \frac{\Delta H}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} - \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

4. **Explain slope-intercept interpretation** (1 mark): slope = ΔH/R (positive since desorption is endothermic), intercept = −ΔS/R.

5. **Engineering significance** (2 marks):
   - Predicts the operating T required to deliver H₂ at a target pressure (e.g., 1 bar for PEMFC).
   - Allows comparison of candidate alloys: smaller ΔH → lower release T → safer, cheaper system.
   - Two experimentally measured (T, P_eq) points fully characterise the material.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A candidate alloy shows P_eq = 1.0 bar at 350 K and P_eq = 5.0 bar at 400 K. Calculate ΔH and ΔS of desorption. Will this alloy release H₂ above 1 bar at 298 K? Justify with calculation.

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. Use two-point Van't Hoff form (2 marks):

$$\ln \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{\Delta H}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

2. Substitute (T₁ = 350 K, P₁ = 1 bar), (T₂ = 400 K, P₂ = 5 bar) (1 mark):

$$\ln 5 = \frac{\Delta H}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{400}\right)$$

$$1.609 = \frac{\Delta H}{8.314}\left(\frac{400-350}{350 \cdot 400}\right)$$

$$1.609 = \frac{\Delta H}{8.314} \cdot \frac{50}{140000}$$

$$1.609 = \frac{\Delta H}{8.314} \cdot 3.571 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta H = \frac{1.609 \cdot 8.314}{3.571 \times 10^{-4}} = 37{,}460\ \text{J/mol} \approx 37.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

3. Solve for ΔS using one of the points (2 marks):

$$\Delta S = \frac{\Delta H}{T_1} - R \ln P_1 = \frac{37460}{350} - 8.314 \cdot \ln 1$$

$$\Delta S = 107.0 - 0 = 107.0\ \text{J/mol·K}$$

4. Check at 298 K (2 marks):

$$\ln P_{eq}(298) = \frac{37460}{8.314 \cdot 298} - \frac{107}{8.314} = 15.12 - 12.87 = 2.25$$

$$P_{eq}(298\ \text{K}) = e^{2.25} = 9.5\ \text{bar}$$

**Conclusion:** Yes, the alloy releases H₂ at ≈ 9.5 bar even at 298 K — *above* the 1 bar PEMFC inlet requirement — confirming it as a promising near-ambient storage material.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly forget to convert ΔH into **J/mol** when the problem gives kJ/mol. Also, taking $\ln 1 = 0$ in Step 3 is a trap — many write 0 = 0 and panic. Remember: $\ln 1 = 0$ *is* the correct answer; use the other point to verify.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare compressed gas, liquid hydrogen, and metal-hydride storage with respect to: (i) energy density, (ii) safety, (iii) infrastructure cost, (iv) reversibility.

**Step-by-step Model Solution (use a comparison table):**

| Criterion | 700 bar CHG | LH₂ (−253 °C) | Metal Hydride (LaNi₅) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Energy density | ~35 kg/m³, 4–6 wt% | ~70 kg/m³, 10–15 wt% | ~90 kg/m³, ~1.4 wt% |
| (ii) Safety | High pressure rupture, hydrogen embrittlement | Cryogenic burns, boil-off, asphyxiation | Low pressure, but pyrophoric powder risk on cycling |
| (iii) Infrastructure | Compressors + Type IV tanks (mature) | Liquefiers consume **~13.6 MJ/kg H₂** (~30% LHV) | Heat-exchange beds + alloy cost |
| (iv) Reversibility | Fully reversible | Boil-off losses (~0.1–0.3 %/day) | Reversible but with hysteresis & degradation |

**Conclusion (1 mark for synthesis):** CHG is the most mature and reversible; LH₂ gives highest volumetric density but heavy infrastructure cost; metal hydrides offer the safest low-pressure option but suffer gravimetric penalty.

#### (b) [7 Marks] With the help of a Pressure-Composition-Isotherm (PCT), explain the three regions observed during hydrogen absorption in a metal hydride. Comment on the role of hysteresis.

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Plot axes** (1 mark): x-axis = H/M atomic ratio, y-axis = log P(H₂).

2. **Region 1 — α-phase solid solution** (2 marks): At low H concentration, H atoms dissolve into the metal lattice forming a dilute solid solution. Pressure rises steeply with composition because few sites are occupied.

3. **Region 2 — α + β plateau** (2 marks): As more H is added, the hydride phase nucleates. α and β coexist; the pressure remains nearly constant — this **plateau pressure = Van't Hoff P_eq at the isotherm temperature**. Length of the plateau = usable reversible capacity.

4. **Region 3 — β-phase** (1 mark): After full conversion to the hydride, further H uptake requires very high pressures; the curve rises steeply again.

5. **Hysteresis** (1 mark): The desorption isotherm lies at lower pressure than the absorption isotherm at the same T. The lost free energy (= RT ln(P_abs/P_des)) is dissipated as lattice strain and heat; it reduces the effective deliverable pressure and must be allowed for in system design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students sketch the PCT with **pressure** on the x-axis — *wrong*. Convention is **composition (H/M) on x-axis, log P on y-axis**. Board examiners deduct 1 mark for axis reversal.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hydrogen storage is classified** into **physical** (CHG, LH₂, CcH₂) and **material-based** (MH, complex hydrides, chemical hydrides, sorbents).
- The **Van't Hoff equation** $\ln P_{eq} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$ is the **single most important equation** in Module 1 — expect a derivation + numerical every exam cycle.
- **Higher ΔH ⇒ hydride too stable** (needs >300 °C to release); **lower ΔH ⇒ unstable at room T** (releases prematurely). The "sweet spot" for near-ambient storage is **ΔH ≈ 30–40 kJ/mol H₂**.
- **PCT isotherms** have three regions: α solid solution, α+β plateau (where Van't Hoff applies), β hydride. The plateau length = usable reversible capacity.
- **Hysteresis** between absorption and desorption is unavoidable; reduce it via alloy design (e.g., substituting Ni in LaNi₅).
- **Liquid H₂** requires cooling to 20.28 K; liquefaction consumes ~**13.6 MJ/kg** (≈ 30% of H₂'s LHV). Boil-off loss is mitigated by MLVI + ortho-para conversion.
- **Type IV tanks** (polymer liner + carbon-fiber overwrap) at **700 bar** are the current commercial standard for automotive FCVs (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo).
- **DoE 2025 targets:** 6.5 wt % system, 50 g H₂/L — no single technology meets both simultaneously.
- Always convert ΔH to **J/mol** before substituting into the Van't Hoff equation.
- Always check **unit consistency**: P_eq in bar (since K_eq uses P° = 1 bar), T in **Kelvin**, R = **8.314 J/mol·K**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dispensing and Utilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dispensing and Utilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydrogen Dispensing — Formal Definition

**Hydrogen Dispensing** is the controlled, metered, and pressurized transfer of gaseous or liquid hydrogen from a storage/supply system into a vehicle's on-board storage tank, performed at a **Hydrogen Refueling Station (HRS)** under strict thermodynamic and safety envelopes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Hydrogen dispensing is the terminal end-point of the hydrogen value chain — analogous to a petrol/diesel pump — where the molecule is delivered at the **correct pressure, temperature, mass flow rate, and purity** so that the end-user (vehicle, industrial plant, or stationary fuel cell) can operate safely and efficiently.

### Key Dispensing Pressure Classes (Per ISO 17268 & SAE J2601)

| Class | Pressure | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **H35** | **350 bar (35 MPa)** | Buses, light commercial vehicles |
| **H70** | **700 bar (70 MPa)** | Passenger FCEV (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) |
| **LH2** | Cryogenic liquid | Heavy-duty trucks, aviation |

> [!NOTE]
> The dispensing protocol is governed by **SAE J2601** (Compressed Hydrogen Vehicle Fueling Connection Devices) and **ISO 17268** (Gaseous hydrogen land vehicle refuelling connection devices). The fill density is limited to a maximum of **2.0 × 10²³ H₂ molecules per mL** of tank volume to prevent over-pressurization damage.

---

## 1.2 Hydrogen Utilization — Formal Definition

**Hydrogen Utilization** refers to the *end-use conversion* of stored hydrogen into useful work, heat, electricity, or chemical feedstock. The hydrogen atom is consumed in three principal pathways:

1. **Electrochemical Conversion** → Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFC), Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFC), Alkaline Fuel Cells (AFC)
2. **Thermochemical Conversion** → Hydrogen Internal Combustion Engines (H₂-ICE), Hydrogen Turbines
3. **Chemical Feedstock** → Haber-Bosch Ammonia, Methanol Synthesis, Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) Steelmaking, Refining Hydrocracking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is hydrogen utilization "special" compared to fossil fuels?**
> When hydrogen reacts with oxygen in a fuel cell, the only by-product is **water (H₂O)**. The theoretical electrical efficiency of a PEMFC stack alone reaches **50–60 %**, with combined heat and power (CHP) systems exceeding **85 % LHV**.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hydrogen ATM & The Hydrogen Bank"

Think of the entire hydrogen ecosystem as a **banking system**:

- **Production** = The "Mint" (creates the currency / H₂ molecules)
- **Storage** = The "Bank Vault" (compressed gas tanks, liquid tanks, underground caverns)
- **Transportation** = The "Armored Truck" (pipeline, tube trailer, LH2 tanker)
- **Dispensing** = The **ATM** ← *You are here* → The customer walks up, inserts a card, enters a PIN, and receives exactly the right amount of "cash" (kg of H₂) at the right "denomination" (pressure & flow rate)
- **Utilization** = What the customer **spends** it on — groceries (car), house renovation (home fuel cell), business (industry)

### The "ATM Transaction" of Hydrogen Dispensing

When a Toyota Mirai pulls up to a 700-bar station, in 3–5 minutes the following "transaction" occurs:

1. The **Nozzle** connects to the receptacle (like an ATM card slot)
2. The **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** negotiates a fueling protocol (like entering a PIN)
3. The **Mass Flow Controller** meters the kg of H₂ delivered
4. The **Heat Exchanger / Pre-cooler** chills the gas to **–40 °C** to prevent the tank from overheating as it compresses
5. The **Payment Terminal** closes the transaction

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for Hydrogen Trade:**
> Unlike petrol (sold by the litre), hydrogen is sold by the **kilogram (kg)**, because energy content per kg is the relevant engineering parameter.
> **1 kg of H₂ ≈ 120 MJ (LHV) ≈ 33.33 kWh.**

> [!TIP]
> **Why pre-cool to –40 °C?**
> When you rapidly compress H₂ into a fixed-volume tank, the gas heats up (the **Joule-Thomson effect** during throttling, and adiabatic compression heating). If the gas exceeds **85 °C** at end-of-fill, the tank's carbon-fiber overwrap may be permanently damaged. Pre-cooling is therefore a *thermal management necessity*, not a luxury.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Hydrogen Refueling Station (HRS)

A modern HRS consists of **five functional blocks**:

### Block 1 — Input / Supply
- Delivered via pipeline (gaseous), tube trailer, or liquid hydrogen tanker
- Typical inlet pressure: **20–80 bar** (gaseous) or saturated liquid at **–253 °C** (LH2)

### Block 2 — Compression / Liquefaction Buffering
- **Diaphragm compressors** (oil-free, contamination-free) are preferred
- Multi-stage compression with intercooling
- Final delivery pressure: **450 bar** (storage) → ramped to **875 bar** (filling H70 vehicles)

### Block 3 — High-Pressure Storage Cascade
- Three banks at progressively higher pressures: **Low (≈ 200 bar)**, **Medium (≈ 400 bar)**, **High (≈ 800–950 bar)**
- Cascade is used to maintain fueling pressure as banks empty

### Block 4 — Pre-cooling System
- **Joule-Thomson (J-T) expansion cooling** OR mechanical refrigeration chiller
- Cools gas to **–40 °C** before entering vehicle tank
- Cooling capacity typically **35–60 kW**

### Block 5 — Dispenser
- Hoses, breakaway couplings, nozzles, mass flow meter, ECU, display
- Communication with vehicle via **IR / CAN bus** per SAE J2799

---

## 2.2 The SAE J2601 Fueling Protocol (Simplified Logic)

The fueling protocol operates in **three distinct states**:

| State | Vehicle SOC | Filling Behavior | Limiting Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **State 1** | 0–10 % | Very fast filling (~4 g/s) | Compressor capacity |
| **State 2** | 10–80 % | Constant pressure ramp | Compressor / heat rejection |
| **State 3** | 80–100 % | Decelerating fill | **End-of-fill temperature ≤ 85 °C** |

---

## 2.3 End-Use Pathway Comparison — KTU Engineering Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam table** — memorize the efficiency ranges and end-products.

| Utilization Mode | Device | Energy Conversion | Electrical Efficiency | By-Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electrochemical | **PEMFC** | H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O + 2e⁻ | **50–60 %** | Pure water + heat |
| Electrochemical | **SOFC** (high-T) | Same chemistry, 600–1000 °C | **55–65 %** | Water + high-grade heat |
| Electrochemical | **AFC** | Alkaline electrolyte | **60–70 %** | Water + KOH |
| Thermochemical | **H₂-ICE** | Combustion in piston engine | **20–35 %** (η_th) | NOₓ, water |
| Thermochemical | **H₂ Gas Turbine** | Modified GT combustion | **35–42 %** (η_comb) | Water, trace NOₓ |
| Chemical | **Haber-Bosch** | N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ | Catalyst-dependent | Ammonia (feedstock) |
| Chemical | **Methanol Synthesis** | CO₂ + 3H₂ → CH₃OH + H₂O | – | Green methanol |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the spine of any Part B numerical.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{H_2} = m \cdot LHV$ | Total chemical energy in mass *m* of H₂ | J (kg × 120 MJ/kg) |
| 2 | $V_{H_2} = \dfrac{m \cdot R \cdot T}{M \cdot P}$ | Real-gas volume from ideal gas law | m³ |
| 3 | $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ where $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol | Moles of hydrogen | mol |
| 4 | $T_{final} = T_{initial} \left(\dfrac{P_{final}}{P_{initial}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$ | Adiabatic compression temperature (γ = 1.405 for H₂) | K |
| 5 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Isentropic compressor work (per mole) | J/mol |
| 6 | $\rho_{H_2, 70MPa} \approx 42 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Compressed gas density at 700 bar, 15 °C | kg/m³ |
| 7 | $\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot I}{LHV \cdot \dot{m}_{H_2}}$ | Fuel cell stack efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $E_{cell} = 1.229 - 0.00085(T - 298) + RT\ln(P_{H_2}P_{O_2}^{0.5})$ | PEMFC Nernst open-circuit voltage | V |
| 9 | $t_{fill} = \dfrac{m_{tank}}{\dot{m}_{disp}} = \dfrac{V_t \rho_{tank}}{\dot{m}_{disp}}$ | Time to fill vehicle tank | s |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{cool} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Chiller heat rejection rate | W |

---

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Pre-Cooling — A Conceptual Walk-Through

**The Problem:** Imagine you have a 700-bar rigid tank. As you pump more H₂ in:

1. Compression raises the gas temperature
2. The tank walls absorb some heat, but the gas is nearly adiabatic
3. Without cooling, end-of-fill temperature would exceed **150 °C**
4. The **Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)** overwrap softens above **85 °C**

**The Solution:** Pre-cool the inlet gas to **–40 °C**. The cold inlet absorbs the compression heat, ensuring the final gas in the tank is below the 85 °C threshold.

**Engineering Consequence:** The chiller is often the **bottleneck of the HRS** — it dictates the maximum fill rate in State 3.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> The thermodynamic limit imposed by Joule-Thomson cooling is the reason why **350 bar stations** can dispense faster than **700 bar stations** — lower pressure ratio means less heating, less chiller load.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Hydrogen Required to Fill a 700-bar Tank

**Given:**
- Tank volume: $V_t = 122$ L = $0.122$ m³ (Toyota Mirai spec)
- Final pressure: $P_2 = 700$ bar
- Final temperature: $T_2 = 15 \degree C = 288.15$ K (after settling)
- Initial empty (vacuum neglected for simplicity)
- Compressibility factor of H₂ at 700 bar, 15 °C: $Z \approx 1.4$

**Step 1 — Apply the Real Gas Equation**

The real gas law is:

$$PV = ZnRT$$

For mass:

$$PV = Z \cdot \frac{m}{M} \cdot R T$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Mass *m***

Rearranging algebraically:

$$m = \frac{P \cdot V \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values**

Using:
- $P = 700 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $V = 0.122$ m³
- $M = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol
- $Z = 1.4$
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T = 288.15$ K

$$m = \frac{(700 \times 10^5)(0.122)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(1.4)(8.314)(288.15)}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 7 \times 10^7 \times 0.122 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 7 \times 10^7 \times 2.4595 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 17216.7$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate Denominator**

$$\text{Denominator} = 1.4 \times 8.314 \times 288.15$$

$$= 11.6396 \times 288.15$$

$$= 3354.4$$

**Step 6 — Final Computation**

$$m = \frac{17216.7}{3354.4} \approx 5.13 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the published spec of the Toyota Mirai tank: **~5.0 kg usable H₂ at 700 bar.** The small discrepancy is due to idealization of the settling temperature profile.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: End-of-Fill Temperature Without Pre-cooling

**Given:**
- Initial tank temperature: $T_1 = 15 \degree C = 288.15$ K
- Final pressure: $P_2 = 700$ bar
- Initial pressure: $P_1 = 1$ atm = 1.013 bar
- $\gamma_{H_2} = 1.405$

**Step 1 — Adiabatic Compression Equation**

For a quasi-adiabatic charging process:

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Pressure Ratio**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{700}{1.013} \approx 691$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Exponent**

$$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{1.405 - 1}{1.405} = \frac{0.405}{1.405} \approx 0.2883$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $691^{0.2883}$**

Using logarithms:

$$\ln(691) = 6.538$$

$$0.2883 \times 6.538 = 1.885$$

$$e^{1.885} = 6.585$$

**Step 5 — Final Temperature**

$$T_2 = 288.15 \times 6.585 \approx 1897 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 \approx 1624 \degree C$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is why pre-cooling is non-negotiable.** Without pre-cooling, the tank would reach ~1624 °C — well above the melting point of steel and the decomposition temperature of the polymer liner. The 85 °C limit is enforced through **chilled inlet gas + moderate fill rate**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Pre-cooling Temperature to Stay Below 85 °C

**Given:**
- Target end temperature: $T_2 = 85 \degree C = 358.15$ K
- Inlet gas enters tank after mixing
- We approximate: inlet temperature must be such that final settled gas is 85 °C
- For a well-mixed assumption, **inlet gas temperature ≈ end temperature** for slow fills
- For fast fills, use mass-weighted average: $T_{avg} = \dfrac{T_{in} + T_{final}}{2}$

**Step 1 — Mass-Weighted Energy Balance**

$$T_{avg} = \frac{T_{in} + T_{final}}{2}$$

For the final to equal 85 °C and the average to also be controlled:

$$T_{in} = 2 T_{final} - T_{initial}$$

**Step 2 — Assume Final = 85 °C, Initial Tank = 15 °C**

$$T_{in} = 2(85) - 15 = 155 \degree C$$

But this is far too hot. So we must restrict fill rate.

**Step 3 — Practical Engineering Rule**

For fast fill (3–5 min), SAE J2601 mandates inlet gas ≤ **–40 °C** so that:

$$T_{in} = -40 \degree C, \quad T_{final} \approx 85 \degree C$$

$$T_{avg} = \frac{(-40) + 85}{2} = 22.5 \degree C$$

This is well within the tank design envelope.

**Step 4 — Verify Chiller Cooling Load**

Mass flow rate of fill: $\dot{m} \approx 5 \text{ kg} / 300 \text{ s} = 0.0167$ kg/s

Specific heat of H₂ at constant pressure: $c_p = 14.31$ kJ/(kg·K)

Temperature drop: $\Delta T = T_{comp,out} - T_{chilled}$

For compressor exit at **~40 °C** (after intercooling) and chilled exit at **–40 °C**:

$$\Delta T = 40 - (-40) = 80 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Cooling Load**

$$\dot{Q}_{cool} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$= 0.0167 \times 14310 \times 80$$

$$= 19120 \text{ W} \approx 19.1 \text{ kW}$$

This matches the typical 20–60 kW chiller capacity of an H70 station.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Comparison

| Parameter | **350 bar Station** | **700 bar Station** |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure ratio | ~350:1 | ~700:1 |
| Compressor stages | 2–3 | 3–4 |
| Pre-cooling need | Modest | **Mandatory (–40 °C)** |
| Chiller size | 10–20 kW | 35–60 kW |
| Energy stored in tank | ~3.3 kg (≈ 400 km) | ~5 kg (≈ 600 km) |
| CapEx | Lower | ~30–40 % higher |
| Fill time | ~3 min | ~4–5 min |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Fuel Cell Stack Power & Efficiency

**Given:**
- A PEMFC stack with $N = 300$ cells
- Cell voltage under load: $V_{cell} = 0.7$ V
- Current: $I = 300$ A
- H₂ flow: $\dot{m} = 0.005$ kg/s
- LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg

**Step 1 — Stack Output Power**

$$P_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I = 300 \times 0.7 \times 300$$

$$P_{stack} = 63000 \text{ W} = 63 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Chemical Input Power**

$$P_{chem} = \dot{m} \cdot LHV = 0.005 \times 120 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{chem} = 600000 \text{ W} = 600 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Stack Electrical Efficiency**

$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{P_{stack}}{P_{chem}} = \frac{63}{600} = 0.105$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 10.5 % looks low because we used only 0.005 kg/s. In reality, for **63 kW output**, the actual H₂ flow needed is:

$$\dot{m}_{required} = \frac{P_{stack}}{LHV \cdot \eta_{stack,real}}$$

For a real stack efficiency of **55 %**:

$$\dot{m}_{required} = \frac{63000}{120 \times 10^6 \times 0.55}$$

$$= 9.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$= 3.44 \text{ kg/h}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** The 10.5 % value is an "input-output mismatch" — for a 63 kW stack, you only need ~1 kg/h, not 18 kg/h. The exercise teaches that **the cell voltage × current / mass flow must all be consistently matched**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Block Architecture of a 700-bar HRS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Supply: Pipeline / Tube Trailer / LH2 Tanker] --> B[Compressor Unit: Diaphragm 3-stage]
    B --> C[Low Pressure Bank ~ 200 bar]
    C --> D[Medium Pressure Bank ~ 450 bar]
    D --> E[High Pressure Bank ~ 900 bar]
    E --> F[Pre-cooling Chiller: Target -40 deg C]
    F --> G[Dispenser ECU and Mass Flow Meter]
    G --> H[Nozzle and Breakaway Coupling]
    H --> I[FCEV Tank: 700 bar Type IV]
    J[Back-up Buffer Storage] --> C
    K[Payment and Data Terminal] --> G
    L[Safety Sensors: H2 Leak IR, Flame, Pressure] --> G
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Hydrogen End-Use Utilization Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H[Hydrogen from Storage or Dispenser] --> M1[PEM Fuel Cell PEMFC 50 to 60 percent]
    H --> M2[SOFC Solid Oxide 55 to 65 percent]
    H --> M3[Alkaline Fuel Cell AFC 60 to 70 percent]
    H --> T1[H2 Internal Combustion Engine]
    H --> T2[H2 Gas Turbine]
    H --> C1[Haber Bosch Ammonia Synthesis]
    H --> C2[Methanol Synthesis CO2 plus 3H2]
    H --> C3[Direct Reduced Iron Steelmaking]
    M1 --> U1[FCEV Passenger Cars]
    M1 --> U2[Stationary CHP Home and Industry]
    M2 --> U3[Grid Scale Power Generation]
    T1 --> U4[Heavy Duty Trucks Retrofit]
    T2 --> U5[Peaking Power Plants]
    C1 --> U6[Fertilizer Industry]
    C2 --> U7[Green Marine Fuel]
    C3 --> U8[Green Steel Production]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — SAE J2601 Three-State Fueling Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State1
    State1: STATE 1: SOC 0 to 10 percent, Fast Fill, Compressor Limited
    State1 --> State2: SOC Reaches 10 percent
    State2: STATE 2: SOC 10 to 80 percent, Constant Pressure Ramp, Compressor and Chiller Limited
    State2 --> State3: SOC Reaches 80 percent
    State3: STATE 3: SOC 80 to 100 percent, Decelerating Fill, Temperature Limited at 85 deg C
    State3 --> [*]: Fill Complete
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Mass and Energy Flow During a Fill Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Station Storage at 900 bar 40 deg C] -->|Compressor Discharge| B[Chiller Inlet at 40 deg C]
    B -->|Joule Thomson and Refrigerant| C[Chiller Outlet at -40 deg C]
    C -->|Dispensing| D[Vehicle Tank 700 bar]
    D -->|End of Fill| E[Tank Settles to 85 deg C max]
    E -->|Vehicle Drives| F[FC Stack: H2 plus O2 to H2O plus Power]
    F -->|Heat Rejected| G[Radiator Thermal Loop]
    F -->|Electrical Output| H[Traction Motor and Battery]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Functional Architecture: Refueling to Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Production
        P1[Electrolysis: PEM / Alkaline / SOEC]
        P2[SMR with Carbon Capture]
        P3[Biomass Gasification]
    end
    subgraph Storage_Transport
        S1[Compression to 200-900 bar]
        S2[Liquefaction at -253 deg C]
        S3[Pipeline Network]
    end
    subgraph Dispensing_HRS
        D1[Storage Cascade]
        D2[Pre-cooling System]
        D3[Dispenser and Nozzle]
    end
    subgraph End_Use
        E1[PEMFC Vehicle]
        E2[Stationary FC CHP]
        E3[Industrial Feedstock]
    end
    Production --> Storage_Transport
    Storage_Transport --> Dispensing_HRS
    Dispensing_HRS --> End_Use
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define hydrogen dispensing. List any two international standards governing hydrogen refueling connectors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Hydrogen dispensing is the controlled transfer of compressed or liquid hydrogen from a station storage system to an end-user's tank at the specified pressure, temperature, and mass flow rate, ensuring safety and quality.

**Two key international standards (1.5 marks each):**
- **ISO 17268** — Gaseous hydrogen land vehicle refuelling connection devices (defines nozzle geometry, receptor dimensions).
- **SAE J2601** — Compressed hydrogen vehicle fueling protocol (defines the pressure-temperature-time envelope and the three-state fueling algorithm).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is hydrogen pre-cooled to –40 °C before entering an FCEV tank? Justify with the Joule-Thomson effect.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When compressed hydrogen is rapidly injected into a fixed-volume CFRP tank, the gas undergoes **adiabatic compression heating**. If the inlet gas is at ambient temperature, the final gas temperature would far exceed the **85 °C thermal limit** of the carbon-fiber overwrap (calculated value: ~1624 °C for ideal adiabatic case).

By pre-cooling the gas to **–40 °C** using a Joule-Thomson expansion chiller or mechanical refrigeration unit, the cold inlet gas absorbs the compression-generated heat, ensuring the **end-of-fill temperature remains ≤ 85 °C**. This protects the tank's polymer liner, the CFRP overwrap, and the thermoplastic boss, and prevents pressure overshoot.

(1.5 marks for the problem statement + 1.5 marks for the –40 °C solution and physics.)

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

---

### 🔹 Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a 700-bar Hydrogen Refueling Station (HRS). Identify the role of the pre-cooling system in achieving the end-of-fill temperature target of 85 °C.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The HRS consists of the following sequential blocks:
- **Input Supply** (pipeline / tube trailer / LH2 tanker)
- **Compressor** (3-stage diaphragm, oil-free)
- **High-Pressure Cascade Storage** (Low / Medium / High pressure banks at 200, 450, 900 bar)
- **Pre-cooling Chiller** (J-T expansion / refrigeration to –40 °C)
- **Dispenser** (ECU, mass flow meter, hose, nozzle, breakaway coupling)
- **Vehicle Tank** (Type IV: polymer liner + CFRP overwrap, 700 bar)

**2. Role of Pre-cooling System (4 marks):**

The end-of-fill temperature limit is **85 °C** as mandated by SAE J2601 and CFRP material specs.

Without pre-cooling, the adiabatic temperature rise from 1 bar to 700 bar would yield:
$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \approx 1624 \degree C$$

This is catastrophic. By pre-cooling the inlet gas to **–40 °C**, the chiller absorbs the heat of compression:
$$\dot{Q}_{chiller} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.0167 \times 14310 \times 80 \approx 19.1 \text{ kW}$$

The chiller is thus the **rate-limiting component** in State 3 of the fueling protocol.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 700-bar Type IV hydrogen tank has a geometric volume of 122 L. Calculate the mass of hydrogen stored when the tank is filled to 700 bar at 15 °C. Use Z = 1.4 for hydrogen at this state. Universal gas constant R = 8.314 J/mol·K, M(H₂) = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Real Gas Equation:**

$$m = \frac{P \cdot V \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$m = \frac{(700 \times 10^5)(0.122)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(1.4)(8.314)(288.15)}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$7 \times 10^7 \times 0.122 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 17216.7$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$1.4 \times 8.314 \times 288.15 = 3354.4$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$m = \frac{17216.7}{3354.4} = 5.13 \text{ kg}$$

**Answer:** The tank stores **≈ 5.13 kg of hydrogen** at 700 bar, 15 °C.

**Energy content:** $E = 5.13 \times 120 \text{ MJ/kg} \approx 616 \text{ MJ} \approx 171 \text{ kWh}$.

(Marks distribution: Equation 2 marks, substitution 2 marks, numerator/denominator evaluation 2 marks, final answer with units 1 mark.)

---

### 🔹 Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the three main pathways of hydrogen utilization with examples. Compare the electrical efficiency of PEMFC and SOFC systems.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Three Pathways of Utilization (4 marks):**

| Pathway | Mechanism | Example | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrochemical** | H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O + 2e⁻ | PEMFC, SOFC, AFC | Electricity + heat |
| **Thermochemical** | Combustion of H₂ in air | H₂-ICE, gas turbines | Mechanical work + heat |
| **Chemical** | H₂ as a reactant | Haber-Bosch (NH₃), methanol, DRI steel | Feedstock molecule |

**2. PEMFC vs SOFC Efficiency Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **PEMFC** | **SOFC** |
|---|---|---|
| Operating temperature | 60–90 °C | 600–1000 °C |
| Electrolyte | Polymer membrane | Yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) |
| Electrical efficiency | 50–60 % | 55–65 % |
| Startup time | Seconds (fast) | Hours (slow) |
| CHP total efficiency | 80–85 % | 85–90 % |
| Application | Vehicles, backup power | Grid, industrial CHP |

SOFC achieves higher electrical efficiency due to higher operating temperature allowing fuel flexibility and reduced activation losses. PEMFC, although slightly less efficient, is preferred for **mobile applications** due to its rapid start-up and low operating temperature.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A PEMFC stack has 300 cells in series. Each cell operates at 0.7 V under a current of 300 A. The stack consumes hydrogen at 0.001 kg/s. Calculate (i) the electrical power output and (ii) the stack efficiency relative to LHV.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 300$ cells
- $V_{cell} = 0.7$ V
- $I = 300$ A
- $\dot{m} = 0.001$ kg/s
- $LHV = 120 \text{ MJ/kg} = 120 \times 10^6$ J/kg

**Step 1 — Stack Voltage:**

$$V_{stack} = N \times V_{cell} = 300 \times 0.7 = 210 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Electrical Power Output:**

$$P_{elec} = V_{stack} \times I = 210 \times 300 = 63000 \text{ W} = 63 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Chemical Input Power:**

$$P_{chem} = \dot{m} \times LHV = 0.001 \times 120 \times 10^6 = 120000 \text{ W} = 120 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Stack Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{P_{elec}}{P_{chem}} = \frac{63}{120} = 0.525 = 52.5\%$$

**Answer:** The stack delivers **63 kW** of electrical power at an efficiency of **52.5 % (LHV basis)**. This is consistent with the typical operating efficiency of a PEMFC stack.

(Marks distribution: Stack voltage 1 mark, electrical power 2 marks, chemical input 2 marks, efficiency 2 marks.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones**
> - **Forgetting the compressibility factor Z** in real-gas hydrogen calculations. For 700 bar H₂, Z ≈ 1.4 — using Z = 1 gives ~40 % error in mass!
> - **Mixing up LHV (120 MJ/kg) and HHV (142 MJ/kg)**. PEMFC exhausts water as vapor, so **LHV is the correct basis**.
> - **Wrong unit for universal gas constant.** Use R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), NOT 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K) when using SI volumes in m³ and pressures in Pa.
> - **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in temperature-dependent equations — silent error that ruins answers.
> - **Using "filling time = 3 min" as a given** in a question that asks for fill time — calculate it from $\dot{m}$ and tank mass.
> - **Stating PEMFC efficiency as "60–70 %"** — that's AFC; PEMFC is **50–60 %**.
> - **Drawing the HRS block diagram without arrows** showing flow direction. Arrows = 1 mark; sequence = 1 mark; labels = 1 mark.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Dispensing** = controlled transfer of H₂ from station to vehicle at specified P, T, flow.
- ✅ **Two governing standards:** ISO 17268 (hardware) + SAE J2601 (protocol).
- ✅ **Pressure classes:** 350 bar (H35), 700 bar (H70), LH2 (cryogenic).
- ✅ **Pre-cooling to –40 °C** is mandatory for 700-bar fast-fill; it limits end-of-fill T to ≤ 85 °C.
- ✅ **Real gas equation** is essential: $m = \dfrac{PVM}{ZRT}$, with $Z \approx 1.4$ at 700 bar.
- ✅ **Adiabatic heating** without cooling would push tank temperature to ~1600 °C — physically destructive.
- ✅ **Three fueling states per SAE J2601:** State 1 (compressor-limited), State 2 (compressor+chiller-limited), State 3 (temperature-limited).
- ✅ **Chiller load ≈ 20–60 kW** for a typical 700-bar station.
- ✅ **Utilization pathways:** Electrochemical (PEMFC/SOFC/AFC), Thermochemical (H₂-ICE, GT), Chemical (NH₃, methanol, DRI).
- ✅ **PEMFC efficiency:** 50–60 % (electrical), up to 85 % (CHP).
- ✅ **SOFC efficiency:** 55–65 % (electrical), up to 90 % (CHP); high T = slow start.
- ✅ **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg = 33.33 kWh/kg.**
- ✅ **Hydrogen is sold by mass (kg), not volume.**
- ✅ **Type IV tanks** = polymer liner + CFRP overwrap; 700 bar max; temperature limit 85 °C.
- ✅ **Toyota Mirai tank:** 122 L, ~5 kg H₂ usable, ~600 km range.
- ✅ **AFC efficiency (60–70 %)** is the highest among low-T fuel cells; AFCs are used in space and niche transport applications.
- ✅ **Cascade storage** uses 3 banks (Low/Medium/High) to maintain fueling pressure as inventory depletes.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Production Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Production Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydrogen Production Plant** is an integrated industrial facility designed to convert a primary energy feedstock (hydrocarbons, water, biomass, coal, or renewable electricity) into molecular hydrogen (H₂) of specified purity, pressure, and flow rate, using a defined sequence of chemical reactors, separation units, purification systems, heat exchangers, and balance-of-plant (BOP) auxiliaries. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, hydrogen production plants are classified based on the **primary energy vector** and the **dominant conversion pathway**, namely:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification of Hydrogen Production Plants**
> 1. **Thermochemical Plants** – Steam Methane Reforming (SMR), Partial Oxidation (POX), Autothermal Reforming (ATR), Coal Gasification, Biomass Gasification.
> 2. **Electrochemical Plants** – Alkaline Electrolyser (AEL), Proton Exchange Membrane Electrolyser (PEMEL), Solid Oxide Electrolyser Cell (SOEC), Anion Exchange Membrane (AEMEL).
> 3. **Photolytic / Photoelectrochemical Plants** – Solar-driven water splitting.
> 4. **Hybrid Plants** – Coupling renewables (solar PV, wind) with electrolyser banks (Green Hydrogen pathway).

The **plant capacity** is conventionally expressed in **Nm³/h** (Normal cubic metres per hour) or **kg/day** of H₂ produced, with industrial mega-plants ranging from **1,000 Nm³/h** to over **100,000 Nm³/h** (~200 tonnes/day).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Hydrogen Kitchen" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Think of a Hydrogen Production Plant as a "Specialised Kitchen"**
> - The **feedstock** (water, methane, coal) is the raw ingredient.
> - The **reactor** is the oven — it provides the *energy* (heat, electricity, light) to break the molecules apart.
> - The **separator/purifier** is the strainer — it filters out the unwanted by-products (CO₂, CO, H₂S).
> - The **compressor/storage tank** is the serving container — it stores and dispenses pure H₂.
> - The **Balance of Plant (BOP)** is the kitchen's plumbing, electricity, ventilation, and safety systems.

A *steam methane reformer* is like cooking rice in a steam pot (heat + steam + catalyst releases H₂ from methane). An *electrolyser* is like using a battery to split water into its constituent gases — a pure electric "un-cooking" of H₂O. The choice of "kitchen" determines cost, purity, and carbon footprint.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Constants Used in Plant Design**
> - Faraday Constant: **F = 96,485 C/mol**
> - Molar mass of H₂: **M = 2.016 g/mol**
> - Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂: **141.86 MJ/kg** (≈ **39.4 kWh/kg**)
> - Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂: **119.96 MJ/kg** (≈ **33.33 kWh/kg**)
> - Thermoneutral voltage of water electrolysis: **E_th = 1.481 V** at 25 °C
> - Reversible (minimum) cell voltage: **E_rev = 1.229 V** at 25 °C
> - Standard reference conditions: **T = 298.15 K, P = 101.325 kPa**

---

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) Plant

The SMR plant is the **workhorse of global hydrogen production**, accounting for ~**76%** of dedicated H₂ manufacture worldwide. Its operation unfolds in four sequential stages:

1. **Feedstock Desulphurisation** — Natural gas (primarily CH₄) is passed over a **ZnO guard bed** to remove sulphur compounds (H₂S, COS) that would poison the downstream nickel catalyst.
2. **Primary Reforming Reactor** — Inside catalyst-filled tubes operating at **800–900 °C** and **20–30 bar**, the endothermic reaction occurs:
   CH₄ + H₂O ↔ CO + 3H₂     (ΔH = +206 kJ/mol)
3. **Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reactors** — Two-stage shift conversion (High-Temp Shift at 350 °C, Low-Temp Shift at 200 °C) maximises H₂ yield:
   CO + H₂O ↔ CO₂ + H₂     (ΔH = –41 kJ/mol)
4. **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** — Removes residual CO₂, CO, CH₄ to deliver H₂ of **99.99%+ purity**.

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown of an Electrolyser Plant

1. **Water Purification Unit** — De-ionised water with conductivity < **1 µS/cm** is supplied to maintain membrane integrity and avoid scaling.
2. **Electrolyser Stack** — DC power splits water at the electrodes. Three dominant technologies:
   - **Alkaline (AEL)** — Operates 60–80 °C, 30% KOH electrolyte, Ni electrodes, capital-mature.
   - **PEM (PEMEL)** — Operates 50–80 °C, acidic Nafion™ membrane, Pt/Ir catalysts, dynamic load-following.
   - **Solid Oxide (SOEC)** — Operates 700–850 °C, steam-fed, highest efficiency (~85% LHV), but thermal-cycling sensitive.
3. **Gas Drying & Compression** — H₂ is dried (to < –60 °C dew point) and compressed to storage pressure (350–700 bar for vehicles, 30–80 bar for pipelines).
4. **Rectifier / Power Conditioning** — Converts AC grid or renewable power to low-ripple DC at high current (1,000–10,000 A per stack).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.3 — High-Yield Formula Reference for Hydrogen Plant Calculations**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Mass of H₂ produced (Faraday's 1st Law) | m = (M · I · t) / (n · F) | grams | n = 2 for H₂ |
| 2 | Theoretical specific energy consumption (HHV) | SEC_HHV = (n · F · E_cell) / (M · 3.6) | kWh/kg | At E_cell = 1.481 V → 39.4 kWh/kg |
| 3 | Electrolyser plant efficiency (HHV) | η = (SEC_theoretical / SEC_actual) × 100 | % | PEM: 65–75%, AEL: 60–70%, SOEC: 75–85% |
| 4 | SMR thermal efficiency (HHV basis) | η_SMR = (m_H₂ · HHV_H₂) / (E_natural_gas + E_external_heat) | % | Typical: 70–85% |
| 5 | Carbon intensity of grey H₂ (SMR) | CI = (m_CO₂ / m_H₂) | kg CO₂ / kg H₂ | ~ 9–10 kg CO₂ / kg H₂ |
| 6 | Carbon intensity of green H₂ (renewable electrolysis) | CI = (emissions from electricity) | kg CO₂ / kg H₂ | Approaches zero if RE is dedicated |
| 7 | Stoichiometric H₂ yield from SMR | Y_H₂ = (4 · m_CH₄) / (1 · M_CH₄) = 4 mol H₂ / mol CH₄ | mol | Theoretical maximum |
| 8 | Compressor work (ideal, isentropic) | W_c = [γ/(γ–1)] · R · T₁ · [(P₂/P₁)^((γ–1)/γ) – 1] | J/kg | γ_H₂ ≈ 1.405 |
| 9 | Volume of H₂ from mass | V = m · V_m / M | Nm³ | V_m = 22.414 Nm³/kmol |

**Important Notation Note:** In markdown tables, use `\vert` instead of `|` to represent absolute-value bars inside cells to avoid syntax breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Relevance**
> Hydrogen production plants form the **upstream backbone** of the entire hydrogen value chain — ammonia synthesis (NH₃ uses ~70% of global H₂), petroleum hydrodesulphurisation, methanol production, direct-reduced iron (DRI) steelmaking, and the rapidly emerging **fuel-cell electric vehicle (FCEV)** ecosystem. Plant scale and choice of technology directly dictate the **levelised cost of hydrogen (LCOH)**, which in 2024–2026 ranges from **USD 1–2/kg** for grey SMR to **USD 3–6/kg** for green electrolyser-based H₂.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass of Hydrogen Produced in an Electrolyser

Starting from **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**, the mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total charge passed through the electrolyte.

**Given:**
- Current: I (Amperes)
- Time: t (seconds)
- Number of electrons per H₂ molecule: n = 2
- Faraday constant: F = 96,485 C/mol
- Molar mass of H₂: M = 2.016 g/mol
