**Step 1:** Total electric charge transferred is the product of current and time.

$$
Q = I \cdot t
$$

**Step 2:** Moles of electrons transferred equals total charge divided by Faraday's constant.

$$
n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}
$$

**Step 3:** Moles of H₂ produced equals moles of electrons divided by the electron count per molecule (n = 2).

$$
n_{H_2} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 4:** Mass of H₂ equals moles of H₂ multiplied by molar mass.

$$
m_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \cdot M = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 5 (Compact result):**

$$
\boxed{\,m_{H_2} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}\,}
$$

**Step 6 (Numerical Example):** A PEM electrolyser stack operates at **I = 2,000 A** for **t = 1 hour = 3,600 s**.

$$
m_{H_2} = \frac{2.016 \times 2000 \times 3600}{2 \times 96485}
$$

$$
m_{H_2} = \frac{14{,}515{,}200}{192{,}970} \approx 75.22 \text{ g H}_2
$$

**Step 7 — Conversion to volume at Normal conditions** (V_m = 22.414 Nm³/kmol, M = 2.016 kg/kmol):

$$
V_{H_2} = \frac{m_{H_2}}{M} \cdot V_m = \frac{75.22 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}} \times 22.414 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Nm}^3/\text{mol}
$$

$$
V_{H_2} = 37.31 \text{ mol} \times 0.022414 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{mol} \approx 0.836 \text{ Nm}^3
$$

**Step 8 — Specific energy consumption check** at a cell voltage of 2.0 V:

$$
SEC = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot E_{cell}}{M \cdot 3600} = \frac{2 \times 96485 \times 2.0}{2.016 \times 3600} \approx 53.17 \text{ kWh/kg}
$$

This is consistent with state-of-the-art PEM electrolysers operating at ~2.0 V.

## 3.2 Derivation: SMR Stoichiometric H₂ Yield and CO₂ Emissions

**Step 1 — Primary reforming reaction:**

$$
CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2
$$

**Step 2 — Water-gas shift reaction:**

$$
CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2
$$

**Step 3 — Net overall reaction** (adding Step 1 and Step 2):

$$
CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical H₂ yield per kmol of CH₄:**

$$
Y_{H_2} = 4 \text{ kmol H}_2 \text{ per kmol CH}_4 = \frac{4 \times 2.016}{16.04} \approx 0.5027 \text{ kg H}_2 \text{ per kg CH}_4
$$

**Step 5 — CO₂ emitted per kg of H₂ (carbon balance, 1 kmol CH₄ basis):**

$$
\frac{44 \text{ kg CO}_2}{4 \times 2.016 \text{ kg H}_2} = \frac{44}{8.064} \approx 5.46 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg H}_2
$$

**Step 6 — Realistic value** accounting for process heating, furnace fuel, and utility steam (typically 1.6–1.8 kmol CH₄ combusted externally for every 1 kmol reformed):

$$
CI_{grey,realistic} \approx 9 \text{ to } 10 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg H}_2
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.3 — Plant Technology Comparison (Aligned with KTU 2024 Outcomes)**

| Parameter | SMR (Grey) | PEM Electrolysis (Green) | Alkaline Electrolysis | Coal Gasification (Brown) |
|-----------|------------|--------------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Feedstock | Natural gas + steam | Water + RE electricity | Water + grid/RE power | Coal + steam + O₂ |
| Reaction T (°C) | 800–900 | 50–80 | 60–80 | 900–1300 |
| H₂ purity (%) | 99.99 (after PSA) | 99.999 | 99.5–99.9 | 95–99 |
| Plant efficiency (HHV) | 70–85% | 65–75% | 60–70% | 50–60% |
| CO₂ footprint (kg/kg H₂) | ~9–10 | < 0.5 (with RE) | ~0.5–1 (with RE) | ~18–20 |
| CapEx (USD/kW) | 500–900 | 800–1,500 | 500–900 | 1,200–2,000 |
| Cold-start time | Hours | Seconds–minutes | 5–30 min | Hours |
| Suitability with renewables | Poor | Excellent | Moderate | Poor |

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NG[Natural Gas Feed] --> DES[Desulphurisation ZnO Bed]
    DES --> PR[Primary Reformer 800 to 900 C]
    STM[Superheated Steam] --> PR
    PR --> HTS[High Temp Shift Reactor 350 C]
    HTS --> LTS[Low Temp Shift Reactor 200 C]
    LTS --> PSA[Pressure Swing Adsorption]
    PSA --> H2P[Pure H2 Product 99.99 percent]
    PSA --> CO2[CO2 Byproduct]
    PR --> FLU[Reformer Flue Gas]
    FLU --> ECON[Heat Recovery Steam Generator]
    ECON --> STM
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Green Hydrogen Electrolyser Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POWER[Renewable Power Source]
        SOL[PV Array or Wind Farm]
        SOL --> INV[AC DC Rectifier]
    end
    subgraph PLANT[Electrolyser Plant]
        INV --> STK[Electrolyser Stack Array]
        WTR[Deionised Water Unit] --> STK
        STK --> SEP[Gas Liquid Separator]
        SEP --> DRY[H2 Drying Unit]
        DRY --> CMP[H2 Compressor]
   
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Storage and Delivery]
        CMP --> TANK[High Pressure Storage 350 to 700 bar]
        TANK --> DSK[Dispenser for Mobility or Pipeline]
    end
```

## 4.3 Comparative Plant Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrogen Demand Identified] --> B{Renewable Electricity Available?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Power Profile Stable?}
    C -- Variable --> D[PEM Electrolyser Plant]
    C -- Stable --> E[Alkaline or SOEC Plant]
    B -- No --> F{Is Carbon Capture Feasible?}
    F -- Yes --> G[SMR with CCS Blue Hydrogen]
    F -- No --> H{Feedstock Coal Available?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Coal Gasification with CCUS]
    H -- No --> J[Biomass Gasification Plant]
```

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List the four main stages of a Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) plant and state the operating temperature of the primary reformer."**

**Model Answer:**
The four main stages are:
1. **Feedstock desulphurisation** using a ZnO guard bed (to protect Ni catalyst from sulphur poisoning).
2. **Primary reforming** in catalyst-filled tubes at **800–900 °C** and 20–30 bar.
3. **Water-gas shift (WGS) conversion** in two stages — high-temperature (350 °C) and low-temperature (200 °C) — to maximise H₂ yield from CO.
4. **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** for purification to **99.99% H₂ purity**.

The primary reformer operates at **800–900 °C**.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define 'Green Hydrogen' and state the typical specific energy consumption (SEC) of a state-of-the-art PEM electrolyser plant in kWh/kg."**

**Model Answer:**
**Green Hydrogen** is hydrogen produced by electrolysis of water using electricity derived exclusively from **renewable energy sources** (solar PV, wind, hydro, geothermal), resulting in near-zero well-to-gate CO₂ emissions (typically < 0.5 kg CO₂ / kg H₂).
The typical specific energy consumption of a state-of-the-art PEM electrolyser plant is **50–55 kWh/kg H₂** (HHV basis), compared to the thermodynamic minimum of **39.4 kWh/kg**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) plant** for hydrogen production. Discuss the role of the **water-gas shift reactor** and the **PSA unit**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PEM electrolyser operates at an average cell voltage of **2.0 V** and a current of **2,500 A** for **8 hours**. Calculate (i) the mass of H₂ produced, (ii) the volume of H₂ at NTP, and (iii) the specific energy consumption (SEC) in kWh/kg. Compare it with the theoretical minimum. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) SMR Plant Working:**
- **Feedstock Preparation:** Natural gas is first desulphurised to < 50 ppb sulphur using a **ZnO bed** to prevent catalyst poisoning.
- **Primary Reformer:** CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ at **800–900 °C**, 20–30 bar, on a Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst. This endothermic reaction is fired in a top-fired or side-fired furnace.
- **Water-Gas Shift Reactor:** Converts the CO produced into additional H₂ and CO₂:
   CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂
   This is done in **two stages** (HTS at 350 °C using Fe/Cr catalyst; LTS at 200 °C using Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst) to maximise conversion and minimise residual CO to < 0.4%.
- **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA):** The shifted gas is passed through beds of activated carbon, zeolite, and alumina at high pressure. Selective adsorption of CO₂, CO, CH₄, N₂ gives **99.99% pure H₂**.
- **Heat Integration:** Reformer flue gas is used to generate superheated steam, preheat feed, and reboil amine regeneration, achieving > 90% thermal integration efficiency.
- **Block diagram** (refer Section 4.1 above).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** E_cell = 2.0 V; I = 2,500 A; t = 8 h = 28,800 s; n = 2; F = 96,485 C/mol; M = 2.016 g/mol.

**(i) Mass of H₂:**

$$
m_{H_2} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{2.016 \times 2500 \times 28800}{2 \times 96485}
$$

$$
m_{H_2} = \frac{145{,}152{,}000}{192{,}970} \approx 752.07 \text{ g} = 0.752 \text{ kg H}_2
$$

**(ii) Volume at NTP** (V_m = 22.414 L/mol at NTP):

$$
n_{H_2} = \frac{0.752}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} = 373.0 \text{ mol}
$$

$$
V_{H_2} = 373.0 \times 22.414 \times 10^{-3} \approx 8.36 \text{ Nm}^3
$$

**(iii) Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$
SEC = \frac{E_{cell} \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot 3600} = \frac{2.0 \times 2 \times 96485}{2.016 \times 3600}
$$

$$
SEC \approx 53.17 \text{ kWh/kg H}_2
$$

**Theoretical minimum (E_th = 1.481 V):**

$$
SEC_{min} = \frac{1.481 \times 2 \times 96485}{2.016 \times 3600} \approx 39.4 \text{ kWh/kg H}_2
$$

**Comparison:** Plant SEC of 53.17 kWh/kg is **~34.9% higher** than the theoretical minimum — corresponding to a plant efficiency of 39.4 / 53.17 ≈ **74.1% (HHV)**, which is realistic for state-of-the-art PEM electrolysers.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2025 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare **Alkaline, PEM, and Solid Oxide** electrolyser technologies with respect to operating temperature, electrolyte, efficiency, and dynamic load-following capability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SMR plant consumes **500 Nm³/h** of natural gas (assume 100% CH₄) at standard conditions. Using the overall reaction CH₄ + 2H₂O → CO₂ + 4H₂, calculate (i) the volumetric flow rate of H₂ produced, (ii) the mass flow rate of CO₂ emitted, and (iii) the carbon intensity in kg CO₂ per kg H₂. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Alkaline (AEL) | PEM (PEMEL) | Solid Oxide (SOEC) |
|---------|----------------|-------------|--------------------|
| Operating T | 60–80 °C | 50–80 °C | 700–850 °C |
| Electrolyte | 25–30% KOH liquid | Nafion™ polymer membrane | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilised Zirconia) ceramic |
| Catalyst | Ni / Ni-Mo | Pt (cathode), IrO₂ (anode) | Ni-YSZ cermet / LSM |
| Efficiency (HHV) | 60–70% | 65–75% | 75–85% |
| Load-following | Slow (5–30 min) | Fast (seconds) | Poor (thermal stress) |
| CapEx (USD/kW) | 500–900 | 800–1,500 | 1,500–3,000 |
| Maturity | Commercial (large scale) | Commercial (rapid scale-up) | Pilot / Demonstration |

PEM is best for **dynamic** renewable input; SOEC offers **highest efficiency** but needs steady-state heat; AEL is **lowest CapEx** and most mature.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** V_CH₄ = 500 Nm³/h, pure CH₄. At NTP, V_m = 22.414 Nm³/kmol.

**(i) Molar flow rate of CH₄:**

$$
\dot{n}_{CH_4} = \frac{500}{22.414} = 22.31 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Stoichiometry:** 4 mol H₂ per 1 mol CH₄.

$$
\dot{n}_{H_2} = 4 \times 22.31 = 89.24 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{H_2} = 89.24 \times 22.414 \approx 2{,}000.5 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

**(ii) Mass flow rate of CO₂** (1 mol CO₂ per mol CH₄; M_CO₂ = 44 kg/kmol):

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2} = 22.31 \times 44 = 981.6 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**(iii) Carbon Intensity:**

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = 89.24 \times 2.016 = 179.9 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
CI = \frac{\dot{m}_{CO_2}}{\dot{m}_{H_2}} = \frac{981.6}{179.9} \approx 5.46 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg H}_2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to multiply by **4** in the H₂ yield from the *overall* SMR reaction — students often wrongly use the *primary reformer* stoichiometry (3 H₂ per CH₄) and miss the WGS contribution.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **HHV and LHV** of hydrogen (141.86 vs 119.96 MJ/kg) — always verify which basis the question requires.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Reporting SEC in **J/kg** instead of **kWh/kg** — KTU expects kWh/kg for unit consistency with grid electricity tariffs.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Neglecting the **realistic 9–10 kg CO₂/kg H₂** for grey SMR — the *theoretical 5.46 kg/kg* is stoichiometric only; actual values are higher due to combustion of external fuel for reformer heat. **Both values are valid answers**, but examiners expect the realistic value to be *mentioned* in descriptive answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hydrogen Production Plants**
> - ☐ **SMR dominates** global H₂ production (~76%); operates at **800–900 °C** with Ni catalyst; produces **grey hydrogen** with ~**9–10 kg CO₂/kg H₂**.
> - ☐ **Overall SMR reaction:** CH₄ + 2H₂O → CO₂ + 4H₂ (stoichiometric yield = 4 mol H₂ per mol CH₄).
> - ☐ **Electrolysis** splits water using electricity; **Faraday's Law:** m = (M·I·t) / (n·F) is the foundational equation.
> - ☐ **Thermodynamic minimum SEC** of electrolysis (HHV basis) = **39.4 kWh/kg** at 1.481 V; reversible limit = 1.229 V.
> - ☐ **PEM electrolysers** = 50–55 kWh/kg (best for renewables); **Alkaline** = 53–60 kWh/kg (lowest CapEx); **SOEC** = 45–50 kWh/kg (highest efficiency, T > 700 °C).
> - ☐ **Green hydrogen** CO₂ intensity < 0.5 kg/kg H₂ when paired with dedicated renewable electricity.
> - ☐ **PSA** delivers 99.99% H₂ purity in SMR plants; H₂ drying requires **< –60 °C dew point** for PEM fuel-cell grade.
> - ☐ **Compressor work** uses the isentropic relation: W_c = [γ/(γ–1)] · R · T₁ · [(P₂/P₁)^((γ–1)/γ) – 1].
> - ☐ **Hydrogen colour codes:** Grey (SMR), Blue (SMR + CCS), Green (renewable electrolysis), Pink (nuclear electrolysis), Brown (coal gasification), Turquoise (methane pyrolysis).
> - ☐ **KTU 2024 focus areas:** SMR process flow, Faraday's law derivations, electrolyser comparison tables, and carbon-intensity calculations.
> - ☐ **Always state assumptions:** Isothermal vs adiabatic, HHV vs LHV, theoretical vs realistic yield.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Process - Steam Reformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Process - Steam Reformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Steam Reforming (SR)** is a **thermocatalytic endothermic process** in which light hydrocarbons (primarily methane, CH₄) react with high-temperature steam (H₂O) over a nickel-based catalyst at **700 °C – 850 °C** and **3 – 25 bar** to produce a synthesis gas (syngas) mixture rich in hydrogen (H₂), carbon monoxide (CO), and carbon dioxide (CO₂). The CO produced is subsequently converted to additional H₂ via the **Water-Gas Shift (WGS)** reaction. It is the **most industrially mature and economically dominant** route for hydrogen production, accounting for **~48 % of global dedicated hydrogen production** and **~95 % of hydrogen produced in the United States**.

The overall stoichiometry after both stages is:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2 \qquad \Delta H_{298\text{K}} = +165\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

This produces **"Grey Hydrogen"** when the CO₂ vented is un-captured, **"Blue Hydrogen"** when CO₂ is captured via Carbon Capture, Utilisation & Storage (CCUS), and is the foundational chemistry of **"Turquoise Hydrogen"** when paired with methane pyrolysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE] **Plain-English Analogy**
> Think of steam reforming like **boiling water in a pressure cooker to "split" a hydrocarbon fuel into hydrogen**. Imagine a sausage (methane, CH₄) made of carbon and hydrogen atoms bound tightly together. You place this sausage in a very hot furnace (~800 °C — hotter than lava) along with superheated steam (H₂O molecules). A nickel-based "magic knife" (catalyst) gently cuts the bonds. The hydrogen atoms escape the carbon and pair up with hydrogen from the steam, forming H₂ gas, while the carbon pairs with oxygen to make CO and CO₂. To squeeze out *even more* hydrogen, you take the leftover CO and pass it again with steam — this time it grabs another oxygen to become CO₂, freeing yet another H₂. The final product is essentially clean hydrogen fuel, ready to be purified and pressurised.

### 1.3 Salient Process Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant Data)

| Parameter | Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **700 – 850 °C** |
| Operating Pressure | **3 – 25 bar** |
| Steam-to-Carbon Ratio (S/C) | **2.5 – 3.5** |
| Primary Catalyst | **Ni / Al₂O₃ – MgO** |
| Thermal Efficiency | **70 – 85 %** |
| Global H₂ Share | **~48 %** (dominant route) |
| CO₂ Emission (Grey H₂) | **~9 – 10 kg CO₂ / kg H₂** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two-Stage Reaction Mechanism

The Steam Reforming process is fundamentally a **two-step tandem reaction sequence**, both governed by heterogeneous catalysis on porous nickel pellets.

#### **Stage 1 — Steam Reforming Reaction (SRR)**

This is the primary, highly endothermic reaction occurring inside the radiant-fired **Primary Reformer (PFR-type tubular reactor)**:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2 \qquad \Delta H_{298\text{K}}^{o} = +206.2\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Mechanistic Steps (Surface Chemistry on Ni):**
1. **Adsorption:** CH₄ and H₂O adsorb dissociatively onto active Ni sites (Ni⁰).
2. **C–H Bond Cleavage:** Sequential dehydrogenation of CH₄ → CH₃* → CH₂* → CH* → C*.
3. **OH Migration:** Surface hydroxyls (OH*) attack surface carbon (C*) → CO*.
4. **Desorption:** CO and H₂ desorb into the bulk gas phase.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why this step is endothermic:** Breaking four C–H bonds in CH₄ and one O–H bond in H₂O requires substantial energy (~206 kJ/mol). The heat is supplied by burning a portion of the feedstock (natural gas) in the reformer furnace.

#### **Stage 2 — Water-Gas Shift Reaction (WGSR)**

The CO-rich syngas then enters **two adiabatic shift reactors** (High-Temperature Shift + Low-Temperature Shift):

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \qquad \Delta H_{298\text{K}}^{o} = -41.2\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

The WGS reaction is **mildly exothermic**, **equal-molar** (Δn = 0), and therefore **pressure-insensitive** but **temperature-sensitive** — favoring lower temperatures thermodynamically (which is why it is split into HT + LT stages).

#### **Overall Reaction (Net Process)**

Adding SRR + 3 × WGSR:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2 \qquad \Delta H_{298\text{K}}^{o} = +165\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

### 2.2 Thermodynamic Equilibrium Analysis (Le Chatelier Application)

Applying **Le Chatelier's Principle** to the Steam Reforming reaction:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2$$

- **Temperature:** Endothermic (ΔH > 0) → **High T favors forward (H₂) production.** ✅
- **Pressure:** Δn_gas = +2 (4 moles products – 2 moles reactants) → **Low P favors forward.** ✅
- **Concentration:** Excess steam shifts equilibrium right → Higher S/C ratio improves CH₄ conversion. ✅

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] All formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE Module-2 questions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description & Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2$ | Primary reforming reaction, ΔH = **+206.2 kJ/mol** |
| 2 | $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ | Water-gas shift, ΔH = **−41.2 kJ/mol** |
| 3 | $\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2$ | Overall reaction, ΔH = **+165 kJ/mol** |
| 4 | $K_p^{\text{SR}} = \dfrac{p_{\text{CO}} \cdot p_{\text{H}_2}^{3}}{p_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}$ | Equilibrium constant of primary reformer |
| 5 | $K_p^{\text{WGS}} = \dfrac{p_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot p_{\text{H}_2}}{p_{\text{CO}} \cdot p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}$ | Equilibrium constant of WGS reactor |
| 6 | $\ln K_p = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{o}}{RT}$ | Gibbs–Helmholtz relation, **R = 8.314 J/mol·K** |
| 7 | $\dfrac{d(\ln K_p)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{o}}{RT^{2}}$ | van 't Hoff equation (temperature sensitivity) |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\text{S}}{\text{C}} = \dfrac{\text{moles of H}_2\text{O}_{\text{in}}}{\text{moles of C}_{\text{in}}}$ | Steam-to-Carbon molar ratio (**2.5 – 3.5**) |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{\text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2} \times \dot{n}_{\text{H}_2}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{feed}} \times \dot{n}_{\text{feed}} + Q_{\text{external}}}$ | Thermal efficiency (**70 – 85 %**) |
| 10 | $E_{\text{CO}_2} = 9 - 10\ \dfrac{\text{kg CO}_2}{\text{kg H}_2}$ | Grey-H₂ carbon intensity |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Hydrogen:** SMR provides the bulk of H₂ used in hydrodesulfurization (HDS) and hydrocracking of crude oil.
- **Ammonia Synthesis:** H₂ from SMR is reacted with N₂ (Haber–Bosch) to produce NH₃ fertiliser.
- **Methanol Production:** CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH synthesis uses SMR-derived syngas.
- **Fuel Cells (PEMFC):** Blue H₂ from SMR + CCUS is the bridge fuel for hard-to-abate sectors (steel, shipping).
- **Green Hydrogen Benchmark:** SMR cost (**$1–2/kg H₂**) sets the cost benchmark that electrolysis must beat.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the van 't Hoff Equation for the Steam Reforming Reaction

The **van 't Hoff equation** is the cornerstone thermodynamic relation that quantifies how the reforming equilibrium constant depends on temperature — and is a guaranteed **KTU Part-B (14-mark) derivation**.

**Starting Point — Fundamental Thermodynamic Identity:**

At any temperature T, the change in the standard Gibbs free energy relates to the equilibrium constant by:

$$\Delta G^{o} = -RT \ln K_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln K_p = -\frac{\Delta G^{o}}{RT}$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate both sides with respect to temperature T (at constant pressure):

$$\frac{d(\ln K_p)}{dT} = -\frac{1}{R} \cdot \frac{d}{dT}\left(\frac{\Delta G^{o}}{T}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Quotient Rule** of differentiation to the right-hand term $\frac{\Delta G^{o}}{T}$:

$$\frac{d}{dT}\left(\frac{\Delta G^{o}}{T}\right) = \frac{T \cdot \frac{d(\Delta G^{o})}{dT} - \Delta G^{o} \cdot 1}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation**, which states that:

$$\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G^{o}/T)}{\partial T}\right)_p = -\frac{\Delta H^{o}}{T^{2}}$$

Substituting into Step 2 yields:

$$\frac{d}{dT}\left(\frac{\Delta G^{o}}{T}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{o}}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Step 3 back into Step 1:

$$\frac{d(\ln K_p)}{dT} = -\frac{1}{R} \cdot \left(-\frac{\Delta H^{o}}{T^{2}}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^{o}}{RT^{2}}$$

**Final Result — van 't Hoff Equation:**

$$\boxed{\frac{d(\ln K_p)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{o}}{RT^{2}}}$$

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** This differential equation tells us that for an **endothermic** reforming reaction (ΔH° > 0), the derivative $\frac{d(\ln K_p)}{dT}$ is **positive**, meaning $K_p$ — and hence hydrogen yield — **increases with temperature**. This is precisely why industrial reformers operate at **800 °C+** to maximise methane conversion.

### 3.2 Integrated Form of van 't Hoff (Constant ΔH° Assumption)

If ΔH° is assumed constant over the temperature range [T₁, T₂], the differential form integrates to the **integrated van 't Hoff equation**:

$$\ln\left(\frac{K_{p_2}}{K_{p_1}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{o}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Application to Steam Reforming:**

At T₁ = 298 K (standard conditions), K_{p₁} ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻²⁸ (very small — reaction negligible at room temp).
At T₂ = 1100 K (reformer outlet), determine K_{p₂}:

$$\ln\left(\frac{K_{p_2}}{1.2 \times 10^{-28}}\right) = -\frac{206200}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{1100} - \frac{1}{298}\right)$$

**Compute bracket term:**

$$\frac{1}{1100} - \frac{1}{298} = 9.09 \times 10^{-4} - 3.356 \times 10^{-3} = -2.447 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Compute coefficient:**

$$-\frac{206200}{8.314} = -24802\ \text{K}$$

**Compute product (exponent):**

$$-24802 \times (-2.447 \times 10^{-3}) = +60.69$$

**Solve for K_{p₂}:**

$$\frac{K_{p_2}}{1.2 \times 10^{-28}} = e^{60.69} \approx 2.30 \times 10^{26}$$

$$K_{p_2} = 1.2 \times 10^{-28} \times 2.30 \times 10^{26} \approx 2.76 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** At 1100 K, K_p grows by ~**28 orders of magnitude** compared to 298 K. This huge shift is exactly why SMR reactors must operate in the **800 – 1100 K range** to achieve industrially viable conversion (>85 %).

### 3.3 Hydrogen Yield Calculation (Stoichiometric Basis)

For 1 kmol of CH₄ fed with S/C = 3.0 (i.e., 3 kmol H₂O):

**Extent of reaction (ξ) for SRR:**

| Species | Initial (kmol) | Change | Equilibrium |
|---|---|---|---|
| CH₄ | 1 | −ξ | 1 − ξ |
| H₂O | 3 | −ξ | 3 − ξ |
| CO | 0 | +ξ | ξ |
| H₂ | 0 | +3ξ | 3ξ |

Total moles = (1 − ξ) + (3 − ξ) + ξ + 3ξ = 4 + 2ξ

At equilibrium, with $K_p = 2.76 \times 10^{-2}$ at 1100 K and P = 15 bar, the mole fractions yield a quadratic in ξ that solves to **ξ ≈ 0.92**, giving **92 % methane conversion** — matching industrial reformer data.

$$\text{H}_2 \text{ produced} = 3\xi + (\text{WGS conversion}) \approx 3.5\ \text{kmol per kmol CH}_4 \rightarrow \sim 7\ \text{kg H}_2 \text{ per kmol CH}_4$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FEEDSTOCK NATURAL GAS CH4"] --> B["DESULFURIZER H2S REMOVAL ZnO BED"]
    B --> C["PRIMARY REFORMER TUBULAR 800C Ni CATALYST"]
    D["SUPERHEATED STEAM 900C 35 BAR"] --> C
    E["REFORMER FURNACE NATURAL GAS FIRED"] --> C
    C --> F["SYNGAS CO H2 CO2 H2O"]
    F --> G["HT SHIFT REACTOR 350C Fe3O4 Cr2O3"]
    G --> H["LT SHIFT REACTOR 200C Cu ZnO Al2O3"]
    H --> I["CO2 REMOVAL MEA ABSORBER OR PSA"]
    I --> J["METHANATOR CO CO2 REMOVAL"]
    J --> K["PURE HYDROGEN H2 99.99 PERCENT"]
    I --> L["CO2 STREAM CCUS READY"]
    K --> M["H2 COMPRESSOR 200 BAR STORAGE"]
    M --> N["END USER AMMONIA REFINERY FUEL CELL"]
```

### 4.2 Process Stage Description

| Stage Block | Functional Purpose | Operating Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| **FEEDSTOCK** | Natural gas or naphtha as CH₄ source | Pipeline pressure |
| **DESULFURIZER** | Remove H₂S to <0.1 ppm (prevents Ni poisoning) | 350 °C, ZnO bed |
| **PRIMARY REFORMER** | Endothermic CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ | 700 – 850 °C, 3 – 25 bar, Ni/Al₂O₃ |
| **FURNACE** | External radiant heat supply | Natural gas-fired burners |
| **HT SHIFT** | Bulk CO conversion (HT favorable for kinetics) | 320 – 360 °C, Fe₃O₄–Cr₂O₃ |
| **LT SHIFT** | Residual CO polishing (LT favorable for equilibrium) | 200 – 250 °C, Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ |
| **CO₂ REMOVAL** | Separation (MEA amine, Selexol, or PSA) | 30 – 50 °C |
| **METHANATOR** | Trace CO/CO₂ → CH₄ (avoiding PEMFC poisoning) | 300 °C, Ni catalyst |
| **PURE H₂** | Final product specification | 99.99 % purity |

### 4.3 Energy Integration Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOT["HOT STREAM SUPPLY"]
        H1["Reformer Furnace 850C"]
    end
    subgraph COLD["COLD STREAM DEMAND"]
        C1["Steam Generation 250C"]
        C2["Feedstock Preheating 400C"]
        C3["Process Water 100C"]
    end
    H1 --> C1
    H1 --> C2
    H1 --> C3
```

> **Engineering Note:** Modern SMR plants achieve **70 – 85 % thermal efficiency** primarily through extensive **Heat Recovery Steam Generation (HRSG)** — capturing the reformer flue-gas heat to preheat feedstock, generate process steam, and superheat the reformer steam input. This is a critical point for KTU questions on plant optimisation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define steam reforming of methane. Write the stoichiometric equation with enthalpy change.**

**Model Answer:**
Steam reforming of methane (SRM) is a high-temperature, endothermic thermocatalytic process in which methane reacts with steam in the presence of a nickel catalyst to produce syngas (CO + H₂). The primary reaction is:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2 \qquad \Delta H^{o}_{298\text{K}} = +206.2\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

It is industrially followed by the exothermic water-gas shift reaction (CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂) to maximise H₂ yield.

---

**Q2. Why is the steam-to-carbon (S/C) ratio maintained above 2.5 in industrial reformers?**

**Model Answer:**
A high S/C ratio (2.5–3.5) is maintained for three reasons: **(i)** it shifts the reforming equilibrium to the right (Le Chatelier), improving CH₄ conversion (>85 %); **(ii)** it prevents carbon deposition (coking) on the Ni catalyst via the Boudouard reaction suppression; and **(iii)** it provides the steam needed for the downstream WGS reaction. However, excessively high S/C (>4) wastes energy in steam superheating.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, explain the **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** process for industrial hydrogen production. Label the temperature, pressure, and catalyst at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The SMR process for H₂ production consists of **five sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Desulfurisation:** Natural gas (CH₄ source) is passed through a **ZnO fixed-bed adsorber** at 350 – 400 °C to remove H₂S to <0.1 ppm. Sulphur compounds permanently poison the downstream Ni catalyst, making this step critical.

**Stage 2 — Primary Reforming:** The desulfurised feedstock is mixed with superheated steam (S/C = 2.5 – 3.5) and fed into a **tubular fired reformer** operating at **800 – 850 °C** and **15 – 25 bar** over a **Ni/Al₂O₃–MgO** catalyst. The endothermic reaction CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ occurs, achieving 85 – 92 % CH₄ conversion. External heat is supplied by burning natural gas in the radiant section of the furnace.

**Stage 3 — Water-Gas Shift (WGS):** The CO-rich syngas is cooled and passed through two shift reactors. The **High-Temperature Shift (HTS)** at 320 – 360 °C over Fe₃O₄–Cr₂O₃ converts bulk CO, followed by the **Low-Temperature Shift (LTS)** at 200 – 250 °C over Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ to push equilibrium to >99 % CO conversion.

**Stage 4 — CO₂ Removal:** CO₂ is captured by **chemical absorption** (monoethanolamine, MEA, or activated MDEA) or **pressure swing adsorption (PSA)**, yielding a H₂-rich stream (95 – 98 %).

**Stage 5 — Methanation & Purification:** Residual CO/CO₂ (<1 %) is converted back to CH₄ over a Ni catalyst at 300 °C, producing **>99.99 % pure H₂** suitable for PEM fuel cells or ammonia synthesis.

| Stage | T (°C) | P (bar) | Catalyst |
|---|---|---|---|
| Desulfuriser | 350 | 25 | ZnO |
| Primary Reformer | 800 | 15 | Ni/Al₂O₃ |
| HTS Reactor | 350 | 15 | Fe₃O₄/Cr₂O₃ |
| LTS Reactor | 220 | 15 | Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ |
| Methanator | 300 | 15 | Ni |

**(b)** Explain the **thermodynamic and kinetic factors** governing the steam reforming reaction. Apply the **van 't Hoff equation** to justify the choice of reformer temperature. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The net steam reforming reaction is endothermic and produces a net increase in gas moles:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2 \qquad \Delta H^{o} = +206\ \text{kJ/mol},\ \Delta n = +2$$

**Thermodynamic Factors (Equilibrium):** By Le Chatelier's Principle, high temperature (ΔH > 0) and low pressure (Δn > 0) favour hydrogen production. The van 't Hoff equation gives:

$$\frac{d(\ln K_p)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{o}}{RT^{2}} > 0$$

Since ΔH° > 0, $K_p$ **increases monotonically** with temperature. At 298 K, $K_p \approx 10^{-28}$ (negligible conversion); at 1100 K, $K_p \approx 2.76 \times 10^{-2}$ (substantial conversion). This 28-order-of-magnitude shift in equilibrium constant justifies the **800 – 850 °C operating temperature** in industrial reformers.

**Kinetic Factors (Rate):** The reforming reaction is surface-reaction-controlled on Ni. The intrinsic rate follows a **power-law or Langmuir–Hinshelwood form**:

$$r = \frac{k \cdot p_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{(1 + K_{\text{CO}} p_{\text{CO}} + K_{\text{H}_2} p_{\text{H}_2})^2}$$

Arrhenius temperature dependence ($k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$, E_a ≈ 100 – 240 kJ/mol) also dictates high temperature. Thus the **800 – 850 °C window** represents the engineering optimum — high enough for favourable thermodynamics and kinetics, but below the metallurgical limit (~950 °C) of reformer tube alloys (e.g., HK40, HP40).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the **van 't Hoff equation** starting from the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation. Show its integrated form and compute the equilibrium constant Kp at **1100 K** for steam reforming, given Kp = 1.2 × 10⁻²⁸ at 298 K and ΔH° = +206.2 kJ/mol. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The derivation proceeds as detailed in Section 3.1 above, arriving at:

$$\boxed{\frac{d(\ln K_p)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{o}}{RT^{2}}}$$

Integrating (assuming constant ΔH°):

$$\ln\left(\frac{K_{p_2}}{K_{p_1}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{o}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Numerical Computation:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{K_{p_2}}{1.2 \times 10^{-28}}\right) = -\frac{206200}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{1100} - \frac{1}{298}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{1100} - \frac{1}{298} = -2.447 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\text{RHS} = -24802 \times (-2.447 \times 10^{-3}) = +60.69$$

$$K_{p_2} = 1.2 \times 10^{-28} \times e^{60.69} = 2.76 \times 10^{-2}$$

**(b)** Discuss **Grey, Blue, and Green hydrogen** in the context of steam reforming and electrolysis. Why is SMR still preferred despite high CO₂ emissions? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Category | Production Route | CO₂ Emission |
|---|---|---|
| **Grey H₂** | SMR without CCUS | ~9 – 10 kg CO₂/kg H₂ |
| **Blue H₂** | SMR + CCUS (95 % CO₂ capture) | ~0.5 – 1.5 kg CO₂/kg H₂ |
| **Green H₂** | Water electrolysis (renewable electricity) | ~0 kg CO₂/kg H₂ |
| **Turquoise H₂** | Methane pyrolysis (solid carbon) | ~0.5 kg CO₂/kg H₂ |

**Why SMR still dominates globally:**
1. **Mature technology** — over 80 years of industrial operation; predictable scale-up.
2. **Lowest current cost** — $1 – 2/kg H₂ vs $3 – 6/kg for electrolysis.
3. **High plant capacity** — single SMR trains can produce 100,000 – 250,000 Nm³ H₂/day.
4. **Established infrastructure** — pipelines, storage, and end-users (refineries, ammonia plants) already exist.
5. **Energy density of feedstock** — natural gas has high volumetric energy, requiring smaller reactor volumes than renewable-electricity-based electrolysers of equivalent throughput.

The future trajectory is shifting toward **Blue H₂ (SMR + CCUS)** as a transition technology and **Green H₂** as the long-term sustainable goal, especially with declining renewable electricity costs.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Marks lost** for omitting the **water-gas shift reaction** — it is integral to total H₂ yield.
> - Marks lost for confusing **WGS catalyst** (Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃) with **reformer catalyst** (Ni/Al₂O₃).
> - Forgetting the **Le Chatelier justification** for high T / low P is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> - Numerical errors in van 't Hoff: forgetting to convert ΔH° to **J/mol** before substitution (use **206200 J/mol**, not 206.2).
> - Not stating that WGS is **pressure-insensitive** because Δn = 0.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Steam Reforming** = CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (ΔH = +206 kJ/mol, **endothermic**).
- **Water-Gas Shift** = CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ (ΔH = −41 kJ/mol, **exothermic**).
- **Overall:** CH₄ + 2H₂O → CO₂ + 4H₂ (ΔH = +165 kJ/mol).
- **Operating Conditions:** 700 – 850 °C, 3 – 25 bar, S/C = 2.5 – 3.5, Ni catalyst.
- **Two-Stage WGS:** HTS (350 °C, Fe₃O₄) → LTS (220 °C, Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃) for >99 % CO conversion.
- **van 't Hoff Equation:** $\dfrac{d(\ln K_p)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{o}}{RT^{2}}$ — explains why **high T** favours reforming.
- **Le Chatelier:** Endothermic + Δn = +2 → **High T, Low P, High S/C** all favour H₂ production.
- **Grey H₂ emission factor:** ~9 – 10 kg CO₂/kg H₂ (the reason for CCUS integration).
- **Catalyst poisoning:** Sulphur compounds deactivate Ni → **desulfurisation is mandatory**.
- **Energy efficiency:** 70 – 85 % via HRSG integration.
- **Industrial dominance:** ~48 % global hydrogen production; refinery + ammonia feedstock.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Methods of Steam Reforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced Methods of Steam Reforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** is a well-established industrial process for hydrogen production, but it suffers from inherent thermodynamic limitations — namely, equilibrium-limited conversion, high endothermicity, and the need for external heat input. **Advanced Methods of Steam Reforming** refer to a class of intensified, integrated process configurations that combine reaction engineering with separation science (sorption or membrane-based) and/or process intensification (heat exchange, autothermal operation) to overcome the classical limitations of conventional SMR.

The four principal advanced configurations covered in KTU Module 2 are:

1. **Sorption-Enhanced Steam Reforming (SESR / SE-SMR)** — In-situ CO₂ capture using a solid sorbent.
2. **Membrane Steam Reforming (MSR)** — In-situ H₂ separation using a selective membrane.
3. **Autothermal Reforming (ATR)** — Coupling of endothermic reforming with exothermic partial oxidation in a single reactor.
4. **Heat-Exchange Reforming (HEX / Gas-Heated Reformer)** — Compact reformer using hot process gas as heat source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes **process intensification** — the integration of reaction + separation + heat transfer in a single unit to achieve higher conversion, lower energy penalty, and compact hydrogen production.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are boiling water in a covered pot. Steam escapes through a tiny hole (conventional SMR — H₂ leaves as a mixture, and the reaction stops once equilibrium is reached). Now imagine three different "advanced" tricks:

- **Sorption-Enhanced (SESR):** Attach a sponge inside the pot that immediately soaks up the CO₂ produced. Since the "waste" is continuously removed, the reaction is pushed forward, producing more H₂ at lower temperatures. *Like a vacuum cleaner running inside the reactor.*

- **Membrane Reforming (MSR):** Replace the lid with a sieve that only allows H₂ molecules to escape, trapping CO and CO₂ inside to react further. *Like a selective gatekeeper.*

- **Autothermal (ATR):** Instead of an external stove (conventional SMR), burn a little methane directly inside the pot. The flame provides heat exactly where needed. *Self-heating reactor.*

- **Heat-Exchange (HEX):** Wrap the pot's walls with hot exhaust gases from the burner, so heat transfer is 10× faster than a fire-tube furnace. *Compact, conductive heating.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - **Steam-to-Carbon (S/C) Ratio:** Industrial optimum ≈ **3.0 to 3.5**
> - **Reforming Temperature:** **700 – 850 °C** (conventional); advanced methods operate at **450 – 650 °C**
> - **Pressure Range:** **1 – 30 bar**
> - **Le Chatelier's Principle:** The driving force behind all advanced intensification strategies.

---

## 1.3 Why "Advanced"? — Limitations of Conventional SMR

| Limitation of Conventional SMR | How Advanced Methods Address It |
|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Equilibrium-limited conversion (max ~85% at 850 °C) | In-situ removal of H₂ or CO₂ shifts equilibrium (Le Chatelier) → **>95% conversion at lower T** |
| High external heat duty (endothermic ΔH ≈ +206 kJ/mol CH₄) | ATR uses internal heat; HEX recovers waste heat |
| Separate WGS reactor required | Membrane reactors combine WGS + separation |
| Large reformer footprint | Compact, intensified designs (HEX reformers) |
| High catalyst cost (Ni on Al₂O₃) | Lower temperature operation allows novel catalysts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sorption-Enhanced Steam Reforming (SESR)

### Working Principle
SESR integrates **steam reforming (SR)** + **water-gas shift (WGS)** + **CO₂ capture** in a single reactor packed with a reforming catalyst (Ni-based) and a CO₂ acceptor (typically CaO-based or hydrotalcite).

The simultaneous reactions are:

$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2 \quad (\Delta H_{298} = +206 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

$$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2 \quad (\Delta H_{298} = -41 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

$$CaO + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons CaCO_3 \quad (\Delta H_{298} = -178 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overall (carbonation-coupled) reaction:**
> $$CH_4 + 2H_2O + CaO \rightarrow CaCO_3 + 4H_2$$
> The stoichiometric H₂ yield is now **4 mol H₂ per mol CH₄**, compared to the conventional theoretical maximum of **3 mol H₂ per mol CH₄** — a **33% increase in theoretical yield**.

### Operating Cycle
The SESR operates in a **cyclic batch mode**:
1. **Reforming/Carbonation Stage (5–15 min):** Feed CH₄ + steam at 600–700 °C; CaO captures CO₂.
2. **Sorbent Regeneration Stage:** Calcination of CaCO₃ at **>900 °C** (often using sweep steam or air).

### Key Sorbents
- **CaO-based** (limestone, dolomite, CaO/Al₂O₃)
- **Hydrotalcite** (Mg-Al mixed oxides)
- **Li₂ZrO₃**, **Na₂ZrO₃** (regeneration at lower T)

## 2.2 Membrane Steam Reforming (MSR)

### Concept
A hydrogen-selective membrane (typically Pd-based or Pd-Ag alloy) is integrated into the reforming reactor. As H₂ is produced, it is **selectively removed** through the membrane, shifting equilibrium.

The driving force is the **hydrogen partial pressure gradient**:

$$J_{H_2} = \frac{Perm \cdot (p_{H_2}^{retentate} - p_{H_2}^{permeate})}{t_{mem}}$$

Where:
- $J_{H_2}$ = hydrogen flux (mol/m²·s)
- $Perm$ = membrane permeability (mol·m/m²·s·Paⁿ)
- $t_{mem}$ = membrane thickness (m)
- $p$ = partial pressures (Pa)

### Sievert's Law (for Pd membranes)
$$J_{H_2} = \frac{Perm_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}}{t_{mem}} \left( \sqrt{p_{H_2,ret}} - \sqrt{p_{H_2,perm}} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** Pd-Ag alloy membranes offer **infinite H₂ selectivity** (only H₂ passes), avoiding downstream CO₂/CO separation issues.

## 2.3 Autothermal Reforming (ATR)

ATR is a hybrid process where **partial oxidation (POX)** provides heat to drive the **endothermic steam reforming** in a single reactor.

$$CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad (\Delta H_{298} = -36 \text{ kJ/mol}) \quad \text{Exothermic}$$

$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad (\Delta H_{298} = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}) \quad \text{Endothermic}$$

Net reaction (balanced for **thermo-neutrality** when O₂/CH₄ ≈ 0.55–0.6):

$$CH_4 + 0.6\,O_2 + 0.7\,H_2O \rightarrow CO + 2.3\,H_2$$

The **thermal balance** dictates:

$$\Delta H_{SMR} + \alpha \cdot \Delta H_{POX} = 0$$

Solving for the air-to-fuel ratio gives the **thermo-neutral condition**.

## 2.4 Heat-Exchange Reforming (HEX)

HEX reformers use hot **process gas from a secondary reformer** (or flue gas) to drive the endothermic SMR reaction inside compact plate-fin or bayonet tubes. Compared to conventional top-fired furnaces:
- **Heat flux:** up to 100 kW/m² (vs. 30–40 kW/m² in fired heaters)
- **Pressure drop:** lower
- **Footprint reduction:** **50–70%**

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variable Definition | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------|---------------------|----------------|
| **SMR Equilibrium Constant** | $K_{eq} = \frac{p_{CO} \cdot p_{H_2}^3}{p_{CH_4} \cdot p_{H_2O}}$ | Partial pressures of gases | **Bar**; increases sharply with T |
| **WGS Equilibrium Constant** | $K_{WGS} = \frac{p_{CO_2} \cdot p_{H_2}}{p_{CO} \cdot p_{H_2O}}$ | — | **Decreases** with T (exothermic) |
| **S/C Ratio** | $S/C = \frac{F_{H_2O}}{F_{CH_4}}$ | Molar flow rates | Industrial: **3.0 – 3.5** |
| **Hydrogen Yield (Conventional SMR)** | $Y_{H_2}^{max} = 3 \text{ mol H}_2/\text{mol CH}_4$ | Theoretical stoichiometric | — |
| **Hydrogen Yield (SESR)** | $Y_{H_2}^{max} = 4 \text{ mol H}_2/\text{mol CH}_4$ | With CaO acceptor | **33% gain** |
| **Pd Membrane Flux (Sievert)** | $J_{H_2} = \frac{Perm_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}}{t} \left(\sqrt{p_{ret}} - \sqrt{p_{perm}}\right)$ | Sievert's Law | $Perm_0$ has units dependent on n |
| **Carbonation Equilibrium** | $K_{carb} = \frac{p_{CO_2,eq}}{p^0}$ | Equilibrium pCO₂ over CaO/CaCO₃ | Decreases with T (CaO favored below **~900 °C** at 1 bar) |
| **ATR Thermo-neutral** | $\alpha_{O_2/CH_4} \approx 0.55$ | O₂-to-CH₄ molar ratio | Gives ΔH_net ≈ 0 |
| **HEX Heat Flux** | $q'' = U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Overall heat transfer × LMTD | U up to **200 W/m²K** |
| **Methane Conversion** | $X_{CH_4} = \frac{F_{CH_4,in} - F_{CH_4,out}}{F_{CH_4,in}}$ | — | **>95%** in advanced methods |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are directly used in **H₂ plant design** (Linde, Air Products, Topsoe, Air Liquide), **process simulation (Aspen Plus, HYSYS)**, and **fuel cell reformer integration**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Thermodynamic Equilibrium of SMR with Sorption (SESR)

### Problem
Derive the **overall equilibrium constant** for SESR, treating it as a coupling of three equilibria, and show that the equilibrium CH₄ conversion is enhanced compared to conventional SMR at the same temperature.

### Step 1: Write the three coupled reactions

**Reaction (a) — Steam Reforming:**
$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2$$
$$K_1(T) = \frac{p_{CO} \cdot p_{H_2}^3}{p_{CH_4} \cdot p_{H_2O}}$$

**Reaction (b) — Water-Gas Shift:**
$$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2$$
$$K_2(T) = \frac{p_{CO_2} \cdot p_{H_2}}{p_{CO} \cdot p_{H_2O}}$$

**Reaction (c) — Carbonation:**
$$CaO + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons CaCO_3$$
$$K_3(T) = \frac{a_{CaCO_3}}{a_{CaO} \cdot (p_{CO_2}/p^0)}$$

Since CaO and CaCO₃ are pure solids: $a_{CaO} = a_{CaCO_3} = 1$, so:
$$K_3(T) = \frac{p^0}{p_{CO_2,eq}}$$

### Step 2: Combine (a) + (b) → Net Reforming + WGS

Adding reactions (a) and (b) gives:
$$CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + 4H_2$$
$$K_{net}(T) = K_1 \cdot K_2 = \frac{p_{CO_2} \cdot p_{H_2}^4}{p_{CH_4} \cdot p_{H_2O}^2}$$

### Step 3: Couple with Carbonation

When CO₂ is captured by CaO, its partial pressure is held at the **carbonation equilibrium value** $p_{CO_2,eq} = p^0 / K_3(T)$.

Substituting this into $K_{net}$:

$$K_{net}^{eff}(T) = K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3(T) = \frac{(p^0/K_3) \cdot p_{H_2}^4}{p_{CH_4} \cdot p_{H_2O}^2}$$

Rearranging to find the equilibrium H₂ partial pressure:

$$p_{H_2}^4 = K_{net}^{eff}(T) \cdot \frac{p_{CH_4} \cdot p_{H_2O}^2}{p^0} \cdot K_3(T)$$

### Step 4: Quantitative Comparison at 650 °C (923 K)

Using standard thermodynamic data at 923 K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1(923\text{ K}) &\approx 1.20 \times 10^2 \\
K_2(923\text{ K}) &\approx 1.45 \\
K_3(923\text{ K}) &\approx 50 \quad (\text{CaO carbonation}) \\
K_{net}^{eff} &= K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3 \approx 1.20 \times 10^2 \times 1.45 \times 50 \approx 8.7 \times 10^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare with conventional SMR (no sorbent):
$$
K_{net}^{conv} = K_1 \cdot K_2 \approx 1.20 \times 10^2 \times 1.45 \approx 174
$$

**Enhancement factor:**

$$\frac{K_{net}^{eff}}{K_{net}^{conv}} = K_3(T) \approx 50$$

This means the **effective driving force for H₂ production is ~50 times higher** in SESR than in conventional SMR at the same temperature.

### Step 5: Practical Conversion Estimate

For an inlet of 1 mol CH₄ + 3 mol H₂O at 1 bar, applying $K_{net}^{eff} = 8.7 \times 10^3$ in equilibrium calculations yields a **methane conversion > 98%** at 650 °C, compared to **~55%** for conventional SMR at the same temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of derivation:** Sorption enhancement effectively **breaks** the conventional thermodynamic barrier by reducing the CO₂ back-pressure, allowing high conversion at **150–200 °C lower temperatures** than conventional SMR.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Membrane Reformer Hydrogen Recovery Factor

### Problem
Determine the **H₂ recovery** in a Pd-membrane reformer as a function of membrane area, and find the minimum area needed for **95% H₂ recovery**.

### Setup
Assume feed: $F_{CH_4,in} = 1$ mol/s, S/C = 3, T = 600 °C, $P_{shell} = 10$ bar, $P_{perm} = 0.1$ bar (sweep gas side).

### Step 1: Maximum H₂ Produced (full conversion, S/C = 3)
Assuming complete reforming + WGS, the **equilibrium shift due to H₂ removal** allows complete conversion. Theoretical H₂ = 4 mol/s (if all carbon goes to CO₂ in ideal SESR-like stoichiometry with no shift losses). Realistic for MSR: 3 mol H₂/mol CH₄ (typical WGS-coupled SMR).

$$F_{H_2,produced}^{max} = 3 \times F_{CH_4} = 3 \text{ mol/s}$$

### Step 2: Permeation Equation
For Pd-Ag membrane, Sievert's Law at 600 °C:

$$J_{H_2} = \frac{Perm_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}}{t} \left( \sqrt{p_{H_2,ret}} - \sqrt{p_{H_2,perm}} \right)$$

Standard Pd-Ag values: $Perm_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \approx 3.0 \times 10^{-8}$ mol·m/m²·s·Pa⁰·⁵ at 600 °C, $t = 5 \, \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m.

### Step 3: Average Driving Force
Assuming $p_{H_2,ret} \approx 5$ bar (after 50% removal), $p_{H_2,perm} \approx 0.05$ bar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{H_2} &= \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-8}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} \left(\sqrt{5 \times 10^5} - \sqrt{0.05 \times 10^5}\right) \text{ mol/m}^2\text{s} \\
J_{H_2} &= 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \times \left(\sqrt{500000} - \sqrt{5000}\right) \\
J_{H_2} &= 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \times (707.1 - 70.7) \\
J_{H_2} &= 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 636.4 \\
J_{H_2} &\approx 3.82 \text{ mol/m}^2\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Required Membrane Area for 95% Recovery
H₂ to remove: $0.95 \times 3 = 2.85$ mol/s.

$$A_{mem} = \frac{F_{H_2}}{J_{H_2}} = \frac{2.85}{3.82} \approx 0.75 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 5: Engineering Interpretation
For a **1 mol/s CH₄ feed**, a **~0.75 m²** Pd-Ag membrane suffices. With conventional technology needing large PSA units (typically 5–10 m³ adsorbent), MSR achieves an order-of-magnitude **footprint reduction**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Conventional SMR | SESR | MSR | ATR |
|-----------|-----------------|------|-----|-----|
| Operating T (°C) | 800–850 | 600–700 | 550–700 | 850–1000 |
| CH₄ conversion (%) | 85–90 | >95 | >90 | >95 |
| H₂ yield (mol/mol CH₄) | ~2.8 | ~3.9 | ~2.7 | ~2.5 |
| External heat | Yes (large) | Yes (cyclic regen) | Yes | No (autothermal) |
| CO₂ in product | Yes | Very low | Retentate only | Yes |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| Best application | Large-scale plants | Distributed H₂ | PEM fuel cell feed | Refinery/GTL integration |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Integrated Advanced Reforming System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["CH4 Natural Gas Feed"]:::feed
    node2["Steam Generator 600 C S/C 3 to 1"]:::unit
    node3["Pre Reformer 400 C CH4 partial conversion"]:::unit
    node4["Advanced Reformer 700 C Three configurations"]:::unit
    node5["SESR Packed Bed Ni Catalyst and CaO Sorbent"]:::subunit
    node6["MSR Pd Ag Membrane Tubes In Situ H2 Removal"]:::subunit
    node7["ATR Catalyst Bed Top POX Zone and Bottom SR Zone"]:::subunit
    node8["WGS Reactor High T 350 C and Low T 200 C"]:::unit
    node9["PSA H2 Purification 99.99 percent Purity"]:::unit
    node10["H2 Product Compressor Storage 350 700 bar"]:::outlet
    node11["CO2 Sequestration or EOR"]:::outlet
    node12["Heat Recovery Steam Generation HRSG"]:::rec
    node13["Sorbent Calciner 900 C CaCO3 to CaO"]:::rec

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node4 --> node6
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node8
    node5 --> node13
    node13 --> node5
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10
    node9 --> node11
    node8 --> node12
    node12 --> node2

    classDef feed fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef unit fill:#e6f2cc,stroke:#336600,color:#000
    classDef subunit fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef outlet fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef rec fill:#e0d4f5,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue nodes** — Inlet feed streams (CH₄ + H₂O)
- **Green nodes** — Process units (reforming, WGS, PSA)
- **Yellow nodes** — Advanced reformer sub-options (SESR / MSR / ATR)
- **Red nodes** — Product outlets (H₂, CO₂)
- **Purple nodes** — Recycle / heat integration loops

---

## 4.2 Process Flow Block — SESR Cyclic Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Steam Generator S/C 3"]:::aux
    node2["Reformer Reactor Ni Catalyst plus CaO 650 C"]:::main
    node3["H2 Rich Product 4 mol per mol CH4"]:::out
    node4["Calciner 900 C Steam Sweep"]:::aux
    node5["Pure CO2 Stream Sequestration"]:::out
    node6["Regenerated CaO Recycle to Reformer"]:::rec
    node7["Switching Valve Array Timing Controller"]:::ctrl

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node4 --> node6
    node6 --> node2
    node7 -.controls.-> node2
    node7 -.controls.-> node4

    classDef main fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef aux fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef rec fill:#e0d4f5,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333333,color:#000
```

**Key Process Insight:**
The reformer operates ~10 minutes in the carbonation step, then the feed switches and the sorbent is regenerated. Continuous operation is achieved using **multiple parallel reactors** (typically 3–4 beds) with phased switching.

---

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Membrane Steam Reformer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Feed CH4 plus Steam"]:::feed
    nodeB["Catalyst Side Ni on Al2O3 550 to 700 C"]:::main
    nodeC["Pd Ag Membrane Wall 5 to 10 micron thickness"]:::mem
    nodeD["Sweep Steam N2 or Ar Permeate Side 0.1 to 1 bar"]:::aux
    nodeE["Retentate CO plus unconverted CH4 plus H2O Recycled to Burner"]:::out
    nodeF["Permeate Pure H2 plus Sweep Gas Condenser Separates H2"]:::out
    nodeG["Pressure Shell 10 to 30 bar"]:::shell

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeG -.encloses.-> nodeB

    classDef feed fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef main fill:#e6f2cc,stroke:#336600,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef aux fill:#e0d4f5,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef shell fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Membrane Wall:** Hydrogen atoms dissociate on the Pd surface, diffuse through the dense metal lattice as protons + electrons, and re-associate on the permeate side. CO, CO₂, CH₄, H₂O are **rejected**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` 

**Q: List three advantages of Sorption-Enhanced Steam Reforming (SESR) over conventional Steam Methane Reforming (SMR).**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher H₂ yield per mol CH₄** — SESR yields ~4 mol H₂ per mol CH₄ (theoretical) versus 3 mol H₂ per mol CH₄ in conventional SMR, due to in-situ CO₂ capture by CaO and full shift of WGS equilibrium.
2. **Lower operating temperature** — SESR operates at 600–700 °C vs 800–850 °C for SMR, since the sorbent removes the thermodynamic barrier, enabling energy savings.
3. **Higher CH₄ conversion (>95%)** — Because Le Chatelier's principle continuously drives the reaction forward by removing CO₂ from the gas phase, equilibrium conversion is significantly enhanced.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: Differentiate between Autothermal Reforming (ATR) and conventional Steam Methane Reforming (SMR).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Conventional SMR | Autothermal Reforming (ATR) |
|--------|------------------|------------------------------|
| Heat source | External fired furnace | Internal — partial oxidation of CH₄ |
| Reactor type | Tubular (catalyst in tubes) | Catalytic fixed bed (adiabatic) |
| O₂ requirement | None | Yes — limited O₂ for partial oxidation |
| Operating temperature | 800–850 °C | 850–1000 °C |
| Net ΔH | Highly endothermic (+206 kJ/mol) | Near thermo-neutral (≈ 0) |
| H₂/CO ratio | ~3 (high H₂) | ~1.5–2 (synthesis gas) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (KTU 2024):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the two choices. Each choice has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.*

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **Membrane Steam Reforming (MSR)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of Sievert's Law in determining hydrogen flux through the Pd-Ag membrane. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Pd-Ag membrane of thickness **t = 8 μm** operates at **650 °C** in a steam reformer. The retentate H₂ partial pressure is **8 bar** and the permeate side (with N₂ sweep) is **0.08 bar**. The permeability at 650 °C is **Perm = 5.0 × 10⁻⁸ mol·m/m²·s·Pa⁰·⁵**. Calculate the **hydrogen flux** through the membrane. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Working Principle of MSR

**Working Principle:**
Membrane Steam Reforming integrates the catalytic steam reforming reaction with in-situ hydrogen separation using a hydrogen-selective membrane (Pd or Pd-Ag alloy). As H₂ is produced on the catalyst side, it is continuously withdrawn through the membrane, shifting the reforming equilibrium toward higher methane conversion (Le Chatelier's principle).

**Key Components:**
1. **Catalyst side:** Packed with Ni-based reforming catalyst (NiO/MgO or Ni/Al₂O₃) where SMR + WGS occur at 550–700 °C.
2. **Membrane wall:** Dense Pd-Ag alloy (typically 23% Ag, 5–10 μm thick) allows only H₂ to permeate.
3. **Sweep/permeate side:** Low-pressure side (0.05–1 bar) maintained by steam or N₂ sweep, providing the driving force for permeation.

**Hydrogen Transport Mechanism (Solution-Diffusion):**
1. **Dissociative adsorption:** $H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2H_{ads}$ on Pd surface
2. **Bulk diffusion:** H atoms dissolve into Pd lattice and diffuse as protons + electrons
3. **Re-association:** $2H_{ads} \rightleftharpoons H_2$ on permeate side
4. **Desorption:** H₂ released to permeate stream

**Role of Sievert's Law:**
The flux follows Sievert's Law (square-root dependence on partial pressure):

$$J_{H_2} = \frac{Perm}{t} \left( \sqrt{p_{H_2,ret}} - \sqrt{p_{H_2,perm}} \right)$$

This **square-root dependence** reflects the dissociative adsorption mechanism. Sievert's Law allows engineers to:
- Predict flux for given operating conditions
- Optimize membrane thickness and area
- Design sweep gas flow rates to maintain maximum driving force

**Key Advantages:**
- Equilibrium shift → >90% CH₄ conversion at 600 °C
- High purity H₂ (99.999%) directly obtained
- Compact reactor (combined reaction + separation)
- Lower operating temperature than SMR

#### Model Solution (b) — H₂ Flux Calculation

**Given Data:**
- Membrane thickness: $t = 8 \, \mu m = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Temperature: $T = 650 °C = 923$ K
- Retentate H₂ partial pressure: $p_{ret} = 8 \text{ bar} = 8 \times 10^5$ Pa
- Permeate H₂ partial pressure: $p_{perm} = 0.08 \text{ bar} = 0.08 \times 10^5$ Pa = 8000 Pa
- Permeability: $Perm = 5.0 \times 10^{-8}$ mol·m/m²·s·Pa⁰·⁵

**Apply Sievert's Law:**

$$J_{H_2} = \frac{Perm}{t} \left( \sqrt{p_{ret}} - \sqrt{p_{perm}} \right)$$

**Step 1:** Compute the permeance term.

$$\frac{Perm}{t} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-8}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/m}^2\text{s/Pa}^{0.5}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the square-root pressure difference.

$$\sqrt{p_{ret}} = \sqrt{8 \times 10^5} = \sqrt{800000} = 894.4 \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$

$$\sqrt{p_{perm}} = \sqrt{8000} = 89.44 \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$

$$\Delta \sqrt{p} = 894.4 - 89.44 = 804.96 \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$

**Step 3:** Compute flux.

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{H_2} &= 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 804.96 \\
J_{H_2} &= 5.031 \text{ mol/m}^2\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $J_{H_2} \approx \mathbf{5.03 \, mol/m^2 \cdot s}$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
For a CH₄ feed of 1 mol/s producing 3 mol/s H₂, the membrane area needed to recover 90% of H₂ is:

$$A = \frac{0.9 \times 3}{5.03} \approx 0.54 \text{ m}^2$$

This compact footprint demonstrates the major advantage of MSR over conventional SMR + PSA systems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Discuss **Sorption Enhanced Steam Reforming (SESR)**. With appropriate equations, explain how in-situ CO₂ capture using CaO enhances H₂ yield. Mention any two limitations of this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an ATR reformer, methane is fed with steam (S/C = 2.5) and oxygen (O₂/CH₄ = 0.55). Assuming the reforming and partial oxidation reactions reach near-equilibrium at 900 °C and 1 bar, calculate the **mole fraction of H₂** in the dry product gas. Use the following stoichiometric net reaction: $CH_4 + 0.55\,O_2 + 1.5\,H_2O \rightarrow$ products. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — SESR

**Working of SESR:**
SESR combines three reaction steps in a single reactor: (i) steam reforming, (ii) water-gas shift, and (iii) CO₂ capture by a solid sorbent. The sorbent (typically CaO) reacts with CO₂ to form CaCO₃ in the **carbonation zone**, effectively removing CO₂ from the gas phase.

**Reactions and Equations:**

**Steam Reforming:**
$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Water-Gas Shift:**
$$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Carbonation (CO₂ Capture):**
$$CaO + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons CaCO_3 \quad \Delta H = -178 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Overall SESR Reaction (combined):**
$$CH_4 + 2H_2O + CaO \rightarrow CaCO_3 + 4H_2$$

**Mechanism of H₂ Yield Enhancement:**

The CO₂ partial pressure in the reformer is held at the carbonation equilibrium value:
$$p_{CO_2,eq} = p^0 / K_3(T)$$

Since $K_3(T)$ is large (~50 at 650 °C), $p_{CO_2}$ becomes negligibly small. By Le Chatelier's principle:
- Lower $p_{CO_2}$ → WGS equilibrium shifts right → more H₂ produced
- Lower $p_{CO_2}$ → CO concentration drops → SR equilibrium shifts right → more CH₄ converted

**Result:** **4 mol H₂ per mol CH₄** is produced (vs. 3 in conventional SMR) — a **33% increase in theoretical yield**.

**Limitations:**
1. **Cyclic operation** — The sorbent must be regenerated periodically (calcination at >900 °C), requiring multiple reactors in parallel and complex valve sequencing, which reduces continuous availability.
2. **Sorbent degradation** — CaO undergoes sintering and loss of porosity over multiple carbonation/calcination cycles, reducing CO₂ capture capacity by 50–80% after 50 cycles, requiring sorbent replacement.
3. **Heat penalty of regeneration** — Calcination is highly endothermic, requiring ~3.0 GJ per ton CO₂ captured, partially offsetting the energy advantage.

#### Model Solution (b) — ATR Product Composition

**Given:**
- Feed: CH₄ + 1.5 H₂O + 0.55 O₂ (per mol CH₄)
- T = 900 °C, P = 1 bar, S/C = 2.5, O₂/CH₄ = 0.55

**Step 1: Assume reactions reach equilibrium — Define extent of SR (ξ₁) and WGS (ξ₂).**

Initial moles: $n_{CH_4} = 1$, $n_{H_2O} = 1.5$, $n_{O_2} = 0.55$, rest = 0.

After POX (assume complete): $CH_4 + 0.55 O_2 \rightarrow 0.55 CO + 1.1 H_2 + 0.45 CH_4$

Wait — let me redo using a simpler **stoichiometric balance approach**:

The combined ATR reaction with steam typically yields:
- 1 mol CH₄ + 0.55 O₂ → 0.55 CO + 1.1 H₂ + 0.45 CH₄ (unconverted, then SR proceeds)

For **complete conversion** (assume equilibrium favors full CH₄ conversion):

**Carbon balance:** All C → CO (1 mol CO)
**Oxygen balance:** From O₂ (0.55 mol) and H₂O (1.5 mol) → O available = 0.55 × 2 + 1.5 = 2.6 mol O atoms
- 1 mol CO uses 1 O atom → remaining O = 1.6 atoms in H₂O (unreacted) = 0.8 mol H₂O remaining
- H balance: 1.5 × 2 + 0 × 2 + 4 × 1 (from CH₄) = 7 H atoms
  - 0.8 H₂O = 1.6 H atoms; rest as H₂ = (7 - 1.6)/2 = **2.7 mol H₂**

**Step 2: Compute total dry gas moles.**

Dry products = 1 mol CO + 2.7 mol H₂ = **3.7 mol**

(Plus any unconverted CH₄ — assumed zero at high T)

**Step 3: Compute H₂ mole fraction (dry basis).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{H_2} &= \frac{n_{H_2}}{n_{CO} + n_{H_2}} \\
y_{H_2} &= \frac{2.7}{1 + 2.7} = \frac{2.7}{3.7}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{y_{H_2} \approx 0.730 \text{ or } 73.0\%}$$

**Engineering Note:**
ATR typically produces **synthesis gas** with H₂/CO ≈ 1.5–2.5, used for GTL, methanol, and Fischer-Tropsch synthesis. The high H₂ fraction (~73%) here is due to excess steam (S/C = 2.5) which shifts WGS right.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where students lose marks in Advanced Reforming questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "H₂ yield" with "CH₄ conversion."** SESR gives 4 mol H₂/mol CH₄ — this is **yield**, not conversion. Both are reported separately.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Sievert's Law** — flux is in mol/m²·s, not kg. Many students drop the membrane thickness from the equation.
> 3. **Treating ATR as fully exothermic or fully endothermic.** The whole point of ATR is **thermo-neutrality** — never say "ATR is exothermic" or "ATR is endothermic" alone.
> 4. **Writing SMR equilibrium constant with concentration** instead of partial pressures. **Always use partial pressures** in KTU problems.
> 5. **Ignoring cyclic regeneration in SESR** — students often forget that CaCO₃ must be calcined; the process is not continuous without parallel beds.
> 6. **Mixing MSR with membrane filtration** — MSR uses a **dense Pd membrane**, not porous. The mechanism is **solution-diffusion**, not size exclusion.
> 7. **Sign errors in carbonation ΔH** — Carbonation is **exothermic** (-178 kJ/mol), meaning low T favors CaCO₃ formation (good for capture), high T favors CaO regeneration.
> 8. **Incomplete ATR O₂ balance** — Students often forget that POX produces both CO and H₂, with stoichiometry $CH_4 + 0.5 O_2 → CO + 2 H_2$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **SMR baseline:** $\Delta H = +206$ kJ/mol; T = 800–850 °C; max H₂ yield = 3 mol/mol CH₄
- ✅ **S/C ratio:** Industrial optimum = **3.0–3.5** (avoids coking, drives WGS)
- ✅ **Le Chatelier's principle** is the foundation of all advanced methods
- ✅ **SESR:** CaO-based sorbent; 4 mol H₂/mol CH₄; cyclic; 600–700 °C; calcination at 900 °C
- ✅ **MSR:** Pd-Ag membrane (5–10 μm); **Sievert's Law** governs flux; infinite H₂ selectivity
- ✅ **ATR:** POX + SR combined; **thermo-neutral**; O₂/CH₄ ≈ 0.55; adiabatic
- ✅ **HEX reformer:** Heat flux up to 100 kW/m²; 50–70% footprint reduction
- ✅ **Carbonation equilibrium constant** $K_3 \approx 50$ at 650 °C drives SESR enhancement
- ✅ **Pd membrane permeation** follows $J \propto (\sqrt{p_{ret}} - \sqrt{p_{perm}})$
- ✅ **Carbon balance rule:** All inlet carbon → CO (in SMR) → CO₂ (after WGS) → CaCO₃ (in SESR)
- ✅ **KTU favorite exam numbers:** S/C = 3, P = 10–30 bar, T = 600/700/850/1000 °C
- ✅ **Sorbent choice trade-off:** CaO (cheap, high T regen) vs Hydrotalcite (mid T, lower capacity) vs Li₂ZrO₃ (high T, expensive)
- ✅ **Industrial use of ATR:** Syngas for GTL, methanol, ammonia — not for high-purity H₂
- ✅ **MSR advantage over PSA:** Continuous H₂ recovery at high purity, no adsorbent regeneration
- ✅ **ATR H₂/CO ratio ≈ 1.5–2.5** vs SMR ratio ≈ 3 — this is why ATR is preferred for synthesis

### 🎯 KTU Module 2 High-Yield Numerical Patterns

1. Sievert's Law flux calculations with given Pd membrane data
2. Equilibrium conversion comparison: SMR vs SESR vs MSR
3. S/C ratio effect on H₂ yield
4. ATR O₂/CH₄ balance for thermo-neutral operation
5. Carbonation/calcination cycle time and sorbent mass calculations

---

**End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Advanced Methods of Steam Reforming**

*For further reading on KTU 2024 Scheme, refer to standard hydrogen energy textbooks covering SMR thermodynamics, sorption-enhanced processes, and membrane reactors.*
