topics.title: Partial Oxidation Method for Hydrogen Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Partial Oxidation Method for Hydrogen Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Partial Oxidation (POX) of Hydrocarbons** is a thermochemical hydrogen production process in which a hydrocarbon feedstock (methane, natural gas, heavy oils, coal, biomass, or methanol) reacts with a **sub-stoichiometric quantity of pure oxygen** (i.e., less oxygen than required for *complete combustion* to CO₂ and H₂O) at elevated temperatures and pressures to produce a synthesis gas (syngas) mixture rich in **hydrogen (H₂)** and **carbon monoxide (CO)**. The CO produced is subsequently converted to additional H₂ via the **Water–Gas Shift (WGS) reaction**.

The global stoichiometric reaction for the simplest hydrocarbon (methane, CH₄) is:

$$CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298K} = -36 \text{ kJ/mol (exothermic)}$$

For a generic heavy hydrocarbon $C_n H_m$, the POX reaction becomes:

$$C_n H_m + \tfrac{n}{2} O_2 \rightarrow n CO + \tfrac{m}{2} H_2$$

For methanol, a unique direct POX occurs:

$$CH_3OH + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2 + CO_2 \quad \Delta H_{298K} = -192.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE] **Analogy – The Campfire vs. the Gasifier**

Imagine lighting a campfire with dry wood and **plenty of air**. The flames are vigorous, bright yellow-orange, and you get intense heat — but the *only useful gaseous products* are CO₂ and H₂O vapor (the complete combustion products). No useful fuel gas remains.

Now, take the **same wood**, but restrict the air supply to roughly **half of what is normally required** and force it through a sealed, insulated chamber (a *gasifier*). The flame becomes a dull, blue, sooty glow — much less heat per unit mass — but the *exhaust gases* now contain large fractions of **carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H₂)**, the same molecules that power blast furnaces, Fischer–Tropsch diesel, and fuel cells. **Partial Oxidation is the engineering-controlled, industrial-scale version of this "smouldering" gasification**, carefully tuned to maximize H₂ + CO output rather than heat.

A helpful mnemonic:
- **Complete oxidation (combustion):** C → CO₂ (carbon fully oxidized, max heat)
- **Partial oxidation (POX):** C → CO (carbon *halfway* oxidized, energy retained in the gas)

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Process Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Engineering Metrics Used Throughout POX Design**
> - **Standard molar volume (STP):** $V_m = 22.414 \text{ L/mol}$
> - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
> - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel ratio (CH₄):** 17.2 kg air / kg fuel
> - **Operating temperature range:**
>   - *Non-catalytic (thermal) POX:* **1200 – 1500 °C**
>   - *Catalytic Partial Oxidation (CPO):* **800 – 1000 °C**
> - **Operating pressure range:** **1 – 80 bar**
> - **H₂ : CO molar product ratio (methane POX):** ≈ **2 : 1** (ideal)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The POX process exploits a fundamental thermodynamic fact: **at very high temperatures and under oxygen-lean conditions, carbon prefers to form CO rather than CO₂**, because the reaction $C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO$ has a much *lower activation energy* than $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ at $T > 1000 °C$. This kinetic preference is the entire foundation of POX.

**Step-by-step logical flow of the POX process:**

1. **Feed preparation:** Liquid hydrocarbon (heavy oil, methanol) is vaporized; gaseous feedstock (methane, natural gas) is preheated to ≈ 300–400 °C.
2. **Oxygen supply:** High-purity oxygen (typically **≥ 95 mol %**) is obtained from a **cryogenic Air Separation Unit (ASU)** or a **Vacuum Pressure Swing Adsorption (VPSA)** system. Air cannot be used directly because its ~79% N₂ would dilute the syngas and waste compression energy.
3. **Mixing & ignition:** Fuel + O₂ are mixed at the burner head in an O:C ratio of **0.5 – 0.7** (where 1.0 = full combustion stoichiometry).
4. **Reaction zone:** Combustion occurs *briefly and intensely*. Because O₂ is the limiting reagent, only ~50–70% of carbon is oxidized to CO, while the remaining hydrogen in the hydrocarbon is liberated as H₂.
5. **Soot management:** Heavy feedstocks can form soot; downstream **carbon scrubbers** or **water-quench** remove particulates.
6. **Water-Gas Shift (WGS) reactor:** The crude syngas passes over Fe/Cr (HT-shift) and Cu/Zn/Al (LT-shift) catalysts to convert CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂, raising the H₂ yield by another ~30–50%.
7. **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA):** Final purification yields **99.99% pure H₂**.

## 2.2 Reaction Network Inside a POX Reactor

The *real* chemistry is more complex than the single ideal equation suggests. Several reactions occur *simultaneously*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Partial Oxidation:} \quad & CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \Delta H = -36 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
\textbf{Steam Reforming (parallel):} \quad & CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
\textbf{Complete Combustion (side):} \quad & CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O \quad \Delta H = -803 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
\textbf{Water-Gas Shift (downstream):} \quad & CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
\textbf{Boudouard (coking):} \quad & 2CO \rightarrow C_{(s)} + CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -172 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
\textbf{Methane Cracking (coking):} \quad & CH_4 \rightarrow C_{(s)} + 2H_2 \quad \Delta H = +75 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Net Effect:** Because the dominant POX reaction is **exothermic**, the reactor is *autothermal* — once ignited, no external heat input is required. This is a major engineering advantage over the strongly endothermic Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) process.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE] The following table is the **master reference** for solving all KTU numerical and theory problems on POX.

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Defining Equation** | **Standard Unit** | **Typical Range / Value** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| $\dot{n}_{H_2}$ | Molar H₂ production rate | $\dot{n}_{fuel} \cdot X_{fuel} \cdot Y_{H_2}$ | kmol/h | 100 – 100,000 |
| $Y_{H_2}$ | Theoretical H₂ yield per mole fuel | from stoichiometry (e.g., 2 for CH₄) | mol H₂ / mol fuel | 2 – 4 |
| $X_{fuel}$ | Fuel conversion fraction | $1 - \dot{n}_{fuel,out}/\dot{n}_{fuel,in}$ | dimensionless | 0.95 – 0.999 |
| $\eta_{th}$ | Thermal efficiency | $\dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}{\dot{n}_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$ | dimensionless | 0.60 – 0.85 |
| $S/C$ | Steam-to-Carbon molar ratio | $\dot{n}_{H_2O}/\dot{n}_{C,feed}$ | mol/mol | 0.0 – 3.0 |
| $O/C$ | Oxygen-to-Carbon molar ratio | $\dot{n}_{O_2}/\dot{n}_{C,feed}$ | mol/mol | 0.50 – 0.70 |
| $\lambda$ | Equivalence ratio | $(O/C)_{actual}/(O/C)_{stoich}$ | dimensionless | 0.25 – 0.45 |
| $\Delta H_{rxn}$ | Heat of reaction | $\sum \nu_i H_{f,i}^{products} - \sum \nu_i H_{f,i}^{reactants}$ | kJ/mol | depends on rxn |
| $K_{eq}$ | Equilibrium constant | $\exp(-\Delta G°/RT)$ | dimensionless | function of T |
| $\tau$ | Residence time | $V_{reactor}/\dot{V}_{gas}$ | s | 0.1 – 5.0 |
| $LHV_{H_2}$ | Lower Heating Value of H₂ | fixed constant | kJ/mol | **240** |

**Solved numerical reference:** For pure methane POX at $T = 1200\,°C$ and 1 atm, using a 50% stoichiometric air-equivalent oxygen feed, the equilibrium H₂ yield is approximately **2.0 mol per mol CH₄** with ≈ 95% CH₄ conversion. The exact value requires solving a constrained Gibbs free energy minimization — a topic beyond the undergraduate scope but conceptually important.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Partial Oxidation is the **workhorse syngas technology** for:
- **Integrated Gasification Combined Cycle (IGCC)** power plants
- **Fischer–Tropsch** synthetic diesel production (Sasol, Shell Pearl GTL)
- **Ammonia (NH₃) synthesis** (via subsequent H₂ separation)
- **Methanol synthesis** (CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH)
- **Hydrogen refueling stations** (compact on-site POX reformers)
- **Refinery H₂ supply** (oil refineries are the *largest* industrial consumers of H₂)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement for a Generic Hydrocarbon

**Given:** A feed hydrocarbon of formula $C_n H_m$ (e.g., for methane, $n=1, m=4$).

**Goal:** Find the moles of O₂ required for *complete combustion* (the stoichiometric reference) and the *half-stoichiometric* amount required for POX.

### Step 1: Write the complete combustion reaction

$$C_n H_m + \left(n + \tfrac{m}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow n CO_2 + \tfrac{m}{2} H_2O$$

**Logic:** Each C atom needs 2 O atoms → $2n$ O atoms. Each H atom needs $\tfrac{1}{2}$ O atom → $\tfrac{m}{2}$ O atoms. Total O atoms = $2n + \tfrac{m}{2}$, which corresponds to $\left(n + \tfrac{m}{4}\right)$ molecules of O₂.

### Step 2: Stoichiometric O₂ demand

$$
(O_2)_{stoich} = n + \tfrac{m}{4} \quad \text{[mol O}_2 \text{ per mol } C_n H_m\text{]}
$$

### Step 3: POX requires only HALF of this O₂

$$(O_2)_{POX} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(n + \tfrac{m}{4}\right) = \tfrac{n}{2} + \tfrac{m}{8}$$

**Numerical check for methane** ($n=1, m=4$):
$$(O_2)_{POX} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 + 1) = 1 \text{ ??? }$$

⚠️ *Correction:* For complete CH₄ combustion: $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$, so $(O_2)_{stoich} = 2$. Then $(O_2)_{POX} = \tfrac{1}{2}(2) = 1$, giving:

$$CH_4 + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2 \quad \text{(apparent POX with full O}_2\text{)}$$

But wait — the *actual* POX product is CO, not CO₂! The factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ stoichiometry is for the **partial** oxidation that yields CO, meaning the true POX stoichiometry for methane is:

$$CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \checkmark$$

This matches the textbook reaction, confirming the formula.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Maximum H₂ Yield for Methane POX + WGS

**Setup:** One mole of CH₄ is fed. Assume:
- 100% POX conversion: $CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2$
- 100% WGS conversion of the CO produced: $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$

### Step 1: H₂ from POX stage

$$n_{H_2}^{(POX)} = 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 2: CO from POX stage (becomes reactant for WGS)

$$n_{CO}^{(POX)} = 1 \times 1 = 1 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 3: H₂ from WGS stage (1 mol CO → 1 mol H₂)

$$n_{H_2}^{(WGS)} = 1 \times 1 = 1 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 4: Total H₂ per mole of CH₄

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{H_2}^{total} &= n_{H_2}^{(POX)} + n_{H_2}^{(WGS)} \\
&= 2 + 1 \\
&= \boxed{3 \text{ mol H}_2 \text{ per mol CH}_4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight:** Interestingly, this 3 mol H₂/mol CH₄ is the **same theoretical maximum** as plain Steam Methane Reforming (SMR). The difference is that **POX is exothermic** (it generates heat) while **SMR is endothermic** (it consumes heat). POX plants therefore have *no external reformer furnace*, while SMR plants require a giant firebox.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Efficiency of POX

**Definition:**
$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\text{Chemical energy of H}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{Chemical energy of fuel fed}} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}{\dot{n}_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$$

### Step 1: Known LHV values
- $LHV_{H_2} = 240 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $LHV_{CH_4} = 802 \text{ kJ/mol}$

### Step 2: Plug in the maximum-yield case for methane POX + WGS

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th} &= \dfrac{3 \text{ mol} \cdot 240 \text{ kJ/mol}}{1 \text{ mol} \cdot 802 \text{ kJ/mol}} \\
&= \dfrac{720}{802} \\
&= \boxed{0.898 \approx 89.8\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Reality Check:** This 89.8% is the *ideal thermodynamic upper bound*. Real plants achieve **70 – 80%** because of (a) incomplete fuel conversion (~5%), (b) irreversibilities in the WGS equilibrium (~10% unreacted CO), (c) PSA purge losses (~5%), and (d) syngas combustion to heat the reactor.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalence Ratio (λ) and Why It Matters

**Definition:**
$$\lambda = \dfrac{(O_2/C)_{actual}}{(O_2/C)_{stoichiometric}}$$

For methane (1 mol CH₄ needs 2 mol O₂ for complete combustion):
$$(O_2/C)_{stoich} = 2 \text{ mol O}_2 / \text{mol CH}_4$$
$$(O_2/C)_{POX, actual} = 0.5 \text{ mol O}_2 / \text{mol CH}_4$$
$$\lambda_{POX} = \dfrac{0.5}{2.0} = 0.25$$

### Interpretation:
- $\lambda = 1.0$ → complete combustion (engine)
- $\lambda = 0.25$ → ideal POX (this is the operating regime!)
- $\lambda < 0.25$ → *insufficient* oxygen; coking and soot dominate
- $\lambda > 0.5$ → *too much* oxygen; CO₂ + H₂O form, H₂ yield collapses

> [!WARNING] **Critical Operating Window:** $\lambda = 0.20$ to $0.35$ is the *sweet spot* for H₂ maximization. Operating outside this window results in either coking (soot blocking the catalyst bed) or loss of H₂ to over-oxidation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 POX Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid Block-Functional Architecture)

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an industrial Partial Oxidation plant — from feedstock input to pure H₂ output. Each block represents a process unit, and arrows represent the material/energy streams flowing between them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FEED HYDROCARBON CH4 OR HEAVY OIL OR METHANOL"]:::feed --> B["PREHEATER 300 TO 400 C"]
    C["PURE OXYGEN FROM ASU OR VPSA 95 PLUS MOL PCT"]:::feed --> B
    B --> D["POX REACTOR 1200 TO 1500 C NON CATALYTIC OR 800 TO 900 C CPO"]
    D --> E["HEAT RECOVERY BOILER WASTE HEAT TO STEAM"]
    E --> F["SCRUBBER SOOT AND PARTICULATE REMOVAL"]
    F --> G["HT WATER GAS SHIFT REACTOR FE CR CATALYST 350 TO 450 C"]
    G --> H["LT WATER GAS SHIFT REACTOR CU ZN AL CATALYST 200 TO 250 C"]
    H --> I["CO2 REMOVAL MEA OR PSA"]
    I --> J["PRESSURE SWING ADSORPTION PSA H2 PURIFICATION 99.99 PLUS"]
    J --> K["PURE HYDROGEN H2 OUTPUT"]:::product
    J --> L["TAIL GAS FUEL TO POX REACTOR OR EXPANDER"]:::recycle

    classDef feed fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef product fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef recycle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Reaction Pathway Block Diagram (Catalytic POX Mechanism)

The following block diagram illustrates the *catalytic surface mechanism* of CPO — the dominant modern industrial variant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["STEP 1 FUEL AND O2 ADSORB ON NOBLE METAL SURFACE PT RH OR NI"]:::step
    P1 --> P2["STEP 2 O2 DISSOCIATES TO 2 O ATOMS"]
    P2 --> P3["STEP 3 FUEL DEHYDROGENATION CH4 BREAKS TO CHX FRAGMENTS"]
    P3 --> P4["STEP 4 SURFACE PARTIAL OXIDATION FORMS CO AND H2"]
    P4 --> P5["STEP 5 DESORPTION OF CO AND H2 FROM CATALYST"]
    P5 --> P6["STEP 6 SYNGAS LEAVES REACTOR 800 TO 900 C"]:::output
    P6 --> P7["REJECTED HEAT RECOVERY OR WGS"]
    P1 -.heat.-> HX1["AUTOTHERMAL OPERATION EXOTHERMIC POX BALANCES ENDOTHERMIC REFORMING"]

    classDef step fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — POX vs. SMR vs. Autothermal Reforming (ATR)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POX["PARTIAL OXIDATION POX"]
        A1["FUEL"] --> A2["PLUS O2 ONLY LAMBDA 0.25"]
        A2 --> A3["REACTOR EXOTHERMIC AUTOTHERMAL"]
        A3 --> A4["SYN GAS H2 PLUS CO"]
    end

    subgraph SMR["STEAM METHANE REFORMING SMR"]
        B1["FUEL"] --> B2["PLUS H2O STEAM HIGH S OVER C"]
        B2 --> B3["REACTOR ENDOTHERMIC NEEDS FURNACE"]
        B3 --> B4["SYN GAS H2 PLUS CO"]
    end

    subgraph ATR["AUTOTHERMAL REFORMING ATR"]
        C1["FUEL"] --> C2["PLUS O2 PLUS H2O COMBINED"]
        C2 --> C3["REACTOR THERMONEUTRAL"]
        C3 --> C4["SYN GAS H2 PLUS CO"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight from the diagram:** ATR is essentially **POX + SMR hybrid** — oxygen is added in sub-stoichiometric amount (like POX) while steam is also added (like SMR). The exothermic POX reaction provides *exactly* enough heat to drive the endothermic steam reforming in the same reactor. This makes ATR the most thermodynamically efficient of the three routes, and is the technology of choice for modern **GTL (gas-to-liquid)** plants such as Pearl GTL (Qatar).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: Define Partial Oxidation (POX) of hydrocarbons for hydrogen production. Write the stoichiometric reaction for the partial oxidation of methane.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Partial Oxidation (POX) is a thermochemical hydrogen production process in which a hydrocarbon feedstock reacts with a **sub-stoichiometric quantity of pure oxygen** (i.e., less than required for complete combustion) at high temperature to produce a mixture of hydrogen (H₂) and carbon monoxide (CO), commonly known as **syngas**. The CO is then catalytically converted to additional H₂ via the water-gas shift reaction.

The stoichiometric reaction for partial oxidation of methane is:

$$CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298K} = -36 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The reaction is mildly **exothermic** and the H₂:CO molar product ratio is 2:1.

*(Marking: 1 mark definition + 1 mark stoichiometric equation + 1 mark enthalpy & product ratio)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: List any three advantages of Partial Oxidation over Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) for hydrogen production.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Autothermal operation:** POX is exothermic (releases heat), so no external furnace is required, whereas SMR is highly endothermic and needs a large external heat source (~ 50% of total plant cost).

2. **Higher conversion per pass:** POX achieves > 95% CH₄ conversion in milliseconds, while SMR typically requires multiple reactor stages to exceed 90%.

3. **Smaller reactor footprint:** POX reactors are 5–10× smaller than SMR reformers for equivalent H₂ output, lowering capital expenditure.

4. *(Bonus — any one of the following for the third mark)*
   - Capable of handling **heavy hydrocarbons** (coal, residue oil, biomass) that SMR cannot easily process.
   - Faster startup/shutdown, suitable for **intermittent renewable-hydrogen** coupling.

*(Marking: 1 mark per correctly stated advantage, up to 3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. Students frequently write $CH_4 + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2$ as the "POX of methane" — **WRONG**, that is over-oxidized. The correct POX product is **CO, not CO₂**. Marks deducted: **-2**.
> 2. Students forget to include the **WGS reactor** in the process flow diagram. A POX plant without WGS gives only the *first-stage* H₂; the WGS is what boosts yield from 2 to 3 mol H₂/mol CH₄. Marks deducted: **-1 to -2**.
> 3. Students confuse **Catalytic POX (CPO)** with **Non-Catalytic (thermal) POX**. CPO uses noble-metal catalysts at 800–900 °C; thermal POX is flameless and operates at 1200–1500 °C. Marks deducted: **-1**.
> 4. Failing to state the role of the **Air Separation Unit (ASU)** for oxygen supply — students often assume air is used, which dilutes the syngas with inert N₂. Marks deducted: **-1**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat flow diagram, the Partial Oxidation (POX) method for hydrogen production from methane. Discuss the chemistry, operating conditions and the role of the Water-Gas Shift (WGS) reactor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is Catalytic Partial Oxidation (CPO)? Explain its mechanism on a noble metal catalyst and compare it with non-catalytic (thermal) POX. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — POX Flow Diagram & Chemistry (7 marks):**

The POX process converts methane (CH₄) into a hydrogen-rich syngas (H₂ + CO) through a controlled, oxygen-limited combustion.

**Process Steps:**

1. **Feed preparation:** Methane (or natural gas) is preheated to 300–400 °C in a feed preheater. Pure oxygen (≥ 95 mol %) is supplied from a cryogenic Air Separation Unit (ASU). Air is *not* used because the inert nitrogen would dilute the syngas and waste compression work.

2. **Reaction:** The preheated methane and oxygen are mixed at the burner head of the POX reactor in an oxygen-to-carbon (O/C) molar ratio of **0.5** (i.e., 50% of stoichiometric). The reaction occurs at **1200–1500 °C** (thermal POX) or **800–1000 °C** (CPO):

$$CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \Delta H = -36 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

3. **Heat recovery:** The hot reactor effluent (1200+ °C) passes through a waste-heat boiler, generating high-pressure steam (used to drive plant turbines or supply the WGS reactor).

4. **Soot scrubbing:** Particulates and unburned carbon are removed in a water-quench scrubber.

5. **Water-Gas Shift (WGS):** The syngas, now at ~ 350 °C, enters a two-stage WGS reactor:
   - **High-Temperature Shift (HTS)** at 350–450 °C over an Fe/Cr catalyst: $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$
   - **Low-Temperature Shift (LTS)** at 200–250 °C over a Cu/Zn/Al catalyst (to push equilibrium toward H₂).

6. **CO₂ removal & PSA purification:** CO₂ is absorbed in a Monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent; final H₂ purification (99.99%) is achieved by Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA). Tail gas from PSA is recycled as fuel.

**Role of WGS Reactor (3 marks specifically):**
The WGS reactor converts the **CO co-product** of POX into *additional* H₂. Without WGS, the H₂ yield is only 2 mol per mol CH₄. With WGS, it increases to **3 mol per mol CH₄**, a **50% improvement** in H₂ productivity. It also removes CO (a catalyst poison for many downstream applications like PEM fuel cells).

*(Marking: 2 marks flow diagram + 3 marks reactions + 1 mark operating conditions + 1 mark WGS role)*

---

**Part (b) — Catalytic Partial Oxidation (CPO) (7 marks):**

Catalytic Partial Oxidation (CPO) is a sub-variant of POX in which the homogeneous gas-phase combustion of thermal POX is replaced by **heterogeneous surface reactions on a solid catalyst**, typically a **noble metal** (Pt, Rh, Pd, Ru) or a **transition metal** (Ni) supported on a ceramic monolith (e.g., $\alpha$-Al₂O₃ or MgAl₂O₄ foam).

**CPO Mechanism on Noble-Metal Surface (5-step pathway):**

1. **Adsorption:** CH₄ and O₂ molecules adsorb onto the metal surface.
2. **O₂ dissociation:** $O_2 \rightarrow 2O^*$
3. **Methane dehydrogenation:** $CH_4 \rightarrow CH_x^* + (4-x)H^*$ (x = 0 to 3)
4. **Surface oxidation:** $CH_x^* + O^* \rightarrow CO^* + (x/2)H_2^*$
5. **Desorption:** $CO^* \rightarrow CO_{(g)}$, $H_2^* \rightarrow H_{2(g)}$

**Comparison Table: CPO vs. Thermal POX (4 marks specifically):**

| **Parameter** | **Thermal POX** | **Catalytic POX (CPO)** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Operating temperature | 1200 – 1500 °C | 800 – 1000 °C |
| Catalyst required | None | Pt, Rh, Pd, Ni on ceramic |
| Residence time | 1 – 5 seconds | 10 – 100 milliseconds |
| Reactor material | Refractory-lined steel | Metallic monolith with washcoat |
| Soot formation | Significant (heavy feedstocks) | Minimal |
| H₂ selectivity | 85 – 90 % | 90 – 95 % |
| Capital cost | High (refractory + burner) | Moderate (precious metal catalyst) |
| Suitable feedstocks | Heavy oils, coal, biomass | Light gases (CH₄, natural gas) |

*(Marking: 2 marks definition + 3 marks mechanism + 2 marks comparison)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]

**Q: (a)** Compare Partial Oxidation (POX), Steam Methane Reforming (SMR), and Autothermal Reforming (ATR) for hydrogen production. Discuss the heat balance, H₂ yield, and feedstock flexibility of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the key process parameters (O/C ratio, S/C ratio, temperature, pressure) that affect the performance of a POX reactor. How is the equivalence ratio (λ) defined and what is its optimal range for H₂ maximization? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Comparison of POX, SMR, ATR (7 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **POX** | **SMR** | **ATR** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Main reaction | $CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2$ | $CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$ | Combined POX + SMR |
| Heat balance | **Exothermic** (releases 36 kJ/mol) | **Endothermic** (needs 206 kJ/mol) | **Thermoneutral** (balanced) |
| External heating | None | Yes (fired furnace) | None |
| H₂:CO ratio | 2:1 | 3:1 | 2.5:1 (tunable) |
| Theoretical H₂ yield (with WGS) | 3 mol/mol CH₄ | 4 mol/mol CH₄ | 3.5–4 mol/mol CH₄ |
| Feedstock flexibility | Excellent (heavy oils, coal, biomass, methanol) | Limited (light gases only) | Good (light + medium feeds) |
| Operating temperature | 1200–1500 °C (thermal) / 800–1000 °C (CPO) | 700–900 °C | 900–1100 °C |
| Capital cost | Moderate | High (furnace + reformer) | Moderate–High |
| Commercial examples | Texaco, Shell gasifiers | Most global H₂ plants | Topsoe ATR, Haldor Topsøe |

**Key takeaways for KTU answer (3 marks):**
- POX is the *most feedstock-flexible* and *most autothermal* (no furnace).
- SMR has the *highest H₂ yield* (4 mol/mol CH₄) and dominates global H₂ production (~ 80% of merchant H₂).
- ATR is the *best compromise*, used in modern GTL plants (Pearl GTL, Qatar).

*(Marking: 4 marks comparison table + 3 marks key insights)*

---

**Part (b) — Process Parameters Affecting POX Performance (7 marks):**

**1. Oxygen-to-Carbon ratio (O/C) (2 marks):**
The single most critical parameter. Increasing O/C raises temperature and conversion but *reduces H₂ selectivity* by over-oxidizing CO to CO₂. Optimal O/C for methane POX is **0.5–0.6** (corresponding to the half-stoichiometric requirement).

**2. Steam-to-Carbon ratio (S/C) (2 marks):**
Adding steam suppresses coking (Boudouard reaction) and enhances H₂ via in-situ WGS. However, steam dilutes the syngas and requires energy to vaporize water. Optimal S/C is **0.5–2.0** for POX; in pure ATR, S/C ≈ 2.5–3.0.

**3. Temperature (1.5 marks):**
Higher temperatures (> 1200 °C thermal POX) favor H₂ formation thermodynamically (Le Chatelier's principle: endothermic reforming is favored). However, materials of construction limit practical ceiling to ~ 1500 °C for refractory-lined reactors.

**4. Pressure (1.5 marks):**
Higher pressure *inhibits* POX (it produces more moles of gas, so by Le Chatelier, high P shifts equilibrium backward). POX therefore favors **low-to-moderate pressure (1–30 bar)**, although some modern plants operate at 60–80 bar by accepting lower per-pass conversion in exchange for downstream compression savings.

**Equivalence Ratio (λ) — Definition & Optimum (bonus points):**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{(O/C)_{actual}}{(O/C)_{stoichiometric, complete\ combustion}}
$$

- $\lambda = 1.0$: complete combustion (no H₂ produced)
- $\lambda = 0$: no oxygen (pure thermal cracking, mostly coking)
- **Optimal for H₂ maximization: $\lambda = 0.20$ to $0.35$** (i.e., 20–35% of stoichiometric O₂)

Outside this window, either soot/coke formation ($\lambda < 0.20$) or H₂ loss to H₂O ($\lambda > 0.35$) dominates.

*(Marking: 2 marks O/C + 2 marks S/C + 1.5 marks T + 1.5 marks P + bonus 1 mark for λ definition)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Summary — Pin This Before Every Exam**

- ✅ **Partial Oxidation (POX)** uses **sub-stoichiometric O₂** (≈ 50% of full combustion) to convert hydrocarbons into **syngas (H₂ + CO)**.
- ✅ **Methane POX master equation:** $CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2$, $\Delta H = -36$ kJ/mol (**exothermic, autothermal**).
- ✅ **Theoretical H₂ yield:** **2 mol/mol CH₄ from POX alone; 3 mol/mol CH₄ with WGS**; 4 mol/mol CH₄ for SMR+WGS.
- ✅ **Operating temperature:** 1200–1500 °C (thermal POX), 800–1000 °C (Catalytic POX/CPO).
- ✅ **Operating pressure:** 1–80 bar (lower is better thermodynamically; higher saves compression cost).
- ✅ **Equivalence ratio (λ) optimum:** **0.20–0.35**.
- ✅ **O/C optimum:** **0.5–0.7**; **S/C optimum:** **0.5–2.0**.
- ✅ **Oxygen source:** Cryogenic **ASU** or **VPSA** (≥ 95 mol % purity); **never use air** directly.
- ✅ **Catalysts (CPO):** Pt, Rh, Pd, Ru (noble metals) or Ni (transition metal) on ceramic monolith.
- ✅ **Thermal efficiency:** **70–80%** in practice, **~ 90%** theoretical upper bound.
- ✅ **Key downstream reactor:** **Two-stage Water-Gas Shift (HTS + LTS)** boosts H₂ yield by ≈ 50%.
- ✅ **Final H₂ purification:** **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** to ≥ 99.99% purity.
- ✅ **POX vs. SMR vs. ATR:** POX = autothermal + feedstock-flexible; SMR = highest yield + endothermic; ATR = balanced hybrid (used in modern GTL).
- ✅ **Major industrial users:** Refineries (largest), ammonia/fertilizer plants, GTL/diesel plants, H₂ refueling stations.
- ✅ **Major coking reactions to remember:** $2CO \rightarrow C + CO_2$ (Boudouard), $CH_4 \rightarrow C + 2H_2$ (methane cracking).
- ✅ **Critical examination trap:** Do *not* confuse POX with **complete combustion** — POX yields **CO**, not CO₂!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photo Electro Chemical Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Photo Electro Chemical Method # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Photo Electro Chemical (PEC) Method** is a direct solar-to-hydrogen conversion process in which sunlight is absorbed by a **semiconductor photoelectrode** immersed in an aqueous electrolyte, generating electron-hole pairs that drive the **non-spontaneous water-splitting reaction** ($H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2$) into its constituent gaseous products without the need for an external power source. The overall thermodynamic cell reaction is: $$H_2O_{(l)} \longrightarrow H_{2(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} \quad \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = +237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ This is the **electrolysis of water driven entirely by photons** — a hybrid of photochemistry and electrochemistry occurring at the semiconductor–electrolyte interface (the **Helmholtz double layer**). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight — KTU Module 2 Context** > The PEC method is classified under *"Biological & Photo-assisted Hydrogen Production Routes"*. It is fundamentally different from conventional photovoltaic-electrolysis (PV-E) because the **photovoltaic conversion and electrochemical reaction occur on a single, integrated semiconductor surface**, eliminating the need for separate solar cells, wiring, and external electrolyzers. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Leaf" of Hydrogen Imagine a **lotus leaf floating on a pond**. In nature, a similar process occurs in **plant photosynthesis** — chlorophyll absorbs sunlight, water molecules are split, and oxygen is released while energy-rich carbohydrates are produced. The **PEC cell is the artificial chemical cousin of this natural leaf**. Replace chlorophyll with a **semiconductor coating** (like $TiO_2$), the pond with an **aqueous electrolyte**, and the carbohydrate with **molecular hydrogen**. The leaf's "magic" of converting sunshine + water into stored chemical energy is replicated — but in a form that yields a clean, storable fuel ($H_2$). > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook:** PEC = **P**hoto (sunlight) + **E**lectro (charge transfer) + **C**hemical (water splitting). Think of it as a "solar leaf for hydrogen." --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Value | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s | | Speed of light | $c$ | $3.0 \times 10^8$ | m/s | | Elementary charge | $e$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C | | Standard Gibbs free energy for water splitting | $\Delta G^{\circ}$ | **+237.2** | kJ/mol | | Standard cell potential | $E^{\circ}$ | **1.23** | V | | Enthalpy change (HHV basis) | $\Delta H^{\circ}$ | +285.8 | kJ/mol | | Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ | /mol | | Solar constant (AM 1.5G) | $P_{in}$ | **1000** | W/m² | | Faraday constant | $F$ | 96485 | C/mol | --- ## 1.4 Historical & Strategic Context - **Honda & Fujishima (1972):** Discovered that $TiO_2$ (a wide-bandgap semiconductor) illuminated with UV light could split water — the foundational experiment. - **Fujishima later (2017):** Awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for work on photocatalytic water splitting. - PEC lies at the intersection of **material science, electrochemistry, and renewable energy engineering**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle The PEC water-splitting reaction proceeds through the following sequential steps: - **Step 1 — Photon Absorption:** A photon with energy $E_{ph} \geq E_g$ strikes the semiconductor electrode and is absorbed, promoting an electron from the **valence band (VB)** to the **conduction band (CB)**, leaving behind a hole. $$h\nu \geq E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^-_{CB} + h^+_{VB}$$ - **Step 2 — Charge Separation:** The electron-hole pair (exciton) is separated by the **built-in electric field** at the semiconductor–electrolyte junction (analogous to a p-n junction). - **Step 3 — Surface Redox Reactions:** - **At the Photoanode (n-type):** Water is oxidized by holes: $$2H_2O + 4h^+ \longrightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ \quad \quad E^{\circ}_{ox} = +1.23 \text{ V vs. NHE}$$ - **At the Cathode (Pt or p-type):** Protons are reduced by electrons: $$4H^+ + 4e^- \longrightarrow 2H_2 \quad \quad E^{\circ}_{red} = 0.00 \text{ V vs. NHE}$$ - **Step 4 — Gas Evolution:** $H_2$ bubbles evolve at the cathode surface; $O_2$ bubbles evolve at the photoanode. --- ## 2.2 The "Why" — Thermodynamic & Kinetic Justification ### Why the Bandgap Must Exceed 1.23 eV The minimum theoretical bandgap equals the **free-energy potential** of water splitting: $$E_g \geq E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.23 \text{ V}$$ However, due to: - **Overpotential losses** ($\eta_{anode}$, $\eta_{cathode}$), - **Charge-carrier recombination**, - **Kinetic activation barriers**, - **Band-edge misalignment** with redox potentials, the **practical minimum bandgap** is found to be: $$E_g^{practical} \approx 1.6 - 2.4 \text{ eV}$$ This is why $TiO_2$ ($E_g = 3.2$ eV) only absorbs UV (~5% of solar spectrum), whereas $Fe_2O_3$ ($E_g = 2.1$ eV) absorbs ~40%. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU board-exam ready. Master the units and the boundary conditions.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{photon} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ | Photon energy from wavelength | eV | | 2 | $E_g \geq 1.23$ eV | Theoretical minimum bandgap | eV | | 3 | $E_g^{practical} \approx 1.6 - 2.4$ eV | Engineering minimum (accounting for losses) | eV | | 4 | $\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ} \times r_{H_2}}{P_{in} \times A}$ | Solar-to-Hydrogen conversion efficiency | % | | 5 | $\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times 1.23 \times \eta_F}{P_{in}}$ | STH efficiency via photocurrent | % | | 6 | $\eta_{ABPE} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times (1.23 - V_{bias})}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Applied Bias Photocurrent Efficiency | % | | 7 | $\eta_{IPCE}(\lambda) = \dfrac{1240 \times J_{ph}(\lambda)}{P_{mono}(\lambda) \times \lambda}$ | Incident Photon-to-Current Efficiency | % | | 8 | $\eta_F = \dfrac{n_{H_2,produced}}{n_{e^- / 2 \cdot Q}} = \dfrac{2Fn_{H_2}}{Q}$ | Faradaic efficiency | % | | 9 | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{1240}{E_g}$ | Maximum absorption wavelength | nm | | 10 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = nFE^{\circ} = 2 \times F \times 1.23$ | Free energy check | J/mol | > [!NOTE] > **Convention:** $J_{ph}$ is the photocurrent density (mA/cm²), $P_{in}$ is the incident illumination power density (mW/cm²), and $\eta_F$ is the Faradaic efficiency. --- ## 2.4 Cell Architecture Variants (Engineering Utility) | Configuration | Description | Advantage | |---|---|---| | **Wired (tandem)** | Two photoelectrodes connected by wire | Highest demonstrated STH efficiency (~19.3%) | | **Wireless (photochemical diode)** | n- and p-type electrodes in monolithic Z-scheme | No wiring, scalable | | **Particle-based (powder suspension)** | Photocatalyst particles dispersed in water | Lowest cost, easiest scale-up | | **PV-electrolysis hybrid** | Separate PV cell + electrolyzer stack | Decoupled design flexibility | --- ## 2.5 Common Photoelectrode Materials (KTU High-Yield) | Material | Type | $E_g$ (eV) | Key Property | |---|---|---|---| | $TiO_2$ (anatase) | n-type | 3.2 | Stable, UV only | | $\alpha\text{-}Fe_2O_3$ (hematite) | n-type | 2.1 | Abundant, visible light | | $BiVO_4$ | n-type | 2.4 | High photocurrent | | $CdS$ | n-type | 2.4 | Excellent visible absorption | | $Cu_2O$ | p-type | 2.0 | Good for photocathode | | $GaInP_2$ | p-type | 1.83 | High efficiency tandem | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Photon Energy vs. Wavelength The energy of a single photon is given by Planck's relation: $$E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$ Substituting the constants $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c = 3.0 \times 10^8$ m/s: $$E_{photon} = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.0 \times 10^8)}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{\lambda \text{ (in m)}} \text{ J}$$ Converting to **electron-volts** (divide by $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$): $$E_{photon} = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot \lambda (\text{m})} \text{ eV} = \dfrac{1.240 \times 10^{-6}}{\lambda(\text{m})} \text{ eV}$$ Substituting $\lambda$ in **nanometers** ($\lambda_{nm} = \lambda_m \times 10^9$): $$E_{photon} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})} \text{ eV}$$ > **Result:** A photon at 500 nm carries $E = 1240/500 = 2.48$ eV, which is sufficient to excite electrons across the bandgap of $BiVO_4$ (2.4 eV) but not $TiO_2$ (3.2 eV). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Absorption Wavelength from Bandgap For a photon to be absorbed by a semiconductor, its energy must equal or exceed the bandgap: $$\dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{max}} = E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_{max} = \dfrac{1240}{E_g}$$ | Material | $E_g$ (eV) | $\lambda_{max}$ (nm) | Solar Spectrum Capture | |---|---|---|---| | $TiO_2$ | 3.2 | 387 | UV only (~4%) | | $\alpha\text{-}Fe_2O_3$ | 2.1 | 590 | Visible (~40%) | | $BiVO_4$ | 2.4 | 517 | Visible (~30%) | | $Si$ | 1.12 | 1107 | Broad (~80%) | --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Solar-to-Hydrogen (STH) Efficiency By definition, STH efficiency is the ratio of chemical energy stored in hydrogen to the total incident solar energy: $$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{\text{Chemical Energy Output}}{\text{Solar Energy Input}} \times 100\%$$ **Step 1:** Energy stored per mole of $H_2$ produced equals $\Delta G^{\circ}$: $$E_{chem} = \Delta G^{\circ} = 237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}$$ **Step 2:** For a production rate of $r_{H_2}$ (mol/s) under illumination area $A$ (m²): $$\text{Energy output} = r_{H_2} \cdot \Delta G^{\circ} \quad (\text{W})$$ **Step 3:** Solar input under AM 1.5G standard: $$P_{in} = 1000 \cdot A \quad (\text{W})$$ **Step 4:** Combining: $$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{r_{H_2} \cdot \Delta G^{\circ}}{P_{in} \cdot A}$$ Now expressing $r_{H_2}$ in terms of photocurrent. From Faraday's law, the molar production rate is: $$r_{H_2} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \cdot A}{n \cdot F}$$ For water splitting, $n = 2$ electrons per $H_2$ molecule: $$r_{H_2} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \cdot A}{2F}$$ Substituting back and noting $\Delta G^{\circ} = nFE^{\circ} = 2F \times 1.23$: $$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{J_{ph} A}{2F}\right) \cdot 2F \cdot 1.23 \cdot \eta_F}{P_{in} \cdot A} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times 1.23 \times \eta_F}{P_{in}}$$ > **Final Form:** $$\boxed{\eta_{STH}(\%) = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times 1.23 \times \eta_F}{P_{in}} \times 100}$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) > **Problem:** A PEC cell with hematite photoanode produces a short-circuit photocurrent density of $J_{ph} = 2.5$ mA/cm² under AM 1.5G illumination ($P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm²). The Faradaic efficiency is 95%. Calculate the STH efficiency. **Solution:** Given: - $J_{ph} = 2.5$ mA/cm² $= 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ A/cm² - $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm² $= 0.1$ W/cm² - $\eta_F = 0.95$ Apply the STH formula: $$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times 1.23 \times \eta_F}{P_{in}}$$ $$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1.23 \times 0.95}{0.1}$$ $$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{2.921 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1}$$ $$\eta_{STH} = 0.02921 = 2.92\%$$ > **Answer:** $\eta_{STH} \approx 2.92\%$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Applied Bias Photocurrent Efficiency (ABPE) When an external bias $V_{bias}$ is applied (typical in half-cell measurements): $$\eta_{ABPE} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times (E^{\circ}_{rev} - \vert V_{bias} \vert)}{P_{in}} \times 100$$ This metric quantifies the **useful electrical work** delivered by the photoelectrode after subtracting the externally-supplied bias energy. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PEC Water-Splitting Cell — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sunlight
AM 1.5G

hν] end subgraph SEMICONDUCTOR["SEMICONDUCTOR STAGE"] S1[Absorption
Bandgap Eg] S2[Exciton

Separation] S4[Bulk and Surface
Recombination Losses] end subgraph INTERFACE["ELECTRODE ELECTROLYTE INTERFACE"] F1[Helmholtz Layer
Band Bending] F2[Charge Transfer
to Redox Species] end subgraph ELECTROCHEMISTRY["REACTION STAGE"] R1[Anode Reaction
2H2O plus 4h+ → O2 plus 4H+] R2[Cathode Reaction
4H+ plus 4e- → 2H2] R3[Overall: H2O → H2 plus 1/2 O2] end subgraph OUTPUT["PRODUCT STAGE"] O1[Hydrogen Gas
H2] O2[Oxygen Gas
O2] end I1 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S3 --> F1 F1 --> F2 F2 --> R1 F2 --> R2 R1 --> R3 R2 --> R3 R3 --> O1 R3 --> O2 style I1 fill:#fff4b3,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style S1 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 style R1 fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#009933,color:#000 style R2 fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#009933,color:#000 style R3 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style O1 fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000 style O2 fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Bandgap Selection Logic — Decision Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Semiconductor
for PEC Cell] --> B{Is Eg greater than
1.23 eV?} B -- No --> C[Reject
Cannot Drive Reaction] B -- Yes --> D{Covers Visible

Straddling H2O Levels?} F -- No --> G[Add External Bias
or Cocatalyst] F -- Yes --> H{Is Material
Stable in Electrolyte?} H -- No --> I[Apply Protective
Coating Layer] H -- Yes --> J[Optimal Candidate
Example: BiVO4 plus CoPi] style A fill:#fff4b3,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style E fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style G fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style I fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style J fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#009933,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define the Photo Electro Chemical (PEC) method of hydrogen production. State the minimum theoretical bandgap required for a semiconductor to drive the overall water-splitting reaction.** **Model Answer:** The **Photo Electro Chemical (PEC) method** is a sunlight-driven hydrogen production process in which a semiconductor photoelectrode, immersed in an aqueous electrolyte, absorbs photons and generates electron-hole pairs that split water into $H_2$ and $O_2$ at its surface, without requiring any external electrical energy input. The thermodynamic cell potential for the overall reaction $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2$ is **1.23 V**, which translates to a minimum required semiconductor bandgap of: $$E_g \geq 1.23 \text{ eV}$$ --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **Differentiate between a photoanode and a photocathode in a PEC cell. Give one example material for each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Photoanode | Photocathode | |---|---|---| | Semiconductor type | n-type | p-type | | Charge carrier driving reaction | Holes ($h^+$) | Electrons ($e^-$) | | Reaction at surface | Water oxidation: $2H_2O + 4h^+ \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+$ | Proton reduction: $4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2$ | | Product released | $O_2$ gas | $H_2$ gas | | Example material | $TiO_2$, $\alpha\text{-}Fe_2O_3$, $BiVO_4$ | $Cu_2O$, $Si$, $GaInP_2$ | --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** > A common mistake students make in PEC derivations is **forgetting to convert photocurrent units** (mA/cm² → A/cm²) before applying the STH formula. Another frequent error is using **1.23 V** without confirming whether the **NHE reference electrode** and **thermodynamic (not HHV)** basis are intended. Always state your assumptions explicitly. Marks are deducted for **dimensional inconsistency**. --- ### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a Photo Electro Chemical (PEC) cell for hydrogen production. Discuss the role of the semiconductor bandgap and band-edge positions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for Solar-to-Hydrogen (STH) conversion efficiency in terms of photocurrent density and incident power. A hematite-based PEC device delivers $J_{ph} = 3.2$ mA/cm² under 100 mW/cm² illumination with $\eta_F = 0.90$. Calculate its STH efficiency. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Working Principle of a PEC Cell (7 marks)** The PEC cell consists of an **n-type semiconductor photoanode** and a **metal (Pt) counter-electrode** (or a p-type photocathode) immersed in an aqueous electrolyte (typically NaOH or $H_2SO_4$). **Steps:** 1. **Photon Absorption:** Sunlight strikes the photoanode. Photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ are absorbed, exciting electrons from the valence band to the conduction band. 2. **Charge Separation:** The built-in potential at the semiconductor–electrolyte junction separates the photogenerated carriers, sweeping holes to the surface and electrons through the external circuit. 3. **Anodic Oxidation:** Holes oxidize water at the photoanode: $$2H_2O + 4h^+ \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+$$ 4. **Cathodic Reduction:** Electrons travel to the cathode and reduce protons: $$4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2$$ 5. **Role of Bandgap:** The bandgap $E_g$ must exceed 1.23 eV (thermodynamic minimum), and practically be 1.6–2.4 eV to overcome overpotential losses. The conduction band minimum must lie **above** the $H^+/H_2$ reduction potential (0 V vs NHE), and the valence band maximum must lie **below** the $O_2/H_2O$ oxidation potential (+1.23 V vs NHE). **(b) Derivation + Numerical (7 marks)** **Derivation:** As derived in Section 3.3, $$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times 1.23 \times \eta_F}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$ **Numerical:** Given: $J_{ph} = 3.2$ mA/cm² $= 3.2 \times 10^{-3}$ A/cm²; $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm² $= 0.1$ W/cm²; $\eta_F = 0.90$. $$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{(3.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1.23 \times 0.90}{0.1} \times 100\%$$ $$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{3.5424 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{STH} = 3.54\%}$$ --- ### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`** **(a)** List any four semiconductor materials used as photoelectrodes in PEC cells. Compare their bandgaps, light absorption ranges, and major drawbacks. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the major challenges limiting the commercial deployment of PEC hydrogen production? Suggest two engineering strategies to overcome them. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Semiconductor Materials (7 marks)** | Material | $E_g$ (eV) | Absorption Range | Drawback | |---|---|---|---| | $TiO_2$ (anatase) | 3.2 | UV only (<387 nm) | Captures only ~4% of solar spectrum | | $\alpha\text{-}Fe_2O_3$ (hematite) | 2.1 | Visible (~590 nm) | Poor charge transport; short hole diffusion length (~2–4 nm) | | $BiVO_4$ | 2.4 | Visible (~517 nm) | Slow water oxidation kinetics; needs cocatalyst (e.g., CoPi) | | $Cu_2O$ | 2.0 | Visible (~620 nm) | Photocorrodes rapidly in aqueous electrolyte | (Any four with relevant details accepted.) **(b) Major Challenges & Engineering Strategies (7 marks)** **Challenges:** 1. **Photocorrosion** of semiconductors (e.g., $Cu_2O$, CdS) in aqueous electrolytes. 2. **High recombination losses** of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. 3. **Slow oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics** at the photoanode surface. 4. **Mismatch between band-edge positions and water redox potentials**, requiring external bias. 5. **Low STH efficiency** in practice (<5% for most single-material systems). 6. **Material cost and scalability** (e.g., $GaInP_2$ is highly efficient but extremely expensive). **Engineering Strategies:** 1. **Protective coatings:** Deposit thin overlayers of amorphous $TiO_2$ or $NiFe$-layered double hydroxides (LDH) on unstable semiconductors to prevent photocorrosion while allowing hole tunneling for OER. 2. **Cocatalyst deposition:** Apply noble-metal or earth-abundant cocatalysts (e.g., $CoPi$, $NiMo$, $Pt$) on the electrode surface to lower activation overpotentials and accelerate the OER/HER kinetics. 3. **Tandem (Z-scheme) cell design:** Stack two semiconductors with complementary bandgaps (e.g., $BiVO_4$ on top, $Si$ underneath) to absorb a wider solar spectrum and provide sufficient photovoltage without external bias. 4. **Nanostructuring:** Use 1-D nanowires, nanorods, or 3-D inverse opals to shorten charge-carrier diffusion paths and increase surface area for reactions. (Any two strategies with adequate explanation accepted.) --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exams** - **PEC definition:** Semiconductor-electrolyte junction splitting water using sunlight only. - **Minimum theoretical bandgap:** **$E_g \geq 1.23$ eV**. - **Practical bandgap window:** **1.6 – 2.4 eV** (accounts for overpotential and kinetic losses). - **Thermodynamic cell potential:** $E^{\circ} = 1.23$ V; **HHV basis:** $E = 1.48$ V. - **Standard formula:** $\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{J_{ph} \times 1.23 \times \eta_F}{P_{in}}$ — **always quote in %**. - **Photon-energy shortcut:** $E_{photon}(\text{eV}) = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$. - **Honda–Fujishima experiment (1972):** $TiO_2$ + UV light + water → $H_2$ + $O_2$. - **Hematite ($Fe_2O_3$):** Most studied visible-light photoanode due to abundance and 2.1 eV bandgap. - **Main losses:** Recombination, overpotential, photocorrosion, narrow spectrum absorption. - **Best PEC efficiency on record:** ~19.3% (GaInP/GaInAs tandem with $RuO_x$ catalyst). - **KTU must-mention keywords:** Helmholtz double layer, band bending, exciton, OER, HER, Faradaic efficiency, ABPE, IPCE, STH, Z-scheme, cocatalyst.

topics.title: Thermo-Chemical Water Splitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermo-Chemical Water Splitting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermo-Chemical Water Splitting (TCWS)** is a closed-loop, multi-step chemical process that decomposes water ($H_2O$) into its constituent elements—hydrogen ($H_2$) and oxygen ($O_2$)—using **high-temperature heat** as the primary energy input, supplemented by a series of intermediate chemical reactions. The auxiliary reagents (other than water) are **regenerated and recycled** within the cycle, making it a theoretically zero-emission hydrogen production pathway.

The generic net reaction is:
$$H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{High-T Heat + Chemical Reactions}} H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course OEBBT616 specifically emphasizes **closed-cycle** thermochemical routes, where the sum of all intermediate reactions must mathematically collapse to the simple decomposition of water. This is the **figure of merit** (FOM) of any thermochemical cycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to demolish a brick wall (water molecule), but you only have a blowtorch (heat), not a sledgehammer (electricity). Direct heating of water to **>2000°C** would split it, but the materials required to contain such plasma-grade temperatures don't exist.

So, instead, you build a **relay team of chemicals (intermediate species)**:
1. Chemical **A** grabs the **oxygen** from water at a moderate temperature → forms compound **AO**.
2. Compound **AO** hands the oxygen to Chemical **B** at a higher temperature → forms compound **BO**.
3. Compound **BO** releases the oxygen as **O₂ gas** at an even higher temperature, regenerating **B**.
4. The whole relay starts over — net result: water was split using only heat!

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** This is precisely how the Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) cycle works using $I_2$, $SO_2$, and $H_2SO_4$ as the relay runners, with heat sourced from a **high-temperature nuclear reactor** (VHTR — Very High Temperature Reactor) or **concentrated solar power (CSP)** towers.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Gibbs Free Energy of Water Formation:** $\Delta G_f^\circ(H_2O, l) = -237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- **Enthalpy of Vaporization of Water at 25°C:** $\Delta H_{vap} = 40.65 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- **Direct Thermal Decomposition Temperature:** $T_{decomp} \approx 2500\text{–}3000 \text{ K}$
- **Practical Cycle Operating Range:** $800\text{–}1200 \text{ K}$ (enabling use of nuclear/solar heat)
- **Hydrogen Energy Density (LHV):** **120 MJ/kg**

> [!TIP]
> **Why Not Just Heat Water Directly?** At **2500 K**, only ~10% of water dissociates, and at 3000 K the materials science problem is intractable. Thermochemical cycles bypass this by using chemical intermediaries that react with water at much lower temperatures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Generic Thermo-Chemical Cycle

A thermochemical cycle must satisfy **three thermodynamic conditions** simultaneously:
1. **Net Reaction Equivalence:** Sum of all reactions = $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$
2. **Mass Conservation:** All intermediate species are recycled (closed loop).
3. **Energy Source:** The only external energy input is **heat (Q)** at temperatures $T_H$ (high) and $T_L$ (low).

### The Three Universal Reaction Steps:

**Step 1 — Endothermic Reduction (Highest Temperature Step):**
The metal oxide (or analogous species) is reduced by heat, releasing oxygen.
$$MO_{x} \xrightarrow{Q_{H}} MO_{x-\Delta} + \frac{\Delta}{2}O_2$$

**Step 2 — Intermediate Hydrolysis / Re-oxidation (Mid-Temperature):**
The reduced species reacts with water to release hydrogen.
$$MO_{x-\Delta} + H_2O \xrightarrow{} MO_x + H_2$$

**Step 3 — Recycle / Regeneration:**
Intermediate reagents (e.g., acids) are separated and recycled back to Step 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Step Separation:** The high-temperature step requires thermal energy to break strong M–O bonds. The lower-temperature step releases chemical potential energy. Splitting the task into steps allows each reaction to occur at a temperature where its **equilibrium constant is favorable**.

## 2.2 Classification of Thermo-Chemical Cycles

| Cycle Class | Operating Temp (K) | Number of Reactions | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-Temperature Cycles** | 500 – 900 | 3 – 5 | Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl) |
| **Medium-Temperature Cycles** | 900 – 1200 | 2 – 3 | Iron-Oxide, Cerium-Oxide |
| **High-Temperature Cycles** | 1000 – 1300 | 3 – 4 | Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) |
| **Hybrid Cycles** | 800 – 1000 | 2 + 1 electrolysis | Cu-Cl Hybrid, S-I Hybrid |
| **Two-Step Metal-Oxide** | > 1500 | 2 | Zn/ZnO, Fe₃O₄/FeO |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All mathematical symbols below use $\vert$ for absolute value to preserve markdown table integrity. No vertical pipes will be used inside table cells.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Net Reaction (Universal) | $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ | $\Delta H^\circ = +285.83 \text{ kJ/mol}$ |
| 2 | Gibbs Free Energy Change | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | $\Delta G^\circ = +237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$ at 298 K |
| 3 | Equilibrium Constant | $K = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Carnot Heat Engine Limit | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | The absolute upper efficiency bound |
| 5 | Second-Law Efficiency | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{\Delta G_{H_2O}}{\eta_{Carnot} \cdot Q_{in}}$ | Must be $\le 1$ |
| 6 | Practical Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{cycle} = \dfrac{LHV_{H_2}}{Q_{in,tot}}$ | Typical S-I: 40–52% |
| 7 | Figure of Merit (FOM) | $FOM = \dfrac{\text{H}_2 \text{ produced (mol)}}{\text{Heat input (kJ)}}$ | Higher is better |
| 8 | Heat Required (Theoretical) | $Q_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{\eta_{Carnot}}$ | kJ per mol H₂ |
| 9 | S-I Cycle Bunsen Reaction | $I_2 + SO_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4 + 2HI$ | Exothermic (~75°C) |
| 10 | S-I Cycle Sulfuric Acid Decomposition | $H_2SO_4 \rightarrow H_2O + SO_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ | Endothermic (~850°C) |
| 11 | S-I Cycle HI Decomposition | $2HI \rightarrow H_2 + I_2$ | Endothermic (~450°C) |
| 12 | Zn/ZnO Two-Step | $ZnO \rightarrow Zn + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ (above 2000°C) | Solar-only viable |
| 13 | Cu-Cl Cycle Net | $2CuCl_2 + H_2O \rightarrow Cu_2OCl_2 + 2HCl$ | Mid-temp (530°C max) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Cycle Used | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| **Nuclear Hydrogen Production (Generation IV VHTR)** | S-I, Cu-Cl | S-I pairs perfectly with 900–950°C reactor outlet |
| **Solar Thermochemical Hydrogen (Solar Tower)** | Zn/ZnO, CeO₂/CeO | Two-step cycles match concentrated solar flux |
| **Industrial-Scale Pilot Plants** | S-I (e.g., JAEA, General Atomics) | Highest demonstrated TRL |
| **Low-Grade Waste Heat (500–600°C)** | Cu-Cl | Lower max temperature makes it compatible with current plants |
| **Hybrid Grid Storage** | Cu-Cl Hybrid + PEM Electrolyzer | Bridges electrolysis + thermochemistry |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight for KTU 2024:** The S-I cycle is the **most-examined** cycle in KTU papers because it is the only one to have operated as a closed-loop pilot plant. Always cite the **General Atomics GA cycle** (USA) and **JAEA cycle** (Japan) as industrial benchmarks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Verifying the Net Reaction of the Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) Cycle

The S-I cycle (also called the **General Atomics cycle** or **Ispra Mark cycle**) has three principal reactions. We must show that the linear sum collapses to water splitting.

### Step 1: Write Each Reaction Explicitly

**Bunsen Reaction (Exothermic, ~120°C):**
$$I_2 + SO_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2HI + H_2SO_4 \quad \quad (R_1)$$

**Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (Endothermic, ~850°C):**
$$H_2SO_4 \rightarrow H_2O + SO_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \quad \quad (R_2)$$

**Hydrogen Iodide Decomposition (Endothermic, ~450°C):**
$$2HI \rightarrow H_2 + I_2 \quad \quad (R_3)$$

### Step 2: Sum the Three Reactions

Adding $R_1 + R_2 + R_3$ term-by-term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS:} \quad & I_2 + SO_2 + 2H_2O + H_2SO_4 + 2HI \\
\text{RHS:} \quad & 2HI + H_2SO_4 + H_2O + SO_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2 + I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Cancel Recycled Species

Cancel $I_2$ (appears on both sides), $SO_2$ (both sides), $2HI$ (both sides), and $H_2SO_4$ (both sides).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Remaining LHS:} \quad & 2H_2O \\
\text{Remaining RHS:} \quad & H_2O + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify to Net Reaction

Subtracting $H_2O$ from both sides yields:
$$H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \quad \quad \blacksquare$$

> **Interpretation:** The cycle is internally consistent. The **only** net inputs are water and high-temperature heat; the only net outputs are hydrogen and oxygen. All other species are perfectly recycled.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Minimum Heat Input (Carnot-Limited)

For any thermochemical cycle, the **minimum heat input** $Q_{min}$ is bounded by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** because the cycle must drive the non-spontaneous reaction $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ ($\Delta G^\circ = +237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$ at 298 K).

### Step 1: Apply Second Law to Heat Engine

A reversible heat engine operating between a hot source at $T_H$ and cold sink at $T_L$ has Carnot efficiency:
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

### Step 2: Set Up Energy Balance

The minimum useful work output (which must equal $\Delta G$ of water splitting for a reversible process) is:
$$W_{min} = \Delta G^\circ = 237.13 \text{ kJ/mol H}_2$$

### Step 3: Express Heat Input in Terms of Carnot Efficiency

By definition of efficiency:
$$\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{W_{min}}{Q_{min}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{min} = \frac{W_{min}}{\eta_{Carnot}}$$

### Step 4: Substitute

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{min} &= \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}} \\
&= \frac{237.13}{1 - \frac{298}{T_H}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Evaluate at Typical VHTR Temperature ($T_H = 1273$ K, $T_L = 298$ K)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Carnot} &= 1 - \frac{298}{1273} = 0.7658 \\
Q_{min} &= \frac{237.13}{0.7658} = 309.7 \text{ kJ/mol H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Convert to Energy per kg of Hydrogen

Since 1 kg $H_2$ = 500 mol:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{min, \text{per kg}} &= 309.7 \times 500 = 1.548 \times 10^5 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
&= 154.8 \text{ MJ/kg H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with LHV:** The LHV of $H_2$ is **120 MJ/kg**. So the theoretical minimum heat input ($154.8$ MJ/kg) is **higher** than the chemical energy obtained! This is because some heat is *rejected* to the cold sink — the cycle is a heat engine, not a converter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Efficiency of the Two-Step Zn/ZnO Cycle

### Step 1: Identify the Two Reactions

**Solar Reduction (Endothermic, $T > 2000$ K, solar concentrated):**
$$ZnO \rightarrow Zn_{(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \quad \quad (R_1')$$

**Hydrolysis / Oxidation (Exothermic, $T \approx 700$ K):**
$$Zn + H_2O \rightarrow ZnO + H_2 \quad \quad (R_2')$$

### Step 2: Verify Net Reaction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum: } \quad & ZnO + Zn + H_2O \rightarrow Zn_{(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_2 + ZnO + H_2 \\
\text{Cancel } ZnO: \quad & Zn + H_2O \rightarrow Zn_{(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Hmm — the Zn in the gaseous product must be condensed. The actual net requires separation of $O_2$ and quenching. The idealized net becomes:
$$H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

### Step 3: Calculate Solar-to-Hydrogen Efficiency

For a solar reactor at $T_{solar} = 2300$ K with concentration ratio $C$:

$$
\eta_{STH} = \eta_{abs} \cdot \eta_{Carnot}(T_{solar}, T_{amb}) \cdot \eta_{utilization}
$$

where:

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_{amb}}{T_{solar}} = 1 - \frac{300}{2300} = 0.870
$$

If $\eta_{abs} = 0.85$ and $\eta_{utilization} = 0.45$ (fraction of solar energy absorbed that drives the endothermic reaction):

$$
\eta_{STH} = 0.85 \times 0.870 \times 0.45 = 0.333 = 33.3\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Best demonstrated $\eta_{STH}$ for the Zn/ZnO cycle (ETH Zurich / Solar Reactor Demo) is approximately **29–36%**, which already exceeds the best photovoltaic-electrolysis cascade ($\approx 24\%$). This makes the two-step cycle the **front-runner** for solar hydrogen at scale.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equilibrium Constant of Sulfuric Acid Decomposition

For $R_2: H_2SO_4 \rightarrow H_2O + SO_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ at high temperature, we need to know the extent of conversion. Using the van't Hoff equation:

### Step 1: Write the Equilibrium Expression

$$K_p = \frac{p_{H_2O} \cdot p_{SO_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}{p_{H_2SO_4}}$$

### Step 2: Apply Gibbs-Helmholtz

$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$$

### Step 3: Use Tabulated Values at 1100 K

Using standard thermodynamic data: $\Delta H^\circ \approx +98$ kJ/mol, $\Delta S^\circ \approx +228$ J/(mol·K):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln K_{1100} &= -\frac{98000}{8.314 \times 1100} + \frac{228}{8.314} \\
&= -10.72 + 27.42 = 16.70 \\
K_{1100} &= e^{16.70} \approx 1.79 \times 10^7
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** At 1100 K, the equilibrium constant is enormous, meaning $H_2SO_4$ is **almost completely decomposed**. This is precisely why the S-I cycle targets sulfuric acid decomposition at $\sim 1100$ K — the thermodynamics are overwhelmingly favorable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Thermo-Chemical Water Splitting Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["H2O Input (Liquid)"] --> B["Step 1: Bunsen / Endothermic Reaction (T_medium)"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Acid Decomposition (T_high, 800-1100°C)"]
    C --> D["O2 Output (Released)"]
    C --> E["Step 3: Hydride / Reduced Species Decomposition (T_medium)"]
    E --> F["H2 Output (Released)"]
    E --> G["Recycle Stream: Intermediate Species Recovery"]
    G --> B
    H["High-Temperature Heat Source (Nuclear VHTR or Solar CSP)"] -. Q_H .-> C
    I["Low-Temperature Heat Sink (Cooling Tower / Boiler)"] -. Q_L .-> E
    J["Work Input (Pumps, Separation)"] -. W .-> B
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Detailed Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) Cycle Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: H2O, I2, SO2"] --> B["Bunsen Reactor (120°C)"]
    B --> C["H2SO4 + 2HI Mixture"]
    C --> D["Separator (Liquid-Liquid)"]
    D --> E["H2SO4 Side Stream"]
    D --> F["HI Side Stream"]
    E --> G["H2SO4 Vaporizer + Decomposer (850°C)"]
    G --> H["SO2 + H2O + 0.5 O2"]
    H --> I["Purification: SO2 Recycle"]
    I --> J["OUTPUT: O2 (Pure)"]
    I --> A
    F --> K["HI Concentrator (Distillation)"]
    K --> L["HI Decomposer (450°C)"]
    L --> M["H2 + I2"]
    M --> N["Separator"]
    N --> O["OUTPUT: H2 (Pure)"]
    N --> P["I2 Recycle"]
    P --> A
    Q["External Heat: Nuclear or Solar (900-1000°C)"] -.provides QH.-> G
    R["Cooling Water / Heat Rejection"] -.provides QL.-> K
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Two-Step Metal-Oxide (Zn/ZnO) Cycle for Solar Hydrogen

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Concentrated Solar Radiation (C > 2000 suns)"] --> B["Solar Receiver (T > 2000 K)"]
    B --> C["Step 1: Thermal Reduction of ZnO"]
    C --> D["Zn gas + 0.5 O2"]
    D --> E["Quench & Condensation of Zn"]
    D --> F["O2 Output (Vent / Sequester)"]
    E --> G["Step 2: Hydrolysis Reactor (T ~ 700 K)"]
    G --> H["ZnO + H2"]
    H --> I["Separator"]
    I --> J["OUTPUT: H2 (Pure, Pressurized)"]
    I --> K["ZnO Recycle (Solid)"]
    K --> B
    L["Quench Heat Recovery (HRSG)"] -.powers.-> G
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a **process unit** (reactor, separator, or heat exchanger). The dashed arrows represent **energy flows** ($Q_H$ for high-grade heat, $Q_L$ for rejected heat, $W$ for mechanical work), while the solid arrows represent **material streams**. The closed loop of intermediate species ($I_2$, $SO_2$, $ZnO$, $H_2SO_4$, $HI$) is the defining feature of any thermochemical cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
