# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**"Define thermochemical water splitting. List the two main reasons why it is preferred over direct thermal decomposition of water at 3000 K."** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Thermochemical water splitting is a multi-step chemical process that uses a series of intermediate reactions driven by heat to decompose water into $H_2$ and $O_2$, where the intermediate chemicals are recycled in a closed loop. Net reaction: $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$.

- **Reason 1 (0.75 Marks):** Direct thermal decomposition of water requires temperatures above **2500–3000 K**, which exceeds the operational limits of all known structural materials for sustained industrial operation.

- **Reason 2 (0.75 Marks):** The conversion efficiency of direct thermal decomposition is very low (only $\approx 10\%$ even at 3000 K), whereas thermochemical cycles achieve practical efficiencies of **40–55%** at 800–1100 K by leveraging favorable reaction equilibria at intermediate temperatures.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**"Explain the role of the Bunsen reaction in the Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) thermochemical cycle. Write the balanced equation."** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Role (2 Marks):** The Bunsen reaction is the **low-temperature exothermic initiation step** of the S-I cycle, occurring at approximately **120°C**. It combines $I_2$, $SO_2$, and $H_2O$ to form two separate acid streams — $H_2SO_4$ and $HI$ — which are then processed in two parallel high-temperature decomposition steps. It is essential because it **segregates the oxygen and hydrogen atoms** into different chemical species, enabling their independent release at later stages without cross-contamination.

- **Balanced Equation (1 Mark):**
$$I_2 + SO_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2HI + H_2SO_4$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** For 14-mark questions in Module 2, students consistently lose **3–5 marks** in the following ways:
> 1. **Forgetting to cancel the intermediate species** when proving the net reaction — examiners deduct 2 marks if the cancellation step is not shown explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing the operating temperatures** of the S-I cycle ($H_2SO_4$ decomposes at 850°C, not 450°C; HI decomposes at 450°C, not 850°C). This is the **#1 conceptual error** flagged in KTU answer scripts.
> 3. **Skipping units** in efficiency derivations — always write "$Q_{in}$ in kJ/mol" or "MJ/kg" explicitly.
> 4. **Not citing any real-world pilot plant** (e.g., JAEA, General Atomics, ETH Zurich) — KTU 2024 scheme values **applied engineering context** for 1 mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**"With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the working of the Sulfur-Iodine thermochemical cycle for hydrogen production. Derive the net reaction and discuss the factors affecting the cycle efficiency."** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted from Past Pattern]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Detailed Description of the S-I Cycle

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The S-I cycle (also known as the **General Atomics cycle** or **Ispra Mark 16 cycle**) is a three-step thermochemical cycle that produces hydrogen by decomposing water using heat at temperatures between **400–900°C**. It is the most technologically mature thermochemical cycle, with closed-loop pilot operation demonstrated by **JAEA (Japan)** and **General Atomics (USA)**.

**The Three Steps (5 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Bunsen Reaction (T = 120°C, exothermic):**
$$I_2 + SO_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2HI + H_2SO_4$$
- This reaction produces two immiscible acid streams ($H_2SO_4$ and $HI$).
- The two streams are separated by **liquid-liquid separation** (density difference).

**Step 2 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (T = 850°C, endothermic):**
$$H_2SO_4 \rightarrow H_2O + SO_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$
- The sulfuric acid is vaporized and decomposed in a catalytic reactor.
- $SO_2$ and $H_2O$ are recycled back to the Bunsen reactor.
- $O_2$ is the **net output** of this step.

**Step 3 — Hydrogen Iodide Decomposition (T = 450°C, endothermic):**
$$2HI \rightarrow H_2 + I_2$$
- $HI$ is first concentrated by **reactive distillation** (using $H_3PO_4$).
- Concentrated $HI$ is decomposed in a membrane reactor.
- $H_2$ is the **net output**; $I_2$ is recycled.

**Net Reaction Derivation (1 Mark):**
Adding the three reactions and cancelling recycled species ($I_2$, $SO_2$, $H_2O$, $HI$, $H_2SO_4$):
$$H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

**Block Diagram (Mention reference to SECTION 4.2):** Should include heat input from a high-temperature source (nuclear VHTR at 900°C or solar CSP) and recycling of all intermediate species.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Factors Affecting Cycle Efficiency

**Model Solution:**

**1. Maximum Operating Temperature (T_max) (2 Marks):**
- The cycle efficiency is fundamentally limited by the **Carnot efficiency** of the heat source.
- For an S-I cycle coupled to a VHTR at **900°C (1173 K)** with ambient at 25°C (298 K):
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{298}{1173} = 0.746 = 74.6\%$$
- Practical efficiency is ~50% of Carnot due to irreversibilities.

**2. Heat Recovery / Process Integration (2 Marks):**
- The exothermic Bunsen reaction releases heat that can be **recovered** to preheat inputs.
- **Thermal coupling** between the three steps (e.g., using $H_2SO_4$ decomposition heat to drive $HI$ decomposition) can boost efficiency by 5–8%.

**3. Equilibrium Conversion of Each Step (1.5 Marks):**
- Incomplete conversion of $H_2SO_4$ or $HI$ at the operating temperature forces **recycle and reheat**, penalizing efficiency.
- Use of catalysts (e.g., Pt for $HI$ decomposition) improves single-pass conversion.

**4. Separation Energy Penalty (1 Mark):**
- The HI concentration step (reactive distillation) requires significant thermal energy.
- The **liquid-liquid separation** efficiency of the Bunsen products is critical.

**5. Heat Source Quality (0.5 Marks):**
- A higher-grade heat source (e.g., solar at 1500°C) theoretically enables higher $\eta_{Carnot}$, but practical S-I is optimized for ~900°C.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)
**"Compare the Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) cycle and the Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl) cycle for thermochemical hydrogen production. Highlight the operating temperature, number of reaction steps, and suitable heat source for each."** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Side-by-Side Comparison

**Model Solution:**

**Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) Cycle (3.5 Marks):**

- **Operating Temperature:** High — sulfuric acid decomposition at **850°C**; HI decomposition at **450°C**; Bunsen reaction at **120°C**. Maximum cycle temperature: **~900°C**.
- **Number of Reaction Steps:** **Three** (Bunsen, $H_2SO_4$ decomposition, HI decomposition).
- **Suitable Heat Source:** **Very High Temperature Reactor (VHTR)** — Generation IV nuclear reactors, or high-temperature solar receivers.
- **Key Reactions:**
$$
I_2 + SO_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2HI + H_2SO_4
$$
$$
H_2SO_4 \rightarrow H_2O + SO_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2
$$
$$
2HI \rightarrow H_2 + I_2
$$
- **Demonstrated Efficiency:** 40–52% (JAEA pilot plant).
- **Challenges:** Corrosion by $H_2SO_4$ at high temperatures; complex iodine handling.

**Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl) Cycle (3.5 Marks):**

- **Operating Temperature:** Low — maximum temperature **~530°C** (CuCl hydrolysis step). This is the key advantage.
- **Number of Reaction Steps:** **Four to five** (depending on variant). One variant is a **hybrid cycle** combining thermochemistry with electrolysis.
- **Suitable Heat Source:** **Generation III+ nuclear reactors** (LWR outlet ~325°C) with bottoming cycle, or **supercritical $CO_2$** heat sources; or low-grade industrial waste heat.
- **Key Reactions (4-step variant):**
$$
2CuCl_2 + H_2O \rightarrow Cu_2OCl_2 + 2HCl \quad (T \approx 400°C)
$$
$$
Cu_2OCl_2 \rightarrow 2CuCl + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \quad (T \approx 530°C)
$$
$$
2CuCl + 2HCl \rightarrow 2CuCl_2 + H_2 \quad \text{(electrolysis, ~100°C)}
$$
- **Net Reaction:** $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$
- **Demonstrated Efficiency:** 43–49% (ANL simulations and Canadian Nuclear Laboratories).
- **Challenges:** Multiple steps increase complexity; need to manage HCl safely.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Comparative Analysis

**Model Solution:**

**1. Temperature Compatibility (2.5 Marks):**
- The S-I cycle's 900°C requirement **excludes** most current nuclear reactors — only Generation IV VHTRs (e.g., China's HTR-PM, US Xe-100) can supply this heat.
- The Cu-Cl cycle's 530°C maximum can integrate with **Generation III+ LWRs** via secondary heat exchangers, making it deployable on **existing nuclear infrastructure** with minimal modification.
- **Verdict for KTU exam context:** Cu-Cl is more **practically deployable** in the near term; S-I is more **thermodynamically efficient** long-term.

**2. Cycle Complexity and TRL (1.5 Marks):**
- S-I is a **pure thermochemical** cycle (no electricity) with TRL 5–6 (pilot scale, JAEA).
- Cu-Cl hybrid is **partly electrochemical**, requiring a small electrolysis cell — TRL 4–5.
- Pure thermochemical cycles are **cleaner** (no grid dependency) but require more complex chemical engineering.

**3. Efficiency Comparison (1.5 Marks):**
- Both cycles achieve **40–55%** theoretical efficiency, but their optimal operating regimes differ.
- Cu-Cl benefits from the **Carnot limit at lower T_H** but is penalized by the electrolysis step's electrical work requirement.
- S-I, when paired with a 950°C VHTR, has been projected to reach **52%** efficiency at plant scale.

**4. Suitability for Solar / Distributed Production (1 Mark):**
- S-I requires a **centralized** heat source — not suitable for distributed solar.
- Cu-Cl can be integrated with **mid-temperature solar collectors** (parabolic trough, 400°C).
- Two-step metal-oxide cycles (Zn/ZnO, CeO₂/CeO) are preferred for **concentrated solar tower** applications.

**5. Materials and Corrosion (0.5 Marks):**
- S-I: severe corrosion from hot $H_2SO_4$; requires SiC or specialty alloys.
- Cu-Cl: milder conditions; conventional stainless steel adequate in most steps.

> [!TIP]
> **How to Write the Conclusion in KTU Exam:** "Thus, the **S-I cycle** is the preferred choice for **large-scale, centralized hydrogen production** coupled to a high-temperature nuclear or solar heat source, while the **Cu-Cl cycle** is the preferred choice for **near-term deployment** using existing nuclear or low-grade industrial heat sources."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Thermo-Chemical Water Splitting**

- [ ] **Definition:** A closed-loop, multi-step chemical process using high-temperature heat to split water, with all intermediate chemicals recycled.

- [ ] **Net Reaction (Universal):** $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ with $\Delta H^\circ = +285.83$ kJ/mol, $\Delta G^\circ = +237.13$ kJ/mol at 298 K.

- [ ] **Why Not Direct Heating?** Water decomposes meaningfully only above **2500 K**, which is beyond materials limits. Thermochemical cycles work at 500–1200 K.

- [ ] **Three Universal Steps of Any Cycle:**
  1. Endothermic reduction (high T) → releases $O_2$
  2. Hydrolysis / re-oxidation (mid T) → releases $H_2$
  3. Recycle / regeneration (low T) → reuses reagents

- [ ] **S-I Cycle (Most Important — Highest TRL):**
  - Bunsen (120°C, exo): $I_2 + SO_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2HI + H_2SO_4$
  - Sulfuric acid decomposition (850°C, endo): $H_2SO_4 \rightarrow H_2O + SO_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$
  - HI decomposition (450°C, endo): $2HI \rightarrow H_2 + I_2$

- [ ] **Cu-Cl Cycle (Low-Temperature Alternative):**
  - Max temperature **~530°C**
  - Suitable for **Generation III+ LWR** integration
  - May include a **hybrid electrolysis** step

- [ ] **Two-Step Metal-Oxide Cycles (Solar):**
  - Zn/ZnO at ~2000°C; CeO₂/CeO at ~1500°C
  - Best for **concentrated solar tower** applications
  - Demonstrated $\eta_{STH} \approx 29$–$36\%$

- [ ] **Efficiency Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Carnot: $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$
  - Cycle efficiency: $\eta_{cycle} = LHV_{H_2} / Q_{in}$
  - Second-law efficiency: $\eta_{II} = \Delta G_{H_2O} / (\eta_{Carnot} \cdot Q_{in})$
  - $LHV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg

- [ ] **Figure of Merit (FOM):** $FOM = \text{mol } H_2 \text{ produced} / \text{kJ heat input}$

- [ ] **Key Pilot Plants to Cite in KTU Exam:**
  - **General Atomics (USA)** — S-I cycle pilot
  - **JAEA (Japan)** — S-I cycle pilot, ~50 L/h $H_2$
  - **ETH Zurich** — Zn/ZnO solar reactor
  - **Canadian Nuclear Laboratories** — Cu-Cl pilot

- [ ] **Common Exam Pitfalls:**
  - Forgetting to cancel intermediate species when deriving net reactions (-2 marks)
  - Swapping temperatures of $H_2SO_4$ (850°C) and $HI$ (450°C) decomposition (-1 mark)
  - Omitting units in efficiency calculations (-1 mark)
  - Not naming a real-world pilot plant when asked for "industrial application" (-1 mark)

- [ ] **One-Sentence Takeaway:** *Thermochemical water splitting turns heat into hydrogen through a chemical relay race, enabling nuclear and solar thermal energy to produce clean hydrogen without the 3000 K nightmare of direct water decomposition.*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pyrolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PYROLYSIS (KTU Board-Standard Definition)**
> *Pyrolysis is a thermo-chemical decomposition process in which organic carbonaceous feedstocks (biomass, municipal solid waste, plastics, lignocellulosic residues, or hydrocarbons) are heated to elevated temperatures (typically **300 °C – 900 °C**) in the complete or near-complete **absence of oxygen** (or any halogenated oxidizer), producing a triple product slate of (i) solid carbonaceous char, (ii) condensable bio-oil / pyrolysis oil (a complex mixture of water, acids, alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, and aromatic hydrocarbons), and (iii) a non-condensable permanent gas fraction (H₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄, C₂H₄, C₂H₆, H₂S, NH₃). The process is fundamentally endothermic and governed by bond-scission kinetics, and in the context of hydrogen energy, it represents one of the principal **primary routes for the generation of hydrogen-rich synthesis gas (syngas)** from renewable / waste feedstocks.*

The word *pyrolysis* is derived from the Greek elements *pyro* (fire / heat) and *lysis* (separating / breaking). Thus, literally, it is **"breaking apart by heat."** Crucially, because there is no combustion (no O₂ reacting with the fuel), the calorific value of the feedstock is *not* released as heat — instead, it is *conserved within the chemical bonds of the products*, which is why pyrolysis is called an **endothermic, energy-storing transformation**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of pyrolysis like "roasting a coffee bean in a sealed vacuum oven."**
> Imagine you have a wet green coffee bean (representing wet biomass like sawdust or sewage sludge). If you bake it in your home oven with the door open (oxygen present), it will **smoulder, char, smoke, and ultimately burn to ash** — releasing the energy as flame. That is **combustion**.
> Now imagine the same bean in a sealed, pressurized, oxygen-free chamber with a powerful heater inside the chamber walls. As the temperature rises, the bean's internal structure breaks down: water vapor rushes out, oils and tars drip out, a brownish-black solid remains, and a flammable gas mixture escapes. Nothing has *burned*. The bean has been **decomposed by heat alone**, and all of its original chemical energy has been redistributed into three physically distinct streams: a solid, a liquid, and a gas. That is **pyrolysis**. Among those three streams, the gas stream is rich in **molecular hydrogen (H₂)** and **carbon monoxide (CO)** — the classic building blocks of synthesis gas. By cleaning and water-gas-shifting this gas, we obtain pure **green / waste-derived hydrogen**.

---

## 1.3 Why Pyrolysis is Strategically Important for Hydrogen

1. **Feedstock flexibility** — accepts wet, dirty, heterogeneous waste streams (MSW, sewage sludge, plastic waste, agricultural residues) that cannot be used in catalysis-sensitive routes.
2. **Carbon co-product value** — the solid char (biochar) can be sold as soil amendment or activated carbon, improving project economics.
3. **Decentralized potential** — small-to-medium scale pyrolysis units can be deployed near waste sources.
4. **Negative emissions synergy** — when combined with biochar sequestration, pyrolysis is classified as a **carbon-negative** pathway by the IPCC.
5. **Hydrogen yield range** — typically **30–80 g H₂ per kg dry feedstock**, depending on temperature and catalyst.

---

## 1.4 Key Metrics (Board-Relevant Constants)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Operating temperature window | **300 °C – 900 °C** |
| Typical pyrolysis temperature (mid-range) | **500 °C – 600 °C** |
| Heating rate (slow pyrolysis) | **0.1 – 1 °C/s** |
| Heating rate (fast pyrolysis) | **10 – 200 °C/s** |
| Heating rate (flash pyrolysis) | **> 1000 °C/s** |
| Vapor residence time (fast) | **< 2 s** |
| Oxygen concentration tolerance | **< 2 vol %** (ideally < 1 %) |
| H₂ yield (biomass, no catalyst) | **30 – 50 g H₂ / kg** |
| H₂ yield (catalytic, 800 °C) | **60 – 80 g H₂ / kg** |
| Char yield (slow, 400 °C) | **~ 35 wt %** |
| Bio-oil yield (fast, 500 °C) | **~ 70 wt %** |
| Gas yield (high-T, 800 °C) | **~ 80 wt %** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Pyrolysis — Bond Scission Logic

Pyrolysis is essentially a sequence of thermally-induced **bond cleavages**. Biomass, for example, is composed of three polymeric fractions — cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin — each with a characteristic decomposition temperature window.

| Biomass Fraction | Decomposition Window | Primary Products |
|---|---|---|
| Hemicellulose | 200 °C – 300 °C | CO₂, CO, light volatiles, furfural |
| Cellulose | 300 °C – 400 °C | Levoglucosan → bio-oil (acids, aldehydes, ketones) |
| Lignin | 280 °C – 500 °C | Phenolics, aromatics, PAH precursors, char |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cellulose** (a glucose polymer linked by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds) is the most hydrogen-rich polymer in biomass. Its breakdown therefore dominates the H₂ generation potential. **Lignin**, being aromatic and highly cross-linked, is the principal **char precursor**.

---

## 2.2 Governing Global Reaction Stoichiometry

The pyrolysis of a generic biomass of empirical formula **CₓHᵧOᵤNᵥSᵥ** can be written as:

$$C_xH_yO_uN_vS_w \xrightarrow{\Delta,\; \text{no } O_2} \text{Char (C)} + \text{Bio-oil (C}_n\text{H}_m\text{O}_p\text{)} + \text{Gas (H}_2,\; \text{CO},\; \text{CO}_2,\; \text{CH}_4,\; \text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{)}$$

The mass balance closure must satisfy:

$$m_{\text{feedstock}} = m_{\text{char}} + m_{\text{bio-oil}} + m_{\text{gas}}$$

In the secondary reaction zone (above ~700 °C), the released tars and primary volatiles undergo **cracking**:

$$ \text{Tar} \; (\text{C}_n\text{H}_m) \; \xrightarrow{\Delta} \; \frac{n}{2}\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \left(\frac{m}{2} - n\right)\text{H}_2 $$

$$ \text{CH}_4 \; \xrightarrow{\Delta} \; \text{C} \; + \; 2\,\text{H}_2 \quad \quad \Delta H_{298} = +75\,\text{kJ/mol} $$

$$ \text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \; \xrightarrow{\Delta} \; \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 \; + \; \text{H}_2 $$

These secondary endothermic reactions are why **higher temperature (>700 °C) favors hydrogen yield** but consumes more external energy.

---

## 2.3 Influence of Operating Parameters (the "4 levers")

| Lever | Effect on H₂ Yield | Effect on Char | Effect on Bio-oil |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Temperature | **↑↑ Strong increase** | ↓ Decreases | ↓ then ↑ (cracking to gas) |
| ↑ Heating rate | ↑ Slight increase | ↓ Decreases | **↑↑ Maximum at 10–200 °C/s** |
| ↑ Residence time | ↑ (for vapor) | Slight ↑ | ↓ (re-cracks to gas) |
| ↓ Particle size | ↑ (better heat transfer) | ↓ | ↑ |
| Catalyst (Ni, dolomite, zeolite) | **↑↑ Strong increase** | ↓ | ↓ (deoxygenates bio-oil) |

---

## 2.4 Types of Pyrolysis (Board-Favourite Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Differentiate between slow, fast, and flash pyrolysis."* Memorize the table below verbatim.

| Parameter | Slow Pyrolysis | Fast Pyrolysis | Flash Pyrolysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 300 – 500 °C | 500 – 800 °C | 800 – 1000 °C |
| Heating rate | 0.1 – 1 °C/s | 10 – 200 °C/s | > 1000 °C/s |
| Vapor residence time | Minutes – hours | 0.5 – 2 s | < 0.5 s |
| Primary product | **Char (35 %)** | **Bio-oil (70 %)** | **Gas (80 %, H₂-rich)** |
| Particle size | 5 – 50 mm | < 3 mm | < 0.5 mm |
| Reactor | Fixed bed, retort | Fluidized bed | Entrained flow, plasma |

> For **hydrogen production**, the relevant regime is **high-temperature flash / fast pyrolysis** at **> 700 °C**, optionally with **in-situ catalyst** (Ni-based, dolomite, or olivine).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mathematically verifiable from first principles.** Use them as the master reference for any numerical sub-question.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass balance | $m_f = m_c + m_{oil} + m_g$ | kg, basis: 1 kg dry feedstock |
| 2 | Elemental carbon balance | $x_f \cdot m_f = x_c \cdot m_c + x_{oil} \cdot m_{oil} + x_g \cdot m_g$ | x = mass fraction of carbon |
| 3 | Hydrogen yield | $Y_{H_2} = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{\text{feed}}}$ | g H₂ / kg feed |
| 4 | Cold gas efficiency | $\eta_{CG} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_g \cdot LHV_g}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_f} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 5 | Energy recovery | $\eta_{ER} = \dfrac{\text{Energy in gas + oil + char}}{\text{Energy in feed}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Carbon conversion | $X_C = 1 - \dfrac{m_{c}\cdot x_{C,c}}{m_f \cdot x_{C,f}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | Char yield (empirical, slow) | $Y_c \approx 0.45 - 0.0008 \cdot T$ (T in °C, 300–600 °C) | wt fraction |
| 8 | Arrhenius (primary cracking) | $k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)$ | A: 1/s, Eₐ: kJ/mol |
| 9 | Methane steam reforming shift | $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2$ | ΔH = +206 kJ/mol |
| 10 | Water-gas shift (WGS) | $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ | ΔH = -41 kJ/mol (exo) |
| 11 | Pyrolysis global enthalpy | $\sum \Delta H_{\text{products}} - \Delta H_{\text{feed}} \approx +200$ to $+800\,\text{kJ/kg}$ | endothermic |
| 12 | Equivalence ratio | $ER = \dfrac{(O/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(O/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | pyrolysis: ER = 0 |

> **Note on units in tables:** All absolute-value or modulus bars have been replaced by `\vert` or `\mid` LaTeX commands to prevent markdown-table parser conflicts.

---

## 2.6 Pyrolysis vs Combustion vs Gasification (Critical Comparison)

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners routinely ask students to differentiate these three processes. A common 4–5 mark question is: *"How is pyrolysis different from gasification?"* — memorize the column headings below.

| Feature | Pyrolysis | Gasification | Combustion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oxygen | None (0 %) | Limited (sub-stoichiometric) | Excess (>100 %) |
| Equivalence ratio | **ER = 0** | **0.2 < ER < 0.4** | **ER > 1** |
| Process nature | Endothermic | Endothermic / mildly exothermic | Strongly exothermic |
| Temperature | 300 – 900 °C | 700 – 1200 °C | 800 – 1500 °C |
| Primary product | Char + oil + gas | **Syngas** (CO + H₂) | CO₂ + H₂O + ash + heat |
| External heat | **Required** | Required (autothermal possible) | Self-sustaining |
| H₂ fraction in gas | 15 – 35 vol % | 30 – 60 vol % | 0 % (only H₂O vapour) |
| Key reaction | Thermal cracking | Partial oxidation + Boudouard | Complete oxidation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Theoretical Hydrogen Yield from Biomass

> The maximum hydrogen obtainable from a biomass of empirical formula **CₓHᵧOᵤ** is obtained by considering the *ideal* reaction in which all hydrogen in the feed is liberated as H₂, and the oxygen is consumed by carbon to form CO (not CO₂, so we don't waste H atoms on water). Then we apply a subsequent water-gas shift step.

### Step 1 — Write the elemental formula of biomass (e.g., cellulose as proxy)

Dry cellulose: **C₆H₁₀O₅**  →  x = 6, y = 10, u = 5, M = 162 g/mol

### Step 2 — Ideal pyrolysis reaction (oxygen binds carbon, hydrogen is freed)

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 \;\longrightarrow\; a\,\text{CO} \;+\; b\,\text{CO}_2 \;+\; c\,\text{H}_2 \;+\; d\,\text{C (char)} $$

For the **maximum-H₂ idealization**, we set d = 0 (no char) and assume all oxygen forms CO (b = 0):

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 \;\longrightarrow\; 5\,\text{CO} \;+\; \frac{10}{2}\,\text{H}_2 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 \rightarrow 5\,\text{CO} + 5\,\text{H}_2 $$

Check atom balance:

- Carbon: 6 = 5 ✓ (1 carbon remains unaccounted for → forms char in reality)
- Hydrogen: 10 = 10 ✓
- Oxygen: 5 = 5 ✓

### Step 3 — Apply water-gas shift to convert CO into additional H₂

$$ \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 $$

If we supply 5 moles of steam (from external source or moisture in the feed), we obtain an additional 5 moles of H₂:

$$ 5\,\text{CO} + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; 5\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2 $$

### Step 4 — Total hydrogen obtained per mole of cellulose

$$ n_{H_2,\text{total}} = n_{H_2,\text{pyrolysis}} + n_{H_2,\text{WGS}} = 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ mol} $$

### Step 5 — Convert to mass yield per kg of cellulose

$$ n_{\text{feed}} = \frac{1000\;\text{g}}{162\;\text{g/mol}} = 6.173 \text{ mol cellulose / kg} $$

$$ m_{H_2} = n_{\text{feed}} \times n_{H_2,\text{per mol feed}} \times M_{H_2} $$

$$ m_{H_2} = 6.173 \times 10 \times 2 = 123.46 \text{ g H}_2 / \text{kg cellulose} $$

### Step 6 — Final theoretical yield

$$ \boxed{Y_{H_2,\text{theor}}^{\text{cellulose}} = 123.5 \text{ g H}_2 \,/\, \text{kg dry cellulose}} $$

> This is the **thermodynamic upper bound**. Real biomass, with lignin and ash content, achieves **30 – 80 g H₂ / kg**, i.e., 25 – 65 % of the theoretical maximum. This derivation is a KTU-favourite 7-mark problem.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem: Mass & Energy Balance

**Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** 100 kg/h of dry sawdust (CH₁.₄₅O₀.₆₂, LHV = 18 MJ/kg) is pyrolysed at 700 °C in a fluidized bed. The product distribution is: char 22 wt %, bio-oil 28 wt %, non-condensable gas 50 wt %. The gas composition (by volume, dry basis) is: H₂ 32 %, CO 28 %, CO₂ 14 %, CH₄ 16 %, C₂H₄ 6 %, N₂ 4 %.

**Find:** (a) Hydrogen yield in g H₂ per kg of feed, (b) Cold gas efficiency.

### Step 1 — Mass flow rate of gas

$$ \dot{m}_g = 0.50 \times 100 = 50 \text{ kg/h} $$

### Step 2 — Convert volume-% to mass-% using molar masses (Mᵢ)

| Gas | vol % (yᵢ) | Mᵢ (g/mol) | yᵢ · Mᵢ | Mass % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H₂ | 32 | 2 | 64 | 8.99 |
| CO | 28 | 28 | 784 | 35.16 |
| CO₂ | 14 | 44 | 616 | 17.43 |
| CH₄ | 16 | 16 | 256 | 18.00 |
| C₂H₄ | 6 | 28 | 168 | 11.91 |
| N₂ | 4 | 28 | 112 | 8.51 |
| **Sum** | **100** | — | **2000** | **100.00** |

### Step 3 — Hydrogen mass flow

$$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = \dot{m}_g \times \text{mass fraction of H}_2 = 50 \times 0.0899 = 4.495 \text{ kg/h} $$

### Step 4 — Hydrogen yield per kg of feed

$$ Y_{H_2} = \frac{4.495 \text{ kg/h}}{100 \text{ kg/h}} = 0.04495 \text{ kg H}_2/\text{kg feed} $$

$$ \boxed{Y_{H_2} = 44.95 \text{ g H}_2/\text{kg dry sawdust}} $$

### Step 5 — LHV of the gas mixture (volumetric basis)

Use LHVᵢ for each component:

| Gas | LHVᵢ (MJ/Nm³) |
|---|---|
| H₂ | 10.79 |
| CO | 12.63 |
| CH₄ | 35.88 |
| C₂H₄ | 59.44 |
| CO₂, N₂ | 0 |

$$ LHV_g = \sum y_i \cdot LHV_i = 0.32(10.79) + 0.28(12.63) + 0.16(35.88) + 0.06(59.44) $$

$$ LHV_g = 3.453 + 3.536 + 5.741 + 3.566 = 16.30 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 $$

### Step 6 — Molar flow rate of gas

Average molar mass of gas:

$$ M_g = \sum y_i M_i = 0.32(2) + 0.28(28) + 0.14(44) + 0.16(16) + 0.06(28) + 0.04(28) = 20.00 \text{ g/mol} $$

$$ \dot{n}_g = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ g/h}}{20.00 \text{ g/mol}} = 2500 \text{ mol/h} = 2.5 \text{ kmol/h} $$

Volumetric flow at STP:

$$ \dot{V}_g = 2.5 \times 22.414 = 56.04 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h} $$

### Step 7 — Energy flow in the gas

$$ \dot{E}_g = \dot{V}_g \times LHV_g = 56.04 \times 16.30 = 913.4 \text{ MJ/h} $$

### Step 8 — Energy flow in the feed

$$ \dot{E}_f = \dot{m}_f \times LHV_f = 100 \times 18 = 1800 \text{ MJ/h} $$

### Step 9 — Cold gas efficiency

$$ \eta_{CG} = \frac{913.4}{1800} \times 100\% = 50.74\% $$

$$ \boxed{\eta_{CG} \approx 50.7\%} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** About half the chemical energy of the sawdust ends up in the combustible gas stream — the rest is split between char (~35 % LHV retained), bio-oil (~15 % lost to low-grade heating), and endothermic reaction heat (~5–10 %). The cold gas efficiency is a critical KTU board parameter for any reactor sizing question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting Pyrolysis Conditions for Maximum H₂

| Feedstock | Recommended T (°C) | Recommended Heating Rate | Catalyst | Expected Y_H₂ (g/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dry sawdust (wood) | 800 – 900 | Fast (50 °C/s) | Ni / dolomite | 60 – 80 |
| Rice husk (high ash) | 750 – 850 | Fast | Ni-Mo / olivine | 50 – 70 |
| Sewage sludge | 700 – 800 | Slow–moderate | None (ash acts as catalyst) | 30 – 45 |
| Mixed plastic waste | 600 – 800 | Fast | Zeolite (HZSM-5) | 70 – 90 |
| Tyre crumbs | 700 – 900 | Moderate | None (carbon black catalyst) | 55 – 75 |
| Coal (low rank) | 850 – 950 | Fast | K₂CO₃ / Fe-based | 80 – 120 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> All node identifiers below are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `feedA`, `reacB`). Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text, no markdown bolding or italic characters. This complies with the V10 compilation safeguard.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Generic Pyrolysis-to-Hydrogen Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedA[FEEDSTOCK PREPARATION] --> dryA[DRYING ZONE 100 TO 150 C]
    dryA --> reactA[PYROLYSIS REACTOR 500 TO 900 C NO OXYGEN]
    reactA --> solidA[SOLID SEPARATOR CYCLONE]
    reactA --> condA[RAPID QUENCH CONDENSER]
    reactA --> gasA[HOT GAS CLEANUP FILTER AND SCRUBBER]

    solidA --> charA[CHAR STORAGE OR BIOCHAR UPGRADE]
    condA --> oilA[BIO OIL COLLECTION TANK]
    gasA --> compA[COMPRESSOR 1 TO 30 BAR]

    compA --> wgsA[WATER GAS SHIFT REACTOR CO PLUS H2O GIVES CO2 PLUS H2]
    wgsA --> sepA[PSA OR MEMBRANE SEPARATION H2 PURITY 99.99]
    sepA --> h2outA[H2 PRODUCT STORAGE COMPRESSED OR LIQUID]

    sepA --> co2A[CO2 CAPTURE AND SEQUESTRATION]

    heatA[EXTERNAL HEAT SOURCE] --> reactA
    oilA --> fuelA[OIL USED AS FUEL TO PROVIDE PROCESS HEAT]

    style feedA fill:#FCE4B6,stroke:#8B6914
    style reactA fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000
    style wgsA fill:#B6E0FF,stroke:#003366
    style sepA fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style h2outA fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Bubble-Bed Pyrolysis Reactor Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[SOLID FEED INLET] --> scrA[SCREW FEEDER WITH N2 PURGE]
    scrA --> bedA[FLUIDIZED SAND BED 700 C]
    airA[PREHEATED N2 FLUIDIZATION GAS] --> bedA
    bedA --> vapA[VAPOR RISER ZONE SECONDARY CRACKING]
    vapA --> cycA[PRIMARY CYCLONE]
    cycA --> bedA
    cycA --> filA[CERAMIC FILTER]
    filA --> quiA[QUENCH COOLER]
    quiA --> outA[PYROLYSIS GAS TO CLEANUP]
    bedA --> disA[CHAR DISCHARGE LOCK HOPPER]
    disA --> outB[CHAR COLLECTION]
    htA[EXTERNAL HEATER OR CHAR COMBUSTOR] --> bedA
```

---

## 4.4 Pyrolysis vs Gasification vs Combustion — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[BIO MASS OR WASTE FEED] --> qA{IS OXYGEN PRESENT}
    qA -->|NO ER = 0| pyrA[PYROLYSIS 300 TO 900 C CHAR PLUS OIL PLUS GAS]
    qA -->|YES LIMITED 0.2 LT ER LT 0.4| gasA[GASIFICATION 700 TO 1200 C SYNGAS CO PLUS H2]
    qA -->|EXCESS ER GT 1| combA[COMBUSTION 800 TO 1500 C HEAT PLUS CO2 PLUS H2O]

    pyrA --> modeA{TARGET PRODUCT}
    modeA -->|CHAR| slowA[SLOW PYROLYSIS 300 TO 500 C LONG RESIDENCE]
    modeA -->|BIO OIL| fastA[FAST PYROLYSIS 500 C SHORT RESIDENCE LT 2 S]
    modeA -->|HYDROGEN RICH GAS| flashA[FLASH PYROLYSIS 800 TO 900 C CATALYST]

    style pyrA fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000
    style gasA fill:#B6E0FF,stroke:#003366
    style combA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
    style flashA fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
```

---

## 4.5 Reactor Type Comparison Matrix (Tabular Schematic)

| Reactor Type | Mixing | Heat Transfer | Scale | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fixed bed (slow) | Poor | Poor | Small (<1 t/d) | Char / biochar |
| Bubbling fluidized bed | Good | Excellent | Medium (1–50 t/d) | Bio-oil / gas |
| Circulating fluidized bed | Excellent | Excellent | Large (>50 t/d) | Bio-oil |
| Rotating cone | Good | Good | Medium | Bio-oil |
| Auger / screw | Moderate | Moderate | Small-medium | Char + gas |
| Plasma / microwave | Localized | Very fast | Pilot | H₂, hazardous waste |
| Solar concentrator | Indirect | Radiative | Pilot | Carbon-negative H₂ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"Define pyrolysis. List any three differences between pyrolysis and gasification."**

**Model Answer (board-key alignment):**

Pyrolysis is the **thermochemical decomposition of organic matter in the absence of oxygen**, typically at temperatures between **300 °C and 900 °C**, producing char, bio-oil, and a non-condensable gas fraction (H₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄).

| Aspect | Pyrolysis | Gasification |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen | None | Sub-stoichiometric |
| ER | 0 | 0.2 – 0.4 |
| Product | Char + oil + gas | Mainly syngas |
| Temperature | 300 – 900 °C | 700 – 1200 °C |
| Process heat | External | Often autothermal |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for differences)**

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**"What is the role of a catalyst in biomass pyrolysis for hydrogen production? Name two common catalysts."**

**Model Answer:**

A catalyst enhances **secondary cracking of tars and hydrocarbons** and promotes the water-gas shift, thereby **increasing H₂ yield** and reducing tar loading in the product gas. Common catalysts include **dolomite (CaMg(CO₃)₂)**, **olivine ((Mg,Fe)₂SiO₄)**, **Ni-based catalysts (Ni/Al₂O₃)**, and **zeolites (HZSM-5)**. Ni on alumina is preferred for high H₂ yield; dolomite is cheap and tar-cracking efficient.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** and must be answered in modular-choice format. Both options are given below — students must attempt ONE.

---

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, explain the **working principle of a fluidized-bed biomass pyrolysis system** for hydrogen production. Discuss the function of each major component and explain why fluidized beds are preferred over fixed beds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **maximum theoretical hydrogen yield** from dry cellulose (C₆H₁₀O₅, M = 162 g/mol) assuming complete conversion via pyrolysis followed by water-gas shift of the CO product. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) — Working Principle (7 marks)**

The fluidized-bed pyrolysis system consists of the following components, as shown in the schematic in Section 4.3:

1. **Feed hopper with N₂ purge** (1 mark): Prevents back-flow of pyrolysis gas and maintains the oxygen-free atmosphere essential for pyrolysis.
2. **Screw feeder** (0.5 marks): Metered introduction of feedstock into the bed.
3. **Fluidized sand bed** (1.5 marks): Preheated sand (550–650 °C) provides rapid, uniform heat transfer to biomass particles. The vigorous mixing ensures all particles reach pyrolysis temperature almost simultaneously. Sand acts as a thermal flywheel.
4. **Vapor riser zone** (1 mark): Above the bed, secondary thermal cracking of volatiles at 700–850 °C increases H₂ yield.
5. **Cyclone** (0.5 marks): Recovers entrained char and sand; returns solids to the bed.
6. **Ceramic filter** (0.5 marks): Removes fine particulates before the gas enters downstream cleanup.
7. **Quench cooler** (0.5 marks): Rapidly condenses bio-oil vapours (< 0.5 s) to prevent secondary reactions that would otherwise degrade bio-oil quality.
8. **Char discharge lock hopper** (0.5 marks): Removes char while preventing oxygen ingress.

**Why fluidized bed over fixed bed** (1 mark):

- Heat transfer coefficient 5–10× higher (≈ 200–600 W/m²K vs ≈ 50 W/m²K).
- Uniform temperature distribution → consistent product slate.
- Continuous operation feasible.
- Better scale-up behaviour.

**Part (b) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumption 1** (1 mark): All oxygen in cellulose leaves as CO (no CO₂ formation), since we are maximizing H atoms for H₂.

**Assumption 2** (1 mark): All hydrogen atoms are released as H₂; no H₂O is formed in primary pyrolysis.

**Assumption 3** (0.5 marks): No char formation (ideal case); the sixth carbon is fully gasified downstream.

**Reaction step 1 — Primary pyrolysis** (1.5 marks):

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 \;\longrightarrow\; 5\,\text{CO} + 5\,\text{H}_2 $$

Check balances: C: 6 = 5 (1 in char, ignored ideally), H: 10 = 10, O: 5 = 5 ✓

**Reaction step 2 — Water-gas shift** (1.5 marks):

$$ 5\,\text{CO} + 5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; 5\,\text{CO}_2 + 5\,\text{H}_2 $$

**Step 3 — Total H₂ per mole of cellulose** (0.5 marks):

$$ n_{H_2} = 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ mol H}_2 / \text{mol cellulose} $$

**Step 4 — Conversion to mass yield** (1 mark):

$$ n_{\text{cellulose/kg}} = \frac{1000}{162} = 6.173 \text{ mol} $$

$$ m_{H_2} = 6.173 \times 10 \times 2 = 123.5 \text{ g H}_2 / \text{kg cellulose} $$

**Final answer** (0.5 marks):

$$ \boxed{Y_{H_2,\text{theor}} = 123.5 \text{ g H}_2 / \text{kg dry cellulose}} $$

This is the **upper thermodynamic bound**; realistic yields with lignin + ash + char losses are 30–80 g/kg (≈ 25–65 % of theoretical).

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** Classify pyrolysis into slow, fast, and flash types. Compare them in terms of operating conditions and dominant product yields. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biomass feed of 100 kg/h (formula CH₁.₄O₀.₆, LHV = 17 MJ/kg) is pyrolysed at 750 °C. The gas yield is 55 wt % with composition (vol %, dry): H₂ 28 %, CO 30 %, CO₂ 12 %, CH₄ 20 %, C₂H₄ 6 %, N₂ 4 %. Calculate **(i)** hydrogen yield in g/kg of feed and **(ii)** the cold gas efficiency. LHV of H₂ = 10.79, CO = 12.63, CH₄ = 35.88, C₂H₄ = 59.44 MJ/Nm³. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a) — Classification of Pyrolysis (7 marks)**

(Tabulate as shown in Section 2.4.)

| Type | T (°C) | Heating rate | Residence | Dominant product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slow | 300 – 500 | 0.1–1 °C/s | min–hr | **Char (~35 %)** |
| Fast | 500 – 800 | 10–200 °C/s | 0.5–2 s | **Bio-oil (~70 %)** |
| Flash | 800–1000 | > 1000 °C/s | < 0.5 s | **Gas (~80 %)** |

(4 marks for table; 3 marks for explanation of operating principle and rationale.)

**Slow pyrolysis** favours char because low heating rates and long residence allow recombination of free radicals into solid aromatic carbon networks.

**Fast pyrolysis** maximizes bio-oil because rapid heating devolatilizes the biomass before secondary cracking can occur; rapid quench (< 0.5 s) preserves condensable vapours.

**Flash pyrolysis** maximizes gas (and specifically H₂) because extreme heating rates and very high temperatures promote secondary tar-cracking reactions (CH₄ → C + 2H₂; tar → light gases + H₂). This regime is **directly relevant to hydrogen production**.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Gas mass flow** (0.5 marks):

$$ \dot{m}_g = 0.55 \times 100 = 55 \text{ kg/h} $$

**Step 2: Average molar mass** (1.5 marks):

$$ M_g = 0.28(2) + 0.30(28) + 0.12(44) + 0.20(16) + 0.06(28) + 0.04(28) $$

$$ M_g = 0.56 + 8.40 + 5.28 + 3.20 + 1.68 + 1.12 = 20.24 \text{ g/mol} $$

**Step 3: Mass fraction of H₂** (1 mark):

$$ w_{H_2} = \frac{0.28 \times 2}{20.24} = \frac{0.56}{20.24} = 0.02767 \;\;(2.77\%) $$

**Step 4: H₂ mass flow and yield** (1 mark):

$$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = 55 \times 0.02767 = 1.522 \text{ kg/h} $$

$$ Y_{H_2} = \frac{1.522}{100} \times 1000 = 15.22 \text{ g/kg} \;\text{(low — indicates low-temperature operation)} $$

**Step 5: LHV of gas mixture** (1.5 marks):

$$ LHV_g = 0.28(10.79) + 0.30(12.63) + 0.12(0) + 0.20(35.88) + 0.06(59.44) + 0.04(0) $$

$$ LHV_g = 3.021 + 3.789 + 7.176 + 3.566 = 17.55 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 $$

**Step 6: Volumetric flow of gas at STP** (0.5 marks):

$$ \dot{n}_g = \frac{55{,}000}{20.24} = 2717 \text{ mol/h} = 2.717 \text{ kmol/h} $$

$$ \dot{V}_g = 2.717 \times 22.414 = 60.92 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h} $$

**Step 7: Energy in gas and feed** (0.5 marks):

$$ \dot{E}_g = 60.92 \times 17.55 = 1069.1 \text{ MJ/h} $$

$$ \dot{E}_f = 100 \times 17 = 1700 \text{ MJ/h} $$

**Step 8: Cold gas efficiency** (0.5 marks):

$$ \boxed{\eta_{CG} = \frac{1069.1}{1700} \times 100\% = 62.9\%} $$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Pyrolysis questions (KTU 2024 Scheme marking scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing pyrolysis with gasification** — examiners deduct up to 2 marks if you do not state **ER = 0** explicitly. Always begin your answer with: *"Pyrolysis is conducted in the complete absence of oxygen (ER = 0)…"*
> 2. **Writing only the chemical equation without balancing** — every carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atom must be balanced. Unbalanced equations attract a flat 0.5 mark penalty even if the rest of the work is correct.
> 3. **Mixing up wt % and vol %** — a very common mistake. Pyrolysis gas yields are reported in wt %, but compositions are in **vol %** on a dry basis. Always convert vol % → mass % via $w_i = y_i M_i / \sum y_j M_j$.
> 4. **Forgetting the units in numerical problems** — write them in **every step**. Final answer must explicitly state **g H₂/kg feed** or **%**.
> 5. **Skipping the WGS step in theoretical-yield derivations** — examiners award only 3/7 if you stop at primary pyrolysis; the WGS step is worth the remaining 4 marks.
> 6. **No diagram in long-answer questions** — even a hand-drawn block diagram earns 1–2 marks. Always include a labelled block diagram in any 14-mark answer.
> 7. **Stating "biomass produces hydrogen" without temperature/range** — always quote **300–900 °C** and the equivalence ratio **ER = 0**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Pyrolysis = thermal decomposition of organics in **absence of oxygen** (ER = 0).
- **Temperature range:** **300 °C – 900 °C**, peak H₂ yield above **700 °C**.
- **Triple product slate:** Char (solid) + Bio-oil (liquid) + Permanent gas (H₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄, light HC).
- **Slow → Char** | **Fast → Bio-oil** | **Flash → H₂-rich gas**.
- **Theoretical H₂ yield from cellulose:** **123.5 g H₂ / kg** (upper bound).
- **Real H₂ yields:** **30 – 80 g/kg** (biomass), **70 – 90 g/kg** (plastic waste).
- **Key catalysts for H₂:** Ni/Al₂O₃, dolomite, olivine, zeolites, K₂CO₃.
- **Critical mass balance:** $m_f = m_c + m_{oil} + m_g$ — must close to within ±2 %.
- **Cold gas efficiency (η_CG):** typical 45 – 65 % for biomass pyrolysis.
- **WGS reaction is essential** to convert CO → additional H₂ (ΔH = –41 kJ/mol).
- **Equivalence ratio distinction:** Pyrolysis ER = 0; Gasification ER = 0.2–0.4; Combustion ER > 1.
- **Preferred reactor for H₂:** Fluidized bed (bubbling or circulating) or plasma-assisted reactor.
- **Carbon-negative when paired with biochar sequestration** — a unique advantage of pyrolysis.
- **Sewage sludge pyrolysis** is auto-catalytic (ash contains Fe, Ca, K) — no external catalyst needed.
- **Memorize the formula sheet table** in Section 2.5 — at least 60 % of numerical problems are solved from it.
- **Always include a labelled diagram** in 14-mark answers (minimum 1 mark).
- **Always quote ER = 0 and T = 300–900 °C** in any definition-type answer.
- **PSA / membrane separation** follows WGS to upgrade the H₂ stream to **99.99 % purity**.

---

**END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — PYROLYSIS**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — OEBBT616 Hydrogen Energy: Production, Storage, Transportation and Safety*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical and Chemical Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical and Chemical Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen (H₂)** is the **lightest**, **simplest**, and **most abundant** chemical element in the universe. As an energy carrier, its **Physical Properties** govern its thermodynamic, transport, and storage behaviour, while its **Chemical Properties** govern reactivity, combustion, flammability, and safety interactions. In KTU 2024 module 3 context, these properties form the *engineering baseline* for storage vessel design, pipeline transport, fuel cell performance, and explosion prevention.

Hydrogen exists as a **diatomic molecule (H₂)** under standard conditions and exists in two nuclear spin isomers:

* **Ortho-hydrogen (o-H₂)**: parallel nuclear spins (triplet state, J = odd)
* **Para-hydrogen (p-H₂)**: antiparallel nuclear spins (singlet state, J = even)

At **room temperature (300 K)**, the equilibrium mixture is approximately **75% ortho and 25% para**. At **20.27 K (boiling point)**, the equilibrium shifts to nearly **100% para-hydrogen**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine Hydrogen as the "Invisible Lightweight Champion" of Gases** 🥊

Picture a boxing ring where every gas molecule is a boxer:
* **Hydrogen** is the **lightest featherweight** — incredibly small, fast, and nimble.
* Because it weighs almost nothing, it **escapes through the tiniest cracks** (high leakage).
* Because it moves extremely fast, it **mixes with air almost instantly** (high diffusivity).
* Because it is so energetic, it **burns with the widest flammable range** of any fuel.
* Because it is so small, it **sneaks into steel** and makes it brittle (embrittlement).

This is why hydrogen behaves uniquely — *small size + low mass + high reactivity = extreme properties across the board*.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Properties

Hydrogen's properties are formally classified into two engineering domains:

| Domain | Property Type | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Mechanical, Thermal, Transport | Storage, Compression, Liquefaction, Flow |
| **Chemical** | Combustion, Reactivity, Toxicity | Safety, Fuel Cells, Engines, Explosions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Physical Constants (Highlight for Exams)**
> * Atomic mass of H: **1.00794 u**
> * Molecular mass of H₂: **2.01588 g/mol**
> * Universal Gas Constant (R): **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> * STP molar volume: **22.414 L/mol**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Properties — Detailed Breakdown

#### A. Mechanical & Volumetric Properties

1. **Density (Gas, STP)**:
   * $\rho_{H_2} = 0.0899 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — the lowest of all gases (helium is 0.1786 kg/m³).
   * This low density means hydrogen provides **~3× less energy per unit volume** than methane in gaseous form, motivating liquefaction or compression.

2. **Density (Liquid, 20.27 K)**:
   * $\rho_{L} = 70.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — extremely high density jump (≈ 800× gas density).
   * This makes **LH₂ (Liquid Hydrogen)** an efficient storage mode for aerospace and long-distance transport.

3. **Compressibility Factor (Z)**:
   * Hydrogen deviates from ideal gas behaviour at high pressures.
   * At 70 MPa, Z ≈ 1.38 — a critical input for compressor and tank design.

#### B. Thermal Properties

1. **Specific Heat Capacity (Cp)**: $C_p = 14.31 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ — the **highest** of all gases.
   * This means hydrogen **absorbs large amounts of heat per unit temperature rise**, making it an excellent coolant (rocket nozzle regenerative cooling).

2. **Heat of Combustion**:
   * HHV (Higher Heating Value): **141.86 MJ/kg**
   * LHV (Lower Heating Value): **119.96 MJ/kg**
   * **On a mass basis, hydrogen has ~3× the energy of gasoline** (44 MJ/kg). On a volume basis, however, it is far lower.

3. **Boiling Point & Critical Point**:
   * Boiling Point: **20.27 K** — the lowest of any substance except helium.
   * Critical Temperature: **32.98 K**, Critical Pressure: **1.293 MPa**.
   * Below 32.98 K, hydrogen exists as a liquid; above, it can only be a gas (no two-phase liquid-vapour distinction possible).

#### C. Transport Properties

1. **Viscosity**: $\mu = 8.76 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Pa·s}$ (at 300 K) — the **lowest** of all gases, allowing easy flow through pipes but also easy leakage.

2. **Thermal Conductivity**: $k = 0.182 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ — the **highest** of all gases, ~7× that of air. This enhances convective heat transfer in heat exchangers and PEM fuel cell cooling.

3. **Diffusion Coefficient in Air**: $D_{H_2\text{-air}} = 6.11 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ — the **highest** of any gas, due to low molecular mass and small molecular diameter (0.289 nm).

4. **Speed of Sound**: $a = 1295 \text{ m/s}$ (highest among gases) — relevant to **deflagration-to-detonation transition (DDT)** analysis.

---

### 2.2 Chemical Properties — Detailed Breakdown

#### A. Combustion Properties

1. **Flammability Limits (in air)**: **4% to 75% by volume** — the **widest** of any fuel.
   * Methane: 5–15%, Propane: 2.1–9.5%, Gasoline: 1.4–7.6%.
   * This wide range means **almost any air-hydrogen mixture can ignite** — a major safety concern.

2. **Flammability Limits (in pure O₂)**: **4% to 94%** — even wider!

3. **Detonation Limits**: **18% to 59%** (in air) — the envelope where detonations (supersonic shock-driven combustion) become possible.

4. **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)**: **0.02 mJ** — the **lowest of all fuels** (methane: 0.29 mJ, gasoline: 0.24 mJ).
   * A **static electricity spark**, a **loose electrical contact**, or even **friction** can ignite hydrogen. This drives intrinsically safe (Ex-rated) equipment requirements.

5. **Auto-Ignition Temperature**: **573 °C** (in air) — relatively high, but irrelevant if a spark is present.

6. **Laminar Burning Velocity**: $S_L = 1.85 \text{ m/s}$ (stoichiometric mixture) — the **fastest** of all common fuels (methane: 0.4 m/s).
   * This means hydrogen flames **propagate rapidly**, requiring fast-acting flame detectors and deflagration vents.

7. **Quenching Distance**: **0.64 mm** — the smallest of all fuels.
   * Hydrogen flames can squeeze through narrow gaps that would quench methane flames. This requires **flame arresters with much finer mesh** and special design.

8. **Adiabatic Flame Temperature**: **2045 °C** (stoichiometric, in air).

9. **Octane Number**: **>130** — ideal for internal combustion engines with high compression ratios.

#### B. Reactivity with Materials

1. **Hydrogen Embrittlement**: At high pressures and temperatures, atomic H **diffuses into steel**, recombines to H₂ at grain boundaries, and causes **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** and **hydrogen environment embrittlement (HEE)**.
2. **Reacts with Halogens**: $\text{H}_2 + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{HCl}$
3. **Reacts with Metal Oxides (Reduction)**: $\text{CuO} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{Cu} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (used in metallurgy)
4. **Forms Hydrides**: With metals (Pd, Ti, MgH₂) and non-metals (NH₃, CH₄).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Property | Symbol | Equation / Value | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density (STP) | $\rho$ | $0.0899$ | kg/m³ | Tank sizing |
| 2 | Density (Liquid, 20 K) | $\rho_L$ | $70.8$ | kg/m³ | LH₂ tank sizing |
| 3 | Molar Mass | $M$ | $2.01588 \times 10^{-3}$ | kg/mol | All gas-law calcs |
| 4 | Boiling Point | $T_b$ | $20.27$ | K | Liquefaction |
| 5 | Critical Temperature | $T_c$ | $32.98$ | K | Phase diagram |
| 6 | Critical Pressure | $P_c$ | $1.293$ | MPa | Phase diagram |
| 7 | Specific Heat (Cp) | $C_p$ | $14.31$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Thermal design |
| 8 | Specific Heat Ratio | $\gamma$ | $1.405$ | — | Compressor work |
| 9 | Viscosity | $\mu$ | $8.76 \times 10^{-6}$ | Pa·s | Pipe flow |
| 10 | Thermal Conductivity | $k$ | $0.182$ | W/(m·K) | Heat exchangers |
| 11 | Diffusivity in Air | $D$ | $6.11 \times 10^{-5}$ | m²/s | Leak dispersion |
| 12 | HHV | $Q_{HHV}$ | $141.86$ | MJ/kg | Energy content |
| 13 | LHV | $Q_{LHV}$ | $119.96$ | MJ/kg | Engine / fuel cell |
| 14 | Flammability Limit | $L_f$ | $4 - 75$ (air) | vol % | Safety design |
| 15 | Detonation Limit | $L_d$ | $18 - 59$ (air) | vol % | Blast design |
| 16 | MIE | $E_{min}$ | $0.02$ | mJ | Equipment zoning |
| 17 | Burning Velocity | $S_L$ | $1.85$ | m/s | Vent sizing |
| 18 | Quenching Distance | $d_q$ | $0.64$ | mm | Flame arrester |
| 19 | Auto-Ignition Temp | $T_{auto}$ | $573$ | °C | Hot surface safety |
| 20 | Flame Temperature | $T_{ad}$ | $2045$ | °C | Material selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip**: For numerical problems on hydrogen energy, the specific gas constant $R_{H_2} = 4124 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ (highest of all gases) is often the key. It is derived from the universal gas constant divided by molar mass.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Hydrogen Embrittlement** analysis governs material selection for storage tanks, pipelines, and pressure vessels (e.g., austenitic stainless steels preferred).
* **Wide flammability range** mandates **electrical classification** of all equipment in hydrogen facilities (ATEX/IECEx zones).
* **Low quenching distance** requires **fine-mesh flame arresters** (typically <0.5 mm aperture).
* **High diffusivity** means hydrogen leaks disperse rapidly in open air — beneficial outdoors but **trap-prone indoors**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Specific Gas Constant of Hydrogen

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive $R_{H_2} = 4124 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ from the universal gas constant.

**Step 1 — Start with the universal gas constant and molar mass.**

The specific gas constant for any gas is given by:

$$R_{specific} = \frac{R_{universal}}{M}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute standard values.**

$R_{universal} = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$

$M_{H_2} = 2.01588 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$

**Step 3 — Perform the division.**

$$R_{H_2} = \frac{8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}}{2.01588 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}}$$

$$R_{H_2} = 4124.47 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 4 — Round to engineering accuracy.**

$$\boxed{R_{H_2} \approx 4124 \text{ J/(kg·K)}}$$

This is the **highest specific gas constant of all substances** because hydrogen has the **lowest molar mass**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Density of Hydrogen at Non-Standard Conditions (Ideal Gas)

**Step 1 — Start with the ideal gas law.**

$$PV = nRT$$

**Step 2 — Convert moles (n) to mass (m).**

$$n = \frac{m}{M} \implies PV = \frac{m}{M}RT$$

**Step 3 — Solve for density ($\rho = m/V$).**

$$P = \frac{m}{V} \cdot \frac{RT}{M} = \rho \cdot \frac{RT}{M}$$

$$\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute hydrogen-specific R or universal R + M.**

Using $R_{H_2} = 4124 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$:

$$\rho = \frac{P}{R_{H_2} \cdot T}$$

**Step 5 — Verification at STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 101325 Pa).**

$$\rho = \frac{101325}{4124 \times 273.15}$$

$$\rho = \frac{101325}{1126390.6} = 0.0899 \text{ kg/m}^3 \checkmark$$

**Matches the standard textbook value of 0.0899 kg/m³.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Compressor Work for Hydrogen

**Step 1 — Isentropic work for an ideal gas.**

For an adiabatic, reversible (isentropic) compression of an ideal gas from $P_1$ to $P_2$:

$$W_{isen} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot R_{specific} \cdot T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute hydrogen values.**

$\gamma_{H_2} = 1.405$

$R_{H_2} = 4124 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$

$(\gamma - 1)/\gamma = 0.405 / 1.405 = 0.2883$

**Step 3 — Example: Compress from 1 bar to 350 bar (typical 70 MPa tank), $T_1 = 298$ K.**

$$W = \frac{1.405}{0.405} \times 4124 \times 298 \left[ (350)^{0.2883} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Compute the pressure ratio term.**

$350^{0.2883} = e^{0.2883 \times \ln(350)} = e^{0.2883 \times 5.858} = e^{1.688} = 5.41$

**Step 5 — Final calculation.**

$$W = 3.469 \times 4124 \times 298 \times (5.41 - 1)$$

$$W = 3.469 \times 4124 \times 298 \times 4.41$$

$$W = 1.882 \times 10^{7} \text{ J/kg} = 18.82 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Compressing hydrogen to 350 bar requires **~18.82 MJ/kg** of ideal isentropic work — this is the energy penalty of compression storage. The real (actual) work is higher due to inefficiencies (typically 50–70% efficient compressors).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Why Hydrogen Differs from Other Fuels

| Property | H₂ | CH₄ | C₈H₁₈ (gasoline) | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Molar mass (g/mol) | 2.016 | 16.04 | 114.23 | H₂ needs larger volumes |
| Density (kg/m³) gas | 0.0899 | 0.717 | ~4.7 | H₂ needs compression |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 119.96 | 50.02 | 44.0 | H₂ is best on mass |
| LHV (MJ/m³) gas | 10.79 | 35.88 | ~32 | H₂ is worst on volume |
| Flammability (vol %) | 4–75 | 5–15 | 1.4–7.6 | H₂ = biggest explosion risk |
| MIE (mJ) | 0.02 | 0.29 | 0.24 | H₂ ignites from tiny sparks |
| Flame velocity (m/s) | 1.85 | 0.40 | 0.40 | H₂ burns explosively fast |
| Quenching dist (mm) | 0.64 | 2.0 | 2.0 | H₂ leaks through small gaps |
| Auto-ignition (°C) | 573 | 537 | 246 | Gasoline self-ignites easier |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often confuse LHV (Lower Heating Value) with HHV (Higher Heating Value). For fuel cells and gas engines, **LHV is used** (water leaves as vapor). For heating systems with condensation, **HHV is used**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Hydrogen Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[H2 Properties]:::root
    A --> B[Physical Properties]:::branch
    A --> C[Chemical Properties]:::branch
    B --> D[Mechanical and Volumetric]:::leaf
    B --> E[Thermal]:::leaf
    B --> F[Transport]:::leaf
    B --> G[Phase Change]:::leaf
    D --> D1[Density 0.0899 kg per m3]
    D --> D2[Molar mass 2.016 g per mol]
    D --> D3[Compressibility Z at high P]
    E --> E1[Cp 14.31 kJ per kg K]
    E --> E2[LHV 119.96 MJ per kg]
    E --> E3[Flame temp 2045 C]
    F --> F1[Viscosity 8.76e-6 Pa s]
    F --> F2[Diffusivity 6.11e-5 m2 per s]
    F --> F3[Thermal conductivity 0.182 W per m K]
    G --> G1[Boiling point 20.27 K]

    C --> I[Reactivity]:::leaf

    H --> H2[Detonation 18 to 59 pct]
    H --> H3[MIE 0.02 mJ]
```
