## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State any four physical properties of hydrogen that make it unique as a fuel carrier.*

**Model Answer (5 key points):**

1. **Lowest density** of all gases: **0.0899 kg/m³ at STP** (about 1/14th that of air).
2. **Lowest molar mass**: **2.016 g/mol** — leads to highest specific gas constant $R_{H_2} = 4124 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$.
3. **Highest thermal conductivity** among gases: **0.182 W/(m·K)** — beneficial for heat exchangers.
4. **Widest flammability range in air**: **4–75% by volume** — most reactive fuel.
5. **Lowest minimum ignition energy (MIE)**: **0.02 mJ** — easy to ignite from stray sparks.
6. **Highest specific heat (Cp)**: **14.31 kJ/(kg·K)** — excellent coolant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split**: 4 properties × 0.5 mark each + 0.5 for any supporting number = 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define (i) Flammability Limit and (ii) Quenching Distance with respect to hydrogen.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Flammability Limit:**
The range of hydrogen concentration in an oxidizer (air/oxygen) mixture within which the mixture can be ignited and propagate a flame. For hydrogen in air, it is **4% to 75% by volume** — the widest of any common fuel.

**(ii) Quenching Distance:**
The minimum gap width through which a flame cannot propagate (the flame is "quenched" by heat loss to the walls). For hydrogen, the quenching distance is **0.64 mm** — the smallest among common fuels, meaning hydrogen flames can squeeze through narrower gaps than methane or gasoline flames.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split**: (i) 1.5 marks + (ii) 1.5 marks = 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With neat sketches and standard property values, describe the **physical properties of hydrogen** that influence its **storage and transport** as a fuel. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **chemical properties of hydrogen** related to **combustion and flammability**, and explain why these pose **unique safety challenges** in hydrogen energy systems. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a): — Physical Properties for Storage & Transport**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Hydrogen exists as H₂ gas at STP, with physical properties that demand specialized storage and transport technologies.

**1. Density & Volumetric Energy (2 marks):**
* Gas density: $\rho = 0.0899$ kg/m³ → very low energy per unit volume (LHV ≈ 10.79 MJ/m³).
* Liquid density at 20.27 K: $\rho_L = 70.8$ kg/m³ → 800× denser.
* **Engineering Implication:** Gaseous hydrogen must be **compressed (350–700 bar)** or **liquefied (20.27 K)** to be stored efficiently. This requires heavy, thick-walled tanks.

**2. Boiling & Critical Point (2 marks):**
* $T_b = 20.27$ K, $T_c = 32.98$ K, $P_c = 1.293$ MPa.
* **Engineering Implication:** LH₂ storage requires **cryogenic insulation** (multi-layer vacuum + perlite). Boil-off losses of 0.1–0.5% per day occur even in well-insulated tanks.

**3. Diffusivity & Viscosity (2 marks):**
* Diffusivity in air: $D = 6.11 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s (highest of all gases).
* Viscosity: $\mu = 8.76 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa·s (lowest of all gases).
* **Engineering Implication:** Hydrogen **leaks through the smallest cracks**, requiring **welded (not threaded) connections**, **metal gaskets**, and **periodic leak testing** with H₂-specific sensors.

---

**Model Solution (b): — Chemical Properties: Combustion & Safety**

**1. Flammability Limits (2 marks):**
* In air: 4–75% (vol). In O₂: 4–94%.
* **Safety Challenge:** Almost any air–hydrogen mixture can burn. Facilities must follow **ATEX zone classification** and use **Ex-certified equipment**.

**2. Minimum Ignition Energy (2 marks):**
* MIE = 0.02 mJ (lowest of all fuels).
* **Safety Challenge:** Even **static electricity** from clothing can ignite H₂. Personnel must wear **anti-static suits** and **grounding straps**.

**3. Flame Velocity & Quenching Distance (2 marks):**
* $S_L = 1.85$ m/s, $d_q = 0.64$ mm.
* **Safety Challenge:** Fast flame propagation means **deflagration vents** and **flame arresters** must be sized appropriately, with very fine mesh (<0.5 mm).

**4. Detonation Limits (1 mark):**
* 18–59% in air. In confined/unobstructed regions, flame acceleration can transition to **detonation** (Mach 5–10 shock-driven flame). Buildings and equipment must withstand **blast overpressures up to 1 MPa**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** Derive the **specific gas constant of hydrogen** and use it to calculate the **density of hydrogen** at 25 °C and 200 bar. Compare with its STP density. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **hydrogen embrittlement** in detail. What materials are used to prevent it in storage tanks and pipelines? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a): — Derivation & Density Calculation**

**Step 1 — Definition of specific gas constant (1 mark):**
The specific gas constant is the universal gas constant per unit mass:

$$R_{specific} = \frac{R_u}{M}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (1 mark):**
$R_u = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
$M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$

**Step 3 — Derive (1 mark):**

$$R_{H_2} = \frac{8.314}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} = 4124 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 4 — Density formula (1 mark):**

$$\rho = \frac{PM}{RT} = \frac{P}{R_{H_2} \cdot T}$$

**Step 5 — Plug in conditions (1 mark):**
T = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 K, P = 200 × 10⁵ Pa = 2 × 10⁷ Pa.

**Step 6 — Compute (1 mark):**

$$\rho = \frac{2 \times 10^{7}}{4124 \times 298.15} = \frac{2 \times 10^{7}}{1.2295 \times 10^{6}} = 16.27 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 7 — Compare with STP (1 mark):**
* STP: 0.0899 kg/m³
* 200 bar: 16.27 kg/m³
* **Density increase: 16.27 / 0.0899 ≈ 181×** (ideal gas prediction; real Z-factor correction: Z ≈ 1.05, so actual ≈ 15.5 kg/m³).

---

**Model Solution (b): — Hydrogen Embrittlement**

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
Hydrogen embrittlement is a **permanent loss of ductility and tensile strength** in metals caused by **atomic hydrogen ingress** into the metal lattice, typically at high pressures (>10 MPa) and moderate temperatures.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
1. H₂ molecule **dissociates** on the metal surface (catalyzed by Fe, Ni, Pt).
2. Atomic H **diffuses** into the metal lattice.
3. H atoms **recombine at grain boundaries, voids, or inclusions**, forming internal H₂ pressure.
4. This causes **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)**, **decohesion**, and **stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)**.
5. Embrittlement is **most severe** in **high-strength steels** (e.g., AISI 4140).

**Material Selection (2 marks):**
* **Austenitic stainless steels** (SS 304L, 316L) — resistant due to FCC structure.
* **Aluminium alloys** (6061-T6, 7075-T6) — common in Type IV tanks.
* **Carbon-fibre reinforced polymers (CFRP)** — for 70 MPa Type IV tanks.
* **Inconel, Monel, Hastelloy** — for severe service.
* **Avoid** high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steels without barriers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Molar mass confusion**: H atom ≠ H₂ molecule. Use **2.016 g/mol** for the molecule, not 1.008 g/mol.
> 2. **HHV vs LHV**: Examiners often specify "heating value" — if not specified, **state both** to be safe.
> 3. **Ignoring compressibility (Z)**: At 70 MPa, Z ≠ 1. Real-gas corrections can change answers by 5–15%.
> 4. **Units**: Be consistent — convert bar to Pa, °C to K, mol to kgmol. Marks are deducted for unit errors.
> 5. **Flammability limits**: Some students write "4% to 75%" without specifying **volume % in air** — lose 0.5 mark.
> 6. **MIE = 0.02 mJ**: Often confused with auto-ignition temperature (573 °C) — these are different concepts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Properties of Hydrogen**

**Physical Properties (Must Memorize for KTU 2024):**
* ☐ Molar mass = **2.016 g/mol** (lightest molecule)
* ☐ Gas density (STP) = **0.0899 kg/m³** (lowest of all gases)
* ☐ Liquid density = **70.8 kg/m³** at 20.27 K
* ☐ Boiling point = **20.27 K** (lowest, after He)
* ☐ Critical T, P = **32.98 K, 1.293 MPa**
* ☐ Cp = **14.31 kJ/(kg·K)** (highest of all gases)
* ☐ $\gamma = C_p/C_v = $ **1.405**
* ☐ $R_{specific} = $ **4124 J/(kg·K)** (highest of all substances)
* ☐ Thermal conductivity = **0.182 W/(m·K)** (highest of all gases)
* ☐ Diffusivity in air = **$6.11 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s** (highest of all gases)
* ☐ LHV = **119.96 MJ/kg**; HHV = **141.86 MJ/kg**
* ☐ LHV (per volume) = **10.79 MJ/m³** (very low!)

**Chemical Properties (Safety Critical):**
* ☐ Flammability in air = **4–75% vol** (widest)
* ☐ Flammability in O₂ = **4–94% vol**
* ☐ Detonation in air = **18–59% vol**
* ☐ MIE = **0.02 mJ** (lowest of all fuels)
* ☐ Auto-ignition T = **573 °C**
* ☐ Laminar burning velocity = **1.85 m/s**
* ☐ Quenching distance = **0.64 mm** (smallest)
* ☐ Adiabatic flame T = **2045 °C**
* ☐ Octane number = **>130**
* ☐ Embrittlement — affects high-strength steels

**Engineering Implications (One-liners for Viva/Exam):**
* ☐ Low density → compress or liquefy
* ☐ Wide flammability → ATEX zone classification mandatory
* ☐ Low MIE → intrinsically safe (Ex) equipment
* ☐ Small quenching distance → fine-mesh flame arresters
* ☐ High diffusivity → leak-proof welded joints
* ☐ Embrittlement → use SS 316L or CFRP-lined tanks

**Universal Quick Formula:**
* ☐ $\rho = PM/RT = P/(R_{specific} \cdot T)$
* ☐ $R_{specific} = R_u/M = 4124$ J/(kg·K) for H₂
* ☐ Energy density: H₂ LHV per volume ≈ 1/3 of gasoline, but 3× per mass

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Storage Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** General Storage Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Storage (Definition):**
> Hydrogen storage refers to the engineered containment of molecular hydrogen ($H_2$) in a usable form — gaseous, liquid, or solid-bound — such that it can be reliably released on demand at acceptable purity, pressure, and flow rate, while satisfying volumetric, gravimetric, thermodynamic, kinetic, and safety constraints for a given end-use application.

The **"General Storage Methods"** classification refers to the **four canonical physical/chemical states** in which hydrogen is practically stockpiled in the energy economy:

1. **Compressed Gas Hydrogen (CGH₂)** — gaseous $H_2$ at high pressure (typically **350 bar** or **700 bar**).
2. **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)** — cryogenic $H_2$ at **20.3 K** (boiling point at 1 atm).
3. **Solid-State Hydrogen** — $H_2$ bound to a host material (metal hydrides, complex hydrides, chemical hydrides, adsorbents).
4. **Underground Hydrogen Storage (UHS)** — large-scale geological storage in salt caverns, depleted gas/oil reservoirs, or aquifers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why General Storage is a Bottleneck:**
> Hydrogen has an extremely low ambient-condition density ($\rho_{H_2} = 0.0838 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 1 atm, 298 K) and a tendency to embrittle metals, making its storage fundamentally more difficult than that of liquid hydrocarbons or natural gas.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine hydrogen as a **hyperactive puppy (H₂ molecule)** that:
- **Refuses to stay still** → It leaks through almost every microscopic crack (hydrogen embrittlement + small molecular diameter of 2.89 Å).
- **Takes up enormous "room"** → A 1 kg chunk of hydrogen occupies **11.9 m³** as an ideal gas at 1 atm (the size of a small bus).
- **Escapes cold** → To shrink it, you must squeeze it (compression) or freeze it to cryogenic temperatures (liquefaction).
- **Can be "caged" in a sponge** → Some metals and frameworks chemically or physically grab $H_2$ molecules and lock them in (solid-state).

Therefore, we use four "tricks" to control this puppy: **squeeze it, freeze it, cage it, or bury a giant kennel for it underground**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metrics (bolded as required by KTU 2024 rubric):**
> - **LHV of H₂** = **120 MJ/kg**
> - **HHV of H₂** = **142 MJ/kg**
> - **Density of LH₂** = **70.85 kg/m³**
> - **Density of H₂ gas (1 atm, 298 K)** = **0.0838 kg/m³**
> - **Universal Gas Constant** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
> - **Molar mass of H₂** $M = 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$
> - **Boiling point of H₂** at 1 atm = **20.3 K (−252.87 °C)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Hydrogen Storage is Engineering-Critical

Hydrogen must be stored because production (electrolysis, SMR, etc.) and consumption (fuel cells, combustion, refining) are **temporally and spatially decoupled**. The choice of storage method is governed by four KTU-defined pillars:

- **Gravimetric Density** $\rho_m$ — kg of H₂ per kg of storage system (kg/kg).
- **Volumetric Density** $\rho_v$ — kg of H₂ per m³ of storage volume.
- **Thermodynamic Reversibility** — energy cost to *put in* and *take out* hydrogen.
- **Safety & Cycle Life** — leakage, embrittlement, fatigue over thousands of cycles.

### 2.2 Method 1: Compressed Gas Hydrogen (CGH₂)

**Operating principle:** $H_2$ gas is pressurised into Type III (metal liner + carbon-fiber wrap) or Type IV (polymer liner + carbon-fiber wrap) cylinders.

**Thermodynamic backbone — Ideal Gas Law:**

$$PV = nRT$$

For a fixed mass of hydrogen ($n = m/M$):

$$P V = \frac{m}{M} R T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_v = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{PM}{RT}$$

**Why high pressure?** Because $\rho_v \propto P$ linearly. Increasing P from 1 bar to 700 bar raises density by a factor of 700.

**Engineering challenges:**
- Compression work is real and non-trivial; isothermal work $W = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$.
- Multi-stage compression with intercooling is required to approach isothermal conditions.
- Carbon-fiber composite tanks are expensive but provide $\rho_m \approx 0.05 - 0.07$ kg/kg system-level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DoE 2025 Targets (for board exam reference):**
> - Gravimetric: **0.055 kg H₂/kg system**
> - Volumetric: **0.040 kg H₂/L system**
> - Cost: **$9/kWh stored**

### 2.3 Method 2: Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)

**Operating principle:** Cool $H_2$ below its critical temperature ($T_c = 33.2$ K) and critical pressure ($P_c = 13.0$ bar), then condense to liquid at **20.3 K** and atmospheric pressure.

**Liquefaction chain (simplified):**
1. Gaseous H₂ at 298 K
2. Cool → 80 K (pre-cooling with LN₂)
3. Ortho-para conversion catalyst (must convert ortho-H₂ to para-H₂ to avoid boil-off)
4. Joule-Thomson expansion → 20.3 K → liquid

**Why ortho-para conversion matters:**
- Normal H₂ at room temperature is **75% ortho, 25% para**.
- At 20.3 K, equilibrium is **99.8% para-H₂**.
- The conversion is exothermic and slow; without catalyst, the heat release boils off ~50% of LH₂ in <1 day.

**Energy cost of liquefaction (Board favourite):**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}} = 1 - \frac{20.3}{298} \approx 0.932$$

Minimum theoretical work $\approx 3.9$ kWh/kg, but actual industrial liquefiers consume **10 – 13 kWh/kg**, i.e., **~30–40% of the LHV** of hydrogen is spent just to liquefy it.

**Storage challenges:**
- **Boil-off losses** ≈ 0.1 – 1% per day due to heat ingress.
- Need **super-insulated cryogenic dewars** (MLI: Multi-Layer Insulation).
- Highly trained handling required.

### 2.4 Method 3: Solid-State Storage

**3a) Metal Hydrides (e.g., LaNi₅H₆, MgH₂, TiFeH₁.₉):**

Hydrogen atoms occupy interstitial sites in a metal lattice via chemisorption:

$$M + \frac{x}{2} H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x \quad + \Delta H$$

Thermodynamics of absorption/desorption obey the **van't Hoff equation** — the central formula for metal-hydride system selection:

$$\ln(P_{eq}) = -\frac{\Delta H}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

where $P_{eq}$ is the equilibrium dissociation pressure, $\Delta H$ is enthalpy of hydride formation (negative, exothermic), and $\Delta S$ is entropy change.

| Hydride | $\Delta H$ (kJ/mol H₂) | $P_{eq}$ at 298 K | Practical T to release |
|---|---|---|---|
| LaNi₅H₆ | −30.8 | ~2 atm | 25–80 °C |
| TiFeH₁.₉ | −28.1 | ~5 atm | 25–120 °C |
| MgH₂ | −75.0 | ~1 atm (high T) | 300–350 °C |
| NaAlH₄ | −37 (1st step) | low | 200–250 °C |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"Lighter hydrides = stronger bonds = higher release temperature."*
> MgH₂ stores 7.6 wt% H₂ (theoretically) but is sluggish; LaNi₅ is fast but stores only ~1.4 wt%.

**3b) Complex Hydrides** — e.g., NaAlH₄, LiBH₄, LiNH₂ — release $H_2$ through chemical decomposition, often irreversibly or with poor kinetics.

**3c) Chemical Hydrides (Ammonia, LOHC):**
- **NH₃** = 17.8 wt% H₂, decomposed catalytically at 500–700 °C.
- **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHCs)** — e.g., dibenzyltoluene (DBT) — absorb/release H₂ at 200–300 °C; liquid at ambient.

**3d) Adsorbents (MOFs, Activated Carbon, CNTs):**
- Physisorption via van der Waals forces.
- Operate at cryogenic temperatures (77 K LN₂) to enhance uptake.
- Theoretical capacities up to 8–10 wt% for some MOFs.

### 2.5 Method 4: Underground Hydrogen Storage (UHS)

**Principle:** Inject $H_2$ into porous geological formations, recovered via wells.

| Storage Type | Capacity | Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| Salt caverns | $10^5 - 10^6$ m³ | Excellent (low leakage, high cycling) |
| Depleted gas fields | $>10^8$ m³ | Good (large scale, requires cushion gas) |
| Aquifers | $10^7 - 10^8$ m³ | Variable (depends on mineralogy) |

**Critical engineering issues:**
- **Geochemical reactivity:** $H_2$ reacts with sulfate-reducing bacteria, producing $H_2S$ — toxic and corrosive.
- **Purity loss** upon cycling.
- **Cushion gas** requirement (irrecoverable H₂ left in formation to maintain pressure).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal gas law | $PV = nRT$ | Pa·m³ | All storage calculations start here |
| 2 | Volumetric density of gas | $\rho_v = PM/(RT)$ | kg/m³ | Linear in P |
| 3 | Isothermal compression work | $W = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | J | Minimum for CGH₂ |
| 4 | Carnot liquefaction efficiency | $\eta = 1 - T_c/T_h$ | dimensionless | 0.932 for LH₂ |
| 5 | van't Hoff equation | $\ln(P_{eq}) = -\Delta H/(RT) + \Delta S/R$ | atm | Metal-hydride equilibria |
| 6 | LHV energy of stored H₂ | $E = m_{H_2} \times 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | J | Use LHV unless specified |
| 7 | Gravimetric density | $\rho_m = m_{H_2}/m_{system}$ | kg/kg | DoE target 0.055 |
| 8 | Density of LH₂ | $\rho_{LH_2} = 70.85$ | kg/m³ | At 20.3 K, 1 atm |
| 9 | LH₂ boil-off rate | 0.1 – 1 | %/day | Dewar-dependent |
| 10 | Volumetric energy density LH₂ | $E_v = 70.85 \times 120 = 8502$ | MJ/m³ | ~3.4× gasoline LHV basis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These formulas power fuel-cell vehicle range prediction, large-scale renewable energy storage design, and the cost-benefit analysis of $H_2$ transport vs. on-site production.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Density of Compressed Hydrogen (700 bar, 298 K)

**Given:** Storage cylinder at $P = 700$ bar = $700 \times 10^5$ Pa, ambient temperature $T = 298$ K.

**Goal:** Find $\rho_v$ in kg H₂ per m³ of cylinder volume.

**Step 1:** Start with the ideal gas law applied to $H_2$:

$$P V = n R T$$

**Step 2:** Solve for molar concentration $n/V$:

$$\frac{n}{V} = \frac{P}{R T}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$\frac{n}{V} = \frac{700 \times 10^{5}}{8.314 \times 298} = \frac{7.0 \times 10^{7}}{2477.572}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the denominator:

$$\frac{n}{V} = \frac{7.0 \times 10^{7}}{2477.572} = 28{,}254 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Convert molar density to mass density using $m = n \cdot M$ with $M = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol:

$$\rho_v = \frac{n}{V} \times M = 28{,}254 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\rho_v = 56.96 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5:** Compare with LH₂ density:

$$\frac{\rho_{v,CGH_2}}{\rho_{LH_2}} = \frac{56.96}{70.85} = 0.804$$

i.e., CGH₂ at 700 bar is only **80% as dense as liquid hydrogen**, confirming why CGH₂ needs even higher pressures (Type IV tanks at 700 bar) and why LH₂ is preferred where space is at a premium.

**Step 6:** Compute the volumetric energy density of CGH₂:

$$E_{v,CGH_2} = 56.96 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 120 \text{ MJ/kg} = 6835 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

**Step 7:** Isothermal work to compress from $P_1 = 1$ bar to $P_2 = 700$ bar at 298 K (per kg H₂):

$$W = n R T \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{1000}{2.016} \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \ln(700)$$

$$W = 496.0 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times 6.551$$

$$W = 496.0 \times 2477.572 \times 6.551$$

$$W = 8{,}050{,}000 \text{ J/kg} \approx 8.05 \text{ MJ/kg} \approx 2.24 \text{ kWh/kg}$$

This is **~6.7% of the LHV** of the stored hydrogen.

### 3.2 Derivation: van't Hoff Equation from Thermodynamic Equilibrium

The reaction $M + \frac{x}{2} H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x$ has equilibrium governed by Gibbs free energy:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$$

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0 \Rightarrow T = \Delta H / \Delta S$ (this defines the decomposition temperature at 1 atm).

For non-trivial pressures, $\Delta G = -RT \ln(K)$, where $K = P_{eq}^{-x/2}$ (for solid + gas equilibria, $K$ depends only on $H_2$ pressure).

Equating the two expressions for $\Delta G$:

$$-RT \ln(P_{eq}^{-x/2}) = \Delta H - T \Delta S$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$ and using $\ln(P^{-x/2}) = -\frac{x}{2} \ln(P)$:

$$RT \cdot \left(-\frac{x}{2}\right) \ln(P_{eq}) = \Delta H - T \Delta S$$

Solving for $\ln(P_{eq})$:

$$\ln(P_{eq}) = -\frac{\Delta H}{R T} \cdot \frac{2}{x} + \frac{\Delta S}{R} \cdot \frac{2}{x}$$

Absorbing the $2/x$ factor into the constants (since it is the same for a given hydride), the standard textbook form is:

$$\ln(P_{eq}) = -\frac{\Delta H}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

**Verification for LaNi₅H₆** ($x = 6$, $\Delta H = -30.8$ kJ/mol H₂, $\Delta S = -108$ J/(mol·K)) at $T = 298$ K:

$$\ln(P_{eq}) = -\frac{(-30{,}800)}{8.314 \times 298} + \frac{-108}{8.314}$$

$$= +12.43 - 12.99 = -0.56$$

$$P_{eq} = e^{-0.56} = 0.57 \text{ atm} \approx 0.6 \text{ atm}$$

This is consistent with the empirical pressure-composition isotherm (PCI) data for LaNi₅ at 25 °C. ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Four Methods

| Parameter | CGH₂ (700 bar) | LH₂ (20.3 K) | Metal Hydride (LaNi₅) | Underground (Salt Cavern) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric density (kg H₂/m³)** | 40 – 57 | 70.85 | 80 – 100 (crystal) | 5 – 15 |
| **Gravimetric density (system, kg/kg)** | 0.05 – 0.07 | 0.10 – 0.35 | 0.013 – 0.075 | negligible tank mass |
| **Operating T (K)** | 298 | 20.3 | 298 – 600 | 298 – 350 |
| **Operating P (bar)** | 350 – 700 | 1 – 10 | 1 – 30 | 50 – 200 |
| **Energy in/out cost** | ~8 MJ/kg | ~36–47 MJ/kg (≈30% LHV) | 15 – 25 MJ/kg | ~1–3 MJ/kg (compression only) |
| **Cycle life** | >5000 | boil-off continuous | 1000 – 5000 | seasonal/annual |
| **Safety concern** | High-pressure rupture | Cryogenic burns, $H_2$ embrittlement, asphyxiation | Pyrophoric dust | Subsurface leak, $H_2S$ |
| **Best application** | FCEV vehicles, stationary backup | Aerospace, long-haul trucks | Stationary, small portable | Grid-scale, seasonal |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **"List any three general methods of hydrogen storage and state the principle behind each in one line."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Compressed Gas Hydrogen (CGH₂):** Hydrogen gas is stored at high pressure (350 / 700 bar) in Type III/IV composite cylinders; principle: increase density by isothermal compression ($PV = nRT$).
2. **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂):** Hydrogen is cooled below its critical temperature to 20.3 K and stored in cryogenic dewars; principle: phase change to liquid increases density to 70.85 kg/m³.
3. **Metal Hydride Storage:** Hydrogen dissociatively absorbs into interstitials of metals like LaNi₅ or Mg; principle: reversible chemisorption governed by van't Hoff thermodynamics.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **"Why is ortho-para hydrogen conversion critical during LH₂ storage? Give the equilibrium para fraction at 20.3 K."**

**Model Answer:**
Normal hydrogen at 298 K is 75% ortho / 25% para. At the liquid storage temperature (20.3 K), equilibrium composition is **99.8% para-H₂**. The conversion is exothermic and self-driven by heat release, which causes continuous **boil-off losses** if not catalysed (using Fe(OH)₃ or similar catalysts). Without catalytic conversion, ~50% of liquid hydrogen boils off within a day, making long-duration storage economically impossible.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 3]**
> *(a)* Classify the general methods of hydrogen storage with a neat block diagram. Discuss the working principle, advantages, and limitations of compressed gas and liquid hydrogen storage.
> *(b)* A fuel-cell car uses a 700-bar Type IV cylinder with internal volume 120 L. Estimate (i) the mass of hydrogen stored at 298 K assuming ideal gas behaviour, (ii) the volumetric energy density, and (iii) the minimum isothermal work to fill the tank from 1 bar. Use LHV = 120 MJ/kg.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification & Discussion — 7 marks**

Refer to the classification block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.

**Compressed Gas (CGH₂):**
- *Principle:* Isothermal compression of $H_2$ to 350/700 bar inside Type III/IV tanks.
- *Advantages:* Mature technology; reversible; high round-trip efficiency; fast refuelling (~3 min).
- *Limitations:* Low volumetric density; high-pressure safety risk; expensive carbon-fiber tanks.

**Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂):**
- *Principle:* Cool below 33.2 K → condense to liquid at 20.3 K, store in MLI-insulated dewars.
- *Advantages:* Highest practically usable volumetric density; ambient pressure storage.
- *Limitations:* Boil-off losses (0.1 – 1%/day); 30–40% of LHV lost in liquefaction; ortho-para issue.

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

(i) Mass of hydrogen stored:

$$n = \frac{PV}{RT} = \frac{(700 \times 10^5)(120 \times 10^{-3})}{(8.314)(298)} = \frac{8.4 \times 10^{6}}{2477.572}$$

$$n = 3390 \text{ mol}$$

$$m = n \times M = 3390 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 6.83 \text{ kg}$$

(ii) Volumetric energy density:

$$E_v = \frac{m \times LHV}{V} = \frac{6.83 \times 120}{0.120} = 6830 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

(iii) Minimum isothermal work to fill from 1 bar:

$$W = nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = 3390 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \ln(700)$$

$$W = 3390 \times 2477.572 \times 6.551 = 55{,}011{,}000 \text{ J} \approx 55.0 \text{ MJ}$$

Work per kg of $H_2$: $W/m = 55.0 / 6.83 = 8.05$ MJ/kg, i.e., 6.7% of LHV.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> *(a)* Explain solid-state hydrogen storage in metal hydrides. Derive the van't Hoff equation and compute the equilibrium pressure of LaNi₅H₆ at 298 K given $\Delta H = -30.8$ kJ/mol H₂ and $\Delta S = -108$ J/(mol·K).
> *(b)* Compare the four general storage methods on the basis of volumetric density, gravimetric density, energy cost, and typical application. State the U.S. Department of Energy 2025 onboard storage targets.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solid-State Storage & van't Hoff Derivation — 7 marks**

Solid-state storage works by reversible chemisorption of hydrogen atoms into a host metal lattice:

$$M + \frac{x}{2} H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x \quad + \Delta H \text{ (exothermic)}$$

**Derivation:** (See Section 3.2 of these notes for the full derivation.)

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$, but the more general formulation is $-RT \ln(K) = \Delta H - T\Delta S$, where $K = P_{eq}^{-x/2}$. Rearranging and absorbing $2/x$ into the constant:

$$\ln(P_{eq}) = -\frac{\Delta H}{R T} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

**Plugging in values for LaNi₅H₆ at 298 K:**

$$\ln(P_{eq}) = -\frac{(-30{,}800)}{(8.314)(298)} + \frac{-108}{8.314}$$

$$\ln(P_{eq}) = +12.43 - 12.99 = -0.56$$

$$P_{eq} = e^{-0.56} = 0.57 \text{ atm} \approx 0.6 \text{ atm}$$

This is well below atmospheric, confirming that LaNi₅H₆ is **thermodynamically too stable** to release H₂ at 25 °C without external heating. In practice, mild heating (50–80 °C) drives the desorption forward, raising $P_{eq}$ above 1 atm.

**(b) Comparative Analysis & DoE Targets — 7 marks**

(Refer to the Analytical Matrix in Section 3.3 of these notes.)

**DoE 2025 Targets (Board favourite):**
- Gravimetric density: **5.5 wt% (0.055 kg H₂/kg system)**
- Volumetric density: **0.040 kg H₂/L system**
- Cost: **$9 per kWh stored**
- Cycle life: **>5000 cycles**
- Operating temperature: **−40 to 85 °C**

**Method Selection Summary:**
- *CGH₂*: Best for light-duty FCEV (Toyota Mirai uses 700 bar Type IV tanks).
- *LH₂*: Best for aerospace and long-distance trucks.
- *Metal hydride*: Best for small portable, stationary backup, and safety-critical applications.
- *Underground*: Best for grid-scale, seasonal, and industrial hydrogen storage (cf. HyStor project in the UK).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not use HHV (142 MJ/kg) by default** — KTU board typically expects **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** unless the question states "higher heating value."
> 2. **For metal hydrides, sign conventions matter**: $\Delta H$ is **negative** (exothermic formation). Students often drop the negative sign, producing absurd answers like $10^{10}$ atm.
> 3. **For CGH₂, always convert bar → Pa** (multiply by $10^5$). Many students lose 1–2 marks by writing $P = 700$ and getting wrong units.
> 4. **Ortho-para question trap**: Mentioning the percentage is not enough — you must also state the catalyst requirement to earn full 3 marks.
> 5. **Carnot efficiency trap**: For liquefaction, students often compute the *reverse* ratio; the correct formula is $1 - T_c/T_h$, not $T_h/T_c - 1$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Hydrogen's ambient density is **0.0838 kg/m³**; storage is fundamentally a **density problem**.
- Four canonical methods: **CGH₂, LH₂, Solid-State (Metal Hydrides, Complex Hydrides, LOHC, MOFs), Underground (Salt Cavern / Depleted Reservoir / Aquifer)**.
- **CGH₂** is governed by $PV = nRT$; $\rho_v = PM/(RT)$; isothermal compression work $W = nRT\ln(P_2/P_1)$.
- **LH₂** stored at **20.3 K, 1 atm**, density **70.85 kg/m³**; liquefaction costs **~30–40% of LHV**; boil-off **0.1–1 %/day**.
- **Ortho-para conversion** is essential; equilibrium at 20.3 K is **99.8% para-H₂**; catalysts like Fe(OH)₃ are used.
- **van't Hoff equation** governs metal-hydride equilibria: $\ln(P_{eq}) = -\Delta H/(RT) + \Delta S/R$.
- LaNi₅H₆: $\Delta H \approx -30.8$ kJ/mol H₂, $P_{eq} \approx 0.6$ atm at 298 K.
- MgH₂: high storage (~7.6 wt%) but requires **300–350 °C** to release.
- **Underground salt caverns** are the only economically viable **large-scale, seasonal** storage.
- **DoE 2025 targets**: 5.5 wt%, 0.040 kg H₂/L, $9/kWh, >5000 cycles.
- LHV of $H_2$ = **120 MJ/kg** (default unless stated).
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol.
- For compressed hydrogen at 700 bar and 298 K, $\rho_v \approx 57$ kg/m³, i.e., ~80% of LH₂ density.
- Compression work alone consumes ~6–7% of LHV; liquefaction consumes 30–40% — therefore **CGH₂ is thermodynamically more efficient** than LH₂ for storage.
- Metal hydrides offer excellent safety and reversibility but suffer from low **system-level** gravimetric density due to heavy host metal mass.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressed Storage - Composite Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compressed Storage - Composite Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Composite Cylinder** for compressed hydrogen storage is a high-pressure vessel constructed from a non-load-bearing (or semi-load-bearing) **polymer liner** completely overwrapped with a continuous, load-bearing **fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) composite**, typically carbon fiber embedded in an epoxy matrix. According to KTU 2024 syllabus and ISO 11439 / UN/ECE R134 standards, these are classified as **Type IV pressure vessels** and are the industry benchmark for 350 bar and 700 bar on-board automotive hydrogen storage, achieving gravimetric efficiencies of **~6 wt%** and volumetric capacities of **~25–35 g H₂/L**.

### 1.2 Classification of Compressed Hydrogen Storage Vessels
| Type | Liner Material | Reinforcement | Typical Use |
|:----:|:--------------:|:-------------:|:-----------:|
| Type I | Steel (none) | None | Industrial, stationary |
| Type II | Steel | Hoop-wrapped glass/carbon | CNG buses |
| Type III | Aluminum | Full composite wrap | CNG vehicles |
| **Type IV** | **HDPE / Polymer** | **Full carbon-fiber composite** | **FCV (70 MPa)** |
| Type V | None (all-composite) | All composite | Under research |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, focus on **Type IV composite cylinders**, as they are the de-facto standard for Fuel Cell Vehicles (FCVs) like the Toyota Mirai and Hyundai NEXO.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine trying to carry a fragile, pressurized soda can (the **polymer liner**) containing a very volatile gas. The soda can alone would burst easily. Now imagine wrapping that can tightly with thousands of strong, lightweight fishing lines (the **carbon fibers**) glued together with superglue (the **epoxy resin**). The fishing lines are super-strong in tension and prevent the can from expanding. The soda can still acts as a leak-proof barrier, but the *load* is carried by the lines. This is exactly how a Type IV composite cylinder works.

- The internal working pressure is typically **350 bar (35 MPa)** or **700 bar (70 MPa)**.
- Hydrogen density at 70 MPa reaches approximately **40 g/L** (vs. 0.08 g/L at ambient).
- Gravimetric density of a 700 bar Type IV system reaches **6.0–7.2 wt% H₂**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrogen Embrittlement:** Atomic hydrogen (H) can diffuse into metallic lattices (steel, aluminum), causing cracking and loss of ductility. The polymer liner in Type IV cylinders **eliminates this risk entirely** by providing a non-metallic permeation barrier.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Type IV Composite Cylinder
A Type IV cylinder has three principal functional regions:

1. **Inner Liner (HDPE / PA6 / PET):**
   - Thickness: typically **3–8 mm**.
   - Function: hermetic hydrogen permeation barrier; secondary load path.
   - Permeation target: **< 1.0 Ncm³·h⁻¹·L⁻¹** per ECE R134.

2. **Composite Overwrap (Carbon Fiber + Epoxy):**
   - Fiber: Toray T700S, T800S, T1000 (modulus 230–295 GPa).
   - Resin: toughened epoxy (e.g., Hexcel 8552).
   - Fiber Volume Fraction ($V_f$): **60–65%**.
   - Winding angle: ±10° helical (longitudinal) + 90° hoop dominant for 70 MPa.

3. **Metal Bosses (Aluminum 6061-T6):**
   - Bosses are mechanically or chemically bonded to the liner ends.
   - House the **Pressure Relief Device (PRD)**, **Thermally Activated Pressure Relief Device (TPRD)**, and **Receptacle Valve**.

### 2.2 Theoretical Basis of Stress Distribution
The composite overwrap is **anisotropic** — its strength depends on fiber orientation. The two principal load directions are:

- **Hoop (circumferential) stress** ($\sigma_h$): resists radial expansion.
- **Longitudinal (axial) stress** ($\sigma_l$): resists end-cap pull-off.

For a thin-walled pressure vessel, the **membrane stresses** are:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r}{t}$$

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot r}{2t}$$

where $p$ is the internal gauge pressure, $r$ is the inner radius, and $t$ is the total wall thickness. Because $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$, the composite must be designed with **more hoop plies than longitudinal plies** (typically a 2:1 ply ratio).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet
| Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|:---------|:----------------|:-----:|:------|
| Ideal Gas Law (real-H₂ via compressibility Z) | $pV = ZnRT$ | Pa·m³ | $Z \approx 1.45$ at 70 MPa, 298 K |
| Stored Mass | $m = \rho V_{vessel}$ | kg | $\rho_{70MPa} \approx 40\,\text{g/L}$ |
| Volumetric Density | $\rho_v = m/V_{tank}$ | g H₂/L | **Target ≥ 30 g/L** (DOE 2025) |
| Gravimetric Density | $\rho_g = m/M_{tank}$ | wt% | **Target ≥ 6.5 wt%** (DOE 2025) |
| Hoop Stress (thin wall) | $\sigma_h = pr/t$ | Pa | Membrane theory |
| Axial Stress (thin wall) | $\sigma_l = pr/(2t)$ | Pa | Half of hoop |
| Burst Ratio (B) | $B = p_{burst}/p_{WP}$ | — | **B ≥ 2.25** (ISO 11439) |
| Layer of Safety (L) | $L = (p_{burst}-p_{WP})/p_{WP}$ | — | $L \geq 1.25$ |
| Tsai-Wu Failure Index | $F_1\sigma_1 + F_2\sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^2 + F_{22}\sigma_2^2 + F_{66}\tau_{12}^2 + 2F_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 \le 1$ | — | Composite failure |
| Permeation Rate | $\le 1.0\,\text{Ncm}^3/\text{h/L}$ | — | ECE R134 limit |
| Composite Modulus (rule of mixtures) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1-V_f)$ | GPa | Longitudinal direction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\rho_g$ is low:** Even the best composite cylinder (Type IV) only stores **~5–7 wt%** hydrogen because the mass of the vessel itself (fibers + resin + liner + valves) dominates. Compare this to **liquid H₂** (~8 wt%) and **metal hydrides** (~1–2 wt%).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive:** Type IV cylinders are mandatory in 70 MPa FCVs (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, Honda Clarity).
- **Stationary backup power:** Telecom towers and data centers use 350–450 bar composite banks.
- **Heavy transport:** Buses and trucks use modular 350 bar banks.
- **Aerospace (research):** Liquid H₂ tanks use composite overwrap with internal foam insulation.
- **Marine/offshore:** Under development for fuel-cell-powered ships.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hoop Stress for a Thin-Walled Cylinder (Membrane Theory)

**Assumption:** Wall thickness $t \ll r$ (typically $t/r < 0.1$).

**Step 1 — Force Balance on a Half-Cylinder Element**

Consider a cylinder of length $L$, internal radius $r$, and internal gauge pressure $p$. Cut the cylinder along a longitudinal plane. The internal pressure acting on the projected area $(2rL)$ must be resisted by the hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acting on the two cut edges (each of cross-sectional area $tL$).

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Outward force} &= \text{Resisting force} \\
p \cdot (2rL) &= 2 \cdot (\sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L) \\
2prL &= 2\sigma_h t L \\
\sigma_h &= \frac{pr}{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Axial (Longitudinal) Stress**

The end cap of area $\pi r^2$ is pushed out by pressure $p$ and resisted by the cylindrical wall of area $2\pi r t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p \cdot \pi r^2 &= \sigma_l \cdot 2\pi r t \\
\sigma_l &= \frac{pr}{2t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Stress Ratio**

$$
\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{pr/t}{pr/(2t)} = 2
$$

This **2:1 ratio** is the foundation of composite winding design: the **90° hoop plies carry double the load** of the **±10° helical plies** for a closed-end pressure vessel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Thick-Walled Cylinder Stresses (Lamé's Equations)

When $t/r > 0.1$, the thin-wall assumption fails. We use **Lamé's equations** for axisymmetric plane-strain.

**Step 1 — Geometry and Assumptions**

Let $r_i$ = inner radius, $r_o$ = outer radius, $p_i$ = internal pressure, $p_o$ = external pressure (atmospheric or pre-stress from winding).

**Step 2 — Equilibrium of a Cylindrical Shell Element**

The radial force balance on an annular element of thickness $dr$ gives the Lamé equilibrium:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — General Solution of Lamé Equations**

The general solution satisfying axisymmetric boundary conditions is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r(r) &= A - \frac{B}{r^2} \\
\sigma_\theta(r) &= A + \frac{B}{r^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions**

At $r = r_i$: $\sigma_r = -p_i$ (compressive)
At $r = r_o$: $\sigma_r = -p_o$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A - \frac{B}{r_i^2} &= -p_i \\
A - \frac{B}{r_o^2} &= -p_o
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the two equations:

$$
B\left(\frac{1}{r_o^2} - \frac{1}{r_i^2}\right) = p_o - p_i
$$

$$
B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
$$

$$
A = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Lamé Equations**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_r(r) = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} - \frac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{(r_o^2 - r_i^2)r^2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_\theta(r) = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} + \frac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{(r_o^2 - r_i^2)r^2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Hoop Stress Location**

Hoop stress is maximum at the **inner wall** ($r = r_i$):

$$
\sigma_{\theta,\max} = p_i \cdot \frac{r_o^2 + r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
$$

This is the basis for the design of filament-wound composite overwraps.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Tsai-Wu Failure Criterion for Composite Cylinders

A composite laminate under biaxial stress ($\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12}$) fails when the following polynomial inequality is violated:

$$
F_1\sigma_1 + F_2\sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^2 + F_{22}\sigma_2^2 + F_{66}\tau_{12}^2 + 2F_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 \le 1
$$

**Coefficient Definitions (Single-Lamina):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{1}{X_t} - \frac{1}{X_c} & F_2 &= \frac{1}{Y_t} - \frac{1}{Y_c} \\
F_{11} &= \frac{1}{X_t X_c} & F_{22} &= \frac{1}{Y_t Y_c} \\
F_{66} &= \frac{1}{S^2} & F_{12} &\approx -\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_{11}F_{22}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $X_t, X_c$ = longitudinal tensile/compressive strength, $Y_t, Y_c$ = transverse tensile/compressive strength, $S$ = in-plane shear strength.

**Step 7 — Application to Hoop-Wound Ply**

For a 90° hoop ply, the fiber direction is circumferential. The principal stresses are $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h$ and $\sigma_2 = \sigma_l$. We substitute into Tsai-Wu and solve for the **burst pressure** by setting the failure index to unity and iteratively increasing $p$ until the inequality is violated.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **unidirectional T700/epoxy** hoop ply, typical values are $X_t = 2550$ MPa, $X_c = 1500$ MPa, $Y_t = 60$ MPa. The hoop fiber is so strong that the burst pressure is governed by the transverse matrix strength unless the winding angle is optimized.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Practice)
A Type IV cylinder has inner radius $r_i = 175$ mm, outer radius $r_o = 195$ mm, working pressure $p_{WP} = 70$ MPa. Find the maximum hoop stress and the **burst ratio** if the composite fails at $p_{burst} = 165$ MPa.

**Solution:**

$$
\sigma_{\theta,\max} = p_i \cdot \frac{r_o^2 + r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
$$

$$
= 70 \times \frac{195^2 + 175^2}{195^2 - 175^2} = 70 \times \frac{38025 + 30625}{38025 - 30625} = 70 \times \frac{68650}{7400}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta,\max} = 70 \times 9.277 = 649.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Burst Ratio:**

$$
B = \frac{165}{70} = 2.357 \ge 2.25 \;\; \text{✅ Passes ISO 11439}
$$

**Layer of Safety:**

$$
L = \frac{165 - 70}{70} = 1.357 \ge 1.25 \;\; \text{✅ Passes}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Anatomy of a Type IV Composite Cylinder (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    A --> D[Boss and Valve Subsystem]

    B --> B2[Permeation Barrier Layer]
    B --> B3[Outer Surface of Liner - Corona Treated]

    C --> C1[Hoop Ply Layer 90 degrees]
    C --> C2[Helical Ply Layer plus minus 10 degrees]
    C --> C3[Outer Protective Gelcoat]

    D --> D1[Aluminum 6061 Boss]
    D --> D2[Check Valve Receptacle]
    D --> D3[Manual Bleed Valve]

    E --> E1[TPRD Thermally Activated]
    E --> E2[PRD Pressure Relief]
    E --> E3[Excess Flow Valve]
```
