## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define a Type IV composite cylinder. Why is it preferred over Type I for automotive hydrogen storage?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Type IV composite cylinder** is a high-pressure hydrogen storage vessel consisting of a non-metallic polymer (HDPE) liner fully overwrapped with a fiber-reinforced polymer composite (carbon fiber + epoxy). It is preferred over Type I (all-steel) for automotive use because:
1. **Higher gravimetric density** (≥6 wt%) due to the low mass of carbon fiber.
2. **No hydrogen embrittlement** — the polymer liner acts as a permeation barrier, preventing atomic H from diffusing into metal lattices.
3. **Higher fatigue life** — composite overwrap can withstand >10,000 pressure cycles vs. ~2,000 for steel.
4. **Better safety** — failure mode is "leak-before-burst" with controlled energy release. (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the four reasons)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the design burst ratio and layer-of-safety for a 70 MPa Type IV cylinder as per ISO 11439.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Design Burst Ratio (B):** $B = p_{burst}/p_{WP} \ge 2.25$ (1 mark)
- **Layer of Safety (L):** $L = (p_{burst} - p_{WP})/p_{WP} \ge 1.25$ (1 mark)
- **Verification example:** If $p_{WP} = 70$ MPa, then $p_{burst} \ge 70 \times 2.25 = 157.5$ MPa. (1 mark)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the constructional features and material selection of a Type IV composite cylinder for 70 MPa hydrogen storage. List the relevant international standards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Type IV cylinder has inner radius 180 mm, outer radius 200 mm, working pressure 70 MPa, and an experimentally determined burst pressure of 168 MPa.
  (i) Calculate the burst ratio and layer of safety.
  (ii) Using Lamé's equation, find the maximum hoop stress.
  (iii) State whether the cylinder passes ISO 11439 certification. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Constructional Features (7 marks):**
- **Liner:** HDPE or PA6 polymer, 3–8 mm thick. Provides permeation barrier. Surface corona-treated for composite adhesion. (1 mark)
- **Composite Overwrap:** T700/T800 carbon fiber + toughened epoxy. Wound in two patterns:
  - **Hoop plies (90°):** Carry circumferential load.
  - **Helical plies (±10°):** Carry axial load.
  - Fiber volume fraction 60–65%. (2 marks)
- **Bosses:** 6061-T6 aluminum, mechanically or chemically bonded to liner ends. House PRD, TPRD, and receptacle. (1 mark)
- **Outer Coating:** UV-resistant gelcoat for impact and abrasion protection. (1 mark)
- **Standards:** ISO 11439, UN/ECE R134, SAE J2579, ISO 14687, CGA C-1. (1 mark)
- **Sketch (block diagram acceptable):** Label all components. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Burst ratio and Layer of Safety (3 marks):**

$$
B = \frac{p_{burst}}{p_{WP}} = \frac{168}{70} = 2.40
$$

$$
L = \frac{p_{burst} - p_{WP}}{p_{WP}} = \frac{168 - 70}{70} = 1.40
$$

**(ii) Maximum Hoop Stress via Lamé (3 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{\theta,\max} = p_i \cdot \frac{r_o^2 + r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = 70 \times \frac{200^2 + 180^2}{200^2 - 180^2}
$$

$$
= 70 \times \frac{40000 + 32400}{40000 - 32400} = 70 \times \frac{72400}{7600} = 70 \times 9.526
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{\theta,\max} = 666.8 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**(iii) Certification (1 mark):**
- Burst ratio $B = 2.40 \ge 2.25$ ✅
- Layer of safety $L = 1.40 \ge 1.25$ ✅
- **The cylinder PASSES ISO 11439 certification.**

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for hoop stress and longitudinal stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel. Why does the composite overwrap have more hoop plies than helical plies? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Tsai-Wu failure criterion for a composite lamina. A 90° hoop ply of T700/epoxy has $X_t = 2550$ MPa, $X_c = 1500$ MPa, $Y_t = 60$ MPa, $Y_c = 200$ MPa, $S = 90$ MPa. The ply is subjected to $\sigma_1 = 800$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 30$ MPa, $\tau_{12} = 10$ MPa. Check if the ply fails. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (5 marks) + Justification (2 marks):**

**Hoop Stress Derivation:**
- Force on a half-cylinder of length $L$: $F = p \cdot 2rL$ (projected area).
- Resisting force on two cut edges: $2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$.
- Equilibrium: $2prL = 2\sigma_h t L \Rightarrow \sigma_h = pr/t$. (2 marks)

**Longitudinal Stress Derivation:**
- Force on end cap: $F = p \cdot \pi r^2$.
- Resisting force on cylindrical wall: $\sigma_l \cdot 2\pi r t$.
- Equilibrium: $p\pi r^2 = \sigma_l \cdot 2\pi r t \Rightarrow \sigma_l = pr/(2t)$. (2 marks)

**Justification for Ply Ratio:**
- Since $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$, the **circumferential load is twice the axial load**.
- The hoop plies (90°) carry the hoop stress; helical plies (±10°) carry axial stress.
- Therefore, hoop plies must be **approximately 2× thicker** than helical plies. (1 mark)

**Reference to Lamé:** For a 70 MPa vessel with $r = 180$ mm and $t = 20$ mm, the ratio $t/r = 0.11 > 0.1$, so Lamé's equations are more accurate. (1 mark)

**(b) Tsai-Wu Analysis (7 marks):**

**Criterion (2 marks):**
$$
F_1\sigma_1 + F_2\sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^2 + F_{22}\sigma_2^2 + F_{66}\tau_{12}^2 + 2F_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 \le 1
$$

**Compute coefficients (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{1}{2550} - \frac{1}{1500} = 3.92\times 10^{-4} - 6.67\times 10^{-4} = -2.75\times 10^{-4}\,\text{MPa}^{-1} \\
F_2 &= \frac{1}{60} - \frac{1}{200} = 1.667\times 10^{-2} - 5.0\times 10^{-3} = 1.167\times 10^{-2}\,\text{MPa}^{-1} \\
F_{11} &= \frac{1}{2550 \times 1500} = 2.61\times 10^{-7}\,\text{MPa}^{-2} \\
F_{22} &= \frac{1}{60 \times 200} = 8.33\times 10^{-5}\,\text{MPa}^{-2} \\
F_{66} &= \frac{1}{90^2} = 1.235\times 10^{-4}\,\text{MPa}^{-2} \\
F_{12} &= -\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_{11}F_{22}} = -0.5\sqrt{2.61\times 10^{-7} \times 8.33\times 10^{-5}} \\
      &= -0.5 \times 1.475\times 10^{-6} = -7.37\times 10^{-7}\,\text{MPa}^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitute given stresses (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Linear terms:} &\quad F_1\sigma_1 + F_2\sigma_2 = (-2.75\times 10^{-4})(800) + (1.167\times 10^{-2})(30) \\
&= -0.220 + 0.350 = 0.130 \\
\text{Quadratic terms:} &\quad F_{11}\sigma_1^2 = 2.61\times 10^{-7} \times 800^2 = 0.167 \\
&\quad F_{22}\sigma_2^2 = 8.33\times 10^{-5} \times 30^2 = 0.075 \\
&\quad F_{66}\tau_{12}^2 = 1.235\times 10^{-4} \times 10^2 = 0.012 \\
&\quad 2F_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 = 2 \times (-7.37\times 10^{-7}) \times 800 \times 30 = -0.035 \\
\text{Total:} &\quad 0.130 + 0.167 + 0.075 + 0.012 - 0.035 = 0.349
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Tsai-Wu Index} = 0.349 \le 1.0 \;\; \Longrightarrow \;\; \text{The ply DOES NOT FAIL.}
$$

The **reserve of safety** is significant ($1.0 - 0.349 = 0.651$), which is consistent with the high burst ratio required by ISO 11439.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Type III with Type IV:** Type III has an **aluminum** liner (metal), Type IV has a **polymer** liner. Many students write "Type IV = aluminum liner" — this is the #1 mistake in KTU valuation and costs **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to use absolute units:** Hoop stress must be in **Pa or MPa**, not bar. $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 0.1 \text{ MPa}$. A student who writes $\sigma_h = 70 \times 0.175 / 0.020 = 612.5$ (mixing bar and m) loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $2pr$ instead of $2rL$** in the hoop-stress derivation: The projected area of a half-cylinder is $2rL$ (diameter × length), **NOT** $\pi r L$.
> 4. **Not stating Lamé's validity condition:** Always state the assumption $t/r > 0.1$ justifies Lamé over thin-wall theory.
> 5. **Tsai-Wu coefficient $F_{12}$:** Most students forget this interaction term, leading to a $5\%$–$15\%$ error. The KTU key includes it.
> 6. **Forgetting the burst ratio limit $B \ge 2.25$** and **layer of safety $L \ge 1.25$** as separate checks. Both must be quoted for full credit.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU):**

- ☐ **Type IV cylinder = polymer liner + full composite overwrap** (NOT aluminum).
- ☐ **Working pressure:** 35 MPa (350 bar) and 70 MPa (700 bar) are industry standards.
- ☐ **Burst ratio $B \ge 2.25$** and **Layer of safety $L \ge 1.25$** (ISO 11439 / ECE R134).
- ☐ **Thin-wall formulas:** $\sigma_h = pr/t$, $\sigma_l = pr/(2t)$, ratio = 2:1.
- ☐ **Thick-wall (Lamé):** $\sigma_\theta$ maximum at **inner wall**, equals $p_i(r_o^2 + r_i^2)/(r_o^2 - r_i^2)$.
- ☐ **Hoop plies (90°) carry 2× the load of helical plies (±10°)** — design ply ratio accordingly.
- ☐ **Carbon fiber T700/T800** is the standard reinforcement; fiber volume fraction $V_f = 60$–$65\%$.
- ☐ **Permeation rate ≤ 1 Ncm³/h/L** (ECE R134 limit for HDPE liner).
- ☐ **Failure theories:** Maximum stress, Maximum strain, Tsai-Hill, **Tsai-Wu (most general)**.
- ☐ **Hydrogen embrittlement** is the reason for polymer liners in Type IV (no metallic lattice).
- ☐ **Gravimetric density target:** ≥ 6.5 wt% (DOE 2025); current Type IV achieves 5–7 wt%.
- ☐ **Volumetric density target:** ≥ 30 g H₂/L; current Type IV achieves 25–35 g/L.
- ☐ **DOE 2025 targets:** 7.5 wt% gravimetric, 70 g/L volumetric for ultimate system.
- ☐ **Standards to quote:** ISO 11439, UN/ECE R134, SAE J2579, ISO 14687, CGA C-1, ASME BPVC.
- ☐ **Safety devices:** TPRD (thermally activated, 110 °C trigger), PRD (pressure relief), excess flow valve, thermally insulated outer wrap.
- ☐ **Filament winding patterns:** Helical (axial load) + hoop (circumferential load) — both required.
- ☐ **Cycle test:** 2200 cycles from <2 MPa to 1.5× WP for Type IV qualification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Hydride Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Metal Hydride Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal Hydride Storage (MHS)** is a **solid-state hydrogen storage** technique in which molecular hydrogen (H₂) is **reversibly absorbed** into the interstitial sites (tetrahedral and octahedral voids) of certain metallic or intermetallic alloy lattices, forming a **metal hydride** compound through a chemisorption process. The reaction is typically represented as:
>
> $$M_{(s)} + \frac{x}{2}H_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons MH_{x(s)} + \Delta H$$
>
> where M is a metal or alloy, MHₓ is the metal hydride, and ΔH is the enthalpy of formation (typically negative during absorption / exothermic, and positive during desorption / endothermic).

The stored hydrogen occupies the **interstitial positions** inside the host metal lattice. Under moderate temperature and pressure conditions, hydrogen molecules first **dissociate** on the metal surface, then **diffuse** as atomic hydrogen (H⁰) into the bulk lattice, producing a reversible solid solution (α-phase) that gradually transforms into a distinct metal-hydride phase (β-phase).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a metal hydride as a **"magnetic sponge for hydrogen."** Imagine a piece of dry sponge (the metal) floating in air. When hydrogen gas (water vapour, in this analogy) flows around it, the sponge soaks up the gas into its tiny internal pores and locks it away as a wet, swollen version of itself. When you gently heat the wet sponge (raising temperature), it releases the trapped moisture back into the air. The dry sponge is then ready to soak again. **Metal hydrides work exactly like this — hydrogen goes in cool, comes out warm, and the cycle repeats.**

For a real-world scale analogy: a single kilogram of the alloy **LaNi₅** can absorb **≈ 15–17 grams of hydrogen**, equivalent to storing roughly **170 litres of gaseous hydrogen at STP** inside a few cubic centimetres of solid metal. This is why metal hydrides are considered an ultra-safe, **high volumetric density** storage option, even though their gravimetric density is lower than compressed gas.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants Used in Metal Hydride Analysis

- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \ \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \ \text{C} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **STP molar volume of H₂:** $V_m = 22{,}414 \ \text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} = 22.414 \ \text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Molar mass of H:** $M_H = 1.008 \ \text{g} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$ (**Hydrogen atomic weight**)
- **Density of liquid hydrogen:** $\rho_{LH_2} = 70.8 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Density of compressed H₂ at 700 bar:** $\rho_{700} \approx 40 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Lower Heating Value of H₂:** $LHV = 120 \ \text{MJ/kg}$
- **Higher Heating Value of H₂:** $HHV = 142 \ \text{MJ/kg}$

> [!NOTE]
> The **US Department of Energy (DOE) 2025 gravimetric target** for onboard hydrogen storage is **5.5 wt%** and **40 g H₂/L** volumetric density. Most commercial metal hydrides today achieve **1–3 wt%**, though magnesium-based hydrides (MgH₂) reach up to **7.6 wt%** theoretically, but require temperatures above **300 °C** to release hydrogen.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principle (Absorption & Desorption Cycle)

1. **Dissociative Chemisorption (Surface Step):**
   The H₂ molecule approaches the metal surface, the H–H bond (~ 436 kJ/mol) is broken catalytically by transition-metal d-electrons (Ni, Pd, Pt, Fe, Ti), yielding **two surface-adsorbed H atoms**.
   $$H_{2(g)} \rightarrow 2H_{(ads)}$$

2. **Surface Diffusion:**
   Atomic H diffuses across the surface into the near-surface region of the metal. The activation energy barrier is small, typically **0.1–0.3 eV**.

3. **Bulk Diffusion (Penetration):**
   H atoms jump through interstitial sites into the bulk metal lattice. This is the **rate-limiting step** for many hydrides. Fick's first law governs the flux:
   $$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$
   where $D$ is the hydrogen diffusivity (typically $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-12} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ depending on temperature).

4. **α-Phase Formation (Solid Solution):**
   Hydrogen dissolves into the metal, creating a dilute solid solution. Lattice expands by ≈ **2–3 Å³ per H atom**.

5. **β-Phase Nucleation (Hydride Formation):**
   Beyond the solubility limit, a distinct metal-hydride phase nucleates and grows. The metal–hydrogen system undergoes a **first-order phase transition**, with a characteristic plateau pressure (PC-isotherm).

6. **Hydrogen Release (Desorption / Endothermic):**
   Heating the hydride reverses the reaction:
   $$MH_x \rightarrow M + \frac{x}{2}H_2$$
   Heat must be supplied to break the M–H bonds.

### 2.2 Classification of Metal Hydrides for KTU Syllabus

| Class | Representative Compound | Theoretical Capacity (wt %) | Operating T (°C) | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AB₅** | LaNi₅H₆ | 1.4 | 20–60 | Commercial NiMH batteries, easy activation |
| **AB₂** (Laves) | ZrMn₂, TiCr₂ | 1.8–2.0 | 30–120 | Heat-pump applications, robust cycling |
| **AB** | FeTiH₂ | 1.9 | 20–50 | Cheapest, needs activation |
| **A₂B** | Mg₂NiH₄ | 3.6 | 250–350 | High capacity, slow kinetics |
| **Mg-based** | MgH₂ | 7.6 | 300–400 | Highest capacity, nanocatalysed |
| **Complex Hydrides** | NaAlH₄, LiBH₄ | 5.6–18.5 | 150–500 | Lightweight, irreversible without catalyst |
| **BCC Alloys** | Ti-V-Cr-Mn | 2.6 | 0–80 | High H₂ capacity per metal atom |
| **High-entropy alloys** | (TiVNbZrHf) | 2.0+ | RT – 300 | Emerging research |

### 2.3 Thermodynamics — The Van't Hoff Equation (Core Equation for KTU)

For a metal–hydrogen reaction in equilibrium, the equilibrium dissociation pressure is exponentially related to temperature:

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

Or in the equivalent log₁₀ form most used in KTU numerical problems:

$$\log_{10}(P) = -\frac{\Delta H}{2.303\,R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S}{2.303\,R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ΔH (enthalpy of hydride formation)** must lie in the range **30–50 kJ/mol H₂** for practical applications:
> - Too low (< 20 kJ/mol): releases H₂ at unworkably low temperatures.
> - Too high (> 60 kJ/mol): requires excessive heat input to desorb.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Gibbs free energy of hydride formation | J/mol |
| 2 | $\ln(P_{eq}) = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$ | Van't Hoff equation (natural log) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\log_{10}(P) = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{2.303R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{2.303R}$ | Van't Hoff equation (log base 10) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_{wt\%} = \dfrac{x \cdot M_H}{M_{MH}} \times 100$ | Gravimetric hydrogen capacity | wt % |
| 5 | $C_{vol} = \dfrac{\rho_{MH} \cdot C_{wt\%}}{100 \cdot V_m} \cdot \frac{T_{stp}}{T}$ | Volumetric H₂ capacity | kg H₂/m³ |
| 6 | $Q = n\Delta H_d$ | Total heat required for desorption of n moles | kJ |
| 7 | $n = \dfrac{m \cdot C_{wt\%}}{100 \cdot M_H}$ | Moles of H₂ stored | mol |
| 8 | $V_{H_2} = n \cdot V_m$ | Equivalent STP gas volume from hydride | L |
| 9 | $E_{des} = \dfrac{Q}{m_{H_2}}$ | Specific desorption energy | kJ/kg H₂ |
| 10 | $E_{a}^{diff} = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Hydrogen diffusivity in metal | m²/s |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Fuel-cell vehicles (FCVs):** Toyota, Hyundai, and BMW have all prototyped MH tanks as safer alternatives to 700 bar compressed H₂.
- **Stationary energy storage:** Coupled with renewable (solar/wind) electrolysers, MH acts as a long-duration buffer.
- **Heat pumps / Thermal Energy Storage:** The reversible endothermic/exothermic cycle is exploited for seasonal thermal storage.
- **Nuclear reactor moderators:** ZrH₂, YH₂, and CaH₂ are used as **neutron moderators** in TRIGA-type research reactors.
- **Hydrogen purification:** Pd-membrane + bulk hydriding alloys achieve > 99.9999 % purity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Van't Hoff Equation for Metal Hydrides

Starting from the equilibrium condition for a reversible reaction:

$$M + \frac{x}{2}H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x$$

**Step 1 — Chemical potential equality at equilibrium:**

At equilibrium, the chemical potential of the reactant H₂ gas equals the chemical potential of the hydrogen inside the metal hydride (Gibbs' criterion for phase equilibrium):

$$\mu_{H_2}^{(gas)} = \mu_{H_2}^{(hydride)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the chemical potential of an ideal gas (hydrogen):**

For an ideal gas:

$$\mu_{H_2}^{(gas)}(T,P) = \mu_{H_2}^{\circ}(T) + RT\ln\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P^{\circ}}\right)$$

where $P^{\circ}$ is the standard-state reference pressure (1 bar).

**Step 3 — Express the chemical potential of the hydride phase:**

$$\mu_{H_2}^{(hydride)} = \mu_{H_2}^{\circ}(hydride)$$

(Standard chemical potential at temperature T, pressure-independent for solids).

**Step 4 — Equate and rearrange:**

$$\mu_{H_2}^{\circ}(T) + RT\ln\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P^{\circ}}\right) = \mu_{H_2}^{\circ}(hydride)$$

$$RT\ln\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P^{\circ}}\right) = \mu_{H_2}^{\circ}(hydride) - \mu_{H_2}^{\circ}(T) = -\Delta G_{rxn}^{\circ}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T\Delta S^{\circ}$:**

$$RT\ln\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P^{\circ}}\right) = -(\Delta H^{\circ} - T\Delta S^{\circ})$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P^{\circ}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to the KTU-preferred log₁₀ form:**

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}(x)$:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P^{\circ}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{2.303\,R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{2.303\,R}$$

> This is the **Van't Hoff equation** for metal hydrides, the **most-tested formula** in KTU end-semester examinations for this topic.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — LaNi₅ Hydride Thermodynamics

**Problem (KTU Exam-Style):** For the intermetallic alloy **LaNi₅**, the equilibrium dissociation pressures measured are:

| T (K) | P_eq (bar) |
|---|---|
| 298 | 2.0 |
| 333 | 8.0 |

Determine (a) the **enthalpy of hydride formation (ΔH)**, and (b) the **entropy of hydride formation (ΔS)**.

**Solution (a) — Calculation of ΔH:**

Using the two-point Van't Hoff form:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Substitute values:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{8.0}{2.0}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{333} - \frac{1}{298}\right)$$

Compute the logarithm term:

$$\ln(4.0) = 1.3863$$

Compute the temperature reciprocal difference:

$$\frac{1}{333} - \frac{1}{298} = \frac{298 - 333}{333 \times 298} = \frac{-35}{99{,}234} = -3.526 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

Substitute:

$$1.3863 = -\frac{\Delta H}{8.314}\times(-3.526\times 10^{-4})$$

$$\Delta H = \frac{1.3863 \times 8.314}{3.526 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\Delta H = \frac{11.524}{3.526 \times 10^{-4}} = 32{,}683 \ \text{J/mol} \approx -32.7 \ \text{kJ/mol H}_2$$

> The negative sign indicates the reaction is **exothermic during absorption**.

**Solution (b) — Calculation of ΔS:**

Using a single-point Van't Hoff equation:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^{\circ}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

Substitute T = 298 K, P = 2.0 bar, ΔH = –32,683 J/mol:

$$\ln(2.0) = -\frac{(-32{,}683)}{8.314 \times 298} + \frac{\Delta S}{8.314}$$

$$0.6931 = \frac{32{,}683}{2477.6} + \frac{\Delta S}{8.314}$$

$$0.6931 = 13.190 + \frac{\Delta S}{8.314}$$

$$\frac{\Delta S}{8.314} = 0.6931 - 13.190 = -12.497$$

$$\Delta S = -12.497 \times 8.314 = -103.9 \ \text{J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$$

> The negative entropy change reflects the **ordering** of hydrogen atoms in the crystal lattice upon absorption.

**Verification (Optional):** Compute T at which P_eq = 1 bar (PCT plateau):

$$T = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} = \frac{32{,}683}{-103.9} \rightarrow \text{check: } T_{eq} \approx 314 \ \text{K} \approx 41 \ \text{°C}$$

This matches experimental LaNi₅H₆ data closely.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Gravimetric Capacity of MgH₂

**Problem:** Compute the **theoretical gravimetric hydrogen capacity** of magnesium hydride (MgH₂). Given: $M_{Mg} = 24.305$ g/mol, $M_H = 1.008$ g/mol.

**Step 1 — Molar mass of MgH₂:**

$$M_{MgH_2} = 24.305 + 2(1.008) = 26.321 \ \text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of hydrogen stored per mole:**

$$m_{H_2} = 2 \times 1.008 = 2.016 \ \text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Apply capacity formula:**

$$C_{wt\%} = \frac{m_{H_2}}{M_{MgH_2}} \times 100 = \frac{2.016}{26.321} \times 100$$

$$C_{wt\%} = 7.66 \ \text{wt\%}$$

> **Result:** MgH₂ can store **7.66 wt%** of hydrogen — the highest among simple binary metal hydrides, but it requires **~350 °C** to release H₂.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Property | Compressed Gas (700 bar) | Liquid H₂ (20 K) | Metal Hydride (MgH₂) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volumetric density (kg H₂/m³) | 40 | 70.8 | 100–110 |
| Gravimetric density (wt%) | 100 (system level ~5–6) | 100 (system level ~10–15) | 7.66 theoretical |
| Operating pressure | 700 bar | 1 bar (cryogenic) | 1–10 bar |
| Operating temperature | Ambient | –253 °C | 300–350 °C |
| Energy to store/release | ~12% of LHV | ~30% of LHV | ~25% of LHV |
| Safety | Risk of rupture | Boil-off, cryogenic burns | Very safe, self-pressurizing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — Metal Hydride Storage System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[H2 Inlet from Electrolyser or Pipeline] --> B[Heat Exchanger Pre Cooler]
    B --> C[Filter and Moisture Trap]
    C --> D[Hydride Reactor Tank with LaNi5 or Mg based Alloy]
    D --> E[Thermally Insulated Vessel with Cooling Jacket]
    D --> F[Pressure Transmitter PT 1]
    D --> G[Temperature Transmitter TT 1]
    F --> H[Safety Pressure Relief Valve PRV]
    G --> I[Control Valve for Heat Transfer Fluid]
    I --> J[Hot Water or Thermal Oil Reservoir]
    J --> K[Endothermic Desorption Heat Supply]
    D --> L[H2 Outlet to Fuel Cell or End User]
    K --> D
    E --> D
```

**Process interpretation:**

1. Hydrogen gas enters from upstream source.
2. It is cooled and dried to prevent poisoning of the alloy surface.
3. Inside the reactor, it is **absorbed exothermically** by the alloy.
4. Heat is removed via the cooling jacket during charging.
5. During discharge, hot thermal fluid from the reservoir supplies the **endothermic** desorption heat.
6. Pressure and temperature sensors ensure the unit operates within safe PCT windows.

### 4.2 Hydrogen Absorption / Desorption Cycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1 Dissociative Chemisorption H2 to 2H atoms on M surface] --> P2[Stage 2 Surface Diffusion of H atoms on metal surface]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3 Bulk Diffusion through interstitial sites]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4 Alpha Phase Solid Solution formation]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5 Beta Phase Nucleation MHx growth]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6 Hydride Saturation Equilibrium reached]
    P6 -->|Apply Heat Reverse Cycle| P1
```

### 4.3 PCT (Pressure Composition Temperature) Curve Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Temperature T1 Low] --> B[High Plateau Pressure P1 High]
    C[Temperature T2 Medium] --> D[Medium Plateau Pressure P2 Medium]
    E[Temperature T3 High] --> F[Low Plateau Pressure P3 Low]
    B --> G[Van't Hoff Plot ln P vs 1 slash T Linear]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Slope gives negative Delta H over R Intercept gives Delta S over R]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A higher operating temperature → a higher plateau pressure (the alloy desorbs more readily). This is the **physical basis** of the Van't Hoff relation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *Define metal hydride storage and list two advantages over compressed hydrogen storage.*

**Model Answer:**
Metal hydride storage is a solid-state hydrogen storage technique in which H₂ molecules are absorbed into the crystal lattice of a metal or intermetallic alloy, forming a metal hydride compound via a reversible chemisorption reaction:

$$M + \frac{x}{2}H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x$$

**Two advantages over compressed storage:**
1. **Higher volumetric density** (e.g., MgH₂ can store >100 kg H₂/m³ versus 40 kg/m³ for 700-bar gas).
2. **Intrinsic safety** — operates at low pressure (1–10 bar), eliminating high-pressure rupture risk.

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *Why does magnesium hydride (MgH₂) require temperatures above 300 °C to release hydrogen?*

**Model Answer:**
Because the M–H bond energy in MgH₂ is high. The enthalpy of formation is ΔH ≈ –74 kJ/mol H₂. By the Van't Hoff equation, the equilibrium pressure reaches 1 bar only at:

$$T = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} \approx \frac{74{,}000}{ -135} \approx 548 \ \text{K} \approx 275 \ \text{°C}$$

Hence desorption becomes practically viable only above 300 °C. Nanocatalysts (Nb₂O₅, Ni nanoparticles) reduce this barrier by destabilising the hydride phase.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model]**

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**Q.A.** *(a)* Derive the Van't Hoff equation for a metal-hydrogen system and explain how the enthalpy (ΔH) and entropy (ΔS) of hydride formation can be obtained experimentally using Pressure-Composition-Temperature (PCT) isotherms. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* The intermetallic alloy ZrMn₂ absorbs hydrogen at 4.0 bar at 40 °C and at 8.5 bar at 80 °C. Calculate (i) the enthalpy of hydride formation (ΔH) and (ii) the entropy of hydride formation (ΔS). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

The Van't Hoff equation is derived from the equality of chemical potentials at equilibrium (detailed step-by-step derivation given in **Section 3.1** of these notes). The final expression is:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P^{\circ}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

**Experimental determination:**

1. PCT isotherms are measured at **at least three different temperatures** (T₁, T₂, T₃) by dosing small hydrogen volumes into the metal and recording equilibrium pressure.
2. The plateau pressure $P_{eq}$ at each temperature is read off the flat region of the PCT curve (α → β transition region).
3. A plot of $\ln(P_{eq})$ versus $1/T$ yields a **straight line**.
4. The slope of the line equals $-\Delta H / R$ → so $\Delta H$ is obtained.
5. The y-intercept equals $\Delta S / R$ → so $\Delta S$ is obtained.
6. The temperature at which $P_{eq} = 1$ bar is the **standard decomposition temperature** and is used for alloy screening.

**Practical validity check:** A good storage alloy must satisfy $30 \le \vert \Delta H \vert \le 50$ kJ/mol H₂ for ambient-temperature applications.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given data:**
- $T_1 = 40 \text{ °C} = 313 \ \text{K}$, $P_1 = 4.0$ bar
- $T_2 = 80 \text{ °C} = 353 \ \text{K}$, $P_2 = 8.5$ bar

**Step (i) — Calculate ΔH:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{8.5}{4.0}\right) = \ln(2.125) = 0.7538$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{353} - \frac{1}{313} = \frac{313 - 353}{353 \times 313} = \frac{-40}{110{,}489} = -3.620 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

Substitute:

$$0.7538 = -\frac{\Delta H}{8.314}\times(-3.620\times 10^{-4})$$

$$\Delta H = \frac{0.7538 \times 8.314}{3.620 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{6.266}{3.620 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\Delta H = 17{,}308 \ \text{J/mol} \approx -17.31 \ \text{kJ/mol H}_2$$

**Step (ii) — Calculate ΔS:**

Using Van't Hoff equation at T₁ = 313 K:

$$\ln(4.0) = -\frac{(-17{,}308)}{8.314 \times 313} + \frac{\Delta S}{8.314}$$

$$1.3863 = \frac{17{,}308}{2602.3} + \frac{\Delta S}{8.314} = 6.651 + \frac{\Delta S}{8.314}$$

$$\frac{\Delta S}{8.314} = 1.3863 - 6.651 = -5.265$$

$$\Delta S = -5.265 \times 8.314 = -43.78 \ \text{J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$$

**Result:**
- $\Delta H = -17.31$ kJ/mol H₂
- $\Delta S = -43.78$ J/mol·K

> **Note for KTU Examiner:** The relatively low ΔH suggests this ZrMn₂ alloy is suitable for **low-temperature applications (heat-pump / buffer storage)** but releases hydrogen at slightly elevated pressure even near ambient temperature.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q.B.** *(a)* With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a metal hydride hydrogen storage tank. Discuss the absorption and desorption cycles, highlighting the role of heat exchange. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* The alloy Mg₂Ni forms the hydride Mg₂NiH₄. Compute its (i) theoretical gravimetric hydrogen storage capacity, and (ii) the volume of hydrogen gas at STP released by 1 kg of fully charged Mg₂NiH₄. Given: $M_{Mg} = 24.305$ g/mol, $M_{Ni} = 58.693$ g/mol. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

A metal hydride storage tank consists of:

1. **Reactor vessel (stainless steel / aluminium pressure shell)** packed with the alloy powder (typically 100–500 µm particle size).
2. **Heat exchanger coils** inside the bed for heat transfer fluid (water/thermal oil).
3. **Hydrogen inlet/outlet manifold** with check valves and pressure regulators.
4. **Sintered metal filter** (5–10 µm pore size) to retain the powder and prevent fluidisation.
5. **Pressure and temperature sensors** for feedback control.
6. **Safety pressure-relief device** (typically set at 1.5× design pressure).

**Absorption cycle (charging):**
- Coolant flows through the coils, removing the exothermic heat of hydride formation.
- H₂ is fed at 10–30 bar; exothermic reaction ($MH_x$ forming) drives uptake.
- Typical charging time: 30–60 minutes for a 5 kg tank.

**Desorption cycle (discharging):**
- Hot fluid (80–120 °C for LaNi₅, 300–350 °C for MgH₂) flows through coils.
- Heat supplied breaks the M–H bond endothermically; H₂ desorbs.
- Plateau pressure rises, hydrogen flows to the consumer (fuel cell).

The unit acts as both a **storage medium** and a **thermal reservoir**, with efficiency dictated by the alloy's ΔH.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Molar mass of Mg₂NiH₄:**

$$M_{Mg_2NiH_4} = 2(24.305) + 58.693 + 4(1.008)$$

$$= 48.610 + 58.693 + 4.032 = 111.335 \ \text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of H in 1 mole of Mg₂NiH₄:**

$$m_{H} = 4 \times 1.008 = 4.032 \ \text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical gravimetric capacity:**

$$C_{wt\%} = \frac{4.032}{111.335} \times 100 = 3.62 \ \text{wt\%}$$

**Step 4 — Moles of Mg₂NiH₄ in 1 kg:**

$$n = \frac{1000}{111.335} = 8.981 \ \text{mol}$$

**Step 5 — Moles of H₂ released:**

Per mole of Mg₂NiH₄ → 2 moles of H₂ (since 4 H atoms = 2 H₂ molecules).

$$n_{H_2} = 8.981 \times 2 = 17.962 \ \text{mol}$$

**Step 6 — Volume of H₂ at STP:**

$$V_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \times 22{,}414 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$$

$$V_{H_2} = 17.962 \times 22{,}414 = 402{,}672 \ \text{cm}^3 \approx 402.7 \ \text{L}$$

> **Result:** Each kg of Mg₂NiH₄ releases **~403 litres of H₂ at STP**, equivalent to **~36 grams** of H₂.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones for Metal Hydride Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention blunder:** Students often report ΔH as **positive** during absorption. KTU expects the convention: **ΔH < 0 for exothermic absorption, ΔH > 0 for endothermic desorption.** (1 mark penalty per error).
>
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Van't Hoff constants must use **R = 8.314 J/mol·K** consistently. Mixing with R = 1.987 cal/mol·K without converting ΔH loses 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Moles vs. atoms confusion:** A formula like MHₓ stores **x/2 moles of H₂ gas**, not x moles. Always divide hydrogen atoms by 2 when reporting moles of H₂.
>
> 4. **PCT plateau misreading:** The plateau pressure must be read at the **mid-plateau**, not the endpoints, for accurate Van't Hoff analysis.
>
> 5. **Ignoring activation energy:** Even with a thermodynamically favourable ΔH, kinetics may be sluggish. Real systems often need a **catalyst** (Pd, Ni, Nb₂O₅) and this distinction is worth 2 marks in a 14-mark question.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Metal hydride storage** = reversible chemisorption of H₂ into intermetallic alloys forming MHₓ.
- ✅ The **Van't Hoff equation** $\ln P = -\Delta H/(RT) + \Delta S/R$ is the **core analytical tool**.
- ✅ ΔH should lie in **30–50 kJ/mol H₂** for practical engineering use.
- ✅ **AB₅ (LaNi₅)** is the easiest to activate; **MgH₂** has the highest capacity (7.66 wt%) but needs > 300 °C.
- ✅ Gravimetric capacity formula: $C_{wt\%} = \frac{x M_H}{M_{MH}} \times 100$.
- ✅ Volumetric capacity of metal hydrides **exceeds** compressed H₂ at 700 bar.
- ✅ The process is **exothermic on absorption, endothermic on desorption** — heat management is the central engineering challenge.
- ✅ **PCT curves** show three regions: α-phase, plateau (α+β), β-phase.
- ✅ **Catalysts and nano-structuring** are critical to overcome kinetic barriers.
- ✅ US DOE 2025 targets: **5.5 wt%** and **40 g H₂/L** — most current metal hydrides fall short on gravimetric density.
- ✅ Real-world applications: **NiMH batteries, FCV prototypes, heat pumps, stationary renewables storage, neutron moderators**.
- ✅ Always convert temperatures to **Kelvin** before using the Van't Hoff equation.
- ✅ The slope of $\ln P$ vs. $1/T$ plot gives $-\Delta H/R$, the intercept gives $\Delta S/R$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbon-Based Materials for Hydrogen Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Carbon-Based Materials for Hydrogen Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Carbon-based materials for hydrogen storage** are a class of nanoporous or nanostructured allotropes and engineered forms of carbon — including **Activated Carbons (AC), Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs), Graphene, Graphene Oxide (GO), Carbon Nanofibers (CNFs), Fullerenes (C₆₀), and Templated/Mesoporous Carbons** — used as solid-state adsorbents to reversibly store molecular hydrogen (H₂) primarily through **physisorption** (weak van der Waals interactions, ~4–10 kJ/mol H₂) onto high-surface-area carbon skeletons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Carbon materials are valued because they offer (i) ultra-high specific surface area (**SSA up to ~3000 m²/g**), (ii) tunable pore architecture (micro/mesopores), (iii) low mass density, (iv) chemical stability, and (v) reversible H₂ uptake without forming chemical bonds.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **microscopic kitchen sponge made of pure carbon**. The sponge has millions of tiny holes (nanopores). When hydrogen gas is forced into the sponge under pressure or cooled to cryogenic temperatures, the H₂ molecules "stick" to the walls of these holes — like dew condensing on a cold windowpane at dawn. When you release the pressure or warm it up, the hydrogen "evaporates" back out, ready to be used. **No chemical reaction, no burning, no permanent capture — just a clean, reversible, physical adsorption.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use a metal tank?**
> A 700-bar compressed gas tank stores hydrogen at ~**40 g/L** volumetric density but weighs heavily and poses safety risks. Carbon adsorbents aim to deliver similar volumetric density at **much lower pressures (10–100 bar)** by packing H₂ onto internal surfaces.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Carbon-Based H₂ Storage

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Target (DOE 2025) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravimetric capacity | $w_t$ | **≥ 5.5** | wt% |
| Volumetric capacity | $\rho_v$ | **≥ 40** | g H₂/L |
| Operating temperature | $T$ | −40 to 85 | °C |
| Operating pressure | $P$ | 5–100 | bar |
| BET surface area | $S_{BET}$ | **> 2500** | m²/g |
| Pore volume | $V_p$ | > 1.5 | cm³/g |
| Heat of adsorption | $\Delta H_{ads}$ | **15–25** | kJ/mol H₂ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — BET Surface Area ($S_{BET}$):**
> The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area is the accessible internal + external surface area of a porous solid measured by N₂ adsorption at **77 K**, calculated from the multilayer adsorption isotherm.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Storage Regimes in Carbon Materials

Carbon-based hydrogen storage operates through **three principal mechanisms**, each dominating at a different temperature/pressure regime:

**(A) Physisorption (Dominant in AC, CNTs, Graphene, COFs)**
- Van der Waals interaction: ΔH ≈ 4–10 kJ/mol H₂
- Fully reversible, fast kinetics
- Requires **cryogenic (77 K)** or **moderate pressure (50–100 bar)** for practical capacity

**(B) Chemisorption (Spillover mechanism on Pd-, Pt-, Ni-decorated carbons)**
- Hydrogen dissociates on metal catalyst, then migrates ("spills over") onto carbon surface as H-atoms
- ΔH ≈ 40–70 kJ/mol
- Higher capacity possible but **slower kinetics**, requires catalyst

**(C) Kubas-type Interaction (Metal-organic hybrids on carbon)**
- σ-bond donation from H₂ to transition metal d-orbitals
- ΔH ≈ 20–30 kJ/mol — the "sweet spot" for ambient T storage

### 2.2 Why Carbon? The Material Design Logic

Carbon's unique position comes from these **engineering advantages**:

1. **Allotropy flexibility** — same element (C, Z=6) forms 0D (fullerene), 1D (CNT), 2D (graphene), 3D (graphite, AC) structures
2. **Tunable porosity** — activation with KOH, ZnCl₂, CO₂ creates micropores (<2 nm), mesopores (2–50 nm)
3. **Chemical inertness** — stable in air, water, acids (except strong oxidizers)
4. **Lightweight** — atomic mass = 12 g/mol → gravimetric benefit
5. **Scalable synthesis** — from coconut shells (AC), CH₄ CVD (CNTs), Hummers' method (GO)

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

For a typical hydrogen adsorption measurement on activated carbon:

**Step 1** — Degassing: Sample heated to **300 °C** under vacuum (<10⁻³ Pa) for 12 h to remove adsorbed moisture/gases.

**Step 2** — Sieverts' Method (volumetric): A known volume $V$ at pressure $P_1$ is allowed to expand into the sample cell. The pressure drops to $P_2$ due to H₂ uptake.

**Step 3** — Mass balance:
$$n_{adsorbed} = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) \cdot V_{system}}{R \cdot T}$$

**Step 4** — Gravimetric capacity:
$$w_t \, (\%) = \frac{m_{H_2}}{m_{carbon}} \times 100 = \frac{n_{adsorbed} \cdot M_{H_2}}{m_{carbon}} \times 100$$

**Step 5** — Isotherm fitting: Data fitted to **Langmuir**, **Freundlich**, or **Dubinin–Astakhov (DA)** models to extract $V_{micro}$ and characteristic adsorption energy $E_0$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Equation | Use | Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w_t = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{carbon}+m_{H_2}} \times 100$ | Gravimetric capacity | 1–8 wt% (AC at 77 K) |
| 2 | $\rho_v = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{V_{total}}$ | Volumetric capacity | 20–50 g/L |
| 3 | $\theta = \dfrac{bP}{1+bP}$ (Langmuir) | Monolayer coverage | $b$ = affinity const. |
| 4 | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m c} + \dfrac{(c-1)}{V_m c}\cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ (BET) | Surface area calc | $V_m$ = monolayer vol. |
| 5 | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot a_s}{V_{molar}}$ | BET surface area | $a_s$ = 0.162 nm² (N₂) |
| 6 | $W = W_0 \exp\left[-\left(\dfrac{A}{E_0}\right)^n\right]$ (DA) | Micropore filling | $E_0$ = characteristic energy |
| 7 | $A = RT \ln(P_0/P)$ | Adsorption potential | — |
| 8 | $q_{st} = -R \dfrac{\partial \ln P}{\partial (1/T)}\vert_n$ | Isosteric heat of adsorption | 4–10 kJ/mol (physis.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Trade-off (KTU Favourite Question):**
> Increasing $S_{BET}$ alone does NOT linearly increase H₂ uptake. **Pore size must match the H₂ kinetic diameter (0.289 nm)**. Micropores < 1 nm show enhanced uptake due to overlap of potential wells from opposite pore walls (Cheng–Yang model).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Langmuir Isotherm Applied to Hydrogen on Activated Carbon

The Langmuir isotherm assumes:
- Monolayer adsorption
- Uniform adsorption sites
- No interaction between adsorbed molecules

Let $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered by H₂.

**Rate of adsorption** (molecules hitting + sticking):
$$r_{ads} = k_a P (1 - \theta)$$

**Rate of desorption** (molecules leaving):
$$r_{des} = k_d \theta$$

At equilibrium: $r_{ads} = r_{des}$.

$$k_a P (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a P = \theta (k_d + k_a P)$$

$$\theta = \frac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $k_d$:

$$\theta = \frac{(k_a/k_d) P}{1 + (k_a/k_d) P}$$

Define the Langmuir constant $b = k_a / k_d$:

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{bP}{1 + bP}}$$

The **volumetric uptake** $V$ is related to monolayer volume $V_m$:

$$V = \frac{V_m \, b P}{1 + b P}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: BET Surface Area from N₂ Adsorption at 77 K

Starting from the BET linear form:

$$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m c} + \frac{(c - 1)}{V_m c} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$$

**Step 1** — Plot experimental data: $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)}$ vs $\dfrac{P}{P_0}$ for $0.05 \le P/P_0 \le 0.30$.

**Step 2** — Linear regression yields:
- Slope $= \dfrac{c-1}{V_m c}$
- Intercept $= \dfrac{1}{V_m c}$

**Step 3** — Solving simultaneously:

$$V_m = \frac{1}{\text{slope} + \text{intercept}}$$

**Step 4** — Compute specific surface area:

$$S_{BET} = \frac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot a_s}{V_{mol} \cdot m_{sample}}$$

where:
- $N_A$ = **6.022 × 10²³ /mol** (Avogadro)
- $a_s$ = **0.162 nm²** (cross-section of N₂)
- $V_{mol}$ = **22,414 cm³/mol** (molar volume at STP)
- $m_{sample}$ = sample mass (g)

**Step 5** — Numerical Example:
Given slope = 4.20 cm³·g⁻¹, intercept = 0.15 cm³·g⁻¹.

$$V_m = \frac{1}{4.20 + 0.15} = \frac{1}{4.35} = 0.230 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g (STP)}$$

$$S_{BET} = \frac{0.230 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.162 \times 10^{-18}}{22{,}414}$$

$$S_{BET} = \frac{2.244 \times 10^{4}}{22{,}414} = 1.001 \text{ m}^2/\text{g (per unit }V_m\text{)}$$

Scaling: $S_{BET} \approx 4.35 \times V_m \, (\text{m}^2/\text{g})$ for N₂.

$$\boxed{S_{BET} \approx 4.35 \times 0.230 = 1001 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}}$$

This is typical of a **moderately activated carbon**; KOH-activated carbons reach **> 3000 m²/g** ("activated carbon supercapacitor grade").

### 3.3 Derivation: Hydrogen Gravimetric Capacity from Sieverts' Data

**Given:**
- Sample mass $m_c = 0.500$ g activated carbon
- System volume $V_{sys} = 25.0$ cm³
- Initial pressure $P_1 = 50.0$ bar at $T = 77$ K
- Equilibrium pressure $P_2 = 42.5$ bar
- $R = 83.14$ cm³·bar/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Moles of H₂ admitted initially** (assume reference empty cell):
$$n_1 = \frac{P_1 V_{sys}}{RT} = \frac{50.0 \times 25.0}{83.14 \times 77.0}$$

$$n_1 = \frac{1250}{6401.8} = 0.1953 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of H₂ remaining in gas phase at equilibrium:**
$$n_2 = \frac{P_2 V_{sys}}{RT} = \frac{42.5 \times 25.0}{83.14 \times 77.0} = \frac{1062.5}{6401.8} = 0.1660 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Moles adsorbed:**
$$n_{ads} = n_1 - n_2 = 0.1953 - 0.1660 = 0.0293 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of H₂ adsorbed:**
$$m_{H_2} = n_{ads} \times M_{H_2} = 0.0293 \times 2.016 = 0.0591 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 — Gravimetric capacity:**
$$w_t = \frac{0.0591}{0.500 + 0.0591} \times 100 = \frac{0.0591}{0.5591} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{w_t \approx 10.57 \text{ wt\%}}$$

This is an **excellent value** — at 77 K, well-designed ACs reach 5–8 wt%; values above 10 wt% are reported only in extreme surface area carbons (e.g., MOF-derived).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Carbon Allotropes for H₂ Storage

| Material | $S_{BET}$ (m²/g) | $w_t$ at 77 K / 1 bar | $w_t$ at RT / 100 bar | $\Delta H_{ads}$ (kJ/mol) | Cost | Maturity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Activated Carbon (AC)** | 1000–3500 | 4–6 wt% | 0.5–1.0 wt% | 5–7 | Low | Commercial |
| **SWCNTs (single-walled)** | 200–1300 | 2–4 wt% | 0.4–0.8 wt% | 6–9 | High | Lab-scale |
| **MWCNTs (multi-walled)** | 100–500 | 1.5–3 wt% | 0.2–0.5 wt% | 5–8 | Medium | Pilot |
| **Graphene / GO** | 260–1500 | 1–3 wt% | 0.3–0.6 wt% | 4–6 | Medium | Lab-scale |
| **Carbon Nanofibers (CNF)** | 50–300 | 1–2 wt% | 0.2–0.4 wt% | 5–7 | Medium | Pilot |
| **Fullerene C₆₀** | < 50 (exterior) | 0.5–1.0 wt% | < 0.2 wt% | 5–8 | Very High | Research |
| **Templated Carbon** | 1500–3500 | 5–7 wt% | 0.7–1.2 wt% | 6–9 | Medium | Lab-scale |
| **MOF-derived Carbon** | 2500–4000 | 6–9 wt% | 1.0–1.5 wt% | 7–10 | High | Research |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Carbon Allotrope Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CARBON-BASED H2 STORAGE MATERIALS"]
    A --> B["Zero-Dimensional 0D"]
    A --> C["One-Dimensional 1D"]
    A --> D["Two-Dimensional 2D"]
    A --> E["Three-Dimensional 3D"]

    B --> B1["Fullerene C60"]
    B --> B2["Carbon Onions"]
    B --> B3["Carbon Dots"]

    C --> C1["Single Walled CNT SWCNT"]
    C --> C2["Multi Walled CNT MWCNT"]
    C --> C3["Carbon Nanofibers CNF"]

    D --> D1["Graphene"]
    D --> D2["Graphene Oxide GO"]
    D --> D3["Reduced Graphene Oxide rGO"]

    E --> E1["Activated Carbon AC"]
    E --> E2["Templated Mesoporous Carbon"]
    E --> E3["MOF Derived Carbon"]
    E --> E4["Activated Carbon Fibers ACF"]

    C1 --> F["H2 Physisorption on External and Internal Surfaces"]
    C2 --> F
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
    F --> G["Operating Conditions: 77 K Cryogenic OR RT with 50 to 100 bar"]
    G --> H["Target: greater than 5.5 wt% DOE 2025"]
```

### 4.2 Hydrogen Adsorption Mechanism on Activated Carbon (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1["PHYSISORPTION PATHWAY"]
        H2A["H2 Gas Molecule 0.289 nm"] --> VA["Van der Waals Force 4 to 10 kJ per mol"]
        VA --> SA["Sticking on Carbon Pore Wall"]
        SA --> MC["Monolayer or Multimolecular Filling"]
    end

    subgraph P2["CHEMISORPTION PATHWAY SPILLOVER"]
        H2B["H2 Gas Molecule"] --> DISS["Dissociation on Metal Catalyst Pd Pt Ni"]
        DISS --> SPILL["H Atom Spillover onto Carbon"]
        SPILL --> CH["Strong C H Bond Formation"]
    end

    subgraph P3["DESORPTION REVERSIBLE"]
        SA --> DES["Pressure Drop OR Temperature Rise"]
        CH --> DES2["Heating to 200 to 400 C"]
        DES --> H2OUT["H2 Released for Use"]
        DES2 --> H2OUT
    end

    H2OUT --> APP["Application: Fuel Cells, ICE, Industrial"]
```

### 4.3 Pore Size Distribution Effect on H₂ Uptake

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["PORE SIZE in NANOMETERS"]
    P --> Q1["Micropores less than 2 nm"]
    P --> Q2["Mesopores 2 to 50 nm"]
    P --> Q3["Macropores greater than 50 nm"]

    Q1 --> R1["ULTRA HIGH UPTAKE"]
    R1 --> R1A["Potential wells overlap from opposite walls"]
    R1A --> R1B["Stronger effective binding energy"]
    R1B --> R1C["Ideal: 0.6 to 0.9 nm for H2"]

    Q2 --> R2["MODERATE UPTAKE"]
    R2 --> R2A["Useful for transport pathways"]
    R2A --> R2B["Enhances kinetics and diffusion"]

    Q3 --> R3["LOW UPTAKE"]
    R3 --> R3A["Mostly bulk gas filling"]
    R3A --> R3B["Minimal surface contribution"]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow: From Precursor to Hydrogen-Loaded Activated Carbon

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["PRECURSOR: Coconut Shell, Coal, Biomass, Pitch"] --> S2["CARBONIZATION: 400 to 900 C in Inert N2 Atmosphere"]
    S2 --> S3["CHAR PRODUCT Low Surface Area"]
    S3 --> S4["ACTIVATION STEP"]
    S4 --> S4A["Physical Activation: CO2 or Steam at 800 to 1000 C"]
    S4 --> S4B["Chemical Activation: KOH, ZnCl2, H3PO4 at 600 to 800 C"]
    S4A --> S5["HIGH SURFACE AREA ACTIVATED CARBON"]
    S4B --> S5
    S5 --> S6["CHARACTERIZATION: BET N2 77 K, SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS"]
    S6 --> S7["H2 ADSORPTION TEST: Sieverts or Gravimetric"]
    S7 --> S8["DATA: Isotherm, Capacity wt%, qst"]
    S8 --> S9["EVALUATION vs DOE TARGETS 5.5 wt%"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define physisorption and chemisorption in the context of hydrogen storage on carbon materials. Which mechanism dominates in activated carbon and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Physisorption (1 mark):** Physical adsorption of H₂ molecules on the carbon surface via weak van der Waals forces; ΔH ≈ 4–10 kJ/mol; fully reversible; no activation energy required.
- **Chemisorption (1 mark):** Chemical adsorption involving dissociation of H₂ and formation of C–H bonds; ΔH ≈ 40–200 kJ/mol; requires activation energy; often irreversible at low T.
- **Dominance in AC (1 mark):** Physisorption dominates because activated carbon has no catalytic metal sites to dissociate H₂ and its curved/graphitic surfaces provide weak, uniform binding; storage is fully reversible at 77 K with moderate pressures.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is the BET surface area alone not a sufficient predictor of hydrogen storage capacity in carbon materials?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Pore size mismatch (1 mark):** H₂ kinetic diameter = 0.289 nm. Macropores contribute surface area but little H₂ uptake; only micropores < 1 nm give enhanced uptake via potential-well overlap.
2. **Surface chemistry (1 mark):** Functional groups (–OH, –COOH) on GO may attract water but block H₂ access; oxygen content can reduce capacity by 30–50%.
3. **Pore connectivity (1 mark):** Closed pores (e.g., in fullerenes) contribute to BET area in some methods but are inaccessible to H₂; tortuosity affects diffusion kinetics.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Discuss the hydrogen storage mechanisms in **(i) activated carbon, (ii) carbon nanotubes, and (iii) metal-decorated graphene**. Compare their gravimetric capacities at 77 K and room temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Langmuir isotherm equation** for hydrogen adsorption on a carbon surface. A sample of activated carbon (mass = 0.8 g) adsorbs 0.045 mol of H₂ at 77 K and 30 bar. Calculate the **(i)** Langmuir constant $b$, given monolayer capacity $V_m = 0.060$ mol, and **(ii)** the surface coverage $\theta$. State one limitation of the Langmuir model for H₂ storage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Material | Mechanism | $w_t$ at 77 K | $w_t$ at RT | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Activated Carbon | Microporous physisorption | 4–6 wt% | 0.5–1 wt% | Ultra-high SSA 3000 m²/g |
| Carbon Nanotubes | Channel + groove sites | 2–4 wt% (SWCNT) | 0.4–0.8 wt% | 1D geometry, curvature effect |
| Metal-decorated Graphene | Kubas / spillover | 3–6 wt% (theoretical) | 1–2 wt% | Ti, Sc, Ca atoms tune binding |

**Comparison (3 marks):**
- At 77 K, AC > CNT > Graphene due to microporosity.
- At RT, all physisorbents < 1 wt%; metal-decorated graphene offers enhancement via Kubas interaction (ΔH ≈ 20–30 kJ/mol — closer to DOE sweet spot).
- AC is the most commercially mature; CNTs/Graphene remain lab-scale due to synthesis cost.

**Engineering significance (1 mark):**
AC-based cryo-compressed tanks (e.g., **Lawrence Livermore National Lab**) achieved **~ 40 g H₂/L** at 77 K + 150 bar — comparable to 700-bar compressed gas but at much lower pressure.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Derivation (3 marks):** Already derived in Section 3.1.

$$r_{ads} = k_a P (1 - \theta), \quad r_{des} = k_d \theta$$

At equilibrium: $\theta = \dfrac{bP}{1 + bP}$ where $b = k_a/k_d$.

For a finite monolayer capacity $V_m$:

$$V = V_m \cdot \frac{bP}{1 + bP}$$

**(ii) Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $V_{ads} = 0.045$ mol, $V_m = 0.060$ mol, $P = 30$ bar.

From Langmuir:
$$0.045 = 0.060 \cdot \frac{b \cdot 30}{1 + b \cdot 30}$$

$$\frac{0.045}{0.060} = \frac{30 b}{1 + 30 b}$$

$$0.75 = \frac{30 b}{1 + 30 b}$$

$$0.75 (1 + 30 b) = 30 b$$

$$0.75 + 22.5 b = 30 b$$

$$0.75 = 7.5 b$$

$$\boxed{b = 0.10 \text{ bar}^{-1}}$$

Surface coverage:
$$\theta = \frac{0.045}{0.060} = 0.75 \text{ or } 75\%$$

**Limitation (1 mark):**
Langmuir assumes **uniform, identical adsorption sites and no H₂–H₂ interaction** — invalid at high pressure (> 50 bar) and for heterogeneous carbon surfaces with both micro/mesopores and surface oxides. BET or DA models are preferred.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Sieverts' volumetric method** for measuring hydrogen storage capacity of a carbon adsorbent. List two sources of error in the technique. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An activated carbon with $S_{BET} = 2800$ m²/g and micropore volume $V_{micro} = 1.1$ cm³/g is tested for H₂ storage at 77 K and 1 bar. Using the **Cheng–Yang correlation**:
$$w_t \, (\text{wt\%}) = 0.000268 \cdot S_{BET}$$
estimate the maximum theoretical hydrogen capacity. If the **Dubinin–Astakhov characteristic energy** $E_0 = 8.5$ kJ/mol, compute the **isosteric heat of adsorption** at fractional filling $\theta = 0.5$ using:
$$q_{st} = q_{st}^0 \cdot (1 - \alpha \theta)$$
where $q_{st}^0 = 1.4 \cdot E_0$ and $\alpha = 0.3$. Comment on whether this carbon is suitable for **ambient-temperature storage**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Diagram description (3 marks):**
The Sieverts apparatus consists of:
- **Reservoir volume** $V_R$ with known pressure $P_R$ and temperature $T_R$ (kept at RT)
- **Sample cell** $V_S$ containing the carbon, immersed in a **77 K liquid nitrogen dewar**
- **Valves** $V_1$ (between reservoir and sample) and $V_2$ (to vacuum pump)
- **Pressure transducers** $PT_1$ (reservoir) and $PT_2$ (sample cell)

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Sample degassed under vacuum at 300 °C for 12 h.
2. Reservoir filled with H₂ at $P_R$.
3. Valve $V_1$ opened; H₂ expands into sample cell.
4. Pressure drops from $P_R$ to equilibrium $P_{eq}$.
5. Moles adsorbed = $\dfrac{(P_R V_R + P_R V_S) - P_{eq}(V_R + V_S)}{RT}$ (cold-volume correction needed for 77 K).
6. Repeat at multiple pressures to obtain isotherm.

**Sources of error (1 mark):**
- **Cold dead volume** miscalculation (volume of cell fittings at 77 K differs from RT).
- **Leakage** during long isotherm runs (especially with cryogenic seals).
- **Non-ideality** of H₂ at high pressures (Z-factor correction required above 100 bar).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Gravimetric capacity estimate (2 marks):**

$$w_t = 0.000268 \times 2800 = 0.7504 \text{ wt\% (at 77 K, 1 bar)}$$

Comment: This matches typical AC measurements (0.5–1.5 wt% at 77 K, 1 bar); full DOE target of 5.5 wt% requires either **higher pressure (50–100 bar)** or **higher surface area (> 4000 m²/g)**.

**(ii) Isosteric heat calculation (3 marks):**

$$q_{st}^0 = 1.4 \times 8.5 = 11.9 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$q_{st}(\theta = 0.5) = 11.9 \times (1 - 0.3 \times 0.5) = 11.9 \times 0.85$$

$$\boxed{q_{st} = 10.115 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**(iii) Comment on ambient-T suitability (2 marks):**
- DOE ideal range: **15–25 kJ/mol** for room-temperature reversible storage.
- Our value (10.1 kJ/mol) **is too low** — at 298 K, thermal energy ($RT = 2.48$ kJ/mol) easily overcomes physisorption binding, leading to negligible ambient uptake (< 0.5 wt%).
- **Conclusion:** This carbon is **suitable only for cryogenic (77 K) storage**, not for ambient-temperature applications. To reach RT viability, one must **(i) introduce metal dopants (Pd, Pt, Ni) for spillover, (ii) use Kubas-type organometallic complexes, or (iii) narrow pore size below 0.7 nm** to strengthen physisorption.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students frequently confuse **BET surface area (measured at 77 K with N₂)** with **hydrogen-accessible surface area**. N₂ (0.364 nm) is larger than H₂ (0.289 nm), so some ultramicropores counted in BET may be H₂-inaccessible, while others may be H₂-selective. Always cross-check with **H₂ isotherm at 77 K + DA analysis**. Also remember to convert the system pressure to **absolute (gauge + 1.013 bar)** before applying the ideal gas law.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Carbon allotropes** for H₂ storage: AC, SWCNT, MWCNT, Graphene, GO, CNF, Fullerene C₆₀, Templated Carbon, MOF-derived Carbon.
- **Dominant mechanism in pristine carbon:** **Physisorption** (van der Waals, ΔH = 4–10 kJ/mol); fully reversible; cryogenic (77 K) operation for practical capacity.
- **Surface area metric:** BET N₂ at 77 K; DOE target **> 2500 m²/g**; world-record AC ≈ 3700 m²/g (KOH activation).
- **Optimal pore size for H₂:** **0.6–0.9 nm** (Cheng–Yang overlap regime).
- **Three capacity equations to remember:**
  - Gravimetric: $w_t = m_{H_2}/(m_c + m_{H_2}) \times 100$
  - Volumetric: $\rho_v = m_{H_2}/V_{total}$
  - Langmuir: $\theta = bP/(1 + bP)$
- **DOE 2025 targets:** **5.5 wt%** gravimetric, **40 g/L** volumetric, at near-ambient conditions.
- **Critical limitations of carbon physisorbents:**
  1. Too low ΔH for ambient T operation.
  2. Capacity drops 80–90% from 77 K → 298 K.
  3. Material packing density loss at high SSA (gravimetric vs volumetric trade-off).
- **Modification strategies:** (i) **Metal doping** (Pd, Pt, Ni) for spillover, (ii) **Heteroatom doping** (N, B, S) to polarize surface, (iii) **Pore-size tuning** via templating, (iv) **Composites with MOFs/hydrides**.
- **Measurement techniques:** Sieverts (volumetric), gravimetric (magnetic suspension microbalance), TPD (temperature-programmed desorption), PCT (pressure–composition–temperature).
- **Industrial milestones:** Hydrogenious LOHC (NOT carbon), but Ampere's **MOF-based cryo-adsorption** tanks (≈ 41 g/L at –196 °C) commercialized 2023.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-liner for KTU viva:** *"Activated carbon gives 4–6 wt% H₂ at 77 K via physisorption, but only ~ 1 wt% at room temperature — making it a cryogenic-storage material, not an ambient-temperature solution."*

---

# Module 3 — Hydrogen Safety Aspects

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrogen Safety** is defined as the integrated discipline encompassing the identification, evaluation, mitigation, and control of physical, chemical, and operational hazards associated with hydrogen throughout its lifecycle — production, compression, liquefaction, storage, distribution, and end-use application.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, hydrogen safety is not a single sub-system but a **risk-management engineering loop** comprising four pillars:

| Pillar | KTU Engineering Function |
|---|---|
| **Hazard Identification** | Recognition of hydrogen's intrinsic physical–chemical properties |
| **Risk Quantification** | Estimation of leak, fire, and explosion probabilities & consequences |
| **Mitigation Engineering** | Design of barriers, ventilation, detection, and material selection |
| **Operational Governance** | Codes, standards, training, and emergency response protocols |

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Flammable Ghost" Analogy

Imagine **natural gas (methane) as a slippery cat** in your kitchen — it pools, lingers, and you can smell the mercaptan added to it. Now replace it with a **silent, invisible, ultra-light, ultra-fast hare** that:

- Rises straight up and dissipates within seconds (high buoyancy + high diffusivity),
- Catches fire from a *spark too small for you to even feel* (0.02 mJ ignition energy — about the static discharge from your fingertip),
- Burns with a **pale, almost invisible flame in daylight**,
- Has no odour whatsoever.

This "Flammable Ghost" analogy explains *why* hydrogen behaves so differently from fossil fuels and *why* it demands bespoke safety engineering.

### 1.3 Hydrogen's Safety-Relevant Intrinsic Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why hydrogen is uniquely hazardous** — these metrics are universally cited in KTU/HSE board answers:

| Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Methane (CH₄) | Gasoline (vapour) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flammability Range (in air)** | **4 – 75 %** by vol. | 5 – 15 % | 1 – 6 % |
| **Detonation Range** | 18.3 – 59 % | 6.3 – 13.5 % | ~1.1 – 6.0 % |
| **Minimum Ignition Energy** | **0.02 mJ** | 0.29 mJ | 0.24 mJ |
| **Auto-Ignition Temperature** | 500 °C | 537 °C | 257 °C |
| **Flame Temperature (adiabatic)** | 2045 °C | 1955 °C | 2200 °C |
| **Laminar Burning Velocity** | 2.7 – 3.3 m/s | 0.4 m/s | 0.4 – 0.6 m/s |
| **Detonation Velocity** | ~ 1500 – 2000 m/s | 1800 m/s | ~ 1700 m/s |
| **Diffusion Coefficient in Air (1 atm, 25 °C)** | 0.61 cm²/s | 0.16 cm²/s | 0.05 cm²/s |
| **Buoyant Velocity (release)** | 1.2 – 9 m/s (strongly upward) | Neutral / slight downward | Strongly downward |
| **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** | **142 MJ/kg** | 55 MJ/kg | 46 MJ/kg |
| **Energy per unit volume (STP)** | 12.7 MJ/m³ | 38 MJ/m³ | 33 MJ/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Answers**: Hydrogen has the **widest flammability range**, the **lowest ignition energy**, and the **highest diffusivity** of any common fuel. However, because of its extreme buoyancy and diffusivity, in *open, well-ventilated* environments it disperses 4× faster than methane and 12× faster than gasoline vapour — reducing explosion risk outdoors.

### 1.4 Categories of Hydrogen Hazards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favoured Hazard Classification:**

1. **Physical Hazards** — Pressure (up to 700 bar for compressed gas H₂), Cryogenic (–253 °C for LH₂), Mechanical projectile.
2. **Chemical Hazards** — Flammability, Explosion (deflagration / detonation), High-temperature flame.
3. **Health Hazards** — Asphyxiation (simple — displaces O₂), Frostbite (cryogenic LH₂).
4. **Materials Hazards** — Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE), Hydrogen Induced Cracking (HIC), Stress Corrosion Cracking.
5. **Operational Hazards** — Leakage, Unintended mixing with oxidiser, Inadequate ventilation, Electrolyte spills (alkaline/PEM).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrogen Safety Triangle

Hazard magnitude is governed by the **simultaneous presence** of three factors — the **Fire Triangle** extended for hydrogen:

```
   ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
   │  FUEL    │    │  OXYGEN  │    │  IGNITION│
   │  (H₂)    │    │  (Air)   │    │  SOURCE  │
   └────┬─────┘    └────┬─────┘    └────┬─────┘
        └────────────────┴────────────────┘
                (All THREE required)
```

For prevention, **engineers must break at least one leg**:
- Fuel elimination → Leak-tight joints, automated valves, double-walled piping.
- Oxygen control → Inerting with N₂/Ar, controlled ventilation.
- Ignition elimination → Bonding & grounding, EX-proof electrical, intrinsically safe instruments.

## 2.2 Hazard Mechanisms Explained

### 2.2.1 Flammability vs Detonability

- **Flammability (Deflagration)**: Combustion front propagates by thermal conduction & radiation at **sub-sonic** speed (sub-1 m/s to ~100 m/s).
- **Detonation**: Shock-coupled combustion at **supersonic** speed (~1500–2000 m/s for H₂). Produces a *shock wave* and destructive overpressure.
- Hydrogen's narrow *Detonation Cell Size* (~1.5 cm at stoichiometric) means detonation is achievable in smaller geometries than for hydrocarbon fuels — **a critical KTU safety consideration**.

### 2.2.2 Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)

When atomic hydrogen diffuses into a metal lattice, it locally reduces ductility and tensile strength. Affected materials include **high-strength steels**, certain austenitic stainless steels under stress, and titanium alloys. Mitigations:
- Use of **low-carbon austenitic SS 304L/316L**.
- **Internal coating** (Ni, Cu, polymer liners).
- **Bake-out cycles** for steel cylinders before re-pressurisation.
- Selection of **aluminium or fibre-composite overwrapped** pressure vessels (Type IV tanks).

### 2.2.3 Cryogenic Hazards (LH₂)

- Boiling point: **–253 °C (20.28 K)**.
- 1 litre of LH₂ expands to **≈ 850 litres** of gaseous H₂ at NTP.
- Risk: Frostbite, structural embrittlement of carbon steel, oxygen enrichment of cold surfaces leading to violent combustion of any condensate.

### 2.2.4 Invisible Flame

Hydrogen flames emit predominantly in the **UV and IR**, very little in the visible band. Daylight flame is virtually invisible. Solution: **FLIR thermal imagers** or **UV/IR flame detectors** (mandatory in KTU board answers for indoor H₂ facilities).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — every KTU Part-B on Hydrogen Safety uses 2–3 of them.**

### 2.3.1 Choked (Sonic) Leak Mass-Flow Rate for Hydrogen

When pressure ratio P₀/P_a ≥ (γ+1)/2, flow is choked (sonic) at the orifice:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R_{sp}\,T_0} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Hydrogen Value |
|---|---|---|
| $C_d$ | Discharge coefficient | 0.62 – 0.99 |
| $A$ | Leak cross-sectional area (m²) | — |
| $P_0$ | Upstream stagnation pressure (Pa) | — |
| $\gamma$ | Specific heat ratio | **1.41** |
| $R_{sp}$ | Specific gas constant for H₂ | **4124 J/(kg·K)** |
| $T_0$ | Upstream temperature (K) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Valuation**: Hydrogen, due to its *very low molecular weight*, has the *highest choked-flow velocity* of any gas (~ 1300 m/s). Even a **1 mm² leak at 200 bar** can release tens of kg/hour.

### 2.3.2 TNT Equivalent Mass (for blast-overpressure estimation)

$$m_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m_{H_2} \cdot HHV_{H_2}}{E_{TNT}}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta$ | TNT equivalence factor | **0.10 – 1.0** (typically **1.0** for conservative design) |
| $m_{H_2}$ | Released hydrogen mass (kg) | — |
| $HHV_{H_2}$ | Higher heating value of H₂ | **142 × 10⁶ J/kg** |
| $E_{TNT}$ | Energy of TNT | **4.52 × 10⁶ J/kg** |

### 2.3.3 Scaled Distance for Blast Damage Zoning

$$Z = \dfrac{R}{\sqrt[3]{m_{TNT}}}$$

where $R$ is the radial distance (m) and $m_{TNT}$ is in kg.

| Damage Level | Overpressure (kPa) | Z (m/kg^(1/3)) |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy structural damage | > 83 | < 3 |
| Moderate damage | 35 – 83 | 3 – 6 |
| Window breakage | 7 – 17 | 6 – 12 |
| Minor / mostly safe | < 3.5 | > 12 |

### 2.3.4 Required Ventilation Air-Flow (Indoor Compressor / Electrolyser Room)

$$Q_{vent} = \dfrac{V \cdot n \cdot LFL \cdot SF}{100}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_{vent}$ | Required ventilation flow (m³/min or m³/h) | — |
| $V$ | Room volume (m³) | — |
| $n$ | Air-changes per hour | 6 – 12 |
| $LFL$ | Lower Flammability Limit of H₂ | **4 %** |
| $SF$ | Safety factor | 4 – 6 |

### 2.3.5 Concentration Build-up from Continuous Leak

$$C(t) = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{leak}}{Q_{vent}} \left(1 - e^{-\,\dfrac{Q_{vent}}{V}\,t}\right)$$

Steady-state limit: $C_\infty = \dot{V}_{leak}/Q_{vent}$. Must remain $< 25\% \, LFL = 1\%$ H₂ for ATEX unclassified; $< LFL/4$ is the typical KTU board standard.

### 2.3.6 Overpressure from a Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)

$$\Delta P = f(Z), \quad \text{using the } \text{Kingery–Bulmash polynomial curves}$$

For initial estimate within the KTU scope, the TNT-equivalent approach (above) and standard TNT blast curves (Kingery–Bulmash) are sufficient.

### 2.3.7 Heat Release / Fireball Diameter (BLEVE-type pool of LH₂)

$$D_{max} = 5.8 \cdot m_{fuel}^{1/3}, \qquad t_{duration} = 0.41 \cdot m_{fuel}^{1/3}$$

where $D_{max}$ is in metres and $t$ in seconds, $m_{fuel}$ in kg — applicable to catastrophic LH₂ spills.

### 2.3.8 Dose-Effect & Lethal Distance

$$PD\% = \dfrac{Q_{H_2}}{4 \pi R^2} \quad ; \quad LFL = 4\% \; \text{volume fraction at } R_{LFL}$$

Used to define hazard zones and exclusion radii in KTU layout-design problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Safety-Critical Function |
|---|---|
| **Hydrogen Refuelling Stations** | Use all 8 formulas above to define safety zones, detector placement, dispenser setback distances. |
| **On-board Vehicle Tanks (Type IV)** | Embrittlement-resistant composite overwrap; PRD (Pressure Relief Device) vents sizing uses choked-flow equation. |
| **LH₂ Tanker Ships** | Diked double-hold design, fireball sizing per (2.3.7), boil-off gas handling. |
| **PEM Electrolyser Stacks** | H₂ crossover to O₂-side < 2 % LFL is enforced via (2.3.5) — danger of O₂ / H₂ mixing. |
| **Underground Cavern Storage** | Blast zoning per (2.3.3) and overpressure assessment per (2.3.6). |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert**: Students often quote the **wider flammability range** as "more dangerous" without acknowledging hydrogen's **higher diffusivity and buoyancy** that make it *less dangerous* in open-air releases. Always write a **balanced** answer.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Hydrogen Hazard → Mitigation Mapping

| Hazard | Cause | Consequence | Mitigation Engineering Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unintended Release | Joint failure, valve packing | Jet fire, VCE | Leak-tight fittings, NDT, dual-seal valves, BFS (double block & bleed) |
| Accumulation indoors | Poor ventilation | Flammable cloud at 4 – 75 % | Continuous H₂ sensors, forced ventilation per (2.3.4) |
| Ignition | Static, hot work | Deflagration / detonation | Bonding/grounding, EX-rated equipment, hot-work permits |
| Embrittlement | Atomic H diffusion | Brittle fracture of pipe | Use 316L SS, aluminium, composite, internal coatings, bake-out |
| Overpressure | Thermal, fire exposure | Cylinder rupture | PRD (Pressure Relief Devices), thermal fuses |
| Cryogenic burn | LH₂ splash | Frostbite, lung damage | Cryogenic PPE, vacuum-jacketed lines |
| Asphyxiation | O₂ displacement in confined space | Loss of consciousness | O₂ monitors, confined-space entry permits |

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Choked Leak from Compressed Hydrogen Tank

> A hydrogen cylinder stores H₂ at **P₀ = 200 bar (20 MPa)** and **T₀ = 298 K**. A leak orifice of diameter **d = 2 mm** develops. The discharge coefficient is **C_d = 0.85**. Compute the mass leak rate and the time to release 1 kg of H₂.

**Step 1 — Verify choked-flow condition**

$$\dfrac{P_0}{P_a} = \dfrac{20}{0.101325} = 197.4$$

$$\left(\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = \left(\dfrac{1.41 + 1}{2}\right)^{1.41/0.41} = (1.205)^{3.439} = 1.78$$

Since 197.4 ≫ 1.78, **flow is fully choked**.

**Step 2 — Compute the choked-flow constant**

$$K_{choked} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R_{sp}\,T_0}\left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)}}$$

Computing the bracketed term:
$$\left(\dfrac{2}{2.41}\right)^{2.41/0.41} = (0.8299)^{5.878} = 0.2587$$

$$K_{choked} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.41}{4124 \times 298}\times 0.2587} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.41 \times 0.2587}{1.229 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.3648}{1.229 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{2.968 \times 10^{-7}} = 5.449 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{s/m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute leak area**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.002)^2}{4} = 3.142 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute mass flow rate**

$$\dot{m} = C_d \, A \, P_0 \, K_{choked}$$

$$= 0.85 \times 3.142\times 10^{-6} \times 20\times 10^{6} \times 5.449\times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 0.85 \times 3.142 \times 20 \times 5.449 \times 10^{-6+6-4}$$

$$= 0.85 \times 3.142 \times 20 \times 5.449 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 291.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.85 \;\; \text{(simplified)} = 0.0342 \;\; \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 5 — Time to release 1 kg of H₂**

$$t = \dfrac{m}{\dot{m}} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.0342} \approx 29.2 \;\; \text{seconds}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note**: This single result — *less than 30 seconds to release 1 kg* — is the entire reason hydrogen safety engineering is *not* optional. Use this type of calculation in any "discuss" Part-B.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — TNT Equivalent Blast Damage Zoning

> An outdoor release of **m_H₂ = 50 kg** of H₂ forms a vapour cloud that explodes. Using **η = 1.0**, find the distance at which window breakage (ΔP = 7 kPa) is expected.

**Step 1 — Total chemical energy**

$$E_{H_2} = m_{H_2}\cdot HHV_{H_2} = 50 \times 142 \times 10^{6} = 7.10 \times 10^{9} \;\; \text{J}$$

**Step 2 — TNT equivalent mass**

$$m_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot E_{H_2}}{E_{TNT}} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 7.10 \times 10^{9}}{4.52 \times 10^{6}} = 1570.8 \;\; \text{kg TNT}$$

**Step 3 — Scaled distance at ΔP = 7 kPa**

From the Kingery–Bulmash chart, the scaled distance for 7 kPa is approximately $Z \approx 13$ m/kg^(1/3).

**Step 4 — Actual distance**

$$R = Z \cdot \sqrt[3]{m_{TNT}} = 13 \times \sqrt[3]{1570.8} = 13 \times 11.61 \approx 151 \;\; \text{m}$$

> **Interpretation**: A 50 kg hydrogen release requires a **150 m exclusion radius** for window-damage prevention — a critical KTU layout-design consideration.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Indoor Hydrogen Detector Sizing

> An indoor electrolyser room has dimensions **5 m × 4 m × 3 m (V = 60 m³)**. Continuous operation releases hydrogen at $\dot{V}_{leak} = 0.5 \text{ L/min}$ at STP. Calculate the steady-state concentration and decide whether ventilation suffices.

**Step 1 — Convert leak to m³/min**

$$\dot{V}_{leak} = 0.5 \; \text{L/min} = 0.5\times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 2 — Compute required ventilation for steady-state ≤ 25 % LFL**

$$C_\infty = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{leak}}{Q_{vent}} \le 0.25 \times 4\% = 1\% = 0.01$$

$$Q_{vent} \ge \dfrac{\dot{V}_{leak}}{0.01} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01} = 0.05 \; \text{m}^3/\text{min} = 3 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalently, ensure ≥ 6 air changes/hour**

$$Q_{air-changes} = 60 \times 6 = 360 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h} \gg 3 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

Ventilation is **vastly oversized** for this leak — additional safety factor is large, demonstrating why ATEX zoning for indoor electrolyser rooms typically results in **non-classified** status if sensors operate correctly.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Time to Reach LFL from a Continuous Leak

> A compressor room of **V = 100 m³** has forced ventilation **Q_vent = 600 m³/h**. A leak of $\dot{V}_{leak} = 0.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ (gas-phase, at room T, P). Find the **time** to reach the LFL (4 %).

**Step 1 — Steady-state concentration**

$$C_\infty = \dfrac{0.2}{600} = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0333\%$$

Since $C_\infty < LFL$, the room is *intrinsically safe* under steady-state. But for **time to reach 25% LFL = 1%**:

$$C(t) = C_\infty \left(1 - e^{-\,\dfrac{Q_{vent}}{V}\,t}\right) = 0.01$$

**Step 2 — Solve for t**

$$1 - e^{-\,\dfrac{600}{100}\,t \cdot (1/3600)} = \dfrac{0.01}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}} = 30$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Mathematically impossible** — the leak can never reach 1 % concentration. This is the **purpose of ventilation engineering** — to demonstrate by calculation that release scenarios remain sub-LFL, removing the need for explosion-proof certification (a major KTU design objective).

## 3.6 Comparison — Hydrogen vs Other Fuels Safety

| Parameter | H₂ | CNG (Methane) | Petrol | LPG |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Buoyancy | Strongly ↑ | Neutral | Strongly ↓ | ↓ |
| Ignition energy | **0.02 mJ** | 0.29 mJ | 0.24 mJ | 0.30 mJ |
| Detectability (smell) | None | Odourised | Strong | Odourised |
| Flame visibility | Invisible | Slight yellow | Yellow | Yellow |
| Flame temp. | 2045 °C | 1955 °C | 2200 °C | 2000 °C |
| Water solubility | Low | Low | Low | Low |
| Toxicity | None | None | Toxic (benzene, etc.) | Slight |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion**: Hydrogen trades *lower chemical toxicity* and *high buoyancy* for *wider flammability range*, *lower ignition energy*, and *invisibility of flame*. Engineering compensates with: **sensors, ventilation, leak-tight design, EX-rated equipment, and invisible-flame detectors**.
