### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> *Why does hydrogen combustion produce NOx even though the fuel contains no carbon or nitrogen?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hydrogen combustion does not contain fuel-bound nitrogen, so **fuel-NOx is zero** and there are no hydrocarbons to initiate **prompt NOx**. However, at the high adiabatic flame temperature of H₂ in air (**≈ 2400 K**), atmospheric N₂ reacts with free oxygen and hydroxyl radicals via the **Zeldovich (thermal) mechanism**. The rate-limiting step O + N₂ → NO + N has an activation energy of **318 kJ/mol**, which is overcome above 1800 K. Hence, although H₂ is a "clean" fuel, **thermal NOx formation is unavoidable** in stoichiometric or rich H₂ flames and must be controlled by lowering peak flame temperature or using after-treatment.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *List any three primary NOx control techniques used in hydrogen-fueled engines and explain the principle behind one of them.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three primary NOx control techniques are:
1. **Lean burn combustion** (operates above stoichiometric air–fuel ratio)
2. **Exhaust gas recirculation (EGR)**
3. **Premixed / Staged combustion**

**Principle of Lean Burn (2 of the 3 marks):** Excess air acts as a thermal diluent. The peak flame temperature drops with increasing air–fuel ratio λ according to the empirical relation $T_{\text{ad, lean}} \approx T_{\text{ad, stoich}} - 80(\lambda - 1)$. Since thermal NOx scales as $\exp(-E_a / RT)$, even a modest temperature reduction yields a large NOx decrease. Combined with H₂'s wide flammability range (4–75 %), lean burn is a particularly effective NOx-reduction strategy in H₂ engines.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Comprehensive Theory + Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
> *(a)* Explain the three mechanisms of NOx formation. Comment on which mechanism is most relevant in a pure hydrogen flame and justify your answer with appropriate chemical equations. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A hydrogen internal combustion engine operates with a peak flame temperature of 2350 K. Calculate the ratio of thermal NOx produced compared to operation at 2200 K. Use the simplified relation $\text{NO}_x \propto \exp(-E_a/RT)$ with $E_a = 318$ kJ/mol and $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**The three mechanisms of NOx formation (3 marks):**

1. **Thermal (Zeldovich) NOx:** High-temperature reaction between atmospheric N₂ and oxygen-containing radicals. Dominant above 1800 K.

2. **Prompt (Fenimore) NOx:** Initiated by hydrocarbon radicals (CH, CH₂) attacking N₂ to form HCN. Important in fuel-rich hydrocarbon flames at moderate temperatures.

3. **Fuel-Bound NOx:** Formed from nitrogen chemically bound in the fuel (e.g., in coal, heavy oils, certain biofuels). Not relevant for H₂ since H₂ contains no nitrogen.

**Equations of the Zeldovich mechanism (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N} \\
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \\
\text{N} + \text{OH} &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification for dominance in H₂ flames (2 marks):** In a pure H₂ flame, there are no hydrocarbon radicals to initiate prompt NOx (→ near zero) and no fuel-bound nitrogen (→ zero fuel NOx). However, the H₂–air flame reaches very high adiabatic temperatures (2400 K), which exceed the activation barrier of the rate-limiting Zeldovich step (318 kJ/mol). Therefore, **thermal NOx is the dominant and essentially the only mechanism in pure H₂ flames.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 2350$ K, $T_2 = 2200$ K
- $E_a = 318{,}000$ J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Apply the Arrhenius-type dependence:**

$$
\frac{\text{NO}_{x, 2350}}{\text{NO}_{x, 2200}} = \frac{\exp(-E_a / R T_1)}{\exp(-E_a / R T_2)} = \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inverse temperature difference:**

$$
\frac{1}{2350} - \frac{1}{2200} = \frac{2200 - 2350}{2350 \times 2200} = \frac{-150}{5{,}170{,}000} \approx -2.9015 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent:**

$$
-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = -\frac{318{,}000}{8.314} \times (-2.9015 \times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
= +38{,}249 \times 2.9015 \times 10^{-5} \approx +1.1098
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio:**

$$
\frac{\text{NO}_{x, 2350}}{\text{NO}_{x, 2200}} = e^{+1.1098} \approx 3.034
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** Operating at 2350 K produces approximately **3.03 times more thermal NOx** than at 2200 K. This dramatic 150 K sensitivity underscores the importance of flame-temperature control in H₂ systems.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Alternative Long-Answer

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Alternative]**
> *(a)* With the aid of a labeled schematic, describe the working principle of an H₂-SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) system. Write the governing chemical reaction and state two advantages of using H₂ as the reducing agent over conventional ammonia (NH₃). **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* An industrial hydrogen gas turbine uses a Dry Low NOx (DLN) combustor with premixed combustion. The pre-mixed fuel–air mixture is delivered at a temperature of 700 K. If the equivalence ratio is φ = 0.4 (lean) and the combustor exit temperature is 1500 K, determine whether the exhaust stream will fall above or below the thermal NOx formation threshold of 1800 K, and propose a primary control measure to keep NOx below 50 ppm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Schematic description (2 marks):** An H₂-SCR system consists of an H₂ dosing unit, a mixer, a catalytic reactor (Pt on alumina or Cu-zeolite), and a downstream NOx sensor. Engine exhaust containing NO/NO₂ enters the mixer, where a metered quantity of H₂ is injected. The mixture then passes over the catalyst bed, where NOx is reduced to N₂ and H₂O.

**Governing chemical reaction (2 marks):**

$$
2\text{NO} + 4\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt or Cu-zeolite}} \text{N}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Two advantages of H₂ over NH₃ as reducing agent (2 marks):**
1. **No secondary pollution:** H₂ reacts to form only H₂O; NH₃ slip can release unreacted ammonia into the atmosphere, contributing to PM₂.₅ and eutrophication.
2. **On-board availability:** For H₂-fuelled systems, the reductant (H₂) is the *same fuel* already stored onboard, eliminating the need for a separate urea/DEF tank, associated infrastructure, and refilling logistics.

**Efficiency (1 mark):** H₂-SCR systems achieve **80–95 % NOx conversion** at 200–400 °C with Pt catalysts, making them highly effective for stationary and large-engine H₂ applications.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compare peak temperature with NOx threshold (3 marks):**
The combustor exit temperature is **1500 K**, which is **below the thermal NOx formation threshold of 1800 K**. Therefore, under the given conditions, **thermal NOx formation is suppressed** at the exit. However, the *local peak* temperature within the flame front can still exceed 1800 K due to localized rich pockets or incomplete premixing — this is where most NOx is generated.

**Step 2 — Identify the dominant risk (1 mark):**
Flashback and pre-ignition risks arise when using H₂ in premixed mode because H₂ has a high flame speed and a wide flammability range. Lean premixing (φ = 0.4) reduces both NOx and flashback risk.

**Step 3 — Propose a primary control measure (3 marks):**
**Recommendation: Use staged premixed combustion with over-fire air (OFA):**
- Primary zone: φ = 0.5 (moderately lean) → lower peak temperature
- Secondary zone: additional air injected to complete burnout
- This divides the heat release, keeping the peak below 1700 K and ensuring total NOx < 50 ppm

Alternative measures include:
- EGR at 10–20 % to dilute the charge
- Water/steam injection for additional temperature depression
- Active control of the premixer swirl to avoid hot spots

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight):**
> 1. **Confusing fuel-NOx and thermal-NOx in H₂ flames** — Students often wrongly state that "hydrogen has no NOx" because it lacks nitrogen. While fuel-NOx is zero, thermal-NOx is the *dominant* mechanism in H₂ flames and must be acknowledged. **(−1 mark typical)**
> 2. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — When using $E_a / RT$, always state the result as a dimensionless ratio or in ppm/mg·kWh. **(−0.5 mark)**
> 3. **Skipping the Arrhenius calculation steps** — Examiners award partial credit for each algebraic step; do not jump to the answer. **(−2 marks typical)**
> 4. **Drawing combustion diagrams with reserved Mermaid keywords** — Avoid using `end` or `graph` as node IDs in diagrams submitted in reports.
> 5. **Confusing λ (relative air–fuel ratio) with φ (equivalence ratio)** — Remember: $\lambda = 1/\phi$ and lean burn corresponds to $\lambda > 1$ or $\phi < 1$. **(−1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Hydrogen Emission NOx Control Techniques and Strategies":**

- **H₂ produces zero CO₂, zero CO, zero unburnt hydrocarbons, zero fuel-NOx, and zero prompt-NOx**, but **does produce thermal NOx** via the Zeldovich mechanism when T > 1800 K.
- The **Zeldovich rate-limiting step** is $\text{O} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}$ with $E_a \approx 318$ kJ/mol.
- The **steady-state NO rate** simplifies to $\dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = 2 k_1 [\text{O}][\text{N}_2]$.
- Thermal NOx scales as $\exp(-E_a / RT)$ — a **100 K rise roughly doubles NOx**.
- The **adiabatic flame temperature** of H₂ in air is ≈ **2400 K** (stoichiometric).
- **Lean burn** lowers T<sub>ad</sub> ≈ 80 K per unit λ excess and is highly effective due to H₂'s wide flammability (4–75 %).
- **EGR (5–25 %)** acts as a thermal diluent, reducing NOx by 40–70 %.
- **Water injection** can cut NOx by 50–80 % via latent heat absorption (h_fg = 2257 kJ/kg).
- **Premixed / DLN combustion** in gas turbines reduces NOx to < 25 ppm.
- **Staged combustion** decouples heat release from burnout, reducing peak T.
- **H₂-SCR** uses H₂ itself as the reductant: $2\text{NO} + 4\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$, achieving 80–95 % conversion.
- **Lean NOx Traps (LNT)** store NOx on barium substrates and reduce it during rich purges.
- **Three-Way Catalysts (TWC)** are effective only at stoichiometric λ = 1.
- The **specific NOx emission** $L_{\text{NO}_x}$ is measured in **g/kWh or mg/kWh**; regulatory limits are tightening toward **≤ 50 mg/kWh** for H₂ engines.
- H₂ has **high flame speed** (≈ 3 m/s) and **wide flammability limits** — premixed systems face **flashback** risk, requiring active control.
- In **SOFC fuel cells** (800–1000 °C), trace NOx must be catalytically reduced.
- **Euro VI / EPA Tier 4 Final** are the dominant regulatory benchmarks for H₂-engine NOx.
- Always remember: **$\lambda = 1/\phi$**, **lean ↔ λ > 1 ↔ φ < 1**, **stoichiometric ↔ λ = φ = 1**, **rich ↔ λ < 1 ↔ φ > 1**.
- Arrhenius form: $k_1 = 1.8 \times 10^{14} \exp(-38370/T)$ cm³/(mol·s).
- Key reduction table to memorize: Lean burn (30–50 %), EGR (40–70 %), Water inj. (50–80 %), Premixed (60–85 %), Staged (50–75 %), H₂-SCR (80–95 %), TWC (90–99 %).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Transportation via Hydrogen Pipelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Transportation via Hydrogen Pipelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrogen Pipeline Transport** is defined as the continuous, large-scale conveyance of gaseous hydrogen (GH₂) through closed conduits (pipelines) operating at elevated pressures (typically **1 MPa – 10 MPa** for transmission, and up to **20–100 MPa** in some advanced designs), using either newly constructed dedicated hydrogen pipelines or retrofitted (repurposed) natural gas pipeline networks. It represents the most mature, energy-efficient, and cost-effective method for moving bulk quantities of hydrogen over distances ranging from a few kilometres (urban distribution) to several thousand kilometres (intercontinental transmission, as demonstrated by the European Hydrogen Backbone initiative targeting **28,000 km by 2030** and **68,000 km by 2040**).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, hydrogen pipeline transport is classified under the broader umbrella of **"Hydrogen Delivery Infrastructure"** alongside compressed gas trucks, liquid hydrogen tankers, and chemical carrier shipping (LOHC, NH₃, CH₃OH). The pipeline mode dominates delivery economics beyond a specific **threshold distance (≈ 500–1,500 km)** and **threshold volume (≈ 100–500 tonnes/day)**, beyond which trucking and other modes become economically prohibitive.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water Pipe" Analogy (Intuitive Hook)**
>
> Think of a hydrogen pipeline exactly like the municipal water pipeline that supplies your house — but instead of water, pressurised hydrogen gas is being pushed through the pipe. Just as water utilities use large-diameter trunk mains to push water across cities, hydrogen pipelines use large steel or composite conduits to push H₂ molecules from production sites (electrolysers, SMR units) to demand centres (refineries, fertiliser plants, fuel stations, power plants).
>
> The **three engineering challenges** are identical to water-supply engineering:
> 1. **Pumping Energy** (compressor stations spaced every 80–160 km for H₂ vs pump stations every few km for water).
> 2. **Pipe Material Compatibility** (hydrogen is far more "aggressive" than water — it embrittles steel, escapes through microscopic pores, and reacts with certain elastomers).
> 3. **Leakage Management** (H₂ molecules are 8× smaller than methane molecules and diffuse rapidly, demanding tighter sealing).

### 1.3 Classification of Hydrogen Pipelines

Hydrogen pipelines are classified along three primary axes:

| Axis | Type A | Type B |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction Status** | **Dedicated New Pipelines** (purpose-built for 100% H₂) | **Repurposed / Converted Pipelines** (originally natural gas, retrofitted) |
| **Operating Pressure** | **Low-Pressure** (< 1 MPa, distribution networks) | **High-Pressure** (1–10 MPa, transmission trunk lines; up to 20+ MPa advanced) |
| **Location** | **Onshore Pipelines** | **Offshore Pipelines** (subsea, e.g., AquaVentus, NortH2 projects) |

### 1.4 Standard Design Metrics (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Parameters (Must Remember)**
> - **Standard Transmission Pressure:** **7 MPa (≈ 70 bar)**
> - **Maximum Operating Pressure (MOP):** Typically **10 MPa** for new builds
> - **Pipeline Diameter Range:** **DN 100 to DN 1200** (4" to 48")
> - **H₂ Purity at Delivery:** **99.9% (3.0 grade)** for industrial use; **99.999% (5.0 grade)** for fuel cells
> - **Energy Density of Gaseous H₂ at 7 MPa:** ≈ **4.5 MJ/L** (vs gasoline ≈ 32 MJ/L)
> - **H₂ Volumetric Flowrate in a DN 600 pipe at 7 MPa:** ≈ **5,000–15,000 Nm³/h**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Four Pillars of Hydrogen Pipeline Design

Hydrogen pipeline engineering rests on four intertwined pillars:

#### Pillar 1 — Fluid Mechanics (Compressibility & Flow Regimes)
Unlike natural gas, hydrogen's behaviour in pipelines is governed by its **very low molecular weight (M = 2.016 g/mol)** and **high compressibility (Z ≈ 1.0–1.1 at 7 MPa)**. The Reynolds number is intrinsically very high because of hydrogen's low viscosity, meaning flow is almost always **fully turbulent**.

$$ Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} $$

where ρ is hydrogen density, v is velocity, D is pipe diameter, μ is dynamic viscosity. For H₂ at 7 MPa, **Re > 10⁶** is typical, forcing **turbulent flow** modelling.

#### Pillar 2 — Materials Science (Hydrogen Embrittlement)
Hydrogen atoms (atomic radius ≈ 25 pm) readily diffuse into the steel lattice, causing **Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC)**, **Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)**, and **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)**. Mitigation strategies include:
- Use of **API 5L X65/X70 linepipe steels** with controlled hardness (≤ 22 HRC).
- Application of **internal polymer liners** (HDPE, PA11) or **composite reinforcement** (fibreglass, carbon-fibre wrap).
- Purging with **inert nitrogen** during downtime to prevent H₂–O₂ reaction in residual gas pockets.

#### Pillar 3 — Thermodynamics & Compressor Station Sizing
H₂ must be **re-pressurised** periodically because of pressure drop. Each compressor station consumes electrical energy:

$$ W_{comp} = \frac{n \, R \, T_1}{\eta_{isen}} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] $$

For H₂, the isentropic exponent γ = 1.405, and a typical **two-stage compressor with intercooling** is used to limit discharge temperature below **135 °C** (sealing elastomer limit).

#### Pillar 4 — Economics & Throughput
The **levelised cost of hydrogen transport by pipeline** scales with distance and inversely with throughput:

$$ LCOH_{transport} = \frac{CapEx + OpEx}{\dot{m}_{H_2} \cdot t \cdot LHV_{H_2}} $$

Empirically, transport cost ≈ **$0.10–0.30 per kg H₂ per 1,000 km** for large new pipelines; lower (≈ $0.05–0.15) for repurposed lines.

### 2.2 The Three Pipeline Pathway Options

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite — Three Pathways for Pipeline Transport**
>
> 1. **Pathway 1: 100% Dedicated New H₂ Pipeline** — Pure H₂, high-pressure (7–10 MPa). Used for greenfield hydrogen hubs (e.g., NEOM in Saudi Arabia, HyNet North West UK).
>
> 2. **Pathway 2: Repurposed Natural Gas Pipeline (100% H₂)** — Existing steel pipeline inspected, validated, and certified for 100% H₂ service. Requires derating (lower MOP, often 5–7 MPa) and replacement of elastomer seals.
>
> 3. **Pathway 3: Hydrogen Blending (5–20 vol% H₂ in CH₄)** — Existing natural gas pipeline carries a mixture without modification. Requires end-use appliance validation. Most cost-effective short-term option but limited end-use flexibility.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM READY — High-Yield Formula Sheet**

| # | Formula | Application / Use | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Darcy-Weisbach:** $\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pressure drop in long pipelines | f = Darcy friction factor (Fanning × 4) |
| 2 | **Fanning Friction Factor (turbulent):** $f = 0.0791 \, Re^{-0.25}$ | Smooth-pipe approximation for H₂ | Blasius correlation, valid for 4×10³ < Re < 10⁵ |
| 3 | **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime identification | For H₂: Re typically > 10⁶ (turbulent) |
| 4 | **Isentropic Compression Work:** $W = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \cdot R \, T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | Compressor station power | γ_H₂ = 1.405, R_H₂ = 4124 J/(kg·K) |
| 5 | **Mass Flowrate:** $\dot{m} = \rho \, v \, A$ | Pipeline capacity | A = πD²/4 |
| 6 | **Energy Throughput:** $\dot{E} = \dot{m} \cdot LHV_{H_2}$ | Pipeline energy delivery | LHV_H₂ = 119.96 MJ/kg |
| 7 | **H₂ Density (Ideal Gas):** $\rho = \frac{P M_{H_2}}{Z R T}$ | Density at pipeline conditions | M_H₂ = 2.016 g/mol, Z ≈ 1.04 at 7 MPa, 25 °C |
| 8 | **Compressor Station Spacing:** $L_s = \frac{P_1^2 - P_2^2}{K \cdot \dot{m}^2}$ | Distance between recompressors | K = constant depending on friction, diameter |
| 9 | **H₂ Compressibility Factor:** $Z \approx 1 + 0.0006 \cdot P$ | Approximate correction | P in bar, valid up to ~100 bar |
| 10 | **Pipeline Capital Cost (Empirical):** $CapEx = a + b \cdot D + c \cdot L$ | Cost estimation | a, b, c empirical constants |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydrogen pipelines are not a futuristic concept — they are operational today. **Over 5,000 km of dedicated hydrogen pipelines** already exist globally, with the United States operating ≈ **2,700 km** (Gulf Coast region connecting refineries), Europe ≈ **1,600 km** (France, Germany, Netherlands, Belgium), and emerging Asian networks (Korea, China). The **European Hydrogen Backbone (EHB)** initiative by ENTSOG and Gas for Climate projects a pan-European H₂ network of **68,000 km by 2040**, of which **≈ 60% will be repurposed natural gas lines**. Engineers must therefore master both new-construction and repurposing design codes (e.g., ASME B31.12 for hydrogen piping, IGEM/TD/1 for hydrogen pipeline standards in the UK).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Problem 1 — Pressure Drop in a Hydrogen Pipeline (Worked Derivation)

**Problem Statement:**
Hydrogen flows through a horizontal, straight, DN 400 (D = 0.4064 m) steel pipeline at a mass flowrate of **ṁ = 5 kg/s**. The inlet pressure is **P₁ = 7 MPa**, and the temperature is **T = 293 K**. The pipeline length is **L = 100 km**. The H₂ viscosity at this condition is **μ = 8.76 × 10⁻⁶ Pa·s**. Compute the **exit pressure P₂** assuming isothermal flow.

**Step 1: Compute the hydrogen density at inlet conditions (Ideal Gas).**

$$ \rho_1 = \frac{P_1 \, M_{H_2}}{R_u \, T} $$

where $R_u$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $M_{H_2}$ = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol.

$$ \rho_1 = \frac{7 \times 10^6 \cdot 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \cdot 293} = \frac{14112}{2436.0} \approx 5.79 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$

**Step 2: Calculate cross-sectional area of the pipe.**

$$ A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.4064)^2}{4} \approx 0.1297 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 3: Determine average velocity.**

$$ v_1 = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_1 \, A} = \frac{5}{5.79 \cdot 0.1297} \approx \frac{5}{0.7513} \approx 6.655 \text{ m/s} $$

**Step 4: Compute the Reynolds number.**

$$ Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{5.79 \cdot 6.655 \cdot 0.4064}{8.76 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{15.66}{8.76 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.787 \times 10^6 $$

Since Re >> 4,000, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

**Step 5: Apply the Blasius friction factor for smooth pipes (turbulent).**

$$ f_{Blasius} = 0.0791 \, Re^{-0.25} = 0.0791 \cdot (1.787 \times 10^6)^{-0.25} $$

$$ (1.787 \times 10^6)^{0.25} = (1.787)^{0.25} \times 10^{1.5} \approx 1.155 \times 31.62 \approx 36.52 $$

$$ f = \frac{0.0791}{36.52} \approx 2.166 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 6: Apply the Darcy-Weisbach equation for pressure drop.**

$$ \Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} $$

$$ \Delta P = 2.166 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{100{,}000}{0.4064} \cdot \frac{5.79 \cdot (6.655)^2}{2} $$

$$ \Delta P = 2.166 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.461 \times 10^5 \cdot \frac{5.79 \cdot 44.29}{2} $$

$$ \Delta P = 2.166 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.461 \times 10^5 \cdot 128.22 $$

$$ \Delta P = 2.166 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3.155 \times 10^7 \approx 6.834 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.068 \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 7: Determine exit pressure (assuming isothermal, neglecting density variation — approximation).**

$$ P_2 \approx P_1 - \Delta P = 7 - 0.068 = 6.932 \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 8: Refine with the compressible flow correction (General isothermal flow equation for compressible gas).**

For compressible gas, the more rigorous equation is:

$$ P_1^2 - P_2^2 = \frac{16 \, f \, L \, \dot{m}^2 \, R_u \, T}{\pi^2 D^5 M_{H_2}} $$

$$ P_1^2 = (7 \times 10^6)^2 = 4.9 \times 10^{13} \text{ Pa}^2 $$

The term on the right:

$$ RHS = \frac{16 \cdot 2.166 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 100{,}000 \cdot 25 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 293}{\pi^2 \cdot (0.4064)^5 \cdot 2.016 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ (0.4064)^5 = 0.4064^2 \cdot 0.4064^2 \cdot 0.4064 = 0.1652 \cdot 0.1652 \cdot 0.4064 \approx 0.01109 $$

$$ RHS = \frac{16 \cdot 2.166 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10^5 \cdot 25 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 293}{9.8696 \cdot 0.01109 \cdot 2.016 \times 10^{-3}} $$

Numerator: $16 \cdot 2.166 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10^5 \cdot 25 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 293$

$$ = 16 \cdot 2.166 \times 10^2 \cdot 25 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 293 $$

$$ = 3.466 \times 10^3 \cdot 25 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 293 = 86{,}640 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 293 $$

$$ = 86{,}640 \cdot 2436.0 \approx 2.111 \times 10^8 $$

Denominator: $9.8696 \cdot 0.01109 \cdot 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1094 \cdot 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2.206 \times 10^{-4}$

$$ RHS = \frac{2.111 \times 10^8}{2.206 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 9.57 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}^2 $$

**Step 9: Solve for P₂.**

$$ P_2^2 = P_1^2 - RHS = 4.9 \times 10^{13} - 9.57 \times 10^{11} = 4.804 \times 10^{13} $$

$$ P_2 = \sqrt{4.804 \times 10^{13}} \approx 6.93 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 6.93 \text{ MPa} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Insight:** The compressible-flow correction produces a very similar result to the simplified incompressible approximation for moderate pressure drops (< 10%). This validates the use of either approach for preliminary design; the compressible form is mandatory for high-ΔP cases (e.g., long pipelines approaching 0.5 MPa drop).

### 3.2 Derivative Problem 2 — Compressor Station Power Requirement

**Problem Statement:**
A 1,000 km hydrogen pipeline transports 5 kg/s of H₂ at an inlet pressure of 3 MPa and temperature of 25 °C. Compression stations are spaced every 100 km such that each station raises the pressure back to 7 MPa. Each compression is polytropic with efficiency **η_p = 0.75** and γ = 1.405. Compute the **total compressor power** for the entire pipeline and the **energy penalty per kg of H₂ delivered**.

**Step 1: Compressor pressure ratio per station.**

$$ r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{7 \text{ MPa}}{3 \text{ MPa}} = 2.333 $$

**Step 2: Specific compression work (ideal gas, adiabatic).**

$$ w_s = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \cdot R_{H_2} \cdot T_1 \left[ r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right] / \eta_p $$

where $R_{H_2} = R_u / M_{H_2} = 8.314 / 0.002016 \approx 4124$ J/(kg·K), $T_1$ = 298 K, $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.405/1.405 \approx 0.2883$.

$$ r_p^{0.2883} = (2.333)^{0.2883} \approx 1.305 $$

$$ w_s = \frac{1.405}{0.405} \cdot 4124 \cdot 298 \cdot [1.305 - 1] / 0.75 $$

$$ w_s = 3.469 \cdot 4124 \cdot 298 \cdot 0.305 / 0.75 $$

$$ w_s = 3.469 \cdot 1229.4 \cdot 10^3 \cdot 0.305 / 0.75 $$

$$ w_s = 4.264 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.305 / 0.75 $$

$$ w_s = 1.301 \times 10^6 / 0.75 \approx 1.734 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg} = 1.734 \text{ MJ/kg per stage} $$

**Step 3: Number of compressor stations over 1,000 km.**

$$ N = \frac{1000 \text{ km}}{100 \text{ km/station}} = 10 \text{ stations} $$

**Step 4: Total compression power.**

$$ \dot{W}_{total} = \dot{m} \cdot w_s \cdot N = 5 \cdot 1.734 \times 10^6 \cdot 10 = 8.67 \times 10^7 \text{ W} = 86.7 \text{ MW} $$

**Step 5: Energy penalty per kg H₂ delivered.**

$$ E_{penalty} = w_s \cdot N = 1.734 \times 10^6 \cdot 10 = 1.734 \times 10^7 \text{ J/kg} = 17.34 \text{ MJ/kg} $$

**Step 6: Express as percentage of LHV.**

$$ \% \text{LHV loss} = \frac{17.34}{119.96} \times 100 \approx 14.45\% $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Verdict:** Long-distance (≥ 1,000 km) hydrogen transport by gaseous pipeline consumes **10–18% of the hydrogen's chemical energy** just for recompression. This is a major driver for the development of **Liquid H₂ (LH₂) pipelines** (cryogenic, near-isothermal with low ΔP) and **ammonia/H₂ carrier pipelines** for ultra-long distances.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Pipeline vs Other Transport Modes

| Criterion | Gaseous H₂ Pipeline | LH₂ Ship/Tanker | Compressed H₂ Truck | H₂ Carrier (NH₃, LOHC) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacity per delivery** | Unlimited (continuous) | 200–10,000 t per voyage | 200–450 kg per truck | 1,000–5,000 t per ship |
| **Range / Distance** | 0–3,000 km economic | > 5,000 km | 50–500 km | > 5,000 km |
| **Cost ($/kg per 1,000 km)** | **0.10–0.30** | 0.50–1.20 | 2.00–4.00 (short) | 0.40–0.80 |
| **Energy penalty (%)** | 10–18% | 25–35% (liquefaction) | 8–12% | 20–40% (conversion) |
| **Maturity** | High (industrial use) | Medium | High | Low–Medium |
| **Suitable for KTU Topic** | ✓ **Primary focus** | ✗ (Module 4) | ✗ (Module 4) | ✗ (Module 4) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrogen Pipeline System — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```
[Mermaid Diagram - Block-Level Architecture]

nodeH2Source    [H2 PRODUCTION SOURCE]
nodeCompressor1 [COMPRESSOR STATION 1]
nodePipeSection [PIPELINE SEGMENT]
nodeCompressorN [COMPRESSOR STATION N]
nodeCityGate    [CITY GATE STATION]
nodeStorage     [BUFFER STORAGE TANK]
nodeEndUser     [END USER LOAD]

nodeH2Source    -->  nodeCompressor1   : "Pressure 1-3 MPa"
nodeCompressor1 -->  nodePipeSection    : "Pressurised H2"
nodePipeSection -->  nodeCompressorN    : "Pressure drop over distance"
nodeCompressorN -->  nodeCityGate       : "Re-pressurised 7 MPa"
nodeCityGate    -->  nodeStorage        : "Pressure regulated"
nodeStorage     -->  nodeEndUser        : "Distributed H2"

[LEGENDS - Safety and Monitoring Subsystems]
nodeSCADA       [SCADA MONITORING]
nodeLeakDetect  [LEAK DETECTION SENSORS]
nodeSafetyValve [EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN VALVES]

nodeSCADA       -.->  nodePipeSection   : "Telemetry, pressure"
nodeLeakDetect  -.->  nodePipeSection   : "Real-time monitoring"
nodeSafetyValve -.->  nodePipeSection   : "Auto-isolation"
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows (→) represent the **primary mass flow** of hydrogen from source to user. Dashed arrows (-.->) represent **auxiliary control & safety telemetry**. Each compression station is a critical node — failure of any one station disrupts the entire pipeline.

### 4.2 Pipeline Cross-Section — Material Layer Architecture

```
[Mermaid Diagram - Cross-Section Schematic]

pipeOuterCoat   [EXTERNAL ANTI-CORROSION COATING (3LPE or FBE)]
pipeSteelWall   [STEEL PIPE WALL - API 5L X65/X70]
pipeInnerLiner  [INTERNAL LINER - HDPE / PA11 / Epoxy]
pipeHydrogenGas [FLOWING H2 GAS STREAM]
pipeCenterAxis  [PIPE CENTRELINE]

pipeOuterCoat   --> pipeSteelWall    : "Protects against soil moisture"
pipeSteelWall   --> pipeInnerLiner   : "Pressure containment"
pipeInnerLiner  --> pipeHydrogenGas  : "Barrier to H2 diffusion"
pipeHydrogenGas --> pipeCenterAxis   : "High velocity turbulent flow"
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Multi-Layer?** The **internal polymer liner** is the single most important design feature for repurpose-grade hydrogen pipelines. It prevents direct H₂–steel contact, eliminating hydrogen embrittlement of the structural wall. The **external 3-layer polyethylene (3LPE) coating** prevents external corrosion from soil moisture and stray currents (cathodic protection backup).

### 4.3 Three Transport Pathway Decision Flow

```
[Mermaid Diagram - Decision Flow for Pipeline Strategy]

decisionStart    [START - HYDROGEN TRANSPORT NEED]
checkVolume      {Distance and Volume?}
pathA_NewPipe    [PATH A: NEW DEDICATED H2 PIPE]
pathB_Repurpose  [PATH B: REPURPOSED NG PIPE]
pathC_Blend      [PATH C: H2 BLENDING (5-20%)]
pathEndA         [Use for industrial purity H2 demand]
pathEndB         [Use for moderate purity, large-scale]
pathEndC         [Use for short term, minimal investment]

decisionStart   --> checkVolume
checkVolume     --> pathA_NewPipe   : "Long distance, high purity"
checkVolume     --> pathB_Repurpose : "Moderate distance, cost-sensitive"
checkVolume     --> pathC_Blend     : "Short term, existing NG assets"
pathA_NewPipe   --> pathEndA
pathB_Repurpose --> pathEndB
pathC_Blend     --> pathEndC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define hydrogen pipeline transport and list the three major classification pathways for delivering hydrogen via pipelines.*

**Model Answer:**
Hydrogen pipeline transport is the continuous conveyance of gaseous hydrogen through closed conduits at elevated pressures (1–10 MPa), enabling bulk delivery of hydrogen over distances of 0–3,000 km. The three major classification pathways are:
1. **Dedicated New Pipelines** — purpose-built for 100% H₂.
2. **Repurposed Pipelines** — existing natural gas lines retrofitted for 100% H₂ service.
3. **Hydrogen Blending** — 5–20 vol% H₂ mixed with natural gas in unmodified existing pipelines.
*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for naming all three pathways with brief distinctions)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the standard transmission pressure for hydrogen pipelines and explain why hydrogen causes embrittlement in carbon steel.*

**Model Answer:**
The standard transmission pressure for dedicated hydrogen pipelines is **7 MPa (≈ 70 bar)**, with maximum operating pressures (MOP) up to **10 MPa** for new builds.
Hydrogen embrittlement in carbon steel occurs because atomic hydrogen (radius ≈ 25 pm) diffuses into the steel's ferrite lattice, occupies interstitial sites, and at stress-concentration points (cracks, inclusions) recombines into molecular H₂. This generates extremely high internal pressures (several GPa) that propagate micro-cracks, leading to **Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC)**, **Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)**, and **Stress-Oriented Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (SOHIC)**. The phenomenon is exacerbated by high-pressure gaseous H₂ environments, sour-service conditions, and hardness above **22 HRC**.
*(3 marks: 1 mark pressure value + 2 marks mechanism)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### Question A — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A horizontal hydrogen pipeline of diameter **D = 0.508 m** (DN 500) carries H₂ at a mass flow rate of **ṁ = 8 kg/s**. Inlet pressure is **P₁ = 7 MPa**, temperature **T = 300 K**. Take γ_H₂ = 1.405, M_H₂ = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol, μ_H₂ = 8.8 × 10⁻⁶ Pa·s, and assume smooth-pipe turbulent flow. Calculate the **Reynolds number, friction factor, and pressure drop per 100 km of pipeline length**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **materials selection challenge** for hydrogen pipelines, comparing carbon steel, austenitic stainless steel (SS 316L), and polymer-lined composite pipelines. Mention embrittlement resistance, cost, and typical use-cases.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Density at inlet:

$$ \rho_1 = \frac{7 \times 10^6 \cdot 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \cdot 300} = \frac{14112}{2494.2} \approx 5.66 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$

**Step 2:** Cross-section:

$$ A = \frac{\pi (0.508)^2}{4} \approx 0.2027 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 3:** Velocity:

$$ v = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho A} = \frac{8}{5.66 \cdot 0.2027} = \frac{8}{1.1473} \approx 6.972 \text{ m/s} $$

**Step 4:** Reynolds number:

$$ Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{5.66 \cdot 6.972 \cdot 0.508}{8.8 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{20.04}{8.8 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.277 \times 10^6 $$

**Step 5:** Friction factor (Blasius):

$$ f = 0.0791 \cdot Re^{-0.25} = 0.0791 \cdot (2.277 \times 10^6)^{-0.25} $$

$$ (2.277 \times 10^6)^{0.25} = (2.277)^{0.25} \cdot 10^{1.5} \approx 1.227 \cdot 31.62 \approx 38.79 $$

$$ f = 0.0791 / 38.79 \approx 2.04 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 6:** Pressure drop (Darcy-Weisbach):

$$ \Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = 2.04 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{100{,}000}{0.508} \cdot \frac{5.66 \cdot 48.61}{2} $$

$$ = 2.04 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.969 \times 10^5 \cdot 137.6 = 2.04 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.710 \times 10^7 \approx 5.528 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} $$

$$ \Delta P \approx 0.055 \text{ MPa over 100 km} $$

> **Pressure drop per km:** ≈ 5.5 × 10² Pa/km, indicating a need for compressor stations every **~150 km** to maintain pressure within design limits.

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Material | Embrittlement Resistance | Cost (Relative) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbon Steel (API 5L X65/X70)** | Low–Moderate (controlled hardness ≤ 22 HRC, inhibitor injection needed) | Low (1× baseline) | New transmission pipelines, repurposed NG lines |
| **Austenitic SS 316L** | High (FCC lattice resists H₂ diffusion; Ni-rich) | High (3–5×) | Refinery hydrogen headers, fuel-cell delivery |
| **Polymer-lined Composite** | Very High (H₂ never contacts steel; HDPE/PA11 barrier) | Medium–High (2–3×) | Repurposed NG lines, urban distribution, subsea |

Discussion points:
- Carbon steel is **economical but susceptible** to HIC; needs hardness control, internal coating, regular inspection (ILI — in-line inspection pigs).
- Stainless steel 316L is the **default for high-purity fuel-cell applications** because it does not introduce contaminants and resists HE.
- Composite-lined systems (e.g., **Flexpipe**, **Strohm TCP**) combine **polymer inner liner + fibre-reinforced outer layer**, eliminating the steel–H₂ interface entirely. They are gaining traction for offshore and repurposing applications.

*(7 marks: 3 marks comparison table + 4 marks discussion of mechanisms and use-cases)*

---

#### Question B — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 500 km hydrogen pipeline operates between two compression stations. The inlet pressure is **P₁ = 7 MPa** and the exit pressure at the next station is **P₂ = 3 MPa**. Using the isothermal compressible flow equation, derive the **expression for the mass flowrate ṁ** and compute its value for the following data: D = 0.4064 m, L = 500 km, f = 0.0025, T = 290 K, M_H₂ = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol, R_u = 8.314 J/mol·K.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **cost economics of hydrogen pipeline transport**, including the break-even distance relative to truck transport, the role of repurposing vs new-build, and the impact of compressor station energy on delivered hydrogen cost.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Derivation of mass flowrate:**

Starting from the isothermal compressible flow equation:

$$ P_1^2 - P_2^2 = \frac{16 f L \dot{m}^2 R_u T}{\pi^2 D^5 M_{H_2}} $$

Solving for ṁ:

$$ \dot{m} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \sqrt{\frac{(P_1^2 - P_2^2) \, \pi \, D \, M_{H_2}}{16 \, f \, L \, R_u \, T}} $$

Equivalently:

$$ \dot{m} = \sqrt{\frac{(P_1^2 - P_2^2) \, \pi^2 \, D^5 \, M_{H_2}}{16 \, f \, L \, R_u \, T}} $$

**Numerical Evaluation:**

$$ P_1^2 - P_2^2 = (7 \times 10^6)^2 - (3 \times 10^6)^2 = 49 \times 10^{12} - 9 \times 10^{12} = 40 \times 10^{12} \text{ Pa}^2 $$

$$ D^5 = (0.4064)^5 \approx 0.01109 \text{ m}^5 $$

Numerator:

$$ N = 40 \times 10^{12} \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 0.01109 \cdot 2.016 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ = 40 \times 10^{12} \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 0.01109 \cdot 2.016 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ = 40 \times 10^{12} \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 2.236 \times 10^{-5} $$

$$ = 40 \times 10^{12} \cdot 2.207 \times 10^{-4} = 8.828 \times 10^9 $$

Denominator:

$$ D_{en} = 16 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot 500{,}000 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 290 = 16 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot 5 \times 10^5 \cdot 2411.06 $$

$$ = 16 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot 1.2055 \times 10^9 = 4.822 \times 10^7 $$

$$ \dot{m} = \sqrt{\frac{8.828 \times 10^9}{4.822 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{183.1} \approx 13.53 \text{ kg/s} $$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Cost economics of hydrogen pipeline transport are governed by the following:

1. **Break-even distance** — Truck transport (compressed H₂ tube trailers at 20–50 MPa) costs approximately **$2.00–4.00 per kg per 1,000 km**, dominated by fuel and driver costs. Pipeline transport costs **$0.10–0.30 per kg per 1,000 km** for large-scale operations. The break-even distance is typically **500–1,500 km**, beyond which pipelines dominate economically. For shorter distances and smaller volumes, trucking remains competitive.

2. **Repurposing vs new-build** — Repurposing an existing natural gas pipeline costs **10–30% of new-build cost** (≈ $0.4–1.2 million/km vs $3–8 million/km for new H₂ pipeline). The cost saving is dramatic, but is conditional on (i) pipeline integrity assessment (ILI, hydrostatic testing), (ii) elastomer seal replacement, (iii) compressor station retrofitting, and (iv) derated operating pressure (typically 5–7 MPa instead of 10 MPa).

3. **Compressor station energy penalty** — Recompression consumes **10–18% of LHV** for long-distance pipelines (see Section 3.2 calculation). At an electricity cost of **$0.05/kWh** and H₂ LHV = 119.96 MJ/kg, this translates to **$0.15–0.30 per kg of H₂** as an additional delivered cost. This energy penalty is the single largest operating expense for long-distance H₂ pipelines.

4. **Throughput economics** — Pipeline economics are strongly volume-sensitive. The cost per kg decreases as a power-law with throughput: doubling throughput reduces per-kg cost by approximately 30–40% due to fixed CapEx amortisation over larger volumes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert units** (Pa vs MPa, kg/s vs kg/h) in pressure-drop problems, losing 2–3 marks.
> - For compressible flow, students sometimes incorrectly apply the **incompressible Darcy-Weisbach formula** without the pressure-squared correction, leading to wrong exit pressures for long pipelines. Always use $P_1^2 - P_2^2$ form when ΔP > 10% of inlet.
> - In cost-economics questions, students often **omit the energy penalty of compression** — this is a 3-mark deduction that examiners regularly apply.
> - For embrittlement questions, students fail to mention the **hardness limit (≤ 22 HRC)** which is a critical specification per ASME B31.12.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Hydrogen Pipeline Transport**

- [x] Hydrogen pipelines operate at **standard transmission pressure of 7 MPa**, MOP up to **10 MPa**.
- [x] Three pathways: **Dedicated New, Repurposed, Blending**.
- [x] Reynolds number for H₂ flow is almost always **> 10⁶ (fully turbulent)**.
- [x] Use **Blasius correlation** for smooth-pipe turbulent friction factor: $f = 0.0791 Re^{-0.25}$.
- [x] For compressible flow, use the **$P_1^2 - P_2^2$ form** of Darcy-Weisbach equation.
- [x] Compressor station spacing is typically **80–160 km** for long-distance H₂ pipelines.
- [x] Recompression energy penalty is **10–18% of LHV** for ≥ 1,000 km pipelines.
- [x] **Hydrogen embrittlement** mitigation: hardness ≤ 22 HRC, internal polymer liner, regular ILI inspection.
- [x] **Stainless steel 316L** is preferred for high-purity fuel-cell applications.
- [x] **Polymer-lined composite pipes** are the emerging choice for repurposed NG lines.
- [x] **Break-even distance** vs trucking: ~500–1,500 km.
- [x] **Repurposing cost** ≈ 10–30% of new-build; **new-build cost** ≈ $3–8 million/km.
- [x] Hydrogen molecules are **8× smaller than methane** → higher leakage rate, need tighter seals.
- [x] Use **ASME B31.12** and **IGEM/TD/1** as the primary design codes.
- [x] Hydrogen blending (5–20 vol% in CH₄) is the **short-term lowest-cost pathway**.
- [x] Energy throughput: $\dot{E} = \dot{m} \cdot LHV_{H_2}$, with $LHV_{H_2} = 119.96$ MJ/kg.
- [x] Existing global H₂ pipeline network: **> 5,000 km**, dominated by US Gulf Coast and European industrial clusters.
- [x] European Hydrogen Backbone target: **68,000 km by 2040** (≈ 60% repurposed).
- [x] **Safety systems**: SCADA telemetry, optical/electrochemical leak detection, ESD (Emergency Shutdown) valves, flame arrestors at compressor stations.
- [x] **Hydrogen embrittlement mechanisms**: HIC, HE, SOHIC, SSC (sulfide stress cracking) — all governed by hydrogen fugacity, steel hardness, and stress state.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long Distance Hydrogen Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Long Distance Hydrogen Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Long Distance Hydrogen Transmission** refers to the bulk transportation of hydrogen (H₂) in gaseous, liquid, or chemically bound forms across distances typically ranging from **several hundred to several thousand kilometres**, connecting centralized production facilities (e.g., electrolyzer hubs, steam methane reforming (SMR) plants, or large-scale renewable hydrogen sites) to distributed end-users such as refineries, ammonia/methanol plants, fuel-cell vehicle stations, power-generation utilities, and industrial clusters.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, this encompasses four primary transmission modalities:
1. **Dedicated gaseous hydrogen (GH₂) pipelines** (pure H₂)
2. **Repurposed natural gas pipelines** (with or without blending)
3. **Liquid hydrogen (LH₂) transport via cryogenic tankers/ships**
4. **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHCs)** — e.g., toluene/methylcyclohexane, dibenzyltoluene

The governing technical considerations include pressure drop, compressor/pump work, material compatibility (hydrogen embrittlement), energy efficiency, and capital expenditure (CAPEX) per unit energy delivered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For Module 3, students are expected to understand *why* hydrogen transmission technology choice depends on **distance, volume, end-use form, and existing infrastructure** — not merely list the methods. Examiners consistently award marks to answers that quantitatively compare modalities.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are moving water from a mountain reservoir to a city **1000 km away**. You have three choices:

- **Option A – A big pipeline (like a water aqueduct):** Continuous flow, low operating cost once built, but you need pumps (compressors) at intervals to push the water uphill (overcome friction). This is analogous to a **gaseous H₂ pipeline** — efficient for steady, large-volume flow.
- **Option B – A fleet of cryogenic tanker trucks (like milk tankers keeping milk cold):** You liquefy the water (hydrogen) at –253 °C, load it into insulated trucks/ships, and deliver it. Works flexibly, but cooling costs energy. This is **liquid H₂ transmission**.
- **Option C – A chemical "Trojan horse" (like a sponge soaked in water):** You bind hydrogen to a liquid carrier (e.g., toluene becomes methylcyclohexane), transport it like ordinary diesel in trucks/ships at ambient conditions, and release H₂ at the destination. This is the **LOHC method** — like mailing a sponge that you can squeeze dry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Scale:** Today, there are approximately **5,000+ km of dedicated hydrogen pipelines** worldwide, mostly in the USA, Germany, and Belgium. The longest dedicated H₂ pipeline in Europe (~240 km) runs in the Ruhr Valley, Germany. The planned **European Hydrogen Backbone (EHB)** targets **40,000+ km by 2030** (mostly repurposed natural gas pipelines).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants for Long-Distance H₂ Transmission

| Property | Value | Significance |
|----------|-------|--------------|
| Density of H₂ (STP) | **0.0899 kg/m³** | Low density → large volumes needed |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **120 MJ/kg** | Energy per kilogram |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **142 MJ/kg** | With latent heat of water recovery |
| Volumetric energy density (STP) | **10.79 MJ/m³** | Roughly 1/3 of methane's energy/m³ |
| Critical temperature | **–240 °C (33 K)** | Must be cooled below this for liquefaction |
| Critical pressure | **13.1 bar** | Defines pipeline supercritical region |
| Boiling point (1 atm) | **–252.87 °C (20.3 K)** | For LH₂ transport |
| Diffusion coefficient in air | **0.61 cm²/s** | High → leak detection is critical |
| Minimum ignition energy | **0.017 mJ** | 10× lower than methane — highly flammable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because H₂ has **3× lower volumetric energy density** than natural gas, transmitting the same energy requires approximately **3× larger volumetric flow rates** (or higher pressures), making pipeline design fundamentally more demanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Long-Distance Transmission is Harder for H₂ than for Natural Gas

Hydrogen behaves very differently from methane in transmission systems. The "Why" behind each engineering challenge is critical for KTU answers:

- **Smaller molecular size (kinetic diameter 2.89 Å vs. CH₄ at 3.8 Å):** Causes higher leakage rates through seals, valves, and even steel micro-pores.
- **Hydrogen Embrittlement:** Atomic H diffuses into the steel lattice, reducing ductility and fracture toughness. This is a *materials science* problem affecting pipeline integrity.
- **Lower energy density per molecule:** Means compressors must handle more moles per kWh delivered.
- **Higher compressibility (Z-factor deviations):** Real-gas effects become more pronounced at transmission pressures (50–100 bar).
- **Joule-Thomson inversion temperature:** During throttling, H₂ *heats up* at room temperature (opposite of methane) → safety risk in rapid depressurization.

### 2.2 Pipeline Transmission: Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Compression at Production Site:** H₂ is compressed from atmospheric pressure (or electrolyzer outlet ~30–80 bar) to transmission pressure (typically **50–100 bar** for trunk lines).
2. **Pipeline Transport:** H₂ flows through high-strength steel or composite pipes (diameters 16″–48″ for trunk lines).
3. **Pressure Drop Along Length:** Due to frictional losses, pressure decreases with distance.
4. **Booster Compressor Stations:** Placed every **80–160 km** to re-elevate pressure back to design value.
5. **End-User Pressure Regulation:** Pressure reduced to user requirement (e.g., 30 bar for refinery, 4 bar for fueling station).
6. **Metering and Quality Control:** Impurities (O₂, H₂O, CO, S) monitored continuously; allowable purity for PEM fuel cells is **99.999% (Grade 5.0)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** The **Hynet North West Project (UK)** and the **H₂ Backbone in the Netherlands** (Nouryon → Shell Pernis) are live examples. Repurposing existing natural gas pipelines saves ~80% of CAPEX vs. new build, but requires liner/coating upgrades to prevent embrittlement and leak risks.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Memorize the *Panhandle B equation* and the *Compressor isentropic work equation* — these are the two most-tested formulas in Module 3 numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = 0.0000239 \cdot D^{2.53} \cdot \left( \dfrac{P_1^2 - P_2^2}{L \cdot T \cdot Z} \right)^{0.539}$ | **Panhandle B equation** for gas flow in long pipelines; $Q$ = flow rate, $D$ = diameter, $P_1, P_2$ = inlet/outlet pressure, $L$ = length, $T$ = avg. temp, $Z$ = compressibility | m³/day, inch, psia, mile, °R, – |
| 2 | $Q = 0.0000675 \cdot D^{8/3} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_1^2 - P_2^2}{L \cdot T \cdot Z}}$ | **Weymouth equation** — for shorter, higher-pressure transmission | same as above |
| 3 | $\dot{W}_{comp} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot \dot{n} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right] \cdot \dfrac{1}{\eta_{isentropic}}$ | **Compressor power** (polytropic, where $n$ ≈ 1.4 for H₂ adiabatic); $\dot{n}$ = molar flow rate | W |
| 4 | $\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot \dfrac{Z \cdot R \cdot T}{\eta_c \cdot M} \cdot \dfrac{k}{k-1} \cdot \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1 \right]$ | **Compressor power per unit mass** — KTU-favorite form | W/kg |
| 5 | $E_{transmission} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{comp} \cdot L_{segment}}{\dot{m} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}$ | **Transmission energy intensity** (% of LHV lost to compression) | dimensionless % |
| 6 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2}$ | **Darcy-Weisbach** — friction pressure drop, $f$ = friction factor (Fanning or Moody) | Pa |
| 7 | $R_{cost} = \dfrac{CAPEX + OPEX \cdot t}{E_{delivered} \cdot t}$ | **Levelized cost of transmission** (\$/kg H₂) | \$/kg |
| 8 | $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol | Molar mass of hydrogen | kg/mol |
| 9 | $R_{specific} = \dfrac{R_{universal}}{M_{H_2}} = \dfrac{8.314}{0.002016} = 4124$ J/(kg·K) | Specific gas constant for H₂ | J/(kg·K) |
| 10 | $\rho_{LH_2}(20.3\,K) = 70.79$ kg/m³ | Density of liquid hydrogen | kg/m³ |
| 11 | $E_{LH_2} = \rho_{LH_2} \cdot LHV = 70.79 \times 120 \times 10^6 \approx 8.5$ GJ/m³ | Volumetric energy density of LH₂ | J/m³ |
| 12 | $P_{boil-off} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{loss}}{h_{fg}} \approx 0.1\%/\text{day}$ for well-insulated LH₂ tanks | Boil-off rate (BOR) — key for LH₂ transport economics | %/day |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When using the Panhandle or Weymouth equation, ensure pressures are in **absolute units (psia or bara)** — students consistently lose 1–2 marks by using gauge pressure.

### 2.4 Choice of Transmission Modality (Decision Logic)

| Modality | Best Suited When | Distance Range | Energy Penalty | CAPEX Intensity |
|----------|------------------|----------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **GH₂ Pipeline (new)** | High-volume, dedicated corridor | 100 – 2,000 km | ~10–15% of LHV | Very High |
| **Repurposed NG Pipeline** | Existing infrastructure, blending permitted | 100 – 1,500 km | ~5–10% of LHV | Medium |
| **LH₂ Tanker (truck)** | Moderate volumes, flexible routing | 100 – 1,000 km | ~30–40% of LHV (liquefaction) | Medium |
| **LH₂ Ship** | Very large international volumes | 1,000 – 10,000 km | ~25–35% of LHV | High |
| **LOHC (truck/ship)** | Ambient-condition long haul | 500 – 10,000 km | ~35–45% of LHV (hydrogenation/dehydrogenation) | Medium-High |
| **H₂ Blending in NG** | Legacy networks, transitional phase | Whole network | Negligible in transport | Very Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Compressor Power Required for H₂ Pipeline

**Statement:** A hydrogen transmission pipeline carries $\dot{m} = 5{,}000$ kg/h of H₂ over a distance $L = 1{,}000$ km with booster compressor stations every $L_s = 100$ km. The gas enters each compressor stage at $P_1 = 40$ bar and is compressed to $P_2 = 80$ bar. Inlet temperature $T_1 = 298$ K. Isentropic efficiency $\eta_c = 0.75$. Specific heat ratio $k = 1.40$. Compressibility factor $Z = 1.05$. Calculate the total compressor power input for the entire transmission corridor.

**Step 1 — Number of Compressor Stations**

$$N_{stations} = \dfrac{L}{L_s} = \dfrac{1000}{100} = 10 \text{ stations}$$

> *Logic:* A compressor station is needed at the entry plus at every 100 km interval. For a 1,000 km line with stations every 100 km, we get 10 intermediate stations. We assume entry compression (from 30 bar production to 40 bar) is included as station 1, or is provided by the production site.

**Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate (SI units)**

$$\dot{m} = 5{,}000 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} = \dfrac{5000}{3600} = 1.389 \, \text{kg/s}$$

> *Logic:* Convert from kg/hr to kg/s by dividing by 3600. This is required for SI power in Watts.

**Step 3 — Specific Gas Constant for H₂**

$$R_{sp} = \dfrac{R_{universal}}{M_{H_2}} = \dfrac{8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}}{0.002016 \, \text{kg/mol}} = 4124.5 \, \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{kg·K}}$$

> *Logic:* $R_{sp}$ is hydrogen-specific. It is *much higher* than for methane (518 J/(kg·K)) — this is *why* compression of H₂ is energy-intensive per kg, even though its density is low.

**Step 4 — Single-Stage Compressor Power (per kg/s)**

$$\dot{W}_{single} = \dot{m} \cdot \dfrac{Z \cdot R_{sp} \cdot T_1}{\eta_c} \cdot \dfrac{k}{k-1} \cdot \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1 \right]$$

Substitute values:

$$\dfrac{k}{k-1} = \dfrac{1.40}{0.40} = 3.50$$

$$\left(\dfrac{k-1}{k}\right) = \dfrac{0.40}{1.40} = 0.2857$$

$$\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.2857} = (80/40)^{0.2857} = 2^{0.2857} = 1.221$$

$$\dot{W}_{single} = 1.389 \times \dfrac{1.05 \times 4124.5 \times 298}{0.75} \times 3.50 \times (1.221 - 1)$$

**Compute intermediate term:**

$$1.05 \times 4124.5 \times 298 = 1{,}290{,}776 \, \text{J/kg}$$

$$\dfrac{1{,}290{,}776}{0.75} = 1{,}721{,}034 \, \text{J/kg}$$

$$3.50 \times 0.221 = 0.7735$$

$$\dot{W}_{single} = 1.389 \times 1{,}721{,}034 \times 0.7735$$

$$\dot{W}_{single} = 1.389 \times 1{,}331{,}150 = 1{,}849{,}000 \, \text{W} \approx 1.85 \, \text{MW}$$

> *Logic:* ~1.85 MW of shaft power is needed at *each* of the 10 compressor stations. This is the power that must be supplied by electric motors (driven preferably by renewable electricity for green H₂).

**Step 5 — Total Compressor Power for 10 Stations**

$$\dot{W}_{total} = 10 \times 1.85 \, \text{MW} = 18.5 \, \text{MW}$$

**Step 6 — Energy Penalty as % of LHV Delivered**

$$\text{Energy Penalty (\%)} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{total}}{\dot{m} \cdot LHV_{H_2}} \times 100\%$$

$$\dot{m} \cdot LHV = 1.389 \times 120 \times 10^6 = 1.667 \times 10^8 \, \text{W} = 166.7 \, \text{MW}$$

$$\eta_{transmission} = \dfrac{18.5}{166.7} \times 100\% \approx 11.1\%$$

> *Logic:* Approximately **11% of the hydrogen's energy content is consumed** just to push it through 1,000 km of pipeline. This is a typical figure for long-distance H₂ transmission — comparable to ~5–8% for natural gas over the same distance. The difference is fundamental and unavoidable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Pressure Drop in a 100-km Pipeline Segment (Panhandle B)

**Statement:** A 24-inch (0.6096 m) diameter hydrogen pipeline carries gas at an average temperature of $T = 288$ K with compressibility $Z = 1.045$. Inlet pressure $P_1 = 80$ bar, outlet $P_2 = 65$ bar. Calculate the volumetric flow rate $Q$ at STP and mass flow rate.

**Step 1 — Convert Units to Imperial (Panhandle B standard)**

$D = 24$ inches  
$L = 100$ km $= 62.14$ miles  
$P_1 = 80$ bar $\approx 1160$ psia  
$P_2 = 65$ bar $\approx 942.7$ psia  
$T = 288$ K $= 520.47 \, °R$  
$Z = 1.045$

**Step 2 — Apply Panhandle B Equation**

$$Q = 0.0000239 \cdot D^{2.53} \cdot \left( \dfrac{P_1^2 - P_2^2}{L \cdot T \cdot Z} \right)^{0.539}$$

Compute $D^{2.53}$:

$$24^{2.53} = 24^2 \times 24^{0.53} = 576 \times 5.378 = 3{,}098$$

Compute $(P_1^2 - P_2^2)$:

$$1160^2 - 942.7^2 = 1{,}345{,}600 - 888{,}683 = 456{,}917 \, \text{psia}^2$$

Compute denominator $L \cdot T \cdot Z$:

$$62.14 \times 520.47 \times 1.045 = 33{,}797$$

Compute inner ratio:

$$\dfrac{456{,}917}{33{,}797} = 13.52$$

Raise to 0.539:

$$13.52^{0.539} = e^{0.539 \cdot \ln(13.52)} = e^{0.539 \times 2.604} = e^{1.404} = 4.07$$

**Step 3 — Final $Q$**

$$Q = 0.0000239 \times 3098 \times 4.07 = 0.301 \, \text{MMscfd}$$

> *(MMscfd = Million Standard Cubic Feet per Day)*

Convert to SI: $Q = 0.301 \times 10^6 \times 0.02832 = 8{,}524 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day (STP)}$

**Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate**

$$\dot{m} = Q_{STP} \times \rho_{H_2, STP} = 8524 \times 0.0899 = 766.3 \, \text{kg/day} \approx 0.00887 \, \text{kg/s}$$

> *Logic:* Even with a 24-inch pipe, mass flow is limited by pressure drop. This is why **larger diameters (36–48 inch)** are considered for hydrogen trunk lines — to reduce velocity and friction losses.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (LH₂ vs. Pipeline vs. LOHC for 1,000 km)

| Parameter | GH₂ Pipeline | LH₂ Tanker (truck) | LH₂ Ship | LOHC (toluene/MCH) |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Energy in transport form | **120 MJ/kg** | **8,494 MJ/m³** | **8,494 MJ/m³** | **~5.5 MJ/kg carrier** |
| Energy penalty | ~10–15% | ~30–40% | ~25–35% | ~35–45% |
| Boil-off / loss | Negligible | 0.05–0.1%/day | 0.03%/day | None (ambient storage) |
| CAPEX ($/kg capacity) | 800–1,500 | 200–400 | 500–1,000 | 300–600 |
| Scalability | Limited to corridor | Flexible routing | Very high volume | High |
| Delivery form | Gaseous H₂ | Cryogenic liquid | Cryogenic liquid | Needs dehydrogenation unit |
| Maturity | High (industrial scale) | Commercial | Pilot (HySTRA, Suiso Frontier) | Pilot–Demo (Chiyoda SPERA H₂) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Answers:** There is **no single "best" technology** — pipeline is best for high-volume steady-state industrial clusters (refineries, steel plants), LH₂ ships are best for very large intercontinental flows, and LOHC is best when end-users have no cryogenic handling infrastructure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Long-Distance Hydrogen Transmission Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hydrogen Production Site] --> B[Purification Unit]
    B --> C[Compressor Station 1]
    C --> D[Pipeline Segment 1]
    D --> E[Compressor Station 2]
    E --> F[Pipeline Segment 2]
    F --> G[Compressor Station N]
    G --> H[Pressure Regulator]
    H --> I[End User Industrial Cluster]
    H --> J[Fuel Cell Vehicle Station]
    H --> K[Power Generation Plant]

    classDef prod fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef trans fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef end fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class A,B prod
    class C,D,E,F,G,H trans
    class I,J,K end
```

**Description:** Hydrogen is produced (via electrolysis, SMR with CCS, or biomass gasification), purified to remove O₂, H₂O, CO, CO₂, and H₂S, then compressed to transmission pressure. The gas flows through segmented pipelines, with booster compressor stations placed every 80–160 km to overcome frictional pressure drop. At the destination, pressure is regulated to match end-user requirements, and the H₂ is delivered to industrial users, refueling stations, or power plants.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Decision Flow for Selecting Transmission Modality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Long Distance H2 Transmission Need] --> S1{Distance}
    S1 -->|less than 500 km| S2{Volume Required}
    S1 -->|500 to 2000 km| S3{Existing Pipeline Available}
    S1 -->|greater than 2000 km| S4[Use LH2 Ship or LOHC]

    S2 -->|High| P1[Gaseous H2 Pipeline]
    S2 -->|Low to Medium| P2[Compressed H2 Tube Trailers or LH2 Trucks]

    S3 -->|Yes| P3[Repurposed Natural Gas Pipeline]
    S3 -->|No| P4[New GH2 Pipeline or LH2 Tanker]

    P1 --> Z[Optimize Compressor Spacing and Diameter]
    P2 --> Z
    P3 --> Z
    P4 --> Z

    classDef start fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef endnode fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class S0 start
    class S1,S2,S3 decision
    class P1,P2,P3,P4 endnode
    class Z endnode
```

**Description:** The selection algorithm for H₂ transmission technology is governed by **distance, volume, and existing infrastructure**. Shorter distances and higher volumes favor pipelines; very long distances and intercontinental flows favor LH₂ shipping or LOHC. Repurposing natural gas pipelines is a transitional strategy that minimizes CAPEX but is constrained by materials compatibility (embrittlement) and end-user gas-quality requirements.

### 4.3 Process Flow: LOHC Hydrogen Transmission Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[H2 Production at Source] --> B[Hydrogenation Reactor at 200 to 300 C]
    B --> C[MCH or Perhydro Dibenzyltoluene Loaded]
    C --> D[Transport by Truck Tanker or Ship]
    D --> E[Ambient Storage at Destination]
    E --> F[Dehydrogenation Reactor at 300 to 350 C]
    F --> G[Purified H2 to End User]
    F --> H[Spent LOHC Recycled back to Source]

    classDef proc fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef trans fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef use fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    class A,B,F,G use
    class C,D,E trans
    class H proc
```

**Description:** LOHC technology uses reversible hydrogenation–dehydrogenation chemistry. For example, toluene (C₇H₈) is hydrogenated to methylcyclohexane (MCH, C₇H₁₄) at the production site, transporting H₂ at ambient conditions. At the destination, MCH is dehydrogenated back to toluene (recycled) and H₂. The Chiyoda SPERA H₂ system in Japan has demonstrated this cycle at the 50,000 ton/year scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
*Compare dedicated gaseous hydrogen pipelines with liquid hydrogen (LH₂) transport for long-distance transmission. Highlight two advantages of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | GH₂ Pipeline | LH₂ Tanker/Ship |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|
| **Advantage 1** | Continuous flow, low marginal cost per kg over pipeline lifetime (1.5 marks) | Cryogenic storage gives very high energy density (~8.5 GJ/m³ vs. ~10 MJ/m³ for GH₂ at 80 bar) (1.5 marks) |
| **Advantage 2** | No boil-off losses; suitable for steady industrial demand (0.5 marks bonus for clarity) | Flexible routing — no fixed corridor required; works for intercontinental transport (0.5 marks bonus) |

*Examiner Note: Full 3 marks require comparison with explicit numbers, not just a list.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*State the Panhandle B equation for gas flow in a pipeline. List the units of each variable as used in this equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$Q = 0.0000239 \cdot D^{2.53} \cdot \left( \dfrac{P_1^2 - P_2^2}{L \cdot T \cdot Z} \right)^{0.539}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| $Q$ | Volumetric flow rate at STP | MMscfd (million std. cubic ft/day) |
| $D$ | Pipe internal diameter | inches |
| $P_1, P_2$ | Inlet and outlet pressures | psia (absolute) |
| $L$ | Pipeline length | miles |
| $T$ | Average gas temperature | °R (Rankine) |
| $Z$ | Compressibility factor | dimensionless |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for equation, 2 marks for units with definitions.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, End-Sem]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the isentropic work required to compress hydrogen from inlet pressure $P_1$ to outlet pressure $P_2$ in a multi-stage pipeline compressor. Define all terms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrogen pipeline 1,200 km long is designed to transport 10,000 kg/h of H₂ at a transmission pressure of 80 bar. Booster compressor stations are placed every 100 km. The inlet pressure to each compressor is 50 bar, the inlet temperature is 298 K, $k = 1.40$, $Z = 1.05$, $\eta_c = 0.75$. Calculate:
- (i) The number of compressor stations required.
- (ii) The compressor power at each station.
- (iii) The total transmission energy penalty as a percentage of the LHV delivered. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the steady-flow energy equation for an adiabatic compressor (assuming no kinetic energy change):

$$\dot{W}_{in} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$$

For an isentropic (reversible adiabatic) process in an ideal gas:

$$h_2 - h_1 = c_p \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1\right]$$

But for a real gas, we introduce the compressibility factor $Z$ and the isentropic efficiency $\eta_c$:

$$\dot{W}_{real} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot Z \cdot R_{sp} \cdot T_1 \cdot k}{\eta_c (k-1)} \cdot \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1\right]$$

(7 marks: 2 marks for steady-flow energy equation, 2 marks for ideal gas isentropic relation, 2 marks for real-gas correction, 1 mark for $\eta_c$ definition.)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of Compressor Stations (2 marks):**

$$N = \dfrac{1200}{100} = 12 \text{ compressor stations}$$

**(ii) Compressor Power at Each Station (3 marks):**

$\dot{m} = 10{,}000 / 3600 = 2.778$ kg/s

$$k/(k-1) = 1.40/0.40 = 3.50, \quad (k-1)/k = 0.2857$$

$$(P_2/P_1)^{0.2857} = (80/50)^{0.2857} = 1.6^{0.2857} = 1.1555$$

$$\dot{W}_{single} = 2.778 \times \dfrac{1.05 \times 4124.5 \times 298}{0.75} \times 3.50 \times (1.1555 - 1)$$

$$= 2.778 \times 1{,}721{,}034 \times 3.50 \times 0.1555$$

$$= 2.778 \times 1{,}721{,}034 \times 0.5443 = 2{,}602{,}500 \, \text{W} \approx 2.60 \, \text{MW}$$

**(iii) Total Energy Penalty (2 marks):**

$$\dot{W}_{total} = 12 \times 2.60 = 31.2 \, \text{MW}$$

$$\dot{m} \cdot LHV = 2.778 \times 120 \times 10^6 = 333.3 \, \text{MW}$$

$$\text{Energy penalty} = \dfrac{31.2}{333.3} \times 100\% = 9.36\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Common student errors:
> 1. Forgetting to convert kg/h to kg/s → loses 1 mark.
> 2. Using $R_{universal}$ instead of $R_{sp}$ (specific gas constant) → loses 1 mark.
> 3. Using $k=1.30$ (some handbooks cite this) but the KTU answer key uses $k=1.40$ for H₂ → 1 mark deducted.
> 4. Reporting energy penalty in absolute MW instead of % → loses 1 mark in interpretation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End-Sem Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carrier (LOHC) method for long-distance hydrogen transmission. List two examples of LOHC systems and justify why LOHC is preferred for intercontinental transport. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare LOHC and LH₂ ship transport for moving 100,000 tonnes/year of H₂ over 5,000 km. Discuss energy efficiency, CAPEX, infrastructure compatibility, and end-use delivery form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) LOHC Method Explanation (7 marks):**

LOHC technology uses reversible catalytic hydrogenation–dehydrogenation cycles. At the production site, H₂ reacts with an unsaturated liquid organic compound (the "lean" LOHC) at 200–300 °C and 30–50 bar in the presence of a catalyst (e.g., Pt/Al₂O₃ or Pd/C) to form a saturated "rich" LOHC. The rich LOHC has a higher hydrogen content and is stable at ambient temperature and pressure, enabling storage and transport in standard liquid-fuel infrastructure (tankers, ships, pipelines).

**Examples (2 marks):**
1. **Toluene ↔ Methylcyclohexane (MCH):** Toluene (C₇H₈) + 3H₂ ⇌ Methylcyclohexane (C₇H₁₄). Carries 6.2 wt% H₂. Used by Chiyoda Corporation in Japan.
2. **Dibenzyltoluene (H0-DBT) ↔ Perhydro-dibenzyltoluene (H18-DBT):** Carries 6.0 wt% H₂. Commercialized by Hydrogenious Technologies (Germany).

**Justification for intercontinental use (3 marks):**
- Ambient storage and transport (no boil-off, no cryogenic tanks).
- Standard liquid-fuel infrastructure reuse → lower CAPEX.
- Long storage time without losses → flexible delivery.
- Safety: no H₂ leak risk during transit.
- Energy penalty: ~35–45% (mainly dehydrogenation at destination).

**(b) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | LOHC | LH₂ Ship |
|-----------|------|----------|
| **Energy Efficiency** | ~55–65% of LHV delivered (3 marks for trade-off discussion) | ~65–75% of LHV delivered (3 marks for trade-off discussion) |
| **CAPEX** | \$1,200–1,800 per kg H₂/year capacity | \$2,000–3,000 per kg H₂/year capacity (capex higher due to cryogenic ship + boil-off management) |
| **Infrastructure** | Reuses diesel/petrol tankers, ports, storage tanks | Requires cryogenic terminals (Linde, Air Products design) and LH₂-class ships |
| **Delivery form** | H₂ gas after dehydrogenation at port | LH₂ — can be regasified or used as LH₂ |
| **Best fit** | Mid-volume, regions without cryogenic handling | Very large volumes, established LH₂ trade (Japan–Australia) |

*Examiner Note: Award full marks only if the student presents a *trade-off* analysis, not just a list.*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 3 — Long Distance H₂ Transmission):**

- ✅ **Four primary modalities:** GH₂ pipeline, repurposed NG pipeline, LH₂ (truck/ship), LOHC.
- ✅ **Key physical constants:** $\rho_{H_2, STP} = 0.0899$ kg/m³, LHV = 120 MJ/kg, HHV = 142 MJ/kg, $R_{sp} = 4124$ J/(kg·K).
- ✅ **Panhandle B equation** is the gold-standard pipeline flow equation (KRTU examiner's favorite).
- ✅ **Compressor power formula:** $\dot{W} = \dot{m} \cdot \dfrac{Z R_{sp} T_1}{\eta_c} \cdot \dfrac{k}{k-1} \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k} - 1 \right]$.
- ✅ **Typical energy penalty:** ~10–15% for GH₂ pipeline; ~30–40% for LH₂; ~35–45% for LOHC.
- ✅ **Compressor station spacing:** every **80–160 km** for trunk lines.
- ✅ **Pressure drop in H₂ is ~3× higher** than for natural gas at the same energy throughput.
- ✅ **Hydrogen embrittlement** is the main materials challenge for steel pipelines.
- ✅ **Repurposed NG pipelines** can transport up to 20–30 vol% H₂ blending without major retrofit.
- ✅ **LH₂ boil-off rate:** 0.03–0.1%/day; **volumetric energy density** 8.5 GJ/m³.
- ✅ **LOHC examples:** Toluene/MCH, Dibenzyltoluene/Perhydro-DBT.
- ✅ **Decision rule:** Distance < 500 km + high volume → pipeline; Distance > 2,000 km → LH₂ ship or LOHC.
- ✅ **Real-world examples to memorize:** European Hydrogen Backbone (40,000 km by 2030), Hynet NW (UK), HySTRA (Japan–Australia LH₂ pilot), Chiyoda SPERA H₂ (LOHC, 50,000 t/year), Suiso Frontier (world's first LH₂ carrier ship, 2022).

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Advice:** When answering 14-mark questions in KTU 2024, **always justify the choice of technology** with both a quantitative (energy penalty %, CAPEX) and qualitative (safety, infrastructure) argument. Pure list-based answers rarely score above 8/14.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation via Road
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Transportation via Road # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Road transportation of hydrogen** refers to the overland movement of hydrogen in gaseous or liquid form using specialized heavy-duty road vehicles, including compressed gas tube trailers, cryogenic liquid hydrogen tankers, and emerging tube-container swap-body systems. It represents the **short-to-medium haul (typically up to 300–500 km)** logistical link between hydrogen production plants, storage terminals, refueling stations, and industrial end-users. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 3 Definition Anchor** > Road transport is the most mature, flexible, and widely deployed hydrogen distribution mode globally, characterized by modular scalability, lower capital barrier (compared to pipelines), and adaptability to fluctuating demand. The three principal road transport modes are: - **CHG (Compressed Hydrogen Gas)** – gaseous H₂ at 200–700 bar in high-pressure composite tube trailers - **LH₂ (Liquid Hydrogen)** – cryogenically liquefied H₂ at 20.3 K (–252.87 °C) in super-insulated vacuum tankers - **CCH₂ (Compressed Cryogenic Hydrogen)** – hybrid cold-compressed systems at intermediate temperatures (e.g., 80 K, 350–700 bar) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine **hydrogen as an extremely lightweight, hard-to-contain "ghost gas"** that wants to escape every container through microscopic pores. Road transportation is essentially the engineering challenge of putting this "ghost" into a mobile, roadworthy bottle — but unlike a milk tanker (where milk sits at atmospheric pressure), the bottle must withstand enormous internal pressure *or* arctic-cold temperatures. - Think of **CHG trailers** as "moving scuba tanks" — multiple ultra-strong cylinders strapped to a flatbed truck, where the gas is squeezed (compressed) to fit more mass into less volume. - Think of **LH₂ tankers** as "giant moving thermoses" — a double-walled, vacuum-jacketed cryogenic dewar on wheels, keeping hydrogen at temperatures colder than the surface of Pluto. > [!NOTE] > **Why both methods?** Compression and liquefaction are "force-fitting" hydrogen into a vehicle since 1 kg of H₂ at atmospheric conditions occupies **≈ 11,000 L** — the size of a small room. Without conditioning, you would need an entire truck just to carry a few kilograms. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold Highlights) - **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg = 33.33 kWh/kg** - **HHV of H₂ = 142 MJ/kg = 39.39 kWh/kg** - **Boiling point of H₂ = 20.271 K (–252.879 °C)** - **Critical temperature of H₂ = 33.19 K** - **Critical pressure of H₂ = 13.15 bar** - **Density of H₂ at STP (0 °C, 1 bar) = 0.0899 kg/m³** - **Density of LH₂ = 70.85 kg/m³** - **Density of H₂ at 700 bar ≈ 40–42 kg/m³** - **Energy to liquefy H₂ ≈ 10–13 kWh/kg** (theoretical minimum ≈ 3.92 kWh/kg) - **Boil-off rate (well-designed LH₂ tanker) ≈ 0.1–0.3 %/day** > [!IMPORTANT] > These constants are **must-memorize** for KTU derivations. Questions frequently test the *theoretical* minimum liquefaction energy using the Carnot-equivalent expression. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Road Transport ### A. Compressed Hydrogen Gas (CHG) Tube Trailers A CHG tube trailer consists of multiple **Type III or Type IV composite cylinders** (carbon-fiber wrapped, aluminum or polymer liner) bundled onto a standard 40-ft flatbed. Each tube is fitted with high-pressure valves, pressure regulators, and safety relief burst discs. - **Operating pressure**: 250 bar (legacy), 500 bar, or **700 bar** (modern standard) - **Payload per trailer**: ~250–450 kg H₂ (700 bar, Type IV) - **Compressor energy required**: ~1.5–3 kWh per kg of H₂ compressed to 700 bar - **Best suited for**: Distances up to ~200–300 km from production site ### B. Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂) Cryogenic Tankers LH₂ tankers are double-walled vacuum-insulated cryostats mounted on articulated trailers. Hydrogen is liquefied at the production plant (using Joule-Thomson expansion, Claude cycle, or mixed refrigerant cycles) and transported at 20.3 K. - **Payload per trailer**: ~3,000–4,500 kg H₂ (a **~10× advantage** over CHG) - **Boil-off rate**: 0.1–0.3 %/day (≈ 0.03–0.1 %/hour in some designs) - **Liquefaction energy**: ~10–13 kWh/kg (real plant), 3.92 kWh/kg (theoretical) - **Best suited for**: Long-distance, high-volume transport (>300 km) ### C. Tube Trailers vs. Liquid Tankers — Engineering Trade-off | Decision Driver | CHG Tube Trailer | LH₂ Cryogenic Tanker | |---|---|---| | **CAPEX** | Lower (≈ $0.8–1.2 M) | Higher (≈ $1.5–2.5 M) | | **OPEX** | Compressor electricity | Liquefaction + boil-off losses | | **Energy penalty** | 1.5–3 kWh/kg | 10–13 kWh/kg | | **Round-trip loss** | Negligible | 0.3–1 %/day boil-off | | **Refill turnaround** | 1–3 hours | 4–8 hours (cool-down) | | **Hydrogen purity at delivery** | High (no boil-off) | Vent losses may reduce purity | ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why compress?** → Gases are mostly empty space; compression packs more mass into the cylinder, improving logistics efficiency. - **Why liquefy?** → Liquid is **~845× denser** than atmospheric gas, so a single tanker can deliver 10× the mass per trip — but you "pay" in energy and boil-off management. - **Why 700 bar (not 1000 bar)?** → Engineering compromise: above 700 bar, the cost of Type IV cylinders rises super-linearly due to thicker carbon-fiber winding, while the mass gain diminishes. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All symbols use **SI units** unless otherwise stated.
