$$V_{\text{cell}} = 1.2117 - 0.10 - 0.15 - 0.05 = 0.9117 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{\text{cell}} \approx 0.912 \text{ V}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of electrolyte concentration, operating temperature, and catalyst choice on the performance of an Alkaline Fuel Cell.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain why AFCs are sensitive to CO₂ contamination. Describe, with equations, two strategies used to mitigate this problem.

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Parametric Effects on AFC Performance

**1. Effect of KOH Electrolyte Concentration:**
- **Higher concentration (35–45 wt%)** → higher ionic conductivity → lower ohmic losses → improved voltage.
- **Too high (> 50 wt%)** → risk of KOH crystallization, viscosity increase, and reduced water availability.
- **Optimum:** ~ **30–35 wt% KOH** for most applications.

**2. Effect of Operating Temperature:**
- **Higher T** → faster reaction kinetics → lower activation losses → higher current density.
- **Higher T** → also higher conductivity of KOH.
- However, the trade-off is water evaporation: above ~ 100 °C, water must be carefully managed; above ~ 250 °C, electrolyte degradation is severe.
- **Optimum Range:** 60–80 °C (low-temp AFC) or 150–200 °C (high-temp AFC with pressurized KOH).

**3. Effect of Catalyst Choice:**
- **Anode:** Raney Ni (very high surface area) is the modern standard. Pt is rarely used in commercial AFCs.
- **Cathode:** Ag is the most common due to its high activity for the ORR in alkaline media and low cost compared to Pt.
- **Advanced Catalysts:** Ag-Pd, Ag-Hg alloys, perovskite oxides (e.g., LaCoO₃) and spinels (e.g., Co₃O₄) are research-grade materials that push the ORR onset potential closer to the thermodynamic limit.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — CO₂ Sensitivity and Mitigation

**The CO₂ Problem:**
When CO₂ enters the AFC (via impure H₂ or air-fed cathode), it reacts with KOH:

$$2\text{KOH} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The K₂CO₃ formed is less soluble than KOH, and it **precipitates within the porous electrodes and matrix**, blocking the active sites, increasing ohmic resistance, and reducing cell performance dramatically. The reaction also consumes OH⁻ ions, reducing electrolyte conductivity.

**Mitigation Strategy 1 — Use Pure Oxygen Instead of Air:**
- For space and military applications, the cathode is fed with **pure O₂ from cryogenic tanks** rather than ambient air, entirely eliminating CO₂ ingress.

**Mitigation Strategy 2 — CO₂ Scrubbing (Inlet Air Treatment):**
- A **soda-lime (Ca(OH)₂ + NaOH) scrubber** is installed in the air supply line.
- The scrubber absorbs CO₂ before it reaches the cathode via:

$$\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$2\text{NaOH} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

- This reduces the CO₂ level from ~ 400 ppm (ambient) to < 10 ppm, which is acceptable for AFC operation.

**Mitigation Strategy 3 (Bonus) — Circulating Electrolyte with Periodic Replacement:**
- The KOH electrolyte is pumped in a closed loop; a fraction is continuously replaced to remove carbonate accumulation. Some systems use **electrolyte regeneration columns** that bubble CO₂-stripped air through heated KOH to release absorbed CO₂.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] > **Common Ways Students Lose Marks in AFC Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cathode–anode flow direction of OH⁻.** OH⁻ moves from **cathode to anode** (not anode to cathode). The H⁺ flow direction in PEMFCs often confuses students.
> 2. **Writing anode half-reaction incorrectly.** Many students write $2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$ — this is the **acidic** half-reaction. In alkaline media, you **must** show the OH⁻ reaction. Losing 1 mark here is very common.
> 3. **Using $p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 0.47$ atm for liquid-water-forming cells** — if the cell operates below 100 °C and produces liquid water, the reference state is the **pure liquid**, so $p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ in the Nernst equation becomes **1 atm** (activity of pure liquid = 1). Many students wrongly substitute saturated vapor pressure.
> 4. **Confusing HHV and LHV.** AFCs are referenced to **HHV (286 kJ/mol)** because they produce liquid water.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram.** The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates 2 marks for a labelled diagram. Always include a Mermaid or hand-drawn sketch.
> 6. **Ignoring the CO₂ point in the question.** Even when not explicitly asked, mentioning CO₂ sensitivity shows examiners you have understood the practical reality of AFCs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Alkaline Fuel Cells":**
> - **Electrolyte:** Aqueous KOH (30–45 wt%); charge carrier is **OH⁻**.
> - **Catalysts:** Ni / Raney Ni (anode), Ag (cathode) — **non-precious metals** = cost advantage.
> - **Operating T:** 60–250 °C (low to high temperature).
> - **Theoretical OCV:** **1.229 V**; practical $V_{\text{cell}}$: **0.7–0.9 V**.
> - **Net Reaction:** $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (liquid water, drinkable).
> - **Anode Half:** $2\text{H}_2 + 4\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 4\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^-$
> - **Cathode Half:** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$
> - **OH⁻ Flow Direction:** Cathode → Anode (opposite of e⁻ flow in the wire).
> - **Key Equations to Memorize:**
>   * Nernst: $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{H}_2}\sqrt{p_{\text{O}_2}}}{p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$
>   * Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \dfrac{237}{286} = 0.83$ (HHV basis)
>   * $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohmic}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$
> - **Biggest Limitation:** **CO₂ poisoning** → forms K₂CO₃ precipitate.
> - **Mitigation:** Use pure O₂, install CO₂ scrubbers, or use circulating electrolyte.
> - **Heritage Applications:** **Apollo, Space Shuttle** (3 × PC-17 AFCs, each 12 kW + water for crew).
> - **Modern Companies:** **AFC Energy (UK)**, **Genis (South Korea)**, **Cummins AFC** (USA).
> - **Comparison Hook:** "AFC = cheap catalysts but CO₂-sensitive; PEMFC = expensive Pt but CO₂-tolerant." Memorize this one-liner for any comparative KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**, also known as a **Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cell**, is an electrochemical energy conversion device that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically **hydrogen gas, H₂**) and an oxidant (**oxygen, O₂**, from air) into electrical energy, with **water** and **heat** as the only by-products. The defining feature of a PEMFC is its use of a **solid, ion-conducting polymer membrane** (commonly a perfluorosulfonic acid polymer such as **Nafion™**) as the electrolyte, which selectively transports **protons (H⁺ ions)** from the anode to the cathode while blocking the direct passage of electrons and reactant gases. The single-cell theoretical open-circuit voltage is **1.229 V at standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm)**. Practical single cells operate in the range of **0.6 V – 0.8 V** at useful current densities (0.5 – 1.5 A/cm²), while multi-cell stacks scale this voltage upward (e.g., a 300-cell stack can produce ~200–250 V). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** > Students must be able to define the operating principle of PEMFCs, identify the role of the MEA (Membrane Electrode Assembly), write the half-cell and overall reactions, describe the polarization curve, and explain water/thermal management challenges. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a PEMFC as a **"charged sandwich"**: - The two outer slices of bread are the **bipolar plates with flow channels** that deliver hydrogen and air, like two separate pipes feeding two separate rooms. - Inside the sandwich, the **gaskets and gas diffusion layers (GDLs)** are like the cheese slices — they distribute the gas evenly. - The **meat of the sandwich is the Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA)** — a three-layer composite consisting of the anode catalyst, the proton-exchange membrane, and the cathode catalyst. Now, when you "bite" into this sandwich (i.e., start the chemical reaction): - **Hydrogen** enters from the anode side, gets split into protons and electrons at the platinum catalyst (like a chef slicing tomatoes). - The **protons** migrate through the membrane (like guests passing through a turnstile at a sports stadium — only H⁺ can pass). - The **electrons** are forced to take the long way around through an external circuit, doing useful electrical work (like water going through a turbine). - At the cathode, **protons + electrons + oxygen** recombine to form water — the harmless exhaust. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants to Memorise for KTU:** > - Faraday's Constant: **F = 96,485 C/mol ≈ 96,500 C/mol** > - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** > - Standard cell potential for H₂/O₂ cell: **E° = 1.229 V (25 °C)** > - Lower Heating Value of H₂: **ΔH_LHV = 241.8 kJ/mol** > - Higher Heating Value of H₂: **ΔH_HHV = 285.8 kJ/mol** > - Gibbs Free Energy of formation of H₂O(l): **ΔG° = –237.2 kJ/mol** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic A PEMFC operates by spatially separating the **hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR)** at the anode from the **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** at the cathode, with the proton-exchange membrane acting as the ionic bridge. ### Step 1 — Fuel Delivery & Adsorption - Humidified **H₂** is fed into the anode flow channel and diffuses through the porous **Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** to reach the **anode catalyst layer**. - Simultaneously, humidified **air (O₂)** is fed into the cathode channel and reaches the **cathode catalyst layer**. ### Step 2 — Anode Half-Reaction (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction — HOR) - At the platinum catalyst surface, H₂ dissociates into protons and electrons. - **Anode:** $\text{H}_2 \;\rightarrow\; 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ - The protons are driven through the hydrated membrane by both **concentration gradient** and the **electrochemical potential difference**. - The electrons, blocked by the membrane, are forced through the external circuit. ### Step 3 — Proton Migration Through the Membrane - The Nafion membrane conducts protons only when **hydrated**. - Proton transport occurs via the **Grotthuss mechanism** (proton hopping along water chains) and **vehicle mechanism** (H₃O⁺ diffusion). - Conductivity drops sharply if the membrane dries out — a critical operational concern. ### Step 4 — Cathode Half-Reaction (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR) - Protons arriving at the cathode catalyst combine with oxygen and the electrons returning from the external circuit. - **Cathode:** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \;\rightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{O}$ - This is the **rate-limiting step** (slow kinetics) and is the source of the largest activation overpotential in a PEMFC. ### Step 5 — Overall Cell Reaction $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \;\rightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \;\text{kJ/mol}, \quad E^{\circ} = 1.229\;\text{V}$$ ## 2.2 Components of a PEMFC — The Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA) The MEA is the **heart** of the PEMFC and consists of: | Component | Material | Function | |---|---|---| | **Anode Catalyst Layer** | Pt nanoparticles on carbon support (Pt/C, ~0.1–0.4 mg/cm²) | Catalyzes H₂ dissociation (HOR) | | **Proton Exchange Membrane** | Perfluorinated sulfonic acid (Nafion™ 115/117/212) | Conducts H⁺, blocks e⁻ and gas crossover | | **Cathode Catalyst Layer** | Pt nanoparticles on carbon (~0.2–0.5 mg/cm²) | Catalyzes O₂ reduction (ORR) | | **Gas Diffusion Layers (GDLs)** | Carbon paper / carbon cloth with PTFE coating | Distributes reactant gases, removes product water, conducts electrons and heat | | **Bipolar Plates** | Graphite composite or metal (coated) | Distribute gases, collect current, manage heat | | **Seals / Gaskets** | Silicone, PTFE | Prevent gas leakage and mixing | ## 2.3 Operating Conditions (Standard Operating Envelope) | Parameter | Typical Range | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Operating Temperature** | 60 – 100 °C (usually ~80 °C) | Limited by membrane dehydration above 100 °C | | **Operating Pressure** | 1 – 3 atm (gauge) | Higher P → higher performance but more parasitic load | | **Current Density** | 0.4 – 1.5 A/cm² | Practical operation window | | **Humidity (RH)** | 80 – 100 % relative humidity | Critical for membrane proton conductivity | | **H₂ Stoichiometry** | 1.1 – 1.5 | Excess to ensure complete utilization | | **Air Stoichiometry** | 2.0 – 3.0 | Excess to avoid O₂ starvation | | **Pt Loading** | 0.1 – 0.5 mg/cm² (total both sides) | Major cost driver | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Master these equations — they form the quantitative backbone of any PEMFC numerical or descriptive question on the KTU board exam. | # | Equation / Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $E^{\circ} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$ | Theoretical open-circuit voltage from Gibbs free energy | | 2 | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\bigl(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}\bigr)$ | **Nernst Equation** for H₂/O₂ cell | | 3 | $\eta_{\text{thermo}} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.829$ | Thermodynamic efficiency (HHV basis) | | 4 | $\eta_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H_{\text{LHV}}} = \dfrac{237.2}{241.8} = 0.981$ | Thermodynamic efficiency (LHV basis) | | 5 | $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$ | **Polarization Equation** (cell voltage breakdown) | | 6 | $\eta_{\text{ohm}} = i\,R_{\text{ohmic}}$ | Ohmic loss (Ohm's law) | | 7 | $\eta_{\text{act}} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\bigl(\tfrac{i}{i_0}\bigr)$ | Activation overpotential (Butler–Volmer, low current) | | 8 | $i = i_0\!\left[\exp\!\Bigl(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\Bigr) - \exp\!\Bigl(\dfrac{-\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\Bigr)\right]$ | **Butler–Volmer Equation** | | 9 | $\eta_{\text{conc}} = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\Bigl(\dfrac{i_L}{i_L - i}\Bigr)$ | Concentration overpotential | | 10 | $P_{\text{el}} = V_{\text{cell}} \times I \times N_{\text{cells}}$ | Stack electrical power output | | 11 | $\eta_{\text{util}} = \dfrac{\mu_{\text{H}_2,\text{consumed}}}{\mu_{\text{H}_2,\text{supplied}}}$ | Fuel utilization factor | | 12 | $\sigma_{\text{mem}} = (0.005139\,\lambda - 0.00326)\,\exp\!\bigl[1267\bigl(\tfrac{1}{303} - \tfrac{1}{T}\bigr)\bigr]$ | **Springer model** for Nafion conductivity vs. water content $\lambda$ and $T$ | ## 2.5 Why & How — The "Engineering Why" Behind PEMFCs | Question | Answer | |---|---| | **Why a solid polymer membrane?** | Avoids the leakage, corrosion, and CO₂ sensitivity of liquid electrolytes (as in PAFCs). Allows compact stacking. | | **Why platinum catalyst?** | Pt provides the lowest activation barrier for both HOR and ORR in the low-temperature regime. | | **Why low temperature (≤ 100 °C)?** | Membrane dehydration and mechanical failure above ~95 °C. Also avoids the need for complex thermal management. | | **Why humidified inlet gases?** | Nafion needs water molecules to form the proton-conducting channels (Grotthuss mechanism). | | **Why is the cathode the slow step?** | O=O double bond cleavage (498 kJ/mol) is kinetically difficult; ORR exchange current density is ~6 orders of magnitude lower than HOR. | | **Where are PEMFCs used in real life?** | Automotive (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, Honda Clarity), backup power for telecom towers, forklifts (Plug Power), space programs (early Gemini missions), portable/military power. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Open-Circuit Voltage (E°) from Thermodynamics The maximum electrical work obtainable from a fuel cell equals the **Gibbs free energy change** of the cell reaction. For the overall reaction: $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \;\rightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$$ **Step 1 — Identify the number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂.** Each H₂ molecule releases 2 electrons at the anode. $$n = 2\;\text{mol }e^-/\text{mol H}_2$$ **Step 2 — Write the Gibbs free energy relation.** $$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange for E°.** $$E^{\circ} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute standard values.** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(\text{H}_2\text{O},\,l) = -237.2\;\text{kJ/mol}$, $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(\text{H}_2) = 0$, $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(\text{O}_2) = 0$ $$\Delta G^{\circ} = (-237.2) - (0) - \tfrac{1}{2}(0) = -237.2\;\text{kJ/mol}$$ **Step 5 — Compute E° numerically.** $$ \begin{aligned} E^{\circ} &= -\dfrac{(-237.2\times 10^{3}\;\text{J/mol})}{(2)(96{,}485\;\text{C/mol})} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}\;\text{V} \\[4pt] &\approx 1.229\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ > **Engineering Insight:** This **1.229 V** is the **thermodynamic ceiling**. No real fuel cell can exceed it under standard conditions. Practical cells operate at **0.6 – 0.7 V** per cell due to irreversibilities. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nernst Equation for the PEMFC For non-standard temperature, pressure, or concentration, the reversible cell potential is given by the Nernst equation: **Step 1 — Write the general Nernst form.** $$E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\bigl(Q_{\text{reaction}}\bigr)$$ **Step 2 — Identify the reaction quotient Q for the H₂/O₂ reaction.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\;\rightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \\[2pt] Q &= \dfrac{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{a_{\text{H}_2}\,\bigl(a_{\text{O}_2}\bigr)^{1/2}} \end{aligned} $$ Since the product is liquid water ($a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 1$) and gases are at low to moderate pressure, we substitute partial pressures (in atm) for activities: $$Q = \dfrac{1}{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the Nernst equation.** $$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\bigl(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}\bigr)$$ This is the standard textbook Nernst form for a fuel cell. Equivalently, since $-\ln(x) = \ln(1/x)$: $$E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\bigl(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}\bigr)$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate at 80 °C (353 K), a typical PEMFC operating point.** $$ \begin{aligned} E &= 1.229 + \dfrac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)}\ln\!\bigl(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}\bigr) \\[4pt] &\approx 1.229 + 0.01521\ln\!\bigl(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}\bigr) \end{aligned} $$ For $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1\;\text{atm}$ and $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21\;\text{atm}$ (air): $$ \begin{aligned} E &\approx 1.229 + (0.01521)\ln\!\bigl(1 \times \sqrt{0.21}\bigr) \\[4pt] &\approx 1.229 + (0.01521)(-0.785) \\[4pt] &\approx 1.229 - 0.012 \\[4pt] &\approx 1.217\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cell Voltage from Polarization Components The actual cell voltage is always **less than** the Nernst voltage due to irreversible losses. The classical fuel cell polarization equation is: $$V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$ **Step 1 — Activation Overpotential (η_act).** Derived from the Butler–Volmer kinetics: $$ \begin{aligned} i &= i_0\!\left[\exp\!\Bigl(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta_{\text{act}}}{RT}\Bigr) - \exp\!\Bigl(\dfrac{-\alpha_c F \eta_{\text{act}}}{RT}\Bigr)\right] \\[4pt] \text{At low }\eta_{\text{act}} \text{ (low current):}\quad \eta_{\text{act}} &\approx \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\bigl(\tfrac{i}{i_0}\bigr) \end{aligned} $$ Typical values: $i_0$ (cathode, ORR) ≈ $10^{-9}\;\text{A/cm}^2$; $\alpha \approx 0.5$. **Step 2 — Ohmic Overpotential (η_ohm).** Pure resistive drop across the membrane, GDLs, and contacts: $$\eta_{\text{ohm}} = i\,R_{\text{ASR}}$$ where $R_{\text{ASR}}$ (Area-Specific Resistance) is measured in $\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$. Typical Nafion 212 membrane ASR ≈ 0.05 $\Omega\cdot$cm² at full hydration. **Step 3 — Concentration Overpotential (η_conc).** Caused by mass-transport limitations at high current when reactant concentration at the catalyst surface drops: $$\eta_{\text{conc}} = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\Bigl(\dfrac{i_L}{i_L - i}\Bigr)$$ where $i_L$ is the **limiting current density** (typically 1 – 3 A/cm²). ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Problem) > **Problem:** A single PEMFC operates at 80 °C with H₂ at 1.0 atm and air at 1.0 atm. Calculate: > (a) the Nernst (reversible) voltage, > (b) the activation overpotential at $i = 0.5\;\text{A/cm}^2$ (take $\alpha = 0.5$, $i_0 = 10^{-4}\;\text{A/cm}^2$ at the anode; neglect cathode activation), > (c) the ohmic overpotential if the membrane ASR is $0.08\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$, > (d) the actual cell voltage. **Given:** - $T = 80\,°\text{C} = 353\;\text{K}$ - $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1.0\;\text{atm}$, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21\;\text{atm}$ - $i = 0.5\;\text{A/cm}^2$, $\alpha = 0.5$, $i_0 = 10^{-4}\;\text{A/cm}^2$, $R_{\text{ASR}} = 0.08\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$ **Solution:** **(a) Nernst voltage** (already computed above): $$E_{\text{Nernst}} \approx 1.217\;\text{V}$$ **(b) Activation overpotential:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{act}} &= \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\Bigl(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\Bigr) \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{(8.314)(353)}{(0.5)(2)(96{,}485)}\ln\!\Bigl(\dfrac{0.5}{10^{-4}}\Bigr) \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{2934.8}{96{,}485}\ln(5000) \\[4pt] &= (0.03042)(8.517) \\[4pt] &\approx 0.259\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **(c) Ohmic overpotential:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{ohm}} &= i \times R_{\text{ASR}} \\[4pt] &= (0.5)(0.08) \\[4pt] &= 0.040\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **(d) Actual cell voltage (assuming η_conc ≈ 0 at 0.5 A/cm²):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{cell}} &= E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}} \\[4pt] &= 1.217 - 0.259 - 0.040 - 0 \\[4pt] &\approx 0.918\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Causes, Effects & Mitigation of Voltage Losses | Loss Mechanism | Physical Cause | Region of Curve | Magnitude (Typical) | Mitigation Strategy | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Activation (cathode, ORR)** | Sluggish O₂ reduction kinetics; high bond energy of O=O | Low current (< 0.1 A/cm²) | 0.2 – 0.4 V | Higher Pt loading, Pt alloys (Pt-Co, Pt-Ni), novel catalysts (Fe-N-C) | | **Ohmic (membrane + contacts)** | Ionic resistance of membrane, electronic resistance of GDL/plates | Linear (mid-current) | 0.05 – 0.15 V | Thinner membranes, higher hydration, better BPP coatings | | **Concentration (mass transport)** | Reactant starvation; product water flooding | High current (> 1 A/cm²) | 0.05 – 0.3 V | Optimised flow field, hydrophobic GDL, higher stoichiometry | | **Mixed potential / crossover** | H₂ crossover through membrane, internal currents | OCV depression | 0.05 – 0.15 V loss in OCV | Thinner dense membranes, better sealing | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PEMFC Single-Cell Block Architecture (Mermaid) The following block diagram maps the functional architecture of a single PEMFC, showing the flow of reactants, ions, electrons, water, and heat. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph inletStreams["Reactant Inlet Streams"] H2in["Humidified H2 Gas
Anode Inlet"] AIRin["Humidified Air
Cathode Inlet"] end subgraph anodeSide["Anode Side of Cell"] BPanode["Bipolar Plate Anode
Flow Channels and Current Collector"] GDLanode["Gas Diffusion Layer Anode
Carbon Paper with PTFE"] CLanode["Anode Catalyst Layer
Pt on Carbon Support
Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction"] end subgraph coreMEA["Membrane Electrode Assembly MEA Core"] MEM["Proton Exchange Membrane
Nafion Perfluorinated Sulfonic Acid
H plus Conductor e- Insulator"] end subgraph cathodeSide["Cathode Side of Cell"] CLcathode["Cathode Catalyst Layer
Pt on Carbon Support
Oxygen Reduction Reaction"] GDLcathode["Gas Diffusion Layer Cathode
Carbon Paper with PTFE"] BPcathode["Bipolar Plate Cathode
Flow Channels and Current Collector"] end subgraph outletStreams["Product and Exhaust Streams"] H2out["Unused H2 and Water Vapour
Anode Outlet Recirculated"] WATERout["Product Water and Excess Air
Cathode Outlet"] end subgraph externalCircuit["External Load Circuit"] LOAD["Electrical Load
e- flow delivers usable power"] end H2in --> BPanode AIRin --> BPcathode BPanode --> GDLanode GDLanode --> CLanode CLanode -- "2H plus crosses membrane" --> MEM MEM -- "H plus reaches cathode" --> CLcathode CLcathode --> GDLcathode GDLcathode --> BPcathode BPcathode --> WATERout CLanode -- "2e- leave anode" --> LOAD LOAD -- "2e- return to cathode" --> CLcathode CLanode -- "Heat released" --> H2out CLcathode -- "H2O produced" --> WATERout ``` ## 4.2 Polarization Curve — V–i Characteristic (Mermaid Block Flow) The following diagram maps how cell voltage decreases with increasing current density through three distinct loss regions. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ocvRegion["Region 1 Open Circuit"] OCV["V equals 1.0 to 1.2 V
Current equals 0
Mixed Potential and Crossover Losses"] end subgraph activationRegion["Region 2 Activation Drop"] ACTDROP["V drops sharply to 0.8 V
Current 0 to 0.1 A per cm2
Sluggish ORR Kinetics on Pt Cathode"] end subgraph ohmicRegion["Region 3 Ohmic Linear Drop"] OHMDROP["V decreases linearly
Current 0.1 to 1.0 A per cm2
Membrane and Contact Resistance"] end subgraph concentrationRegion["Region 4 Concentration Drop"] CONCDROP["V falls steeply to 0
Current approaches Limiting Current iL
Mass Transport Limitation and Flooding"] end OCV --> ACTDROP ACTDROP --> OHMDROP OHMDROP --> CONCDROP ``` ## 4.3 Water and Thermal Management Schematic (Mermaid) Water management is the **single most critical operational challenge** in PEMFCs — both dehydration and flooding are failure modes. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph waterSources["Water Sources In PEMFC"] WS1["Product Water at Cathode
from ORR Reaction"] WS2["Humidifier Inlet Gas
External Humidification"] WS3["Electroosmotic Drag
H plus pulls H2O from Anode to Cathode"] end subgraph waterLosses["Water Losses Out of PEMFC"] WL1["Evaporation into Exhaust Streams"] WL2["Back Diffusion from Cathode to Anode
opposes electroosmotic drag"] WL3["Membrane Dehydration
if T too high or RH too low"] end subgraph failureModes["Failure Modes if Unbalanced"] FM1["DROUGHT
Membrane dries
Proton conductivity drops
Voltage collapses"] FM2["FLOOD
Cathode GDL floods
Oxygen blocked
Voltage collapses"] end WS1 --> FM2 WS2 --> FM1 WS3 --> FM1 WL1 --> FM1 WL2 --> FM2 WL3 --> FM1 ``` ## 4.4 PEMFC System — Balance of Plant (BoP) Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph fuelSubsystem["Hydrogen Fuel Subsystem"] HTANK["High Pressure H2 Tank
350 or 700 bar"] HREG["Pressure Regulator"] HINJ["Hydrogen Injector and Humidifier"] end subgraph airSubsystem["Air Oxidant Subsystem"] FILT["Air Filter"] COMP["Air Compressor or Blower"] AHUM["Air Humidifier"] end subgraph cellStack["PEMFC Stack Core"] STACK["Series Connected Cells
Typical 100 to 400 Cells"] BPP["Bipolar Plates with Flow Channels"] COOL["Coolant Channels between Cells"] end subgraph thermalMgmt["Thermal Management Subsystem"] RAD["Radiator"] PUMP["Coolant Pump"] DEION["Deionizer to maintain purity"] end subgraph powerConditioning["Power Electronics and Control"] DC["DC DC Converter"] INV["DC AC Inverter"] ECU["Electronic Control Unit ECU"] LOADEXT["External Load
Vehicle Motor or Grid"] end HTANK --> HREG --> HINJ --> STACK FILT --> COMP --> AHUM --> STACK STACK --> BPP STACK --> COOL COOL --> PUMP --> RAD PUMP --> DEION --> COOL STACK --> DC --> INV --> LOADEXT ECU -.controls.-> HINJ ECU -.controls.-> COMP ECU -.controls.-> PUMP ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]` **Q: List the half-cell and overall reactions of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell. Also state the theoretical open-circuit voltage at standard conditions.** **Model Answer:** - **Anode (HOR):** $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ - **Cathode (ORR):** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ - **Overall:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ - **Theoretical OCV at 25 °C, 1 atm:** $E^{\circ} = -\Delta G^{\circ}/nF = 237{,}200/(2\times 96{,}485) \approx \mathbf{1.229\;\text{V}}$ --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **Q: What is the Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA) in a PEMFC? Name its three main components.** **Model Answer:** The MEA is the core electrochemical component of a PEMFC where the reactions occur. Its three main components are: 1. **Anode catalyst layer** (Pt/C) — site of hydrogen oxidation. 2. **Proton exchange membrane** (Nafion) — conducts H⁺ ions, separates reactants, blocks electrons. 3. **Cathode catalyst layer** (Pt/C) — site of oxygen reduction. The MEA is sandwiched between two gas diffusion layers (GDLs). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question B1 (Option A — 14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell. Write the electrode and overall reactions.** **Model Solution:** A PEMFC consists of the following layered components (from anode to cathode): 1. **Anode bipolar plate** with flow channels for H₂ distribution. 2. **Anode gas diffusion layer (GDL)** — porous carbon paper that distributes gas uniformly and conducts electrons to the plate. 3. **Anode catalyst layer** — Pt nanoparticles on carbon where H₂ dissociates. 4. **Proton exchange membrane** — Nafion, conducts H⁺ via the Grotthuss mechanism. 5. **Cathode catalyst layer** — Pt nanoparticles where O₂ is reduced. 6. **Cathode GDL** and **cathode bipolar plate** for air distribution. **Working Principle:** Humidified H₂ is fed to the anode; humidified air to the cathode. At the anode catalyst: $$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$ Protons migrate through the hydrated Nafion membrane to the cathode; electrons travel through the external circuit (doing useful work) and return to the cathode, where: $$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Overall:** $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$ (Refer to **Section 4.1** Mermaid block diagram for the architecture.) #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain the V–i polarization curve of a PEMFC, identifying the three major voltage loss regions and the physical cause of each.** **Model Solution:** The polarization (V–i) curve of a PEMFC plots **cell voltage (V_cell)** on the y-axis against **current density (i)** on the x-axis. Four distinct regions are observed: | Region | Current Range | Loss Mechanism | Cause | |---|---|---|---| | **Region 1 — OCV** | $i = 0$ | Mixed potential, gas crossover | H₂ permeating membrane reacts directly with O₂, internal currents | | **Region 2 — Activation drop** | $i \approx 0$ – $0.1\;\text{A/cm}^2$ | Activation overpotential $\eta_{\text{act}}$ | Slow ORR kinetics on Pt; the O=O bond (498 kJ/mol) is hard to break | | **Region 3 — Ohmic drop (linear)** | $i \approx 0.1$ – $1.0\;\text{A/cm}^2$ | Ohmic overpotential $\eta_{\text{ohm}}$ | Ionic resistance of Nafion membrane + electronic resistance of GDL/plates; obeys $V = E - iR$ | | **Region 4 — Concentration drop** | $i \rightarrow i_L$ | Concentration overpotential $\eta_{\text{conc}}$ | Reactant starvation at high current; product water floods GDL | The cell voltage equation: $$V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$ A practical PEMFC delivers ~0.6 – 0.7 V per cell at ~0.6 – 0.8 A/cm², far below the theoretical 1.229 V. --- ### Question B2 (Option B — 14 Marks, Internal Choice to B1) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: A PEMFC operates at 80 °C with H₂ at 1.5 atm and air (21 % O₂) at 1.5 atm total pressure. Calculate (i) the Nernst voltage, (ii) the activation overpotential at $i = 0.6\;\text{A/cm}^2$ with $\alpha = 0.5$ and $i_0 = 10^{-4}\;\text{A/cm}^2$, (iii) the cell voltage if the membrane ASR is $0.07\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$ (neglect concentration loss).** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $T = 353\;\text{K}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485\;\text{C/mol}$, $R = 8.314\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$ - $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1.5\;\text{atm}$, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21 \times 1.5 = 0.315\;\text{atm}$ - $i = 0.6\;\text{A/cm}^2$, $\alpha = 0.5$, $i_0 = 10^{-4}\;\text{A/cm}^2$ - $R_{\text{ASR}} = 0.07\;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$ **(i) Nernst Voltage:** $$ \begin{aligned} E &= E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\bigl(P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}\bigr) \\[4pt] &= 1.229 + \dfrac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)}\ln\!\bigl(1.5 \times \sqrt{0.315}\bigr) \\[4pt] &= 1.229 + (0.01521)\ln(1.5 \times 0.5612) \\[4pt] &= 1.229 + (0.01521)\ln(0.8419) \\[4pt] &= 1.229 + (0.01521)(-0.1722) \\[4pt] &\approx 1.229 - 0.00262 \\[4pt] &\approx 1.226\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Activation Overpotential:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{act}} &= \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\Bigl(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\Bigr) \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{(8.314)(353)}{(0.5)(2)(96{,}485)}\ln\!\Bigl(\dfrac{0.6}{10^{-4}}\Bigr) \\[4pt] &= 0.03042 \times \ln(6000) \\[4pt] &= 0.03042 \times 8.699 \\[4pt] &\approx 0.265\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Cell Voltage:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{ohm}} &= i \times R_{\text{ASR}} = (0.6)(0.07) = 0.042\;\text{V} \\[4pt] V_{\text{cell}} &= E - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}} \\[4pt] &= 1.226 - 0.265 - 0.042 - 0 \\[4pt] &\approx 0.919\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Discuss the water management challenge in PEMFCs. What are the consequences of (i) membrane dehydration and (ii) cathode flooding? Mention two mitigation strategies.** **Model Solution:** **Why water management matters:** Nafion conducts protons only when hydrated. Water in the membrane is essential for the Grotthuss mechanism (proton hopping along H-bonded water chains). Too little or too much water both cause failure. **Sources and Sinks of Water in PEMFC:** - **In:** External humidification of inlet gases, product water from ORR at cathode, electroosmotic drag (1–2.5 H₂O per H⁺ from anode to cathode). - **Out:** Evaporation in exhaust streams, back-diffusion (cathode → anode). **(i) Membrane Dehydration (Drought):** - **Cause:** High temperature (> 95 °C), low inlet RH, high current density (drag dominates). - **Consequence:** Conductivity of Nafion drops exponentially; voltage collapses (ohmic loss skyrockets). - **Visualisation:** Membrane becomes brittle; cracks may form. **(ii) Cathode Flooding:** - **Cause:** Excessive product water at cathode; insufficient evaporation; low gas velocity. - **Consequence:** Liquid water blocks pores of GDL and catalyst layer; O₂ transport to active sites is impeded; concentration overpotential spikes. **Mitigation Strategies:** 1. **Optimised flow field design** — parallel, serpentine, or interdigitated channels to enhance water removal; use of hydrophobic PTFE in GDL to balance wettability. 2. **External membrane humidification** — bubble humidifiers or membrane humidifiers maintain 80–100 % RH in inlet gases. 3. **Operating parameter control** — keep cell temperature ~10 °C below gas dew point; manage current density within optimal window. 4. **Advanced materials** — self-humidifying membranes with embedded hygroscopic oxide nanoparticles (e.g., SiO₂, ZrP). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing ΔG and ΔH:** Many students write the "efficiency" using the wrong denominator. Remember: > - **Voltage efficiency** uses $E/E^{\circ}$. > - **Thermodynamic efficiency** uses $\Delta G/\Delta H$ (HHV basis → 83 %; LHV basis → 98 %). > 2. **Forgetting to convert partial pressure of O₂ in air:** Air is only ~21 % O₂. If the question says "air at 1 atm", the **partial pressure of O₂ is 0.21 atm**, not 1 atm. This mistake can cost 2–3 marks. > 3. **Wrong sign convention for overpotentials:** All losses **subtract** from $E_{\text{Nernst}}$. They are NEVER added. > 4. **Writing "Pt catalyst" without justification:** Always state that Pt is chosen because of its **high exchange current density for HOR and the lowest activation barrier for ORR** among acid-stable metals. > 5. **Ignoring units in Nernst equation:** Always include the conversion factor or use $R = 8.314\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$, $F = 96{,}485\;\text{C/mol}$, and $T$ in Kelvin. > 6. **Calling the membrane "electrolyte":** Strictly, it is a **solid polymer electrolyte** or **ion-exchange membrane**. Use precise terminology for full marks. > 7. **Memorising but not understanding "Grotthuss mechanism":** Examiners reward students who can explain that H⁺ hops between adjacent water molecules along hydrogen-bonded chains rather than physically diffusing. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Memorisation Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - ⭐ PEMFC = **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell** = **Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cell** (both names accepted). - ⭐ Electrolyte: **Nafion** (perfluorinated sulfonic acid polymer); conducts **H⁺ only**; blocks electrons and gases. - ⭐ Operating temperature: **60 – 100 °C** (typical **80 °C**); pressure: **1 – 3 atm**. - ⭐ Charge carrier: **Proton (H⁺)** — NOT OH⁻ (that's alkaline fuel cells). - ⭐ Anode reaction: **H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻** (HOR). - ⭐ Cathode reaction: **½O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O** (ORR — slow step). - ⭐ Overall: **H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O**, $E^{\circ} = \mathbf{1.229\;\text{V}}$ at 25 °C. - ⭐ $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2\;\text{kJ/mol}$, $\Delta H_{\text{HHV}} = 285.8\;\text{kJ/mol}$, $\Delta H_{\text{LHV}} = 241.8\;\text{kJ/mol}$. - ⭐ Thermodynamic efficiency (HHV) = 237.2/285.8 ≈ **83 %**; (LHV) ≈ **98 %**. - ⭐ **Nernst equation:** $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\bigl(P_{\text{H}_2}P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}\bigr)$. - ⭐ Polarization equation: $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$. - ⭐ Activation loss dominates at **low current**; ohmic loss dominates at **mid current**; concentration loss dominates at **high current**. - ⭐ **Water management** is the single biggest operational challenge — drought vs. flooding. - ⭐ **Grotthuss mechanism** = proton hopping along water chains; requires hydrated membrane. - ⭐ Catalyst = **Platinum (Pt)** on carbon support; cathode loading higher than anode (~0.4 vs ~0.1 mg/cm²). - ⭐ **MEA** = Membrane Electrode Assembly = anode catalyst + membrane + cathode catalyst. - ⭐ **GDL** = Gas Diffusion Layer = carbon paper/cloth with PTFE — distributes gas, removes water, conducts e⁻ and heat. - ⭐ **Bipolar plates** distribute gases, collect current, and remove heat; made of graphite or coated metal. - ⭐ **CO poisoning**: even 10 ppm CO in H₂ drastically degrades Pt anode; air bleed or Pt-Ru alloy used as mitigation. - ⭐ Major applications: **transportation** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, Honda Clarity), **backup power**, **forklifts** (Plug Power), **space** (Gemini program). - ⭐ Advantages: low T → fast startup; high power density; solid electrolyte → no leakage. - ⭐ Disadvantages: expensive Pt; CO intolerance; water management complexity; membrane dehydration above ~95 °C.

topics.title: Methanol Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Methanol Fuel Cells ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Direct Methanol Fuel Cell (DMFC)** — A sub-class of Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell in which **liquid methanol (CH₃OH)** is fed directly to the anode where it undergoes electro-catalytic oxidation without any prior reforming step. The liberated protons migrate through a polymer electrolyte membrane, while electrons perform useful work in the external circuit, generating electricity with water and CO₂ as the only by-products. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Methanol Fuel Cells are studied as a critical alternative to gaseous hydrogen-fed PEMFCs, primarily because they circumvent the **hydrogen storage and transportation bottleneck** by leveraging a high-energy-density liquid fuel (methanol). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Battery That Drinks Liquid"** > Think of a conventional lithium-ion battery as a sealed canteen — you must recharge it after it is empty. A methanol fuel cell, in contrast, is like a **reusable coffee mug with a refillable cartridge**. The moment methanol is poured into the anode side, electricity starts flowing continuously. As long as you keep supplying methanol (the "drink") and oxygen from air, the cell keeps producing power. The "empty coffee grounds" (CO₂ + H₂O) are simply vented out, but the mug itself never wears out — making it a true *continuous-energy* device rather than an energy-storage device. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Value / Unit | |---|---| | Molecular mass of CH₃OH | **32.04 g/mol** | | Energy density (LHV) of methanol | **6.09 kWh/kg** (≈ 4.8 kWh/L) | | Theoretical cell voltage (E°) | **1.21 V** at 25 °C | | Number of electrons transferred (n) | **6 e⁻ per CH₃OH molecule** | | Operating temperature | **50 °C – 120 °C** | | Theoretical efficiency (LHV basis) | **96.7 %** | | Practical efficiency | **20 % – 40 %** | | Faraday constant (F) | **96,485 C/mol** | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** DMFC is a **low-temperature fuel cell**. It belongs to the PEMFC family but uses a *liquid* organic fuel instead of gaseous H₂. This is the single most important distinguishing feature for KTU examinations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic A DMFC converts the chemical energy of methanol directly into electrical energy via three simultaneous half-cell reactions separated by a proton-conducting membrane: 1. **Anode Feed:** A dilute aqueous methanol solution (typically 1–3 M) is supplied to the anode chamber. 2. **Anode Reaction (Methanol Oxidation Reaction — MOR):** - CH₃OH + H₂O → CO₂ + 6H⁺ + 6e⁻ (E°ₐ = +0.02 V vs SHE) 3. **Ionic Conduction:** Protons (H⁺) migrate through the **Nafion® PEM**, which is selectively permeable to cations. 4. **Cathode Reaction (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR):** - 1.5 O₂ + 6H⁺ + 6e⁻ → 3 H₂O (E°꜀ = +1.23 V vs SHE) 5. **External Circuit:** Electrons (6 per CH₃OH) flow from anode to cathode through the load, producing DC electricity. 6. **Product Expulsion:** CO₂ is released at the anode; H₂O is released at the cathode. ### 2.2 Cell Architecture Components - **Anode Catalyst:** Pt-Ru (Platinum-Ruthenium) — required because Pt alone is poisoned by **CO intermediates** formed during methanol oxidation. Ru provides oxygenated species (–OH) at lower potentials that strip CO from Pt sites. - **Cathode Catalyst:** Pt (pure) — sufficient because only oxygen reduction occurs. - **Membrane:** Nafion®-115 / Nafion®-117 (sulfonated PTFE) — facilitates H⁺ transport. - **Bipolar Plates:** Graphite or metal with serpentine flow channels for methanol/water and air distribution. - **Diffusion Layers:** Carbon paper/cloth with PTFE coating for gas–liquid management. ### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step - **Why Pt-Ru at anode?** Methanol oxidation produces intermediate **CO** that strongly adsorbs on Pt, blocking active sites. **Ruthenium** undergoes reversible hydroxylation at lower potentials, supplying –OH groups that oxidize Pt–CO to CO₂. - **Why low concentration methanol?** Pure methanol crossover through Nafion is severe. Diluting to ~1 M reduces the concentration gradient driving methanol from anode → cathode. - **Why 50–120 °C operation?** Nafion conductivity requires hydration but methanol vapor pressure is high; balancing these gives optimal T. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Symbols | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$ | Standard reduction potentials | Theoretical OCV computation | | 2 | $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ | n = 6, F = 96,485 C/mol | Maximum electrical work per mole | | 3 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Nernst Equation | Real-time voltage under load | | 4 | $\eta_{\text{LHV}} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H_{\text{LHV}}} \times 100\%$ | Thermodynamic efficiency | Theoretical upper bound | | 5 | $\eta_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{operating}}}{1.21} \times \eta_{\text{LHV}}$ | Real cell efficiency | Practical performance metric | | 6 | $P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{cell}} \times I \times N_{\text{cells}}$ | Stack power output | System-level design | | 7 | $\lambda_{\text{MeOH}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{MeOH}}}{n_{\text{H}_2\text{O (anode)}}}$ | Stoichiometric ratio | Fuel delivery design | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Point:** The LHV of methanol is **ΔH = –638.4 kJ/mol**, while ΔG° = –702 kJ/mol. Since $\vert \Delta G \vert \;\; < \;\; \vert \Delta H \vert$, the theoretical efficiency is **> 100 %** if HHV is used, but the **physically meaningful efficiency is computed using LHV**, giving $\eta_{\text{LHV}} = 702 / 638.4 \times \text{ratio}$... wait, this is the well-known DMFC anomaly: the thermodynamic efficiency *exceeds 100 %* because liquid water is *produced* (a non-spontaneous mixing is essentially recovered). KTU examiners frequently test this anomaly. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Portable Power:** Laptops, mobile phones, military field radios (e.g., SFC Energy, Oorja Protonics). - **Backup Power:** Telecom tower backup systems (1–5 kW range). - **Transportation:** Small electric vehicles, forklifts, auxiliary power units (APUs) in trucks. - **Marine / RV Boats:** Silent on-board power generation. - **Education & Research:** Standard platform to study liquid fuel electrochemistry. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Cell Voltage of DMFC **Step 1 — Write balanced half-reactions with standard reduction potentials (at 25 °C, 1 atm, 1 M):** Anode (oxidation — invert sign for reduction): $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6\text{e}^- \quad E^\circ_{\text{ox}} = -0.02\;\text{V}$$ Cathode (reduction): $$\tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6\text{e}^- \rightarrow 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad E^\circ_{\text{red}} = +1.23\;\text{V}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the cell voltage formula:** $$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode (as reduction)}}$$ $$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = (+1.23) - (-0.04) = 1.21\;\text{V}$$ The anode reduction potential is the negative of the oxidation potential: $-(-0.02) = +0.02$ V when reversed... careful: standard reduction potential of CH₃OH/CO₂ couple is **+0.02 V**; the oxidation potential is the negative. Hence: $$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 1.23 - 0.02 = 1.21\;\text{V} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 3 — Verify using Gibbs free energy:** $$\Delta G^\circ_{\text{cell}} = \Delta G^\circ_f(\text{CO}_2) + 2\Delta G^\circ_f(\text{H}_2\text{O (l)}) - \Delta G^\circ_f(\text{CH}_3\text{OH (l)})$$ Using $\Delta G^\circ_f$ values (kJ/mol): CO₂(g) = $-394.4$, H₂O(l) = $-237.2$, CH₃OH(l) = $-166.4$: $$\Delta G^\circ = -394.4 + 2(-237.2) - (-166.4)$$ $$\Delta G^\circ = -394.4 - 474.4 + 166.4 = -702.4\;\text{kJ/mol}$$ **Step 4 — Convert ΔG to E° using the Faraday relation:** $$E^\circ = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$$ $$E^\circ = -\dfrac{(-702,400\;\text{J/mol})}{(6)(96,485\;\text{C/mol})}$$ $$E^\circ = \dfrac{702,400}{578,910} = 1.213\;\text{V} \approx 1.21\;\text{V} \quad \checkmark$$ Both methods yield **1.21 V**, confirming the derivation. ### 3.2 Derivation: Thermodynamic Efficiency Anomaly **Step 1 — Define HHV and LHV of methanol oxidation:** $$2\text{CH}_3\text{OH(l)} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O(l)} \quad \Delta H_{\text{HHV}} = -1452\;\text{kJ/mol (per 2 CH}_3\text{OH)}$$ Per mole: $\Delta H_{\text{HHV}} = -726\;\text{kJ/mol}$ and $\Delta H_{\text{LHV}} = -638.4\;\text{kJ/mol}$. **Step 2 — Compute efficiencies:** $$\eta_{\text{HHV}} = \dfrac{702.4}{726} \times 100\% = 96.7\%$$ $$\eta_{\text{LHV}} = \dfrac{702.4}{638.4} \times 100\% = 110.0\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Anomaly Explained:** Efficiency *exceeding* 100 % on LHV basis occurs because liquid water is **produced at the cathode** from H⁺ + ½O₂, releasing latent heat of condensation that adds to the useful electrical work beyond the chemical enthalpy released in combustion. This is not a violation of the First Law; it merely means the cell recovers part of the environmental heat into electrical form. ### 3.3 Derivation: Nernst Equation Applied to DMFC For the overall reaction: $$2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Reaction quotient: $$Q = \dfrac{(P_{\text{CO}_2}/P^\circ)^2}{(C_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}})^2 \,(P_{\text{O}_2}/P^\circ)^3}$$ At standard conditions and 25 °C: $$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ For typical operating conditions — $C_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = 1\;\text{M}$, $P_{\text{CO}_2} = 1\;\text{atm}$, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21\;\text{atm}$: $$Q = \dfrac{(1)^2}{(1)^2 (0.21)^3} = \dfrac{1}{0.00926} = 108$$ $$E = 1.21 - \dfrac{(8.314)(298)}{(6)(96,485)} \ln(108)$$ $$E = 1.21 - 0.00428 \times 4.682 = 1.21 - 0.0200 = 1.190\;\text{V}$$ **Operating cell voltage under load (including activation, ohmic, and concentration losses):** $$E_{\text{operating}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohmic}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$ Typical DMFC polarization breakdown: - $E_{\text{Nernst}} \approx 1.19\;\text{V}$ - $\eta_{\text{act,anode}} \approx 0.35\;\text{V}$ (slow MOR kinetics) - $\eta_{\text{act,cathode}} \approx 0.20\;\text{V}$ (crossover-induced mixed-potential) - $\eta_{\text{ohmic}} \approx 0.10\;\text{V}$ - $\eta_{\text{conc}} \approx 0.05\;\text{V}$ **Therefore:** $E_{\text{operating}} \approx 0.49\;\text{V}$ at typical current density (0.2 A/cm²), giving cell efficiency of $\approx 41\%$. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: DMFC vs H₂-PEMFC | Performance Parameter | DMFC | H₂-PEMFC | |---|---|---| | Fuel state | Liquid (CH₃OH/H₂O) | Compressed H₂ gas | | Energy density (system) | **~3300 Wh/kg** | ~600 Wh/kg (with tank) | | Operating T | 50–120 °C | 60–80 °C | | OCV (theoretical) | 1.21 V | 1.23 V | | Practical voltage at 0.2 A/cm² | **0.4–0.5 V** | 0.7–0.8 V | | Peak efficiency | 40 % | 50–60 % | | Crossover issue | Severe (methanol) | Negligible | | Catalyst cost (anode) | Pt-Ru | Pt | | Refuelling time | **< 1 minute** | 3–5 minutes | | CO₂ emission | Yes | None | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 DMFC Operating Principle (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Methanol + Water
Tank] --> B[Anode Flow Plate
Serpentine Channels] B --> C[Anode Catalyst Layer
Pt-Ru on Carbon] C --> D[Proton Exchange
Membrane Nafion] D --> E[Cathode Catalyst Layer
Pt on Carbon] E --> F[Cathode Flow Plate
Air Manifold] F --> G[External Load
DC Bus] G --> H[CO2 Vent
Anode Outlet] F --> I[Water + Heat
Cathode Outlet] C --> H classDef fuel fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,color:#000 classDef rxn fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,color:#000 classDef out fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#555,color:#000 class A fuel class B,C,D,E,F rxn class G,H,I out ``` ### 4.2 Reaction Pathway Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[CH3OH adsorbed on Pt] --> P2[Pt CH2OH] P2 --> P3[Pt COH] P3 --> P4[Pt CO intermediate] P4 --> P5{Ru OH present?} P5 -- Yes --> P6[Pt CO + Ru OH
CO2 + H+ + e-] P5 -- No --> P7[CO poisoning
Kinetic bottleneck] P6 --> P8[Final products
CO2, 6H+, 6e-] P7 --> P4 classDef start fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#daa520,color:#000 classDef good fill:#90ee90,stroke:#228b22,color:#000 classDef bad fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#dc143c,color:#000 class P1,P2,P3,P4 start class P5,P6,P8 good class P7 bad ``` ### 4.3 DMFC Performance Polarization Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Open Circuit Voltage
0.7 to 0.8 V typical] -->|Activation Loss| B[Knee Region
0.4 to 0.5 V] B -->|Ohmic Loss IR| C[Linear Region
Working Zone] C -->|Concentration Loss| D[Limiting Current
0.05 to 0.1 V] classDef ocv fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 classDef knee fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 classDef linear fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 classDef lim fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000 class A ocv class B knee class C linear class D lim ``` ### 4.4 System-Level Stack Assembly (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Stack[DMFC Stack Assembly N cells] direction TB B1[Bipolar Plate Anode] --> B2[Membrane Electrode Assembly MEA] B2 --> B3[Bipolar Plate Cathode] B3 --> B4[Membrane Electrode Assembly MEA] B4 --> B5[Bipolar Plate Cathode] end S1[Methanol Solution Reservoir] -->|Pump| B1 AIR[Air Blower] --> B3 AIR --> B5 B2 -->|CO2 vent| OUT1[Anode Exhaust] B4 -->|H2O + heat| OUT2[Cathode Exhaust] Stack --> CTRL[Power Conditioning Unit DC DC] CTRL --> LOAD[Battery or Load] classDef sub fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333,color:#000 class Stack sub ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Direct Methanol Fuel Cell. List any two advantages of using methanol as a fuel in PEM fuel cells.* **Model Answer:** A Direct Methanol Fuel Cell (DMFC) is a low-temperature PEM-type fuel cell that electrochemically oxidizes **liquid methanol (CH₃OH)** directly at the anode without prior reforming, producing electricity with water and CO₂ as by-products. **Two Advantages:** 1. Methanol is a **liquid at room temperature**, allowing easy storage, transport, and refuelling (energy density ≈ 6.09 kWh/kg) without the cryogenic or high-pressure requirements of hydrogen. 2. The system is **architecturally simple** — no reformer, no humidifier, no complex thermal management — enabling compact portable power sources. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Why is a Pt-Ru bimetallic catalyst used at the anode of a DMFC instead of pure Pt?* **Model Answer:** Pure Pt is rapidly **poisoned by CO intermediate species** generated during the multi-step methanol oxidation reaction (MOR). CO binds strongly to Pt active sites, blocking further adsorption. Ruthenium (Ru) undergoes reversible oxidation (Ru + H₂O → Ru–OH + H⁺ + e⁻) at lower potentials than Pt, supplying **–OH groups** that oxidatively strip CO from Pt as CO₂. The **bifunctional mechanism** in Pt-Ru thus enables continuous MOR activity. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice > **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B sub-question (a) and (b) carries 7 marks. Marks distribution within a 7-mark sub-question typically follows: 2 marks for key definitions/derivation setup, 3 marks for core calculation/explanation, 2 marks for diagram/justification. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the theoretical open-circuit voltage of a DMFC from fundamental thermodynamic principles, using ΔG = –nFE and the standard free energies of formation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Write the overall cell reaction (n = 6):* $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH (l)} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O (l)}$$ *Step 2 — Compute ΔG° using standard free energies of formation:* $$\Delta G^\circ = \sum \Delta G^\circ_f(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta G^\circ_f(\text{reactants})$$ $$\Delta G^\circ = [\Delta G^\circ_f(\text{CO}_2) + 2\Delta G^\circ_f(\text{H}_2\text{O (l)})] - [\Delta G^\circ_f(\text{CH}_3\text{OH (l)}) + \tfrac{3}{2}\Delta G^\circ_f(\text{O}_2)]$$ $$\Delta G^\circ = [(-394.4) + 2(-237.2)] - [(-166.4) + 0] = -702.4\;\text{kJ/mol}$$ *Step 3 — Apply the Faraday relation:* $$E^\circ = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = -\dfrac{(-702,400)}{(6)(96,485)} = +1.213\;\text{V}$$ **∴ Theoretical OCV ≈ 1.21 V** **(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **methanol crossover** in DMFCs. Discuss its effect on performance and any two methods to mitigate it. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Methanol Crossover Definition:* Methanol crossover is the **diffusive and electro-osmotic transport** of unreacted methanol from the anode side, *through* the Nafion membrane, to the cathode catalyst layer. Driven by the concentration gradient and water transport coupled with H⁺ flux. *Effects on Performance:* 1. **Mixed potential at cathode** — methanol reaching the cathode directly reacts with O₂, creating a parasitic short-circuit and reducing the ORR overpotential efficiency. 2. **Loss of fuel** — fuel that should have been oxidized at the anode is wasted. 3. **Cathode catalyst poisoning** — Pt cathode gets contaminated with CO intermediates. 4. **Reduced OCV** — typically OCV drops from theoretical 1.21 V to **0.7–0.8 V** due to crossover. *Two Mitigation Strategies:* 1. **Dilute Methanol Feed (1–3 M):** Reduces the concentration gradient driving crossover. Trade-off: lower energy density and larger water tank. 2. **Modified Membranes:** Use **modified Nafion** (e.g., Nafion/SiO₂ composite), **PBI membranes**, or **sulfonated polyimide** to reduce methanol permeability while maintaining proton conductivity. 3. *(Bonus)* **Barrier Layers:** Thin Pd, Pt, or carbon layers on the membrane anode-side block methanol transport. 4. *(Bonus)* **Higher Operating Temperature & Pressure:** Increases MOR kinetics relative to crossover rate. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Direct Methanol Fuel Cell**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Construction (refer to Section 4.4 diagram):* A DMFC single cell consists of: - **Anode end-plate** with methanol solution inlet and CO₂ outlet. - **Anode flow field plate** (graphite, serpentine channels) for distributing 1 M CH₃OH. - **Anode gas diffusion layer (GDL)** — carbon paper with PTFE. - **Anode catalyst layer** — Pt-Ru (50:50) nanoparticles on carbon black, loading ~2 mg/cm². - **Polymer Electrolyte Membrane** — Nafion®-115/117 (50–175 µm). - **Cathode catalyst layer** — Pt on carbon, loading ~2 mg/cm². - **Cathode GDL** — carbon paper. - **Cathode flow field plate** with air channels. - **Cathode end-plate** with air inlet and water outlet. *Working:* At the anode, methanol and water undergo MOR: $$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6\text{e}^-$$ H⁺ ions migrate through the proton-conducting membrane; electrons flow through the external circuit. At the cathode, ORR occurs: $$\tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6\text{e}^- \rightarrow 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The overall reaction is: $$2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **(b)** Compare DMFC with a **Reformed Methanol Fuel Cell (RMFC)**. Discuss the operating temperature, system efficiency, and start-up time differences. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | DMFC | RMFC | |---|---|---| | Reformer | None — direct feed | External reformer (steam reformer at 200–300 °C) converts CH₃OH + H₂O → 3H₂ + CO₂ | | Operating T | 50–120 °C (low) | 200–300 °C (reformer) + 80 °C (PEM stack) | | System Efficiency | **20–30 %** | **35–45 %** (higher due to better stack performance) | | Start-up Time | **< 5 minutes** (no warm-up) | 15–30 minutes (reformer heating required) | | CO₂ emission | Yes (at anode) | Yes (at reformer + anode) | | CO management | Catalyst poisoning issue | Reformate contains CO — needs water-gas shift + selective oxidation | | Complexity | Simple, compact | Complex (reformer, shift reactor, PROX, thermal management) | | Applications | Portable electronics, soldier power | Stationary power, CHP, vehicular APUs | | Fuel concentration | 1–3 M aqueous | Pure methanol vapour | | Energy density (system) | ~1000 Wh/kg | ~2200 Wh/kg | > **Conclusion:** DMFC trades lower system efficiency for **simplicity and rapid start-up**, making it ideal for portable applications. RMFC trades system simplicity for **higher efficiency**, suited to stationary and mobile auxiliary power. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do not confuse** the theoretical OCV of DMFC (1.21 V) with the *operating* voltage (0.4–0.5 V). Many students quote 1.21 V as the working voltage and lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **The 100 %+ efficiency anomaly** is a favourite KTU question. Always explain it using the LHV vs HHV distinction, **never** state it is a "violation of thermodynamics." > 3. In methanol crossover mitigation, **never recommend pure Pt as anode catalyst** — this is the most common conceptual error. > 4. For the n-value in ΔG = –nFE, **always use n = 6** (electrons per mole of CH₃OH), not n = 1 or 2. Forgetting this loses 1 mark. > 5. **PEM** in DMFC stands for **Proton Exchange Membrane**, *not* Polymer Electrolyte Membrane in some texts — both are accepted in KTU, but specify "sulfonated fluoropolymer" to be safe. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Quick Revision Checklist — Methanol Fuel Cells** - ✅ **DMFC** = Direct Methanol Fuel Cell, a liquid-fuel PEM-type cell operating at **50–120 °C**. - ✅ Overall reaction: $\;2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$ - ✅ Theoretical OCV = **1.21 V** (computed from $\Delta G^\circ = -702.4$ kJ/mol, n = 6). - ✅ Anode catalyst: **Pt-Ru bimetallic** (bifunctional mechanism against CO poisoning). - ✅ Cathode catalyst: **Pt** (ORR only). - ✅ Membrane: **Nafion®**, H⁺ conducting. - ✅ Energy density of methanol: **6.09 kWh/kg (LHV)**. - ✅ **Methanol crossover** is the central performance-limiting phenomenon. - ✅ Practical cell voltage: **0.4–0.5 V**; practical efficiency: **20–40 %**. - ✅ Anode half-reaction: $\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6\text{e}^-$ - ✅ Cathode half-reaction: $\tfrac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6\text{e}^- \rightarrow 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$ - ✅ **RMFC vs DMFC:** RMFC uses external reformer (higher efficiency, slower start-up); DMFC is reformer-less (simpler, faster start-up). - ✅ **The 100 %+ thermodynamic efficiency** anomaly: due to using LHV of methanol; physically valid and arises because liquid water product recovers latent heat. - ✅ **CO₂ vent** is at the anode; **water + heat** are removed at the cathode. - ✅ **Dilute methanol feed** (1–3 M) is standard practice to limit crossover. - ✅ **Stack power formula:** $P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{cell}} \times I \times N_{\text{cells}}$

topics.title: Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cells # Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cells (PAFC) ## 1. Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC)** is a type of acid-based fuel cell that uses **liquid concentrated phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄, ~100 wt%)** immobilized in a porous silicon carbide (SiC) matrix as the electrolyte, operating at intermediate temperatures of **150–220 °C** (typically ~200 °C). It is the **first commercially available fuel cell technology** and the most mature member of the molten/solid acid fuel cell family. ### KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights - PAFC is classified as a **mid-temperature acid electrolyte fuel cell**. - Charge carrier is the **proton (H⁺)** — ionic conduction through H⁺ hopping between H₃PO₄ molecules (Grotthuss mechanism). - Falls under the **1st Generation fuel cell** category for stationary power generation. --- ## 2. Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a PAFC as a "warm lemonade battery." In a lemonade battery, two metals sit in lemon juice (acid) and generate electricity. In a PAFC, hydrogen and oxygen sit on either side of a hot phosphoric-acid-soaked sponge and generate electricity + water — but instead of cold lemons, the acid works best when warmed up to ~200 °C, which makes it conduct protons (H⁺) easily without flooding the electrodes. The "sponge" is a porous silicon-carbide wafer that holds the acid in place, while the metal "strips" are replaced by **porous carbon papers coated with platinum black**, the catalyst that helps hydrogen split into protons and electrons at the anode. --- ## 3. Why Phosphoric Acid? — Justification of Choice | Property | Value/Description | | :--- | :--- | | Acid strength | Weak acid — does not strongly adsorb on Pt catalyst (unlike H₂SO₄) | | Thermal stability | Stable up to ~220 °C without decomposition | | CO₂ tolerance | Stable in presence of CO₂ (carbonate formation minimal) | | CO tolerance | Better than PEMFC — operates with ~1–2 % CO at 200 °C | | Ionic conductivity | ~0.1 S/cm at 200 °C (proton hopping) | | Cost | Cheaper than Nafion™ (PEMFC membrane) | --- ## 4. Position in the Fuel Cell Family $$T_{op}: \text{AFC} \approx 80°C \ < \ \text{PEMFC} \approx 80°C \ < \ \text{PAFC} \approx 200°C \ < \ \text{MCFC} \approx 650°C \ < \ \text{SOFC} \approx 1000°C$$ PAFC occupies the **"intermediate temperature"** niche — high enough to tolerate CO impurities and produce useful heat, but low enough to avoid the molten-carbonate corrosion problems of MCFCs. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 1. Cell Architecture — Component-by-Component Breakdown > [!IMPORTANT] > A PAFC single cell consists of **seven layers**, often called the **MEA + hardware stack**: > (1) Anode current collector (graphite plate with flow channels) → > (2) Anode catalyst layer (Pt or Pt-Ru on carbon black) → > (3) Anode gas diffusion layer (porous carbon paper, teflonated) → > (4) **Electrolyte matrix** (SiC wetted with 100 % H₃PO₄) → > (5) Cathode gas diffusion layer → > (6) Cathode catalyst layer → > (7) Cathode current collector. ### The Electrolyte Matrix — The Heart of PAFC - Material: **Silicon Carbide (SiC) powder**, particle size ~1 µm, porosity ~50 %. - Function: Holds ~100 % H₃PO₄ by capillary action; prevents crossover of H₂ and O₂. - Thickness: ~0.1–0.3 mm. ### Electrodes - **Substrate**: Porous carbon paper or graphite felt, teflonated (hydrophobic) on gas side. - **Catalyst**: Pt black (highly loaded — **0.3–0.5 mg/cm²**) for both anode and cathode; Pt-Co alloys improve cathode performance. - Catalyst support: Vulcan XC-72 carbon black. --- ## 2. Electrode Half-Reactions and Overall Reaction **Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction — HOR):** $$H_2 \longrightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad E^0_{anode} = 0.00\ V\ vs.\ SHE$$ **Cathode (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR):** $$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow H_2O_{(l)} \quad E^0_{cathode} = +1.229\ V\ vs.\ SHE$$ **Overall Cell Reaction:** $$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O_{(l)} \quad E^0_{cell} = 1.229\ V$$ > [!NOTE] > Water is produced at the **cathode** in PAFC (unlike alkaline fuel cells, where water forms at the anode). This prevents anode flooding and allows reactant streams to be managed independently. --- ## 3. Thermodynamic Quantities at 200 °C (473 K) For liquid water product at standard conditions (25 °C): | Quantity | Symbol | Value at 25 °C | Value at 200 °C | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Enthalpy of reaction | ΔH | −285.83 kJ/mol | −241.8 kJ/mol | | Gibbs free energy | ΔG | −237.2 kJ/mol | −220.4 kJ/mol | | Entropy change | ΔS | −163.3 J/(mol·K) | −45.1 J/(mol·K) | | Reversible voltage (ΔG/nF) | E° | 1.229 V | 1.142 V | | HHV-based efficiency limit | η_max | 83 % | 91 % | > [!IMPORTANT] > At higher operating temperature, ΔS becomes **less negative**, which raises the ratio ΔG/ΔH and therefore **increases the theoretical thermodynamic efficiency**. This is a key KTU point: PAFC can theoretically exceed 80 % LHV efficiency at 200 °C. --- ## 4. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Law | Expression | Variables & Units | Application | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Gibbs Reversible Voltage | $E_{rev} = \dfrac{-\Delta G}{nF}$ | $n$ = electrons transferred, $F$ = 96485 C/mol | Theoretical OCV of any H₂/O₂ cell | | 2 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$ | $p$ = partial pressure (atm) | Effect of pressure on cell EMF | | 3 | Thermal Neutral Voltage | $E_{th} = \dfrac{-\Delta H}{nF}$ | ΔH in J/mol | Higher-heating-value reference line | | 4 | Voltage Efficiency | $\eta_V = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}}$ | Dimensionless | Loss due to activation, ohmic, concentration | | 5 | Faraday Efficiency | $\eta_F = \dfrac{I_{actual}}{I_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{2F \cdot \dot{n}_{H_2,used}}{I}$ | Dimensionless | Quantifies fuel crossover | | 6 | Total Electrical Efficiency | $\eta_{el} = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_V \cdot \eta_F$ | Product of three | Practical system efficiency | | 7 | Heat Generated (Reversible) | $q_{rev} = T\Delta S$ | W per mole of H₂ | Co-generation (CHP) opportunity | | 8 | Current Density | $j = \dfrac{I}{A_{cell}}$ | A/cm² | Standardizes cell performance | | 9 | Power Density | $P_d = V_{cell} \cdot j$ | W/cm² | Engineering metric for stack design | | 10 | Butler–Volmer (approx.) | $j = j_0 \left[ e^{\alpha \eta_a F/RT} - e^{-(1-\alpha)\eta_a F/RT} \right]$ | Exchange current density $j_0$, transfer coeff. α | Activation overpotential modelling | | 11 | CO Coverage (Temkin) | $\theta_{CO} = \dfrac{K(T)\, p_{CO}}{1 + K(T)\, p_{CO}}$ | CO partial pressure | Explains CO poisoning at Pt | > [!WARNING] > Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside markdown tables — use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ instead, otherwise the row breaks. --- ## 5. Practical Operating Parameters of a Commercial PAFC | Parameter | Typical Value | | :--- | :--- | | Operating temperature | **180–210 °C** | | Operating pressure | 1–8 atm (atmospheric to pressurized) | | Open-circuit voltage | ~1.0 V per cell | | Practical operating voltage | **0.6–0.7 V** per cell | | Current density (design point) | 150–300 mA/cm² | | Pt loading | 0.3–0.5 mg/cm² (anode & cathode) | | Electrical efficiency | **36–42 %** LHV | | Total CHP efficiency | **70–85 %** LHV | | Stack lifetime | **> 40,000 hours** demonstrated | | Voltage degradation rate | ~2–5 µV/h | --- ## 6. Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Combined Heat & Power (CHP):** PAFC cogeneration plants supply electricity + 60–80 °C hot water to hospitals, hotels, and industrial parks. 2. **Distributed Generation:** 200 kW PC-25™ units (UTC Fuel Cells) deployed at over 200 sites worldwide. 3. **Bridge technology** between low-temperature PEMFC and high-temperature MCFC/SOFC. 4. **Tolerant to reformate hydrogen** from natural gas, methanol, or biogas — robust against ~10 ppm H₂S and ~1–2 % CO. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 1. Derivation of the Reversible Cell Voltage from Thermodynamics The Gibbs free energy of formation of liquid water at 25 °C (298 K) is: $$\Delta G^\circ_f (H_2O_{(l)}) = -237.2 \ \text{kJ/mol}$$ For the overall PAFC reaction: $$H_{2(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2} O_{2(g)} \longrightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$$ The maximum (reversible) electrical work extractable per mole of H₂ consumed equals the negative of the Gibbs free energy change: $$W_{rev} = -\Delta G_{rxn} = 237.2 \ \text{kJ/mol of}\ H_2$$ This electrical work is delivered at the cell terminals. Since the cell voltage $E_{rev}$ represents work per unit charge, and one mole of H₂ transfers $n = 2$ moles of electrons, each carrying charge $F$ (Faraday constant): $$W_{rev} = nFE_{rev}$$ Therefore: $$E_{rev} = \dfrac{W_{rev}}{nF} = \dfrac{-\Delta G_{rxn}}{nF}$$ ### Step-by-step numerical substitution: **Step 1:** Identify the constants and known values. - $\Delta G^\circ_{rxn} = -237200 \ \text{J/mol}$ - $n = 2$ electrons per H₂ molecule - $F = 96485 \ \text{C/mol}$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the formula. $$\begin{aligned} E_{rev} &= \dfrac{-\left(-237200\right)}{(2)\,(96485)} \\ &= \dfrac{237200}{192970} \\ &= 1.229 \ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Interpret. This is the **theoretical open-circuit voltage (OCV)** of any H₂/O₂ fuel cell at 25 °C, 1 atm. For PAFC at 200 °C (473 K), the value reduces to: $$E_{rev}(473\,K) = \dfrac{220400}{(2)(96485)} = 1.142 \ \text{V}$$ --- ## 2. Derivation of the Nernst Equation for PAFC Starting from the fundamental relation between Gibbs free energy and electrochemical potential: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. Substituting $-nFE = \Delta G$ and $-nFE^\circ = \Delta G^\circ$: $$\begin{aligned} -nFE &= -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q \\ E &= E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q \end{aligned}$$ For the PAFC reaction $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \to H_2O_{(l)}$, activity of liquid water = 1, so: $$Q = \dfrac{1}{p_{H_2}\cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$ Substituting and inverting the logarithm sign: $$\boxed{\,E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(p_{H_2}\cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)\,}$$ ### Numerical Example (KTU-style problem) A PAFC operates at 200 °C with $p_{H_2} = 3.5$ atm and $p_{O_2} = 4.0$ atm. Calculate the reversible cell EMF. **Step 1:** Use $E^\circ$ at 200 °C = 1.142 V. **Step 2:** $n = 2$, $T = 473$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). $$\begin{aligned} E &= 1.142 + \dfrac{(8.314)(473)}{(2)(96485)} \ln\!\left(3.5 \cdot 4.0^{1/2}\right) \\ &= 1.142 + \dfrac{3933}{192970}\ln(3.5 \cdot 2.0) \\ &= 1.142 + (0.02039)\ln(7.0) \\ &= 1.142 + (0.02039)(1.9459) \\ &= 1.142 + 0.0397 \\ &= 1.182 \ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > Increasing both reactant partial pressures **above 1 atm raises the OCV** logarithmically — this is why pressurized PAFC stacks (4–8 atm) achieve higher power density. --- ## 3. Derivation of Thermodynamic (HHV) Efficiency The **maximum theoretical efficiency** based on the higher heating value: $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{-\Delta G^\circ}{-\Delta H^\circ} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ}$$ At 25 °C, liquid water product: $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{th} &= \dfrac{237.2}{285.83} \\ &= 0.8298 \\ &= 82.98\ \% \end{aligned}$$ At 200 °C (vapour-phase water): $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{220.4}{241.8} = 91.1\ \%$$ This shows the **temperature advantage** of PAFC. --- ## 4. Voltage Loss Analysis — The Polarization Curve The actual operating cell voltage is always less than $E_{rev}$ due to three irreversibilities: $$V_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$$ ### Activation Overpotential ($\eta_{act}$) Governed by the **Butler–Volmer equation** at high overpotential (Tafel approximation): $$\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{j}{j_0}\right)$$ Where $j_0$ is the **exchange current density** (≈ 10⁻³ A/cm² for HOR, ≈ 10⁻⁹ A/cm² for ORR on Pt). The ORR kinetics dominate losses. ### Ohmic Overpotential ($\eta_{ohm}$) From electrolyte and contact resistance (Ohm's law): $$\eta_{ohm} = j \cdot R_{ASR}$$ $R_{ASR}$ (area-specific resistance) for PAFC electrolyte at 200 °C ≈ 0.2–0.3 Ω·cm². ### Concentration Overpotential ($\eta_{conc}$) At high current density, reactant mass-transport becomes limiting: $$\eta_{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{j}{j_L}\right)$$ where $j_L$ is the limiting current density (~1 A/cm² for PAFC). ### Worked-out example For $j = 200$ mA/cm², $\alpha = 0.5$, $j_0 = 10^{-4}$ A/cm², $T = 473$ K: $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{act} &= \dfrac{(8.314)(473)}{(0.5)(2)(96485)}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.2}{10^{-4}}\right) \\ &= \dfrac{3933}{96485}\ln(2000) \\ &= (0.0408)(7.601) \\ &= 0.310\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$ $$\eta_{ohm} = (0.2)(0.25) = 0.050\ \text{V}$$ Therefore the practical cell voltage: $$V_{cell} = 1.142 - 0.310 - 0.050 - 0.020 = 0.762\ \text{V}$$ which agrees with experimental PAFC operating values of **0.6–0.75 V** at 200 mA/cm². --- ## 5. CO Poisoning — Why PAFC Tolerates More CO than PEMFC Adsorption of CO on Pt catalyst follows **Temkin isotherm**: $$\theta_{CO} = \dfrac{K(T)\,p_{CO}}{1 + K(T)\,p_{CO}}$$ The adsorption constant $K(T)$ obeys van't Hoff: $$K(T) = K_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H_{ads}}{RT}\right)$$ Because $\Delta H_{ads}$ for CO-Pt is **negative** (exothermic adsorption), $K(T)$ **decreases** with rising temperature. Hence at 80 °C (PEMFC), $\theta_{CO}$ is high (> 0.7 at 100 ppm CO → severe poisoning), but at 200 °C (PAFC), $\theta_{CO}$ drops to < 0.2 → tolerance up to ~1–2 % CO in feed gas. > [!IMPORTANT] > This **thermodynamic desorption** is the single most important reason why PAFC is preferred for **reformate-hydrogen** applications where trace CO cannot be fully removed. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 1. PAFC Single-Cell Architecture (Layer-by-Layer Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Hydrogen Inlet
Anode Flow Channels"] --> B["Anode Current Collector
Impregnated Graphite Plate"] B --> C["Anode GDL
Carbon Paper PTFE treated"] C --> D["Anode Catalyst
Pt on Vulcan XC 72"] D --> E["Electrolyte Matrix
SiC + 100 percent H3PO4"] E --> F["Cathode Catalyst
Pt or Pt-Co on Vulcan XC 72"] F --> G["Cathode GDL
Carbon Paper PTFE treated"] G --> H["Cathode Current Collector
Impregnated Graphite Plate"] H --> I["Air Oxygen Inlet
Cathode Flow Channels"] J["H plus ions migrate
through H3PO4 matrix"] -.-> E K["Electrons flow through
external load"] -.-> B K -.-> H L["Water Product
expelled at cathode"] --> M["Cathode Exhaust"] N["Heat 200 C removed by
cooling plates"] --> O["CHP Hot Water Output"] ``` --- ## 2. Charge and Mass Transport Pathway ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["H2 gas
at anode"] --> Q1["Dissociative adsorption
on Pt catalyst"] Q1 --> R1["H2 splits to 2H atoms
H atom loses electron"] R1 --> S1["Electron enters
external circuit"] R1 --> T1["H plus ion hops through
H3PO4 via Grotthuss"] T1 --> U1["H plus reaches
cathode catalyst"] S1 --> V1["External load
does electrical work"] V1 --> S2["Electron returns to
cathode"] S2 --> U1 P2["O2 gas
at cathode"] --> U1 U1 --> W1["Oxygen combines with
H plus and electrons"] W1 --> X1["Water molecule formed
at cathode"] X1 --> Y1["Evaporated by heat
or expelled as liquid"] ``` --- ## 3. Polarization Curve of PAFC ```mermaid graph LR A1["Voltage V cell"] --> B1["Activation Loss Region
Steep drop near OCV"] B1 --> C1["Ohmic Loss Region
Linear middle section"] C1 --> D1["Concentration Loss Region
Sharp drop at high j"] D1 --> E1["Limiting Current Density jL"] A2["Erev equals 1.142 V
at 200 C"] --> B1 A3["Operating point j equals 200 to 300 mA per cm2
V cell equals 0.6 to 0.7 V"] --> C1 ``` --- ## 4. Comparative Fuel Cell Operating Window ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Hydrogen Fuel Cell Family"] --> B["Low Temperature
60 to 100 C"] A --> C["Intermediate Temperature
150 to 250 C"] A --> D["High Temperature
600 to 1000 C"] B --> B1["AFC Alkaline
KOH electrolyte"] B --> B2["PEMFC Proton Exchange
Nafion membrane"] C --> C1["PAFC Phosphoric Acid
H3PO4 in SiC"] C --> C2["DMFC Direct Methanol
Liquid methanol feed"] D --> D1["MCFC Molten Carbonate
Li2CO3 K2CO3"] D --> D2["SOFC Solid Oxide
YSZ electrolyte"] ``` --- ## 5. PAFC Stack Module — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["Fuel Processor
Natural gas steam reformer"] --> S2["CO Shift Converter
