Reduces CO to less than 2 percent"] S2 --> S3["Desulfurizer
ZnO bed removes H2S"] S3 --> S4["Anode Inlet Manifold
Distributes H2 to cells"] S5["Air Blower
Compressed Air Supply"] --> S6["Cathode Inlet Manifold"] S4 --> S7["Fuel Cell Stack
50 to 200 cells in series"] S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8["DC AC Inverter
Converts DC to AC"] S8 --> S9["Power Conditioning Unit
50 or 60 Hz AC Output"] S7 --> S10["Heat Exchanger
Recovered heat to CHP"] S10 --> S11["Thermal Storage and
District Heating"] S7 --> S12["Water Condenser
Cathode exhaust"] S12 --> S13["Water Purification
Potable water output"] ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **"Why is phosphoric acid preferred over sulfuric acid as an electrolyte in fuel cells operating above 150 °C?"** **Model Answer:** > 1. Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) is a **weak acid** — its anions (H₂PO₄⁻) do not strongly adsorb on the Pt catalyst, leaving active sites free for H₂ oxidation (1 mark). > 2. H₂SO₄ is a **strong acid**; the bisulfate anion (HSO₄⁻) strongly adsorbs onto Pt, blocking catalytic sites and causing high activation overpotential (1 mark). > 3. Concentrated H₃PO₄ is thermally stable up to ~220 °C without decomposition, whereas H₂SO₄ corrodes materials and dehydrates above 150 °C (1 mark). ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]** **"State the role of the silicon carbide (SiC) matrix in a PAFC electrolyte layer."** **Model Answer:** > 1. The SiC powder (porosity ~50 %, particle size ~1 µm) acts as a **porous reservoir** that retains ~100 wt% liquid H₃PO₄ by capillary action (1 mark). > 2. It serves as a **physical barrier** preventing direct mixing (crossover) of H₂ and O₂ gas streams, while allowing proton (H⁺) conduction through the wetted pores (1 mark). > 3. It provides **mechanical strength** to the thin (~0.1–0.3 mm) electrolyte layer and electrical insulation between anode and cathode (1 mark). --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – KTU Model Paper 4A]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell. State the half-cell and overall reactions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A PAFC operates at 200 °C and 5 atm total pressure with a gas composition of 70 % H₂, 28 % H₂O, 2 % CO₂ on the anode side and 18 % O₂, 79 % N₂, 3 % H₂O on the cathode side. Calculate the open-circuit voltage using the Nernst equation. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Construction (3 Marks):** A PAFC single cell comprises seven layers: 1. **Anode and cathode current collectors**: Impregnated graphite plates machined with flow channels for gas distribution. 2. **Gas diffusion layers (GDLs)**: Porous carbon paper, PTFE-treated to prevent flooding. 3. **Catalyst layers**: Pt (or Pt-Co alloy) nanoparticles on Vulcan XC-72 carbon black; loading 0.3–0.5 mg Pt/cm². 4. **Electrolyte matrix**: SiC powder wetted with 100 wt% H₃PO₄; thickness 0.1–0.3 mm. **Working Principle (3 Marks):** - H₂ flows through anode channels, diffuses through GDL, and is oxidized at Pt catalyst → releasing protons and electrons. - Protons hop through the H₃PO₄ matrix (Grotthuss mechanism) toward the cathode. - Electrons travel via the external circuit, doing electrical work. - At the cathode, O₂ combines with H⁺ and electrons → forms H₂O (cathode product). - Waste heat is removed by cooling plates embedded in the stack. **Reactions (1 Mark):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Anode:} \quad & H_2 \longrightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- \\ \text{Cathode:} \quad & \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow H_2O_{(l)} \\ \text{Overall:} \quad & H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O_{(l)}, \quad \Delta G^\circ = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1: Determine partial pressures at 5 atm total pressure.** $$\begin{aligned} p_{H_2} &= 0.70 \times 5 = 3.50 \ \text{atm} \\ p_{O_2} &= 0.18 \times 5 = 0.90 \ \text{atm} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Use the Nernst equation for PAFC.** $$E = E^\circ(473K) + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$$ **Step 3: Substitute values.** - $E^\circ(473 K) = 1.142$ V (from thermodynamic tables). - $T = 473$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $n = 2$, $F = 96485$ C/mol. $$\begin{aligned} E &= 1.142 + \dfrac{(8.314)(473)}{(2)(96485)} \ln\!\left(3.50 \cdot \sqrt{0.90}\right) \\ &= 1.142 + (0.02039)\ln(3.50 \cdot 0.9487) \\ &= 1.142 + (0.02039)\ln(3.320) \\ &= 1.142 + (0.02039)(1.2006) \\ &= 1.142 + 0.0245 \\ &= 1.1665 \ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** The open-circuit voltage ≈ **1.167 V** per cell. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – KTU Model Paper 4B]** **(a)** Discuss the thermodynamic efficiency of a PAFC at 200 °C, comparing it with a PEMFC at 80 °C. Explain why the higher operating temperature improves the theoretical efficiency. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 200 kW PAFC stack operates at an average cell voltage of 0.70 V, current density of 250 mA/cm², and 36 % electrical (LHV) efficiency. Calculate: (i) total active cell area required, (ii) number of cells if each cell is 1000 cm², (iii) volume efficiency of the stack assuming average power density 0.175 W/cm² and stack volume 2.0 m³. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Theoretical Efficiency Equation (2 Marks):** $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ}$$ **Numerical Values (2 Marks):** - **PAFC at 200 °C:** $\Delta G^\circ = 220.4$ kJ/mol, $\Delta H^\circ = 241.8$ kJ/mol → $\eta_{th} = 91.1\%$. - **PEMFC at 80 °C:** $\Delta G^\circ = 226.5$ kJ/mol, $\Delta H^\circ = 242.0$ kJ/mol → $\eta_{th} = 93.6\%$. > Wait — actually the *reverse* is true. Let me correct this clearly: **Correct Comparison (2 Marks):** - **PAFC at 200 °C:** $\eta_{th} = 220.4 / 241.8 = 91.1\%$. - **PEMFC at 80 °C:** $\eta_{th} \approx 226.5 / 242.0 = 93.6\%$. So the **PEMFC has marginally higher theoretical efficiency** because at lower temperature $\Delta S$ is more negative, meaning more free energy per unit enthalpy is available. **However**, the practical efficiency of PAFC is comparable because higher temperature gives faster ORR kinetics, lower Pt loading requirements, and better heat utilization (CHP). **Temperature Effect on $\Delta S$ (1 Mark):** Because $\Delta S = (\Delta H - \Delta G)/T$, increasing T **reduces** $\vert \Delta S \vert$ → raises $\Delta G / \Delta H$ ratio → higher Carnot-equivalent efficiency. (The full LHV limit of 100 % is approached as $T \to \infty$.) --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Total Active Cell Area:** **Step 1:** Total power = 200 kW = 200,000 W. **Step 2:** Power density $P_d = V_{cell} \cdot j = 0.70 \times 0.250 = 0.175$ W/cm². **Step 3:** Total area: $$A_{total} = \dfrac{P}{P_d} = \dfrac{200000}{0.175} = 1{,}142{,}857 \ \text{cm}^2 \approx 114.3 \ \text{m}^2$$ **(ii) Number of Cells:** $$N = \dfrac{A_{total}}{A_{cell}} = \dfrac{1{,}142{,}857}{1000} = 1143 \ \text{cells}$$ A practical stack uses ~50–250 cells; hence **~5 parallel stacks of ~230 cells each** or a single tall stack with multiple repeats. **(iii) Volume Efficiency:** $$\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Power Output}}{\text{Stack Volume}} = \dfrac{200{,}000 \ \text{W}}{2.0 \ \text{m}^3} = 100 \ \text{kW/m}^3$$ Equivalent volumetric power density. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PAFC Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting units of R:** Use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), not cal or kJ. −0.5 mark. > 2. **Mixing ΔG° and ΔH°:** Theoretical efficiency is $\Delta G / \Delta H$ (not the reverse). −1 mark. > 3. **Confusing PEMFC and PAFC temperatures:** Always state the operating T and look up the correct $E^\circ$ — do **not** blindly use 1.229 V. −0.5 mark. > 4. **Missing the cathode product:** PAFC produces water at the **cathode**, not anode. −0.5 mark. > 5. **Using liquid-water ΔH for vapour-phase calculations:** At 200 °C water is vapour; use ΔH°(g) = 241.8 kJ/mol, not 285.8 kJ/mol. −1 mark. > 6. **Pressure units:** Nernst requires partial pressure in **atm**, not bar or kPa. −0.5 mark. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cells** - **Electrolyte:** 100 wt% liquid H₃PO₄ retained in porous SiC matrix. - **Operating temperature:** 150–220 °C (intermediate). - **Charge carrier:** H⁺ (proton) via Grotthuss hopping mechanism. - **Catalyst:** Pt (or Pt-Co alloy) on carbon black; loading 0.3–0.5 mg/cm². - **Anode reaction:** $H_2 \to 2H^+ + 2e^-$; **Cathode reaction:** $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \to H_2O$. - **Overall reaction:** $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to H_2O_{(l)}$. - **Theoretical OCV (25 °C):** 1.229 V; **at 200 °C:** 1.142 V. - **Practical operating voltage:** 0.6–0.7 V per cell. - **Nernst equation:** $E = E^\circ + (RT/nF)\ln(p_{H_2}\cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2})$. - **Theoretical efficiency (HHV, 200 °C):** ~91 %; **practical electrical efficiency:** 36–42 % LHV. - **CHP efficiency:** 70–85 % LHV (with heat recovery). - **Key advantage:** Tolerates ~1–2 % CO and ~10 ppm H₂S — ideal for reformate H₂. - **Key disadvantage:** Low power density, expensive Pt catalyst, slow ORR kinetics. - **Water management:** Product water forms at cathode → no anode flooding issue. - **Commercial examples:** UTC PC-25™ (200 kW), Toshiba pure H₂ PAFC (200 kW class). - **Application focus:** Stationary CHP units for hospitals, hotels, data centers. - **Lifetime demonstrated:** > 40,000 hours; degradation ~2–5 µV/h. - **First-generation fuel cell technology:** Earliest commercialized (since early 1990s). - **Why "intermediate" temperature matters:** High enough for CO tolerance & useful heat, low enough to avoid molten-carbonate corrosion issues of MCFC.

topics.title: Molten Carbonate Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Molten Carbonate Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC)** is a high-temperature, second-generation fuel cell that operates in the range of **600 °C to 700 °C** and uses a **molten mixture of alkali metal carbonates** (typically Li₂CO₃–K₂CO₃ or Li₂CO₃–Na₂CO₃) suspended in a porous ceramic **LiAlO₂ matrix** as its electrolyte. The mobile charge carrier is the **carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻)**, which migrates from the cathode to the anode, making it the only fuel cell type in which **CO₂ must be supplied continuously to the cathode inlet** and is **released at the anode exit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** MCFCs are classified as a **"high-temperature molten-salt electrolyte fuel cell"** and are positioned in the syllabus as the bridge between low-temperature (PEMFC/AFC) and ultra-high-temperature (SOFC) systems, because they allow **internal reforming of hydrocarbons** (especially natural gas / methane) due to their elevated operating temperature.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Salt-Bath Stove"

Imagine a kitchen stove where instead of water (as in PEMFCs), the cooking medium is **molten salt (alkaline carbonates)** kept liquid by a steady fire (**600 °C heat**). The hydrogen "ingredient" enters one side of the stove (anode) and the "air ingredient" (oxygen + a pinch of CO₂) enters the other side (cathode). The molten salt carries a "flavor packet" (the **CO₃²⁻ ion**) from the air side to the fuel side. As the packet meets the hydrogen, the chemical reaction releases **electricity, water, and CO₂**, very much like a stove producing both heat and a useful by-product. The leftover CO₂ at the fuel side is **recycled back** to the air side, completing the salt's round trip — exactly as a salt bath circulates in an industrial furnace.

## 1.3 Key Operating Metrics

| Parameter | Typical MCFC Value | KTU Board Emphasis |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **600 – 700 °C** | High-T band |
| Electrical Efficiency | **50 – 60 %** (LHV) | Direct |
| CHP Efficiency | **70 – 85 %** | Cogeneration |
| Open-Circuit Voltage | **~1.04 V** (at 650 °C) | Per cell |
| Operating Cell Voltage | **0.7 – 0.85 V** | Practical |
| Power Density | **0.1 – 0.2 W/cm²** | Moderate |
| Charge Carrier | **CO₃²⁻** | Unique identifier |
| Fuel Flexibility | H₂, CO, CH₄, syngas | Internal reforming |

> [!NOTE]
> **CO₂ Recycling Loop is the signature feature of MCFCs.** No other commercial fuel cell technology requires continuous CO₂ supply at the cathode. Examiners specifically test this concept.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Air + CO₂ enter the cathode.** Oxygen from air combines with the recycled CO₂ and the incoming electrons from the external circuit to form the carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻) inside the porous NiO cathode.
- **Step 2 — Molten salt conducts CO₃²⁻.** Driven by the concentration gradient and the electric field, the CO₃²⁻ ion migrates through the hot, liquid carbonates in the LiAlO₂ matrix from cathode to anode.
- **Step 3 — Fuel is oxidized at the anode.** At the Ni-based porous anode, hydrogen (or CO, or methane via internal reforming) reacts with CO₃²⁻, producing H₂O, CO₂, and releasing 2 electrons per fuel molecule.
- **Step 4 — Electrons do external work.** The released electrons travel through the external load, producing DC electricity, and return to the cathode to close the circuit.
- **Step 5 — CO₂ is recycled.** The CO₂-rich anode exhaust is split: a portion is recycled to the cathode inlet, while the rest (along with the depleted air) is sent to a heat-recovery steam generator for **CHP (Combined Heat and Power)** applications.

## 2.2 Electrode Half-Cell Reactions

**Cathode (oxidation of O₂ + CO₂ reduction):**

$$\frac{1}{2}O_{2} + CO_{2} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow CO_{3}^{2-}$$

**Anode (oxidation of H₂ by CO₃²⁻):**

$$H_{2} + CO_{3}^{2-} \rightarrow H_{2}O + CO_{2} + 2e^{-}$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**

$$H_{2} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2} + CO_{2\,(cathode)} \rightarrow H_{2}O + CO_{2\,(anode)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that CO₂ is **consumed at the cathode** and **produced at the anode** in stoichiometrically equal amounts, which is why a CO₂ recycle loop is **mandatory** for sustained operation.

## 2.3 Why is MCFC operated at 600–700 °C? — The "Why" behind the "How"

- **Ionic Conductivity:** Alkali carbonates become appreciably ionically conductive **only above ~500 °C** (molten state). At 650 °C, conductivity reaches **~1 S/cm**, comparable to aqueous KOH.
- **Internal Reforming:** The high temperature enables the endothermic reforming of CH₄ to occur *inside* the cell stack using the cell's own waste heat, eliminating the need for an external reformer.
- **CO Tolerance:** Platinum-free Ni anodes tolerate hundreds of ppm of CO; in fact, CO is electrochemically oxidized in MCFC.
- **Reaction Kinetics:** Non-precious metal catalysts (Ni, NiO) work efficiently at this temperature, drastically reducing system cost compared to PEMFC.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^{0}(T) = -\dfrac{\Delta G_{f}^{0}(T)}{nF}$ | Standard reversible voltage from Gibbs free energy | V |
| 2 | $E^{0}_{H_{2}/O_{2},25^{\circ}C} = \mathbf{1.229\ V}$ | Standard cell potential at 25 °C (HHV basis) | V |
| 3 | $E^{0}_{MCFC,\,650^{\circ}C} \approx \mathbf{1.04\ V}$ | Reversible voltage at operating temperature | V |
| 4 | $E = E^{0}(T) + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{1/2}\,P_{CO_{2},c}}{P_{H_{2}O,a}\,P_{CO_{2},a}}\right]$ | **Nernst equation for MCFC** | V |
| 5 | $E_{th} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{nF}$ | Thermoneutral voltage (for H₂/O₂ = **1.481 V**) | V |
| 6 | $\eta_{elec} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{th}} \times 100\%$ | Electrical efficiency from voltage ratio | % |
| 7 | $\eta_{LHV} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.83$ | Reversible LHV efficiency of H₂/O₂ | – |
| 8 | $\eta_{CHP} = \eta_{elec} + \eta_{th}$ | Combined Heat & Power efficiency | % |
| 9 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{c}}{T_{h}}$ | Carnot reference at $T_{h}$=923 K, $T_{c}$=298 K ≈ **0.677** | – |
| 10 | $\sigma_{CO_{3}^{2-}}(T) \approx 0.5 - 2.0\ \text{S/cm}$ at 650 °C | Ionic conductivity of molten carbonate | S/cm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard constants used in KTU derivations:** $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $F = 96\,485\ \text{C/mol}$, $n = 2$ for H₂ oxidation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Utility-scale stationary power (200 kW – 50 MW)** for hospitals, data centres, and industrial parks.
- **Natural-gas-fuelled distributed generation** using on-site internal reforming — eliminates the need for hydrogen transport infrastructure.
- **Carbon-capture-ready designs:** the concentrated CO₂ at the anode exit simplifies CO₂ sequestration (a hot topic in 2024 KTU electives).
- Acts as the **commercial benchmark** for molten-salt electrolyte research and is a stepping stone to **Direct Carbon Fuel Cells (DCFC)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation for MCFC

Starting from the **overall cell reaction** at standard state and writing the general Nernst expression:

$$H_{2} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2} + CO_{2,c} \rightleftharpoons H_{2}O + CO_{2,a}$$

For a generic reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$, the Nernst equation is:

$$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q \quad ; \quad Q = \frac{a_{C}^{c}\,a_{D}^{d}}{a_{A}^{a}\,a_{B}^{b}}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the reaction quotient Q for MCFC:**

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_{2}O}\cdot a_{CO_{2},a}}{a_{H_{2}}\cdot a_{O_{2}}^{1/2}\cdot a_{CO_{2},c}}$$

For gas-phase species at moderate pressure, activities are replaced by partial pressures (in atm):

$$Q = \frac{P_{H_{2}O}\cdot P_{CO_{2},a}}{P_{H_{2}}\cdot P_{O_{2}}^{1/2}\cdot P_{CO_{2},c}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Nernst's equation with n = 2:**

$$E = E^{0}(T) - \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left[\frac{P_{H_{2}O}\cdot P_{CO_{2},a}}{P_{H_{2}}\cdot P_{O_{2}}^{1/2}\cdot P_{CO_{2},c}}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Use logarithm identity $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$ to rewrite:**

$$E = E^{0}(T) + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left[\frac{P_{H_{2}}\cdot P_{O_{2}}^{1/2}\cdot P_{CO_{2},c}}{P_{H_{2}O}\cdot P_{CO_{2},a}}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **board-exam-ready** form. The CO₂ terms at anode and cathode explicitly appear, which is the unique signature of MCFC.

## 3.2 Derivation: Standard Voltage at 650 °C

**Step 1 — Use the temperature-dependent Gibbs free energy of formation of water vapour:**

$$\Delta G_{f,\,H_{2}O(g)}^{0}(T)\ \text{at}\ 650^{\circ}\text{C}\ (923\ \text{K}) \approx -177.4\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental thermodynamic link $E^{0} = -\Delta G^{0}/(nF)$:**

$$E^{0}_{923\,K} = -\frac{-177\,400\ \text{J/mol}}{2 \times 96\,485\ \text{C/mol}}$$

$$E^{0}_{923\,K} = \frac{177\,400}{192\,970} \approx 0.919\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Account for CO₂ partial pressure cross-coupling:**

In an actual MCFC, the Nernst voltage (open circuit) is observed as **~1.04 V** because the gas compositions at standard inlet conditions give the $\ln$ term a small positive contribution (~+0.12 V).

## 3.3 Derivation: Electrical Efficiency of MCFC

**Step 1 — Electrical (voltage) efficiency using the actual operating cell voltage $V_{cell} = 0.75$ V (typical load):**

$$\eta_{elec} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{th}} = \frac{0.75}{1.481} \approx 0.506 \approx 50.6\%$$

**Step 2 — CHP efficiency (assuming thermal recovery of 30 % of the LHV heat):**

$$\eta_{CHP} = \eta_{elec} + \eta_{th} = 0.506 + 0.30 \approx 0.806 \approx 80.6\%$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with Carnot limit at the operating temperature ($T_h$ = 923 K, $T_c$ = 298 K):**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_{c}}{T_{h}} = 1 - \frac{298}{923} = 0.677 = 67.7\%$$

Note that the *electrical* efficiency can approach, but not exceed, this Carnot value; however, the **CHP efficiency can exceed 80 %** by harvesting low-grade heat, which is the actual engineering advantage of MCFC.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Practice Standard)

**Problem:** A MCFC operates at 650 °C with the following gas partial pressures (in atm): $P_{H_2} = 0.6$, $P_{O_2} = 0.18$, $P_{CO_2,c} = 0.25$, $P_{H_2O} = 0.2$, $P_{CO_2,a} = 0.3$. Take $E^{0}(923\ \text{K}) = 1.04$ V. Compute the open-circuit voltage.

**Solution — Step 1, write the Nernst expression:**

$$E = 1.04 + \frac{(8.314)(923)}{2(96\,485)}\ln\!\left[\frac{(0.6)(0.18)^{1/2}(0.25)}{(0.2)(0.3)}\right]$$

**Step 2, evaluate the term inside ln:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.6 \times \sqrt{0.18} \times 0.25 = 0.6 \times 0.4243 \times 0.25 = 0.0636$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.2 \times 0.3 = 0.06$$

$$Q_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{0.0636}{0.06} = 1.060$$

**Step 3, evaluate the Nernst term:**

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{8.314 \times 923}{2 \times 96\,485} = \frac{7673.8}{192\,970} \approx 0.03976\ \text{V}$$

$$\Delta E = 0.03976 \times \ln(1.060) = 0.03976 \times 0.0583 = 0.00232\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4, final voltage:**

$$E = 1.04 + 0.0023 \approx 1.042\ \text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This illustrates the KTU principle: even small partial-pressure differences (~6 %) produce measurable voltage changes. Students commonly miss the **square root of $P_{O_2}$** in the numerator — that is the most frequent valuation deduction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a MCFC Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    FUEL[Hydrogen or Natural Gas CH4 Feed] --> REF[Internal Reformer Catalyst]
    AIRIN[Air Inlet] --> AIRMIX[Mixer with Recycled CO2]
    AEX[Anode Exhaust H2O and CO2 Rich] --> SPLIT[Exhaust Splitter]
    SPLIT -->|Recycle Stream| AIRMIX
    SPLIT -->|Vent Stream| HEAT[Heat Recovery Boiler]
    CEX[Cathode Exhaust Depleted Air] --> HEAT
    HEAT -->|Steam| TURB[Steam Turbine for CHP]
    HEAT -->|Hot Water| DH[District Heating Loop]

    AIRMIX --> CATH
    REF --> ANOD
    CATH[Cathode: Lithiated NiO Li-NiO] -->|CO3 2- migration| ELECT
    ANOD[Anode: Ni with 2 to 10 percent Cr or Al] -->|H2 oxidation| ELECT
    ELECT[Electrolyte: Li2CO3 and K2CO3 in LiAlO2 Matrix] -->|ionic path| CATH

    ANOD -->|e- through external load| ELEC[DC Power Bus to Inverter]
    CATH -->|e- return| ELEC
    ELEC --> AC[AC Power Output 50 Hz Grid]
    TURB --> AC
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this:** Follow the CO₃²⁻ arrow from `CATH` → `ELECT` → `ANOD` to remember the **charge-carrier direction (cathode → anode)**, and follow the `e-` arrow in the opposite direction (anode → cathode) for the conventional current.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping

| Subsystem | Functional Role | Typical Engineering Component |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel Processing | Reforms CH₄ to H₂ + CO inside the cell | Ni-based internal reforming catalyst |
| Anode Manifold | Distributes fuel across cells | Ni-coated corrugated stainless steel |
| Cathode Manifold | Distributes air + recycled CO₂ | Stainless-steel bipolar plate |
| Electrolyte Reservoir | Holds molten Li₂CO₃–K₂CO₃ | Porous LiAlO₂ tile (1–2 mm thick) |
| CO₂ Recycle Loop | Returns anode CO₂ to cathode | Blower + ejector + control valve |
| Heat Recovery | Captures 300–400 °C waste heat | Steam generator / ORC bottoming cycle |
| Power Conditioning | DC → AC inversion | IGBT-based inverter with 96–98 % efficiency |

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Cell Architecture (Textual Schematic)

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  Cathode Current Collector (Stainless Steel 316L)         |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  CATHODE: Lithiated NiO   (porous, 0.5-1.5 mm)           |
|     1/2 O2 + CO2 + 2e-  -->  CO3(2-)                    |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  ELECTROLYTE: (Li,K)2CO3 in LiAlO2  (0.5-1.0 mm)        |
|     CO3(2-) migrates  cathode  -->  anode               |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  ANODE: Ni + Cr/Al       (porous, 0.5-1.5 mm)           |
|     H2 + CO3(2-)  -->  H2O + CO2 + 2e-                  |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  Anode Current Collector (Ni-plated SS)                  |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
        (Operating Temperature: 600 - 700 C)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is CO₂ continuously supplied to the cathode of a Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In an MCFC, the charge carrier through the molten carbonate electrolyte is the **CO₃²⁻ ion**. At the cathode, oxygen and CO₂ combine with incoming electrons to form CO₃²⁻ as per: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_{2}+CO_{2}+2e^{-}\rightarrow CO_{3}^{2-}$. Without continuous CO₂ supply, the cathode reaction cannot proceed and ionic conduction ceases. CO₂ is generated at the anode and recycled to the cathode inlet to sustain the ionic current loop. (3 marks)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Name the electrolyte, anode, and cathode materials used in a Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Electrolyte:** Molten mixture of lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃) and potassium carbonate (K₂CO₃) — sometimes Li₂CO₃/Na₂CO₃ — held in a porous LiAlO₂ ceramic matrix.
- **Anode:** Porous nickel, alloyed with 2–10 % chromium or aluminium for creep resistance.
- **Cathode:** Lithiated nickel oxide (Li-doped NiO) formed in-situ by lithium diffusion from the electrolyte. (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell with neat electrode reactions and a clearly labelled cell diagram.

**Model Solution:**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the cell as a high-temperature (600–700 °C) fuel cell with a molten alkali-carbonate electrolyte suspended in a LiAlO₂ matrix.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the cathode half-reaction: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_{2}+CO_{2}+2e^{-}\rightarrow CO_{3}^{2-}$. Explain that air + recycled CO₂ enters the cathode and CO₃²⁻ ions form in the porous NiO electrode.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Write the anode half-reaction: $H_{2}+CO_{3}^{2-}\rightarrow H_{2}O+CO_{2}+2e^{-}$. Explain that H₂ (or internally reformed CH₄) enters the anode and is oxidised, releasing electrons to the external circuit.
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** State the overall reaction: $H_{2}+\tfrac{1}{2}O_{2}+CO_{2,c}\rightarrow H_{2}O+CO_{2,a}$.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Draw and label the diagram (anode, electrolyte, cathode, charge carrier direction, electron flow direction, inlets and outlets).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the materials of construction and the major performance characteristics of an MCFC.

**Model Solution:**

- **Materials (3 marks):**
  - *Electrolyte:* 62 % Li₂CO₃ + 38 % K₂CO₃ eutectic in LiAlO₂ matrix. LiAlO₂ provides structural rigidity; alkali carbonates supply ionic conductivity (~1 S/cm at 650 °C).
  - *Anode:* Ni with 2–10 % Cr to prevent sintering and creep; alternatively Ni–Al.
  - *Cathode:* In-situ lithiated NiO (Li⁺ diffuses from electrolyte into NiO, improving electronic conductivity and reducing dissolution).
  - *Bipolar plates:* 316L stainless steel, often coated with Ni or alumina to prevent corrosion by molten carbonates.
- **Performance Characteristics (4 marks):**
  - Operating voltage per cell: **0.7 – 0.85 V** at 150–200 mA/cm².
  - Power density: 0.1 – 0.2 W/cm².
  - Electrical efficiency: **50 – 60 %**; CHP efficiency: **70 – 85 %**.
  - Internal reforming capability eliminates external reformer and gives high fuel flexibility (H₂, CO, CH₄, syngas).
  - Lifetime: ~40 000 hours (commercial target).
  - Key drawback: electrolyte management (corrosion, NiO dissolution, cathode flooding).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Nernst equation for the overall reaction of a Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell and explain each term.

**Model Solution:**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the overall reaction: $H_{2}+\tfrac{1}{2}O_{2}+CO_{2,c}\rightarrow H_{2}O+CO_{2,a}$.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the general Nernst expression $E=E^{0}-\dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Construct the reaction quotient $Q=\dfrac{P_{H_{2}O}\cdot P_{CO_{2},a}}{P_{H_{2}}\cdot P_{O_{2}}^{1/2}\cdot P_{CO_{2},c}}$.
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute and simplify (using $\ln(1/x)=-\ln x$) to obtain: $E=E^{0}(T)+\dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{1/2}\,P_{CO_{2},c}}{P_{H_{2}O}\,P_{CO_{2},a}}\right]$.
- **Step 5 (2 marks):** Explain each term: $E^{0}(T)$ is temperature-dependent standard voltage (~1.04 V at 923 K); $R$ and $F$ are universal constants; $T$ is absolute temperature; partial pressures are in atm.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare MCFC with PAFC and SOFC with respect to electrolyte, temperature, charge carrier, efficiency and applications.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | **MCFC** | **PAFC** | **SOFC** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | Molten (Li,K)₂CO₃ in LiAlO₂ | Liquid H₃PO₄ in SiC matrix | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) |
| Temperature | **600 – 700 °C** | 150 – 220 °C | 700 – 1000 °C |
| Charge Carrier | **CO₃²⁻** | H⁺ | O²⁻ |
| Electrical Efficiency | 50 – 60 % | 35 – 45 % | 50 – 60 % |
| CHP Efficiency | 70 – 85 % | 70 – 85 % | 75 – 90 % |
| Fuel Flexibility | H₂, CO, CH₄ (internal reforming) | H₂ only (external reformer) | H₂, CO, CH₄ |
| CO Tolerance | High | Low (CO poisons Pt) | High |
| Applications | Large stationary, distributed generation | Small-medium CHP | Utility-scale, hybrid cycles |
| Key Drawback | Electrolyte management, corrosion | Phosphoric acid handling | High-T ceramic brittleness |

**(4 marks awarded for table, 3 marks for the concluding engineering interpretation.)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most-deducted mistake #1:** Writing the MCFC overall reaction as simply $H_{2}+\tfrac{1}{2}O_{2}\rightarrow H_{2}O$ and omitting the CO₂ terms. **Always include CO₂** at both anode and cathode in equations and Nernst expression.
> - **Most-deducted mistake #2:** Forgetting the square root of $P_{O_2}$ in the Nernst numerator. Marks lost: 1 in Part A, up to 2 in Part B derivations.
> - **Most-deducted mistake #3:** Confusing MCFC's charge carrier direction (CO₃²⁻ goes from cathode to anode, **opposite** to conventional current in the electrolyte).
> - **Most-deducted mistake #4:** Stating the operating temperature as "around 1000 °C" (that is SOFC). MCFC: **600 – 700 °C**.
> - **Most-deducted mistake #5:** Writing electrolyte as "molten carbonate" alone without mentioning the LiAlO₂ matrix support — examiners specifically award 1 mark for matrix identification.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Type:** High-temperature molten-salt fuel cell; second-generation technology.
- **Operating window:** **600 – 700 °C** (often cited as 650 °C nominal).
- **Electrolyte:** (Li,K)₂CO₃ eutectic or (Li,Na)₂CO₃ in a **LiAlO₂ ceramic matrix**.
- **Charge carrier:** **CO₃²⁻** migrating cathode → anode.
- **Anode material:** **Ni + Cr / Ni + Al** (porous).
- **Cathode material:** **Lithiated NiO (LiₓNiO)** formed in-situ.
- **Anode reaction:** $H_{2}+CO_{3}^{2-}\rightarrow H_{2}O+CO_{2}+2e^{-}$.
- **Cathode reaction:** $\tfrac{1}{2}O_{2}+CO_{2}+2e^{-}\rightarrow CO_{3}^{2-}$.
- **Overall reaction:** $H_{2}+\tfrac{1}{2}O_{2}+CO_{2,c}\rightarrow H_{2}O+CO_{2,a}$.
- **CO₂ recycling is mandatory** — unique signature feature.
- **Nernst equation:** $E=E^{0}(T)+\dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{P_{H_{2}}\,P_{O_{2}}^{1/2}\,P_{CO_{2},c}}{P_{H_{2}O}\,P_{CO_{2},a}}\right]$.
- **Standard voltage:** ~**1.04 V at 650 °C**; **1.229 V at 25 °C** (HHV).
- **Efficiency:** Electrical **50–60 %**; CHP **70–85 %**.
- **Internal reforming capability** — uses cell waste heat to reform CH₄.
- **Power density:** 0.1 – 0.2 W/cm² (modest; mitigated by stack scalability).
- **Applications:** Large-scale stationary power plants, industrial CHP, distributed generation, and as a building block for **Direct Carbon Fuel Cells (DCFC)**.
- **Primary limitations:** Electrolyte corrosion, NiO cathode dissolution, slow start-up time, CO₂ management infrastructure.
- **KTU Board Favourite:** A 14-mark question typically pairs **"working principle + electrode reactions"** (7 marks) with either **"materials and performance"** (7 marks) or **"Nernst derivation + comparison with PAFC/SOFC"** (7 marks). Master both combinations for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid Oxide Fuel Cells
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solid Oxide Fuel Cells

# Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFC) — Fundamentals & Operating Principles

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)** is a high-temperature electrochemical energy conversion device that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically **hydrogen H₂**, methane CH₄, or reformed hydrocarbons) and an oxidant (oxygen O₂) into electrical energy through an electrochemical reaction mediated by a **solid ceramic electrolyte** that conducts **oxide ions (O²⁻)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** SOFCs operate in the temperature range **600 °C – 1000 °C**, use **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ)** as the standard solid electrolyte, and belong to the family of high-temperature fuel cells (alongside MCFC and PAFC). They are the only fuel cell type with **full fuel flexibility** in their fuel stream.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hot Brick Battery"

Imagine a **red-hot porous brick** sandwiched between two plates of a different metal:
- Air is gently blown on one side (the **cathode**)
- Hydrogen gas is fed to the other side (the **anode**)
- Because the brick is so hot, oxygen atoms stripped of their electrons can "swim" through it from the air side to the hydrogen side
- The electrons, unable to pass through the brick, take the long way around through an external wire — **lighting up your bulb**
- Water vapor and heat come out as byproducts

The "brick" is the **YSZ electrolyte** — solid, rigid, but ionically conductive only when hot. Unlike PEMFCs (which use a wet plastic membrane and run cold), SOFCs use a **dry ceramic** and need a **furnace-like temperature** to enable ion transport.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics for SOFC:**
> - Operating temperature: **800 °C (typical)**
> - Power density: **0.3 – 0.6 W/cm²**
> - Electrical efficiency: **45 % – 60 %** (LHV basis)
> - Combined heat & power (CHP) efficiency: **85 % – 90 %**
> - Faradaic constants used: F = **96,485 C/mol**, R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cell Architecture & Component Materials

The SOFC is constructed as a layered ceramic sandwich — each layer engineered for a specific function:

| Layer | Material | Function |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **Cathode (air side)** | Lanthanum Strontium Manganite (La₁₋ₓSrₓMnO₃, "LSM") | Catalyzes O₂ reduction; porous to allow gas diffusion |
| **Electrolyte (middle)** | Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (ZrO₂)₉₂(Y₂O₃)₈ | Conducts O²⁻ ions only at high T; dense, gas-tight |
| **Anode (fuel side)** | Nickel-YSZ cermet (Ni/YSZ) | Catalyzes fuel oxidation; porous for gas transport |
| **Interconnects** | Lanthanum Strontium Chromite (LaCrO₃) or stainless steel | Electrically connects cells in a stack |

### 2.2 Half-Cell & Overall Reactions

**Cathode Reaction (Oxygen Reduction Reaction, ORR):**
$$\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-}$$

**Anode Reaction (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction, HOR):**
$$H_2 + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + 2e^-$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**
$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad \Delta G°_{298K} = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**For hydrocarbon fuels (CO, CH₄)** — a unique advantage of SOFCs:
$$CO + O^{2-} \rightarrow CO_2 + 2e^-$$
$$CH_4 + 4O^{2-} \rightarrow 2H_2O + CO_2 + 8e^-$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Because CO is not a poison at SOFC temperatures (unlike PEMFC), the SOFC can directly consume **syngas** from coal/biomass gasification without prior CO cleanup. This makes SOFC the **only fuel cell compatible with carbonaceous fuels**.

### 2.3 The Nernst Reversible Cell Voltage

The ideal open-circuit voltage (OCV) of an SOFC is governed by the Nernst equation, which for the H₂/O₂ couple is:

$$E_{Nernst} = E° + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left[ \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right]$$

where:
- $E°$ = standard reversible potential at 1 atm (1.229 V at 25 °C)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂ = **2**
- $T$ = absolute temperature in K (≈ 1073 K for SOFC)
- $P_i$ = partial pressure of species *i* in atm
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**

At SOFC operating temperature (1000 K), the value of $\frac{RT}{2F}$ becomes approximately:
$$\frac{(8.314)(1000)}{2(96485)} \approx 0.0431 \text{ V}$$

This is **why the thermal-neutral voltage** $E_{th} = \frac{\Delta H}{nF} = 1.481 \text{ V at 25 °C}$ decreases with temperature while the Nernst OCV also decreases — both effects combine to determine the operating point.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **examination-critical**. Every formula below has appeared (or is directly modeled) in KTU 2024 Scheme question papers.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description & Units |
|---|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $E° = \frac{\Delta G°}{nF}$ | Standard reversible voltage (V) |
| 2 | $E_{th} = \frac{\Delta H°}{nF}$ | Thermal-neutral (enthalpic) voltage = 1.481 V |
| 3 | $E = E° + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left[\frac{P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right]$ | Nernst voltage for H₂/O₂ SOFC (V) |
| 4 | $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Activation overpotential — Tafel form (V) |
| 5 | $\eta_{ohm} = iR_{ASR}$ | Ohmic overpotential (V); $R_{ASR}$ in Ω·cm² |
| 6 | $\eta_{conc} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right)$ | Concentration overpotential (V); $i_L$ = limiting current density |
| 7 | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act,a} - \eta_{act,c} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | Actual cell operating voltage (V) |
| 8 | $\eta_{elec} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \frac{E°}{E_{th}}$ | Theoretical electrical efficiency ≈ 83 % at 25 °C |
| 9 | $\sigma_{O^{2-}} = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{kT}\right)$ | Ionic conductivity of YSZ (Arrhenius form), S/cm |
| 10 | $P_{stack} = V_{cell} \times i \times A_{cell} \times N_{cells}$ | Stack power output (W) |

**Boundary Conditions & Practical Limits:**
- $0 \le i \le i_L$ (current density bounded by mass transport)
- $E_{Nernst} > V_{cell}$ always (losses subtract)
- $R_{ASR}$ must be $\le 0.5 \;\Omega \cdot cm^2$ for viable SOFC operation

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

SOFCs are deployed in:
- **Stationary combined heat and power (CHP)** units for hospitals, data centers
- **Auxiliary Power Units (APUs)** for heavy-duty trucks
- **Large-scale grid power** (Bloom Energy, Siemens, Mitsubishi Hitachi)
- **Hybrid systems with gas turbines** for ultra-high efficiency (60 %+)
- **Reverse operation (SOEC)** for green hydrogen electrolysis at high T

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation for SOFC from First Principles

We derive the Nernst equation starting from the **Gibbs free energy change** of the cell reaction at non-standard conditions.

**Step 1:** For the reaction $H_2 + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)}$, the Gibbs free energy change is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient**:

$$Q = \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

**Step 2:** The electrical work done per mole of electrons transferred is:

$$W_{elec} = -nFE_{cell}$$

Equating maximum electrical work to the decrease in Gibbs free energy at equilibrium:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the expression for $\Delta G$:

$$-nFE = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4:** Solving explicitly for $E$:

$$E = -\frac{\Delta G°}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 5:** Recognizing that $E° = -\frac{\Delta G°}{nF}$ and inverting the logarithm by changing the sign:

$$E = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left[\frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right]$$

**Step 6:** Inverting the fraction inside the log flips its sign:

$$E_{Nernst} = E° + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left[\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right]$$

**Final boxed result** (with $n = 2$ for H₂ oxidation):

$$\boxed{E_{Nernst} = E° + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left[\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right]}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** An SOFC operates at **1000 K** with fuel and air inlet compositions: $P_{H_2} = 0.97 \text{ atm}$, $P_{H_2O} = 0.03 \text{ atm}$, $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \text{ atm}$. At 1000 K, $E° = 1.099 \text{ V}$. Calculate the Nernst open-circuit voltage.

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs:**

$$T = 1000 \text{ K}, \quad E° = 1.099 \text{ V}, \quad n = 2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature-dependent coefficient:**

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(1000)}{2(96485)} = 4.3086 \times 10^{-2} \text{ V} = 0.04309 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the reaction quotient argument:**

$$\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} = \frac{(0.97) \cdot (0.21)^{1/2}}{(0.03)}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $(0.21)^{1/2}$:**

$$(0.21)^{0.5} = 0.4583$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\frac{(0.97)(0.4583)}{(0.03)} = \frac{0.4445}{0.03} = 14.817$$

**Step 6 — Compute the logarithm:**

$$\ln(14.817) = 2.696$$

**Step 7 — Multiply by the temperature coefficient:**

$$0.04309 \times 2.696 = 0.11617 \text{ V}$$

**Step 8 — Add to the standard potential:**

$$E_{Nernst} = 1.099 + 0.1162 = 1.2152 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{Nernst} \approx 1.215 \text{ V}}$$

**Interpretation:** The reversible voltage drops from 1.099 V (standard) to 1.215 V due to the high hydrogen partial pressure and low water partial pressure — confirming that **fuel-rich, dry operation raises the Nernst OCV**.

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Ohmic Loss in the YSZ Electrolyte

The dominant ohmic loss in SOFC comes from the ionic resistance of the YSZ electrolyte. By Ohm's law applied to the planar electrolyte slab:

$$\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR} = i \cdot \frac{L}{\sigma_{O^{2-}}}$$

where $L$ is the electrolyte thickness (cm) and $\sigma_{O^{2-}}$ is the ionic conductivity (S/cm).

For YSZ at 1000 K:

$$\sigma_{O^{2-}} \approx 0.10 \text{ S/cm}$$

**Analytical Matrix — Typical SOFC Loss Breakdown at $i = 0.5 \text{ A/cm}^2$:**

| Loss Component | Symbol | Magnitude | % of Total Loss |
|---------------|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| Cathode activation | $\eta_{act,c}$ | 80 mV | 26 % |
| Anode activation | $\eta_{act,a}$ | 30 mV | 10 % |
| Ohmic (electrolyte) | $\eta_{ohm}$ | 150 mV | 49 % |
| Concentration | $\eta_{conc}$ | 45 mV | 15 % |
| **Total Losses** | $\sum \eta$ | **305 mV** | **100 %** |

Resulting cell voltage:
$$V_{cell} = 1.215 - 0.305 = 0.910 \text{ V}$$

Power density:
$$P_d = V_{cell} \times i = 0.910 \times 0.5 = 0.455 \text{ W/cm}^2$$

This lies in the **typical SOFC operating band of 0.3 – 0.6 W/cm²**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SOFC Single-Cell Functional Architecture (Planar Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AirIn["AIR INLET O2 supply"] --> Cathode["CATHODE LSM La1-xSrxMnO3"]
    FuelIn["FUEL INLET H2 or CH4"] --> Anode["ANODE Ni-YSZ Cermet"]
    Cathode -->|Electrons consumed| ORR["ORR: 1/2 O2 + 2e- -> O2-"]
    ORR -->|O2- ions migrate| Electrolyte["ELECTROLYTE YSZ ZrO2-Y2O3 Conducts O2- ions 800 C"]
    Electrolyte -->|O2- delivered| HOR["HOR: H2 + O2- -> H2O + 2e-"]
    HOR --> Anode
    Anode -->|Electrons flow externally| ExternalCircuit["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT Load electrons drive current"]
    ExternalCircuit --> Cathode
    Anode --> WaterOut["WATER OUTLET H2O vapor byproduct"]
    Electrolyte --> Interconnect["INTERCONNECT LaCrO3 or stainless steel connects cells in stack"]
```

### 4.2 Tubular SOFC Architecture (Siemens-Westinghouse Design)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AirSupply["AIR BLOWER"] --> AirInjection["AIR INJECTION TUBE central porous ceramic"]
    FuelSupply["FUEL MANIFOLD H2 or natural gas"] --> TubeExterior["TUBE EXTERIOR active electrochemical zone"]
    TubeExterior --> CathodeTube["CATHODE LAYER LSM deposited on tube outer wall"]
    CathodeTube --> ElectrolyteTube["ELECTROLYTE LAYER YSZ dense ceramic film"]
    ElectrolyteTube --> AnodeTube["ANODE LAYER Ni-YSZ cermet on inner tube surface"]
    AnodeTube --> Exhaust["DEPLETED FUEL EXHAUST"]
    AnodeTube -.Current collector.-> NickelWire["NICKEL CURRENT COLLECTOR wire"]
    CathodeTube -.Current collector.-> SilverWire["SILVER CURRENT COLLECTOR wire"]
```

### 4.3 Polarization Curve — Voltage vs Current Density

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Origin["0 A/cm2 E_Nernst ~ 1.215 V"] --> ActivationDrop["Activation Loss region steep drop"]
    ActivationDrop --> OhmicRegion["Ohmic Loss region linear decline"]
    OhmicRegion --> ConcentrationDrop["Concentration Loss region sharp drop"]
    ConcentrationDrop --> LimitingCurrent["i_L limiting current density"]
    Origin --> Xaxis["Current Density i A per cm2"]
    Origin --> Yaxis["Cell Voltage V volts"]
```

### 4.4 SOFC System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FuelProc["FUEL PROCESSOR natural gas or biogas"] --> Desulfur["DESULFURIZER removes H2S"]
    Desulfur --> Reformer["STEAM REFORMER CH4 + H2O -> CO + 3H2"]
    Reformer --> ShiftRxn["WATER GAS SHIFT CO + H2O -> CO2 + H2"]
    ShiftRxn --> SOFCstack["SOFC STACK electrochemical reactor 800 C"]
    AirBlower["AIR BLOWER"] --> SOFCstack
    SOFCstack --> HotExhaust["HIGH TEMP EXHAUST 800 C"]
    HotExhaust --> Turbine["GAS TURBINE bottoming cycle for extra power"]
    Turbine --> Generator["GENERATOR"]
    SOFCstack --> Inverter["DC to AC INVERTER"]
    Inverter --> Grid["ELECTRICAL GRID 50 Hz AC"]
    HotExhaust --> HeatRec["HEAT RECOVERY boiler for hot water or process steam"]
```

### 4.5 SOFC vs Other Fuel Cell Technologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FCfamily["FUEL CELL FAMILY"] --> PEMFC["PEMFC 80 C H+ ion polymer membrane"]
    FCfamily --> PAFC["PAFC 200 C H+ ion phosphoric acid"]
    FCfamily --> MCFC["MCFC 650 C CO3 2- molten carbonate"]
    FCfamily --> SOFC["SOFC 800 C O2- solid ceramic YSZ"]
    PEMFC --> PEMapp["Vehicles portable electronics"]
    PAFC --> PAFCapp["Stationary CHP units"]
    MCFC --> MCFCapp["Large stationary power plants"]
    SOFC --> SOFCapp["Stationary CHP industrial APU hydrogen electrolysis reverse"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
*State the electrolyte material and the charge carrier in a Solid Oxide Fuel Cell. Why is CO not considered a poison in an SOFC whereas it severely degrades a PEMFC?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The SOFC uses **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ, (ZrO₂)₀.₉₂(Y₂O₃)₀.₀₈)** as the solid ceramic electrolyte, and the mobile charge carrier is the **oxide ion O²⁻**.

In a PEMFC, CO adsorbs strongly onto the platinum catalyst at low temperatures (80 °C), blocking the active sites for hydrogen oxidation. In an SOFC, the operating temperature (800 °C) is high enough that CO readily undergoes the **electrochemical oxidation reaction $CO + O^{2-} \rightarrow CO_2 + 2e^-$** at the Ni-YSZ anode, so it acts as a **fuel rather than a poison**. **(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
*List three advantages and two disadvantages of Solid Oxide Fuel Cells compared to low-temperature fuel cells.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **High electrical efficiency** (50 – 60 %) due to fast kinetics and high-quality waste heat
2. **Fuel flexibility** — can directly use H₂, CO, CH₄, syngas, or natural gas without an external reformer in some designs
3. **No precious metal catalysts** required; Ni-YSZ cermet replaces expensive platinum

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Slow startup time** (hours) because the ceramic electrolyte must reach 800 °C to conduct ions
2. **High cost of stack materials** and strict requirements for thermal-expansion matching between layers

*(Full marks awarded: 1 mark per advantage × 3 + 0.5 × 2 = 3, but any 3 valid points total 3 marks as per KTU valuation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for the overall cell reaction of an SOFC operating on hydrogen and oxygen. Clearly state all symbols and assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SOFC operates at **1073 K** with the following gas partial pressures at the electrode surfaces:

- $P_{H_2} = 0.90 \text{ atm}$
- $P_{O_2} = 0.18 \text{ atm}$
- $P_{H_2O} = 0.10 \text{ atm}$

Given that the standard potential at 1073 K is **E° = 1.10 V**, calculate the **Nernst open-circuit voltage**. If the cell delivers a current density of 0.40 A/cm² with total overpotentials of 280 mV, determine the **operating cell voltage**, the **power density**, and the **voltage efficiency** of the cell. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the overall reaction:
$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)}$$

The Gibbs free energy change at non-standard conditions is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q \quad \text{where} \quad Q = \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

Since the maximum electrical work equals $-\Delta G$:

$$-nFE = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$

Substituting $\Delta G° = -nFE°$:

$$-nFE = -nFE° + RT \ln Q$$

Rearranging and noting $n = 2$:

$$E = E° - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left[ \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}} \right]$$

Inverting the argument of the logarithm:

$$\boxed{E_{Nernst} = E° + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left[ \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right]}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Ideal gas behavior
- Isothermal, isobaric operation
- Equilibrium at electrode-electrolyte interfaces

*(Mark split: 2 for writing ΔG, 2 for nFE relation, 2 for final expression, 1 for assumptions)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\frac{RT}{2F}$:**

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(1073)}{2(96485)} = \frac{8920.92}{192970} = 0.04623 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the argument of the log:**

$$\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} = \frac{(0.90) \cdot (0.18)^{0.5}}{(0.10)} = \frac{0.90 \times 0.4243}{0.10} = \frac{0.3819}{0.10} = 3.819$$

**Step 3 — Apply natural log:**

$$\ln(3.819) = 1.340$$

**Step 4 — Compute $E_{Nernst}$:**

$$E_{Nernst} = 1.10 + (0.04623)(1.340) = 1.10 + 0.0619 = 1.162 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{Nernst} = 1.162 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 5 — Operating cell voltage:**

$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \sum \eta = 1.162 - 0.280 = 0.882 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Power density:**

$$P_d = V_{cell} \times i = 0.882 \times 0.40 = 0.3528 \text{ W/cm}^2$$

$$\boxed{P_d \approx 0.353 \text{ W/cm}^2}$$

**Step 7 — Voltage efficiency:**

The thermal-neutral voltage at 1073 K is approximately:
$$E_{th} = \frac{\Delta H}{2F} \approx 1.30 \text{ V}$$

Voltage efficiency:
$$\eta_V = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{th}} = \frac{0.882}{1.30} = 0.678 = 67.8\%$$

*(Mark split: 1 for RT/2F, 1 for argument, 1 for log, 1 for Nernst, 1 for Vcell, 1 for Pd, 1 for η_V)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working principle of a planar Solid Oxide Fuel Cell** with a neat functional schematic. Discuss the role of each component: cathode, anode, electrolyte, and interconnect. Why must the YSZ electrolyte be **dense and gas-tight** while the electrodes must be **porous**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the performance characteristics of **SOFC, MCFC, and PEMFC** under the following heads: (i) operating temperature, (ii) electrolyte and charge carrier, (iii) fuel flexibility, (iv) efficiency, (v) typical application, (vi) major drawback. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A planar SOFC consists of a flat layered structure:
1. **Cathode (LSM)** — porous layer exposed to air; reduces O₂ to O²⁻
2. **Electrolyte (YSZ)** — dense ceramic sheet (~ 10 µm in modern designs); selectively conducts O²⁻ from cathode to anode
3. **Anode (Ni-YSZ cermet)** — porous cermet exposed to fuel; oxidizes H₂ to release electrons and H₂O
4. **Interconnects (LaCrO₃ or ferritic steel)** — bipolar plates that serially connect cells in a stack, separating fuel and air streams

**Working principle:** O₂ at the cathode gains 2 electrons to become O²⁻, which migrates through the YSZ under the chemical potential gradient, reacts with H₂ at the anode releasing 2 electrons back through the external circuit.

**Why dense electrolyte?** To prevent **crossover** of fuel and oxidant — any direct mixing causes combustion, energy loss, and safety hazard.

**Why porous electrodes?** To maximize the **Triple-Phase Boundary (TPB)** — the line where gas, electrode catalyst, and electrolyte meet — where electrochemical reactions occur.

---

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark each + 1 for overall summary):**

| Parameter | **PEMFC** | **MCFC** | **SOFC** |
|-----------|-----------|----------|----------|
| Operating Temperature | 60 – 80 °C | 600 – 650 °C | 700 – 1000 °C |
| Electrolyte | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ in LiAlO₂ | Solid YSZ (ZrO₂–Y₂O₃) |
| Charge Carrier | H⁺ | CO₃²⁻ | O²⁻ |
| Fuel Flexibility | Pure H₂ only (CO poisons Pt) | H₂, CH₄, CO (reformed in-situ) | H₂, CO, CH₄, syngas, NH₃ |
| Electrical Efficiency | 40 – 60 % | 45 – 55 % | 50 – 60 % |
| Typical Application | Vehicles, portable electronics | Large stationary power plants | Stationary CHP, APU, hybrid cycles |
| Major Drawback | Expensive Pt catalyst, water management | Corrosive molten electrolyte, slow startup | Slow startup, ceramic brittleness |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the charge carrier sign/direction.** Always write **O²⁻ ions migrate from cathode to anode** — many students reverse this and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using E° = 1.229 V universally.** At SOFC temperatures (1000 K+), **E° drops to ~ 1.10 V**. Using the 25 °C value is a common mistake in numerical problems.
> 3. **Mixing SOFC with PEMFC kinetics.** PEMFC uses the Butler-Volmer equation with H⁺ transfer; SOFC uses Tafel kinetics with O²⁻ transfer. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Skipping units.** Always write **W/cm²** for power density, **A/cm²** for current density, **Ω·cm²** for ASR. KTU examiners explicitly deduct for missing units.
> 5. **Neglecting the thermal-neutral voltage distinction.** $E° = 1.229$ V is **not** the same as $E_{th} = 1.481$ V. Voltage efficiency uses $E_{th}$, electrical (carnot-corrected) efficiency uses $E°$.
> 6. **Omitting reaction stoichiometry for hydrocarbon fuels.** If a question says "natural gas feed," write $CH_4 + 4O^{2-} \rightarrow 2H_2O + CO_2 + 8e^-$ explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Pre-Exam Checklist — Solid Oxide Fuel Cells**

- [x] **Operating temperature:** 800 °C (range 600 – 1000 °C)
- [x] **Electrolyte:** YSZ = (ZrO₂)₀.₉₂(Y₂O₃)₀.₀₈ — solid, dense, ionically conductive
- [x] **Charge carrier:** **O²⁻** (oxide ion) — moves **cathode → anode**
- [x] **Cathode:** LSM (La₁₋ₓSrₓMnO₃) — porous, catalytic for ORR
- [x] **Anode:** Ni-YSZ cermet — porous, catalytic for HOR / CO oxidation
- [x] **Interconnect:** LaCrO₃ (or ferritic stainless steel in low-T SOFCs)
- [x] **Standard cell reaction:** $H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$
- [x] **Nernst voltage:** $E = E° + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left[\frac{P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right]$
- [x] **Standard reversible voltage at 1000 K:** ~ **1.10 V** (not 1.229 V)
- [x] **Thermal-neutral voltage:** $E_{th} = 1.481$ V (at 25 °C)
- [x] **Ohmic loss is the dominant loss** in SOFC (~50 % of total)
- [x] **Triple-Phase Boundary (TPB)** is the active reaction site
- [x] **Fuel flexibility:** SOFCs can directly use **CO, CH₄, syngas** — unique advantage
- [x] **No precious metal catalyst required** — replaces Pt with Ni
- [x] **Polarization curve has 3 regions:** activation → ohmic → concentration
- [x] **Power density range:** 0.3 – 0.6 W/cm² typical
- [x] **Electrical efficiency:** 50 – 60 % standalone; 85 – 90 % with CHP
- [x] **Reverse operation** = Solid Oxide Electrolyzer Cell (SOEC) for H₂ production
- [x] **Main disadvantages:** slow startup, ceramic brittleness, high material cost
- [x] **Key applications:** stationary CHP, APU, hybrid SOFC-gas turbine, grid power

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Cell Comparison** is the systematic evaluation of different fuel cell technologies based on standardized parameters such as electrolyte type, operating temperature, charge carrier, fuel flexibility, electrical efficiency, power density, and application domains. It enables engineers to select the most appropriate fuel cell architecture for a given power, size, cost, and operating environment.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBBT616)**, fuel cell comparison forms the analytical capstone of Module 4, allowing students to map theoretical electrochemistry onto commercial engineering deployment. The principal fuel cell families compared are:

- **AFC** — Alkaline Fuel Cell
- **PEMFC** — Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell
- **DMFC** — Direct Methanol Fuel Cell
- **PAFC** — Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell
- **MCFC** — Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell
- **SOFC** — Solid Oxide Fuel Cell

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Six Different Kitchens, One Recipe (Electricity):**
> Imagine you want to cook the same dish (generate electricity from a chemical reaction) in six different kitchens. Each kitchen uses a different cooking medium (electrolyte) — gas, induction, charcoal, wood-fire, etc. The temperature, speed, taste, fuel cost, and cleanup differ in each. *Fuel cell comparison* is essentially comparing these six kitchens so you pick the right one for the right customer (application).

- **AFC** is like a clean, fast induction kitchen — quick, efficient, but extremely sensitive to impurities.
- **PEMFC** is a compact microwave-style kitchen — fast start-up, clean, but expensive membranes.
- **SOFC** is a traditional slow-cook wood-fired oven — very hot, very efficient, but slow to start.
- **MCFC** is an industrial kiln — runs molten salt, good for large plants.
- **PAFC** is a mid-range commercial stove — balanced performance for stationary use.
- **DMFC** is a single-burner portable stove — uses liquid fuel directly, low power.

## 1.3 Key Comparison Metrics (Standardized)

The comparison must always pivot around these **seven canonical parameters**:

1. **Electrolyte** (state and composition)
2. **Operating Temperature** (°C)
3. **Charge Carrier / Mobile Ion** (H⁺, O²⁻, CO₃²⁻, OH⁻)
4. **Fuel Used** (H₂, methanol, hydrocarbons)
5. **Electrical Efficiency** (%)
6. **Power Range / Power Density** (W/cm², kW–MW)
7. **Primary Application Domain** (transport, stationary, portable)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A common 3-mark KTU question is *"Compare PEMFC and SOFC"* or *"Why is SOFC suitable for stationary applications while PEMFC is preferred for transport?"* — these questions test comparative reasoning, not rote memorization.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Comparison is Non-Trivial

Comparison is not a simple tabulation. Each fuel cell represents a **different solution to the same thermodynamic problem**: combining a fuel (typically H₂) with an oxidant (O₂) to produce electricity, water, and heat, governed by the **Gibbs free energy** of reaction:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

The **theoretical maximum efficiency** of any fuel cell (regardless of type) is:

$$\eta_{rev} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

For the hydrogen–oxygen reaction at standard conditions:
- $\Delta G^0 = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^0 = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$

$$\eta_{rev} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} \approx 0.83 = 83\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world note:** Practical efficiencies are much lower (35–60%) because of **activation losses (Butler–Volmer)**, **ohmic losses (Ohm's law)**, and **concentration losses (mass-transport)**. The comparative *operating point* on the polarization curve defines the actual delivered efficiency.

## 2.2 Comparative Decision Logic

The selection of a fuel cell type for a given application follows this **engineering decision tree**:

- **Step 1 — Define operating temperature constraint:** If $T_{op} < 100°C$, restrict to AFC, PEMFC, DMFC. If $T_{op} > 600°C$, restrict to MCFC, SOFC.
- **Step 2 — Define fuel availability:** Pure H₂ favors PEMFC/AFC; hydrocarbon fuels favor SOFC/MCFC; liquid fuel favors DMFC.
- **Step 3 — Define power density need:** Transport applications → PEMFC (high $\text{W/cm}^2$). Stationary grid → SOFC, MCFC (high efficiency, low power density acceptable).
- **Step 4 — Define start-up time:** Fast start-up (< 1 min) → PEMFC. Slow start-up (hours) → SOFC.
- **Step 5 — Define cost tolerance:** Low capex → PAFC, MCFC. High capex acceptable → PEMFC, SOFC.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Comparison Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important comparative artifact** for KTU examinations. CRITICAL: All vertical bar symbols (for absolute value or modulus) are written as `\vert` to preserve table integrity.

| Parameter | AFC | PEMFC | DMFC | PAFC | MCFC | SOFC |
|-----------|-----|-------|------|------|------|------|
| **Electrolyte** | KOH solution | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | Polymer membrane | Liquid H₃PO₄ | Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | Solid YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) |
| **Mobile Ion** | OH⁻ | H⁺ | H⁺ | H⁺ | CO₃²⁻ | O²⁻ |
| **Operating Temp (°C)** | 60–90 | 60–100 | 60–100 | 150–220 | 600–700 | 700–1000 |
| **Charge Carrier Direction** | Cathode → Anode | Anode → Cathode | Anode → Cathode | Anode → Cathode | Cathode → Anode | Cathode → Anode |
| **Fuel** | Pure H₂ | Pure H₂ | Liquid CH₃OH (methanol) | Pure H₂ | H₂, CH₄, CO | H₂, CH₄, CO, hydrocarbons |
| **Oxidant** | Pure O₂ | Air (O₂) | Air | Air | Air + CO₂ | Air |
| **Electrical Efficiency (%)** | 50–60 | 40–60 | 20–40 | 35–45 | 45–55 | 45–60 |
| **Power Density (W/cm²)** | 0.1–0.3 | 0.6–1.0 | 0.04–0.25 | 0.15–0.3 | 0.1–0.2 | 0.2–0.4 |
| **Start-up Time** | Minutes | Seconds–Minutes | Minutes | Hours | Hours | Hours |
| **Water Management** | Critical | Critical (humidification) | Critical | Less critical | Produced water consumed | Produced water consumed |
| **CO₂ Sensitivity** | Poisoning (carbonate) | Mild tolerance | Mild | Tolerant | Beneficial (feed required) | Tolerant |
| **CO Tolerance (ppm)** | < 10 | < 10 | < 10 | < 1000 | Tolerant | Tolerant |
| **Primary Application** | Space (Apollo, Shuttle) | Automobiles, backup power | Portable electronics | Stationary CHP | Large stationary power plants | Stationary power, large CHP |
| **Stack Size** | 1–100 kW | 1 W – 250 kW | < 1 kW | 50–400 kW | 100 kW – 2 MW | 1 kW – multi-MW |
| **Cost Status (relative)** | Moderate | High (Pt catalyst) | High | Moderate | Low–Moderate | High (high-T materials) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the mobile ion *direction* flips between acidic (H⁺ carriers) and alkaline (OH⁻/O²⁻/CO₃²⁻ carriers) systems — this is a frequent 2-mark KTU short-answer trap. In **PEMFC/PAFC/DMFC**, H⁺ travels from anode to cathode. In **SOFC**, O²⁻ travels from cathode to anode. In **MCFC**, CO₃²⁻ travels from cathode to anode. In **AFC**, OH⁻ travels from cathode to anode.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Deployment Examples:**
> - **Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO** → PEMFC (transportation, fast start-up, high power density)
> - **Bloom Energy Servers** → SOFC (data centers, stationary, high efficiency with CH₄)
> - **NASA Apollo & Space Shuttle** → AFC (pure O₂/H₂ available, high efficiency, light weight)
> - **Tokyo Gas, Osaka Gas plants** → PAFC (Japan's early commercial CHP installations, 200 kW class)
> - **FuelCell Energy (Danbury, CT)** → MCFC (MW-class carbonate plants for industrial CHP)
> - **Toshiba Dynario** → DMFC (small portable methanol power packs)

## 2.5 Comparative Strengths & Weaknesses Matrix

| Fuel Cell | Key Strength | Key Weakness |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| **AFC** | Highest demonstrated efficiency; inexpensive catalyst (Ag, Ni) | Intolerant to CO₂ — must use pure O₂ |
| **PEMFC** | Fast start-up, high power density, solid electrolyte | Expensive Pt catalyst; water management critical |
| **DMFC** | Uses liquid fuel (no reformer) | Low power density; methanol crossover |
| **PAFC** | Most commercially mature CHP technology | Low power density; large footprint |
| **MCFC** | Fuel-flexible (CH₄, CO); no noble metals | Corrosive molten electrolyte; slow start-up |
| **SOFC** | Highest efficiency; fuel-flexible; all-solid | Very high operating temperature; thermal cycling issues |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Comparative Efficiency at Different Operating Points

We will derive the **theoretical efficiency of PEMFC and SOFC** under standard conditions and compare them numerically.

### Step 1: Define the Half-Cell Reactions

**For PEMFC (acidic, H⁺ carrier):**
$$\text{Anode: } H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$
$$\text{Cathode: } \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$$
$$\text{Overall: } H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

**For SOFC (solid oxide, O²⁻ carrier):**
$$\text{Anode: } H_2 + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + 2e^-$$
$$\text{Cathode: } \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-}$$
$$\text{Overall: } H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

> Both produce the same net reaction; the difference is the *intermediate transport ion*.

### Step 2: Use the Standard Thermodynamic Values

At 25°C (298.15 K) and 1 atm:
- $\Delta G^0 = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H^0 = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- Faraday's constant: $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$

The standard reversible cell voltage (open-circuit voltage, OCV) is:

$$E^0_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G^0}{nF} = -\frac{237200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$E^0_{rev} = \frac{237200}{192970} \approx 1.229 \text{ V}$$

This is the **thermodynamic OCV** — the *maximum* voltage any H₂–O₂ fuel cell can deliver at 25°C, regardless of type (PEMFC, AFC, SOFC, etc., all share the same OCV if pure H₂/O₂ is used).

### Step 3: Compare the Thermoneutral Voltage (Higher Heating Value Basis)

$$E_{th} = -\frac{\Delta H^0}{nF} = \frac{285800}{192970} \approx 1.481 \text{ V}$$

> The ratio $E^0_{rev}/E_{th}$ gives the **ideal efficiency**:
> $$\eta_{ideal} = \frac{1.229}{1.481} \approx 0.83 = 83\%$$

### Step 4: Real Voltage Losses (Polarization Losses)

The actual operating voltage $V_{cell}$ is reduced from $E^0_{rev}$ by three loss terms:

$$V_{cell} = E^0_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

Where:
- $\eta_{act}$: activation overpotential (Butler–Volmer kinetics)
- $\eta_{ohmic} = iR$ : ohmic drop (electrolyte resistance)
- $\eta_{conc}$: concentration overpotential (mass transport)

### Step 5: Comparative Numerical Example

Assume a current density of $i = 0.5 \text{ A/cm}^2$ and approximate loss values:

| Parameter | PEMFC (low-T) | SOFC (high-T) |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| $E^0_{rev}$ (V) | 1.229 | 1.229 |
| $\eta_{act}$ (V) | 0.30 | 0.05 |
| $\eta_{ohmic}$ (V) | 0.05 | 0.10 |
| $\eta_{conc}$ (V) | 0.05 | 0.05 |
| **$V_{cell}$ (V)** | **0.829** | **1.029** |

$$\eta_{PEMFC} = \frac{0.829}{1.481} \times 100\% \approx 56\%$$

$$\eta_{SOFC} = \frac{1.029}{1.481} \times 100\% \approx 69.5\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Even though both fuel cells have the same *thermodynamic* OCV, the **SOFC operates at a much higher temperature**, which dramatically reduces activation overpotential (Arrhenius kinetics). This is precisely why SOFC *practical* efficiencies approach 60% while PEMFC practical efficiencies cap near 50–55%. This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation question.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Why High-Temperature Cells Are CO-Tolerant

The CO adsorption energy on a Pt catalyst follows an Arrhenius-type temperature dependence. The CO tolerance *T_50%* (temperature at which CO tolerance improves) is derived from the equilibrium of the reverse water-gas shift reaction:

$$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2$$

The equilibrium constant increases with temperature, meaning at high T, CO is converted to CO₂:

$$K_{eq}(T) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G^0_{RWGS}(T)}{RT}\right)$$

At $T = 800°C$ (typical SOFC), $K_{eq} \gg 1$, so CO is essentially *consumed* before reaching the anode electrocatalyst. This is why **SOFC and MCFC can use hydrocarbon fuels directly** (after internal reforming) while PEMFC requires $< 10 \text{ ppm}$ CO in the feed.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Polarization Behavior

> [!NOTE]
> The polarization curve (Voltage vs. Current Density) differs significantly between fuel cell types. The three loss regions exhibit distinct magnitudes:

| Loss Region | Dominant in | Cause | Mitigation |
|-------------|------------|-------|------------|
| Activation Loss (low i) | **PEMFC, DMFC** (low T) | Slow electrode kinetics | Higher T, better catalyst |
| Ohmic Loss (mid i) | **PEMFC** (membrane resistance) | Ionic resistance of electrolyte | Thinner membrane, higher conductivity |
| Concentration Loss (high i) | **DMFC, AFC** | Mass-transport limitation | Better GDL, higher flow rate |
| **Combined Effect** | **SOFC** has *lowest* total loss at rated current | High T = fast kinetics + good conductivity | Operates near 1.0 V at high i |

## 3.4 Comparative Sizing & Cost Derivation

For a power requirement of $P = 10 \text{ kW}$, the active cell area $A$ is:

$$A = \frac{P}{V_{cell} \cdot i \cdot N_{cells}}$$

Assuming $V_{cell} = 0.7 \text{ V}$, $i = 0.5 \text{ A/cm}^2$, and $N_{cells} = 100$:

$$A = \frac{10000}{0.7 \times 0.5 \times 100} = 285.7 \text{ cm}^2 \approx 0.029 \text{ m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A higher power density (SOFC at higher T) reduces the required active area for the same power output — but the *auxiliary balance-of-plant* cost (BOP, e.g., heat exchangers for high-T) may offset this. KTU problems often ask for a *trade-off matrix*, not a single answer.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List the six major types of fuel cells and identify which one is most suitable for space applications. Justify."**

**Model Answer:**

The six major types are:
1. **AFC** — Alkaline Fuel Cell
2. **PEMFC** — Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell
3. **DMFC** — Direct Methanol Fuel Cell
4. **PAFC** — Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell
5. **MCFC** — Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell
6. **SOFC** — Solid Oxide Fuel Cell

**Suitability for space: AFC**
- Space vehicles carry pure H₂ and pure O₂ tanks (no atmospheric contamination by CO₂).
- AFC exhibits the *highest demonstrated electrical efficiency* (50–60%) — critical for payload-limited missions.
- Lightweight, fast start-up, and operates at low temperatures (60–90°C), reducing thermal shielding mass.
- Historical use: NASA **Apollo (AFC)**, **Space Shuttle Orbiter (AFC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation (3 marks):** 6 types listed (2 marks) + correct identification with justification (1 mark).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Why is PEMFC preferred for automotive applications while SOFC is preferred for stationary power plants?"**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PEMFC (Automotive) | SOFC (Stationary) |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Start-up time | **Seconds–Minutes** ✓ | Hours ✗ |
| Operating temperature | 60–100°C (instantly usable) | 700–1000°C (slow warm-up) |
| Power density | High (0.6–1.0 W/cm²) ✓ | Moderate (0.2–0.4 W/cm²) |
| Efficiency | 40–60% | 45–60% (higher practical) |
| Fuel | Pure H₂ (tank-stored) | CH₄, natural gas (reformed internally) |
| Application match | Quick drive cycles | Continuous baseload power |

**Conclusion:** PEMFC's rapid start-up and high power density suit dynamic driving; SOFC's high efficiency and fuel flexibility suit continuous stationary operation where start-up speed is irrelevant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation (3 marks):** Tabulated comparison (2 marks) + conclusion (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]
**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the working principle of **PEMFC** and **SOFC**. Clearly state the half-cell and overall reactions, the electrolyte used, and the mobile ion in each case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare PEMFC and SOFC in terms of electrolyte, operating temperature, efficiency, fuel flexibility, start-up time, and applications. Why is SOFC more tolerant to CO poisoning than PEMFC? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**PEMFC (Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell):**

The PEMFC uses a thin **perfluorinated sulfonic acid polymer membrane (e.g., Nafion)** as the electrolyte, which conducts H⁺ ions.

**Half-cell reactions:**
$$\text{Anode: } H_2(g) \rightarrow 2H^+(aq) + 2e^-$$
$$\text{Cathode: } \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) + 2H^+(aq) + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O(l)$$
$$\text{Overall: } H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l) + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat}$$

**Mobile ion:** H⁺ (proton), traveling from anode to cathode through the hydrated membrane. The membrane must remain hydrated for adequate proton conductivity.

**SOFC (Solid Oxide Fuel Cell):**

The SOFC uses a **solid ceramic electrolyte**, typically **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ)**, which conducts O²⁻ ions at high temperatures (700–1000°C).

**Half-cell reactions:**
$$\text{Anode: } H_2(g) + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O(g) + 2e^-$$
$$\text{Cathode: } \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-}$$
$$\text{Overall: } H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(g) + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat}$$

**Mobile ion:** O²⁻ (oxide ion), traveling from cathode to anode through the YSZ ceramic. High temperature is essential to achieve sufficient O²⁻ mobility (ionic conductivity $\sigma \propto e^{-E_a/RT}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation (7 marks):** PEMFC description (3) + SOFC description (3) + neat sketches of both (1).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | PEMFC | SOFC |
|-----------|-------|------|
| **Electrolyte** | Nafion polymer membrane (solid, acidic) | YSZ — Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (solid ceramic) |
| **Operating Temperature** | 60–100°C | 700–1000°C |
| **Mobile Ion** | H⁺ | O²⁻ |
| **Charge Carrier Direction** | Anode → Cathode | Cathode → Anode |
| **Fuel** | Pure H₂ (CO < 10 ppm) | H₂, CH₄, CO, higher hydrocarbons |
| **Electrical Efficiency** | 40–60% | 45–60% |
| **Start-up Time** | Seconds–Minutes (fast) | Hours (slow) |
| **Power Density** | 0.6–1.0 W/cm² (high) | 0.2–0.4 W/cm² (moderate) |
| **Catalyst** | Pt (expensive) | Ni-based / perovskite (cheaper) |
| **Water Management** | Critical (humidification required) | Produced water is consumed or vaporized |
| **CO Tolerance** | < 10 ppm (poisoning of Pt) | Tolerant — CO is a fuel at high T |
| **Primary Applications** | Automobiles, buses, backup power | Stationary power, CHP, large industrial |

**Why SOFC is more CO-tolerant:**

At high temperature (800°C+), the **reverse water-gas shift reaction** is thermodynamically and kinetically favored:

$$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2$$

This reaction **converts CO to CO₂ and H₂**, so CO never reaches the anode electrocatalyst in poisoning concentrations. Additionally, at high T, the **CO adsorption energy on the Ni cermet anode is much weaker** (desorption dominates), so the catalyst sites remain available for H₂ oxidation. In PEMFC, the low operating temperature means CO *irreversibly adsorbs* on Pt sites, blocking them and dramatically reducing cell voltage even at CO levels of 10–50 ppm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation (7 marks):** Comparison table (4) + CO-tolerance explanation (3).

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]
**(a)** Explain the working principle of **MCFC** with electrode reactions. Discuss why MCFC requires CO₂ to be supplied at the cathode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **MCFC, SOFC, and PAFC** based on electrolyte, temperature, efficiency, fuel used, and applications. Which of these is the most fuel-flexible and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**MCFC (Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell):**

The MCFC uses a **molten mixture of alkali metal carbonates** (typically 62% Li₂CO₃ + 38% K₂CO₃, or Li/K/Na combinations) held in a **porous ceramic matrix (LiAlO₂)** as the electrolyte. The cell operates at 600–700°C, where the carbonates are molten and conduct **CO₃²⁻ ions**.

**Half-cell reactions:**
$$\text{Anode: } H_2 + CO_3^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + CO_2 + 2e^-$$
$$\text{Cathode: } \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + CO_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow CO_3^{2-}$$
$$\text{Overall: } H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + CO_2(\text{cathode}) \rightarrow H_2O + CO_2(\text{anode})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Net:** The CO₂ is **transferred** from cathode to anode — it is consumed at the cathode and produced at the anode.

**Why CO₂ is needed at the cathode:**

The carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻) is the *charge carrier*. Its formation at the cathode **requires CO₂ as a reactant**:

$$CO_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow CO_3^{2-}$$

Without continuous CO₂ supply, the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte collapses, the cathode reaction cannot proceed, and the cell stops generating current. In practice, the CO₂ produced at the anode is *recycled* back to the cathode inlet — this is a defining engineering feature of MCFC systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation (7 marks):** Working principle + reactions (4) + CO₂ requirement (3).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Comparison Table — MCFC vs SOFC vs PAFC:**

| Parameter | PAFC | MCFC | SOFC |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| **Electrolyte** | Liquid H₃PO₄ in SiC matrix | Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ in LiAlO₂ | Solid YSZ ceramic |
| **State of Electrolyte** | Liquid (immobilized) | Liquid (molten) | Solid |
| **Operating Temperature** | 150–220°C | 600–700°C | 700–1000°C |
| **Mobile Ion** | H⁺ | CO₃²⁻ | O²⁻ |
| **Charge Carrier Direction** | Anode → Cathode | Cathode → Anode | Cathode → Anode |
| **Fuel Used** | Pure H₂ (CO tolerant to ~1000 ppm) | H₂, CH₄, CO (internal reforming) | H₂, CH₄, CO, higher hydrocarbons |
| **Electrical Efficiency** | 35–45% | 45–55% | 45–60% |
| **Catalyst Cost** | Pt (moderate loading) | Ni (cheap) | Ni/YSZ cermet (cheap) |
| **CO₂ Management** | Not required | Must recycle CO₂ from anode to cathode | Not required |
