| **Start-up Time** | 1–4 hours | ~8 hours | ~12+ hours |
| **Power Range** | 50 kW – 400 kW | 100 kW – 2 MW | 1 kW – multi-MW |
| **Primary Applications** | Stationary CHP (commercial buildings, hospitals) | Industrial-scale power plants, utility grid | Industrial CHP, data centers, utility-scale |
| **Commercial Maturity** | Mature (UTC PureCell, ONSI 200 kW) | Commercial (FuelCell Energy) | Emerging (Bloom Energy, Siemens) |

**Most fuel-flexible: SOFC**

SOFC is the most fuel-flexible because:
1. **Highest operating temperature (700–1000°C)** enables *internal reforming* of methane, natural gas, biogas, and even higher hydrocarbons directly inside the cell — no external reformer is required.
2. CO is not a poison; it is a **usable fuel** at these temperatures, participating directly in the electrochemical reaction:
   $$CO + O^{2-} \rightarrow CO_2 + 2e^-$$
3. The solid YSZ electrolyte is **chemically inert** to a wide variety of hydrocarbon fuels.
4. **No noble metal catalyst** is required — nickel-YSZ cermet anodes are inexpensive.

While MCFC is also fuel-flexible (CH₄, CO), its reliance on CO₂ recycling and lower temperature limit its fuel range compared to SOFC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation (7 marks):** Comparison table (4) + Most fuel-flexible identification with reasoning (3).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing charge-carrier direction** (H⁺ vs O²⁻/OH⁻/CO₃²⁻). Examiner deducts 1–2 marks if the mobile ion is mis-stated.
> 2. **Writing cathode reaction instead of anode reaction** (or vice versa). Always state "at the anode" and "at the cathode" explicitly.
> 3. **Quoting PEMFC efficiency as 80%+** — this is the *reversible* limit. Practical PEMFC is 40–60%.
> 4. **Confusing SOFC's "oxide ion" with "oxygen"** — the mobile species is O²⁻, not O₂.
> 5. **Forgetting CO₂ recycling** in MCFC. This is a **mandatory** point in any MCFC description.
> 6. **Writing MCFC electrolyte as "carbonate"** without specifying it is a *molten* mixture of Li₂CO₃ and K₂CO₃.
> 7. **Not mentioning water management** in PEMFC — a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 8. **Forgetting the role of temperature in CO tolerance** — the reverse water-gas shift equilibrium shift must be invoked, not just stated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fuel Cell Comparison:**

- ☐ **Six fuel cell types:** AFC, PEMFC, DMFC, PAFC, MCFC, SOFC — memorize in increasing temperature order.
- ☐ **Mobile ion table is the heart of comparison:** OH⁻ (AFC), H⁺ (PEM/PAFC/DMFC), CO₃²⁻ (MCFC), O²⁻ (SOFC).
- ☐ **Low-T cells (AFC, PEMFC, DMFC):** Need high-purity H₂, fast start-up, sensitive to CO and CO₂.
- ☐ **Mid-T cell (PAFC):** Most mature CHP technology, liquid H₃PO₄ electrolyte, CO-tolerant up to ~1%.
- ☐ **High-T cells (MCFC, SOFC):** Fuel-flexible (CH₄, CO), high efficiency, slow start-up, no noble metals.
- ☐ **Highest efficiency practical:** SOFC (45–60%); **Highest theoretical:** all H₂–O₂ cells = 83%.
- ☐ **Highest power density:** PEMFC (0.6–1.0 W/cm²) — preferred for transport.
- ☐ **Highest startup speed:** PEMFC (seconds) — preferred for transport.
- ☐ **Space applications:** AFC (pure O₂/H₂, no CO₂ poisoning, high efficiency, lightweight).
- ☐ **Automotive:** PEMFC (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO).
- ☐ **Stationary CHP:** SOFC (Bloom Energy), PAFC (UTC), MCFC (FuelCell Energy).
- ☐ **Portable electronics:** DMFC (methanol cartridge).
- ☐ **MCFC must recycle CO₂** from anode exhaust back to cathode inlet — defining feature.
- ☐ **CO tolerance** scales with operating temperature — SOFC > MCFC > PAFC > PEMFC ≈ AFC.
- ☐ **Reversible cell voltage** $E^0_{rev} = 1.229 \text{ V}$ is identical for all H₂–O₂ cells; differences are in *losses*, not thermodynamics.
- ☐ **Nernst equation** governs how $E$ varies with partial pressures:
   $$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}{p_{H_2O}}\right)$$
- ☐ **Practical efficiency = real cell voltage / thermoneutral voltage (1.481 V).**

> **End of Module 4 — Fuel Cell Comparison Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Combustion Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Combustion Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydrogen Combustion Engine (HCE)** is an internal combustion engine (ICE) that uses **gaseous hydrogen (H₂)** as its primary fuel, combusting it with an oxidizer (typically air) in a controlled thermodynamic cycle to convert the chemical energy of the hydrogen–oxygen reaction into useful mechanical work via a piston–crankshaft assembly.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, hydrogen combustion engines are studied as a **bridge technology** between conventional fossil-fuel ICEs and the longer-term hydrogen fuel-cell powertrain. They include three principal variants:

1. **Hydrogen Spark-Ignition (SI) Engines** — port-fuel injected or direct-injected hydrogen, ignited by spark.
2. **Hydrogen Fumigation Engines** — hydrogen is inducted into a conventional diesel engine as a *supplementary* fuel, while diesel acts as the pilot ignition source.
3. **Hydrogen Dual-Fuel Engines** — hydrogen and a liquid hydrocarbon share combustion duty, often with hydrogen substituting 60–90 % of the energy input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> The scope explicitly covers the **combustion chemistry**, **engine modifications**, **performance & emission characteristics**, and **safety issues** specific to running hydrogen in reciprocating engines. Expect derivation-type and comparison-type questions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Stove That Won't Wait"

Imagine you are cooking dinner on a **natural-gas stove** versus an **hydrogen stove**. Both burn cleanly, but hydrogen ignites *almost instantly* when even a tiny spark is present, and its flame propagates **8–10 times faster** through the mixture. In an engine cylinder, this is the central engineering challenge: hydrogen is so eager to burn that it often **ignites before the spark plug fires** (called **pre-ignition**) or **flashes back through the intake manifold** (called **backfire**).

Think of gasoline as a *cautious, slow-burning log*, diesel as a *patient, hot ember*, and hydrogen as a *volatile, hyper-reactive kindling* — extremely clean and powerful, but it must be handled with deliberate engineering discipline.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Gasoline (reference) |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight | **2.016 g/mol** | ≈ 100 g/mol |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **120 MJ/kg** | 44 MJ/kg |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **142 MJ/kg** | 48 MJ/kg |
| Density (STP, gas) | **0.0899 kg/m³** | ≈ 730 kg/m³ |
| Density (liquid, 20 K) | **70.8 kg/m³** | ≈ 730 kg/m³ |
| Flammability range (in air, vol%) | **4 – 75 %** | 1 – 7.6 % |
| Auto-ignition temperature | **500 – 571 °C** | 246 °C |
| Laminar flame speed | **2.7 – 3.5 m/s** | 0.4 m/s |
| Minimum ignition energy | **0.02 mJ** | 0.24 mJ |
| Quenching distance | **0.64 mm** | 2.0 mm |
| Stoichiometric A/F (mass) | **34.3 : 1** | 14.7 : 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> The **minimum ignition energy of hydrogen is roughly 1/12th that of gasoline**, and its **quenching distance is roughly 1/3rd** — these two facts alone explain nearly every combustion anomaly in hydrogen engines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hydrogen Combustion Reaction

The combustion of hydrogen in pure oxygen is a single-step, highly exothermic reaction:

$$2\,H_2 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,H_2O_{(l)} + 572\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$2\,H_2 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,H_2O_{(g)} + 484\ \text{kJ/mol} \quad (\text{LHV basis})$$

When air (≈ 21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂ by volume) is used, the nitrogen acts as a thermal ballast and reduces the *useful* energy recovered per kilogram of fuel but introduces **NOₓ formation pathways**.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — How a Hydrogen SI Engine Works

Step-by-step breakdown of the **4-stroke hydrogen SI cycle**:

1. **Intake Stroke** — A precisely metered quantity of hydrogen is injected either into the intake port (PFI) or directly into the cylinder (DI). The mixture is *extremely* lean relative to stoichiometric (equivalence ratio φ ≈ 0.3 – 0.6) to suppress backfire and pre-ignition.
2. **Compression Stroke** — The mixture is compressed to a typical ratio of **10:1 to 14:1** (intentionally lower than gasoline's 12:1 – 16:1) because hydrogen's high flame speed and broad flammability range make knock-like pre-ignition otherwise inevitable.
3. **Combustion / Power Stroke** — Spark ignition initiates a flame kernel that propagates rapidly. Because of hydrogen's high flame speed, **burn duration is only 30–40 % of that for gasoline**, yielding a near-constant-volume heat release (high efficiency).
4. **Exhaust Stroke** — The primary product is water vapour. Only trace quantities of CO and unburnt HC are present (no carbon in the fuel), but **NOₓ can be substantial** due to the high adiabatic flame temperature.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Boundary | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{P \cdot \dot{V}_{th}}{Q_{LHV} \cdot \eta_{th}}$ | Specific fuel consumption from power & efficiency | kg/s |
| 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{stoich}}{(F/A)_{actual}}$ | Equivalence ratio (φ = 1 stoich, φ < 1 lean, φ > 1 rich) | — |
| 3 | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{n_{air}\,M_{air}}{n_{fuel}\,M_{fuel}}$ | Stoichiometric mass air-fuel ratio | kg/kg |
| 4 | $Q_{released} = \dot{m}_f \cdot LHV$ | Chemical-to-thermal power conversion | W |
| 5 | $T_{ad} = T_1 + \dfrac{\phi \cdot Q_{LHV}}{c_{p,avg}}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature (ideal gas approximation) | K |
| 6 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | Otto-cycle ideal efficiency (function of compression ratio *r* and γ) | — |
| 7 | $S_L \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha \cdot Y_{H_2}}{\rho_{mix}}}$ | Laminar flame speed scaling (with thermal diffusivity α, mass fraction Y, mixture density ρ) | m/s |
| 8 | $\Delta h_{vap} \approx 0$ | Hydrogen arrives as a gas → no enthalpy of vaporization penalty (unlike gasoline) | — |
| 9 | $\text{BSEC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Energy Consumption | g/kWh |
| 10 | $\eta_{brake} = \dfrac{P_{brake}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** When a problem asks for "the energy released per kg of hydrogen", use the **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** (since exhaust water leaves as vapour). Use **HHV = 142 MJ/kg** only if the problem explicitly states the water is condensed.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydrogen combustion engines are not just an academic exercise — they form the engineering basis for:

- **BMW Hydrogen 7 (2006)** and **Toyota Hydrogen ICE prototype** — demonstration vehicles with hydrogen SI engines.
- **Industrial hydrogen–diesel dual-fuel gensets** used in ports, mining, and marine auxiliary power, where hydrogen injection reduces diesel consumption by 20–40 % and slashes soot/PM emissions by up to 80 %.
- **Hybrid hydrogen–battery off-road vehicles** (e.g., Komatsu mining trucks) using hydrogen ICE as a range extender.

The key advantage is that **existing engine manufacturing lines** can be retrofitted with minimal capital re-investment, unlike fuel cells which require an entirely new supply chain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio for Hydrogen Combustion

**Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation (with air as the oxidizer).**

$$2\,H_2 + (O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \longrightarrow 2\,H_2O + 3.76\,N_2$$

The factor **3.76** arises because air contains 79 % N₂ and 21 % O₂ by volume, giving a mole ratio of $N_2/O_2 = 79/21 \approx 3.76$.

**Step 2 — Compute the masses of reactants (per 2 moles of H₂).**

Molar mass of H₂ = 2 × 1.008 = **2.016 g/mol**

$$\text{Mass of } 2\,H_2 = 2 \times 2.016 = 4.032\ \text{g}$$

Molar mass of air (O₂ + 3.76 N₂) = 32 + (3.76 × 28) = **137.28 g/mol**

$$\text{Mass of air} = 137.28\ \text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Form the mass-based air-to-fuel ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(A/F)_{stoich} &= \dfrac{\text{Mass of air}}{\text{Mass of fuel}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{137.28}{4.032} \\[4pt]
&= 34.04 \approx \mathbf{34.3\ kg\ air\ /\ kg\ H_2}
\end{aligned}$$

> This value is **~2.3× higher than gasoline (14.7:1)**. Implication: a hydrogen engine must induct ~2.3× more mass of mixture for the same energy release, but since gaseous H₂ has very low density, the **volumetric efficiency** of hydrogen engines is markedly reduced — a key design problem.

### 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Otto-Cycle Efficiency for a Hydrogen Engine

**Step 1 — State the ideal Otto efficiency equation.**

$$\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

where *r* = compression ratio, γ = ratio of specific heats (≈ 1.4 for diatomic gases).

**Step 2 — Evaluate for a hydrogen SI engine with r = 12.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th} &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{12^{(1.4-1)}} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \dfrac{1}{12^{0.4}} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.7019} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.3701 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.6299} = \mathbf{62.99\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with gasoline (r = 9, due to knock limit).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th,\,gasoline} &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{9^{0.4}} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.408} = 1 - 0.415 = \mathbf{58.5\%}
\end{aligned}$$

> The ~4.5 percentage-point advantage of hydrogen is partly offset by real-world losses (heat transfer, friction, pumping). Indicated efficiencies of 50–55 % are routinely measured on hydrogen SI engines.

### 3.3 Derivation: Energy Released per Unit Volume of Mixture (Why Hydrogen Engines Lose Power)

**Step 1 — Compute the LHV per mole of hydrogen.**

$$Q_{LHV,\,per\,mole} = \dfrac{242\ \text{kJ/mol}}{1} = 242\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

(Note: 242 kJ/mol corresponds to LHV where H₂O exits as vapour; the precise modern value used in industry is 241.8 kJ/mol.)

**Step 2 — Compute moles of H₂ in 1 m³ at STP.**

$$n = \dfrac{PV}{RT} = \dfrac{101325 \times 1}{8.314 \times 273.15} = 44.615\ \text{mol}$$

$$n_{H_2} = 44.615\ \text{mol} \rightarrow Q = 44.615 \times 242 = 10{,}797\ \text{kJ} = 10.8\ \text{MJ per m}^3 \text{ of pure H}_2$$

**Step 3 — For a stoichiometric mixture (1 mol H₂ : 2.38 mol air), 1 mol H₂ occupies 3.38 mol of mixture. Energy per m³ of *stoichiometric mixture* =**

$$\dfrac{10.8}{3.38} = 3.19\ \text{MJ/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compare to gasoline vapour (modelled as iso-octane vapour at λ=1):**

Gasoline-air mixture at stoich ≈ 3.5 – 3.8 MJ/m³ (gasoline vapour at intake conditions).

> Although hydrogen has **~2.8× the energy density by mass**, its energy density **per unit volume of intake mixture is actually slightly LOWER than gasoline**. This is why **direct injection (DI) of hydrogen** or **supercharging** is essential to recover power density.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hydrogen SI vs Hydrogen Diesel-Fumigation vs Hydrogen CI

| Parameter | Hydrogen SI (DI, lean) | Hydrogen Fumigation (diesel pilot) | Hydrogen CI (pure) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compression ratio | 10 – 14 : 1 | 16 – 18 : 1 (diesel) | 22+ : 1 (impractical) |
| Ignition source | Spark plug | Diesel pilot | Auto-ignition (very hard to control) |
| Load control | Throttle / λ variation | Diesel quantity | Difficult |
| Power output | 80 – 100 % of gasoline baseline | 70 – 90 % of diesel baseline | < 50 %, abnormal combustion |
| NOx emission | High (>1000 ppm at rich) | Moderate (200–500 ppm) | Lowest (lean) |
| HC / CO emission | Very low | Low | Negligible |
| CO₂ emission | Zero (tank-to-wheel) | 30 – 40 % of pure diesel | Zero |
| Combustion anomaly | Backfire, pre-ignition | Misfire, knock | Surface ignition |
| Maturity / TRL | TRL 6–7 (prototype) | TRL 8–9 (commercial gensets) | TRL 2–3 (lab only) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrogen SI Engine — Port-Fuel-Injected System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2TANK["H2 STORAGE TANK 350 bar or 700 bar"] --> REG["PRESSURE REGULATOR two stage"]
    REG --> ECND["ELECTRONICALLY CONTROLLED SOLENOID INJECTOR"]
    INTAKE["INTAKE MANIFOLD low pressure rail"] --> ECND
    ECND --> CYL1["CYLINDER 1 spark ignited"]
    ECND --> CYL2["CYLINDER 2 spark ignited"]
    ECND --> CYL3["CYLINDER 3 spark ignited"]
    ECND --> CYL4["CYLINDER 4 spark ignited"]
    ECU["ENGINE CONTROL UNIT ECU maps lambda and timing"] -.-> ECND
    ECU -.-> SPARK["SPARK PLUG DRIVER"]
    SPARK --> CYL1
    SPARK --> CYL2
    SPARK --> CYL3
    SPARK --> CYL4
    LAMBDA["WIDEBAND LAMBDA SENSOR UEGO type"] -.-> ECU
    CYL1 --> EXH["EXHAUST MANIFOLD primarily H2O vapour"]
    CYL2 --> EXH
    CYL3 --> EXH
    CYL4 --> EXH
    EXH --> CATALYST["THREE WAY CATALYST with NOx trap"]
    CATALYST --> TAIL["TAILPIPE water vapour emission"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** trace the **hydrogen flow path** from tank to cylinder to tailpipe.
- The **dashed arrows** trace the **electronic feedback control loop** — the ECU reads the lambda sensor and modulates the injectors to maintain a precise lean mixture (φ ≈ 0.4 – 0.6).
- The **spark-plug driver line** indicates that each cylinder has its own independent ignition coil for precise ignition timing.

### 4.2 Hydrogen Fumigation in a Diesel Engine — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AIRIN["FRESH AIR INTAKE from turbocharger or atmospheric"] --> DIESELCYL["DIESEL CYLINDER compression ratio 17 to 1"]
    H2SUPPLY["H2 SUPPLY manifold low pressure"] --> H2INJ["H2 INJECTOR pulse width modulated by ECU"]
    H2INJ --> INTAKEMIX["INTAKE MANIFOLD H2 air premix"]
    INTAKEMIX --> DIESELCYL
    DIESELPUMP["DIESEL FUEL INJECTION PUMP pilot quantity only"] --> DIINJ["DIESEL INJECTOR"]
    DIINJ --> DIESELCYL
    DIESELCYL --> POWER["PISTON CRANKSHAFT MECHANICAL POWER OUTPUT"]
    DIESELCYL --> EGAS["EXHAUST GAS reduced soot and PM lower CO2"]
    DIESELCYL --> KNOCK["PRE MIXED KNOCK or ABNORMAL COMBUSTION if H2 mass fraction too high"]
    ECU["ECU dual fuel strategy module"] -.-> H2INJ
    ECU -.-> DIINJ
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Notice the **dual-path fuel architecture**: hydrogen is premixed with air in the intake manifold, while a *small* diesel pilot charge is injected directly to provide the **ignition kernel**.
- The **"PRE MIXED KNOCK"** node is highlighted because it is the **single most common failure mode** of fumigation engines — too much hydrogen or too low cetane of the pilot fuel causes uncontrolled pre-ignition.

### 4.3 Abnormal Combustion Mode Flowchart — Diagnostic Logic for HCE Faults

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Engine running on H2"] --> CHECK{"Knock sensor or pressure transducer signal?"}
    CHECK -->|"abnormal high pressure peak before TDC"| PREIGN["PRE IGNITION auto ignition of H2 ahead of spark"]
    CHECK -->|"flame propagation into intake manifold"| BACKFIRE["BACKFIRE H2 ignites in intake"]
    CHECK -->|"cylinder pressure spike during burn"| KNOCK["KNOCK end gas auto ignition"]
    PREIGN --> FIX1["REDUCE compression ratio by 1 to 2 points or RETARD timing"]
    BACKFIRE --> FIX2["INSTALL flame arrestor at intake and INJECT later in cycle"]
    KNOCK --> FIX3["ENRICH mixture to stoichiometric briefly and RETARD spark"]
    FIX1 --> RUN["Engine stable"]
    FIX2 --> RUN
    FIX3 --> RUN
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is hydrogen considered a near-zero-carbon fuel in an internal combustion engine, but it can still produce harmful emissions?**

> **Model Answer:**
> Hydrogen contains no carbon atoms, so the ideal combustion product is only **water vapour (H₂O)** — therefore carbon-based emissions such as CO, CO₂, and unburnt hydrocarbons are essentially **zero** in a well-tuned hydrogen engine. However, at high flame temperatures (> 1800 K) the nitrogen and oxygen in the intake air react via the **Zeldovich mechanism** to form **nitrogen oxides (NOₓ)**, primarily NO and NO₂. These are regulated pollutants and contribute to smog and acid rain. Hence hydrogen is "near-zero-carbon" but not zero-emission in practice.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the flammability limits and minimum ignition energy of hydrogen in air and explain their significance for engine design.**

> **Model Answer:**
> The flammability limits of H₂ in air are **4 % to 75 % by volume**, and the minimum ignition energy is **0.02 mJ** (compared to 0.24 mJ for gasoline). The significance is twofold:
> 1. The **extremely wide flammability range** means hydrogen can burn in both very lean and very rich mixtures, allowing flexibility in load control, but it also means hot residual gases can still ignite a fresh charge — causing **backfire**.
> 2. The **very low minimum ignition energy** means even stray static discharge, hot carbon deposits, or glowing valve edges can ignite the mixture — causing **pre-ignition and surface ignition**.
> Engine design must therefore use **flame arrestors at the intake**, **cooled exhaust valves**, and **precise ignition timing** to prevent uncontrolled combustion.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU 2024 ESE Module 4 — Internal Choice Pattern]**

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio for hydrogen combustion in air. Show clearly why the value is approximately 34:1. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The balanced equation for hydrogen combustion with air as oxidizer is:

$$2\,H_2 + (O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \longrightarrow 2\,H_2O + 3.76\,N_2$$

**Step 1 — Identify molecular weights.**

M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol; M(O₂) = 32.00 g/mol; M(N₂) = 28.014 g/mol; M(air) = 0.21 × 32 + 0.79 × 28 = 28.84 g/mol (molar average).

**Step 2 — Compute mass of reactants per 2 moles of H₂.**

Mass of 2 mol H₂ = 2 × 2.016 = **4.032 g**

Mass of air required = (1 mol O₂ + 3.76 mol N₂) × molar mass ratio:

$$m_{air} = (1 \times 32) + (3.76 \times 28) = 32 + 105.28 = 137.28\ \text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Form the A/F ratio.**

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{137.28}{4.032} = 34.05 \approx \boxed{34.3 : 1}$$

The value is higher than gasoline's 14.7:1 because **hydrogen has a very low molecular weight**, so a given mass of fuel requires a proportionally larger mass of air to supply the stoichiometric oxygen.

**(b)** A hydrogen-fuelled SI engine delivers 50 kW of brake power at an indicated thermal efficiency of 40 % and a mechanical efficiency of 85 %. Calculate the **mass flow rate of hydrogen** consumed and the **brake specific energy consumption (BSEC)** in g/kWh. Use LHV(H₂) = 120 MJ/kg. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute brake (delivered) thermal efficiency.**

$$\eta_{brake} = \eta_{indicated} \times \eta_{mechanical} = 0.40 \times 0.85 = 0.34$$

**Step 2 — Compute the chemical power input from fuel.**

$$P_{chem} = \dfrac{P_{brake}}{\eta_{brake}} = \dfrac{50}{0.34} = 147.06\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute mass flow rate of H₂.**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{P_{chem}}{LHV} = \dfrac{147.06 \times 10^3}{120 \times 10^6} = 1.226 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx 4.41\ \text{kg/h}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Brake Specific Energy Consumption.**

$$\begin{aligned}
BSEC &= \dfrac{\dot{m}_{H_2} \times 3600}{P_{brake}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1.226 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600}{50} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{4.4136}{50} = 0.08827\ \text{kg/kWh} \\[4pt]
&= \boxed{88.3\ \text{g/kWh}}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** For comparison, a gasoline engine of similar power has BSEC ≈ 240–280 g/kWh. The hydrogen engine consumes **roughly 1/3 the mass flow** because of hydrogen's much higher mass-energy density.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with suitable reasoning the **two principal abnormal combustion phenomena** encountered in a hydrogen-fuelled SI engine. How is each mitigated in modern engine design? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Abnormal Phenomenon 1 — Backfire.**

*Definition:* Backfire is the **combustion of the hydrogen–air mixture inside the intake manifold**, propagating backwards from the cylinder through the intake valve.

*Root Cause:* Hydrogen has a **laminar flame speed of 2.7–3.5 m/s** (8–10× that of gasoline) and a **minimum ignition energy of just 0.02 mJ**. If the intake valve is still slightly open or if residual hot gas lingers in the intake port when the piston is on its intake stroke, the hydrogen charge can ignite from hot residual gases or even from reflected pressure waves from a previous combustion event.

*Mitigation Strategies:*
1. **Late-cycle injection** — Inject H₂ just before or after the intake valve closes (direct injection) so the fuel never resides in the hot intake port long enough to ignite.
2. **Flame arrestors** in the intake manifold — sintered metal plugs that quench any flame front.
3. **Cooled intake ports** with water jackets.
4. **Operate at very lean equivalence ratios (φ < 0.5)** to reduce flame speed and energy density of the charge.

**Abnormal Phenomenon 2 — Pre-ignition.**

*Definition:* Pre-ignition is the **unintended ignition of the end-gas before the spark plug fires**, caused by hot spots inside the combustion chamber igniting the mixture early.

*Root Cause:* Hydrogen's **broad flammability range (4 – 75 % in air)** and **low ignition energy (0.02 mJ)** mean that even **small hot spots** (glowing carbon deposits, hot spark-plug electrodes, hot exhaust valves, or auto-ignition from high compression temperatures) can ignite the mixture. Additionally, hydrogen has a **high auto-ignition temperature of 500–571 °C**, but **localised hot spots in engines routinely exceed 800–1000 °C**.

*Mitigation Strategies:*
1. **Reduce compression ratio** from typical gasoline values (12–14) down to **10–12** to limit end-gas temperature.
2. **Use cooled exhaust valves** (sodium-filled, hollow stems) and **water-cooled spark-plug electrodes**.
3. **Run lean (φ ≈ 0.3 – 0.5)** to keep end-gas below auto-ignition temperature.
4. **Pre-chamber or dual-plug designs** for more controlled flame initiation.
5. **Clean combustion chamber** — eliminate carbon deposits (which form even from lubricating oil).

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe a **hydrogen fumigation system in a diesel engine**. Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of this approach. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**System Description:**
In a hydrogen fumigation system, **gaseous hydrogen is injected into the intake manifold** of a conventional diesel engine, where it mixes with the incoming air. A **small quantity of diesel fuel** is then injected directly into the cylinder near the end of the compression stroke. The diesel auto-ignites under the high compression temperature, providing the **ignition kernel** that subsequently ignites the premixed hydrogen–air charge. The diesel acts purely as a **pilot fuel**, supplying perhaps only 10–20 % of the total energy. An ECU continuously modulates the hydrogen mass-flow based on the load demand.

*(Refer to Section 4.2 — the Mermaid block diagram above serves as the required sketch.)*

**Advantages:**

1. **No engine redesign needed** — existing diesel blocks can be retrofitted with only a hydrogen rail and injectors in the intake.
2. **Significant CO₂ reduction** — 20–40 % depending on hydrogen substitution rate.
3. **Massive soot / PM reduction** — up to 80–90 %, since hydrogen has no carbon to form particulates.
4. **Maintains diesel-class thermal efficiency** (40–45 %), much higher than Otto-cycle engines.
5. **Better combustion stability** because the diesel pilot provides a reliable ignition source.
6. **Lowest capital cost** among hydrogen engine options.

**Limitations:**

1. **Power density reduction** — hydrogen displaces some intake air, lowering volumetric efficiency; power output drops 10–30 % at high substitution rates.
2. **Pre-mixed knock** when hydrogen energy fraction exceeds ~70–80 %; engine damage possible.
3. **Range limited** by onboard H₂ storage (typically 350-bar or 700-bar tanks occupy significant volume).
4. **NOₓ emissions can increase** if not carefully controlled with EGR or SCR.
5. **Dual fuel infrastructure** required on the vehicle (both H₂ and diesel systems).
6. **Hydrogen embrittlement** of high-pressure steel components remains a long-term reliability concern.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 4 Hydrogen Combustion Engine Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LHV vs HHV** — Examiners specifically penalise 1–2 marks if a student uses HHV = 142 MJ/kg in a context where LHV = 120 MJ/kg is appropriate (i.e., when water exits as vapour, which is the *engine-cylinder* condition).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 3.76 nitrogen factor** in the air-fuel ratio derivation — leads to an incorrect value of ~8:1 instead of 34:1. **Always** include the inert N₂ moles when computing air-mass A/F.
>
> 3. **Mixing up pre-ignition and backfire** — Pre-ignition is **inside the cylinder before spark**; backfire is **inside the intake manifold**. Examiners deduct 1 mark if definitions are swapped.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the mechanical efficiency** in two-stage efficiency problems — students frequently compute fuel mass-flow using *indicated* efficiency when the *brake* efficiency is needed (multiply by mechanical efficiency first).
>
> 5. **Stating "hydrogen produces zero emissions"** without qualifying NOₓ — this is a topper's trap. Hydrogen has zero *carbon* emissions but can still emit significant NOₓ.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Hydrogen has **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** and **HHV = 142 MJ/kg**; use LHV for all engine calculations.
- Stoichiometric **A/F by mass = 34.3 : 1** (about 2.3× gasoline's 14.7 : 1).
- Stoichiometric **A/F by volume (gas)** ≈ 2.38 : 1; gaseous hydrogen displaces intake air → volumetric efficiency loss.
- **Flammability range** is 4 – 75 % in air — extremely wide → both advantage (lean operation possible) and disadvantage (backfire risk).
- **Minimum ignition energy** = 0.02 mJ — explains susceptibility to pre-ignition.
- **Laminar flame speed** ≈ 3 m/s — explains backfire and short burn duration.
- **Quenching distance** = 0.64 mm — flame can travel through narrow gaps (e.g., crevice volumes), making hydrogen engines more prone to flame quenching or abnormal propagation.
- **NOₓ** is the **only** pollutant of concern in pure hydrogen engines; controlled via lean burn, EGR, or SCR.
- **Backfire** = combustion in intake manifold; **Pre-ignition** = combustion in cylinder before spark — **do not confuse them**.
- **Hydrogen fumigation** = H₂ in diesel intake, diesel as pilot — most commercially mature variant.
- **Maximum theoretical Otto efficiency** = $1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$; hydrogen engines achieve real efficiencies of **40–45 %** (SI) and **40–42 %** (dual-fuel).
- **Direct injection of hydrogen** is the preferred strategy to recover power density lost to volumetric efficiency.
- **Liquid hydrogen (20 K)** dramatically increases energy density per unit volume but introduces boil-off losses and complex cryogenic storage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Turbines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hydrogen Turbines # 1. Hydrogen Turbines: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydrogen Turbine (H₂ Gas Turbine):** A thermal turbomachine that converts the chemical energy stored in molecular hydrogen (H₂) into mechanical shaft work (and subsequently electrical energy) via an internal combustion thermodynamic cycle (Brayton cycle). The system ingests ambient air through an axial/centrifugal compressor, mixes and combusts it with hydrogen fuel in a high-pressure combustion chamber, and expands the resulting hot, high-pressure gas stream through a turbine coupled to a generator. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, hydrogen turbines are classified into two engineering categories: - **Hydrogen-Ready Turbines:** Existing natural gas turbines modified to accept H₂/CH₄ fuel blends (e.g., 30–50 % H₂ by volume), and eventually 100 % H₂. - **Dedicated Hydrogen Turbines:** Newly designed units optimized exclusively for pure H₂ combustion. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – The "Supercharged Pressure Cooker with a Spinning Blade":** > Imagine a pressure cooker (combustion chamber) being force-fed air by a powerful fan (compressor) and pure hydrogen gas. When ignited, the flame is so intense and fast that the expanding gases push against a multi-bladed fan (turbine), making it spin at thousands of RPM. This spinning shaft can then drive a generator to make electricity. The key difference from a natural gas turbine? Hydrogen burns **14 times faster**, across a much **wider flammability range (4–75 % in air)**, and at a **higher flame temperature (~2,400 °C)** — making it powerful but tricky to control, like a race car engine running on rocket fuel. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Combustion Properties of Hydrogen (Pure H₂ vs. Methane CH₄):** > - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂:** **120 MJ/kg** (≈ 3× that of gasoline) > - **Flammability Limits in Air:** **4 % to 75 % by volume** (methane: 5–15 %) > - **Laminar Flame Speed:** **~2.5–3.5 m/s** (methane: ~0.4 m/s) — roughly **7–8× faster** > - **Auto-Ignition Temperature:** **~500 °C** (≈ 570 K) > - **Minimum Ignition Energy:** **0.02 mJ** (about **10× lower** than methane) > - **Adiabatic Flame Temperature in Air:** **~2,400 °C** > - **Density at STP:** **0.0899 kg/m³** (very light, escapes quickly) > - **Diffusion Coefficient in Air:** **~0.61 cm²/s** (highly diffusive) These metrics govern every design decision in a hydrogen turbine — from blade cooling to combustion liner geometry. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Operating Principle (Open Brayton Cycle) A hydrogen turbine operates on the **Open Brayton Cycle**, comprising four internal processes: 1. **Process 1 → 2 (Isentropic Compression):** Ambient air is compressed in an axial/centrifugal compressor. Pressure ratio typically **8:1 to 23:1** for modern F-class machines, up to **45:1** for advanced H-class units. 2. **Process 2 → 3 (Constant-Pressure Combustion):** Compressed air is mixed with hydrogen in a **Dry Low-NOx (DLN)** or **Sequential Combustion (SEV)** chamber and burned at near-constant pressure. 3. **Process 3 → 4 (Isentropic Expansion):** Hot combustion gases expand through the turbine stages, delivering shaft work. 4. **Process 4 → 1 (Constant-Pressure Heat Rejection):** Exhaust gases are released to the atmosphere (open cycle) or routed to an HRSG in a combined cycle. > [!NOTE] > **Why Hydrogen, Why Now?** > Hydrogen combustion produces **zero carbon (CO₂) emissions** at the point of use, making hydrogen turbines a critical **decarbonization lever** for hard-to-abate sectors like heavy industry, peaking power, and grid balancing (where intermittent renewables need firm, dispatchable backup). ## 2.2 Hydrogen-Specific Combustion Challenges | Challenge | Engineering Root Cause | Mitigation Strategy | |---|---|---| | **Flashback** | High flame speed (3.5 m/s) can travel upstream into injectors | **Micro-Mix Lean Prevaporized (LPP)** combustors; high-velocity jets | | **Auto-Ignition** | Auto-ignition at 500 °C is below compressor discharge temperature (~400–450 °C in modern F-class) | Pre-mixing chamber cooling, fuel staging | | **High NOx** | Flame temperature ~2,400 °C exceeds the **Zeldovich thermal-NOx threshold (1,500 K)** | DLN combustors, lean pre-mix, sequential combustion | | **Material Stress** | High H₂O partial pressure causes **Hot Corrosion** and **High-Temperature Hydrogen Attack (HTHA)** | Inconel 740H blades, TBC (Thermal Barrier Coatings), CMSX-4 superalloys | | **Wide Flammability** | 4–75 % range means leaks form explosive mixtures easily | Inert purging with N₂, leak detection sensors, vented enclosures | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Equations for Hydrogen Turbine Analysis** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dot{W}_{net} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_3 - h_4) - \dot{m}_{air} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | Net specific work output of gas turbine | kJ/kg | | 2 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{net}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$ | Ideal Brayton thermal efficiency (cold air-standard) | dimensionless | | 3 | $\eta_{th,\,ideal} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | Ideal Brayton efficiency as function of pressure ratio $r_p$ | dimensionless | | 4 | $\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{8 \cdot M_{O_2}}{M_{H_2}} \approx 34.3$ | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for pure H₂ | kg air/kg H₂ | | 5 | $\phi = \dfrac{(\text{AFR})_{stoich}}{(\text{AFR})_{actual}}$ | Equivalence ratio (lean: $\phi < 1$; rich: $\phi > 1$) | dimensionless | | 6 | $T_{3,\,actual} = T_2 + \dfrac{\phi \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{comb}}{c_{p,\,air}(1 + \phi \cdot \text{AFR}_{stoich})}$ | Combustion chamber exit temperature | K | | 7 | $\dot{m}_{NOx} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{flame}}\right)$ | Zeldovich thermal-NOx correlation (exponential of flame temperature) | kg/s | | 8 | $\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_{p,exh} \cdot (T_4 - T_{stack})$ | Heat available for Combined Cycle HRSG | kW | | 9 | $\eta_{combined} = \eta_{GT} + \eta_{ST} \cdot (1 - \eta_{GT})$ | Simple Combined Cycle (GT + ST) efficiency approximation | dimensionless | **Boundary Conditions / Validity Notes:** - Eq. 3 assumes **ideal cold-air-standard Brayton cycle** (constant $c_p$, $\gamma = 1.4$). - Eq. 6 assumes **complete combustion** and constant-pressure heat addition. - Eq. 4 uses molecular weights $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol and $M_{O_2} = 32$ g/mol. - All temperatures in **Kelvin (K)** for thermodynamic consistency; convert °C using $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Grid Peaking Plants:** Hydrogen turbines provide **fast-ramp backup power** (ramp rates of 30 MW/min) for grids with high solar/wind penetration (e.g., Siemens Energy Silyzer, GE 7HA.03). - **Industrial Decarbonization:** Replacing CH₄-fired turbines in refineries, steel plants, and chemical complexes. - **Co-firing Transition:** Allows existing gas infrastructure (LNG terminals, pipelines) to be gradually decarbonized through H₂ blending. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Pure Hydrogen > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** Derive the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass basis) for the complete combustion of pure hydrogen in atmospheric air, and verify the value as **34.3 kg air / kg H₂**. ### Step 1 — Write the Balanced Combustion Equation The complete combustion of hydrogen produces water vapor: $$ H_2 + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O + \text{Energy} $$ Multiplying by 2 to avoid fractions: $$ 2H_2 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2H_2O $$ ### Step 2 — Convert to Molar Basis From the balanced equation: - 2 moles of $H_2$ react with 1 mole of $O_2$ - Molar mass of $H_2$ = $2.016$ g/mol - Molar mass of $O_2$ = $32.00$ g/mol ### Step 3 — Account for Air Composition Atmospheric air contains approximately **21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume** (and mole fraction). Therefore, for every 1 mole of $O_2$ consumed, $79/21 \approx 3.76$ moles of $N_2$ are carried in. $$ \text{Air per mole of } H_2 = \frac{1}{2} \text{ mol } O_2 \times \left(1 + \frac{79}{21}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.76 = 2.38 \text{ mol air} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert to Mass Basis $$ \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{n_{air} \cdot M_{air}}{n_{H_2} \cdot M_{H_2}} $$ Where average molecular weight of air $M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol: $$ \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{2.38 \times 28.97}{1 \times 2.016} $$ $$ \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{68.948}{2.016} \approx 34.20 $$ ### Step 5 — Verification $$ \boxed{AFR_{stoich} \approx 34.3 \; \frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg H}_2}} $$ This is **~2.4× higher** than methane's AFR (17.2), meaning hydrogen turbines must move **more air mass** per unit of fuel — a key design driver for compressor sizing. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Ideal Brayton Thermal Efficiency as a Function of Pressure Ratio > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** Starting from the First Law for an open, steady-flow Brayton cycle with cold-air-standard assumptions (constant $c_p$, constant $\gamma = 1.4$), derive the expression $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$. ### Step 1 — Define the Four States - State 1: Compressor inlet (ambient) - State 2: Compressor exit / Combustor inlet - State 3: Turbine inlet (TIT — Turbine Inlet Temperature) - State 4: Turbine exit / Exhaust ### Step 2 — Compressor Work (Process 1→2, Isentropic) For an isentropic process of an ideal gas: $$ T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} $$ Therefore, specific compressor work: $$ w_c = c_p (T_2 - T_1) = c_p \cdot T_1 \cdot \left(r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - 1\right) $$ ### Step 3 — Turbine Work (Process 3→4, Isentropic) Similarly: $$ T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}} $$ $$ w_t = c_p (T_3 - T_4) = c_p \cdot T_3 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}\right) $$ ### Step 4 — Net Work Output $$ w_{net} = w_t - w_c $$ $$ w_{net} = c_p \cdot \left[T_3 \left(1 - r_p^{-(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}\right) - T_1 \left(r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - 1\right)\right] $$ ### Step 5 — Heat Input (Process 2→3, Isobaric) $$ q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = c_p \cdot \left(T_3 - T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}\right) $$ ### Step 6 — Thermal Efficiency $$ \eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} $$ For a **simple cycle where all compressor work is recovered by the turbine** (i.e., $w_{net} = w_t - w_c$, and the cycle is closed back to State 1), under the **cold-air-standard assumption** ($T_1 = T_4$ — i.e., the exhaust temperature equals the inlet temperature): $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}} $$ ### Step 7 — Final Boxed Result $$ \boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}} $$ ### Step 8 — Numerical Illustration (γ = 1.4) For $r_p = 10$ and $\gamma = 1.4$: $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{10^{0.4/1.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{10^{0.2857}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.9307} \approx 0.482 $$ So a 10:1 pressure ratio yields **~48.2 % ideal thermal efficiency**. Real-world F-class turbines achieve ~42–44 % due to component inefficiencies. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: H₂ vs. CH₄ in Turbine Applications > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Quick-Reference Comparison Table** | Parameter | Pure H₂ | CH₄ (Natural Gas) | Engineering Implication for Turbine | |---|---|---|---| | LHV (MJ/kg) | **120** | 50 | Higher energy per kg — smaller fuel flow | | Flame Speed (m/s) | **2.5–3.5** | 0.4 | **7–8× faster** — high flashback risk | | Auto-Ignition (°C) | **500** | 540 | Fires below compressor discharge temp — auto-ignition risk | | Flammability Range (vol%) | **4–75** | 5–15 | Much wider — leak safety critical | | Adiabatic Flame Temp (°C) | **~2,400** | ~1,950 | **Higher** — more NOx unless lean-burn | | AFR Stoichiometric | **34.3** | 17.2 | **2× more air** — bigger compressor needed | | CO₂ per kWh | **0** | ~400 g | **Zero carbon** — key decarbonization driver | | Water Vapor per kWh | High | Moderate | More HRSG steam in combined cycle | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hydrogen Gas Turbine — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Atmospheric Air
Inlet Filter] --> B[Axial Compressor
Pressure Ratio 10:1 to 23:1] B --> C[Compressed Air
at 400 to 450 C] H[H2 Storage
Compressed or LH2 Tank] --> D[H2 Fuel Skid
Pressure Regulator and Heater] D --> E[DLN Combustor
Dry Low NOx] C --> E E --> F[Hot Combustion Gases
Turbine Inlet 1500 to 1600 C] F --> G[Turbine Stages
HP IP and LP] G --> HRSG[Exhaust to HRSG
Combined Cycle Steam] G --> SH[Shaft Coupled to Generator] SH --> GEN[Electrical Generator
50 to 400 MW Output] G --> STACK[Stack to Atmosphere
or Recycle Bypass] E -. NOx and Emissions .-> EM[Emissions Monitoring
NOx below 25 ppm] D -. Leak Detection .-> SAF[H2 Leak Detectors
and N2 Purge System] ``` ## 4.2 Hydrogen Combustion Process Flow (Detailed Sub-System) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph COMPRESSOR_SIDE [Air Compression Section] IN[Ambient Air at 1 atm and 25 C] --> COMP[Multi-Stage Axial Compressor] COMP --> CCIN[High Pressure Air to Combustor Casing] end subgraph FUEL_SECTION [Hydrogen Fuel Delivery] F1[Liquid H2 Storage at -253 C] --> VAP[Cryogenic Vaporizer] VAP --> REG[Pressure Regulator to 30 bar] REG --> FMV[Fuel Metering Valve] end subgraph COMBUSTOR [Dry Low NOx Combustion Chamber] FMV --> MIX[Micro-Mix Lean Pre-Mixed Burner] CCIN --> MIX MIX --> IGN[Spark or Pilot Ignition] IGN --> FLAME[Lean H2 Air Flame at 1600 to 2400 C] FLAME --> TIN[Turbine Inlet Nozzle Vanes] end subgraph TURBINE_SECTION [Expansion and Power Generation] TIN --> HPSTG[High Pressure Turbine Stage] HPSTG --> IPSTG[Intermediate Pressure Stage] IPSTG --> LPSTG[Low Pressure Turbine Stage] LPSTG --> EXH[Exhaust Manifold to HRSG] LPSTG --> SHAFT[Common Shaft to Generator] end subgraph CONTROLS [Safety and Control Loop] MIX -. Temperature Feedback .-> FADEC[FADEC Controller] FADEC -. Fuel Modulation .-> FMV FADEC -. Cooling Air .-> COMP end ``` ## 4.3 Hydrogen-NOx Formation Pathway (Zeldovich Mechanism Block) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[High Flame Temperature
above 1500 K] --> B[O2 Dissociation
into Atomic O] B --> C[N2 plus O yields NO plus N] C --> D[N plus O2 yields NO plus O] D --> E[NO plus N yields N2 plus O] E --> F[Thermal NOx Emission
at Turbine Exhaust] A --> G[Mitigation by Lean Burn
and DLN Premix] G --> H[Flame Temperature below 1850 K] H --> I[NOx below 25 ppm] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram shows the macro-level "Air-In, Power-Out" architecture with explicit safety instrumentation. The second diagram drills into the cryogenic-to-combustion fuel path — note the FADEC (Full Authority Digital Engine Control) feedback loop, which is critical for hydrogen turbines due to the narrow operating window. The third diagram isolates the dominant **Zeldovich thermal-NOx mechanism** that every hydrogen turbine must suppress via lean pre-mixed combustion. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define a hydrogen turbine. List **three** combustion properties of hydrogen that distinguish it from natural gas in turbine applications. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **hydrogen turbine** is a gas turbine that combusts molecular hydrogen (H₂) with compressed air in a continuous-flow combustion chamber and expands the resulting high-temperature gas through turbine stages to produce shaft work/electricity, typically on a Brayton cycle (1 mark). Three distinguishing combustion properties (1 mark each, any three): 1. **Laminar flame speed is ~7–8× higher** (~2.5–3.5 m/s vs. 0.4 m/s for methane) — high flashback risk. 2. **Auto-ignition temperature is lower (~500 °C)** — close to compressor discharge temperatures, risking pre-ignition. 3. **Flammability range is very wide (4–75 % by volume in air)** — leak safety is critical. > **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Why are **Dry Low-NOx (DLN) combustors** preferred over conventional diffusion-flame combustors for hydrogen turbines? **Model Answer (3 marks):** DLN combustors **pre-mix** fuel and air in a lean ratio ($\phi < 1$) **before** the flame zone (1 mark). This achieves: 1. **Lower peak flame temperature** (kept below ~1,850 K), suppressing the **Zeldovich thermal-NOx mechanism** which is exponential in temperature (1 mark). 2. **Uniform, distributed heat release** rather than localized hot spots, reducing thermal stress on combustor liners (0.5 marks). 3. **Compliance with emissions regulations** — modern hydrogen turbines target NOx < 25 ppm (0.5 marks). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer either Question A **OR** Question B in full. ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a)** Derive the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for the complete combustion of pure hydrogen in atmospheric air. Comment on how this ratio influences the design of the air compressor section in a hydrogen gas turbine. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a hydrogen-fueled open-cycle gas turbine. State any **four** engineering challenges specific to hydrogen combustion in such turbines. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Balanced Combustion Equation (1 mark):** $$ 2H_2 + O_2 + 3.76 N_2 \longrightarrow 2H_2O + 3.76 N_2 $$ **Step 2 — Stoichiometric Molar Quantities (1 mark):** - 2 moles of H₂ require 1 mole of O₂ - Air contains 79/21 ≈ 3.76 moles of N₂ per mole of O₂ **Step 3 — Mass Ratio Derivation (3 marks):** $$ AFR_{stoich} = \frac{n_{air} \cdot M_{air}}{n_{H_2} \cdot M_{H_2}} = \frac{4.76 \times 28.97}{2 \times 2.016} $$ $$ AFR_{stoich} = \frac{137.91}{4.032} \approx 34.2 \;\; \frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg H}_2} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical Verification (1 mark):** The LHV of H₂ is 120 MJ/kg, so minimum air required per kW of heat input at 34 % thermal efficiency: $$ \dot{m}_{air} = 34.2 \times \frac{1 \text{ kg/s}}{120 \text{ MJ/kg}} \times \frac{3600 \text{ s/h}}{1} \approx 1026 \text{ kg air per kg H}_2/\text{h} \;\; \text{(per unit energy basis)} $$ **Step 5 — Design Influence (1 mark):** Since $AFR_{stoich} \approx 34.3$ is **~2× higher than methane's 17.2**, the compressor must deliver **roughly twice the air mass flow** per unit of fuel energy. This drives: - Larger compressor cross-sectional area - Higher parasitic load on the shaft (compressor work scales with mass flow) - Requirement for variable inlet guide vanes (VIGV) to throttle air during part-load --- #### **Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 marks):** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` [Air In] → [Filter] → [Compressor] → [Combustor + H₂] → [Turbine] → [Exhaust] ↑ ↓ [Fuel Skid] [Generator ← Shaft] ``` (Students should draw a labeled block diagram showing: Air inlet → Compressor → Combustion Chamber (with H₂ fuel injection) → Turbine → Generator + Exhaust. A full Brayton cycle with arrows indicating working fluid flow is expected.) **Working Principle (2 marks):** Atmospheric air is drawn in and compressed (typically 10:1 to 23:1 pressure ratio). Hydrogen fuel is injected and burned in a continuous-flow combustor at near-constant pressure. The hot, high-pressure gas expands through the turbine, producing shaft work that drives both the compressor and an external generator. The exhaust is released to the atmosphere (open cycle) or fed to a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG) in a combined cycle. **Four Engineering Challenges (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Flashback** — high flame speed can propagate upstream into the fuel injectors. 2. **Auto-ignition** — auto-ignition temp (500 °C) is below modern compressor discharge temps. 3. **High NOx formation** — adiabatic flame temp (~2,400 °C) drives thermal NOx unless lean pre-mix is used. 4. **Material degradation** — high steam content causes Hot Corrosion and Hydrogen Embrittlement in turbine blades. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Starting from First Law principles, derive the expression for the **ideal Brayton cycle thermal efficiency** as a function of the pressure ratio $r_p$. Compute the ideal efficiency for $r_p = 15$ and $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the combustion behavior of **hydrogen** and **methane** in a gas turbine environment across **at least six** parameters. Explain why hydrogen is considered a **zero-carbon** fuel in the context of turbine applications. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Define States and Assumptions (1 mark):** Cold-air-standard Brayton cycle: 1→2 isentropic compression, 2→3 isobaric heat addition, 3→4 isentropic expansion, 4→1 isobaric heat rejection. Constant $c_p$ and constant $\gamma$. **Step 2 — Compressor and Turbine Relations (2 marks):** $$ T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \quad ; \quad T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} $$ **Step 3 — Net Work and Heat Input (2 marks):** $$ w_{net} = c_p (T_3 - T_4) - c_p (T_2 - T_1) \quad ; \quad q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) $$ **Step 4 — Efficiency Expression (1 mark):** $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} $$ **Step 5 — Numerical Computation (1 mark):** For $r_p = 15$ and $\gamma = 1.4$: $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{15^{0.4/1.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{15^{0.2857}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.168} \approx 0.539 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{th,\,ideal} \approx 53.9\%} $$ --- #### **Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 marks):** **Six-Parameter Comparison Table (4.5 marks — 0.75 each parameter):** | Parameter | H₂ | CH₄ | |---|---|---| | LHV (MJ/kg) | 120 | 50 | | Flame Speed (m/s) | 2.5–3.5 | 0.4 | | Auto-Ignition (°C) | 500 | 540 | | Flammability in Air (vol%) | 4–75 | 5–15 | | Stoichiometric AFR | 34.3 | 17.2 | | CO₂ Emission | 0 | High (~400 g/kWh thermal) | **Why Zero-Carbon? (2.5 marks):** The complete combustion of hydrogen in pure oxygen (or air) yields **only water vapor** as the chemical product: $$ 2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O $$ Since the molecular structure of $H_2O$ contains **no carbon atoms**, the reaction inherently **cannot produce CO₂ or CO** at the point of combustion (1.5 marks). This makes hydrogen a **carbon-free energy carrier** at the point of use in the turbine — though lifecycle emissions depend on how the hydrogen itself is produced (e.g., green H₂ from electrolysis vs. grey H₂ from SMR) (1 mark). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Hydrogen Turbine Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the N₂ mass** in the air composition — students often write AFR based on pure O₂ alone, getting ~8 instead of 34.3. Always multiply by (1 + 79/21) = 4.76. > 2. **Using °C instead of K** in the Brayton efficiency derivation — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction under the cold-air-standard model. > 3. **Confusing laminar flame speed with adiabatic flame temperature** — these are different quantities! Flame speed is a kinetic property; flame temperature is a thermodynamic property. > 4. **Omitting the Zeldovich mechanism** when discussing NOx — examiners expect the explicit exponential dependence of NOx on flame temperature, not just a qualitative statement. > 5. **Confusing "hydrogen-ready" with "dedicated hydrogen"** turbines — a hydrogen-ready turbine is **retrofittable**; a dedicated unit is **purpose-built** for 100 % H₂. Mixing these terms costs marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist: Hydrogen Turbines** - [x] **Definition:** Hydrogen turbine = gas turbine burning H₂ on an open Brayton cycle. - [x] **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg** (≈ 2.4× methane). - [x] **Stoichiometric AFR = 34.3 kg air/kg H₂** (≈ 2× methane). - [x] **Laminar flame speed = 2.5–3.5 m/s** (≈ 7× methane) → high flashback risk. - [x] **Flammability range = 4–75 vol% in air** → leak safety critical. - [x] **Auto-ignition = ~500 °C** → pre-ignition risk in modern compressors. - [x] **Adiabatic flame temperature = ~2,400 °C** → drives thermal NOx. - [x] **DLN (Dry Low-NOx) combustors** use lean pre-mix to suppress Zeldovich NOx. - [x] **Ideal Brayton efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$. - [x] **Higher pressure ratio → higher efficiency** (cold-air-standard). - [x] **Brayton Cycle States:** 1 (compressor inlet) → 2 (compressor exit) → 3 (TIT) → 4 (turbine exit). - [x] **Two turbine categories:** hydrogen-ready (retrofittable, H₂/CH₄ blends) vs. dedicated (100 % H₂, purpose-built). - [x] **Key challenges:** flashback, auto-ignition, NOx, HTHA, embrittlement, leak safety. - [x] **Real-world examples:** GE 7HA.03, Siemens Silyzer, MHPS H-25. - [x] **Application domains:** grid peaking, industrial decarbonization, combined-cycle HRSG integration. - [x] **Zero CO₂ at point of use** — only H₂O produced from complete combustion.

topics.title: Hydrogen Gas Burner
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Gas Burner

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Gas Burner (H₂ Burner):** A combustion device specifically engineered to safely and controllably mix hydrogen fuel with an oxidizer (typically air or pure oxygen) and release its chemical energy as a stable, predictable flame for thermal applications such as industrial heating, boiler firing, gas turbines, domestic cooking, and hydrogen-based propulsion systems.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a hydrogen burner is *not* a generic natural gas burner retrofitted to run on H₂. It is a purpose-built component whose geometry, nozzle design, flashback arrestor, and flame retention structure are all optimized for the **unique combustion signature** of hydrogen — namely its ultra-high flame speed, extremely low minimum ignition energy, and broad flammability envelope.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Highly Energetic Puppy"**
> Think of natural gas (methane, CH₄) as a calm, well-trained adult dog. It is predictable, walks at a steady pace (low flame speed ~0.4 m/s), and only reacts when explicitly told to. **Hydrogen is an excitable puppy** — it dashes off in every direction at once (flame speed 2.5–3.5 m/s), gets excited by the tiniest spark (ignition energy 0.02 mJ), and burns happily in a huge range of mixtures (4% to 75% in air). You cannot use a "leash and collar" designed for the adult dog to control the puppy. You need a *purpose-built harness, a fenced yard, and a careful handler.* That special harness is the **Hydrogen Gas Burner.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics of Hydrogen Combustion

| Parameter | Value (Symbol) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Lower Heating Value | **LHV = 119.96 MJ/kg** | Energy usable when water leaves as vapor (flue gas case) |
| Higher Heating Value | **HHV = 141.86 MJ/kg** | Energy when water is condensed to liquid |
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | **AFRₛ = 34.3 : 1** | kg air needed per kg H₂ |
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (volume) | **AFRᵥ = 2.38 : 1** | m³ air per m³ H₂ at NTP |
| Laminar Burning Velocity in air | **S_L ≈ 2.5 – 3.5 m/s** | ~7× faster than methane |
| Adiabatic Flame Temperature (in air) | **T_ad ≈ 2045 °C (2318 K)** | Peak theoretical flame temp |
| Adiabatic Flame Temperature (in O₂) | **T_ad ≈ 2820 °C (3093 K)** | Oxy-hydrogen flame |
| Flammability Limits (in air, vol%) | **4 % – 75 %** | Extremely wide envelope |
| Detonation Limits (in air, vol%) | **18.3 % – 59 %** | Critical safety window |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | **T_ign ≈ 570 °C** | Spontaneous ignition point |
| Minimum Ignition Energy | **MIE = 0.02 mJ** | 1/10 that of methane |
| Flame Visibility | Near-invisible in daylight | Radiative fraction is low |
| Diffusion Coefficient in air | **D_H₂ ≈ 6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** | ~4× faster than methane |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Hydrogen Burner — Why Each Part Exists

A modern H₂ burner contains **four functionally distinct zones**, each addressing one property of hydrogen combustion:

1. **Fuel Nozzle / Injection Stage** — Delivers H₂ at controlled pressure. Because H₂ has the lowest density of any fuel (0.0899 kg/m³ at NTP), the volumetric flow rate is enormous for a given energy release. Nozzles are sized for this.
2. **Flashback Arrestor (Quenching Element)** — Critical safety feature. Since the laminar flame speed of H₂ exceeds the inlet gas velocity at low load, the flame can travel *back up* the fuel pipe. A matrix of small channels (diameter < quenching distance ≈ 0.64 mm) extinguishes any retreating flame by wall heat loss.
3. **Mixing / Premixing Section** — May be fully premixed, partially premixed, or non-premixed (diffusion). The mixing strategy determines NOₓ emissions and flame stability.
4. **Burner Exit / Flame Stabilization Zone** — A bluff body, swirl vane, or porous plug holds the flame in place against the incoming flow.

### 2.2 Classification of Hydrogen Burners

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** The classification below is a guaranteed 3- to 7-mark question.

**A. Premixed Burners**
- H₂ and air are mixed *before* the burner exit.
- **Pros:** Complete combustion, low NOₓ, predictable flame shape.
- **Cons:** High flashback risk, narrow operating range, prone to pre-ignition.

**B. Non-Premixed (Diffusion) Burners**
- H₂ and air meet *at* the flame front; no upstream mixing.
- **Pros:** Inherently flashback-proof, very wide turndown ratio.
- **Cons:** Longer flame, higher NOₓ (thermal), soot-free but radiant.

**C. Porous Media Burners (Surface Combustion)**
- Combustion occurs *inside* a porous ceramic matrix; flame is stabilized within the pores.
- **Pros:** Ultra-low NOₓ (<10 ppm), wide stability range, compact.
- **Cons:** Matrix material limits maximum temperature; expensive.

**D. Catalytic Burners**
- Combustion occurs on a platinum/palladium catalyst surface at temperatures well below auto-ignition (200–400 °C).
- **Pros:** Extremely low NOₓ, safe, flameless.
- **Cons:** Limited power density, catalyst poisoning, expensive.

**E. Oxy-Hydrogen Burners**
- Use pure O₂ instead of air. Avoids nitrogen ballast, doubles flame temperature.
- **Pros:** Highest flame temperature (2820 °C), no NOₓ from N₂.
- **Cons:** High cost of O₂, severe flashback risk.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every numerical in Module 4.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O} \quad \Delta H = -241.8~\mathrm{kJ/mol}$ | Stoichiometric combustion at 298 K |
| 2 | $\mathrm{AFR_s = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \dfrac{4.32 \times 32}{2.016} = 34.3}$ | Mass-based stoichiometric AFR |
| 3 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio (φ<1 lean, φ>1 rich) |
| 4 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times \mathrm{LHV}$ | Heat release rate (W) |
| 5 | $T_{ad} = T_u + \dfrac{Y_{F,u} \cdot \mathrm{LHV}}{c_{p,mix}}$ | Approximate adiabatic flame temperature |
| 6 | $S_{L,H_2} \approx 7 \times S_{L,CH_4}$ | Relative flame speed rule of thumb |
| 7 | $d_q = \dfrac{4 \cdot \lambda}{c_p \cdot \rho \cdot S_L}$ | Peclet-based quenching diameter |
| 8 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot u \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number at burner exit |
| 9 | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | Nozzle mass flow (Cd ≈ 0.95–0.98 for H₂) |
| 10 | $\mathrm{Pe} = \dfrac{S_L \cdot d}{\alpha}$ | Peclet number for flashback judgment |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Stoichiometric Air Requirement for H₂ Combustion

**Statement:** Show that the stoichiometric mass air-fuel ratio of pure hydrogen is **34.3 : 1**.

**Starting equation (balanced):**
$$\mathrm{H_2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{O_2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H_2O_{(g)}}$$

**Step 1 — Molar mass accounting.**
- 1 mole of H₂ = **2.016 g** of fuel.
- ½ mole of O₂ = 0.5 × 32 = **16.00 g** of pure oxygen.

**Step 2 — Convert oxygen to air.** Air is 23.2% O₂ and 76.8% N₂ by mass (standard dry air). Therefore the mass of air required to provide 16.00 g of O₂ is:
$$m_{air} = \dfrac{16.00}{0.232} = 68.97~\mathrm{g}$$

**Step 3 — Form the mass ratio.**
$$\mathrm{AFR_s} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \dfrac{68.97}{2.016} \approx 34.29$$

**Step 4 — Round to engineering precision.**
$$\boxed{\mathrm{AFR_s} \approx 34.3~\mathrm{kg~air/kg~H_2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Notice this ratio is *inverted* from conventional liquid fuels. Gasoline has AFR ≈ 14.7, but hydrogen needs **2.3× more air per unit mass**. This is because hydrogen is already partially "oxidized" (it has no carbon) and carries its own oxygen-bound mass.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Adiabatic Flame Temperature in Air (First-Order Estimate)

**Statement:** Estimate the adiabatic flame temperature (T_ad) of a stoichiometric H₂–air mixture using energy balance.

**Step 1 — Energy balance.** No heat loss, no work. All chemical enthalpy release goes into raising the product gas temperature:
$$H_{reactants}(T_u) = H_{products}(T_{ad})$$

**Step 2 — Form the algebraic statement.**
$$n_{H_2} \cdot \mathrm{LHV} = \sum_i n_{prod,i} \cdot \int_{T_u}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,i}(T)\,dT$$

**Step 3 — Insert numerical values for stoichiometric mixture (per kg of H₂).**
- $n_{H_2}$ reacted = 1 kg / 2.016 g/mol = **495.0 mol**.
- Products per kg H₂: 495 mol H₂O + (0.5 × 495 × 3.76) mol N₂ = 495 + 931.4 ≈ **1426 mol of gas**.
- LHV per kg of H₂ = **119,960 kJ/kg**.
- Assume constant average $c_{p,mix} \approx 30~\mathrm{J/(mol \cdot K)}$ (valid 1500–2300 K range).

**Step 4 — Solve for T_ad.**
$$T_{ad} - T_u = \dfrac{495 \times 241.8 \times 1000}{1426 \times 30}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate.**
$$T_{ad} - 298 = \dfrac{1.197 \times 10^8}{42780} \approx 2798~\mathrm{K}$$

**Step 6 — Add reference temperature.**
$$\boxed{T_{ad} \approx 298 + 2798 = 3096~\mathrm{K} \;\;(\approx 2823~^\circ\mathrm{C})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the textbook value only ~2318 K?** Real flame temperatures are *lower* than the first-order estimate because (a) product dissociation (H₂O → H + OH) absorbs energy above 2000 K, and (b) radiative heat loss is non-negligible. The true value is **~2045 °C (2318 K) in air** and **~2820 °C in pure O₂** — matching the standard data table.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Nozzle Sizing for a Given Thermal Power

**Statement:** Find the throat diameter of a hydrogen nozzle required to deliver **10 kW** of thermal output at **2 bar gauge** supply pressure.

**Step 1 — Convert thermal power to H₂ mass flow.**
$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{\mathrm{LHV}} = \dfrac{10{,}000~\mathrm{W}}{119.96 \times 10^6~\mathrm{J/kg}} = 8.34 \times 10^{-5}~\mathrm{kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to volumetric flow at supply conditions.** Use ideal gas: ρ = PM/RT.
- P = 3 bar abs = 300,000 Pa.
- T = 298 K.
- R(H₂) = 4124 J/(kg·K).
$$\rho = \dfrac{300{,}000}{4124 \times 298} = 0.244~\mathrm{kg/m^3}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow.**
$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{8.34 \times 10^{-5}}{0.244} = 3.42 \times 10^{-4}~\mathrm{m^3/s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply nozzle equation with $C_d = 0.97$.**
$$A = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{C_d \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}}$$

With $\Delta P = 2~\mathrm{bar} = 2 \times 10^5~\mathrm{Pa}$ and $\rho = 0.244$:
$$\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{0.244}} = \sqrt{1.639 \times 10^6} = 1281~\mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute area and diameter.**
$$A = \dfrac{3.42 \times 10^{-4}}{0.97 \times 1281} = 2.75 \times 10^{-7}~\mathrm{m^2}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{3.50 \times 10^{-7}} = 5.92 \times 10^{-4}~\mathrm{m}$$

$$\boxed{D_{throat} \approx 0.59~\mathrm{mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Forgetting to convert gauge to absolute pressure. The student who uses ΔP = 2 bar in the Bernoulli term gets D = 1.27 mm — a *non-orifice* value. Examiner will deduct full marks.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Burner Types

| Property | Premixed | Non-Premixed (Diffusion) | Porous Media | Catalytic | Oxy-Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| NOₓ (ppm) | 5–40 | 50–150 | <10 | <5 | <30 |
| Flashback risk | **Very high** | **None** | Low | None | **Extreme** |
| Turndown ratio | 3:1 | 10:1 | 8:1 | 5:1 | 4:1 |
| Max flame temp (°C) | 2045 | 2045 | 1100 | 400 | **2820** |
| Thermal efficiency | High | Medium | **Very high** | Medium | High |
| Cost | Medium | Low | High | Very high | High |
| Best application | Domestic | Industrial furnace | Low-NOₓ residential | Lab / fuel cell | Cutting / welding |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrogen Burner — Block Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the functional interactions inside a typical **forced-draft, partially-premixed industrial hydrogen burner**, including the safety interlocks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[H2 Supply Line\n200 bar cylinders] --> B[Pressure Regulator\nStage 1: 200 to 10 bar]
    B --> C[Stage 2 Regulator\n10 bar to 50 mbar]
    C --> D[Solenoid Valve\nQuick Shut-off]
    D --> E[Flashback Arrestor\nQuenching matrix d < 0.64 mm]
    E --> F[Venturi Mixer\nPartial premix with air]
    F --> G[Air Blower\nFlow proportional to H2]
    G --> H[Burner Head\nFlame stabilization zone]
    H --> I[Flame Sensor UV/IR]
    I --> J[Combustion Chamber]
    J --> K[Flue Gas Stack]
    I --> L[Control Unit PLC]
    L --> D
    L --> G
    L --> M[Safety Shutdown Loop]
    M --> A
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style E fill:#ffe1e1
    style H fill:#fff4cc
    style I fill:#e1ffe1
```

### 4.2 Flame Structure Inside a Premixed H₂ Burner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unburned\nMixture in] --> B[Pre-heat Zone\nT rising to T_ign]
    B --> C[Reaction Zone\nT at T_ad 2318 K]
    C --> D[Post-flame\nHot Products]
    D --> E[Exhaust]
    A -. flashback .- F[Quenching Wall\nd < d_quench]
    style B fill:#ffeebb
    style C fill:#ffaa66
    style F fill:#ffcccc
```

### 4.3 Classification Tree of H₂ Burners

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrogen Burner] --> B[Premixed]
    A --> C[Non-Premixed Diffusion]
    A --> D[Porous Media]
    A --> E[Catalytic]
    A --> F[Oxy-Hydrogen]
    B --> B1[Bunsen-type domestic]
    B --> B2[Nozzle-mix industrial]
    C --> C1[Co-axial jet]
    C --> C2[Multi-port diffusion]
    D --> D1[Radiant surface]
    D --> D2[Matrix combustor]
    E --> E1[Pre-mixed catalytic]
    E --> E2[Non-premixed catalytic]
    F --> F1[Cutting torch]
    F --> F2[Rocket thruster]
    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style B fill:#e6f2ff
    style C fill:#e6f2ff
    style D fill:#e6f2ff
    style E fill:#e6f2ff
    style F fill:#e6f2ff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three combustion properties of hydrogen that make its handling in burners fundamentally different from natural gas (methane).*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per property):**
1. **Very low Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE ≈ 0.02 mJ)** — about one-tenth that of methane. H₂ can be ignited by the smallest static discharge, demanding strict earthing and flame arrestors.
2. **High Laminar Burning Velocity (S_L ≈ 2.5–3.5 m/s)** — about 7× methane. This makes flashback into the fuel line a major hazard, requiring quenching-diameter flashback arrestors.
3. **Wide Flammability Range (4–75 vol% in air)** — the flame can sustain over an enormous fuel-air composition range, eliminating the safe "off" zone that exists for methane.
   *(Any other valid property: high diffusivity, low density, invisible flame — accept with 1 mark each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "quenching diameter" of a flame. Why is it critical in the design of a hydrogen burner flashback arrestor?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The quenching diameter $d_q$ is the maximum channel diameter below which a flame cannot propagate because heat losses to the wall exceed the chemical heat release rate.
- **H₂ value (1 mark):** For hydrogen in air, $d_q \approx 0.64$ mm, much smaller than for methane ($d_q \approx 2.5$ mm).
- **Design significance (1 mark):** Flashback arrestors in H₂ burners use a sintered metal or ceramic matrix with pore diameter strictly less than 0.64 mm. Any flame retreating upstream is quenched at the matrix and cannot reach the supply line.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Choice — Answer ANY ONE — 14 Marks)

#### ⭐ Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a balanced chemical equation, derive the **stoichiometric mass air-fuel ratio** for hydrogen combustion in air. State clearly the standard composition of dry air used in the calculation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A domestic hydrogen burner is rated at **3.5 kW** thermal output. H₂ is supplied at **1.2 bar gauge** pressure and **25 °C**. Assuming a discharge coefficient of **0.95**, calculate the required **nozzle throat diameter**. Use LHV = 120 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Balanced equation (1 mark):**
$$\mathrm{H_2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{O_2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H_2O_{(g)}}$$

**Step 2 — Molar masses (1 mark):**
- H₂ = 2.016 g/mol
- O₂ = 32.00 g/mol
- Air: 23.2% O₂ + 76.8% N₂ by mass; molecular weight 28.97 g/mol.

**Step 3 — Mass of O₂ needed per kg H₂ (1 mark):**
$$m_{O_2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1000}{2.016} \times 32 = 7936.5~\mathrm{g} = 7.937~\mathrm{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of air required (1 mark):**
$$m_{air} = \dfrac{7.937}{0.232} = 34.21~\mathrm{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Ratio (1 mark):**
$$\mathrm{AFR_s} = \dfrac{34.21}{1.000} = 34.2 \approx 34.3$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation (2 marks):** The very high AFR is because H₂ has only 2 H atoms per molecule, all of which must be supplied oxygen. Combustion in a hydrogen burner therefore requires roughly 2.3× the air-handling capacity of a methane burner of the same energy rating. This dictates the sizing of blowers, venturis, and air registers.

##### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — H₂ mass flow (1 mark):**
$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 10^3}{120 \times 10^6} = 2.92 \times 10^{-5}~\mathrm{kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Density at supply (1 mark):**
$$P_{abs} = 2.2~\mathrm{bar} = 220{,}000~\mathrm{Pa}$$
$$\rho = \dfrac{P}{RT} = \dfrac{220{,}000}{4124 \times 298} = 0.179~\mathrm{kg/m^3}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow (1 mark):**
$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{2.92 \times 10^{-5}}{0.179} = 1.63 \times 10^{-4}~\mathrm{m^3/s}$$

**Step 4 — Effective velocity head (1 mark):**
$$u = C_d \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} = 0.95 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1.2 \times 10^5}{0.179}} = 0.95 \times 1158 = 1100~\mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Throat area and diameter (2 marks):**
$$A = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{u} = \dfrac{1.63 \times 10^{-4}}{1100} = 1.48 \times 10^{-7}~\mathrm{m^2}$$
$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}} = 4.34 \times 10^{-4}~\mathrm{m} = \boxed{0.43~\mathrm{mm}}$$

**Step 6 — Design check (1 mark):** Since the throat diameter 0.43 mm is *less* than the quenching diameter 0.64 mm, the nozzle itself would behave as a flashback arrestor. In practice, a separate arrestor is added downstream for safety redundancy. (1 mark)

---

#### ⭐ Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2025, Model Paper]

**(a)** Compare **premixed** and **non-premixed (diffusion) hydrogen burners** under the heads: (i) flame stability, (ii) flashback risk, (iii) NOₓ emissions, and (iv) typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **flashback** in a hydrogen flame. A premixed H₂–air flame burns in a 12 mm diameter tube. The laminar burning velocity of the mixture is 2.8 m/s and the mean gas velocity at the burner exit is 1.6 m/s. State, with justification, whether flashback will occur. If the burner power is throttled such that the gas velocity drops to 0.9 m/s, what is the remedial design measure? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

| Head | Premixed Burner | Non-Premixed (Diffusion) Burner |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Flame stability (2 marks) | Narrow stability range; sensitive to flow disturbances; prone to blow-off at high velocity. | Very stable; flame anchors on the diffusion interface; can hold flame over a wide range. |
| (ii) Flashback risk (2 marks) | **Very high**, since the flame can propagate upstream against the unburned flow if velocity < S_L. Requires a flashback arrestor. | **Negligible** — mixing and reaction occur at the same location, so no upstream propagation path exists. |
| (iii) NOₓ emissions (1 mark) | Low NOₓ (5–40 ppm) because peak temperature is moderated by premixing and lower flame temperature in lean mixtures. | Higher NOₐ (50–150 ppm) due to stoichiometric core and longer residence time at high T. |
| (iv) Applications (2 marks) | Domestic cooking stoves, lab burners, low-emission industrial furnaces. | Industrial furnaces, boilers, gas turbines, forge furnaces — anywhere robust operation is needed. |

##### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Flashback definition (1 mark):** Flashback is the upstream propagation of the flame front into the unburned gas mixture, typically into the fuel nozzle or mixing chamber, caused when the local gas velocity drops below the laminar burning velocity.

**Step 2 — Stability criterion (1 mark):** For flashback to occur,
$$u_{gas} < S_L \cdot \text{(safety factor)}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at full load (2 marks):** $u_{gas} = 1.6~\mathrm{m/s}$ and $S_L = 2.8~\mathrm{m/s}$. Since $1.6 < 2.8$, **flashback WILL occur** at the stated conditions. The burner as described is unsafe for full power.

**Step 4 — Evaluate at throttled load (1 mark):** At 0.9 m/s, flashback is even more certain. The flame will propagate far into the mixing section.

**Step 5 — Remedial measure (2 marks):** Install a **flashback arrestor** of quenching diameter < 0.64 mm in the fuel line *or* use a **flame holder / bluff body** that anchors the flame downstream of the nozzle. Alternatively, switch the burner design to a **diffusion (non-premixed) configuration** which is inherently flashback-proof. A swirl-stabilized partially premixed burner is the typical industrial solution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. In Q.B(b), students frequently write "flashback occurs when $S_L > u$" but **omit the safety factor** (~2×). Examiners expect you to *acknowledge* that real burners require $u > 2 S_L$ for safe operation. Lose 1 mark if missing.
> 2. In Q.A(b), forgetting to convert **gauge pressure to absolute** is the most common error — costs 2 of 7 marks.
> 3. In Q.A(a), the standard 23.2% O₂ by mass must be stated; using 21% O₂ *by volume* (the more commonly memorized number) gives the wrong AFR of 35.7 instead of 34.3.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Stoichiometric equation:** H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O; ΔH = –241.8 kJ/mol.
- [x] **Stoichiometric AFR (mass) = 34.3 : 1**; (volume) = 2.38 : 1.
- [x] **LHV = 119.96 MJ/kg; HHV = 141.86 MJ/kg.**
- [x] **Adiabatic flame temperature:** ~2045 °C in air; ~2820 °C in pure O₂.
- [x] **Laminar burning velocity:** 2.5–3.5 m/s — about 7× methane.
- [x] **Flammability limits:** 4–75 vol% in air — extraordinarily wide.
- [x] **MIE = 0.02 mJ** — easily ignited by static discharge.
- [x] **Quenching diameter for H₂ ≈ 0.64 mm** — sets flashback arrestor pore size.
- [x] **Auto-ignition ≈ 570 °C** — must pre-heat the burner for cold start.
