- [x] **Five burner types:** premixed, non-premixed, porous, catalytic, oxy-hydrogen.
- [x] **NOₓ ranking (low to high):** catalytic < porous < premixed < non-premixed < oxy.
- [x] **Safety triad for H₂ burners:** flashback arrestor + flame sensor + emergency shutoff.
- [x] **Invisible flame in daylight** — never test an H₂ flame by sight alone; use UV/IR detector.
- [x] **Equivalence ratio φ:** φ < 1 lean, φ = 1 stoichiometric, φ > 1 rich.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Engine Combined Heat and Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gas Engine Combined Heat and Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Gas Engine Combined Heat and Power (CHP) Plant**, also termed a **Gas Engine Cogeneration Plant**, is an integrated energy conversion system in which a reciprocating internal combustion gas engine (operating on natural gas, biogas, syngas, or hydrogen–natural gas blends) drives an electrical generator while simultaneously recovering useful thermal energy from the engine's exhaust gas and jacket-cooling water circuits. The defining design intent is *sequential energy cascading*: high-grade chemical energy of the fuel is first converted into mechanical work (electricity) and the residual low-grade heat is captured for space heating, domestic hot water, absorption chilling, or industrial process heat.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course OEBBT616, Module 4), the focus is on how **hydrogen-rich fuels (H₂, HCNG, hydrogen-enriched natural gas)** can be deployed in such gas-engine CHP architectures for low-carbon, distributed, and on-site power generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A Gas Engine CHP plant differs from a gas turbine CHP. The "engine" here is a **reciprocating piston engine** (Otto or Miller cycle), NOT a Brayton-cycle turbine. This distinction is a frequent KTU exam trap.

### 1.2 Key Design Metrics (Standard CHP Performance Indicators)

> [!NOTE]
> The three canonical efficiency definitions of a CHP plant are listed below. Memorise them — they appear every year in KTU Part A questions.

- **Electrical Efficiency (ηₑ)** : Typically **30 % – 45 %** for reciprocating gas engines.
- **Thermal (Heat) Recovery Efficiency (ηₜₕ)** : Typically **45 % – 55 %** of the fuel input.
- **Total CHP Efficiency / Energy Utilization Factor (ε)** : Typically **80 % – 90 %**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Kettle-on-a-Stove Picture

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine boiling water on a gas stove. The flame heats the kettle (thermal use), but the hot exhaust gases simply escape up the chimney — wasted. Now imagine placing a tiny **turbine + generator** in the flame's path so some of the chemical energy first spins a fan and lights a bulb, while the remaining heat still boils your water. That is CHP. A **gas engine** is simply the "turbine" replaced by a robust piston engine. The **"two outputs from one input"** principle is the soul of cogeneration.

### 1.4 Why Hydrogen-Blended Gas Engines Matter

Hydrogen addition to natural gas (called **HCNG — Hydrogen-Compressed Natural Gas**) modifies the combustion behaviour inside the gas engine in the following measurable ways:

- **Wider flammability limits** → lean-burn operation feasible → higher efficiency.
- **Higher laminar flame speed** → improved flame stability and complete combustion.
- **Higher auto-ignition resistance (high octane number ≈ 130)** → suppression of engine knock, allowing higher compression ratios.
- **Zero carbon content of H₂** → proportionally lower CO₂ emissions per MJ of fuel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Sequential Logic

1. **Fuel Admission:** A gaseous fuel (CH₄, biogas, H₂, or HCNG blend) is mixed with air in the intake manifold of a multi-cylinder reciprocating engine. The fuel–air mixture is regulated by an electronic gas train and a mixer / carburettor / multi-point port injection system.
2. **Compression Stroke:** The mixture is compressed to a pressure of **20 – 35 bar** (compression ratio r ≈ 10:1 to 14:1). Hydrogen's resistance to knock permits operation at the upper end of this range.
3. **Combustion & Power Stroke:** Spark ignition initiates combustion. Peak temperatures reach **1800 – 2200 °C**. The high-pressure gas drives the piston, producing mechanical work on the crankshaft.
4. **Mechanical-to-Electrical Conversion:** The crankshaft drives a synchronous generator (typically **400 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase**) through a flexible coupling.
5. **Heat Recovery (Cascading):**
   - **Jacket Water Circuit (Low-Temperature Heat):** Engine block cooling water exits at **85 – 95 °C** and is used for space heating or domestic hot water.
   - **Exhaust Gas Circuit (High-Temperature Heat):** Exhaust leaves the engine at **400 – 600 °C**. It passes through an exhaust gas heat exchanger (EGHE) — either a *primary surface exchanger* or a *shell-and-tube economiser* — to raise hot water or generate low-pressure steam (≤ 4 bar).
6. **Exhaust Aftertreatment:** Catalytic or thermal oxidiser removes CO and unburnt hydrocarbons; for hydrogen blends, **NOx reduction via exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) or SCR** becomes critical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Heat Recovery?** In a conventional power plant, the jacket and exhaust heat is dumped to cooling towers and the atmosphere — over **50 % of the fuel's energy is wasted**. CHP captures this, pushing the overall energy utilisation from ~35 % (electric-only) to ~85 % (cogeneration).

### 2.2 Thermodynamic Backbone — The Otto Cycle

A spark-ignition gas engine approximates the **Otto cycle**. The ideal thermal efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

where:
- $r$ = compression ratio
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats ($C_p / C_v$)

For methane ($\gamma \approx 1.30$) at $r = 12$:

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto,CH}_4} = 1 - \frac{1}{12^{1.30 - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{12^{0.30}} \approx 0.470 \; (\text{or } 47.0\,\%)
$$

For hydrogen ($\gamma \approx 1.40$) at $r = 14$ (hydrogen tolerates higher $r$):

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto,H}_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{14^{1.40 - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{14^{0.40}} \approx 0.531 \; (\text{or } 53.1\,\%)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The higher $\gamma$ of hydrogen and its superior knock resistance allow a **larger $r$**, raising the theoretical cycle efficiency by ~6 percentage points over methane. This is a frequent KTU Part B question stem.

### 2.3 CHP Energy Balance — The Master Equation

The **first law of thermodynamics** applied to the entire CHP plant boundary yields:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{W}_{\text{electrical}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{thermal,useful}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{losses}}
$$

where $\dot{Q}_{\text{losses}}$ includes radiation, convection, incomplete combustion, and unrecovered exhaust heat.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table must be memorised verbatim. Every KTU exam question on CHP plants is solved using only these relationships.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrical Efficiency | $\eta_e = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}}$ | Electrical output / Fuel LHV input | dimensionless (or %) |
| 2 | Thermal Recovery Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}}$ | Useful heat output / Fuel LHV input | dimensionless (or %) |
| 3 | Total CHP Efficiency (EUF) | $\varepsilon = \eta_e + \eta_{th}$ | Sum of electrical and thermal efficiencies | dimensionless (or %) |
| 4 | Power-to-Heat Ratio | $\text{PHR} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}}}$ | Electricity-to-heat ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | Fuel Energy Balance | $\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{W}_{el} + \dot{Q}_{th} + \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}$ | Energy conservation | kW or MJ/s |
| 6 | Otto Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - r^{-(\gamma-1)}$ | Ideal spark-ignition efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | Exhaust Heat Recovery | $\dot{Q}_{exh} = \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_{p,exh} \cdot (T_{exh,\text{in}} - T_{exh,\text{out}})$ | Sensible heat from exhaust | kW |
| 8 | Jacket Heat Recovery | $\dot{Q}_{jw} = \dot{m}_{w} \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | Sensible heat from cooling water | kW |
| 9 | Fuel LHV for HCNG Blend | $\text{LHV}_{\text{blend}} = x_{H_2}\cdot\text{LHV}_{H_2} + x_{CH_4}\cdot\text{LHV}_{CH_4}$ | Linear mixing rule (by volume or mass) | MJ/Nm³ or MJ/kg |
| 10 | Primary Energy Saving | $\text{PES} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\eta_e}\left[\eta_e + \eta_{th}\right]$ | vs separate heat + power generation | dimensionless |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gas-engine CHP plants are the **workhorse of distributed generation** worldwide. Real-world deployment scenarios:

- **Hospitals & Hotels:** Continuous heat + power demand (PHR ≈ 0.5 – 1.0).
- **Breweries & Food Processing:** Steam + electricity demand favours gas-engine CHP over gas turbines.
- **Hydrogen Refuelling Stations:** On-site H₂ production via electrolysis can use a CHP plant as the primary power source, closing the energy loop.
- **Data Centres (with absorption cooling):** Heat-driven absorption chillers convert recovered heat into cooling, yielding tri-generation (CCHP).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Total CHP Efficiency from First Principles

We define a control volume enclosing the gas engine, generator, and heat-recovery exchangers. The **first law** in steady-state, neglecting kinetic and potential energy:

$$
\dot{E}_{\text{in}} - \dot{E}_{\text{out}} = 0
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{W}_{el} + \dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}
$$

Dividing throughout by $\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}$:

$$
1 = \frac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}} + \frac{\dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}} + \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}}
$$

Defining $\eta_e = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}}$ and $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}}$:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_e + \eta_{th} + \eta_{\text{loss}} = 1\,}
$$

The **Total CHP Efficiency (Energy Utilisation Factor, EUF)** is:

$$
\varepsilon = \eta_e + \eta_{th}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A high EUF (>85 %) means *less than 15 % of the fuel's chemical energy is wasted*. Compare this to a thermal power plant with ~65 % waste heat dumped to a cooling tower — CHP nearly **doubles** the energy utilisation of the same fuel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Power-to-Heat Ratio

By definition:

$$
\text{PHR} = \frac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}}}
$$

Cross-multiplying with the energy balance:

$$
\dot{W}_{el} = \text{PHR} \cdot \dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}}
$$

Substituting back into the fuel balance:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \text{PHR} \cdot \dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}} + \dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}} \left( 1 + \text{PHR} \right) + \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} - \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}}{1 + \text{PHR}}\,}
$$

This relation is the **design pivot**: a facility with low electrical demand but high thermal demand (e.g., a laundry) demands a *low* PHR engine, while a data centre with high electrical load and cooling demand demands a *high* PHR engine.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Exhaust Heat Recovery Rate

Exhaust gases leave the engine at high temperature $T_{exh,\text{in}}$ and are cooled in the exhaust gas heat exchanger (EGHE) to $T_{exh,\text{out}}$. The recoverable sensible heat is:

$$
\dot{Q}_{exh} = \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot \overline{c}_{p,exh} \cdot \left( T_{exh,\text{in}} - T_{exh,\text{out}} \right)
$$

The exhaust mass flow rate is related to the air–fuel ratio $\text{AFR}$ and fuel flow $\dot{m}_f$:

$$
\dot{m}_{exh} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \left( 1 + \text{AFR} \right)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{Q}_{exh} = \dot{m}_f \left( 1 + \text{AFR} \right) \overline{c}_{p,exh} \left( T_{exh,\text{in}} - T_{exh,\text{out}} \right)\,}
$$

For a typical natural gas engine with $T_{exh,\text{in}} = 450\,°C = 723\,\text{K}$, $T_{exh,\text{out}} = 180\,°C = 453\,\text{K}$, and $\overline{c}_{p,exh} \approx 1.10$ kJ/(kg·K), the EGHE can recover approximately **40 – 45 %** of the fuel's LHV.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of LHV of an HCNG (Hydrogen-Methane) Blend

For volumetric blending (mole fraction $x_i$), the LHV of the blend is the **molar-weighted** average:

$$
\text{LHV}_{\text{blend}} = x_{H_2} \cdot \text{LHV}_{H_2} + x_{CH_4} \cdot \text{LHV}_{CH_4}
$$

Standard LHV values:

- $\text{LHV}_{H_2} = 10.788 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$
- $\text{LHV}_{CH_4} = 35.883 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$

For a blend with $x_{H_2} = 0.20$ and $x_{CH_4} = 0.80$:

$$
\text{LHV}_{\text{blend}} = (0.20)(10.788) + (0.80)(35.883)
$$

$$
\text{LHV}_{\text{blend}} = 2.1576 + 28.7064 = 30.864 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Adding hydrogen *reduces* the volumetric energy density of the blend. A 20 % H₂ substitution drops the LHV by **~14 %** compared to pure methane. This means the gas train and mixer must be **re-sized** for the same thermal input — a frequent KTU design question.

---

### 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board Examination Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A 1 MWₑₗ gas-engine CHP plant operates on a HCNG blend (20 % H₂ + 80 % CH₄ by volume). The following data are given:
> - Fuel mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_f = 0.072$ kg/s
> - Fuel LHV: 30.86 MJ/Nm³
> - Density of blend at NTP: $\rho = 0.665$ kg/Nm³
> - Electrical output: $\dot{W}_{el} = 380$ kW
> - Jacket heat recovered: $\dot{Q}_{jw} = 180$ kW
> - Exhaust heat recovered: $\dot{Q}_{exh} = 300$ kW

**Step 1 — Fuel Energy Input (kW):**

$$
\dot{V}_f = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{\rho} = \frac{0.072}{0.665} = 0.1083 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{V}_f \times \text{LHV}_{\text{blend}} = 0.1083 \times 30860 \text{ kJ/Nm}^3 = 3342 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Useful Thermal Output:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}} = \dot{Q}_{jw} + \dot{Q}_{exh} = 180 + 300 = 480 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Electrical Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_e = \frac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{380}{3342} = 0.1137
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_e = 11.37\,\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Thermal Recovery Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{480}{3342} = 0.1436
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{th} = 14.36\,\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Total CHP Efficiency (Energy Utilisation Factor):**

$$
\varepsilon = \eta_e + \eta_{th} = 0.1137 + 0.1436 = 0.2573
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\varepsilon = 25.73\,\%}
$$

**Step 6 — Loss Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{loss}} = 1 - \varepsilon = 1 - 0.2573 = 0.7427
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{\text{loss}} = 74.27\,\%}
$$

**Step 7 — Power-to-Heat Ratio:**

$$
\text{PHR} = \frac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{th,\text{useful}}} = \frac{380}{480} = 0.7917
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{PHR} = 0.792\,}
$$

**Step 8 — Heat Balance Verification:**

$$
\dot{W}_{el} + \dot{Q}_{th} + \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}} = 380 + 480 + 2482 = 3342 \text{ kW} = \dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical Reality Check:** The example above is a *didactic* trace. In practice, a 1 MWₑₗ CHP plant has ηₑ ≈ 38 – 42 %. The numbers above are intentionally low to force students to walk through every algebraic step. **Always show units in KTU exams** — marks are deducted for unit-less final answers.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pure CH₄ vs HCNG vs Pure H₂ Engine

| Parameter | Pure CH₄ | 20 % H₂ + 80 % CH₄ | Pure H₂ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compression Ratio (max) | 12 : 1 | 13 : 1 | 14 : 1 |
| Theoretical Otto η | 47 % | 49 % | 53 % |
| Practical ηₑ | 38 % | 39 % | 41 % |
| Practical ηₜₕ | 47 % | 48 % | 50 % |
| Total EUF | 85 % | 87 % | 91 % |
| Flame Speed (m/s) | 0.4 | 0.7 | 2.5 |
| Knock Tendency | Moderate | Low | Negligible |
| NOx Emissions (g/MJ) | 25 | 18 | 12 |
| CO₂ per MJ fuel | 56 g | 45 g | 0 g |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Gas Engine CHP Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUEL SUPPLY: CH4 / HCNG / H2 BLEND] --> B[GAS TRAIN AND MIXER UNIT]
    B --> C[INTAKE MANIFOLD AND TURBOCHARGER]
    C --> D[RECIPROCATING GAS ENGINE: OTTO OR MILLER CYCLE]
    D --> E[CRANKSHAFT AND GENERATOR COUPLING]
    E --> F[SYNCHRONOUS GENERATOR 400 V 50 HZ]
    F --> G[ELECTRICAL SWITCHGEAR AND GRID]
    D --> H[ENGINE JACKET COOLING WATER CIRCUIT]
    H --> I[JACKET WATER HEAT EXCHANGER]
    I --> J[SPACE HEATING AND DOMESTIC HOT WATER LOAD]
    D --> K[EXHAUST MANIFOLD AT 450 TO 600 DEG C]
    K --> L[EXHAUST GAS HEAT EXCHANGER EGHE]
    L --> M[LOW PRESSURE STEAM OR HOT WATER OUTPUT]
    K --> N[CATALYTIC OXIDISER AND SCR FOR NOX]
    N --> O[STACK EXHAUST TO ATMOSPHERE]
    P[LUBRICATING OIL CIRCUIT] --> D
    Q[CONTROL PLC AND SCADA] --> D
    Q --> F
    Q --> I
    Q --> L
```

### 4.2 Energy Cascade Sankey Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[FUEL CHEMICAL ENERGY 100 PERCENT] --> ENG[GAS ENGINE CONVERSION]
    ENG --> EL[ELECTRICAL OUTPUT 38 PERCENT]
    ENG --> JW[JACKET HEAT 22 PERCENT]
    ENG --> EX[EXHAUST HEAT RECOVERED 25 PERCENT]
    ENG --> LOSS[STACK RADIATION AND LOSSES 15 PERCENT]
    JW --> USEFUL[USEFUL THERMAL OUTPUT 47 PERCENT]
    EX --> USEFUL
    EL --> USEFUL
    LOSS --> WASTE[REJECTED TO ATMOSPHERE]
```

### 4.3 Hydrogen Enrichment Impact on Engine Performance Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H2P[H2 VOLUME PERCENT IN BLEND] --> INFER1[FLAME SPEED INCREASES]
    H2P --> INFER2[FLAMMABILITY LIMITS WIDEN]
    H2P --> INFER3[OCTANE RATING INCREASES]
    H2P --> INFER4[ENERGY DENSITY DECREASES]
    INFER1 --> OUT1[LEAN BURN FEASIBLE]
    INFER2 --> OUT1
    INFER3 --> OUT2[HIGHER COMPRESSION RATIO PERMITTED]
    OUT1 --> FINAL[IMPROVED ELECTRICAL EFFICIENCY]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q.A1. Define a Gas Engine Combined Heat and Power (CHP) plant. List any three advantages of operating it on a hydrogen–natural gas blend compared to pure natural gas.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A Gas Engine CHP plant is an integrated energy system in which a reciprocating internal combustion gas engine drives an electrical generator while the waste heat from the engine jacket cooling water and exhaust gas is recovered for useful thermal applications such as space heating, hot water generation, or absorption cooling.

Three advantages of hydrogen–natural gas (HCNG) blends:

1. **Higher electrical efficiency** is achievable due to the higher octane rating of hydrogen, which permits a higher compression ratio without knock.
2. **Reduced CO₂ emissions** per unit of fuel energy, since hydrogen is carbon-free.
3. **Improved flame stability and lean-burn operation** because hydrogen has a wider flammability range and higher laminar flame speed than methane.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write only "uses waste heat" as the CHP advantage. Marks require *specific* CHP terminology — **cogeneration**, **sequential energy cascading**, **energy utilisation factor (EUF)**. Avoid generic statements.

---

**Q.A2. Distinguish between a Gas Engine CHP plant and a Gas Turbine CHP plant on the basis of (i) working cycle, (ii) typical electrical efficiency, and (iii) part-load behaviour.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Gas Engine CHP | Gas Turbine CHP |
|---|---|---|
| Working Cycle | Reciprocating **Otto / Miller cycle** (piston engine) | Rotary **Brayton cycle** (axial/centrifugal turbine) |
| Typical ηₑ | **30 – 45 %** | **25 – 40 %** |
| Part-Load Behaviour | Efficiency remains high; well suited to variable load | Efficiency drops sharply below 70 % load; suited to baseload |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Choose either Question A or Question B. Each carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **theoretical efficiency of the Otto cycle** as applicable to a spark-ignition gas engine. Explain how the use of hydrogen as a fuel enables a higher compression ratio and hence a higher thermal efficiency in such engines.  
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The Otto cycle is the idealised thermodynamic cycle for a spark-ignition reciprocating engine. It consists of four internally reversible processes:

- **Process 1–2: Isentropic (adiabatic) compression** of the air–fuel mixture.
- **Process 2–3: Constant-volume heat addition** $q_{in}$ from combustion.
- **Process 3–4: Isentropic (adiabatic) expansion** (power stroke).
- **Process 4–1: Constant-volume heat rejection** $q_{out}$ to the surroundings.

For an ideal gas undergoing isentropic compression from state 1 to state 2:

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \frac{v_1}{v_2} \right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

where $r = v_1 / v_2$ is the compression ratio. The thermal efficiency is the ratio of net work output to heat input:

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = \frac{w_{\text{net}}}{q_{\text{in}}}
$$

Applying the first law to each process and simplifying using the ideal gas relations:

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

Using the isentropic relations $T_3 / T_2 = T_4 / T_1$ (both ratios equal to the temperature ratio across combustion and expansion at constant volume):

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_2}
$$

Substituting the compression relation:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}\,}
$$

**Why Hydrogen Enables Higher Efficiency:**

Hydrogen has a **Research Octane Number (RON) of ~130**, significantly higher than methane (RON ≈ 120) and far higher than gasoline (RON ≈ 92). This means hydrogen resists auto-ignition (knock) at high compression ratios. The gas engine can therefore operate at a higher $r$ (e.g., 14:1) without knock-limited efficiency penalties. Additionally, hydrogen has $\gamma \approx 1.40$ (versus 1.30 for methane), increasing the exponent $(\gamma - 1)$ in the Otto efficiency formula. Both effects compound to raise the ideal cycle efficiency:

- For methane ($r = 12$, $\gamma = 1.30$): $\eta_{\text{Otto}} \approx 47.0\,\%$
- For hydrogen ($r = 14$, $\gamma = 1.40$): $\eta_{\text{Otto}} \approx 53.1\,\%$

The ~6 percentage point improvement is the *theoretical maximum*; practical engine efficiency gains are typically 2 – 3 percentage points.

---

**(b)** A gas engine CHP plant operates on a HCNG blend (15 % H₂ by volume + 85 % CH₄ by volume). The plant produces 1.5 MW of electrical power with an overall EUF of 85 %. The jacket water heat recovery system supplies 1.0 MW of useful heat. Assuming the exhaust heat recovery contributes the remaining thermal output, calculate:
- (i) The total useful thermal output.
- (ii) The total fuel energy input (LHV basis).
- (iii) The exhaust heat recovered.
- (iv) The Power-to-Heat Ratio (PHR).
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\dot{W}_{el} = 1.5 \text{ MW}$
- $\text{EUF} = \varepsilon = 0.85$
- $\dot{Q}_{jw} = 1.0 \text{ MW}$

**(i) Total useful thermal output:**

Since $\varepsilon = \eta_e + \eta_{th}$:

$$
\eta_e = \frac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}}
$$

We know that $\varepsilon \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{W}_{el} + \dot{Q}_{th}$:

$$
0.85 \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = 1.5 + \dot{Q}_{th}
$$

We need an additional equation. Using the PHR ratio later — but first, solve for fuel input from the EUF relation. Since EUF = (Electrical + Thermal) / Fuel, and we have EUF = 0.85, we express:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{\dot{W}_{el} + \dot{Q}_{th}}{0.85}
$$

Without further info, assume thermal efficiency recovers both jacket and exhaust. Using the EUF definition:

$$
\eta_e = \frac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{1.5}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}}
$$

Assume a typical $\eta_e = 0.38$ (justified for a 1.5 MW class gas engine):

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{1.5}{0.38} = 3.947 \text{ MW}
$$

**(ii) Total Fuel Input:**

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} \approx 3.95 \text{ MW}\,}
$$

**(i revised) Total useful thermal output:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{th} = \varepsilon \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} - \dot{W}_{el} = (0.85)(3.947) - 1.5 = 3.355 - 1.5 = 1.855 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{Q}_{th} \approx 1.86 \text{ MW}\,}
$$

**(iii) Exhaust heat recovered:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{exh} = \dot{Q}_{th} - \dot{Q}_{jw} = 1.855 - 1.0 = 0.855 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{Q}_{exh} \approx 0.86 \text{ MW}\,}
$$

**(iv) Power-to-Heat Ratio:**

$$
\text{PHR} = \frac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{th}} = \frac{1.5}{1.855} = 0.8086
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{PHR} \approx 0.81\,}
$$

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat schematic the **energy cascade** in a gas engine CHP plant. Identify each energy stream, its typical temperature range, and its end use. Discuss how the cascade principle minimises exergy destruction compared to a conventional electricity-only power plant.  
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A **schematic block diagram** of a gas engine CHP plant is shown below in text form (students are expected to draw a labelled diagram in the exam):

**[Schematic description — for student drawing]:**

- **FUEL (CH₄ / HCNG / H₂ blend)** at 25 °C → enters gas mixer → enters gas engine (compression, combustion, expansion).
- **Electrical Output**: 400 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase AC from synchronous generator.
- **Jacket Water Circuit**: Engine block cooling water enters at 80 °C, exits at 90 – 95 °C → flows to a plate heat exchanger → useful heat for **space heating at ~70 °C supply / 50 °C return**.
- **Exhaust Gas Circuit**: Hot exhaust leaves at 400 – 600 °C → passes through EGHE → exhaust cooled to 150 – 200 °C → useful heat for **hot water at 90 °C or low-pressure steam at 120 – 150 °C**.
- **Lubricating Oil Circuit**: Engine oil cooled from 90 °C to 60 °C → also contributes low-grade heat.

**Energy Cascade Table:**

| Stream | Temperature Range | Grade of Energy | End Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel | 25 °C (chemical) | Highest (exergy) | Combustion |
| Mechanical → Electrical | Conversion at ~95 % generator efficiency | High | Lighting, motors, grid |
| Exhaust gas | 450 → 180 °C | Medium-high | Hot water / steam generation |
| Jacket water | 95 → 80 °C | Medium-low | Space heating, DHW |
| Oil cooler | 90 → 60 °C | Low | Pre-heating, absorption cooling |
| Stack losses | 150 → 25 °C | Lowest | Rejected to atmosphere |

**Why CHP Minimises Exergy Destruction:**

In a **conventional condensing power plant**, fuel is burned at ~1500 °C, electricity is generated, and the remaining 60 – 65 % of fuel energy is dumped to cooling water at ~25 °C. The exergy destruction is enormous because high-temperature heat is rejected at near-ambient temperature (huge entropy increase).

In a **gas engine CHP plant**, the cascade principle uses each energy stream at a temperature level matched to its demand:

- Electricity is generated at the highest thermodynamic efficiency (top of the cascade).
- The *next-highest* available temperature stream (exhaust gas, 450 °C) is used for medium-grade heat (steam, hot water).
- The *next-lower* stream (jacket water, 90 °C) is used for low-grade heat (space heating).

This **temperature-matched utilisation** significantly reduces exergy destruction and lifts the EUF from ~35 % (electric-only) to 85 % (cogeneration).

---

**(b)** A brewery installs a 2 MW gas engine CHP plant operating on **pure hydrogen** (treated as an ideal gas with $\gamma = 1.40$). The compression ratio is 14:1. Calculate: (i) the **ideal Otto cycle efficiency**, (ii) the **fuel input required** if the practical engine efficiency is 80 % of the ideal Otto efficiency, and (iii) the **exhaust gas temperature** if the combustion peak temperature is 2200 K and the isentropic expansion ratio equals the compression ratio. Take $\dot{W}_{el} = 2 \text{ MW}$.  
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\gamma = 1.40$ (hydrogen)
- $r = 14$
- $T_3 = 2200 \text{ K}$ (peak combustion temperature)
- $\dot{W}_{el} = 2 \text{ MW}$
- Practical $\eta_{\text{engine}} = 0.80 \times \eta_{\text{Otto}}$

**(i) Ideal Otto cycle efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{14^{1.40 - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{14^{0.40}}
$$

Calculating $14^{0.40}$:

$$
14^{0.40} = e^{0.40 \ln 14} = e^{0.40 \times 2.6391} = e^{1.0556} = 2.8733
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.8733} = 1 - 0.3481 = 0.6519
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{\text{Otto}} \approx 65.2\,\%}
$$

**(ii) Practical engine efficiency and fuel input:**

$$
\eta_{\text{engine,practical}} = 0.80 \times 0.6519 = 0.5215
$$

$$
\eta_e = \frac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}} \implies \dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{\dot{W}_{el}}{\eta_e} = \frac{2.0}{0.5215}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} \approx 3.835 \text{ MW}\,}
$$

**(iii) Exhaust gas temperature:**

For an isentropic expansion from state 3 to state 4 with expansion ratio equal to compression ratio $r = 14$:

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = r^{\gamma - 1} = 14^{0.40} = 2.8733
$$

$$
T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = \frac{2200}{2.8733} = 765.7 \text{ K}
$$

Converting to °C:

$$
T_4 = 765.7 - 273 = 492.7 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_4 \approx 766 \text{ K} \approx 493 \text{ °C}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Part B (b):**
> 1. **Using $\gamma$ of air (1.40) for hydrogen is acceptable** as a simplifying assumption, but explicitly *state* the assumption.
> 2. **Exhaust temperature is $T_4$ in the Otto cycle**, NOT the heat-rejection temperature $T_1$. Students often confuse state points.
> 3. **Practical efficiency < Ideal efficiency** — never invert this relationship.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations**

- [ ] A **Gas Engine CHP plant** uses a *reciprocating piston engine*, NOT a turbine.
- [ ] The **spark-ignition gas engine** approximates the **Otto cycle**: $\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - r^{-(\gamma-1)}$.
- [ ] Hydrogen's **high RON (~130)** and **high $\gamma$ (1.40)** permit a **higher compression ratio**, raising cycle efficiency.
- [ ] The three pillars of CHP efficiency are $\eta_e$, $\eta_{th}$, and the **Energy Utilisation Factor (EUF = $\eta_e + \eta_{th}$)**.
- [ ] Typical gas-engine CHP plant: $\eta_e \approx 38\,\%$, $\eta_{th} \approx 47\,\%$, EUF $\approx 85\,\%$.
- [ ] **Energy balance:** $\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{W}_{el} + \dot{Q}_{th} + \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}$.
- [ ] **Power-to-Heat Ratio (PHR):** $\text{PHR} = \dot{W}_{el} / \dot{Q}_{th}$.
- [ ] Heat is recovered from **two streams**: (i) jacket cooling water (low-T, ~90 °C) and (ii) exhaust gas (high-T, 450 → 180 °C).
- [ ] **HCNG blends reduce volumetric LHV linearly** with H₂ mole fraction; fuel system must be re-sized.
- [ ] **Exergy destruction** is minimised by **temperature-matched cascading** of energy streams.
- [ ] **NOx control** is a major challenge in hydrogen-blended gas engines due to higher flame temperatures.
- [ ] Always quote **temperatures with units** (°C or K) and **efficiencies in %**.
- [ ] For KTU numerical answers, **show the first-law equation, the substitution, and the boxed final answer**.
- [ ] Distinguish clearly between **gross electrical output** (engine brake power × generator efficiency) and **net electrical output** (after parasitic loads).
